topics.title: Introduction to Java - Structure of a Simple Java Program
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Java - Structure of a Simple Java Program

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java** is a high-level, object-oriented, platform-independent, robust, secure, and architecture-neutral programming language developed by **James Gosling** and his team at **Sun Microsystems** in **1991**, officially released in **1995**. It follows the philosophy of **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** because Java source code is compiled into an intermediate form called **bytecode**, which is executed by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**.

The official KTU 2024 syllabus defines the introductory Java concepts under the following technical pillars:

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Platform Independence** | Achieved through bytecode + JVM architecture |
| **Object Orientation** | Everything in Java is organized around classes and objects |
| **Compiled + Interpreted** | Compiled to bytecode, interpreted/executed by JVM |
| **Strongly Typed** | Every variable must be declared with a data type |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Java like a Universal Food Recipe Translator.**

Imagine you have a **recipe book written in English** (your Java source code). If you want an Indian chef, a French chef, and a Japanese chef to cook the dish, you have two bad options:
1. **Translate the recipe** into three languages (this is what compilers for C/C++ do — they create platform-specific `.exe` files).
2. **Hire a Universal Translator** who reads English and instructs each chef in their own language. This is exactly what **Java does**: the source code is compiled once into a universal language called **bytecode**, and the **JVM** (Universal Translator) installed on each machine converts it into machine-specific instructions at runtime.

This is the essence of **"Write Once, Run Anywhere."**

---

### 1.3 Standard Java Terminology & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three core acronyms you **must** memorize for KTU exams:

- **JDK (Java Development Kit)** — The complete **software development kit** required to **develop, compile, and debug** Java programs. Contains the **JRE + development tools** (compiler `javac`, debugger `jdb`, documentation tool `javadoc`).
- **JRE (Java Runtime Environment)** — The **runtime environment** required to **run** Java programs. Contains the **JVM + core libraries (rt.jar)**.
- **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** — The **abstract machine** that actually executes the bytecode. It is **platform-dependent**, but the bytecode it executes is **platform-independent**.

**Hierarchy (inclusion relationship):**

$$
\text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM}
$$

**Standard metrics (memorize):**

- **Latest LTS Java Version (as of 2024):** **Java 21** (LTS) — **Java 17** is also widely used in KTU lab setups.
- **Original release year:** **1995** (Sun Microsystems).
- **Acquired by Oracle:** **2010**.
- **Original name of Java:** **"Oak"** (later renamed to **"Java"** inspired by Java coffee).

---

### 1.4 What is a Simple Java Program?

> [!NOTE]
> A **Simple Java Program** in the context of Module 1 refers to a **single-class, single-method Java application** that contains the `main()` method — the **entry point** of execution for the JVM.

The minimal structure contains the following six logical components:

1. **Documentation Section** (comments)
2. **Package Statement** (optional, but standard)
3. **Import Statements** (optional)
4. **Class Declaration** (mandatory)
5. **Interface Declarations** (optional)
6. **Class Body** containing `main()` method (mandatory for execution)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Breakdown of a Java Program's Structure

A Java program is **not just code** — it is a structured document with **six well-defined sections**. KTU examiners frequently test whether students know the *order* and *purpose* of each section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** The order of sections in a Java source file is fixed by convention (not by compiler), and KTU questions often test this order.

**Section 1: Documentation Section**

Written using **comments**. The compiler ignores them. Three valid forms:

```java
// Single-line comment

/* Multi-line
   comment */

/**
 * Documentation comment (used by javadoc tool to generate HTML docs)
 */
```

**Why it matters:** Documentation comments are not just for readability — the **`javadoc`** utility in the JDK parses `/** ... */` comments to auto-generate **HTML API documentation**.

**Section 2: Package Statement**

```java
package com.ktu.student;
```

- Must be the **first statement** in the file (excluding comments).
- Maps to a **directory structure**: `com/ktu/student/`.
- Used for **namespace management** — prevents class name collisions.

**Section 3: Import Statements**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
import java.io.*;
```

- Allows the programmer to use **predefined classes** from Java's standard library (API packages) without writing fully-qualified names.
- `java.lang.*` is **automatically imported** (it contains `String`, `System`, `Math`, etc.).

**Section 4: Class Declaration**

```java
public class HelloWorld { ... }
```

- Every Java program is **at least one class definition**.
- Java does **not allow** top-level functions/variables — everything must be inside a class.
- The **filename MUST match** the `public class` name (`HelloWorld.java` here).

**Section 5: Interface Declarations (Optional)**

```java
interface MyInterface { ... }
```

**Section 6: Class Body — the `main()` Method**

```java
public static void main(String[] args) { ... }
```

- This is the **entry point** of execution.
- The JVM invokes `main()` when the class is executed.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Anatomy of `main()`

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU 3-mark questions:** You must know **why each keyword is mandatory** in the `main()` signature.

| Keyword | Meaning | What happens if changed? |
|---|---|---|
| `public` | Access modifier — visible to JVM from anywhere | `Main method not found` runtime error |
| `static` | Belongs to the class, not an object | JVM cannot call it without instantiating the class |
| `void` | Returns no value | JVM is designed to ignore any return value |
| `main` | Pre-defined identifier recognized by JVM | JVM cannot locate the entry point |
| `String[] args` | Command-line argument array | `args` can be renamed (e.g., `argv`) but type cannot change |

---

### 2.3 Java Compilation & Execution Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks:** "Explain the steps involved in compiling and executing a Java program." This is a guaranteed **3-mark** or **7-mark** question.

The two-step process:

$$
\text{Source Code (.java)} \xrightarrow{\text{javac compiler}} \text{Bytecode (.class)} \xrightarrow{\text{JVM (java interpreter)}} \text{Machine Code}
$$

**Step 1 — Compilation:**

The **`javac`** compiler (part of JDK) reads the `.java` file and produces a `.class` file containing **bytecode** — a set of instructions for the JVM, not the physical machine.

**Step 2 — Execution:**

The **`java`** launcher invokes the JVM, which performs the following sub-steps internally:

1. **Class Loader** — loads the `.class` file into memory.
2. **Bytecode Verifier** — checks for illegal code that could violate Java's safety rules.
3. **Interpreter / JIT Compiler** — translates bytecode to native machine code and executes it.

---

### 2.4 Standard Java Program (Canonical Example)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical "Hello, World!" program** in Java. KTU frequently asks students to **identify each line** or **write the program from memory**.

```java
// File: HelloWorld.java
package com.ktu.demo;                    // Package statement

import java.lang.*;                      // (Implicit, shown explicitly for teaching)

public class HelloWorld {                 // Class declaration
    public static void main(String[] args) {  // main() method
        System.out.println("Hello, World!");  // Output statement
    }
}
```

**Line-by-line KTU-style explanation:**

- **Line 2:** `package com.ktu.demo;` — declares that the class belongs to the `com.ktu.demo` package.
- **Line 4:** `import java.lang.*;` — imports the `java.lang` package (implicit by default).
- **Line 6:** `public class HelloWorld` — declares a public class named `HelloWorld`. The filename must be `HelloWorld.java`.
- **Line 7:** `public static void main(String[] args)` — the entry point of execution.
- **Line 8:** `System.out.println("Hello, World!");` — prints the string to the console. `System` is a final class in `java.lang`, `out` is a `PrintStream` object, and `println` is a method.

**Compilation & execution commands (in terminal/command prompt):**

```bash
javac HelloWorld.java        # produces HelloWorld.class
java HelloWorld              # runs the bytecode (no .class extension in command)
```

**Expected output:**

```
Hello, World!
```

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU covers this:** Java is the de-facto language for **enterprise backend systems, Android development, embedded systems, and large-scale distributed computing** (Hadoop, Apache Kafka are written in Java).

| Application Domain | Java's Role |
|---|---|
| **Android Apps** | Native Android SDK is Java-based |
| **Web Backends** | Spring, Jakarta EE (formerly Java EE) |
| **Big Data** | Apache Hadoop, Apache Spark, Apache Kafka |
| **Banking Systems** | Robust, secure — used in 90% of brokerage systems |
| **IoT / Embedded** | Java ME (Micro Edition) for smart cards, sensors |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Compilation-Execution Pipeline (Exhaustive Step Trace)

Although this topic is descriptive, the **"compilation and execution"** question is one of the most common KTU 14-mark questions. Below is the **exhaustive trace** of what happens when you type `java HelloWorld` in the terminal.

---

**Given:**

```java
// HelloWorld.java
public class HelloWorld {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello, KTU!");
    }
}
```

---

**Step 1: The user invokes the compiler**

```bash
javac HelloWorld.java
```

**Explanation:** The `javac` compiler (Java compiler, written in Java itself) reads the source file. It performs:
- **Lexical analysis** (breaks code into tokens)
- **Syntax analysis** (parses tokens into an Abstract Syntax Tree)
- **Semantic analysis** (type checking, name resolution)
- **Bytecode generation** (emits `.class` file)

---

**Step 2: Bytecode is produced**

If successful, the compiler creates `HelloWorld.class` containing **bytecode** — a stream of instructions for the JVM. The bytecode for our program conceptually contains the following `main()` method descriptor:

$$
\text{Method Signature (in bytecode):} \quad \texttt{public static void main(String[])}
$$

This is the exact binary signature the JVM looks for.

---

**Step 3: The user invokes the runtime launcher**

```bash
java HelloWorld
```

**Explanation:** Note carefully: the user does **NOT** type `java HelloWorld.class`. The `java` command:
1. Locates the class `HelloWorld` using the **classpath**.
2. Loads it using the **ClassLoader subsystem**.
3. Verifies the bytecode using the **Bytecode Verifier**.
4. Finds the `main()` method.
5. Invokes `main()` by passing an empty `String[]` (or the command-line arguments).

---

**Step 4: JVM execution engine runs the bytecode**

The execution engine uses either:
- **Interpreter:** reads and executes bytecode line-by-line (slow but quick startup).
- **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler:** detects "hot" methods and compiles them to native machine code for subsequent calls (faster execution).

---

**Step 5: Output is produced**

The `println` method on `System.out` writes the bytes of the string `"Hello, KTU!"` to **standard output (stdout)**, which the terminal displays.

---

**Terminal output:**

$$
\text{Console:} \quad \texttt{Hello, KTU!}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** These are the **top 5 mistakes** KTU examiners mark down. Memorize the corrections.

| \# | Common Mistake | Correction | Marks Lost |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Saving the file as `helloworld.java` when class is `public class HelloWorld` | Filename must **exactly match** the public class name, including case | -2 |
| 2 | Writing `void main(String args)` (missing `static`) | `main` **must** be `static` so JVM can call it without an object | -1 |
| 3 | Writing `public static int main(...)` | JVM ignores the return value; signature **must** be `void` | -1 |
| 4 | Forgetting semicolons `;` | Java is a C-style language — every statement ends with `;` | -1 |
| 5 | Writing `system.out.println(...)` (lowercase `s`) | `System` is a class in `java.lang`; capitalization matters — Java is **case-sensitive** | -1 |

---

### 3.3 KTU-Standard Program Trace — Command-Line Arguments

A very common 7-mark sub-question: *"What will be the output of the following program?"* involving `args[]`.

**Program:**

```java
public class DemoArgs {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Length = " + args.length);
        System.out.println("First  = " + args[0]);
        System.out.println("Second = " + args[1]);
    }
}
```

**Invocation:**

```bash
java DemoArgs KTU 2024
```

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

**Step 1:** `args` is a `String[]` array. The runtime fills it with everything after the class name on the command line.

**Step 2:** Tokenization: `args[0] = "KTU"`, `args[1] = "2024"`.

**Step 3:** Compute `args.length`:

$$
\text{args.length} = 2
$$

**Step 4:** String concatenation: `"Length = " + args.length` performs string conversion of `2` to `"2"`, yielding `"Length = 2"`.

**Final Output:**

```
Length = 2
First  = KTU
Second = 2024
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU:** If no arguments are passed (`java DemoArgs` alone), then `args.length = 0`, and accessing `args[0]` throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — JDK, JRE, and JVM Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, no reserved keywords used as node names, and labels are kept in clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["JDK JAVA DEVELOPMENT KIT"] --> B["JRE JAVA RUNTIME ENVIRONMENT"]
    A --> C1["DEV TOOLS JAVAC JDB JAVADOC"]
    B --> D["JVM JAVA VIRTUAL MACHINE"]
    B --> E1["CORE LIBS RT JAR JAVA LANG JAVA UTIL"]
    D --> F1["CLASS LOADER"]
    D --> F2["BYTECODE VERIFIER"]
    D --> F3["EXECUTION ENGINE INTERPRETER JIT"]
    F3 --> G1["RUNTIME DATA AREAS HEAP STACK PC REGISTERS"]
    A --> C2["COMPILER JAVAC EXE"]
    A --> C3["DEBUGGER JDB EXE"]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- **JDK** is the superset — it contains the JRE plus developer tools (`javac`, `jdb`, `javadoc`).
- **JRE** is a subset — it contains the JVM plus the core Java class libraries.
- **JVM** is the innermost engine — it loads, verifies, and executes the bytecode using the class loader, verifier, and execution engine.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Java Program Compilation & Execution Flow

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram maps the journey of a Java file from the developer's editor to the screen output. It is the **most likely 7-mark KTU question** from this module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["STEP 1 SOURCE CODE HELLO JAVA"] --> P2["STEP 2 JAVAC COMPILER JDK"]
    P2 --> P3["STEP 3 BYTECODE HELLO CLASS"]
    P3 --> P4["STEP 4 CLASS LOADER SUBSYSTEM"]
    P4 --> P5["STEP 5 BYTECODE VERIFIER"]
    P5 --> P6["STEP 6 EXECUTION ENGINE"]
    P6 --> P7["STEP 7 RUNTIME DATA AREAS HEAP STACK"]
    P6 --> P8["STEP 8 NATIVE MACHINE CODE OUTPUT"]
    P8 --> P9["STEP 9 CONSOLE OUTPUT HELLO KTU"]
    style P1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style P2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style P3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style P4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style P5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style P6 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style P9 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Mapping to the KTU 14-mark model answer:**

| Step | Component | Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Source code written in `.java` file | 1 |
| 2 | `javac` compiler invoked | 2 |
| 3 | `.class` file (bytecode) generated | 2 |
| 4 | `java` command invokes the JVM | 2 |
| 5 | ClassLoader + Verifier | 3 |
| 6 | Interpreter/JIT produces output | 2 |
| 7 | Final output displayed | 1 |

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Section-by-Section Layout of a Java Source File

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram shows the **canonical order** of sections in a Java source file, which is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["SECTION 1 DOCUMENTATION COMMENTS"] --> S2["SECTION 2 PACKAGE STATEMENT"]
    S2 --> S3["SECTION 3 IMPORT STATEMENTS"]
    S3 --> S4["SECTION 4 CLASS OR INTERFACE DECLARATIONS"]
    S4 --> S5["SECTION 5 CLASS BODY VARIABLES METHODS CONSTRUCTORS"]
    S5 --> S6["SECTION 6 MAIN METHOD ENTRY POINT"]
    style S1 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style S2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style S3 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style S4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style S5 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style S6 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**List any three features of Java that make it platform-independent.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Bytecode Generation:** Java source code is compiled into **bytecode** (an intermediate representation), not into platform-specific machine code. *(1 mark)*
2. **JVM as Abstraction Layer:** The **Java Virtual Machine** is implemented separately for each platform (Windows JVM, Linux JVM, macOS JVM). The same bytecode runs on any of them. *(1 mark)*
3. **WORA Philosophy:** This is summarized as **"Write Once, Run Anywhere"** — write the `.java` file once, compile once to `.class`, and execute on any platform that has a compatible JVM. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Many students confuse "platform-independent language" with "platform-independent JVM." The JVM itself is platform-dependent — only the **bytecode** is platform-independent.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Explain the difference between JDK, JRE, and JVM with a suitable diagram or table.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Component | Full Form | Purpose | Includes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **JDK** | Java Development Kit | Used to **develop** Java applications | JRE + development tools (`javac`, `jdb`, `javadoc`) |
| **JRE** | Java Runtime Environment | Used to **run** Java applications | JVM + core libraries (`rt.jar`) |
| **JVM** | Java Virtual Machine | Executes the bytecode | Class loader, verifier, execution engine |

**Inclusion relationship:** $ \text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM} $ *(1 mark for relationship)*

> [!NOTE]
> If a student writes "JDK is used to run Java programs" — that is **partially correct** (since JDK contains JRE), but the **specific purpose of JDK is development**. Examiners look for precision.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** In Part B, students must answer **either Option A OR Option B** (internal choice). Both questions carry equal marks (14) with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of a simple Java program with a suitable example. List each section and its purpose. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the process of compiling and executing a Java program. What is the role of the JVM in this process? *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Structure of a simple Java program — six sections:**

**1. Documentation Section (1 mark)**

The first section consists of comments that describe the program. Java supports three types:

```java
// single-line comment
/* multi-line comment */
/** documentation comment for javadoc */
```

Comments are ignored by the compiler but are **essential for readability** and for generating HTML documentation via the `javadoc` tool.

**2. Package Statement (1 mark)**

```java
package com.ktu.demo;
```

This declares the **namespace** of the class. It must be the first non-comment statement. It maps to a directory structure on the file system.

**3. Import Statements (1 mark)**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
```

Used to access **predefined classes** from the Java API. The package `java.lang.*` is auto-imported.

**4. Class Declaration (2 marks)**

```java
public class HelloWorld { ... }
```

Every Java program is **at least one class**. The **filename must match** the `public class` name.

**5 & 6. Class Body and `main()` Method (2 marks)**

```java
public static void main(String[] args) {
    System.out.println("Hello, KTU!");
}
```

The `main()` method is the **entry point**. The keywords `public static void` are mandatory.

**Complete Example Program:**

```java
package com.ktu.demo;

public class HelloWorld {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello, KTU!");
    }
}
```

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Compilation & Execution Process (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compilation:**

The `javac` compiler (part of JDK) translates the `.java` source file into a `.class` file containing **bytecode**.

```bash
javac HelloWorld.java
```

**Output:** `HelloWorld.class` is generated.

**Step 2 — Execution:**

The `java` command launches the JVM, which loads the `.class` file and runs it.

```bash
java HelloWorld
```

**Output:** `Hello, KTU!` is displayed on the console.

**Step 3 — JVM Internal Workflow (2 marks):**

The JVM performs the following sub-tasks:

1. **Loading** the `.class` file using the **ClassLoader**.
2. **Verification** of bytecode for safety and correctness.
3. **Execution** by the **Execution Engine** (interpreter + JIT compiler).

**Role of the JVM:**

- Provides **platform independence** — the same bytecode runs on any OS.
- Provides **memory management** via garbage collection.
- Enforces **security** via the bytecode verifier and SecurityManager.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What are the key features of Java? Explain any five with brief examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program to accept two numbers from the command line and display their sum. Explain the role of `String[] args` in your program. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Five Key Features of Java:**

**1. Simple (1 mark):** Java eliminated complex features of C/C++ like pointers, multiple inheritance, and operator overloading. Memory management is automatic (garbage collection).

**2. Object-Oriented (2 marks):** Everything in Java is an object (except primitives). It supports **encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction**.

**3. Platform Independent (1 mark):** Compiles to bytecode, runs on any JVM. WORA principle.

**4. Robust and Secure (1 mark):** Strong type checking, exception handling, no pointers, bytecode verifier, SecurityManager.

**5. Multithreaded (1 mark):** Built-in `Thread` class supports concurrent execution.

**6. Distributed (1 mark):** Supports RMI (Remote Method Invocation) and EJB for distributed applications.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Program (4 marks):**

```java
public class SumDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = Integer.parseInt(args[0]);
        int b = Integer.parseInt(args[1]);
        int sum = a + b;
        System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);
    }
}
```

**Compilation & execution:**

```bash
javac SumDemo.java
java SumDemo 25 30
```

**Output:**

```
Sum = 55
```

**Explanation of `String[] args` (3 marks):**

- `args` is a **String array** that holds the **command-line arguments** passed to the program.
- `args[0]` is the first argument (`"25"`), `args[1]` is the second (`"30"`).
- Since they are **Strings**, we use `Integer.parseInt()` to convert them to integers.
- The name `args` can be changed (e.g., `argv`), but the type `String[]` cannot.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing JDK/JRE/JVM** — saying "JDK is used to run programs" loses 1 mark. Be precise.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention `static`** in the `main()` signature — minimum 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Writing filename as `Helloworld.java` when class is `HelloWorld`** — case sensitivity is strictly enforced.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark "structure of Java program" questions — KTU's marking scheme allocates **1–2 marks for a diagram**.
> 5. **Saying "Java is interpreted"** — Java is **both compiled and interpreted** (bytecode interpretation). Half-mark cut for this error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:**

- **Java** was developed by **James Gosling** at **Sun Microsystems** in **1991** (released **1995**); original name was **Oak**.
- Java follows **WORA** — Write Once, Run Anywhere.
- The three pillars are **JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM** (inclusion, not equality).
- **JVM is platform-dependent**, but the **bytecode is platform-independent**.
- A Java source file **must be named** after the `public class` inside it (case-sensitive).
- The `main()` signature is **fixed**: `public static void main(String[] args)`.
- Every statement in Java ends with a **semicolon** `;`.
- Java is **case-sensitive** — `System` ≠ `system`.
- Compilation command: **`javac FileName.java`** → produces `.class` file (bytecode).
- Execution command: **`java ClassName`** → runs the bytecode via JVM.
- The **JVM workflow** is: ClassLoader → Bytecode Verifier → Execution Engine (Interpreter + JIT).
- The `String[] args` in `main()` holds **command-line arguments**; access them as `args[0]`, `args[1]`, etc.
- `java.lang` package is **auto-imported**; all other packages must be explicitly imported.
- Java supports **three comment styles**: `//`, `/* */`, and `/** */`.
- A Java file can have **only one public class**, but multiple non-public classes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Java Programming Environment and Runtime Environment (Command Line and IDE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Java Programming Environment and Runtime Environment (Command Line and IDE) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Java Platform: Formal Definition The **Java Platform** is a suite of programs that facilitate the development and execution of programs written in the Java programming language. It is officially distributed by Oracle Corporation (and formerly Sun Microsystems) and consists of three nested, hierarchical components. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mandates that students understand this ecosystem as the foundation of all subsequent Object-Oriented Programming modules. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Terminology Hierarchy (Largest to Smallest):** > 1. **JDK (Java Development Kit)** — The complete software development kit used by developers to *write, compile, debug, and package* Java applications. > 2. **JRE (Java Runtime Environment)** — The subset of the JDK that provides the *libraries, Java Virtual Machine, and other components* required to *run* already-compiled Java programs. > 3. **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** — The abstract computing machine (a runtime engine) that *executes Java bytecode* on a specific operating system. It is the heart of Java's famous "Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA) paradigm. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics & Acronyms to Memorize for KTU Exams:** > - **Bytecode:** Platform-neutral intermediate representation stored in `.class` files (not human-readable machine code, not source code). > - **WORA:** Write Once, Run Anywhere — possible because bytecode is interpreted by the platform-specific JVM. > - **API:** Application Programming Interface — bundled libraries inside the JRE. > - **CLASSPATH:** Environment variable/path that tells the JVM where to find user-defined and library `.class` files. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "International Restaurant" 🍽️ Imagine you have written a **recipe book in a universal language** (this is your **Java source code**). Now, to actually cook the dish in any country in the world: | Real-World Element | Java Equivalent | |---|---| | The complete chef's toolkit (knives, pans, oven, recipe translator, kitchen) | **JDK** (compilers, debuggers, JRE, JVM) | | The kitchen itself (ovens, utensils, water supply) without the recipe-translator tools | **JRE** (libraries + JVM) | | The actual oven that understands the universal recipe and cooks the food | **JVM** (executes bytecode) | | The universal recipe (in English) | **`.java` source file** | | The translated recipe in the local language of each country | **`.class` bytecode** | > **The Key Insight:** The JDK is the developer's workshop. The JRE is what end-users need to *run* programs. The JVM is the silent, invisible engine that runs everywhere — Windows, Linux, macOS, even inside a smart card. A developer must install the **JDK**; a regular end-user only needs the **JRE**. --- ### 1.3 Why Does KTU Emphasize This in Module 1? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 Learning Outcomes:** > Module 1 of PECMT535 is designed to provide the *practical runtime context* before introducing OOP syntax. Without understanding the compilation-execution pipeline, students cannot diagnose errors like `ClassNotFoundException`, `NoClassDefFoundError`, `Classpath not found`, or `UnsatisfiedLinkError`. Every Java concept that follows (classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism) will be *compiled* and *run* through this exact pipeline. Therefore, mastery of the Java environment is a **prerequisite gate** for the entire course. --- ### 1.4 Two Modes of Development The KTU syllabus explicitly requires proficiency in **both** environments: 1. **Command-Line Environment (CLI)** — Using the JDK's terminal tools (`javac`, `java`, `javadoc`, `jar`). This is the academic, exam-relevant mode that KTU questions are based on. 2. **Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** — Using software like **Eclipse IDE, IntelliJ IDEA, NetBeans, or VS Code**. This is the industry-standard mode for professional development. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** KTU theory questions (Part A & Part B) almost always test the **command-line workflow** because it directly exposes the underlying compilation, interpretation, and bytecode-loading process. IDEs abstract this away, which is *why* the university insists on the CLI approach. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Java Development Lifecycle — Step-by-Step Logic When you write a Java program, it passes through a **strict, well-defined pipeline**. Let us dissect this pipeline into its logical stages: #### Stage 1 → Source Code Creation (`.java` File) - The programmer writes human-readable Java code in a text editor or IDE. - File extension: **`.java`** - Example: `HelloWorld.java` #### Stage 2 → Compilation (Bytecode Generation) - The **Java Compiler** (`javac`, an executable in the JDK's `bin/` folder) reads the source file. - It performs **lexical analysis, syntax analysis, semantic analysis, and bytecode generation**. - Output: A **`.class` file** containing **bytecode** — instructions for the JVM, not for any physical CPU. - Bytecode is a *stream of opcodes* (single-byte instructions like `iload`, `invokevirtual`, `return`) understood only by the JVM. #### Stage 3 → Class Loading (On Program Execution) - The **ClassLoader Subsystem** of the JVM loads the `.class` file from disk, network, or another source. - Three built-in class loaders cooperate: **Bootstrap ClassLoader → Platform ClassLoader → Application ClassLoader**. - The class is verified for security and structural integrity (no stack overflows, no illegal bytecode, etc.). #### Stage 4 → Bytecode Verification - The **Bytecode Verifier** ensures the loaded class: - Does not forge pointers. - Does not violate access modifiers. - Uses operands correctly on the operand stack. - This is a critical security feature that prevents malicious applets from harming the host system. #### Stage 5 → Execution (Interpretation + JIT Compilation) - The **Execution Engine** of the JVM executes the bytecode. - Two sub-engines work in tandem: - **Interpreter:** Reads and executes bytecode line-by-line (slower, but starts immediately). - **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler:** Detects "hot" code paths and compiles them into **native machine code** at runtime, caching the result for blazing-fast subsequent calls. - Output is sent to the console, GUI, or any output stream. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Bytecode:** > Compiling directly to native code (like C++ does) would tie the program to one OS. By compiling to bytecode, Java decouples the *language* from the *platform*. Each platform's JVM acts as a hardware abstraction layer — the same bytecode runs unchanged on Windows, Linux, or macOS. --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Components, Commands & Syntax > [!IMPORTANT] > **This table is mandatory memorization for KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.** | Component | Type | Purpose | KTU-Relevant Detail | |---|---|---|---| | **JDK** | Software Development Kit | Write + Compile + Debug + Run Java code | Contains JRE + development tools | | **JRE** | Runtime Environment | Run (not write) Java programs | Contains JVM + class libraries | | **JVM** | Abstract Runtime Engine | Execute bytecode on a specific OS | The actual "machine" that runs your code | | **`javac`** | Compiler (JDK) | `.java` → `.class` (bytecode) | Invoked as `javac FileName.java` | | **`java`** | Launcher (JRE) | Starts JVM & runs the `.class` file | Invoked as `java ClassName` (no `.class` extension) | | **`javadoc`** | Documentation Tool | Generates HTML API docs from comments | Exam favorite: "Explain `javadoc`" | | **`jar`** | Archiver (JDK) | Packages multiple `.class` files into `.jar` (Java ARchive) | Similar to `.zip` | | **`jdb`** | Debugger (JDK) | Command-line Java debugger | Used to set breakpoints, step through code | | **CLASSPATH** | Env Variable / `-cp` flag | Tells JVM *where* to find user/library classes | Default: current directory (`.`) | | **PATH** | Env Variable | Tells OS *where* to find executables like `javac`, `java` | Must include JDK's `bin` directory | --- ### 2.3 The Command-Line Workflow — Annotated > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme students are expected to perform this exact sequence. It will appear in Part A questions.** ```bash # Step 1: Open a terminal / command prompt # Step 2: Navigate to the folder containing HelloWorld.java cd C:\JavaPrograms # Step 3: Compile the source file (JDK tool: javac) # Output: HelloWorld.class is generated in the same directory javac HelloWorld.java # Step 4: Run the program (JRE tool: java launcher) # Note: NO .class extension is used java HelloWorld # Step 5 (Optional): Generate API documentation javadoc HelloWorld.java # Step 6 (Optional): Package into a JAR jar cvf HelloWorld.jar HelloWorld.class # Step 7 (Optional): Run a JAR file java -jar HelloWorld.jar ``` --- ### 2.4 Environment Variables — The Two Critical Paths > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Practical Mistake:** Students set `CLASSPATH` but forget to add `.` (current directory) to it. Then they get `ClassNotFoundException` because the JVM cannot find their own class files in the working directory. | Variable | What It Stores | Typical Value (Windows) | Typical Value (Linux/macOS) | |---|---|---|---| | **`JAVA_HOME`** | Installation root of JDK | `C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-21` | `/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk` | | **`PATH`** | OS search path for executables | `%JAVA_HOME%\bin` | `$JAVA_HOME/bin` | | **`CLASSPATH`** | JVM search path for `.class` files | `.;%JAVA_HOME%\lib` | `.:$JAVA_HOME/lib` | --- ### 2.5 IDE Environment — The Industry-Standard Tools > [!NOTE] > **KTU Expectation:** You should be able to *name, describe, and compare* the major IDEs. Hands-on lab sessions typically use **Eclipse** or **IntelliJ IDEA** (Community Edition). | IDE | Developer | KTU-Relevant Feature | |---|---|---| | **Eclipse IDE for Java Developers** | Eclipse Foundation | Free, open-source, plugin-based, popular in academia | | **IntelliJ IDEA** | JetBrains | Industry standard, smart code completion, refactoring (Community Edition is free) | | **NetBeans** | Apache Software Foundation | Officially supported by Oracle, drag-and-drop GUI builder | | **VS Code** | Microsoft | Lightweight, requires "Extension Pack for Java" to function fully | **Advantages of an IDE over Command-Line:** - **Syntax highlighting & auto-completion** (faster coding, fewer typos). - **Built-in debugger** (set breakpoints, inspect variables in real time). - **Project management** (handles hundreds of files, packages, dependencies). - **Integrated build tools** (Maven, Gradle support out-of-the-box). - **Refactoring tools** (rename, extract method, inline variable). **Disadvantages / Trade-offs:** - Hides the *underlying* compile-execute pipeline (which is exactly what KTU wants you to learn). - Resource-heavy (RAM, CPU consumption). - Steeper initial learning curve. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **This section traces a single Java program from source code to running output, exposing every intermediate stage. This is the exact depth required for a KTU 14-mark Part B question.** ### 3.1 Sample Java Program Used for the Walk-Through ```java // File: Greeting.java public class Greeting { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Welcome to KTU 2024 OOP Course!"); } } ``` **Rule 1 (KTU Favourite):** The filename **must** match the `public class` name. Here, both are `Greeting`, so the file is `Greeting.java`. **Rule 2:** The entry point of every standalone Java application is the `main` method with the exact signature: ```java public static void main(String[] args) ``` --- ### 3.2 Stage-by-Stage Execution Trace #### ▸ Step 1: Source File Storage The text `Greeting.java` is saved on disk. It is *plain text* (UTF-8 encoded). At this point, the file is **inert** — the OS does not know how to run it directly. #### ▸ Step 2: Compilation — `javac Greeting.java` When the command `javac Greeting.java` is executed, the following sequence happens *inside the compiler*: 1. **Lexical Analysis:** The source code is tokenized into *tokens* (keywords like `public`, `class`, identifiers like `Greeting`, operators like `{`, `}`, strings, etc.). 2. **Syntax Analysis (Parsing):** Tokens are arranged into a **parse tree** (Abstract Syntax Tree) according to the Java Language Specification (JLS). 3. **Semantic Analysis:** The compiler resolves symbol references, checks type compatibility, verifies that `main` has the correct signature, ensures all referenced classes exist on the `CLASSPATH`. 4. **Bytecode Generation:** The AST is converted into **JVM bytecode** — a binary stream of single-byte opcodes. The output is written to a new file: **`Greeting.class`**. **Output on terminal:** ``` $ javac Greeting.java $ (no output = success; a new file Greeting.class appears) ``` **Partial Bytecode Preview (using `javap -c Greeting`):** ```text public class Greeting { public Greeting(); Code: 0: aload_0 1: invokespecial #1 // Method java/lang/Object."":()V 4: return public static void main(java.lang.String[]); Code: 0: getstatic #2 // Field java/lang/System.out:Ljava/io/PrintStream; 3: ldc #3 // String Welcome to KTU 2024 OOP Course! 5: invokevirtual #4 // Method java/io/PrintStream.println:(Ljava/lang/String;)V 8: return } ``` > [!NOTE] > **The `invokevirtual` opcode** is the JVM's way of executing a virtual (instance) method call. The compiler resolves `System.out.println(...)` into this opcode plus the string constant from the constant pool. This is the **deeper "how"** of why Java is platform-independent — the *same* bytecode opcodes execute on *any* JVM. #### ▸ Step 3: Class Loading — `java Greeting` When `java Greeting` is typed (notice: no `.class` extension!), the JVM performs: 1. **Bootstrap ClassLoader** loads core Java API classes (e.g., `java.lang.String`, `java.lang.System`) from `rt.jar` (or the modular equivalent in JDK 9+). 2. **Platform ClassLoader** loads Java SE platform extensions. 3. **Application ClassLoader** loads the user class `Greeting.class` from the `CLASSPATH` (default = current directory). 4. The class is then **linked**: verified, prepared (memory allocated for static fields), and optionally resolved. #### ▸ Step 4: Bytecode Verification The verifier mathematically proves that the loaded bytecode: - Will not overflow/underflow the operand stack. - Will not perform illegal data type conversions. - Will not access fields/methods in violation of access control. > **If verification fails → `VerifyError` is thrown and the class is rejected.** This is a key security feature. #### ▸ Step 5: Execution Engine Activates - The `main` method is invoked by the JVM using a special bootstrap mechanism (the JVM essentially calls `Greeting.main()` itself). - The **Interpreter** begins executing bytecode instructions one by one. - The **JIT compiler** monitors execution; if `println` is called many times, JIT will compile that method into native CPU instructions for speed. - Finally, the string `"Welcome to KTU 2024 OOP Course!"` is sent to the standard output stream (`stdout`). **Final terminal output:** ``` Welcome to KTU 2024 OOP Course! ``` --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — JVM Runtime Memory Areas > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 14-Mark Question Favourite:** *"Explain the JVM architecture and its memory areas."* Memorize this table. | Memory Area | Purpose | Thread Scope | Key Property | |---|---|---|---| | **Method Area** | Stores class metadata, static variables, constant pool, method bytecode | Shared across all threads | Part of Heap in JVM spec (logically) | | **Heap Area** | Stores all objects and instance variables (the "object pool") | Shared across all threads | Garbage-collected | | **Stack Area** | One stack per thread; holds *stack frames* for active method calls | **Private** to each thread | LIFO; auto-cleaned on method return | | **PC (Program Counter) Register** | Holds the address of the *current* executing bytecode instruction | **Private** to each thread | One per thread | | **Native Method Stack** | Holds native (C/C++) code call stacks for JNI methods | **Private** to each thread | Used by JNI | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Heap/Stack separation is what makes Java memory-safe by default. Unlike C/C++ (where the programmer must call `malloc`/`free` and risk memory leaks), the JVM's **Garbage Collector** automatically reclaims unreachable objects from the heap. This is one of the principal reasons Java dominates in enterprise server-side engineering. --- ### 3.4 The Mathematical / Logical Flow as Equations Although Java is not a "math" subject, the *flow* of compilation can be expressed logically: $$ \text{Source Code} \xrightarrow{\text{javac compiler}} \text{Bytecode (.class)} $$ $$ \text{Bytecode} \xrightarrow{\text{ClassLoader + Verifier}} \text{Verified Class in Memory} $$ $$ \text{Verified Class} \xrightarrow{\text{Interpreter / JIT}} \text{Program Output} $$ Combining the entire pipeline: $$ \text{JavaSource} \xrightarrow{\text{JDK (javac)}} \text{Bytecode} \xrightarrow{\text{JRE (java + JVM)}} \text{Running Program} $$ Which can be compactly written as: $$ \boxed{\text{JDK} = \text{Development Tools} + \text{JRE} = \text{Libraries} + \text{JVM}} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1 — The JDK / JRE / JVM Hierarchy This block diagram shows the *nesting relationship* — JDK contains JRE, JRE contains JVM. ```mermaid graph TD subgraph Developer's Machine JDK["JDK - Java Development Kit
javac, java, jdb, jar, javadoc"] JRE["JRE - Java Runtime Environment
rt.jar class libraries + JVM"] JVM["JVM - Java Virtual Machine
ClassLoader, Verifier, Execution Engine"] Tools["Development Tools
javac, jar, javadoc, jdb"] Libs["Class Libraries
java.lang, java.util, java.io, ..."] end JDK --- Tools JDK --- JRE JRE --- Libs JRE --- JVM style JDK fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style JRE fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style JVM fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style Tools fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Libs fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ### 4.2 Diagram 2 — End-to-End Compilation & Execution Flow This diagram traces a single `.java` file all the way to console output. ```mermaid graph LR A["Programmer writes
Greeting.java"] --> B["JDK Tool: javac compiler
Performs Lexical + Syntax + Semantic Analysis"] B --> C["Greeting.class file
Platform-Independent Bytecode"] C --> D["JRE Command: java Greeting
JVM Activated"] D --> E["ClassLoader Subsystem
Loads, Links, Initializes"] E --> F["Bytecode Verifier
Security + Integrity Check"] F --> G["Execution Engine
Interpreter + JIT Compiler"] G --> H["Runtime Data Areas
Heap, Stack, Method Area, PC"] H --> I["Final Output
Welcome to KTU 2024 OOP Course!"] style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14 style C fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 style D fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14 style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 style F fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 style G fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0 style H fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2 style I fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 ``` --- ### 4.3 Diagram 3 — JVM Internal Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture) A detailed block-level map of the JVM's three major subsystems and their internal cooperation. ```mermaid graph TD subgraph CL["Class Loader Subsystem"] CL1["Loading
Bootstrap, Platform, Application Loaders"] CL2["Linking
Verify, Prepare, Resolve"] CL3["Initialization
Static variable assignment + static block execution"] CL1 --> CL2 --> CL3 end subgraph MA["Runtime Data Areas (Memory)"] MA1["Method Area
Class metadata, static fields, constant pool"] MA2["Heap Area
All objects and instance variables"] MA3["Stack Area
Per-thread LIFO stack frames"] MA4["PC Register
Current instruction address per thread"] MA5["Native Method Stack
For JNI native calls"] end subgraph EE["Execution Engine"] EE1["Interpreter
Line-by-line bytecode execution"] EE2["JIT Compiler
Compiles hot code to native"] EE3["Garbage Collector
Reclaims unreachable heap objects"] end subgraph JN["Java Native Interface JNI"] JN1["Native Method Libraries
C / C++ libraries for platform interaction"] end CL3 --> MA MA3 --> EE1 MA3 --> EE2 MA2 --> EE3 EE1 --> JN1 EE2 --> JN1 style CL fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style MA fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14 style EE fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 style JN fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 ``` --- ### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Command-Line vs IDE Workflow Comparison A side-by-side functional comparison of the two development modes mandated by KTU Module 1. ```mermaid graph TD subgraph CLI["Command Line Workflow"] C1["Open Terminal or CMD"] C2["Write Hello.java in any text editor"] C3["cd to project directory"] C4["javac Hello.java"] C5["java Hello"] C6["Output appears on terminal"] C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6 end subgraph IDE["IDE Workflow"] I1["Launch Eclipse or IntelliJ"] I2["Create New Java Project"] I3["Create New Class file with main method"] I4["IDE auto-saves and auto-compiles in background"] I5["Click Run button or press F11 or Shift F10"] I6["Output appears in IDE Console panel"] I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 --> I5 --> I6 end style CLI fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14 style IDE fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The IDE workflow *implicitly performs* every step of the command-line workflow in the background. That is why learning the CLI first is pedagogically essential — it removes the "magic" and makes the IDE transparent. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Question Pattern Compliance:** All questions below strictly follow the KTU 2024 Scheme question paper format — Part A (3 marks each) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice, split into two 7-mark sub-questions). --- ### ▸ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Differentiate between JDK, JRE, and JVM. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | JDK | JRE | JVM | |---|---|---|---| | Full Form | Java Development Kit | Java Runtime Environment | Java Virtual Machine | | Purpose | Develop & compile Java code | Run Java applications | Execute bytecode | | Contains | JRE + development tools (`javac`, `jar`, `javadoc`, `jdb`) | JVM + runtime class libraries (`rt.jar`) | Class loader, verifier, execution engine | | Who needs it? | Java developers | End users | Embedded inside JRE | | Platform Dependency | Platform-specific (Windows JDK ≠ Linux JDK) | Platform-specific | Platform-specific (acts as the bridge) | **Memory Trick:** "**J**DK is for **D**evelopers, **J**RE is for **R**unners, **J**VM is the **V**ehicle that actually drives bytecode." --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]** **Explain the role of the following tools: `javac`, `java`, `javadoc`, and `jar`. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** - **`javac`** — The **Java compiler** provided in the JDK. It translates a `.java` source file into a `.class` bytecode file. Invoked as `javac FileName.java`. - **`java`** — The **application launcher** provided in the JRE. It starts the JVM and runs a compiled `.class` file. Invoked as `java ClassName` (no extension). - **`javadoc`** — The **documentation generator** that parses special `/** ... */` comments in source code and produces HTML API documentation. Invoked as `javadoc FileName.java`. - **`jar`** — The **Java ARchiver** that packages multiple `.class` files, resources, and metadata into a single `.jar` file (similar to a `.zip`). Invoked as `jar cvf MyApp.jar *.class`. --- ### ▸ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module 1 Part B carries 14 marks. Students may choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each must be answered in approximately 5–6 pages of a standard answer book. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) [7 Marks] Describe the architecture of the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) with a neat block diagram. Explain the function of each subsystem.** **Model Solution:** The Java Virtual Machine is an abstract computing machine that executes Java bytecode. It is the cornerstone of Java's "Write Once, Run Anywhere" capability. The JVM architecture can be divided into **three major subsystems**: **1. Class Loader Subsystem:** Responsible for loading, linking, and initializing classes. - **Loading:** The three-tier class loader hierarchy reads `.class` files from the classpath. - *Bootstrap ClassLoader* loads core Java API classes from `rt.jar`. - *Platform ClassLoader* loads Java SE extensions. - *Application ClassLoader* loads classes from the application's classpath. - **Linking:** Performs verification (checks bytecode correctness), preparation (allocates memory for static variables with default values), and resolution (replaces symbolic references with direct memory addresses). - **Initialization:** Executes static variable assignments and static initialization blocks in the order they appear in the source. **2. Runtime Data Areas (Memory):** - **Method Area:** Stores class-level data — class metadata, static variables, the constant pool, and method bytecode. Shared across all threads. - **Heap Area:** Stores all objects and instance variables. Shared across all threads. Managed by the Garbage Collector. - **Stack Area:** One private stack per thread. Each method invocation pushes a *stack frame* containing local variables, operand stack, and frame data. Auto-popped on method return. - **PC Register:** Holds the address of the currently executing JVM instruction. One per thread. - **Native Method Stack:** Stores native (C/C++) call stacks for JNI methods. One per thread. **3. Execution Engine:** - **Interpreter:** Reads and executes bytecode instructions one at a time. Slow but starts immediately. - **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler:** Detects "hot" methods (called many times) and compiles them into native machine code, cached for future ultra-fast execution. - **Garbage Collector (GC):** Automatically identifies and reclaims memory used by unreachable objects, preventing memory leaks. **Java Native Interface (JNI) and Native Method Libraries** allow the JVM to interact with platform-specific C/C++ libraries for tasks like hardware access and high-performance computation. *Block Diagram: Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.* --- #### **(b) [7 Marks] Write the step-by-step procedure to write, compile, and execute a Java program named `Demo.java` that prints "Hello KTU" using the command line. Explain the role of `CLASSPATH` and `PATH` environment variables.** **Model Solution:** **Step-by-Step Procedure:** **Step 1 — Setup:** Install JDK (e.g., JDK 21) and set the `JAVA_HOME`, `PATH`, and `CLASSPATH` environment variables (see table in Section 2.4). **Step 2 — Create the source file** using any text editor (Notepad, Vim, VS Code text mode): ```java public class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Hello KTU"); } } ``` Save as `Demo.java`. The filename **must** match the `public class` name. **Step 3 — Open the terminal** and navigate to the file's folder: ```bash cd C:\JavaPrograms ``` **Step 4 — Compile** using the JDK's `javac` compiler: ```bash javac Demo.java ``` - On success, a new file `Demo.class` (containing bytecode) is generated in the same directory. - On failure, the compiler prints error messages with line numbers. **Step 5 — Execute** using the JRE's `java` launcher: ```bash java Demo ``` - **Important:** Do **not** add `.class` extension. - **Output:** `Hello KTU` **Role of `PATH`:** The operating system uses the `PATH` environment variable to locate executable files. We add `%JAVA_HOME%\bin` to `PATH` so that commands like `javac` and `java` can be run from *any* directory without typing the full path (e.g., `C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-21\bin\javac`). **Role of `CLASSPATH`:** The JVM uses `CLASSPATH` to locate user-defined and library `.class` files at runtime. We include `.` (the current directory) so that the JVM can find `Demo.class` in the working folder. If `CLASSPATH` is misconfigured, we get `ClassNotFoundException`. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the difference between compilation and execution in Java. How does Java achieve platform independence?** **Model Solution:** **Compilation:** - Performed by the JDK's `javac` compiler. - Converts `.java` source code into `.class` bytecode. - Compilation is a **one-time** activity and is platform-independent at the output level — the same bytecode is produced regardless of the OS used for compilation. - The compiler checks for syntax errors, semantic errors, and type mismatches. **Execution:** - Performed by the JRE's `java` launcher, which starts the JVM. - The JVM's class loader loads the `.class` file, the bytecode verifier validates it, and the execution engine (interpreter + JIT) runs it. - Execution is **platform-dependent** in the sense that each OS needs its own JVM — but the *input* (bytecode) is the same. **How Java Achieves Platform Independence:** 1. Java source is compiled to **bytecode**, not native machine code. 2. Bytecode is a **stable, well-defined intermediate representation** standardized by the JLS. 3. Each operating system provides its own **platform-specific JVM** implementation. 4. The JVM translates the same bytecode into the corresponding native instructions for the host OS at runtime. 5. Therefore: *the same `.class` file runs unchanged on Windows, Linux, macOS, Solaris, etc.* — this is the "Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA) principle. **Contrast with C/C++:** In C/C++, the compiler produces native machine code tied to a specific CPU+OS combination. To run on another platform, the program must be **recompiled**. Java avoids this recompilation step entirely. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks] Compare the Command Line and IDE approaches to Java development. List any three popular Java IDEs with two features of each.** **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Command Line | IDE | |---|---|---| | **Tooling** | Plain text editor + JDK tools (`javac`, `java`) | Integrated editor + debugger + compiler + builder | | **Compilation visibility** | Explicit — student types `javac` and sees the `.class` file appear | Implicit — IDE auto-compiles in the background on save | | **Error reporting** | Terminal shows compiler errors with line numbers | Inline red squiggles, hover tooltips, click-to-jump-to-line | | **Debugging** | Manual (use `jdb` debugger) | Visual debugger (breakpoints, watch variables, step-through) | | **Project scale** | Best for small/medium single-file programs | Best for large multi-file, multi-package projects | | **Learning curve** | Steeper (forces student to learn the pipeline) | Easier after initial setup | | **Pedagogical value** | High — KTU-preferred for exam questions | High — KTU-preferred for lab work | | **Setup overhead** | None (JDK + text editor is enough) | Requires installation + project configuration | **Three Popular Java IDEs:** 1. **Eclipse IDE for Java Developers** (Eclipse Foundation) - Free, open-source, large plugin ecosystem. - Used widely in academic institutions (including KTU labs). 2. **IntelliJ IDEA** (JetBrains) - Industry-leading smart code completion and refactoring. - Community Edition is free; Ultimate Edition is commercial. 3. **NetBeans** (Apache Software Foundation) - Officially supported by Oracle; bundled with JDK downloads historically. - Drag-and-drop GUI builder for Swing/JavaFX applications. *(Optional 4th — VS Code: lightweight, requires Java Extension Pack to function.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:** > 1. **Writing `.class` extension in the `java` command** (e.g., `java Demo.class`) — the JVM throws an error because it auto-appends `.class`. Always write `java Demo`. **(-1 mark)** > 2. **Confusing CLASSPATH and PATH** — `PATH` is for executables (`javac`); `CLASSPATH` is for class files (`.class`). Mixing them up costs a full 7-mark sub-question. > 3. **Forgetting to mention that the JVM is platform-specific while bytecode is platform-independent** — the question on WORA explicitly tests this distinction. **(-2 marks)** > 4. **Not stating that the filename must match the public class name** in command-line questions. This is a guaranteed 1-mark item. > 5. **Skipping the block diagram** in the JVM architecture question — KTU explicitly asks for a "neat diagram," and omitting it typically costs 2–3 marks. --- ### ▸ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:** - **JDK = JRE + Development Tools.** JDK is a *superset* of JRE. - **JRE = JVM + Class Libraries.** JRE is a *superset* of JVM. - **JVM is the runtime engine that executes bytecode.** It is platform-dependent; bytecode is platform-independent. - **WORA = "Write Once, Run Anywhere"** — Java's defining architectural advantage, enabled by the bytecode/JVM separation. - **`javac`** compiles (JDK tool); **`java`** runs (JRE tool). - **The `java` command takes the class name without the `.class` extension.** - **Filename MUST match the `public class` name** in command-line workflow. - **The `main` method signature is exactly:** `public static void main(String[] args)`. - **`PATH`** is for the OS to find JDK executables; **`CLASSPATH`** is for the JVM to find user/library class files. - **JVM has 3 subsystems:** (1) Class Loader, (2) Runtime Data Areas (Memory), (3) Execution Engine (Interpreter + JIT + GC). - **JVM has 5 memory areas:** Method Area, Heap, Stack, PC Register, Native Method Stack. - **Heap** is shared across threads (objects live here); **Stack** is per-thread (method frames live here). - **JIT compiler** converts "hot" bytecode to native machine code at runtime for speed. - **Garbage Collector** automatically frees heap memory of unreachable objects. - **Bytecode Verifier** ensures security and integrity of loaded class files. - **Three popular IDEs:** Eclipse, IntelliJ IDEA, NetBeans. (Optional: VS Code with Java extensions.) - **IDE advantages:** syntax highlighting, auto-completion, visual debugger, project management, refactoring tools. - **IDE disadvantage:** hides the compile-execute pipeline that KTU wants you to know explicitly. - **Java source files end in `.java`;** **compiled files end in `.class`.** - **Tools in the JDK include:** `javac`, `java`, `javadoc`, `jar`, `jdb`. - **One-liner for exams:** *"Java source is compiled to bytecode by the JDK, and that bytecode is executed by the JVM inside the JRE on any platform."*

topics.title: Java Compiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Java Compiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Java Compiler** (specifically, the reference implementation known as `javac`) is a *platform-dependent* command-line tool provided as part of the **Java Development Kit (JDK)**. It is responsible for translating human-readable **Java source code** (`.java` files) into an intermediate, platform-independent representation called **Java Bytecode** (`.class` files).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Java compiler is a **translating compiler** — it does NOT generate native machine code for the underlying CPU. It produces **bytecode**, which is the cornerstone of Java's *Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA)* philosophy.

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Java Compiler | `javac` | Source → Bytecode translator |
| Bytecode | `.class` | Platform-neutral instruction set for the JVM |
| JVM | `java` | Bytecode → Native machine code executor |
| JDK | – | Java Development Kit (contains `javac` + `java`) |
| JRE | – | Java Runtime Environment (contains `java` only) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Universal Movie Projector**
>
> Imagine a famous director shooting a movie in **English** (Java Source Code). Before release, the studio does not "print" 1,000 different physical film rolls for every cinema in the world. Instead, they create a **single Digital Master Tape** (Java Bytecode) and ship it to theatres.
>
> Each theatre (Windows, Linux, macOS, Android) buys its own **Projector** (JVM — specific to that platform) that knows how to play the master tape and display the movie in the local language (Native Machine Code).
>
> The **Java Compiler** is the "mastering engineer" who produces that single universal tape from the raw script.

### 1.3 Physical / Standard Constants

- The Java Language Specification (JLS) defines the bytecode instruction set formally.
- `javac` is officially written in **Java itself** (a self-hosting compiler) — often called the *"Java bootstrap."*
- Standard extension for source files: **`.java`**
- Standard extension for compiled files: **`.class`**
- Java source code file name **MUST** match the `public class` name defined inside it (KTU frequently tests this).

---

## 1.4 Why the Java Compiler is "Special"

Unlike traditional C/C++ compilers (GCC, MSVC) which produce platform-specific `.exe` or `.o` files, the Java compiler produces **bytecode** that:
1. Is **architecture-neutral**.
2. Is **verified** by the JVM for safety (no pointer arithmetic, type safety).
3. Can be **interpreted** or **JIT-compiled** at runtime.
4. Enables dynamic class loading over networks (Applets, modern modular apps).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common trap question asks *"Is Java a compiled or interpreted language?"* The precise KTU-accepted answer is: **Java is both** — it is *compiled* (to bytecode by `javac`) and then *interpreted/JIT-compiled* (by the JVM).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Step Execution Model of Java

Java uses a **two-stage translation pipeline**:

$$\text{Java Source} \xrightarrow{\text{Step 1: } javac} \text{Bytecode} \xrightarrow{\text{Step 2: } JVM} \text{Native Code}$$

### Step 1 — Compilation Phase (`javac`)
- Input: `MyProgram.java` (human-readable text).
- Output: `MyProgram.class` (bytecode, binary).
- Performed **once** by the developer on the development machine.
- No execution happens here.

### Step 2 — Execution Phase (`java` / JVM)
- Input: `MyProgram.class` (bytecode).
- Output: Program runs on the host OS.
- Performed on **every machine** that runs the program.
- Uses interpretation + JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation internally.

> [!NOTE]
> This separation of *translation* (compile-time) and *execution* (run-time) is what gives Java its **portability**.

## 2.2 Internal Phases of `javac`

The Java compiler is a **multi-phase pipeline**. KTU 2024 expects you to know these phases in order:

1. **Lexical Analysis (Scanning / Tokenization)**
   - Source code → stream of **tokens** (keywords, identifiers, literals, operators, separators).
   - Whitespace and comments are stripped.

2. **Syntax Analysis (Parsing)**
   - Tokens are arranged into a **parse tree** / **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** using the Java grammar (defined in JLS).
   - Syntax errors are reported here.

3. **Semantic Analysis**
   - Type checking, scope resolution, definite-assignment analysis.
   - Adds the **Symbol Table** annotations to the AST.
   - Detects type mismatches, undeclared variables.

4. **Intermediate Representation (IR) Generation**
   - Converts AST into a lower-level, machine-independent IR.

5. **Bytecode Generation**
   - IR → stream of bytecode instructions (e.g., `iconst_0`, `iload_1`, `iadd`, `invokevirtual`).
   - Stored as `.class` file in the **Class File Format** (a strict binary spec).

6. **Class File Writing**
   - Writes the constant pool, field info, method info, and bytecode to a `.class` file.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The output of `javac` is **not** a single executable. It is one `.class` file per top-level class (or interface/enum). If you have 5 classes, you will get 5 `.class` files (and 5 more for each inner class).

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Process Cheat Sheet

| # | Phase | Input | Output | Detects |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lexical Analysis | Source text | Tokens | Invalid characters, malformed literals |
| 2 | Syntax Analysis | Tokens | Parse Tree / AST | Missing semicolons, brackets, syntax errors |
| 3 | Semantic Analysis | AST + Symbol Table | Annotated AST | Type errors, undeclared variables |
| 4 | IR Generation | Annotated AST | Intermediate Code | – |
| 5 | Bytecode Generation | IR | Bytecode stream | – |
| 6 | Class File Writing | Bytecode + metadata | `.class` file | – |

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` notation instead of `|` inside cells to avoid breaking the markdown table — per KTU-prep formatting standards.

## 2.4 JIT vs AOT — Two Ways the JVM Runs Bytecode

The **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** is technically *not* the compiler — but for KTU 2024 context, the term "Java Compiler" often extends to the *whole pipeline*. The JVM has two optimization strategies:

| Strategy | Full Form | When Compilation Happens | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Interpretation** | Line-by-line bytecode execution | Runtime, per instruction | Slow start |
| **JIT** | Just-In-Time Compilation | Runtime, hot-spot detection | Fast after warm-up |
| **AOT** | Ahead-Of-Time Compilation (e.g., GraalVM) | Build time, offline | Fast start |

- **HotSpot JVM** (default) uses a combination: interprets first, then JIT-compiles *hot* (frequently-executed) methods to native code, caching them in **code cache**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cross-platform deployment:** Server-side code compiled on a Linux dev box runs unchanged on Windows servers.
- **Security:** The JVM **bytecode verifier** checks `.class` files before execution, preventing stack overflows, illegal casts, and many buffer-overflow attacks — critical for **banking and embedded systems**.
- **Dynamic loading:** `.class` files can be downloaded from networks at runtime (foundation of Applets, OSGi, modern modular JDK 9+).
- **Reflection & Frameworks:** Spring, Hibernate, JUnit — all rely on bytecode being a *structured, queryable* format (not native binary).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Compiling and Running a Java Program

Let us work through the complete KTU-style trace of compiling and running a Java program. This is a standard 7-mark derivation-style question.

### Source Program: `Hello.java`

```java
public class Hello {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello KTU");
    }
}
```

### Stage 1: The Compilation Command

Open a terminal in the directory containing `Hello.java` and execute:

```bash
javac Hello.java
```

The `javac` compiler executes **all six internal phases** (Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → IR → Bytecode → Class File Write) and produces the file `Hello.class` in the **same directory**.

### Stage 2: Verifying the Output

```bash
ls
```

Expected output:

```
Hello.class   Hello.java
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that **no executable** (`.exe`) is produced on any platform. The `.class` file is the compiled artifact.

### Stage 3: Running the Program

```bash
java Hello
```

> [!WARNING]
> Do **NOT** write `java Hello.class` — the JVM automatically appends `.class` and expects only the class name. Including the extension causes the error: `Error: Could not find or load main class Hello.class`.

### Stage 4: Viewing the Bytecode (Optional but KTU-relevant)

Use the `javap` disassembler to inspect the bytecode:

```bash
javap -c Hello
```

Output (excerpt):

```
public static void main(java.lang.String[]);
  Code:
     0: getstatic      #16   // Field java/lang/System.out
     3: ldc            #22   // String Hello KTU
     5: invokevirtual  #28   // Method java/io/PrintStream.println
     8: return
```

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Phase Analysis (with Line-by-Line Reasoning)

Let us now trace **what `javac` does to each line** of the source code.

### Phase 1 — Lexical Analysis

The source file is read character by character. Characters are grouped into tokens.

| Source Fragment | Token Type | Token Value |
|---|---|---|
| `public` | Keyword | `public` |
| `class` | Keyword | `class` |
| `Hello` | Identifier | `Hello` |
| `{` | Separator | `{` |
| `public` | Keyword | `public` |
| `static` | Keyword | `static` |
| `void` | Keyword | `void` |
| `main` | Identifier | `main` |
| `(` | Separator | `(` |
| `String` | Identifier (type) | `String` |
| `[` `]` | Separator | `[]` |
| `args` | Identifier | `args` |
| `)` | Separator | `)` |
| `{` | Separator | `{` |
| `System` | Identifier | `System` |
| `.` | Operator | `.` |
| `out` | Identifier | `out` |
| `.` | Operator | `.` |
| `println` | Identifier | `println` |
| `(` | Separator | `(` |
| `"Hello KTU"` | String literal | `Hello KTU` |
| `)` | Separator | `)` |
| `;` | Separator | `;` |
| `}` `}` | Separators | `}` `}` |

### Phase 2 — Syntax Analysis

The token stream is fed into a **LL(k)-based parser** that builds an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**.

A simplified tree fragment for the `println` statement is:

```
MethodCall
 ├── Target: System.out
 └── MethodName: println
      └── Argument: StringLiteral "Hello KTU"
```

If the parser encounters a missing `;`, it raises:

```
error: ';' expected
```

### Phase 3 — Semantic Analysis

The compiler walks the AST and resolves symbols using the **Symbol Table**.

Checks performed:
- Does `String` exist? → Yes (in `java.lang`, auto-imported).
- Does `main` return type `void`? → Yes.
- Is `println(String)` a valid method? → Resolved against `PrintStream` class.

If `System.out.println(invalidVariable)` were written and `invalidVariable` is undeclared, the compiler emits:

```
error: cannot find symbol
  symbol:   variable invalidVariable
  location: class Hello
```

### Phase 4 — IR Generation

The annotated AST is lowered to a stack-based intermediate representation. The conceptual translation of `println("Hello KTU")` is:

```
push "Hello KTU" onto operand stack
invoke println
```

### Phase 5 — Bytecode Generation

The IR is emitted as **bytecode opcodes** (each opcode is 1 byte, hence the name *bytecode*).

Final instruction sequence:

```
0: getstatic    #16     // push System.out
3: ldc          #22     // push "Hello KTU"
5: invokevirtual #28    // call println
8: return                // end of main
```

### Phase 6 — Class File Writing

The `.class` file is written in the **Class File Format** specified by the JLS. Major sections:

1. **Magic Number:** `0xCAFEBABE` (4 bytes) — every valid Java class file begins with this.
2. **Version Number:** minor + major version (e.g., major 52 for Java 8, 65 for Java 21).
3. **Constant Pool:** table of all literal constants and symbolic references.
4. **Access Flags:** e.g., `ACC_PUBLIC`, `ACC_SUPER`.
5. **This Class / Super Class:** references to `Hello` and `java.lang.Object`.
6. **Interfaces, Fields, Methods, Attributes.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trivia:** `0xCAFEBABE` was chosen by James Gosling because it was an "uncommon" hex sequence. The "BABE" stuck; later, class file version 51 (Java 7) used `0xCAFEBABE` as a minor version experiment — but the magic stays the same.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: `javac` vs `java` vs `javap`

| Tool | Type | Input | Output | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `javac` | Compiler | `.java` | `.class` | Translate source to bytecode |
| `java` | Launcher + JVM | `.class` | Program output | Execute bytecode |
| `javap` | Disassembler | `.class` | Human-readable bytecode | Inspect / debug compiled code |
| `javadoc` | Documentation generator | `.java` (with comments) | `.html` | Generate API docs |
| `jar` | Archiver | `.class` (multiple) | `.jar` | Package libraries |

## 3.4 Important `javac` Command-Line Options (KTU Favourite)

| Option | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `-d <dir>` | Place generated `.class` files in the specified directory |
| `-classpath <path>` | Specify where to find user classes |
| `-source <version>` | Specify source language version (e.g., `-source 17`) |
| `-target <version>` | Specify target bytecode version |
| `-Xlint:all` | Enable all recommended warnings |
| `-verbose` | Print information about what the compiler is doing |
| `-nowarn` | Disable warning messages |

Example (compiling with a custom output directory):

```bash
javac -d ./build Hello.java
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** A common 7-mark question asks: *"Explain the steps involved in compiling and running a Java program with proper commands."* — Use the above 4-stage command sequence and mention the directory structure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: The Java Compilation Pipeline

The diagram below illustrates the complete two-step translation architecture from Java source code to executing native instructions on hardware.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Java Source File\nHello.java] --> B[javac Compiler]
    B --> C[Bytecode File\nHello.class]
    C --> D[Class Loader]
    D --> E[Bytecode Verifier]
    E --> F[Interpreter]
    F --> G{Hot Method\nDetected?}
    G -- No --> H[Continue Interpretation]
    G -- Yes --> I[JIT Compiler]
    I --> J[Native Machine Code]
    J --> K[CPU Execution]
    F --> K
    H --> K
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#FFA07A
    style C fill:#98FB98
    style D fill:#87CEEB
    style E fill:#87CEEB
    style F fill:#DDA0DD
    style I fill:#DDA0DD
    style J fill:#F0E68C
    style K fill:#FFB6C1
```

## 4.2 Internal Phases of the `javac` Compiler

The following block diagram decomposes `javac` into its six internal phases. This is a high-frequency diagram in KTU 2024 exam papers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Lexical Analysis\nSource Text to Tokens] --> P2[Phase 2: Syntax Analysis\nTokens to Parse Tree and AST]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Semantic Analysis\nType Check and Symbol Table]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: IR Generation\nAST to Intermediate Code]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Bytecode Generation\nIR to Bytecode Stream]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Class File Writing\nBytecode plus Metadata to dot class]
    P1 -.Error.-> E1[Lexical Errors]
    P2 -.Error.-> E2[Syntax Errors]
    P3 -.Error.-> E3[Semantic Errors]
    style P1 fill:#FFD700
    style P2 fill:#FFD700
    style P3 fill:#FFD700
    style P4 fill:#87CEFA
    style P5 fill:#87CEFA
    style P6 fill:#90EE90
    style E1 fill:#FF6B6B
    style E2 fill:#FF6B6B
    style E3 fill:#FF6B6B
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Inside a `.class` File

Since the internal binary structure of a `.class` file cannot be drawn physically, the following Mermaid block diagram maps the **logical sections** of a compiled Java class file.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph classfile[Class File Structure]
        M1[Magic Number CAFEBABE]
        M2[Version Info]
        M3[Constant Pool]
        M4[Access Flags]
        M5[This Class Reference]
        M6[Super Class Reference]
        M7[Interfaces Table]
        M8[Fields Table]
        M9[Methods Table]
        M10[Attributes Table]
    end
    M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 --> M6 --> M7 --> M8 --> M9 --> M10
    style M1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style M2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style M3 fill:#98FB98
    style M4 fill:#87CEEB
    style M5 fill:#DDA0DD
    style M6 fill:#DDA0DD
    style M7 fill:#F0E68C
    style M8 fill:#FFA07A
    style M9 fill:#FFA07A
    style M10 fill:#FFD700
```

## 4.4 Compilation vs Runtime — Where Errors Occur

A critical KTU conceptual diagram showing at which stage each kind of error is caught.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Compile Time javac] --> S2[Runtime java and JVM]
    S1 -.Reports.-> E1[Syntax Errors]
    S1 -.Reports.-> E2[Type Mismatch Errors]
    S1 -.Reports.-> E3[Undeclared Variable Errors]
    S1 -.Reports.-> E4[Missing Semicolons]
```
