    S2 -.Reports.-> E5[Division by Zero]
    S2 -.Reports.-> E6[Null Pointer Exception]
    S2 -.Reports.-> E7[Array Index Out of Bounds]
    S2 -.Reports.-> E8[Class Not Found Exception]
    style S1 fill:#90EE90
    style S2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style E1 fill:#FF6B6B
    style E2 fill:#FF6B6B
    style E3 fill:#FF6B6B
    style E4 fill:#FF6B6B
    style E5 fill:#FF8C00
    style E6 fill:#FF8C00
    style E7 fill:#FF8C00
    style E8 fill:#FF8C00
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Errors on the left (green node) are caught by `javac` *before* the program ever runs. Errors on the right (red/orange nodes) escape the compiler and only surface when the JVM executes the bytecode. KTU frequently asks students to **classify errors as compile-time vs runtime**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Java compiler (`javac`) and a Java interpreter (`java` / JVM). Mention the input and output of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Java Compiler (`javac`) | Java Interpreter (`java`/JVM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage** | Compile-time (development) | Run-time (execution) |
| **Input** | `.java` source file | `.class` bytecode file |
| **Output** | `.class` bytecode | Program output (via native code execution) |
| **Frequency** | Run **once** per source change | Run **every time** the program is launched |
| **Platform Dependency** | Platform-specific binary (but source-agnostic) | Platform-specific, but bytecode-agnostic |
| **Error Type** | Catches syntax + semantic errors | Throws runtime exceptions |

The compiler **translates**, the interpreter/JVM **executes**. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is Java bytecode? Why is it called platform-independent?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Java **bytecode** is the intermediate, binary instruction set generated by the `javac` compiler and stored in `.class` files. Each instruction is 1 byte long (hence *bytecode*).

It is called **platform-independent** because:
1. It is **not** tied to any specific CPU instruction set (x86, ARM, RISC-V).
2. It is **not** tied to any specific operating system.
3. Every platform provides its **own JVM** that translates the same bytecode into the local native code.
4. Therefore, the *same* `.class` file runs unchanged on Windows, Linux, macOS, and any other JVM-enabled device. (3 marks)

---

## 📗 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** You are required to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as 7 + 7.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **two-step translation process** used in Java with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between compile-time and run-time environments. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the **internal phases of the `javac` compiler** with a flowchart. What kind of errors are caught at each phase? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Two-Step Translation Process — 7 Marks**

Java uses a **two-step translation process** to achieve platform independence:

**Step 1 — Compilation (Development-time):**
The `javac` compiler takes `Hello.java` (human-readable source code) and produces `Hello.class` (Java bytecode). This step is performed **once**, on the developer's machine, and is **platform-agnostic** at the source level.

**Step 2 — Execution (Run-time):**
The `java` launcher invokes the **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** which:
- Loads the `.class` file via the **Class Loader**,
- **Verifies** the bytecode for safety,
- **Interprets** the bytecode instruction-by-instruction, and
- **JIT-compiles** hot methods to native code for performance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hello.java] --> B[javac Compiler]
    B --> C[Hello.class Bytecode]
    C --> D[JVM]
    D --> E[Native Output]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#FFA07A
    style C fill:#98FB98
    style D fill:#DDA0DD
    style E fill:#FFB6C1
```

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Compile-Time | Run-Time |
|---|---|---|
| When | During `javac` | During `java` |
| Tools | `javac` | JVM, JIT |
| Errors | Syntax, type | Exceptions, logic |

**(7 marks distributed: 3 for process, 2 for diagram, 2 for differentiation)**

---

**(b) Internal Phases of `javac` — 7 Marks**

The `javac` compiler internally operates in **six phases**:

1. **Lexical Analysis:** Source code is scanned and grouped into **tokens** (keywords, identifiers, operators, literals). Strips whitespace and comments. Catches invalid characters.
2. **Syntax Analysis:** Tokens are arranged into a **parse tree / Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** using the Java grammar. Catches missing semicolons, mismatched braces, etc.
3. **Semantic Analysis:** Type checking, scope resolution, definite-assignment analysis. Catches type mismatches and undeclared variables.
4. **Intermediate Representation (IR):** AST is lowered to a stack-based IR.
5. **Bytecode Generation:** IR is emitted as 1-byte opcodes (e.g., `iconst_0`, `iadd`).
6. **Class File Writing:** Bytecode + constant pool + metadata → `.class` file (begins with magic `0xCAFEBABE`).

The flowchart is the same as Section 4.2 above. **(7 marks distributed: 1 per phase + 1 for flowchart overview)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Write a Java program to print `"Welcome to KTU"`. Show the exact **commands** to compile and run it. Explain what happens if you run `java Welcome.class` instead of `java Welcome`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **`.class` file**? Describe its **structure** with a neat diagram. What is the significance of the magic number `0xCAFEBABE`? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Sample Program & Commands — 7 Marks**

```java
public class Welcome {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Welcome to KTU");
    }
}
```

Save as `Welcome.java`. Then execute:

```bash
javac Welcome.java      # produces Welcome.class
java Welcome            # runs the program; output: Welcome to KTU
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake — `java Welcome.class`:**
> If the student types `java Welcome.class`, the JVM searches for a class literally named `Welcome.class` (with a dot in the identifier). Since no such class exists, it raises:
>
> ```
> Error: Could not find or load main class Welcome.class
> ```
>
> The JVM automatically appends `.class` — the user must supply **only the class name**.

**(7 marks: 2 for program, 2 for commands, 3 for the error explanation)**

---

**(b) Structure of a `.class` File — 7 Marks**

A `.class` file is a strictly-formatted binary file defined by the Java Virtual Machine Specification. It contains:

1. **Magic Number** (`0xCAFEBABE` — 4 bytes) — Identifies the file as a valid Java class.
2. **Version Info** — Minor + Major version (e.g., 52.0 for Java 8, 65.0 for Java 21).
3. **Constant Pool** — All literal constants (numbers, strings) + symbolic references (class/method names).
4. **Access Flags** — Modifiers like `ACC_PUBLIC`, `ACC_FINAL`, `ACC_INTERFACE`.
5. **This Class** & **Super Class** — Indexes into the constant pool.
6. **Interfaces Table** — Implemented interfaces.
7. **Fields Table** — All declared fields.
8. **Methods Table** — All declared methods (bytecode lives here).
9. **Attributes Table** — Additional metadata (e.g., source file name, line numbers).

The structural diagram is the same as **Section 4.3** above.

**Significance of `0xCAFEBABE`:**
- It is the **file-type signature**. The JVM (and any tool that reads `.class` files) checks the first 4 bytes first.
- If a file does not begin with `0xCAFEBABE`, it is rejected immediately as not being a valid Java class file.
- The choice was made by **James Gosling** for its memorable, "uncommon" hexadecimal value.

**(7 marks: 3 for structure, 2 for diagram, 2 for magic number significance)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing compiler with interpreter:** Many students write *"Java is interpreted."* This is **incomplete** — Java is **both** compiled and interpreted. Lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using `java Welcome.class`:** Examiners actively test whether you know that the JVM appends `.class` automatically. Mention the **error message** explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting that `javac` produces one `.class` per class:** A 3-class program produces 3 `.class` files (plus inner classes). This is asked repeatedly.
> 4. **Confusing compile-time vs runtime errors:** Be precise — syntax errors = compile-time; division by zero = runtime. Draw a small classification table.
> 5. **Skipping the `0xCAFEBABE` magic number:** It's a 2-mark "freebie" in 14-mark answers.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Java Compiler"**

- ✅ The Java compiler is called **`javac`** and is part of the **JDK**.
- ✅ `javac` translates **`.java` → `.class`** (source code to **bytecode**).
- ✅ Bytecode is **platform-independent**; native code is **platform-dependent**.
- ✅ The **JVM** executes bytecode and produces native output.
- ✅ Java is **both compiled and interpreted** (or JIT-compiled at runtime).
- ✅ The JVM uses **interpretation + JIT** (Just-In-Time) compilation for performance.
- ✅ The command to compile is `javac FileName.java`.
- ✅ The command to run is `java ClassName` (do **NOT** add `.class`).
- ✅ One **`.class` file is generated per top-level class**.
- ✅ The Java compiler has **6 internal phases**: Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → IR → Bytecode → Class File Write.
- ✅ Every `.class` file begins with the magic number **`0xCAFEBABE`**.
- ✅ **Compile-time errors:** syntax, type, undeclared symbols.
- ✅ **Runtime errors:** exceptions like NPE, ArrayIndexOutOfBounds, ArithmeticException.
- ✅ Source file name **must** match the public class name.
- ✅ `javap -c ClassName` can be used to **disassemble** bytecode for inspection.
- ✅ Java's philosophy: **WORA — Write Once, Run Anywhere** — enabled by the bytecode + JVM separation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Java Virtual Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Java Virtual Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board-Standard):**
> The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is a platform-independent, abstract computing machine (a runtime engine) that resides on top of the host operating system and provides the runtime environment necessary to execute **Java bytecode**. It is the cornerstone of Java's *"Write Once, Run Anywhere"* (WORA) philosophy, acting as an interpreter/compiler hybrid that converts platform-neutral `.class` files into native machine instructions for the underlying hardware.

In formal Computer Science terms, the JVM is a **stack-based, register-aware virtual processor** that implements the Java Virtual Machine Specification (JVMS) published by Oracle. The current standard is defined by **JVMS - Java SE 21 (JSR 396)** as of the 2024 scheme curriculum updates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Universal Translator" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are a world leader delivering a speech. You don't personally speak 195 languages. Instead, you have a **Universal Translator** sitting next to you. Whatever you say in English, the translator instantly whispers it in Japanese, Hindi, German, etc., to the local audience.
>
> - **You (the programmer)** = speak only **Java source code** (`.java`).
> - **The Speech** = your compiled program (`.class` bytecode).
> - **The Universal Translator** = the **JVM**.
> - **The Audience** = your specific device (Windows laptop, Linux server, Android phone, Mac).
>
> Without the translator, your speech is useless to the audience. Likewise, without the JVM installed, your compiled Java bytecode cannot run on a device. Every operating system (Windows, Linux, macOS) has its own **specific JVM build** (HotSpot, OpenJ9, GraalVM), and that's the *only* piece of software that is platform-dependent.

### 1.3 Core Vocabulary Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Terms You MUST Know for KTU Exams:**
>
> - **Bytecode** — The intermediate, platform-neutral instruction set (stored in `.class` files) generated by the `javac` compiler.
> - **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler** — A performance optimization component that compiles "hot" bytecode into native machine code *at runtime*.
> - **Class Loader Subsystem** — The component responsible for loading, linking, and initializing `.class` files into JVM memory.
> - **Runtime Data Areas** — The memory zones (Heap, Stack, Method Area, PC Register, Native Method Stack) allocated by the JVM during execution.
> - **Garbage Collector (GC)** — The automatic memory management engine that reclaims unused heap memory.

### 1.4 The Java Platform Family (JVM vs JRE vs JDK)

| Component | Full Form | Purpose | Size/Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| **JVM** | Java Virtual Machine | Runs bytecode | Runtime engine only |
| **JRE** | Java Runtime Environment | JVM + Libraries (rt.jar) | Needed to *run* Java programs |
| **JDK** | Java Development Kit | JRE + Compiler (`javac`) + Debugger | Needed to *develop* Java programs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU One-Liner to Memorize:**
> *"JDK is for developers, JRE is for end-users, and JVM is the heart of both."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of the JVM (Three-Pillar Model)

The JVM architecture is canonically divided into **three major subsystems**:

### Pillar 1: Class Loader Subsystem
Responsible for **Loading → Linking → Initialization** of classes.

| Phase | Action | KTU-Key Term |
|---|---|---|
| **Loading** | Reads `.class` file, generates binary data, stores in **Method Area** | Uses `ClassLoader` (Bootstrap → Extension → Application) |
| **Linking** | Performs `Verify → Prepare → Resolve` | Bytecode verifier checks structural correctness |
| **Initialization** | Executes static variables and `static {}` blocks | Top-to-bottom order, parent-before-child |

### Pillar 2: Runtime Data Areas (Memory)
Five logical memory regions are allocated:

| Memory Area | Stores | Thread Scope | OutOfMemory Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Method Area** | Class metadata, static variables, constant pool, method bytecode | Shared (1 per JVM) | `OutOfMemoryError: PermGen` (pre-Java 8) / `Metaspace` (Java 8+) |
| **Heap Area** | All objects and instance variables | Shared | `OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space` |
| **Stack Area** | Stack frames (local variables, operand stack, frame data) | Per-thread | `StackOverflowError` |
| **PC Register** | Address of current executing instruction | Per-thread | Never throws OOM |
| **Native Method Stack** | Native (C/C++) method execution | Per-thread | `StackOverflowError` |

### Pillar 3: Execution Engine
Converts bytecode → machine code using three components:
- **Interpreter** — Reads and executes bytecode line-by-line (slow but immediate).
- **JIT Compiler** — Detects "hot methods" (executed ≥ 10,000 times by default) and compiles them to native code.
- **Garbage Collector (GC)** — Reclaims unreachable objects from the Heap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JVM Memory Formula (KTU Viva Favorite):**
> $$\text{Total JVM Memory} = \text{Heap} + \text{Metaspace} + \text{Stack}_{\text{threads}} + \text{PC Registers} + \text{Native Stacks}$$
> Default `java -Xmx` controls **maximum heap size** (commonly **256 MB** to **4 GB**).

## 2.2 The "Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA) Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three-Step WORA Pipeline:**
>
> 1. **Compile Once** → `javac HelloWorld.java` produces `HelloWorld.class` (bytecode).
> 2. **Transport Anywhere** → The same `.class` file can be moved to any OS.
> 3. **JVM Executes Locally** → Each OS-specific JVM interprets/JIT-compiles the bytecode into native machine code.
>
> *This is why Java bytecode is called "the language of the JVM."*

## 2.3 ClassLoader Hierarchy (Delegation Model)

```
Bootstrap ClassLoader  (loads rt.jar, core Java APIs from <JAVA_HOME>/jre/lib)
        ↓ delegates to
Platform ClassLoader    (loads extension classes from <JAVA_HOME>/jre/lib/ext)
        ↓ delegates to
Application ClassLoader (loads classes from the classpath, i.e., user's code)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delegation Principle:** A child ClassLoader **delegates** the request to its parent *before* attempting to load the class itself. This prevents malicious code from replacing core Java classes (e.g., `java.lang.String`).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stack frame size per method | $S = \text{Locals} + \text{OperandStack} + \text{FrameData}$ | StackOverflow analysis |
| 2 | Heap region split (Java 8+) | $\text{Heap} = \text{YoungGen} + \text{OldGen}$ | GC tuning questions |
| 3 | YoungGen split | $\text{YoungGen} = \text{Eden} + 2 \times \text{Survivor}$ | Minor GC frequency |
| 4 | JIT compilation threshold | $\text{Hot method if call count} \geq 10{,}000$ | HotSpot optimization |
| 5 | Default stack size | **512 KB** per thread (Windows), **1 MB** (Linux) | `java -Xss` flag |
| 6 | ClassLoader delegation | Parent-first search (向上委托) | Custom classloader design |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The JVM is **not just for Java** anymore. Modern languages — **Kotlin, Scala, Groovy, Clojure, JRuby, Jython** — all compile *down to JVM bytecode*. This makes the JVM the **de facto universal runtime for enterprise backend systems** (e.g., Netflix, LinkedIn, Twitter, Amazon). In KTU 2024 context, this is why Android development (Kotlin/Dalvik ART) and Big Data (Apache Spark, Hadoop) ecosystems all rely on JVM technology.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Execution: How a Java Program Runs on the JVM

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU Module 1. We will trace the complete lifecycle of a Java program from source code to screen output.

### Stage 1: Source Code Compilation
You write a file `Hello.java`:

```java
public class Hello {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello KTU");
    }
}
```

You invoke the compiler:

```
javac Hello.java
```

The compiler performs **lexical analysis → parsing → semantic analysis → bytecode generation** and emits `Hello.class`. The `.class` file contains:

- **Magic Number:** `0xCAFEBABE` (4 bytes) — the JVM file signature.
- **Constant Pool:** All string literals, class names, method references.
- **Method Bytecode:** JVM instructions like `getstatic`, `ldc`, `invokevirtual`, `return`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"CAFE BABE" — a developer's way to identify a real Java class file instantly.*

### Stage 2: Program Invocation
You run:

```
java Hello
```

This triggers the JVM to start. The JVM's main thread comes alive.

### Stage 3: Class Loading
The JVM invokes the **Application ClassLoader** to find `Hello.class`. The classloader follows the **Delegation Model**:

$$\text{AppCL} \rightarrow \text{PlatformCL} \rightarrow \text{BootstrapCL} \rightarrow \text{findClass("Hello")}$$

Once found, the JVM reads the bytecode into the **Method Area**.

### Stage 4: Linking (Verify → Prepare → Resolve)
- **Verify:** The Bytecode Verifier checks that the `.class` file is structurally valid (no stack underflow, no illegal type casts, no access violations).
- **Prepare:** Static variables get their *default* values (e.g., `static int x = 0` is assigned `0` here, not the actual value).
- **Resolve:** Symbolic references (like `"java/lang/System"`) are converted into direct memory references.

### Stage 5: Initialization
The `static` fields are assigned their actual values, and `static {}` blocks are executed **top to bottom**.

### Stage 6: Execution
The JVM locates `main(String[])` and creates a new **Stack Frame** for it on the main thread's **Stack**. The **PC Register** is set to point to the first instruction of `main`. The **Execution Engine** begins:

1. **Interpreter** mode reads each bytecode instruction.
2. The `ldc "Hello KTU"` instruction pushes the string constant onto the **Operand Stack**.
3. The `getstatic` instruction obtains a reference to `System.out`.
4. The `invokevirtual` instruction calls `println()`, which eventually becomes a native call to the OS.
5. The **JIT Compiler** monitors execution. If `println` is called many times, JIT compiles it to native code for speed.

### Stage 7: Output
The text **"Hello KTU"** appears on your screen.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Memory Errors and Their Causes

| Error Name | Memory Region | Typical Cause | KTU 3-Mark Favorite |
|---|---|---|---|
| `StackOverflowError` | Stack Area | Deep or infinite recursion | Fibonacci/method calling itself infinitely |
| `OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space` | Heap Area | Allocating huge arrays or object leaks | `new int[Integer.MAX_VALUE]` |
| `OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace` | Method Area (post-Java 8) | Classloader memory leak in app servers | Tomcat redeploying apps repeatedly |
| `OutOfMemoryError: PermGen` | Method Area (pre-Java 8) | Same as above, old JVMs | Java 7 and below |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Garbage Collection Eligibility

Consider the following code:

```java
public class GCTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        GCTest a = new GCTest();
        GCTest b = new GCTest();
        a = null;
        b = new GCTest();
        System.gc();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**

1. **Line 3** — Object 1 (`Obj1`) is created in the **Eden** space of the Young Generation. Reference `a` points to it.
2. **Line 4** — Object 2 (`Obj2`) is created in Eden. Reference `b` points to it.
3. **Line 5** — `a = null;` removes the only reference to `Obj1`. `Obj1` is now **eligible for GC** (no live reference in any stack frame).
4. **Line 6** — `Obj3` is created in Eden. `b` now points to `Obj3`. **`Obj2` is also eligible for GC** because its only reference (`b`) was reassigned.
5. **Line 7** — `System.gc()` is a *hint* to the JVM to run GC. After GC, `Obj1` and `Obj2` may be reclaimed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often think `System.gc()` *forces* immediate collection. It does NOT — it is only a *request*. The JVM may ignore it.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 JVM High-Level Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef engine fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef memory fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loader fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef native fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    cls[".class Bytecode File"]:::loader
    cl1["Class Loader Subsystem: Loading Linking Initialization"]:::loader
    ma["Runtime Data Areas: Method Area, Heap, Stack, PC Register, Native Method Stack"]:::memory
    ee["Execution Engine"]:::engine
    interp["Interpreter"]:::engine
    jit["JIT Compiler"]:::engine
    gc["Garbage Collector"]:::engine
    jni["JNI + Native Method Libraries"]:::native

    cls --> cl1
    cl1 --> ma
    ma --> ee
    ee --> interp
    ee --> jit
    ee --> gc
    ee --> jni

    jit -.compiles hot code into.-> ma
    gc -.reclaims memory.-> ma
```

## 4.2 ClassLoader Delegation Model (Parent-First Search)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef boot fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    classDef plat fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#277da1,stroke:#333,color:#fff

    req["Application requests class MyClass"]:::user
    app["Application ClassLoader: Check classpath"]:::app
    plat["Platform ClassLoader: Check jre/lib/ext"]:::plat
    boot["Bootstrap ClassLoader: Check rt.jar core APIs"]:::boot
    found["Class found, loaded into Method Area"]:::user
    fail["ClassNotFoundException"]:::user

    req --> app
    app -->|not found| plat
    plat -->|not found| boot
    boot -->|found| found
    boot -->|not found| fail
    app -->|found| found
```

## 4.3 JVM Memory Layout Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef shared fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef thread fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef heapg fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    subgraph SG1["Shared Memory (1 per JVM)"]
        direction TB
        ma["Method Area: Class metadata, static vars, constant pool"]:::shared
        heap["Heap: Young Gen Eden Survivor Old Gen Tenured"]:::heapg
    end

    subgraph SG2["Per-Thread Memory (1 per thread)"]
        direction TB
        pc["PC Register: Current instruction address"]:::thread
        stack["Stack: Stack frames per method"]:::thread
        native["Native Method Stack: C/C++ calls via JNI"]:::thread
    end

    threadN["Thread 1"] -.uses.-> pc
    threadN -.uses.-> stack
    threadN -.uses.-> native
    threadM["Thread N"] -.uses.-> pc
    threadM -.uses.-> stack
    threadM -.uses.-> native
```

## 4.4 WORA Pipeline Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef src fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    classDef bc fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef jvm fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    classDef hw fill:#577590,stroke:#333,color:#fff

    src["Hello.java on Windows"]:::src
    compile["javac compiler"]:::hw
    bc["Hello.class Bytecode identical everywhere"]:::bc
    winJVM["Windows JVM HotSpot"]:::jvm
    linJVM["Linux JVM HotSpot"]:::jvm
    macJVM["macOS JVM HotSpot"]:::jvm
    winOut["Hello KTU on Windows"]:::hw
    linOut["Hello KTU on Linux"]:::hw
    macOut["Hello KTU on macOS"]:::hw

    src --> compile --> bc
    bc --> winJVM --> winOut
    bc --> linJVM --> linOut
    bc --> macJVM --> macOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **identical `.class` file** sits in the center of the diagram — this visually reinforces the **WORA principle** that students must explain in any 7-mark KTU essay question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define JVM. Why is it called "virtual"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is a software-implemented abstract computing machine that provides the runtime environment to execute Java bytecode. It is called "virtual" because it is **not a physical machine** with real hardware components like registers or ALUs — instead, it is a **software simulation** of a CPU that emulates a real processor's instruction-fetch-decode-execute cycle entirely in code. **(2 marks)** The JVM is the only component in the Java ecosystem that is platform-dependent, allowing the same bytecode to run on any OS. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Q2. Differentiate between JDK, JRE, and JVM.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | JDK | JRE | JVM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Java Development Kit | Java Runtime Environment | Java Virtual Machine |
| **Contains** | JRE + `javac` + debugger | JVM + Library classes (rt.jar) | Runtime engine |
| **Used By** | Developers | End users | Both (internal) |
| **Includes Compiler?** | Yes | No | No |

JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM (superset relationship). **(1 mark for the relationship statement.)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - ESE Model Paper]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **internal architecture of the JVM** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each subsystem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **bytecode**? Explain the **execution flow** of a Java program from `.java` source file to screen output, mentioning the role of the **Class Loader**, **Method Area**, and **Execution Engine**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a)

The JVM architecture consists of **three main subsystems**:

1. **Class Loader Subsystem** — Handles the lifecycle of `.class` files.
   - **Loading:** Reads the binary data from the `.class` file and stores it in the Method Area. Uses the three-tier delegation: **Bootstrap → Platform → Application** ClassLoader. **(2 marks)**
   - **Linking:** Performs verification (structural correctness of bytecode), preparation (default values for static variables), and resolution (symbolic → direct reference conversion). **(1 mark)**
   - **Initialization:** Executes static variable assignments and `static {}` blocks in top-to-bottom order. **(1 mark)**

2. **Runtime Data Areas (Memory):**
   - **Method Area (shared):** Class-level data — fields, method bytecode, constant pool, static variables.
   - **Heap Area (shared):** All objects and instance variables. Divided into Young Gen (Eden + 2 Survivors) and Old Gen (Tenured).
   - **Stack Area (per thread):** Holds stack frames — local variables, operand stack, frame data.
   - **PC Register (per thread):** Holds address of the currently executing JVM instruction.
   - **Native Method Stack (per thread):** Holds native C/C++ method invocations. **(2 marks)**

3. **Execution Engine:** Converts bytecode into machine code.
   - **Interpreter:** Line-by-line execution.
   - **JIT Compiler:** Compiles "hot methods" (executed ≥ 10,000 times) into native code for performance.
   - **Garbage Collector:** Auto reclaims unreferenced objects from the Heap. **(1 mark)**

> A proper labeled block diagram earns the remaining marks. **(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b)

**Bytecode** is the platform-neutral, intermediate instruction set generated by the `javac` compiler. It is stored in `.class` files and is the *only language the JVM understands directly*. Each bytecode instruction is exactly **1 byte** long (hence the name "byte-code"), and the JVM has approximately **200+ opcode instructions** (e.g., `aload`, `astore`, `invokevirtual`, `getstatic`, `ireturn`). **(2 marks)**

**Execution Flow:**

1. **Compilation:** `javac Hello.java` → produces `Hello.class` (bytecode with magic number `0xCAFEBABE`). **(1 mark)**
2. **Invocation:** `java Hello` triggers JVM startup. **(0.5 marks)**
3. **Class Loading:** The Application ClassLoader requests `Hello.class`, which propagates up the delegation chain to Bootstrap if not found. The class is loaded into the Method Area. **(1 mark)**
4. **Linking:** Verifier checks bytecode safety; static fields get default values; symbolic references are resolved. **(1 mark)**
5. **Initialization:** `main()` method is identified. Static initializers run. **(0.5 marks)**
6. **Execution:** A new stack frame is created for `main()` on the main thread's stack. The PC register points to the first instruction. The Execution Engine runs the bytecode. Hot methods get JIT-compiled to native code. **(1 mark)**

> **Result:** The program output appears on the OS console via JNI calls. **(Refer to Section 3.1 for the full lifecycle diagram.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is the **WORA (Write Once, Run Anywhere)** principle? Explain how the **JVM enables platform independence** with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **ClassLoader Delegation Model** in detail. Why is this model important for **Java security**? List the three standard classloaders. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a)

**WORA Principle:** A Java program, once compiled into bytecode, can run on **any operating system or hardware architecture** without recompilation, provided a compatible JVM is installed. **(2 marks)**

**How JVM Enables Platform Independence:**

The `.java` source file is compiled by `javac` (which is platform-independent and part of the JDK) into a `.class` bytecode file. The `.class` file is *not* native machine code — it is an **abstract instruction set** designed for the JVM. When you run the program with `java`, the **OS-specific JVM** (HotSpot for Windows, HotSpot for Linux, etc.) translates each bytecode instruction into the **native machine code of the host platform**. **(3 marks)**

**Example:** A `Hello.java` file compiled on Windows produces the exact same `Hello.class` file that was compiled on Linux. When the same `Hello.class` is run:
- On **Windows** → Windows JVM invokes Windows API to print to console.
- On **Linux** → Linux JVM invokes Linux `write()` syscall.
- On **macOS** → macOS JVM invokes Cocoa APIs.

The **only platform-dependent part** is the JVM itself; the bytecode and source code are platform-independent. **(2 marks)**

> **Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.4 (WORA Pipeline Flow).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b)

The **ClassLoader Delegation Model** is a hierarchical class-loading mechanism in the JVM where every classloader (except the Bootstrap) has a parent, and a child classloader **delegates** the class-loading request to its parent first. Only if the parent fails to find the class does the child attempt to load it itself. **(2 marks)**

**Three Standard ClassLoaders (top to bottom):**

1. **Bootstrap ClassLoader** — The root, native (C/C++) loader. Loads core Java APIs from `<JAVA_HOME>/jre/lib/rt.jar` (e.g., `java.lang.*`, `java.util.*`). It has no parent. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Platform (Extension) ClassLoader** — Loads extension classes from `<JAVA_HOME>/jre/lib/ext/`. Its parent is the Bootstrap. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Application (System) ClassLoader** — Loads classes from the **classpath** (user-written code, third-party JARs). Its parent is the Platform. **(1 mark)**

**Why Important for Security:**

The delegation model **prevents malicious code from overriding core Java classes**. For example, if an attacker writes a fake `java.lang.String` class containing a `main()` method that runs malicious code, the Application ClassLoader would delegate the request upward. The Bootstrap ClassLoader would find the *real* `java.lang.String` in `rt.jar` and load it — the attacker's class is **never loaded**. This is called the **"sandbox security model"** of Java. **(1 mark)**

> **Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.2.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on JVM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing JDK with JVM** — Saying *"JDK executes bytecode"* is wrong. **JVM executes bytecode; JDK compiles source code.**
> 2. **Forgetting the ClassLoader order** — The delegation is **PARENT FIRST**, not child first. Writing *"Application ClassLoader loads it first"* loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Saying "JVM is a physical machine"** — It is purely **software**. "Virtual" is the key word.
> 4. **Missing the magic number** — In a bytecode execution question, mentioning `0xCAFEBABE` earns an extra 1 mark and signals deep understanding.
> 5. **Confusing `System.gc()` with forced GC** — It is a *request*, not a *command*. The JVM may ignore it.
> 6. **Forgetting the 5 Runtime Data Areas** — A common 7-mark question asks you to *list and explain* all five. Missing even one loses 1.5 marks.
> 7. **Saying "Stack stores objects"** — Objects are in the **Heap**. Stack stores **references** and **primitive local variables** only.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — JVM (Module 1):**
>
> - **JVM = Java Virtual Machine** = software-implemented abstract processor that runs bytecode.
> - **WORA** works because `.class` files are platform-neutral; each OS has its own JVM.
> - **JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM** (superset relationship). JDK is for devs, JRE is for users.
> - **Three subsystems of JVM:** ClassLoader + Runtime Data Areas + Execution Engine.
> - **ClassLoader phases:** Loading → Linking (Verify → Prepare → Resolve) → Initialization.
> - **Delegation model:** Parent-first, three-tier (Bootstrap → Platform → Application).
> - **5 Memory Areas:** Method Area, Heap (shared) + Stack, PC Register, Native Method Stack (per-thread).
> - **Heap = Young Gen (Eden + 2 Survivors) + Old Gen (Tenured)**.
> - **StackOverflowError** = deep recursion; **OutOfMemoryError: heap space** = object leak.
> - **Execution Engine** = Interpreter + JIT Compiler + Garbage Collector.
> - **JIT threshold** ≈ 10,000 calls → native code compilation.
> - **`System.gc()`** is a *request*, not a *command*.
> - **Magic number of `.class` file** = `0xCAFEBABE`.
> - **Bytecode instructions** = 1 byte each, ~200 opcodes (e.g., `getstatic`, `invokevirtual`).
> - **Security:** Delegation model prevents malicious `java.lang.*` class spoofing.
> - **JVM is platform-dependent**; bytecode is platform-independent.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primitive Data Types and Wrapper Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Primitive Data Types and Wrapper Types # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Primitive Data Types > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Primitive data types* are the **fundamental, pre-defined, built-in data types** provided by the Java language that serve as the basic building blocks for data manipulation. They are not objects and hold their values directly in memory (typically on the stack). In Java (the canonical language for KTU OOP concepts), there are exactly **8 primitive data types**: | # | Type | Size | Default Value | Range / Description | |---|------|------|---------------|---------------------| | 1 | `byte` | **8-bit** | `0` | $-128$ to $127$ | | 2 | `short` | **16-bit** | `0` | $-32{,}768$ to $32{,}767$ | | 3 | `int` | **32-bit** | `0` | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ | | 4 | `long` | **64-bit** | `0L` | $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ | | 5 | `float` | **32-bit** | `0.0f` | IEEE 754 single precision | | 6 | `double` | **64-bit** | `0.0d` | IEEE 754 double precision | | 7 | `char` | **16-bit** | `'\u0000'` | Unicode character (0 to 65,535) | | 8 | `boolean` | **1-bit (logical)** | `false` | `true` or `false` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Primitives in Java are **not objects** — they do **not** inherit from `Object` and have **no methods**. They are stored *by value* in stack memory, making them extremely fast and memory-efficient. ## Wrapper Types > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Wrapper classes* are **object counterparts of primitive data types** defined in the `java.lang` package. They "wrap" a primitive value inside an object, thereby enabling primitives to participate in object-oriented operations such as being added to `Collections`, used with *Generics*, and synchronized across threads. The **8 wrapper classes** in Java are: `Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`, `Character`, `Boolean`. > [!TIP] > **Naming Convention:** The wrapper class name is simply the **capitalized** version of the primitive name — except for `int` → `Integer` and `char` → `Character`. ## Conceptual Analogy (Plain English) > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** > Think of a **primitive** as a **raw, unlabeled grocery item** (loose rice, loose sugar) — fast to grab, cheap, and lightweight. A **wrapper class** is the **same grocery item but sealed inside a branded, labeled plastic packet** — heavier, but now it has a barcode, a brand name, an expiry date, and can be stacked on shelves (Collections), shipped in standardized boxes (Generics), and tracked in inventory (Object methods like `toString()`, `equals()`). The Java compiler (since JDK 5) acts as an **automatic packaging machine** that can seal/unseal items at runtime — this magic is called **Autoboxing** and **Unboxing**. ## Why Do We Need Wrappers? > [!IMPORTANT] > 1. **Collections Framework** (`ArrayList`, `HashMap`, etc.) only store **objects**, not primitives. > 2. **Generics** (`List`, `Map`) require type parameters to be **reference types**. > 3. **Utility methods**: parsing (`Integer.parseInt()`), conversions, and constants (`Integer.MAX_VALUE`). > 4. **Object behaviors**: `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `toString()`, `compareTo()`. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanics ### A. Autoboxing (Primitive → Wrapper) Autoboxing is the **automatic conversion** performed by the Java compiler that converts a primitive value into an instance of its corresponding wrapper class. **Logic Steps:** 1. Compiler detects a primitive being assigned to a wrapper-type variable (or passed as an object argument). 2. Compiler inserts an implicit call to the wrapper's `valueOf()` static factory method. 3. The wrapper object is created (or retrieved from cache) on the heap. 4. The reference to the wrapper object is stored in the variable. **Example:** ```java Integer x = 25; // Compiler internally: Integer x = Integer.valueOf(25); List list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add(10); // Compiler internally: list.add(Integer.valueOf(10)); ``` ### B. Unboxing (Wrapper → Primitive) Unboxing is the **reverse automatic conversion** — extracting a primitive value from its wrapper object. **Logic Steps:** 1. Compiler detects a wrapper object being used in a context that requires a primitive (arithmetic, assignment to primitive variable). 2. Compiler inserts an implicit call to the wrapper's `xxxValue()` instance method. 3. The primitive value is extracted and used directly. **Example:** ```java Integer a = 50; int b = a; // Compiler internally: int b = a.intValue(); int c = a + 10; // Compiler internally: int c = a.intValue() + 10; ``` ### C. The Integer Cache (Memory Optimization Trick) > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Concept:** The wrapper classes cache frequently used values to save memory. > - `Integer` caches values in the range **$-128$ to $127$** (default, configurable via `-XX:AutoBoxCacheMax`). > - `Boolean` returns cached instances `Boolean.TRUE` and `Boolean.FALSE`. > - `Character` caches values from **$0$ to $127$** (ASCII range). > - `Byte`, `Short`, `Long` cache the entire range. > - `Float` and `Double` **do NOT cache** (because the range is too vast). **Consequence (common exam trap!):** ```java Integer a = 127; Integer b = 127; // a == b → TRUE (same cached object) Integer c = 128; Integer d = 128; // c == d → FALSE (different objects) ``` ### D. Immutability of Wrappers > [!IMPORTANT] > All wrapper objects in Java are **immutable** — once created, the wrapped primitive value **cannot be changed**. Any "modification" produces a *new object*. This makes them inherently **thread-safe**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility | |---|---------|----------------|---------------------| | 1 | Size of `int` | $2^{32}$ distinct values, half negative, half non-negative | Memory budgeting in embedded systems | | 2 | Range of any signed integer type of *n* bits | $-2^{n-1}$ to $2^{n-1}-1$ | Sensor data storage design | | 3 | Floating-point precision (`float`) | ~7 significant decimal digits | Scientific simulation | | 4 | Floating-point precision (`double`) | ~15-17 significant decimal digits | Financial/Engineering computing | | 5 | Autoboxing rule | `wrapperRef = primitiveValue` ⇒ calls `valueOf()` | Type-bridging in generic APIs | | 6 | Unboxing rule | `primitiveVar = wrapperRef` ⇒ calls `xxxValue()` | Arithmetic on collections | | 7 | Integer cache range | $[-128, \ 127]$ | Identity comparison pitfalls | | 8 | Identity vs Equality | `==` compares references; `.equals()` compares values | Writing correct comparison logic | | 9 | Parsing rule | `Integer.parseInt(String) → int` | I/O & GUI input handling | | 10 | Default values | Numeric $\to 0$, `boolean \to false$, `char \to '\u0000'` | Class-field initialization | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded Systems & IoT:** Choosing `byte` or `short` saves precious RAM in microcontrollers. - **Scientific Computing:** `double` provides IEEE 754 double-precision required for numerical simulations (CFD, FEM). - **Database & Web Apps:** Wrapper classes allow primitives to be stored in `HashMap`-based caches, used in `ArrayList`, and serialized over the network. - **Financial Systems:** `BigDecimal` (related wrapper concept) prevents floating-point rounding errors. - **Unicode-aware applications:** `char` holding 16-bit Unicode enables internationalization (i18n) — crucial in KTU projects handling multi-language input. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Range of a Signed Integer Type > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Prove why an *n-bit* signed integer has the range $-2^{n-1}$ to $2^{n-1}-1$. ### Step 1 — Total Representable Patterns For an *n*-bit binary number, the total number of unique bit-patterns is: $$2^{n}$$ **Reasoning:** Each of the *n* bits has 2 possibilities (0 or 1), and by the multiplication principle, total combinations = $\underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{n \text{ times}} = 2^{n}$. ### Step 2 — Split for Sign (2's Complement) To represent both positive and negative numbers, we use **2's complement representation**: - Reserve **1 bit** (the *most significant bit*) as the sign bit. - Remaining *n − 1* bits encode magnitude. - Total non-negative numbers representable: $2^{n-1}$ (from $0$ to $2^{n-1}-1$). - Total negative numbers representable: $2^{n-1}$ (from $-2^{n-1}$ to $-1$). ### Step 3 — Combine $$\text{Range} = [-2^{n-1}, \ 2^{n-1} - 1]$$ ### Step 4 — Apply to Specific Primitives **Case A: `int` (n = 32)** $$\text{Range}_{\text{int}} = [-2^{31}, \ 2^{31}-1] = [-2{,}147{,}483{,}648, \ 2{,}147{,}483{,}647]$$ **Case B: `byte` (n = 8)** $$\text{Range}_{\text{byte}} = [-2^{7}, \ 2^{7}-1] = [-128, \ 127]$$ **Case C: `long` (n = 64)** $$\text{Range}_{\text{long}} = [-2^{63}, \ 2^{63}-1]$$ These are the exact limits observable via `Integer.MIN_VALUE` and `Integer.MAX_VALUE` in Java. ## 3.2 Derivation: Autoboxing's Internal Code Transformation Let the high-level source code be: ```java Integer box = 50; int prim = box; ``` ### Compiler Translation Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Identify Context** Compiler sees a primitive `50` being assigned to reference type `Integer` → triggers **autoboxing**. **Step 2 — Lookup Factory** Compiler substitutes the literal with a call to `Integer.valueOf(50)`: $$\text{Integer box = Integer.valueOf(50);}$$ **Step 3 — Identify Reverse Context** Compiler sees wrapper `box` being assigned to primitive `int` → triggers **unboxing**. **Step 4 — Lookup Extractor** Compiler substitutes the assignment with a call to `box.intValue()`: $$\text{int prim = box.intValue();}$$ **Step 5 — Final Bytecode-Level Equivalent** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Integer box} &\Rightarrow \text{Integer.valueOf(50)} \\ \text{int prim} &\Rightarrow \text{box.intValue()} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Memory Footprint Comparison | Type | Size (bytes) | Stored As | On Stack / Heap | Access Speed | Default | |------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|--------------|---------| | `int` | 4 | raw value | Stack (local) / Heap (field) | **Fastest** | `0` | | `Integer` | 16 (header + value) | object reference | Heap (object) + Stack (ref) | Slower | `null` | | `double` | 8 | raw value | Stack (local) | **Fastest** | `0.0d` | | `Double` | 24 | object reference | Heap + Stack | Slower | `null` | | `char` | 2 | raw value | Stack | **Fastest** | `'\u0000'` | | `Character` | 16 | object reference | Heap + Stack | Slower | `null` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** For a million-element numeric array, using `int[]` consumes ~4 MB, while `Integer[]` consumes ~16 MB — a **4x memory blow-up** plus extra GC pressure. Use primitives for hot loops; use wrappers for storage in collections. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Wrapper Class Method Reference | Wrapper | `valueOf()` Input | `xxxValue()` Output | Key Constants | Parsing Method | |---------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------|----------------| | `Integer` | `int`, `String` | `intValue()` | `MAX_VALUE = 2^{31}-1`, `MIN_VALUE = -2^{31}` | `parseInt(String)` | | `Double` | `double`, `String` | `doubleValue()` | `MAX_VALUE`, `MIN_VALUE`, `NaN`, `POSITIVE_INFINITY` | `parseDouble(String)` | | `Character` | `char` | `charValue()` | `MAX_RADIX`, `MIN_RADIX` | N/A | | `Boolean` | `boolean`, `String` | `booleanValue()` | `TRUE`, `FALSE` | `parseBoolean(String)` | | `Long` | `long`, `String` | `longValue()` | `MAX_VALUE = 2^{63}-1$` | `parseLong(String)` | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Java Data Types ```mermaid graph TD A[All Java Types] --> B[Primitive Types] A --> C[Reference Types] B --> B1[byte] B --> B2[short] B --> B3[int] B --> B4[long] B --> B5[float] B --> B6[double] B --> B7[char] B --> B8[boolean] C --> C1[Wrapper Classes] C --> C2[User-Defined Classes] C --> C3[Arrays] C1 --> W1[Byte] C1 --> W2[Short] C1 --> W3[Integer] C1 --> W4[Long] C1 --> W5[Float] C1 --> W6[Double] C1 --> W7[Character] C1 --> W8[Boolean] B1 -.->|wrapped by| W1 B2 -.->|wrapped by| W2 B3 -.->|wrapped by| W3 B4 -.->|wrapped by| W4 B5 -.->|wrapped by| W5 B6 -.->|wrapped by| W6 B7 -.->|wrapped by| W7 B8 -.->|wrapped by| W8 ``` ## 4.2 Autoboxing & Unboxing — Data Flow ```mermaid graph LR P[Primitive Value 25] -->|Autoboxing
valueOf| H1[Heap: Integer object] H1 -->|Unboxing
intValue| P2[Primitive Value 25] H1 -->|Stored in| L[List Integer] L -->|Used in arithmetic| P3[Primitive Result] P3 -->|Autoboxing| H2[New Integer object] ``` ## 4.3 Integer Cache Mechanism — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD SRC[Source: Integer x = 127] --> CHK{Value in range
-128 to 127?} CHK -->|Yes| CACHE[Return cached Integer
from internal array] CHK -->|No| NEW[new Integer object
allocated on heap] CACHE --> REF1[Reference A points to
shared cached object] NEW --> REF2[Reference B points to
freshly created object] REF1 --> CMP1[== comparison: TRUE
same memory address] REF2 --> CMP2[== comparison: FALSE
different memory addresses] ``` ## 4.4 Wrapper Class Internal Architecture (e.g., Integer) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph HEAP[Heap Memory] OBJ[Integer Object] OBJ --> F1[final int value] OBJ --> M1[Method intValue] OBJ --> M2[Method equals] OBJ --> M3[Method hashCode] OBJ --> M4[Method toString] OBJ --> M5[Static parseInt] end subgraph STACK[Stack Memory] REF[Reference variable] end REF -->|points to| OBJ ``` ## 4.5 Boxing / Unboxing in a Generic Collection ```mermaid graph LR A[Coder writes list.add 10] --> B[Compiler detects autoboxing] B --> C[Compiles to list.add Integer.valueOf 10] C --> D[Integer cached object returned] D --> E[Stored inside ArrayList internal array] E --> F[Coder writes int x = list.get 0] F --> G[Compiler detects unboxing] G --> H[Compiles to int x = Integer.intValue] H --> I[Primitive 10 returned to x] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Differentiate between primitive data types and wrapper classes in Java. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Primitive | Wrapper | |--------|-----------|---------| | Nature | Not an object | An object | | Storage | Stack (direct value) | Heap (object + stack reference) | | Memory | Small (1–8 bytes) | Larger (header + value) | | Default | `0`, `false`, `'\u0000'` | `null` | | Usage | Arithmetic, local variables | Collections, Generics, Object methods | | Example | `int x = 10;` | `Integer x = 10;` | *(Any 3 correct differences with example = 3 marks)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is autoboxing? Show with an example how the Java compiler handles it internally.** **Model Answer:** Autoboxing is the automatic conversion of a primitive value into a corresponding wrapper object by the Java compiler. **Example:** `Integer obj = 50;` **Internal Compilation:** `Integer obj = Integer.valueOf(50);` The compiler substitutes the literal `50` with a call to the `valueOf()` static factory method of the `Integer` class. *(3 marks)* --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain the eight primitive data types in Java with their size and range. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With suitable code examples, explain autoboxing and unboxing. Discuss the Integer caching mechanism and its significance. *(7 marks)* --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — The 8 Primitive Data Types The eight primitive data types in Java are pre-defined by the language and store raw values directly in memory. **1. `byte` — 8-bit signed integer** Range: $-128$ to $127$ Default: `0` Use: Memory-constrained arrays, binary file I/O. **2. `short` — 16-bit signed integer** Range: $-32{,}768$ to $32{,}767$ Default: `0` Use: Small-range integer arithmetic. **3. `int` — 32-bit signed integer** *(most commonly used)* Range: $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ (approximately $\pm 2.1 \times 10^{9}$) Default: `0` Use: General-purpose integer arithmetic, loop counters, array indices. **4. `long` — 64-bit signed integer** Range: $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ Default: `0L` Use: Large counters (epoch milliseconds, file sizes in bytes), cryptography. **5. `float` — 32-bit IEEE 754 floating-point** Precision: ~7 decimal digits Default: `0.0f` Use: Graphics, low-precision scientific calculations. **6. `double` — 64-bit IEEE 754 floating-point** *(default for decimals)* Precision: ~15–17 decimal digits Default: `0.0d` Use: Scientific computing, engineering simulations, financial math (with care). **7. `char` — 16-bit Unicode character** Range: `0` to $65{,}535$ (`\u0000` to `\uffff`) Default: `'\u0000'` Use: Single-character storage, Unicode text processing. **8. `boolean` — logical type** Values: `true` or `false` Default: `false` Use: Conditional logic, flags. **Memory representation** — primitives are stored on the stack (locals) or inline in objects (fields). They are accessed by value and are not objects, meaning they have no methods and cannot be `null`. #### Part (b) — Autoboxing, Unboxing & Integer Cache **Autoboxing** (since JDK 1.5) is the automatic conversion of a primitive into its corresponding wrapper object. ```java Integer x = 50; // autoboxes int 50 into Integer Double d = 3.14; // autoboxes double 3.14 into Double List list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add(100); // autoboxes int 100 into Integer ``` **Unboxing** is the reverse — automatic extraction of a primitive from a wrapper. ```java Integer a = 200; int b = a; // unboxes Integer a to int b int c = a + 50; // unboxes, then adds int d = list.get(0); // unboxes the Integer returned by get() ``` **Integer Caching Mechanism:** For memory efficiency, `Integer.valueOf(int)` returns **cached objects** for values in the range $-128$ to $127$ (inclusive). This range is the default cache size of the `IntegerCache` inner class. ```java Integer p = 127; Integer q = 127; // p == q → TRUE (same cached object) Integer r = 128; Integer s = 128; // r == s → FALSE (two new objects) ``` **Significance:** 1. Saves memory by avoiding thousands of duplicate `Integer` objects. 2. Improves performance for common small-value scenarios (loop counters, ASCII codes, small array indices). 3. Creates a *subtle pitfall*: using `==` instead of `.equals()` for value comparison may give unexpected results outside the cache range. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the cache range** — saying "all Integers are cached" is **WRONG** (only $-128$ to $127$). > 2. **Confusing `==` with `.equals()`** — `==` compares references (object addresses), `.equals()` compares wrapped values. > 3. **Ignoring NullPointerException** — unboxing a `null` wrapper throws `NullPointerException` at runtime. > 4. **Forgetting that `Float` and `Double` are NOT cached** — they always create new objects. > 5. **Missing that wrapper objects are immutable** — methods like `Integer.intValue()` return primitives, not modify the object. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** What are wrapper classes in Java? List all eight wrapper classes with their corresponding primitive types. Explain any five utility methods of the `Integer` class. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the immutability of wrapper classes. Why is immutability important? With a code example, show how autoboxing and unboxing can lead to a `NullPointerException`. *(7 marks)* --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Wrapper Classes Wrapper classes are object-oriented counterparts of the eight primitive data types, defined in the `java.lang` package. They "wrap" a primitive value inside an object, enabling primitives to be used wherever objects are required (e.g., `Collections`, `Generics`, `Object` methods). **Mapping Table (must-memorize for KTU):** | Primitive | Wrapper | Constructor (deprecated) | |-----------|---------|--------------------------| | `byte` | `Byte` | `new Byte(byte)` | | `short` | `Short` | `new Short(short)` | | `int` | `Integer` | `new Integer(int)` | | `long` | `Long` | `new Long(long)` | | `float` | `Float` | `new Float(float)` | | `double` | `Double` | `new Double(double)` | | `char` | `Character` | `new Character(char)`| | `boolean` | `Boolean` | `new Boolean(boolean)`| **Five Utility Methods of `Integer`:** 1. **`Integer.parseInt(String s)`** — Parses a decimal `String` and returns an `int`. `int n = Integer.parseInt("123");` → returns `123` 2. **`Integer.valueOf(int i)`** — Returns an `Integer` instance (from cache if in range $-128$ to $127$). `Integer obj = Integer.valueOf(50);` 3. **`Integer.toString(int i)`** — Converts an `int` to its `String` representation. `String s = Integer.toString(456);` → returns `"456"` 4. **`Integer.intValue()`** — Unboxes the `Integer` to a primitive `int`. `int x = obj.intValue();` 5. **`Integer.compare(int a, int b)`** — Returns negative, zero, or positive for `<`, `==`, `>`. `int r = Integer.compare(10, 20);` → returns `-1` Other useful methods: `Integer.MAX_VALUE`, `Integer.MIN_VALUE`, `Integer.bitCount(int)`, `Integer.reverse(int)`, `Integer.sum(int,int)`. #### Part (b) — Immutability & NullPointerException **Immutability** means that once a wrapper object is created, its internal primitive value **cannot be changed**. All wrapper classes in Java are declared `final` and their internal `value` field is `private final`. Any operation that appears to "modify" the wrapper actually returns a **new object**. **Why immutability is important:** 1. **Thread-safety:** Immutable objects can be shared freely across threads without synchronization. 2. **Security:** Prevents accidental modification of critical values (e.g., passwords, keys). 3. **Caching support:** Enables the Integer cache mechanism ($[-128, 127]$). 4. **Hash stability:** `hashCode()` is constant — safe to use as `HashMap` keys. 5. **Predictable behavior:** Eliminates aliasing bugs. **NullPointerException via Unboxing:** ```java public class DemoNPE { public static void main(String[] args) { Integer wrapped = null; // allowed: reference is null int primitive = wrapped; // unboxing triggers NPE! System.out.println(primitive); } } ``` **Output:** `Exception in thread "main" java.lang.NullPointerException` **Explanation:** The compiler inserts `wrapped.intValue()`. Since `wrapped` is `null`, the JVM cannot invoke an instance method on it, and throws `NullPointerException`. This is a common runtime hazard when retrieving wrapper values from `Map.get()` (which returns `null` for missing keys) and then unboxing them. **Mitigation:** Always check for `null` before unboxing, or use `Optional`, or use `Integer.parseInt()` only on validated non-null strings. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam!)** - ☐ **Exactly 8 primitive types** in Java: `byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean`. - ☐ Primitives are **not objects**; they are stored **by value** in stack memory. - ☐ **Wrapper classes** = object form of primitives; all reside in `java.lang` (auto-imported). - ☐ Naming: `int → Integer`, `char → Character`; others are simply capitalized. - ☐ `Integer`/`Byte`/`Short`/`Long`/`Character` cache commonly used values; **`Float` and `Double` do NOT cache**. - ☐ Default `Integer` cache range: **$-128$ to $127$**. - ☐ **Autoboxing:** primitive → wrapper (compiler inserts `valueOf()`). - ☐ **Unboxing:** wrapper → primitive (compiler inserts `xxxValue()`). - ☐ Unboxing a `null` wrapper throws **NullPointerException**. - ☐ All wrapper objects are **immutable** and hence **thread-safe**. - ☐ Use `==` for reference comparison; use **`.equals()` for value comparison** of wrappers. - ☐ Range formula for *n*-bit signed integer: $-2^{n-1}$ to $2^{n-1}-1$. - ☐ `char` in Java is **16-bit Unicode**, not 8-bit ASCII. - ☐ Use **primitives** for performance-critical loops; use **wrappers** for Collections/Generics. - ☐ For binary data → `byte`; for large counters → `long`; for decimals → `double` (default). > **End of Module 1 Notes — Primitive Data Types and Wrapper Types**

topics.title: Casting and Autoboxing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Casting and Autoboxing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Type Casting — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Type Casting** is the process of converting a value of one data type into another data type. In Java (the reference language for PECMT535), casting is broadly classified into **Primitive Type Casting** (between fundamental data types) and **Reference Type Casting** (between objects of related classes via inheritance).

### Primitive Type Casting
- **Widening Casting (Implicit / Automatic):** Converting a *smaller* data type to a *larger* data type. Performed by the compiler automatically because no information is lost.
- **Narrowing Casting (Explicit / Manual):** Converting a *larger* data type to a *smaller* data type. Must be written explicitly by the programmer using the cast operator `(type)`, because precision or magnitude may be lost.

### Reference Type Casting
- **Upcasting (Implicit):** Converting a subclass reference to a superclass reference. Always safe.
- **Downcasting (Explicit):** Converting a superclass reference back to a subclass reference. Must be explicitly written, and is only valid if the object is actually an instance of the target subclass (else `ClassCastException`).

---

## 1.2 Autoboxing & Unboxing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion that the Java compiler makes between a **primitive type** and its corresponding **wrapper class object** (e.g., `int` → `Integer`). The reverse process, converting a wrapper object back into its primitive, is called **Unboxing** (e.g., `Integer` → `int`). Both features were introduced in **Java 5 (JDK 1.5)**.

Wrapper class mappings: `byte → Byte`, `short → Short`, `int → Integer`, `long → Long`, `float → Float`, `double → Double`, `char → Character`, `boolean → Boolean`.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Type Casting:** Think of casting like pouring water between containers of different sizes. Pouring from a small glass into a large bucket (**widening**) is easy and automatic — nothing spills. But pouring from a large bucket into a small glass (**narrowing**) requires careful manual effort, and some water *may* spill — you must explicitly tell the system what you are doing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Autoboxing:** Imagine a **primitive** (`int x = 5;`) is a *raw coin* sitting in your pocket, while a **wrapper object** (`Integer x = 5;`) is that same coin neatly sealed inside a labeled envelope. **Autoboxing** is the automatic act of the cashier sealing your coin into an envelope. **Unboxing** is the automatic act of tearing open the envelope to use the coin again. The wrapping is "free" and invisible to you.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- The **default cache range** for autoboxed `Integer`, `Short`, `Byte`, `Character`, and `Long` values is **−128 to +127**. Values in this range return the *same* cached object, while values outside return *new* objects.
- The cache size is tunable via the JVM flag: `-XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=<size>`.
- The default `Boolean` cache holds exactly **2** instances: `Boolean.TRUE` and `Boolean.FALSE`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Primitive Casting — Operational Logic Steps

1. **Identify the source and target types** and place them in the numeric promotion hierarchy:
   `byte → short → int → long → float → double`
   (Note: `char` is a *separate* branch that promotes to `int`.)
2. **Determine direction:**
   - If target is to the **right** of source → **Widening (Implicit).**
   - If target is to the **left** of source → **Narrowing (Explicit).**
3. **Compiler check:** Widening is always legal. Narrowing requires the cast operator `(targetType)` and may throw at runtime if the value exceeds the target's range (truncation/overflow).
4. **Compile-time vs Runtime:** The cast itself is a *compile-time* operation; the overflow/truncation is a *runtime* bit-reinterpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is widening safe?** Because a larger type holds every distinct value of a smaller type. **Why is narrowing risky?** Because the larger type may contain values that the smaller type cannot represent — bits are simply *reinterpreted* modulo the smaller type's range.

---

## 2.2 Reference Casting — Operational Logic Steps

1. **Upcasting (Subclass → Superclass):**
   - Done *implicitly*.
   - Used in **polymorphism** — the reference type is widened so the object can be treated as a more general type.
   - Always succeeds at runtime.
2. **Downcasting (Superclass → Subclass):**
   - Must be written *explicitly* with the cast operator.
   - Compiler checks the *static* type hierarchy (IS-A relationship must exist).
   - JVM checks at runtime via `instanceof` semantics — if the actual object is **not** a subtype, a `ClassCastException` is thrown.

---

## 2.3 Autoboxing / Unboxing — Operational Logic Steps

1. The compiler inserts the equivalent of `valueOf()` calls for autoboxing and `intValue()` / `doubleValue()` etc. for unboxing.
2. This happens in:
   - Assignment: `Integer i = 10;`
   - Method arguments: `list.add(5);`
   - Arithmetic / comparison operations.
3. **Caching:** For `Integer`, values in `[−128, 127]` use the `IntegerCache`, so `Integer a = 127; Integer b = 127; a == b` is `true`, but the same may be `false` for `128`.
4. **NullPointerException risk:** Unboxing a `null` wrapper throws `NullPointerException`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `==` Trap (KTU favourite):** Always use `.equals()` to compare wrapper objects. The `==` operator compares **references** (for objects), not values. This is a high-frequency KTU question.

---

## 2.4 KTU Reference Cheat Sheet (Rules & Truth Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **minimum** set of rules a student must memorize for KTU ESE and internal evaluations. No mathematical formulas with vertical bars are used — `\vert` substitution is avoided entirely for table safety.

| # | Rule Category | Specific Rule | Result / Behaviour |
|---|---------------|---------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Widening Primitive | `byte` → `short` → `int` → `long` → `float` → `double` | **Automatic**, no cast needed |
| 2 | Narrowing Primitive | `double` → `int`, `long` → `short`, `int` → `byte` | **Explicit cast required** `(type)` |
| 3 | Boolean Casting | `boolean` cannot be cast to/from any numeric type | **Compile-time error** |
| 4 | Upcasting | `Dog d = new Dog(); Animal a = d;` | **Implicit**, always legal |
| 5 | Downcasting | `Animal a = new Dog(); Dog d = (Dog) a;` | **Explicit**, may throw `ClassCastException` |
| 6 | Autoboxing | `Integer i = 10;` | Compiler inserts `Integer.valueOf(10)` |
| 7 | Unboxing | `int n = new Integer(20);` | Compiler inserts `intValue()` |
| 8 | Integer Cache | `Integer.valueOf(n)` for n in `[-128, 127]` | Returns **cached** instance |
| 9 | `==` on Wrappers | Compares **reference** for same type | `Integer 127 == Integer 127` → **true** ; `128 == 128` → **false** |
| 10 | Null Unboxing | `int x = (Integer) null;` | Throws **NullPointerException** |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Casting** is the backbone of *polymorphic collections* (e.g., a `List<Animal>` storing `Dog`, `Cat`, `Bird`).
- **Autoboxing** is the bridge that lets primitives participate in *Generics* (`List<Integer>`), which Java forbids for raw primitives.
- Both features power **Collections Framework** APIs, **streams**, and **lambda expressions** in modern Java enterprise systems (Spring, Android, Big Data).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Widening Primitive Casting

```java
int    a = 100;
double b = a;          // implicit widening int -> double
System.out.println(b); // 100.0
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
1. `a` is of type `int` (32-bit signed).
2. The compiler knows `int → double` is a *widening* conversion (target is to the right in the hierarchy).
3. No cast operator is required; the compiler silently performs the promotion.
4. The result is `100.0` (the bit pattern of `100` is preserved exactly because `double` is a 64-bit IEEE-754 type that can represent every `int`).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Narrowing Primitive Casting (Truncation)

```java
double x = 9.78;
int    y = (int) x;    // explicit narrowing double -> int
System.out.println(y); // 9  (fractional part TRUNCATED, not rounded)
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
1. `x` holds `9.78` (a `double`).
2. `int` cannot hold fractional values; conversion requires explicit cast.
3. The JVM *truncates toward zero* — `.78` is dropped, leaving `9`.
4. **No rounding** occurs; the value is bit-truncated.

**Mathematical expression of truncation:**

$$
y = \lfloor x \rfloor \quad \text{(for positive x)}, \qquad y = \lceil x \rceil \quad \text{(for negative x)}
$$

So for `x = -9.78` cast to `int`, the result is `-9` (truncation toward zero, not floor).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Reference Upcasting & Downcasting

```java
class Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("..."); } }
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override void sound() { System.out.println("Bark"); }
    void fetch() { System.out.println("Fetch!"); }
}
```

**Step 1 — Upcasting (Implicit):**

```java
Dog d = new Dog();
Animal a = d;       // implicit upcast; safe
a.sound();          // runtime dispatch -> "Bark" (polymorphism)
// a.fetch();       // COMPILE ERROR: Animal has no fetch()
```

**Step 2 — Downcasting (Explicit, validated):**

```java
Animal a2 = new Dog();
if (a2 instanceof Dog) {           // safe guard
    Dog d2 = (Dog) a2;             // explicit downcast
    d2.fetch();                    // OK now
}
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
1. The compiler allows the cast `a2 → Dog` because `Dog` IS-A `Animal` (static check passes).
2. At runtime, the JVM verifies the *actual* object type. Since the object truly is a `Dog`, the cast succeeds.
3. If the object had been `new Cat()` cast to `Dog`, the JVM would throw `ClassCastException`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Forgetting the `instanceof` check before downcasting is one of the most common runtime-crash causes in Java enterprise code. KTU frequently tests this in Part B.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Autoboxing & Unboxing Mechanics

```java
Integer  a = 127;        // autoboxing: Integer.valueOf(127)
Integer  b = 127;
System.out.println(a == b);   // true  (cache hit)

Integer  c = 128;        // autoboxing: Integer.valueOf(128)
Integer  d = 128;
System.out.println(c == d);   // false (out of cache range)

Integer  e = 1000;       // autoboxing
int      f = e;          // unboxing: e.intValue()
System.out.println(f);        // 1000
```

**Step-by-step reasoning (with cache mechanics):**

$$
\text{Integer.valueOf}(n) = \begin{cases}
\text{cached\_Integer}[n + 128] & \text{if } -128 \le n \le 127 \\
\text{new Integer}(n) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

1. For `n = 127`: `valueOf` returns the *cached* object reference at index `255`. Both `a` and `b` point to the **same** reference → `a == b` is `true`.
2. For `n = 128`: `valueOf` constructs **two distinct** `Integer` objects. `c == d` compares distinct references → `false`.
3. The unboxing `f = e` calls `e.intValue()` internally.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Autoboxing with `null` (Trap Question)

```java
Integer g = null;
int     h = g;          // throws NullPointerException at runtime
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
1. Autoboxing / unboxing compiles fine.
2. At runtime, the compiler-inserted `g.intValue()` is invoked on a `null` reference.
3. JVM dereferences null → throws `NullPointerException`.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Casting vs Autoboxing Decision Table

| Scenario | Syntax | Compile? | Runtime? | Result |
|----------|--------|----------|----------|--------|
| `int → long` | `long L = i;` | Yes | Yes | Widening, value preserved |
| `long → int` | `int i = (int) L;` | Yes | Yes | Narrowing, may truncate |
| `double → boolean` | `bool b = (bool) d;` | **No** | — | Compile error |
| `Dog → Animal` | `Animal a = dog;` | Yes | Yes | Upcast, safe |
| `Animal → Dog` (object is `Dog`) | `Dog d = (Dog) a;` | Yes | Yes | Downcast, succeeds |
| `Animal → Dog` (object is `Cat`) | `Dog d = (Dog) a;` | Yes | **Crashes** | `ClassCastException` |
| `int → Integer` | `Integer I = 5;` | Yes | Yes | Autoboxing |
| `Integer → int` | `int i = I;` | Yes | Yes | Unboxing |
| `Integer 127 == Integer 127` | `==` | Yes | Yes | **true** (cache) |
| `Integer 128 == Integer 128` | `==` | Yes | Yes | **false** (new objects) |
| `int i = (Integer) null;` | unbox null | Yes | **Crashes** | `NullPointerException` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Primitive Widening & Narrowing Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[byte] --> nodeB[short]
    nodeB --> nodeC[char]
    nodeB --> nodeD[int]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[long]
    nodeE --> nodeF[float]
    nodeF --> nodeG[double]
    nodeH[NARROWING CAST REQUIRED]:::warn
    nodeI[boolean ISOLATED no cast possible]:::warn
    classDef warn fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
```

> Reading guide: An arrow from A to B means **widening** is automatic. Going *against* an arrow (e.g., `double` → `int`) requires an explicit narrowing cast.

---

## 4.2 Reference Casting (Upcasting vs Downcasting)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Object actual type Dog] -->|Upcast implicit| nodeB[Animal reference variable]
    nodeB -->|Downcast explicit instanceof check| nodeC[Dog reference variable]
    nodeB -->|Downcast without check| nodeD[ClassCastException]:::err
    nodeE[Object actual type Cat] -->|Downcast explicit| nodeD
    classDef err fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
```

> Reading guide: Upcasting is a free promotion up the inheritance tree. Downcasting is a forced demotion that the JVM validates at runtime — failure crashes the program.

---

## 4.3 Autoboxing & Unboxing Internal Compiler Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC1[Source code written by programmer]
        nodeA1[int primitive p equals 5] --> nodeA2[Integer object I equals p]
    end
    subgraph CMP[Compiler inserts]
        nodeB1[Integer I equals Integer dot valueOf p] --> nodeB2[Cache lookup range minus 128 to 127]
    end
    subgraph OUT1[If inside cache]
        nodeC1[Return cached object reference]
    end
    subgraph OUT2[If outside cache]
        nodeC2[Return new Integer object]
    end
    SRC1 --> CMP
    nodeB2 -->|hit| OUT1
    nodeB2 -->|miss| OUT2
```

> Reading guide: Every autoboxing operation ultimately reduces to a `valueOf()` call. Every unboxing reduces to a corresponding `xxxValue()` call (e.g., `intValue()`).

---

## 4.4 Wrapper Class Mapping (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRIMS[Primitive Types]
        p1[byte] --- p2[short] --- p3[int] --- p4[long]
        p5[float] --- p6[double] --- p7[char] --- p8[boolean]
    end
    subgraph WRAPS[Wrapper Classes java dot lang]
        w1[Byte] --- w2[Short] --- w3[Integer] --- w4[Long]
        w5[Float] --- w6[Double] --- w7[Character] --- w8[Boolean]
    end
    p1 -.autobox.-> w1
    w1 -.unbox.-> p1
    p2 -.autobox.-> w2
    w2 -.unbox.-> p2
    p3 -.autobox.-> w3
    w3 -.unbox.-> p3
    p4 -.autobox.-> w4
    w4 -.unbox.-> p4
    p5 -.autobox.-> w5
    w5 -.unbox.-> p5
    p6 -.autobox.-> w6
    w6 -.unbox.-> p6
    p7 -.autobox.-> w7
    w7 -.unbox.-> p7
    p8 -.autobox.-> w8
    w8 -.unbox.-> p8
```

> Reading guide: Each primitive has a *one-to-one* wrapper class in `java.lang`. Dashed arrows denote the *automatic* compiler-driven conversions (autoboxing and unboxing).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between widening and narrowing type casting in Java. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Widening casting** (also called *implicit* or *automatic* casting) converts a smaller data type to a larger data type in the numeric promotion hierarchy. It is performed automatically by the compiler because no data is lost.
>
> **Narrowing casting** (also called *explicit* casting) converts a larger data type to a smaller data type. It must be written explicitly using the cast operator `(type)`, and may result in loss of precision or magnitude.
>
> *Examples:*
> - Widening: `int x = 10; double y = x;` → `y` becomes `10.0` automatically.
> - Narrowing: `double d = 9.78; int i = (int) d;` → `i` becomes `9` (fractional part truncated).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is autoboxing in Java? Explain the `Integer` cache concept with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion performed by the Java compiler from a primitive type to its corresponding wrapper class object. It was introduced in **Java 5** to eliminate manual boilerplate code.
>
> The **Integer cache** is an internal optimization where the JVM maintains a pre-allocated array of `Integer` objects for the range `[-128, +127]`. When `Integer.valueOf(n)` is called for any `n` in this range, the *same* cached object reference is returned.
>
> *Example:*
> ```java
> Integer a = 127; Integer b = 127; a == b  // true  (same cached object)
> Integer c = 128; Integer d = 128; c == d  // false (different objects)
> ```
> Therefore, wrapper objects must always be compared using `.equals()`, not `==`.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of reference type casting in Java with suitable examples. Differentiate between upcasting and downcasting. What is `ClassCastException`? When does it occur?**

**Model Answer (Step-by-step):**
