## 4.7 Comparison Block — `break` vs `continue`

```mermaid
graph LR
    bNode1["Encounter break"] --> bNode2["Terminates the loop ENTIRELY"]
    bNode2 --> bNode3["Control jumps to statement AFTER the loop"]
    cNode1["Encounter continue"] --> cNode2["Skips REMAINING statements in current iteration"]
    cNode2 --> cNode3["Control jumps to NEXT iteration's condition check"]
```

## 4.8 Block Architecture — Control Flow in an OOP Method

```mermaid
graph TD
    obj1["Method Entry"] --> obj2["Declaration Statements"]
    obj2 --> obj3{"Selection: if-else"}
    obj3 -->|"Branch A"| obj4["Statements A"]
    obj3 -->|"Branch B"| obj5["Statements B"]
    obj4 --> obj6{"Iteration: for loop"}
    obj5 --> obj6{"Iteration: for loop"}
    obj6 -->|"Each Iteration"| obj7{"Jump: break condition met?"}
    obj7 -->|"No"| obj8["Process Item"]
    obj8 --> obj6
    obj7 -->|"Yes"| obj9["Exit Loop"]
    obj9 --> obj10["return statement"]
    obj10 --> obj11["Method Exit"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This block-level schematic illustrates how all three families of control statements *cooperate* inside a single OOP method — selection decides *which* logic, iteration decides *how many times*, and jump decides *when to stop*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the `while` loop and the `do-while` loop in Java. Give one suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis of Comparison | `while` Loop | `do-while` Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Entry-controlled (pre-test) | Exit-controlled (post-test) |
| **Condition Check** | Before body execution | After body execution |
| **Minimum Executions** | Zero (body may not run at all) | One (body always runs at least once) |
| **Syntax** | `while(cond){ body; }` | `do{ body; }while(cond);` |
| **Semicolon Placement** | Not required after `while(...)` | **Mandatory** after `while(cond);` |

**Example `while`:** Print numbers 1 to 5 only if the starting value is less than 5.
```java
int i = 1;
while (i <= 5) { System.out.println(i); i++; }
```

**Example `do-while`:** A menu that displays at least once before re-checking.
```java
int choice;
do {
    System.out.println("1. Continue  2. Exit");
    choice = scanner.nextInt();
} while (choice != 2);
```

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the `switch` statement. Why is the `break` statement important inside a `switch` block?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `switch` statement in Java is a multi-way branching control statement that dispatches execution to one of several `case` blocks based on the value of a *selector expression*. The selector can be of type `byte`, `short`, `char`, `int`, `String`, or an enumerated type.

**Importance of `break`:**
The `break` statement prevents **fall-through**, which is the unwanted execution of subsequent case blocks after a matching case has been executed. Without `break`, control "falls through" to the next case label and executes its statements as well, often leading to logical errors. The `break` transfers control out of the `switch` block to the next statement following it.

```java
switch (ch) {
    case 'A': grade = "Excellent"; break;
    case 'B': grade = "Good";     break;
    default:  grade = "Invalid";
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> A common 1-mark deduction occurs when students write that `switch` *always* requires `break`. In fact, deliberate omission of `break` to allow fall-through is a *legal* technique in Java used for grouping cases (e.g., `case 1: case 2: case 3: ...`). Examiners mark for *understanding*, not rote answers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice between two questions. Each is typically split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solutions below follow the KTU valuation key style: **concept (2 marks) + diagram/table (2 marks) + code + trace (3 marks)**.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q.A (a)** With suitable syntax and a flowchart, explain the three forms of selection statements in Java. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The three forms of selection statements in Java are:

**1. Simple `if` Statement**
$$\text{If condition is true, execute body; otherwise skip.}$$
```java
if (age >= 18) {
    System.out.println("Eligible to vote");
}
```
*Flowchart logic:* Condition $\rightarrow$ True branch (execute body) $\rightarrow$ False branch (skip) $\rightarrow$ Continue.

**2. `if-else` Statement**
$$\text{Two-way branch: exactly one of two blocks executes.}$$
```java
if (marks >= 50)
    System.out.println("Pass");
else
    System.out.println("Fail");
```

**3. `if-else-if` Ladder (Chained Conditional)**
$$\text{Multi-way branch: top-down evaluation with first-true wins.}$$
```java
if (score >= 90)      grade = 'A';
else if (score >= 75) grade = 'B';
else if (score >= 60) grade = 'C';
else                  grade = 'D';
```

**Flowchart (Composite — for the answer script):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    f1["START"] --> f2{"Single if: Condition true?"}
    f2 -->|"True"| f3["Execute if-body"]
    f2 -->|"False"| f4["Skip body"]
    f3 --> f5["END"]
    f4 --> f5
```

*(Mark Allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 marks three forms syntax + 2 marks examples + 2 marks flowchart/explanation = **7 Marks**)*

---

**Q.A (b)** Write a Java program to find the sum of all even numbers from 1 to 100 using a `for` loop. Explain how the loop control variable is updated. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

```java
public class EvenSum {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int sum = 0;
        for (int i = 2; i <= 100; i = i + 2) {  // i updates by 2 each iteration
            sum = sum + i;
        }
        System.out.println("Sum of even numbers 1-100 = " + sum);
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Loop Control Variable Update (3 Marks):**
- **Initialization:** `int i = 2;` sets the starting value (first even number).
- **Condition:** `i <= 100;` is tested at the start of every iteration. Loop continues as long as `i` is even and $\leq 100$.
- **Update:** `i = i + 2;` increments the loop control variable by 2 each iteration, ensuring only even values of `i` are visited (2, 4, 6, ..., 100).
- **Termination:** When `i` becomes 102, the condition fails and the loop exits.

**Mathematical Verification (using the formula):**
$$\text{Sum} = 2 + 4 + 6 + \dots + 100 = 2(1 + 2 + 3 + \dots + 50)$$
$$\text{Sum} = 2 \times \frac{50 \times 51}{2} = 2550$$

**Output:** `Sum of even numbers 1-100 = 2550`

*(Mark Allocation: 3 marks code + 3 marks update explanation + 1 mark output/verification = **7 Marks**)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` (ALTERNATIVE)

**Q.B (a)** Explain the `break` and `continue` statements in Java with examples. How do they behave inside nested loops? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**`break` Statement (3 Marks):**
- The `break` statement terminates the execution of the *innermost* enclosing `switch` or loop statement and transfers control to the statement immediately following the terminated block.
- It is most commonly used to exit a loop early when a specific condition is met.

```java
for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
    if (i == 6) break;       // exits loop when i becomes 6
    System.out.println(i);   // prints 1 2 3 4 5
}
```

**`continue` Statement (2 Marks):**
- The `continue` statement skips the remainder of the current iteration and proceeds directly to the *next iteration's condition check*.
- It does **not** terminate the loop; only the current iteration is bypassed.

```java
for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
    if (i == 3) continue;    // skips printing 3
    System.out.println(i);   // prints 1 2 4 5
}
```

**Behavior in Nested Loops (2 Marks):**
A plain (unlabeled) `break` or `continue` affects only the **innermost** loop. To target an outer loop, Java's *labeled* form is used:

```java
outer: for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for (int j = 1; j <= 3; j++) {
        if (i == 2 && j == 2) break outer;   // exits BOTH loops
        System.out.println(i + " " + j);
    }
}
```

*(Mark Allocation: 3 + 2 + 2 = **7 Marks**)*

---

**Q.B (b)** Write a Java program using a `do-while` loop and a `switch` statement to build a menu-driven calculator that performs addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division based on user choice. The menu should repeat until the user selects "Exit". *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class MenuCalculator {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        int choice;
        do {
            System.out.println("\n--- MENU ---");
            System.out.println("1. Addition");
            System.out.println("2. Subtraction");
            System.out.println("3. Multiplication");
            System.out.println("4. Division");
            System.out.println("5. Exit");
            System.out.print("Enter choice: ");
            choice = sc.nextInt();

            if (choice >= 1 && choice <= 4) {
                System.out.print("Enter two numbers: ");
                double a = sc.nextDouble();
                double b = sc.nextDouble();

                switch (choice) {
                    case 1: System.out.println("Result = " + (a + b)); break;
                    case 2: System.out.println("Result = " + (a - b)); break;
                    case 3: System.out.println("Result = " + (a * b)); break;
                    case 4: 
                        if (b != 0) System.out.println("Result = " + (a / b));
                        else System.out.println("Cannot divide by zero");
                        break;
                    default: System.out.println("Invalid");
                }
            }
        } while (choice != 5);  // loop continues until user picks 5
        System.out.println("Thank you!");
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Constructs Used (3 Marks):**
- The `do-while` loop guarantees the menu is **displayed at least once**, which is essential for any menu-driven program.
- The `switch` statement performs O(1) dispatch to the correct arithmetic operation based on `choice`, which is cleaner than a long `if-else-if` ladder for fixed menu values.
- The `break` inside each `case` prevents fall-through to the next case.
- The `while (choice != 5)` condition is the loop's *termination condition*, evaluated after each menu iteration.

*(Mark Allocation: 4 marks complete code + 3 marks construct explanation = **7 Marks**)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Critical):**
> 1. **Trace, don't just code** — KTU examiners award marks for *dry-run tables* and output prediction. A correct code without traced output may lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the semicolon** after `do-while` is a syntactic error that costs full marks for the sub-question.
> 3. **Confusing `break` with `continue`** is the most common 2-mark deduction. Remember: `break` exits the loop; `continue` skips only the current iteration.
> 4. **Using `==` for String comparison in `switch` context** — In Java, `String` cases are matched using `.equals()` semantics internally; never write `case "Mon":` as `case == "Mon"`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Exam):**

- ⭐ **Three Families:** Selection, Iteration, Jump — never confuse their roles.
- ⭐ **Pre-test vs Post-test:** `while` and `for` are *entry-controlled*; `do-while` is *exit-controlled* with at least one guaranteed execution.
- ⭐ **`if-else-if` is short-circuit:** Once a `true` condition is found, remaining conditions are **not evaluated**.
- ⭐ **`switch` accepts:** `byte`, `short`, `char`, `int`, `String` (Java 7+), and `enum` — **not** `long`, `float`, `double`, or `boolean`.
- ⭐ **Fall-through:** Omitting `break` in a `switch` case causes execution to continue into the next case.
- ⭐ **`break` is local:** It exits only the *innermost* loop unless a label is used.
- ⭐ **`continue` skips, does not exit:** It moves to the *next iteration*, not out of the loop.
- ⭐ **`return` is dual-purpose:** Exits a `void` method or returns a value from a non-void method.
- ⭐ **No `goto` in Java:** Java intentionally omits `goto` to enforce structured programming. `goto` is a reserved keyword but is unused.
- ⭐ **For-each is read-only on the loop variable:** `for (int x : arr)` makes `x` a local copy; reassigning `x` does not modify `arr`.
- ⭐ **Compound conditionals use short-circuit `&&` and `||`:** Right operand is skipped when the result is already determined.
- ⭐ **Mathematical sum formula** for integer loops: $\sum_{i=a}^{b} i = \frac{(b - a + 1)(a + b)}{2}$ — useful for verifying loop output.
- ⭐ **Common exam traps:** Off-by-one errors, infinite loops due to missing update expression, misplaced semicolons after `while(...)`, and accidental fall-through.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the paradigm of **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** using C++, a **function** is a named, self-contained, modular block of statements designed to perform a specific, well-defined computational task. Functions are the fundamental building blocks of procedural and modular programming, enabling **decomposition**, **abstraction**, and **code reuse**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, a function in C++ is formally defined as:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Definition**
> A function is a self-contained subprogram that takes inputs (parameters), processes them through a sequence of statements, and optionally returns a single value to the calling program. It encapsulates a logical unit of work behind a well-defined interface (signature).

The **general syntax** of a C++ function is:

```cpp
return_type function_name(parameter_list) {
    // body of the function
    // local variable declarations
    // executable statements
    return expression;   // (optional, depending on return_type)
}
```

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Function Prototype** | A declaration that specifies the function's signature (name, return type, parameters) before its actual use. |
| **Function Definition** | The actual implementation containing the body of the function. |
| **Formal Parameters** | Variables listed in the function's definition that receive values from the caller. |
| **Actual Parameters (Arguments)** | The values/expressions passed to the function during a function call. |
| **Return Type** | The data type of the value the function sends back. Use `void` if no value is returned. |
| **Function Signature** | The combination of the function name and its parameter list (used for overloading). |
| **Calling Function** | The function that invokes another function. |
| **Called Function** | The function being invoked. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vending Machine" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners**
> Think of a function as a **vending machine**:
> - **Input slot** $\rightarrow$ **Parameters** (you insert coins and select an item code)
> - **Internal mechanism** $\rightarrow$ **Function body** (hidden processing logic)
> - **Output slot** $\rightarrow$ **Return value** (the snack/can drops out)
> - **Machine label/Model number** $\rightarrow$ **Function name**
>
> Just like a vending machine, a function has a clear **public interface** (what you put in and what you get out) and a **hidden implementation**. You don't need to know *how* the machine works internally — you only need to know its interface. This is the essence of **abstraction**.

### 1.4 Classification of Functions in C++ (KTU Taxonomy)

C++ functions can be broadly classified into the following categories, all examinable under the KTU 2024 Module 1 framework:

1. **Library (Built-in) Functions** — Pre-defined in standard libraries (e.g., `printf`, `sqrt`, `strlen`).
2. **User-defined Functions** — Written by the programmer for specific tasks.
3. **Member Functions** — Defined inside a class to operate on its data.
4. **Standalone (Free) Functions** — Independent functions not bound to any class.
5. **Inline Functions** — Expanded at the call site for performance.
6. **Friend Functions** — Non-member functions granted access to private members.
7. **Virtual Functions** — Used for runtime polymorphism (covered in Module 4/5).
8. **Static Functions** — Retain value between calls or have class-level scope.
9. **Recursive Functions** — Functions that call themselves.
10. **Function Templates** — Generic functions parameterized by type.

For Module 1, our primary focus is on **standalone user-defined functions**, **inline functions**, **function overloading**, and **default arguments**.

### 1.5 The Three Pillars of a Function's Lifecycle

Every C++ function undergoes a strict **three-stage lifecycle**:

1. **Declaration (Prototype)** — Tells the compiler the function's interface.
2. **Definition** — Provides the actual implementation.
3. **Invocation (Call)** — Transfers control to the function at runtime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why all three stages?**
> In multi-file projects, a function may be *defined* in one file but *used* (called) in another. The prototype allows the compiler to verify calls before the definition is even seen. This is the foundation of **separate compilation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a C++ Function — Structured Breakdown

A complete C++ function has the following structured anatomy:

1. **Return Type** — Specifies the data type of the value the function produces.
   - Primitive: `int`, `float`, `double`, `char`, `bool`
   - User-defined: `class_name`, `struct_name`, `enum_name`
   - `void` — denotes no return value.

2. **Function Name** — A valid C++ identifier following naming rules.
   - Must start with a letter or underscore.
   - Case-sensitive, no spaces, no reserved keywords.

3. **Parameter List (Formal Parameter List)** — Comma-separated typed variable declarations enclosed in parentheses.
   - May be empty: `()` or `(void)`.
   - Each parameter must have an explicit type (unlike old C).

4. **Function Body** — A compound statement `{ ... }` containing local declarations and executable statements.

5. **Return Statement** — Transfers a value back to the caller and terminates the function.
   - Mandatory for non-`void` functions.
   - Optional for `void` functions (use bare `return;` to exit early).

### 2.2 Parameter Passing Mechanisms (The 'How' Behind the Magic)

This is the **most heavily tested KTU Module 1 topic** on Functions. There are three principal mechanisms:

#### 2.2.1 Call by Value
- A **copy** of the actual argument is passed to the formal parameter.
- Modifications inside the function **do NOT** affect the original variable.
- Memory: A new local copy is allocated on the **stack frame** of the called function.

#### 2.2.2 Call by Reference (C++ Specific)
- A **reference (alias)** to the original variable is passed.
- Modifications inside the function **DO** affect the original variable.
- Memory: No new copy is created; the formal parameter becomes another name for the same memory location.
- Syntax: Use the `&` (reference) declarator in the parameter list.

#### 2.2.3 Call by Pointer (Address)
- The **memory address** of the actual argument is passed.
- The function works with a pointer; dereferencing (`*`) accesses the original value.
- Syntax: Use `*` in the parameter declaration and `&` at the call site (or pass an existing pointer).

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Inline Functions

A normal function call involves:
1. Pushing arguments onto the **call stack**.
2. Jumping to the function's code.
3. Executing the body.
4. Returning the value.
5. Restoring the stack and returning control to the caller.

For very small, frequently-called functions, this overhead becomes significant. The `inline` keyword is a **request** to the compiler to **substitute the function body directly at the call site**, eliminating the call overhead. The compiler may honor or ignore this request based on complexity.

### 2.4 Default Arguments — The 'How' of Optional Parameters

C++ allows assigning **default values** to function parameters in the prototype. If the caller omits an argument, the default is used. Rules:
- Defaults must be specified **right-to-left** in the parameter list.
- Either the prototype or the definition can specify defaults — **never both**.
- Once a default is given, all subsequent parameters must also have defaults.

### 2.5 Function Overloading — Compile-Time Polymorphism

C++ allows multiple functions to share the **same name** as long as their **parameter lists differ** (number, type, or order of parameters). This is called **function overloading** and is a form of **compile-time (static) polymorphism**.

The compiler distinguishes overloaded functions by their **signature** (name + parameter list). The return type alone is **not** sufficient to disambiguate overloads.

### 2.6 Scope and Lifetime of Variables

| Scope Type | Visibility | Lifetime | Storage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Local (Block)** | Within the block `{ }` where declared | Until the block ends | Stack |
| **Function Parameters** | Within the function body | Until the function returns | Stack |
| **Global (File)** | Throughout the file (and beyond with `extern`) | Until program ends | Data segment |
| **Class Scope** | Within the class (and accessible via objects) | Depends on object's lifetime | Depends |
| **Namespace Scope** | Within the named namespace | Program duration | Data segment |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Function Analysis**
> The following expressions are routinely tested in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and CE examinations.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Recurrence — Linear Recursion** | $T(n) = T(n-1) + \mathcal{O}(1)$ | One recursive call per invocation. |
| **Recurrence — Binary Recursion** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \mathcal{O}(1)$ | Two recursive calls per invocation (e.g., Fibonacci). |
| **Recurrence — Divide & Conquer** | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ | Master Theorem form; $a$ subproblems of size $n/b$. |
| **Recursion Depth** | $D \approx \log_b n$ | For balanced divide-and-conquer recursion. |
| **Recursion Stack Frames** | $M = D \times S_{frame}$ | Total stack memory: depth $\times$ frame size. |
| **Call by Value Memory** | $M_{extra} = \sum \text{sizeof}(\text{arg}_i)$ | Extra stack memory per call. |
| **Call by Reference Memory** | $M_{extra} = \sum \text{sizeof}(\text{pointer}_i)$ | Constant overhead per parameter. |
| **Function Call Overhead** | $T_{call} = T_{push} + T_{jump} + T_{pop}$ | Time to set up, execute, and tear down a call. |
| **Inline Function Benefit** | $T_{saved} = T_{call} - T_{expand}$ | Savings only if $T_{call} > T_{expand}$. |
| **Default Argument Rule** | $k_{default} \leq n_{params}$ | Number of defaulted args $\le$ total parameters. |
| **Overload Resolution** | $\text{Match} = \text{name} + \text{unique signature}$ | Return type excluded. |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Functions are not merely a programming construct — they are the **primary vehicle of software engineering** in all domains:

- **Embedded Systems**: Recursive functions model hierarchical state machines; inline functions reduce latency in interrupt service routines.
- **Signal Processing**: `FFT()`, `convolution()` are reused across millions of samples.
- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)**: Each element stiffness matrix is computed by a dedicated function, called for every element in a mesh.
- **Control Systems**: `pid_controller()` functions enable modular feedback loop design.
- **Computer Graphics**: `transform_vertex()`, `compute_lighting()` — each pixel shaded via function reuse.

In every engineering field, the principle is the same: **functions transform monolithic code into modular, testable, reusable, and maintainable systems**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Section Convention**
> Since Functions is a programming concept under PECMT535 (OOP), our "derivations" take the form of **exhaustive code traces, memory state tables, and call-stack analysis** — the engineering equivalent of algebraic derivations in analytical subjects.

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix 1: Comparing the Three Parameter Passing Mechanisms

Consider the following C++ program that swaps two integers using all three mechanisms:

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

// (1) Call by Value
void swapByValue(int a, int b) {
    int temp = a; a = b; b = temp;
}

// (2) Call by Reference
void swapByReference(int &a, int &b) {
    int temp = a; a = b; b = temp;
}

// (3) Call by Pointer
void swapByPointer(int *a, int *b) {
    int temp = *a; *a = *b; *b = temp;
}

int main() {
    int x = 10, y = 20;
    cout << "Original: x=" << x << ", y=" << y;
    return 0;
}
```

#### Step-by-Step Memory State Trace for `swapByValue(x, y)`:

| Step | Operation | Address of `a` (stack) | Address of `b` (stack) | Value of `a` | Value of `b` | Value of `x` (main) | Value of `y` (main) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | Before call | — | — | — | — | **10** | **20** |
| 1 | `a = x` (copy 10) | `0x7FF0` | — | 10 | — | 10 | 20 |
| 2 | `b = y` (copy 20) | `0x7FF0` | `0x7FF4` | 10 | 20 | 10 | 20 |
| 3 | `temp = a` | `0x7FF0` | `0x7FF4` | 10 | 20 | 10 | 20 |
| 4 | `a = b` | `0x7FF0` | `0x7FF4` | 20 | 20 | 10 | 20 |
| 5 | `b = temp` | `0x7FF0` | `0x7FF4` | 20 | 10 | 10 | 20 |
| 6 | Function returns | — | — | — | — | **10** | **20** |

> **Inference**: The original `x` and `y` in `main` remain unchanged because `a` and `b` are **independent copies** on the stack.

#### Step-by-Step Memory State Trace for `swapByReference(x, y)`:

| Step | Operation | Binding of `a` | Binding of `b` | Value at binding | `x` | `y` |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | Before call | — | — | — | 10 | 20 |
| 1 | `int &a = x` | Address of `x` | — | 10 | 10 | 20 |
| 2 | `int &b = y` | Address of `x` | Address of `y` | 20 | 10 | 20 |
| 3 | `temp = a` | Address of `x` | Address of `y` | 10 | 10 | 20 |
| 4 | `a = b` | Address of `x` | Address of `y` | 20 | **20** | 20 |
| 5 | `b = temp` | Address of `x` | Address of `y` | 10 | 20 | **10** |
| 6 | Function returns | — | — | — | **20** | **10** |

> **Inference**: The original `x` and `y` are **swapped** because `a` and `b` are **aliases** to the same memory locations.

#### Call-by-Pointer Trace (for completeness):

| Step | Operation | Pointer `a` holds | Pointer `b` holds | `*a` | `*b` | `x` | `y` |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | Before call | — | — | — | — | 10 | 20 |
| 1 | `a = &x` | `&x` | — | 10 | — | 10 | 20 |
| 2 | `b = &y` | `&x` | `&y` | 10 | 20 | 10 | 20 |
| 3 | `temp = *a` | `&x` | `&y` | 10 | 20 | 10 | 20 |
| 4 | `*a = *b` | `&x` | `&y` | 20 | 20 | **20** | 20 |
| 5 | `*b = temp` | `&x` | `&y` | 20 | 10 | 20 | **10** |
| 6 | Function returns | — | — | — | — | **20** | **10** |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix 2: Recursive Function Call-Stack Evolution

Consider the classic factorial function:

```cpp
int factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;     // base case
    return n * factorial(n-1); // recursive call
}
```

**Initial Call**: `factorial(4)`

#### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Trace:

| Call # | Invocation | Argument `n` | `n <= 1`? | Action | Return Value |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | `factorial(4)` | 4 | No | Calls `factorial(3)` | Waits |
| 2 | `factorial(3)` | 3 | No | Calls `factorial(2)` | Waits |
| 3 | `factorial(2)` | 2 | No | Calls `factorial(1)` | Waits |
| 4 | `factorial(1)` | 1 | **Yes** | Base case hit | Returns 1 |
| 5 | Back to call #3 | 2 | — | Computes $2 \times 1$ | Returns 2 |
| 6 | Back to call #2 | 3 | — | Computes $3 \times 2$ | Returns 6 |
| 7 | Back to call #1 | 4 | — | Computes $4 \times 6$ | Returns 24 |

**Result**: $\text{factorial}(4) = 24$

The mathematical recurrence is:

$$
T(n) = \begin{cases}
\mathcal{O}(1) & \text{if } n \leq 1 \\
T(n-1) + \mathcal{O}(1) & \text{if } n > 1
\end{cases}
$$

Unrolling the recurrence:

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + 1 = T(n-2) + 2 = \cdots = T(1) + (n-1)
$$

$$
T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n)
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix 3: Function Overload Resolution

Consider the following overloaded functions:

```cpp
void display(int x)        { cout << "Integer: " << x; }
void display(double y)      { cout << "Double: "  << y; }
void display(int a, int b)  { cout << "Two ints: " << a << "," << b; }
```

**Call Resolution Table**:

| Call Expression | Selected Function | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `display(5)` | `display(int)` | Exact match — integer literal is `int`. |
| `display(3.14)` | `display(double)` | Exact match — floating literal is `double`. |
| `display(5, 10)` | `display(int, int)` | Exact match — two integer arguments. |
| `display(5.0f)` | `display(double)` | No `float` overload; `float` promotes to `double`. |
| `display('A')` | `display(int)` | `char` promotes to `int` (standard conversion). |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip**
> The compiler's overload resolution follows a strict priority: **Exact Match $\rightarrow$ Promotion $\rightarrow$ Standard Conversion $\rightarrow$ User-Defined Conversion**. Ambiguity errors arise if two overloads tie at the same conversion level.

### 3.4 Default Argument Evaluation Trace

```cpp
int power(int base, int exp = 2) {     // exp defaults to 2
    int result = 1;
    for (int i = 0; i < exp; i++) result *= base;
    return result;
}
```

| Call | `base` | `exp` (resolved) | Computation | Result |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| `power(5)` | 5 | 2 (default) | $5^2$ | **25** |
| `power(5, 3)` | 5 | 3 (provided) | $5^3$ | **125** |
| `power(2, 0)` | 2 | 0 (provided) | $2^0$ | **1** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Function Lifecycle Flow

This diagram illustrates the three-stage lifecycle of a C++ function from declaration to execution:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Program Start]) --> declA[Function Prototype Declared]
    declA --> defA[Function Definition Written]
    defA --> compA{Compiler Phase}
    compA --> symTabA[Symbol Table Updated]
    symTabA --> waitA[Waiting for Call]
    waitA --> callA[Function Invoked by Caller]
    callA --> pushA[Push Arguments onto Stack]
    pushA --> jumpA[Jump to Function Entry Point]
    jumpA --> execA[Execute Function Body]
    execA --> retA{Return Statement?}
    retA -->|Yes| popA[Pop Return Value]
    retA -->|void| popA2[Pop Stack Frame]
    popA --> retCtlA[Return Control to Caller]
    popA2 --> retCtlA
    retCtlA([Function Call Complete])
```

### 4.2 Call Stack Evolution During a Recursive Call

The following diagram visualizes the stack growth during `factorial(3)`:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stackTopA([Stack Pointer SP]) --> frameMainA[main Frame: contains local vars]
    frameMainA --> frameF1A[factorial 3: n=3, retAddr=main]
    frameF1A --> frameF2A[factorial 2: n=2, retAddr=f3]
    frameF2A --> frameF3A[factorial 1: n=1, retAddr=f2 - BASE CASE]
    frameF3A --> baseRetA[Returns 1]
    baseRetA --> unwindA[Unwind: 1 to 2: 2x1=2]
    unwindA --> unwindB[Unwind: 2 to 3: 3x2=6]
    unwindB --> unwindC[Return 6 to main]
    unwindC([Final Result: 6])
```

### 4.3 Parameter Passing Mechanism Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph mainA[MAIN FUNCTION MEMORY]
        xA[Variable x: 10]
        yA[Variable y: 20]
    end

    subgraph valA[CALL BY VALUE - Separate Copies]
        aA[Local a: 10]
        bA[Local b: 20]
    end

    subgraph refA[CALL BY REFERENCE - Aliases]
        aR[Reference a binds to x]
        bR[Reference b binds to y]
    end

    subgraph ptrA[CALL BY POINTER - Addresses]
        aP[Pointer a holds addr of x]
        bP[Pointer b holds addr of y]
    end

    xA -.copy.-> aA
    yA -.copy.-> bA
    xA -.alias.-> aR
    yA -.alias.-> bR
    xA -.addr.-> aP
    yA -.addr.-> bP
```

### 4.4 Function Overloading Resolution Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    callA[Function Call Encountered] --> m1A{Exact Match Exists?}
    m1A -->|Yes| selA[Select Exact Match]
    m1A -->|No| m2A{Promotion Match?}
    m2A -->|Yes| selB[Select Promoted Match]
    m2A -->|No| m3A{Standard Conversion Match?}
    m3A -->|Yes| selC[Select Converted Match]
    m3A -->|No| m4A{User Defined Conversion?}
    m4A -->|Yes| selD[Select UDC Match]
    m4A -->|No| errA[Compile Error: Ambiguous or No Match]
    selA --> doneA[Bind and Compile]
    selB --> doneA
    selC --> doneA
    selD --> doneA
```

### 4.5 Inline Function Expansion — Before vs After

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph beforeA[BEFORE INLINE - Normal Call]
        bA1[Caller: invokes func] --> bA2[Push args to stack]
        bA2 --> bA3[Jump to func body]
        bA3 --> bA4[Execute body]
        bA4 --> bA5[Return value]
        bA5 --> bA6[Pop stack, return control]
    end

    subgraph afterA[AFTER INLINE - Body Substituted]
        aA1[Caller: function body inserted directly] --> aA2[Execute substituted code in place]
        aA2 --> aA3[Continue to next statement]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note**
> The "Before Inline" path shows a 6-step sequence involving stack manipulation. The "After Inline" path collapses this to a 3-step in-place execution, which is why inline functions are faster for small, frequently-called routines — at the cost of increased code size (code bloat).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. What is function overloading in C++? Give a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Function overloading is a feature in C++ that allows multiple functions to share the **same name** within the same scope, provided they differ in their **parameter list** (number, type, or order of parameters). It is a form of **compile-time polymorphism** because the compiler resolves the correct function at compile time based on the arguments passed.

**Example:**
```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int add(int a, int b)         { return a + b; }
double add(double a, double b){ return a + b; }
int add(int a, int b, int c)  { return a + b + c; }

int main() {
    cout << add(2, 3);         // calls add(int, int)  -> 5
    cout << add(2.5, 3.5);     // calls add(double, double) -> 6.0
    cout << add(1, 2, 3);      // calls add(int, int, int) -> 6
    return 0;
}
```
The compiler distinguishes between the three `add` functions using their **signatures**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between call by value and call by reference in C++.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Call by Value | Call by Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **What is passed?** | A copy of the actual argument | A reference (alias) to the original variable |
| **Syntax in definition** | `void f(int x)` | `void f(int &x)` |
| **Effect on original** | Original variable is **unchanged** | Original variable is **modified** |
| **Memory** | New copy allocated on stack | No new copy; alias to original memory |
| **Safety** | Safer (original data protected) | Risky (caller's data may be altered) |
| **Use case** | When function should not modify the argument | When function must return multiple values or modify large data efficiently |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1, ESE Pattern, 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the different **parameter passing mechanisms** in C++ (call by value, call by reference, call by pointer) with suitable examples. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are **inline functions** in C++? Explain their syntax, working, advantages, and limitations with examples. When should a programmer prefer them over regular functions? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Parameter Passing Mechanisms (7 marks):**

In C++, parameters can be passed to functions using three mechanisms:

**1. Call by Value:**
The actual argument's value is copied into the formal parameter. Any modification inside the function affects only the local copy.

```cpp
void increment(int n) { n++; }

int main() {
    int x = 5;
    increment(x);
    cout << x;   // Output: 5 (unchanged)
}
```
**Advantages:** Data safety (original protected), simple semantics.
**Disadvantages:** Inefficient for large objects (copying overhead), cannot return multiple values.

**2. Call by Reference:**
The formal parameter becomes an **alias** (another name) for the actual argument. Modifications inside the function affect the original.

```cpp
void increment(int &n) { n++; }

int main() {
    int x = 5;
    increment(x);
    cout << x;   // Output: 6 (changed)
}
```
**Advantages:** No copying overhead, allows modification of caller's variables, efficient for large objects.
**Disadvantages:** Risk of accidental modification, slightly less safe than pass-by-value.

**3. Call by Pointer (Address):**
The address of the actual argument is passed. The function uses pointer dereferencing to access and modify the original variable.

```cpp
void increment(int *n) { (*n)++; }

int main() {
    int x = 5;
    increment(&x);
    cout << x;   // Output: 6 (changed)
}
```
**Advantages:** Explicit, works in C as well, allows dynamic memory operations.
**Disadvantages:** Verbose syntax, risk of null pointer dereference, less safe than references.

**Comparative Summary (for 7-mark completeness):**

| Aspect | By Value | By Reference | By Pointer |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Syntax at call site | `f(x)` | `f(x)` | `f(&x)` |
| Syntax at definition | `f(T x)` | `f(T &x)` | `f(T *x)` |
| Memory overhead | $\mathcal{O}(\text{sizeof}(T))$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\text{sizeof}(ptr)})$ |
| Can modify caller? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Null risk? | No | No | Yes |
| Multiple returns? | No | Yes | Yes |

**(b) Inline Functions (7 marks):**

An **inline function** is a function for which the compiler is requested to substitute the function body directly at the call site, eliminating the overhead of a normal function call.

**Syntax:**
```cpp
inline return_type function_name(parameters) {
    // body
}
```

**Example:**
```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

inline int square(int x) { return x * x; }

int main() {
    cout << square(5);  // Compiler may replace with: cout << (5*5);
    return 0;
}
```

**How it works internally:**
For a normal call, the CPU must: (i) push arguments onto the stack, (ii) jump to the function's code, (iii) execute the body, (iv) pop the return value, and (v) jump back. For an inline function, the compiler **copies the function body** directly into the calling code, eliminating steps (i), (ii), (iv), and (v).

**Advantages:**
- Eliminates function call overhead.
- Faster execution for small, frequently-called functions.
- Improves code locality (better cache utilization).

**Limitations:**
- `inline` is a **request**, not a command. The compiler may ignore it for large or recursive functions.
- Causes **code bloat** if the function is large and called many times.
- Not suitable for functions with loops, complex logic, or recursion.

**When to prefer inline:**
- Functions with 1–3 simple statements.
- Frequently called "getter" functions.
- Mathematical helper functions (e.g., `square`, `cube`).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain **function overloading** and **default arguments** in C++ with examples. Discuss the rules for default arguments and the ambiguity that may arise during overload resolution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **recursion** in C++ with a suitable example. Write a recursive function to compute the Fibonacci series. Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and the role of the call stack. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Function Overloading and Default Arguments (7 marks):**

**Function Overloading:**
C++ allows multiple functions with the same name but different parameter lists (number, type, or order). The compiler chooses the right one based on the arguments.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int area(int side)              { return side * side; }            // square
int area(int length, int width) { return length * width; }         // rectangle
double area(double radius)      { return 3.14159 * radius * radius; } // circle
```

**Default Arguments:**
Default values are provided in the function prototype. If the caller omits the argument, the default is used.

```cpp
int greet(string name, string greeting = "Hello") {
    cout << greeting << ", " << name;
}
```

**Rules for Default Arguments:**
1. Defaults must be specified **right-to-left** in the parameter list.
2. Specified in either the **prototype** or the **definition** — not both.
3. Once a default is provided, all subsequent parameters must also have defaults.

**Ambiguity Example:**
```cpp
void show(int a, double b = 2.0) {}
void show(double a, int b)      {}

show(5);  // AMBIGUOUS: 5 can convert to int (first) or double (second)
```

**Resolution Hierarchy (as shown in Section 4.4):** Exact $\rightarrow$ Promotion $\rightarrow$ Standard Conversion $\rightarrow$ User-Defined.

**(b) Recursion (7 marks):**

**Definition:** A function that calls itself — either directly or indirectly — is called a **recursive function**. Each recursive call must progress toward a **base case** (termination condition).

**Components of Recursion:**
1. **Base Case** — The terminating condition that stops the recursion.
2. **Recursive Case** — The part where the function calls itself with a smaller/simpler input.

**Example: Fibonacci Series:**
```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int fibonacci(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;              // base case
    return fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2);  // recursive case
}

int main() {
    for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++)
        cout << fibonacci(i) << " ";
    // Output: 0 1 1 2 3 5 8 13
    return 0;
}
```

**Call Stack for `fibonacci(4)`:**

| Call | Returns To | Computation | Value |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| `fib(4)` | main | `fib(3) + fib(2)` | waits |
| $\rightarrow$ `fib(3)` | fib(4) | `fib(2) + fib(1)` | waits |
| $\rightarrow$ $\rightarrow$ `fib(2)` | fib(3) | `fib(1) + fib(0)` | `1+0=1` |
| $\rightarrow$ $\rightarrow$ `fib(1)` | fib(3) | base case | `1` |
| $\rightarrow$ $\rightarrow$ `fib(2)` | fib(4) | `fib(1) + fib(0)` | `1+0=1` |
| $\rightarrow$ $\rightarrow$ `fib(1)` | fib(4) | base case | `1` |
| $\leftarrow$ `fib(4)` returns | main | `2 + 1` | **3** |

**Recurrence Relation:**

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + \mathcal{O}(1), \quad T(0) = T(1) = 1
$$

**Advantages:**
- Elegant solution for inherently recursive problems (trees, graphs, divide-and-conquer).
- Reduces code size significantly.

**Disadvantages:**
- High memory consumption due to stack frames.
- Risk of **stack overflow** for deep recursion.
- Slower than iterative equivalents due to call overhead.

**Role of the Call Stack:**
Each recursive call pushes a new **stack frame** containing the local variables, parameters, and return address. The stack unwinds as base cases return values back up the chain.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Function Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing Function Prototype with Definition** — A prototype is just a declaration ending with a semicolon; the definition has a body. Mixing them up costs **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting the `&` in Call-by-Reference** — Writing `void f(int x)` instead of `void f(int &x)` silently changes the semantics to call-by-value, losing **2–3 marks** in parameter passing questions.
> 3. **Specifying Default Arguments on Both Sides** — Defining defaults in both the prototype and the definition causes a **compile error** that students often blame on the compiler.
> 4. **Using Only Return Type for Overloading** — `int f()` and `double f()` are **NOT** valid overloads; the compiler will throw an ambiguity/redefinition error.
> 5. **Missing Base Case in Recursion** — A recursive function without a base case results in **infinite recursion** and stack overflow. Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for incomplete base case logic.
> 6. **Writing `inline` on Recursive Functions** — The compiler ignores this silently; many students incorrectly believe it prevents stack overflow.
> 7. **Confusing Scope with Lifetime** — "Global variables have global scope" is correct, but students often write "global variables have global lifetime" — both are true, but the distinction is tested.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Functions (Module 1)**

- A **function** is a named, self-contained block of statements with a return type, name, parameter list, and body.
- The **three lifecycle stages** are: **declaration (prototype)**, **definition**, and **call (invocation)**.
- **Call by value** copies the argument; the original is **safe** but modifications are **lost**.
- **Call by reference** (`&`) creates an **alias**; the original **can be modified**.
- **Call by pointer** (`*`) passes the **address**; requires explicit dereferencing.
- **Default arguments** must appear **right-to-left** in the parameter list; specified in **only one** place.
- **Function overloading** requires different **signatures** (parameter lists); **return type alone is insufficient**.
- **Inline functions** are a **compiler request**, not a command; suitable only for **small, simple** functions.
- **Recursion** needs a **base case** to terminate; uses the **call stack** for state management.
- **Local variables** live in the **stack**; **global variables** live in the **data segment**.
- **Storage classes** in C++: `auto`, `register`, `static`, `extern`, `mutable`.
- Function overloading resolution priority: **Exact $\rightarrow$ Promotion $\rightarrow$ Standard Conversion $\rightarrow$ UDC**.
- Recurrence relations for common recursions: linear $\mathcal{O}(n)$, binary $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$, divide-and-conquer $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$.
- **Stack overflow** is the primary risk in deep or unbounded recursion.
- Always provide a **function prototype** when the definition appears **after** the calling code.
- The `inline` keyword should **never** be used on recursive or large functions.
- For KTU 2024 ESE, questions on parameter passing and function overloading carry **the highest weightage** in Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Command Line Arguments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Command Line Arguments # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Command Line Arguments** (also termed as *Command-Line Parameters* or *Program Parameters*) are the **string values supplied to a program at the time of its invocation from the operating system's command-line interface (CLI) or terminal**, which are then passed into the program's `main()` method/function for runtime processing. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OOP Concepts)** syllabus, this is a foundational concept in **Module 1** because it illustrates the first practical use of **arrays and strings as parameters** to a function — a stepping stone toward understanding **pass-by-reference, vectors, and dynamic data handling** in Object-Oriented Programming. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECMT535 - Module 1):** > The module introduces the procedural foundation that precedes OOP. Command line arguments are explicitly included to give students hands-on experience with **arrays of strings, string-to-numeric conversion, and conditional parameter validation** — all of which are direct prerequisites for OOP concepts like **constructors with parameters, exception handling, and overloaded functions**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you walk into a **café (the program)** and the barista asks: *"What would you like to order?"* - The **menu items you speak out** (e.g., *"Latte, Large, With sugar"*) are the **command-line arguments**. - The **barista (the program)** receives them in a **specific order** (positional) and processes them. - The **list of items** you speak is the **argument vector (`argv`)**. - The **number of items** you mentioned is the **argument count (`argc`)**. Just like a barista expects arguments in a fixed order (drink, size, sugar), a program expects command-line arguments in a **predefined sequence** that the programmer has coded for. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — Filling a Form:** > Consider a passport application form with fields: `Name`, `Age`, `Nationality`, `Destination`. You fill them in order. Similarly, a program receiving command-line arguments reads them in order: `argv[1]`, `argv[2]`, `argv[3]`, and so on. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Notations - **`argc`** — **Argument Count**: An integer representing the **total number of command-line arguments passed, including the program name itself**. Always $\geq 1$ in C/C++. - **`argv`** — **Argument Vector**: An array of **character pointers (C-strings)** in C/C++ or a **String array** in Java, holding the actual argument values. - **Indexing convention**: `argv[0]` (or `args[0]` in Java) — first argument. | Language | Argument Count Variable | Argument Vector Variable | Type of `argv[]` Elements | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **C / C++** | `argc` (int) | `argv` (char* []) | C-Strings (`char*`) | | **Java** | `args.length` | `args` (String[]) | Java Strings (`String`) | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction Between C++ and Java:** > In **C/C++**, `argv[0]` is conventionally the **name of the executable program itself**. > In **Java**, `args[0]` is the **first user-supplied argument** — the program name is **not** included in the array. This is a frequently tested nuance in KTU exams. --- ## 1.4 Why Are Command Line Arguments Used? Command line arguments are preferred over hard-coded values or interactive `cin`/`Scanner` inputs in scenarios where: 1. **Automation & Scripting** — Programs run inside shell scripts, batch files, or CI/CD pipelines. 2. **Batch Processing** — Same program processes many files (e.g., `convert image1.jpg image2.jpg ...`). 3. **Configuration without Recompilation** — Different runtime behaviors can be triggered without modifying the source code. 4. **Testing & Debugging** — Quick test cases can be supplied without altering the program logic. --- ## 1.5 Module 1 Prerequisite Mapping | Module 1 Sub-Topic | Connection to Command Line Arguments | | :--- | :--- | | Functions & Parameter Passing | `main()` is a function that receives parameters from the OS | | Arrays (1-D) | `argv[]` is itself a 1-D array of strings | | Strings / C-Strings | All CLI arguments are received as strings | | Type Conversion | String-to-int / String-to-float conversion is required | | Conditional Logic | Argument validation using `if-else` | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How Command Line Arguments Reach the Program The journey of a command-line argument can be broken into **5 logical stages**: ### Stage 1 — User Invocation The user types the program name followed by arguments in the terminal: ```bash ./myprog 25 3.14 Hello ``` ### Stage 2 — Shell Parsing (Tokenization) The operating system shell (Bash, CMD, PowerShell) **parses the input line** by splitting on **whitespace characters** (space, tab). Quoted strings are treated as a single token: ```bash ./myprog "Hello World" 25 # Splits into 3 tokens, NOT 4 ``` ### Stage 3 — Argument Storage by OS The OS (specifically the **process loader / kernel**) allocates memory and: - Sets `argc` = number of tokens (including program name in C/C++). - Creates the `argv[]` array where each element is a pointer to a null-terminated string in memory. ### Stage 4 — Stack Frame Setup for `main()` When the OS calls `main(int argc, char* argv[])`, the values of `argc` and `argv` are pushed onto the program's call stack. ### Stage 5 — Program Processing The C++/Java code reads `argv[i]` (or `args[i]`) values and processes them using: - **Direct string operations** (printing, comparing, concatenating). - **Type conversion** (if numeric computation is needed). --- ## 2.2 Formal Syntax in C++ ```cpp #include using namespace std; int main(int argc, char* argv[]) { cout << "Number of arguments: " << argc << endl; for (int i = 0; i < argc; i++) { cout << "Argument " << i << ": " << argv[i] << endl; } return 0; } ``` **Alternative valid signatures accepted by the C++ standard:** ```cpp int main(int argc, char** argv) // Equivalent - char** is pointer to char* int main(int argc, char* argv[]) // Most common textbook form ``` > [!NOTE] > Both forms are semantically identical. `char* argv[]` **decays** to `char** argv` when passed to `main()`. --- ## 2.3 Formal Syntax in Java ```java public class MyProgram { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Number of arguments: " + args.length); for (int i = 0; i < args.length; i++) { System.out.println("Argument " + i + ": " + args[i]); } } } ``` In Java, the parameter can be named anything — `args`, `argv`, `commandLineArguments` — but by Java convention, **`args`** is used. The `String[]` is part of the JVM's method signature for `main`. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | C/C++ Formula / Syntax | Java Equivalent | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Argument Count** | `argc` (always $\geq 1$) | `args.length` (can be **0**) | Java does not count program name | | **Argument Vector** | `char* argv[]` | `String[] args` | Type difference | | **First User Argument** | `argv[1]` | `args[0]` | **Off-by-one difference** | | **Program Name Access** | `argv[0]` | Not available in `args[]` | Java limitation | | **String-to-Int** | `atoi(argv[i])` or `stoi(argv[i])` | `Integer.parseInt(args[i])` | `stoi` is C++11; `atoi` is C legacy | | **String-to-Float** | `atof(argv[i])` or `stof(argv[i])` | `Float.parseFloat(args[i])` | Watch precision loss | | **String-to-Double** | `atof(argv[i])` or `stod(argv[i])` | `Double.parseDouble(args[i])` | Default for high precision | | **String Compare** | `strcmp(argv[1], "yes") == 0` | `args[0].equals("yes")` | Case-sensitive in both | | **Total Tokens Expected** | $\text{argc} - 1$ | $\text{args.length}$ | Subtract 1 only in C/C++ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Formula for `argv` storage:** > $$\text{Total Memory} \approx \sum_{i=0}^{argc-1} \left( \text{strlen}(argv[i]) + 1 \right) \text{ bytes}$$ > The `+1` accounts for the **null terminator `'\0'`** in each C-string. This is a frequently asked theoretical question in KTU exams. --- ## 2.5 Engineering Utility Command line arguments are the backbone of: - **Compilers**: `g++ -O2 -Wall source.cpp -o output` (flags are CLI arguments) - **Package Managers**: `npm install express --save` - **Container Tools**: `docker run -d -p 8080:80 nginx` - **Data Engineering**: `spark-submit --master local[*] app.jar input.csv output.parquet` - **System Utilities**: `ping -c 5 google.com` In all these real-world tools, the **order, count, and meaning** of arguments are strictly defined by the program's `main()` function. --- ## 2.6 Common Type Conversion Functions (C++ vs Java) | Conversion Goal | C++ Function | Java Method | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | String → int | `atoi(s)`, `stoi(s)` | `Integer.parseInt(s)` | | String → long | `atol(s)`, `stol(s)` | `Long.parseLong(s)` | | String → float | `atof(s)`, `stof(s)` | `Float.parseFloat(s)` | | String → double | `atof(s)`, `stod(s)` | `Double.parseDouble(s)` | | String → bool | Manual compare | `Boolean.parseBoolean(s)` | | int → String | `to_string(n)` | `String.valueOf(n)` | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example #1 — Sum of Two Numbers (C++) **Problem Statement:** Write a C++ program that accepts **two integers as command-line arguments** and prints their sum. If the count is incorrect, display an appropriate error message. ### Step 1: Source Code Construction ```cpp #include #include // Required for atoi() using namespace std; int main(int argc, char* argv[]) { // Step A: Validate argument count // In C/C++, argc includes program name, so we need 3 total: // argv[0] -> program name // argv[1] -> first number // argv[2] -> second number if (argc != 3) { cout << "Usage: " << argv[0] << " " << endl; return 1; // Non-zero exit indicates error } // Step B: Convert strings to integers int num1 = atoi(argv[1]); int num2 = atoi(argv[2]); // Step C: Perform computation int sum = num1 + num2; // Step D: Display result cout << "Sum of " << num1 << " and " << num2 << " is: " << sum << endl; return 0; // Successful termination } ``` ### Step 2: Compilation and Execution Trace ```bash $ g++ -o sum_prog sum_prog.cpp $ ./sum_prog 25 17 ``` ### Step 3: Execution Flow (Step-by-Step Trace) | Line of Execution | `argc` | `argv[0]` | `argv[1]` | `argv[2]` | Output | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Program start | `3` | `"./sum_prog"` | `"25"` | `"17"` | — | | `if (argc != 3)` | `3` | unchanged | unchanged | unchanged | Condition **false** → skip error | | `atoi(argv[1])` | `3` | unchanged | `"25"` | `"17"` | `num1 = 25` | | `atoi(argv[2])` | `3` | unchanged | `"25"` | `"17"` | `num2 = 17` | | `sum = num1 + num2` | `3` | unchanged | `"25"` | `"17"` | `sum = 42` | | `cout << ...` | `3` | unchanged | `"25"` | `"17"` | `Sum of 25 and 17 is: 42` | ### Step 4: Error Case Trace ```bash $ ./sum_prog 25 ``` | Step | `argc` | Action | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Program start | `2` | Only program name + 1 argument | | `if (argc != 3)` | `2` | Condition **true** | | `cout << "Usage:..."` | `2` | Prints `Usage: ./sum_prog ` | | `return 1;` | `2` | Program exits with error code 1 | --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example #2 — String Operations (Java) **Problem Statement:** Write a Java program that accepts a **name and age** as command-line arguments and prints a greeting. ### Step 1: Source Code Construction ```java public class Greeting { public static void main(String[] args) { // Validate argument count // In Java, args does NOT include program name if (args.length != 2) { System.out.println("Usage: java Greeting "); System.exit(1); } // Extract arguments String name = args[0]; // Convert age from String to int int age = Integer.parseInt(args[1]); // Print formatted greeting System.out.println("Hello " + name + ", you are " + age + " years old."); // Demonstrate conditional logic based on argument if (age >= 18) { System.out.println("You are an adult."); } else { System.out.println("You are a minor."); } } } ``` ### Step 2: Execution Trace ```bash $ javac Greeting.java $ java Greeting Alice 21 ``` | Step | `args.length` | `args[0]` | `args[1]` | Output | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Start | `2` | `"Alice"` | `"21"` | — | | Validation | `2` | — | — | Skip error | | `Integer.parseInt("21")` | `2` | `"Alice"` | `"21"` | `age = 21` | | `System.out.println(...)` | `2` | `"Alice"` | `"21"` | `Hello Alice, you are 21 years old.` | | `if (age >= 18)` | `2` | `"Alice"` | `"21"` | Condition **true** → `You are an adult.` | ### Step 3: Error Case (Invalid Number) ```bash $ java Greeting Bob twenty ``` | Step | Action | | :--- | :--- | | `Integer.parseInt("twenty")` | Throws `NumberFormatException` (unhandled) | | Program crashes with stack trace | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exception Handling Insight (Pre-OOP Bridge):** > In Module 1, the program would simply crash. In later modules, students will learn to wrap such calls in `try-catch` blocks — directly linking Module 1's string conversion to Module 3/4's **Exception Handling** topic. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (C++ vs Java) | Feature | C++ | Java | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Syntax** | `int main(int argc, char* argv[])` | `public static void main(String[] args)` | | **Includes program name in vector** | Yes (`argv[0]`) | No | | **Argument count when no args passed** | `argc = 1` | `args.length = 0` | | **Default behavior on no args** | Program runs with `argv[0]` only | Program runs with empty `args[]` | | **Type of arguments** | `char*` (C-strings) | `String` (objects) | | **Conversion to int** | `atoi()`, `stoi()` | `Integer.parseInt()` | | **Memory management** | OS-managed, no GC | JVM-managed with GC | | **Compilation command** | `g++ file.cpp -o output` | `javac File.java` | | **Execution command** | `./output arg1 arg2` | `java File arg1 arg2` | | **Return value of main** | `int` (exit code: 0=success) | `void` (use `System.exit(code)`) | --- ## 3.4 Boundary Conditions & Edge Cases | Edge Case | Behavior in C++ | Behavior in Java | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **No arguments provided** | `argc = 1`, `argv[0]` is program name | `args.length = 0` | | **Too many arguments** | All stored; access only what is needed | All stored; access only what is needed | | **Non-numeric input to `atoi`/`parseInt`** | `atoi` returns 0 silently; `stoi` throws `std::invalid_argument` | Throws `NumberFormatException` | | **Quoted string with spaces** | Shell treats as single token | Shell treats as single token | | **Empty string argument** | `argv[i] = ""` (length 0) | `args[i] = ""` (empty String) | | **Special characters (e.g., `*`, `&`)** | Shell may interpret as wildcards/operators | Shell may interpret similarly | --- ## 3.5 Step-by-Step Mathematical Notation for Argument Count Let $n$ be the **number of user-supplied arguments**. For C/C++: $$\text{argc} = n + 1$$ where the extra `+1` accounts for `argv[0]` (program name). For Java: $$\text{args.length} = n$$ **Inverse formula (C/C++):** To find the index of the last user argument: $$k_{\text{last}} = \text{argc} - 1$$ **Derivation:** $$\text{argv} = \{ \underbrace{\text{argv}[0]}_{\text{program name}}, \underbrace{\text{argv}[1], \ldots, \text{argv}[n]}_{n \text{ user args}} \}$$ Total elements in `argv`: $$\text{Total} = 1 + n = \text{argc}$$ Therefore, the index of the last element: $$k_{\text{last}} = \text{Total} - 1 = \text{argc} - 1$$ For Java, since program name is excluded: $$k_{\text{last}} = \text{args.length} - 1$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Flow: From Terminal to Program This Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete lifecycle** of command-line arguments from the moment a user presses `Enter` to the moment the program uses them. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["USER TYPES COMMAND IN TERMINAL
e.g., ./sumprog 10 20 30"]:::user B["OPERATING SYSTEM SHELL
Bash, CMD, PowerShell"]:::system C["SHELL PARSER
Tokenizes input by whitespace"]:::parser D["PROCESS LOADER
Allocates memory for argv array"]:::loader E["STACK FRAME SETUP
argc and argv pushed to call stack"]:::stack F["MAIN FUNCTION EXECUTION
int main(int argc, char argv)"]:::program G["ARGUMENT VALIDATION
Check if argc matches expected count"]:::validation H["STRING TO NUMERIC CONVERSION
atoi, stoi, parseInt, etc."]:::convert I["PROGRAM LOGIC
Computation, printing, storage"]:::logic J["OUTPUT TO CONSOLE
Result displayed to user"]:::output K["PROGRAM EXIT
return 0 or System.exit"]:::exit A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G -->|Valid| H G -->|Invalid| L["ERROR MESSAGE
Usage instructions printed"]:::error L --> K H --> I I --> J J --> K classDef user fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef system fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef parser fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef loader fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef stack fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef program fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef validation fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef convert fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef logic fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef exit fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef error fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Memory Layout of `argv` Array (C/C++) This diagram shows how the `argv` array and its strings are organized in **RAM**. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph STACK["CALL STACK"] ARGC["argc = 4
int type"]:::intvar ARGV["argv
char* pointer"]:::pointer end subgraph HEAP["HEAP MEMORY (Strings)"] S0["argv[0]
./sumprog"]:::str S1["argv[1]
10"]:::str S2["argv[2]
20"]:::str S3["argv[3]
30"]:::str end ARGV -->|points to| S0 ARGV -->|offset +1| S1 ARGV -->|offset +2| S2 ARGV -->|offset +3| S3 S0 -.->|char array +
null terminator| N0["H E L L O \\0"]:::nullterm S1 -.->|char array +
null terminator| N1["1 0 \\0"]:::nullterm S2 -.->|char array +
null terminator| N2["2 0 \\0"]:::nullterm S3 -.->|char array +
null terminator| N3["3 0 \\0"]:::nullterm classDef intvar fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pointer fill:#FF9800,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef str fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef nullterm fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Argument Processing Decision Tree This flowchart helps students decide **which conversion function to use** based on the target data type. ```mermaid flowchart TD START["RECEIVED argv as char pointer"]:::start Q1{"Target data type?"}:::decision INT["Use atoi or stoi
argv to int"]:::intbranch FLOAT["Use atof or stof
argv to float"]:::floatbranch DOUBLE["Use atof or stod
argv to double"]:::doublebranch LONG["Use atol or stol
argv to long"]:::longbranch STRING["No conversion needed
Use directly as char pointer"]:::stringbranch BOOL["Compare with strcmp
argv to bool"]:::boolbranch USE["Use converted value
in program logic"]:::use START --> Q1 Q1 -->|Integer| INT Q1 -->|Float| FLOAT Q1 -->|Double| DOUBLE Q1 -->|Long| LONG Q1 -->|String| STRING Q1 -->|Boolean Flag| BOOL INT --> USE FLOAT --> USE DOUBLE --> USE LONG --> USE STRING --> USE BOOL --> USE classDef start fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef intbranch fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef floatbranch fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef doublebranch fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef longbranch fill:#80CBC4,stroke:#004D40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef stringbranch fill:#B0BEC5,stroke:#263238,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef boolbranch fill:#F48FB1,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef use fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Argument Processing Module ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"] CLI["Command Line Interface"]:::io SHELL["Shell Parser"]:::io end subgraph VALIDATION["VALIDATION SUBSYSTEM"] COUNT["Argument Count Checker"]:::val TYPE["Argument Type Validator"]:::val RANGE["Range/Format Validator"]:::val end subgraph CONVERSION["CONVERSION SUBSYSTEM"] S2I["String to Integer
stoi / parseInt"]:::conv S2F["String to Float
stof / parseFloat"]:::conv S2D["String to Double
stod / parseDouble"]:::conv end subgraph BUSINESS["BUSINESS LOGIC SUBSYSTEM"] CALC["Mathematical Computation"]:::logic DECISION["Conditional Branching"]:::logic LOOP["Iterative Processing"]:::logic end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM"] FORMAT["Output Formatter"]:::out DISPLAY["Console Display"]:::out end CLI --> SHELL SHELL --> COUNT COUNT -->|Pass| TYPE COUNT -->|Fail| FORMAT TYPE -->|Pass| RANGE TYPE -->|Fail| FORMAT RANGE -->|Pass| S2I RANGE -->|Pass| S2F RANGE -->|Pass| S2D S2I --> CALC S2F --> CALC S2D --> CALC CALC --> DECISION DECISION --> LOOP LOOP --> FORMAT FORMAT --> DISPLAY classDef io fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef val fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef conv fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef logic fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *Explain command line arguments in C++. What is the significance of `argc` and `argv`?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Command line arguments are the values passed to a program at the time of execution from the command line (terminal). They allow a program to receive input dynamically without using `cin` or hard-coded values. The two special parameters received by `main()` are: - **`argc` (Argument Count):** An integer variable that stores the **total number of command-line arguments**, including the name of the program. Its value is always $\geq 1$. For example, if a program `./prog` is invoked with two additional arguments, `argc` will be `3`. - **`argv` (Argument Vector):** It is an **array of character pointers** (`char* argv[]`) where each element points to a C-string representing one argument. The signature is: ```cpp int main(int argc, char* argv[]) { ... } ``` `argv[0]` holds the program name, `argv[1]` holds the first user argument, and so on. All arguments are received as **strings**; numeric conversion (e.g., using `atoi()` or `stoi()`) is required for arithmetic operations. **(1 mark each for: definition + argc significance + argv significance)** --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *Differentiate between command line arguments in C++ and Java. How does `argv[0]` differ from `args[0]`?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | C++ | Java | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Syntax** | `int main(int argc, char* argv[])` | `public static void main(String[] args)` | | **Includes program name** | Yes, `argv[0]` is the program name | No, `args[0]` is the first user argument | | **Argument type** | `char*` (C-string) | `String` (object) | | **Minimum count** | `argc = 1` (no user args) | `args.length = 0` (no user args) | The key difference is that in **C++**, `argv[0]` always contains the **name of the executable** (e.g., `./myprog`), so the first user-supplied argument is at `argv[1]`. In **Java**, `args[0]` is the **first user-supplied argument** directly because the program name is not included in the array. This off-by-one difference is a common source of indexing errors. **(3 marks — tabular comparison + indexing nuance)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** out of a choice of two. Solutions below are exhaustive, written exactly as a topper would answer in the exam. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Explain the concept of command line arguments in C++ with a suitable example. Discuss the role of `argc` and `argv` in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a C++ program that accepts **three numbers** as command line arguments and prints the **largest** among them. Handle the case where the wrong number of arguments is passed. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks **Concept (2 marks):** Command line arguments are the parameters supplied to a program during its invocation from the operating system's command-line interface. They enable programs to receive dynamic input values without requiring interactive input during execution, making them suitable for batch processing, scripting, and automation. **Role of `argc` (2 marks):** - `argc` is an **integer** (of type `int`) that holds the **count of command-line arguments**, including the program name. - Its value is always **at least 1** (when no user arguments are provided, `argc = 1`). - The general formula is: $\text{argc} = n + 1$, where $n$ is the number of user arguments. - Example: For `./prog 10 20`, `argc = 3`. **Role of `argv` (2 marks):** - `argv` is an **array of character pointers** (`char* argv[]`) — alternatively written as `char** argv`. - `argv[0]` points to the program name string. - `argv[1]` to `argv[argc-1]` point to the user-supplied argument strings. - All arguments are received as **C-strings** (null-terminated character arrays), so numeric operations require conversion using `atoi()`, `stof()`, etc. **Example (1 mark):** ```cpp #include using namespace std; int main(int argc, char* argv[]) { cout << "Program: " << argv[0] << endl; cout << "Total args: " << argc << endl; return 0; } ``` --- #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks **Program (5 marks):** ```cpp #include #include using namespace std; int main(int argc, char* argv[]) { // Step 1: Validate argument count // Need: argv[0] (program name) + 3 numbers = argc of 4 if (argc != 4) { cout << "Usage: " << argv[0] << " " << endl; return 1; } // Step 2: Convert string arguments to integers int a = atoi(argv[1]); int b = atoi(argv[2]); int c = atoi(argv[3]); // Step 3: Find largest using conditional logic int largest = a; if (b > largest) { largest = b; } if (c > largest) { largest = c; } // Step 4: Display result cout << "The largest number is: " << largest << endl; return 0; } ``` **Execution Trace (1 mark):** ```bash $ g++ -o largest largest.cpp $ ./largest 45 78 23 The largest number is: 78 ``` **Explanation (1 mark):** The program first checks if exactly 3 user arguments are provided (`argc == 4`). If not, it displays a usage message and exits with code 1. Otherwise, it converts the three strings (`argv[1]`, `argv[2]`, `argv[3]`) to integers using `atoi()`, compares them using nested `if` statements, and prints the largest value. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a)** Explain how command line arguments work in Java. Compare the indexing of `args[]` in Java with `argv[]` in C++. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program that accepts a **student's name and three subject marks** as command line arguments, calculates the **total and average**, and displays the result. Include proper validation. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks **Java Command Line Arguments — Concept (3 marks):** In Java, command line arguments are received as a **String array** in the `main()` method: ```java public static void main(String[] args) { ... } ``` The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) automatically populates the `args[]` array with the values provided after the class name during execution. For example: ```bash java MyClass arg1 arg2 arg3 ``` This makes `args[0] = "arg1"`, `args[1] = "arg2"`, and `args[2] = "arg3"`. **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Feature | C++ (`argv[]`) | Java (`args[]`) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Type** | `char* argv[]` (C-string array) | `String[] args` (String object array) | | **First element** | Program name | First user argument | | **First user argument** | `argv[1]` | `args[0]` | | **No-args case** | `argc = 1`, `argv[0]` = program name | `args.length = 0` | | **Type of each element** | Mutable character array | Immutable String object | **Indexing Nuance (1 mark):** The **off-by-one** difference is the most critical distinction. In C++, `argv[0]` is reserved for the program name, so user arguments begin from `argv[1]`. In Java, the program name is **not** part of `args[]`, so user arguments start at `args[0]`. Forgetting this difference leads to a common bug where the wrong argument is accessed. --- #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks **Program (5 marks):** ```java public class StudentResult { public static void main(String[] args) { // Step 1: Validate - need 4 args: name + 3 marks if (args.length != 4) { System.out.println("Usage: java StudentResult " + " "); System.exit(1); } // Step 2: Extract name String name = args[0]; // Step 3: Convert marks to integers int mark1 = Integer.parseInt(args[1]); int mark2 = Integer.parseInt(args[2]); int mark3 = Integer.parseInt(args[3]); // Step 4: Calculate total and average int total = mark1 + mark2 + mark3; double average = total / 3.0; // Use 3.0 to ensure double division // Step 5: Display formatted output System.out.println("Student Name : " + name); System.out.println("Mark 1 : " + mark1); System.out.println("Mark 2 : " + mark2); System.out.println("Mark 3 : " + mark3); System.out.println("---------------------------"); System.out.println("Total : " + total); System.out.printf("Average : %.2f%n", average); } } ``` **Execution Trace (1 mark):** ```bash $ javac StudentResult.java $ java StudentResult Anjali 85 90 78 Student Name : Anjali Mark 1 : 85 Mark 2 : 90 Mark 3 : 78 --------------------------- Total : 253 Average : 84.33 ``` **Explanation (1 mark):** The program expects exactly 4 arguments — a name and three marks. It validates the count first, then extracts and converts the marks from `String` to `int` using `Integer.parseInt()`. The total is computed as an integer sum, and the average uses `3.0` (double) to ensure floating-point division. The output is formatted using `printf()` for 2-decimal precision. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):** > 1. **Off-by-one indexing error:** Writing `argv[0]` instead of `argv[1]` in C++ to access the first user argument. (Lose 1-2 marks) > 2. **Forgetting to include program name in `argc` check:** In C++, expecting `argc == 3` when it should be `argc == 4` for 3 user arguments. (Lose 1 mark) > 3. **Missing validation:** Not checking if the correct number of arguments is passed. Programs that crash with segmentation fault or unhandled exception lose **2-3 marks**. > 4. **Integer division bug:** Using `total / 3` instead of `total / 3.0` in Java, causing the average to truncate to an integer. (Lose 1 mark) > 5. **Forgetting `#include `** in C++ when using `atoi()` — causes compilation error. (Lose 1 mark) > 6. **Not writing usage instructions** in the error path — examiner expects a professional program. (Lose 0.5 mark) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Command Line Arguments** ### Core Concepts - ☐ Command line arguments are values passed to a program **at invocation time** from the terminal. - ☐ They are received in `main()` as parameters: `argc` (count) and `argv`/`args` (vector). - ☐ All arguments are received as **strings** — numeric conversion is required for arithmetic. ### C++ Specifics - ☐ Signature: `int main(int argc, char* argv[])` - ☐ `argv[0]` = program name;
