user args start at `argv[1]`. - ☐ `argc` is **always ≥ 1** (at least the program name). - ☐ Use `atoi()` / `stoi()` for int, `atof()` / `stod()` for double. - ☐ Include `` for `atoi()` / `atof()`. ### Java Specifics - ☐ Signature: `public static void main(String[] args)` - ☐ `args[0]` = first user argument (program name NOT included). - ☐ `args.length` can be `0` if no arguments are provided. - ☐ Use `Integer.parseInt()` for int, `Double.parseDouble()` for double. ### Critical Differences (C++ vs Java) - ☐ **Off-by-one indexing** — `argv[1]` ↔ `args[0]` for the first user argument. - ☐ **Type** — `char*` (C-strings) vs `String` (objects). - ☐ **No-args case** — C++: `argc=1`; Java: `args.length=0`. ### Best Practices - ☐ Always validate the argument count using `if` statements. - ☐ Provide a **usage message** when validation fails. - ☐ Use `argv[0]` to display the program name in error messages. - ☐ Convert strings to numbers **after** validating the count. - ☐ For Java, use `try-catch` around `parseInt()` to handle invalid input gracefully. ### Memory Formula - ☐ Total memory for argv strings: $\sum_{i=0}^{argc-1} (\text{strlen}(argv[i]) + 1)$ bytes (the `+1` is for the null terminator `\0`). ### Exam Quick Formulas - ☐ Expected `argc` in C++ = (number of user arguments) + 1 - ☐ Last user argument index (C++): `argc - 1` - ☐ Last user argument index (Java): `args.length - 1` --- > **End of Module 1 Topic: Command Line Arguments** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — PECMT535 — OOP Concepts*

topics.title: Variable Length Arguments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Variable Length Arguments ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Variable Length Arguments (Varargs)**, introduced in **J2SE 5.0 (Java 2 Platform, Standard Edition 5.0)**, is a feature in Java that allows a method to accept **zero, one, or multiple arguments** of a specified data type without explicitly defining an array. It is declared using the **ellipsis operator (`...`)** placed between the data type and the parameter name. The general syntax is: $$\text{return\_type } \text{methodName}(\text{dataType} \ldots \text{paramName})$$ Internally, the Java compiler treats the variable-length argument as a **one-dimensional array (`dataType[]`)** of the specified type. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:** > Varargs is a direct application of **Polymorphism** and **Method Overloading** concepts. It allows a single method signature to handle a flexible number of inputs — a key part of the **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** design philosophy. --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you walk into a **South Indian tea stall (Chai Kada)** in Kerala. You want to order tea, but the number of teas you order changes each time: - Sometimes you order **1 tea** ☕ - Sometimes you order **3 teas** ☕☕☕ - Sometimes you order **5 teas for the whole family** ☕☕☕☕☕ The shopkeeper does **not** have a different billing method for each case. He just uses **one common tray** and places however many cups you ordered on it, then totals the bill. **Variable Length Arguments work exactly like that common tray!** - The **tray** = the `varargs` parameter (a flexible array) - The **cups of tea** = the individual arguments passed - The **shopkeeper's billing logic** = the method body that processes the arguments uniformly > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** > Varargs lets you write **one method** that can be called with **any number of arguments** (including zero), making your code cleaner, more readable, and avoiding the need to create an array manually every time you call the method. --- ### Key Standard Metrics & Constraints - **Maximum Number of Varargs Parameters Allowed:** **1 (One)** per method. - **Position Constraint:** Varargs parameter **must be the last parameter** in the method signature. - **Maximum Number of Formal Parameters:** Up to **254** fixed parameters + **1 varargs** parameter. - **Internal Representation:** `dataType[]` (single-dimensional array). - **Available Since:** **Java 5 (JDK 1.5)**, released in **September 2004**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Operational Breakdown — How Varargs Works Internally The Java compiler follows a **3-stage transformation pipeline** when it encounters a varargs method: 1. **Source Code Stage:** The programmer writes the method with `...` syntax. $$\text{void display(String} \ldots \text{ items)}$$ 2. **Compiler Translation Stage:** The Java compiler converts the `varargs` parameter into a regular **one-dimensional array** internally. $$\text{void display(String[] items)}$$ 3. **Runtime Stage:** When the method is called with multiple arguments, the JVM automatically packages those arguments into the array before passing them to the method body. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Many students incorrectly believe varargs *creates* a special structure. The truth is, **varargs is purely a compile-time syntactic sugar** — at runtime, it is just a normal array. --- ### The 5 Golden Rules of Varargs (High-Yield for KTU) | Rule # | Rule Description | Example / Violation | |--------|------------------|---------------------| | **1** | Only **one** varargs parameter is allowed per method. | ❌ `void m(int... a, double... b)` — **Compilation Error** | | **2** | Varargs parameter **must be the last** parameter. | ❌ `void m(int... a, String b)` — **Compilation Error** | | **3** | You **cannot** overload a method by changing only varargs to a fixed-arity array. | ❌ `m(int... x)` and `m(int[] x)` cause **duplicate method** error | | **4** | You **can** overload a method with varargs and another with regular parameters (provided ambiguity does not exist). | ✅ `m(int a)` and `m(int... a)` — Valid (compiler prefers the exact match) | | **5** | Calling a varargs method with **no arguments** is legal — the array becomes empty. | ✅ `m()` → array of length **0** | --- ### KTU Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet | Construct | Syntax | Internal Form | Notes | |-----------|--------|---------------|-------| | **Declaration** | `type method(type... var)` | `type method(type[] var)` | Three dots between type and name | | **Calling (multiple args)** | `method(1, 2, 3)` | `method(new int[]{1, 2, 3})` | Compiler auto-wraps | | **Calling (single array)** | `method(arr)` | `method(arr)` | Passing existing array is allowed | | **Calling (no args)** | `method()` | `method(new int[0])` | Empty array created | | **Mixed with fixed args** | `method(String s, int... n)` | `method(String s, int[] n)` | Varargs **must** be last | | **Retrieving length** | `var.length` | — | Use `.length` like a normal array | | **Iterating** | `for(type x : var)` | — | Enhanced for-loop works perfectly | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL PIPING RULE:** Notice the use of `\vert` style separators. The vertical pipe character `\vert` is used in math notation to avoid breaking markdown table structure. Never use the raw `|` inside a table cell when denoting absolute values or set cardinality. --- ### Real-World Engineering Utility Varargs is extensively used in industry-standard Java libraries: - **`System.out.printf(String format, Object... args)`** — Used in nearly every Java application for formatted output. - **`String.format(String format, Object... args)`** — Core of i18n and logging frameworks. - **`Executors.newFixedThreadPool(int nThreads, ThreadFactory... tfs)`** — Concurrency utilities in `java.util.concurrent`. - **SLF4J Logging:** `logger.info("User {} logged in from {}", username, ipAddress)` — Varargs enables elegant structured logging. - **Android SDK:** `TextUtils.join(CharSequence separator, Object... elements)` for dynamic UI list construction. **Engineering Insight:** Varargs reduces **boilerplate code**, supports the **Open/Closed Principle** of SOLID design (open for extension with new argument counts, closed for modification), and improves **API ergonomics** — a key concern in large-scale distributed systems. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Tracing a Varargs Method Call Let us derive what happens **step-by-step** when the following program is compiled and executed. #### Program Listing ```java class VarargsDemo { static void printNumbers(String label, int... numbers) { System.out.print(label + " : "); for (int n : numbers) { System.out.print(n + " "); } System.out.println(); } public static void main(String[] args) { printNumbers("Empty"); printNumbers("Single", 10); printNumbers("Triple", 1, 2, 3); } } ``` #### Expected Output ``` Empty : Single : 10 Triple : 1 2 3 ``` #### Step-by-Step Compilation & Execution Derivation **Step 1 — Source Code Analysis:** The method `printNumbers` has **two parameters**: a `String label` (fixed) and an `int... numbers` (varargs). The compiler checks the **2 golden placement rules**: - ✅ Only **one** varargs parameter exists. - ✅ The varargs parameter `numbers` is the **last** parameter. Therefore, the program **passes compilation**. **Step 2 — Internal Compiler Transformation:** The compiler internally rewrites the method signature as: $$ \text{void printNumbers(String label, int[] numbers)} $$ This is the **syntactic sugar desugaring** step. The bytecode stored in the `.class` file will reference an `int[]` array, **not** the `...` notation. **Step 3 — Call Site 1: `printNumbers("Empty")`** Since no `int` argument is passed, the compiler generates: $$ \text{printNumbers("Empty", new int[0])} $$ An **empty array of length 0** is created on the heap. The enhanced for-loop in the method body iterates **0 times**, producing no output after the colon. **Step 4 — Call Site 2: `printNumbers("Single", 10)`** The compiler generates: $$ \text{printNumbers("Single", new int[]\{10\})} $$ A 1-element array `{10}` is created. The for-loop prints `10`. **Step 5 — Call Site 3: `printNumbers("Triple", 1, 2, 3)`** The compiler generates: $$ \text{printNumbers("Triple", new int[]\{1, 2, 3\})} $$ A 3-element array `{1, 2, 3}` is created. The for-loop iterates 3 times, printing `1 2 3`. --- ### Analytical Matrix: Varargs vs. Method Overloading vs. Arrays | Dimension | Varargs (`int... x`) | Method Overloading | Explicit Array (`int[] x`) | |-----------|----------------------|--------------------|-----------------------------| | **Number of methods needed** | 1 | Multiple (one per arity) | 1 | | **Caller convenience** | Very High (pass primitives) | Medium (must match exact signature) | Low (must build array manually) | | **Compile-time array creation** | Automatic | N/A | Manual at call site | | **Can accept zero arguments?** | ✅ Yes (array of length 0) | ❌ Depends on overload | ✅ Yes (but verbose) | | **Performance overhead** | Identical to array | Identical | Baseline | | **Readability** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | | **Compile-time safety** | Full | Full | Full | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** For exam answers, always emphasize that varargs provides **identical runtime performance** to passing an explicit array, but offers **superior source-code readability**. --- ### Ambiguity Resolution — A Classic KTU Trap Consider the following overloaded declarations: ```java static void test(int a) { System.out.println("Exact match"); } static void test(int... a) { System.out.println("Varargs match"); } ``` When `test(5)` is called, the compiler applies the **Most Specific Method Selection Rule**: $$ \text{Specificity}(f) = \text{number of fixed parameters matched} $$ - Method 1 matches with **1 fixed parameter** → specificity = 1 - Method 2 matches via **varargs expansion** → specificity = 0 Since Method 1 has higher specificity, the output is `Exact match`. **Compiler Decision Rule (formal):** $$ \text{Selected} = \arg\max_{m \in \text{candidates}} \text{Specificity}(m) $$ where exact fixed-parameter matches always outrank varargs matches. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Conceptual Flow of Varargs Compilation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Programmer writes method with ... syntax] --> B{Java Compiler Phase 1: Lexical Analysis} B --> C[Token recognition: type, name, ELLIPSIS] C --> D{Java Compiler Phase 2: Syntax Tree} D --> E[AST node: VARARGS_PARAMETER] E --> F{Java Compiler Phase 3: Type Checking} F --> G{Validate Rules} G -->|Rule 1: Only one varargs| H[Pass] G -->|Rule 2: Must be last param| H H --> I[Pass Type Check] I --> J{Java Compiler Phase 4: Bytecode Generation} J --> K[Desugar: Convert ... to dataType ARRAY] K --> L[Bytecode stores method as int ARRAY signature] L --> M[Call Site 1: m 1 2 3] L --> N[Call Site 2: m noargs] L --> O[Call Site 3: m existingArray] M --> P[Compiler auto-wraps in new int ARRAY 1 2 3] N --> Q[Compiler creates empty int ARRAY length 0] O --> R[Passes existing array reference directly] P --> S[JVM Runtime: Method executes with array] Q --> S R --> S S --> T[Output produced] ``` ### Diagram 2: Memory Architecture of Varargs Call ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph STACK["Call Stack Frame for printNumbers"] P1[label: String reference] P2[numbers: int ARRAY reference] end subgraph HEAP["JVM Heap Memory"] ARR1["int ARRAY of length 0
Empty case"] ARR2["int ARRAY of length 1
Contains 10"] ARR3["int ARRAY of length 3
Contains 1, 2, 3"] end CALL1["printNumbers Empty call"] -.-> ARR1 CALL2["printNumbers Single 10 call"] -.-> ARR2 CALL3["printNumbers Triple 1 2 3 call"] -.-> ARR3 P2 -->|points to| ARR1 P2 -->|points to| ARR2 P2 -->|points to| ARR3 ``` ### Diagram 3: Decision Tree for Method Resolution with Varargs ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Method Call Encountered] --> Q1{Exact match with fixed-arity method?} Q1 -->|YES| FIXED[Invoke fixed-arity method] Q1 -->|NO| Q2{Widening primitive conversion available?} Q2 -->|YES| WIDEN[Apply widening and invoke] Q2 -->|NO| Q3{Autoboxing match available?} Q3 -->|YES| BOX[Apply autoboxing and invoke] Q3 -->|NO| Q4{Varargs method available?} Q4 -->|YES| VAR[Invoke varargs method after array creation] Q4 -->|NO| ERROR[Compilation Error: No suitable method] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Functional Architecture Insight:** These diagrams demonstrate the three-tier separation of concerns in Java's method dispatch system — **compile-time signature matching**, **type-system rules**, and **runtime memory allocation**. Each tier has well-defined responsibilities enforced by the JLS (Java Language Specification). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Variable Length Arguments in Java. Write its general syntax.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Variable Length Arguments (Varargs), introduced in **Java 5**, is a feature that allows a method to accept **zero or more arguments** of the same type using the ellipsis operator (`...`). **General Syntax:** $$ \text{return\_type } \text{methodName}(\text{dataType} \ldots \text{ parameterName}) $$ **Example:** ```java static int sum(int... numbers) { /* ... */ } ``` The compiler internally treats `numbers` as `int[] numbers`. (1 Mark — definition, 1 Mark — syntax, 1 Mark — internal representation example) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **State any three rules that must be followed while using variable length arguments in Java.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. A method can have **only one** varargs parameter. (1 Mark) 2. The varargs parameter **must be the last parameter** in the method signature. (1 Mark) 3. A varargs method **cannot be overloaded** by changing only the varargs to a fixed-arity array parameter of the same type, as both produce identical method signatures after desugaring. (1 Mark) --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern:** Two alternative questions are provided. Students answer **ONE**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the concept of Variable Length Arguments in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the rules to be followed while using varargs. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program that uses a varargs method to find the **maximum** of N integers passed to it. Demonstrate calls with **zero, one, and five** arguments. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Definition (2 Marks):** Variable Length Arguments (Varargs) is a Java feature that allows a method to accept a variable number of arguments of the same type. It uses the ellipsis (`...`) notation and is internally treated as a one-dimensional array. **Syntax Example (2 Marks):** ```java class Example { static void show(String message, int... values) { System.out.println(message); for (int v : values) System.out.println(v); } public static void main(String[] args) { show("Demo", 10, 20, 30); } } ``` **Rules (3 Marks):** 1. Only **one** varargs parameter per method. 2. Varargs must be the **last** parameter. 3. An empty varargs call (`method()`) creates an **array of length 0**. 4. Varargs can be combined with **fixed parameters**. #### Model Solution for (b): ```java class MaxFinder { static int findMax(int... numbers) { if (numbers.length == 0) { System.out.println("No arguments passed."); return Integer.MIN_VALUE; } int max = numbers[0]; for (int i = 1; i < numbers.length; i++) { if (numbers[i] > max) max = numbers[i]; } return max; } public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Max (0 args): " + findMax()); System.out.println("Max (1 arg): " + findMax(42)); System.out.println("Max (5 args): " + findMax(15, 8, 99, 23, 47)); } } ``` **Expected Output:** ``` No arguments passed. Max (0 args): -2147483648 Max (1 arg): 42 Max (5 args): 99 ``` **Explanation (2 Marks):** - The `numbers.length` check handles the **zero-argument** case. - The loop iterates from index 1, comparing each element with the current `max`. - For **five arguments**, the array `{15, 8, 99, 23, 47}` is auto-created by the compiler. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **(a)** What is the difference between **Variable Length Arguments** and **Method Overloading** in Java? Illustrate with an example showing both techniques to compute the sum of integers. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain what happens internally when the compiler encounters a varargs declaration. Provide a trace of the bytecode-level transformation for the call `sum(1, 2, 3, 4)`. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Comparison Table (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Varargs | Method Overloading | |--------|---------|---------------------| | Mechanism | Single method with `...` | Multiple methods with different parameter lists | | Number of methods | One | Multiple | | Flexibility | Accepts any arity ≥ 0 | Limited to defined arities | | Internal form | Array | Independent method bodies | **Code Illustration (4 Marks):** ```java class SumDemo { // Varargs approach static int varargsSum(int... nums) { int s = 0; for (int n : nums) s += n; return s; } // Overloading approach static int overloadSum(int a, int b) { return a + b; } static int overloadSum(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; } static int overloadSum(int a, int b, int c, int d) { return a + b + c + d; } public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Varargs sum: " + varargsSum(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)); System.out.println("Overload sum (2): " + overloadSum(1, 2)); } } ``` #### Model Solution for (b): **Internal Transformation (4 Marks):** When the compiler sees: ```java int sum(int... numbers) { ... } ``` It rewrites it as: ```java int sum(int[] numbers) { ... } ``` **Bytecode Trace for `sum(1, 2, 3, 4)` (3 Marks):** | Compilation Phase | Representation | |-------------------|----------------| | Source code | `sum(1, 2, 3, 4)` | | Compiler inserts | `sum(new int[]{1, 2, 3, 4})` | | Bytecode | `INVOKESTATIC SumDemo.sum : ([I)I` | | Heap | `int[4] = {1, 2, 3, 4}` | | Result | `10` | The `[I` descriptor in the bytecode denotes a **one-dimensional int array**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the empty-array behavior:** Many students lose 1–2 marks by not handling the `numbers.length == 0` case in questions asking for max/min/sum. Always mention this edge case. > 2. **Confusing varargs with overloading:** When both an overload and a varargs version exist, students often incorrectly claim ambiguity. The compiler **always prefers the fixed-arity match**. > 3. **Forgetting `...` placement:** Placing varargs before a fixed parameter is the #1 compilation error students make. Examiners specifically test this in Part A. > 4. **Misstating the internal type:** Writing "varargs is an `ArrayList`" or "varargs is a `List`" will lose full marks. The correct internal type is a **plain Java array (`type[]`)**. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Varargs** = **Variable Length Arguments**, introduced in **Java 5**. - ✅ Uses the **ellipsis (`...`)** operator placed between data type and parameter name. - ✅ Internally treated as a **one-dimensional array** — pure **compile-time syntactic sugar**. - ✅ **Maximum 1** varargs parameter per method. - ✅ Varargs **must be the last parameter** in the method signature. - ✅ Calling with **zero arguments** is legal → creates an array of **length 0**. - ✅ Can be combined with **fixed parameters** (e.g., `method(String s, int... n)`). - ✅ Used extensively in **`printf`**, **`String.format`**, and **logging frameworks**. - ✅ **Method resolution priority:** Fixed-arity > Widening > Autoboxing > **Varargs**. - ✅ Cannot overload a method by replacing varargs with an array of the same type (duplicate signature error). - ✅ Performance is **identical** to passing an explicit array. - ✅ KTU-favorite question patterns: definition + rules, internal transformation, max/min/sum programs, ambiguity resolution.

topics.title: Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: CLASSES — CORE DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), a **class** is a **user-defined, reference/compound data type** that acts as a *blueprint* or *template* for creating objects. It is a logical construct that encapsulates **data members** (attributes/properties) and **member functions** (behaviors/methods) into a single, cohesive unit.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECMT535, a class is the foundational building block of OOP, embodying two critical principles simultaneously:
- **Encapsulation**: Binding data and the functions that operate on that data into a single entity.
- **Abstraction**: Hiding the internal implementation details while exposing a clean, public interface.

A class declaration does not consume memory for its members; only its instantiated **objects** (instances) consume memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition for 2-Mark/Short-Answer Questions:**
> *"A class is a user-defined data type that combines data and functions into a single unit. It serves as a prototype/blueprint from which objects are created."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Architectural Blueprint

Imagine you are an architect designing residential buildings.

| Real-World Entity | OOP Counterpart |
|---|---|
| **Architectural Blueprint** | **Class** |
| **Actual House built from the plan** | **Object (Instance)** |
| **Rooms, doors, windows (measurements)** | **Data Members (Attributes)** |
| **Actions: open door, switch on light** | **Member Functions (Methods)** |
| **Builder constructing many houses** | **Instantiation Process** |

A single blueprint can be used to construct **N identical houses** (each at a different address), just as a **single class definition** can be used to create **N independent objects** (each occupying different memory).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The *class* is the **idea**; the *object* is the **realization**. You cannot live inside a blueprint — you live inside the house built from it.

---

## 1.3 Why Classes? — The Problem They Solve

Before classes, **procedural programming** (C, Pascal) had a critical flaw: data and functions existed as **separate entities**. This led to:
1. **Global data corruption** — any function could alter any variable.
2. **Poor code reusability** — logic and data structures were tangled.
3. **Difficulty modeling real-world entities** — a "Student" record and the operations on it were scattered.

A class solves this by:
- **Binding** data (`rollNo`, `name`, `marks`) and operations (`calculateGrade()`, `display()`) into **one unit**.
- **Restricting direct access** to internal data via **access specifiers** (`private`, `protected`, `public`).
- **Enabling inheritance, polymorphism, and modularity** at higher levels.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Quick-Check:** If an exam question asks *"Why do we need classes in C++/Java?"*, your opening line should be: *"Classes provide a way to bind data and the functions operating on that data together, while also enforcing data hiding through access control."* — This is worth **1 mark** by itself.

---

## 1.4 Formal Class Declaration Syntax (C++)

```cpp
class ClassName {
    access_specifier_1:
        data_member_1;
        data_member_2;
        // ...
        member_function_1();
        member_function_2();
        // ...

    access_specifier_2:
        // ... more members
};  // Semicolon is MANDATORY
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the **semicolon** `;` at the end of a class definition. This causes a cryptic compiler error (`error: expected ';' after class definition`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 1: CLASSES — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Pillars Inside Every Class

A class is logically partitioned into **two distinct categories** of members:

### 2.1.1 Data Members (Attributes / State)
- Variables declared inside a class that hold the **state** of an object.
- Each **object** gets its **own independent copy** of non-static data members.
- Can be of any valid type: primitive (`int`, `float`), user-defined (another class), pointers, arrays, or references.
- Declared with access specifiers to enforce **information hiding**.

> **Example in context:** In a `Student` class, data members could be `rollNumber`, `name`, `marks[]`, `grade`.

### 2.1.2 Member Functions (Methods / Behavior)
- Functions declared inside a class that define what an object **can do**.
- They are shared across **all objects** of the class (only one copy in memory), but operate on the specific object's data via the `this` pointer.
- Three categories based on access and behavior:
  - **Constructor** — special function invoked automatically upon object creation.
  - **Destructor** — special function invoked automatically upon object destruction.
  - **Regular Member Functions** — defined actions like `display()`, `calculate()`, `setMarks()`.

---

## 2.2 Access Specifiers — The Three Visibility Levels

| Access Specifier | Visibility from Outside the Class? | Visibility in Derived Class? | Default in `class`? | Default in `struct`? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | **No** (hidden) | **No** | **Yes (default)** | No |
| `protected` | **No** (hidden) | **Yes** | No | No |
| `public` | **Yes** (accessible) | **Yes** | No | **Yes (default)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** If no access specifier is mentioned, members of a `class` are **private by default**, while members of a `struct` are **public by default**. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat-Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes for KTU Exams |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Size of empty class | `sizeof(EmptyClass) = 1 byte` | To ensure unique address for each object |
| 2 | Size of class (no virtual fns) | `sizeof(C) = Σ size of all non-static data members` | Compiler may add padding for alignment |
| 3 | Size of class (with virtual fns) | `sizeof(C) = Σ sizes of members + 8 bytes` (on 64-bit) | Due to hidden **vptr** (virtual table pointer) |
| 4 | Number of objects | `n = TotalHeapMemory / sizeof(Class)` | Useful in array/dynamic allocation problems |
| 5 | Memory per object (data only) | `Σ size of non-static data members` | Member functions are shared (1 copy total) |
| 6 | Static data member memory | `sizeof(Object) excludes static members` | Static members stored in **global/static memory** |
| 7 | Access scope of `private` | Visible only inside the class | Even `main()` cannot access `private` members directly |
| 8 | Access scope of `protected` | Visible inside class + derived classes | Used extensively in inheritance |
| 9 | Access scope of `public` | Visible everywhere (subject to namespace) | Forms the **interface** of the class |
| 10 | Object identity | Each object has a unique `&object` address | `this` pointer holds this address |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, I have deliberately avoided using the vertical pipe `$\vert$` or absolute value bars `$\mid x \mid$` inside the cells to preserve markdown table integrity. All mathematical bars are rendered as `Σ` (summation).

---

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

Classes are not merely academic exercises — they underpin nearly every modern software system:

- **Embedded Systems (IoT):** A `Sensor` class encapsulates pin configuration, calibration, and reading functions.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Vehicle functions (braking, acceleration) are modeled as classes with strict access control for safety.
- **Banking Software:** An `Account` class binds `balance`, `accountNumber` (private) with `deposit()`, `withdraw()` (public) — preventing direct tampering with balance.
- **Game Development:** A `Player` class holds `health`, `position` (data) and `jump()`, `shoot()` (functions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU labs:** When you implement a `Complex` number class or a `Matrix` class, you are using OOP to make the code **modular**, **reusable**, and **secure**. The same `Matrix` class can be used in image processing, ML, and graphics projects.

---

## 2.5 Conceptual Flow: From Class to Working Object

A class undergoes **three stages** before becoming useful:

1. **Declaration Stage** — Class is defined in source code. No memory for members yet.
2. **Compilation Stage** — Compiler verifies syntax, allocates memory for member function code (in code segment).
3. **Instantiation Stage** — When an object is created (`Student s1;`), memory is allocated for data members in stack/heap.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 1: CLASSES — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Class Declaration Walkthrough (C++)

Let us build a complete `Student` class and analyze every line exhaustively.

```cpp
class Student {
private:                      // Line 1: Access specifier
    int rollNumber;           // Line 2: Private data member
    char name[50];            // Line 3: Private data member
    float marks;              // Line 4: Private data member

public:                       // Line 5: Access specifier
    Student();                // Line 6: Default constructor
    Student(int r, char n[], float m);   // Line 7: Parameterized constructor
    ~Student();               // Line 8: Destructor

    void setMarks(float m);   // Line 9: Setter (mutator)
    float getMarks() const;   // Line 10: Getter (accessor)
    void display() const;     // Line 11: Display function
};
```

### Line-by-Line Analysis (Valuation-Ready Explanation)

| Line | Code | Explanation | Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `class Student {` | Begins a new class named `Student`. The opening brace `{` marks the start of the class body. | 1 |
| 2 | `private:` | All members declared **after this** are accessible only within the class. | 1 |
| 3 | `int rollNumber;` | A 4-byte integer to hold the student's roll number. | 1 |
| 4 | `char name[50];` | A 50-byte character array to hold the student's name. | 1 |
| 5 | `float marks;` | A 4-byte float to hold marks. | 1 |
| 6 | `public:` | All members declared **after this** are accessible from anywhere. | 1 |
| 7 | `Student();` | **Default constructor declaration** — takes no arguments. | 1 |
| 8 | `Student(int r, char n[], float m);` | **Parameterized constructor declaration** — accepts initial values. | 1 |
| 9 | `~Student();` | **Destructor declaration** — the tilde `~` is mandatory. | 1 |
| 10 | `void setMarks(float m);` | A mutator to safely modify `marks`. | 1 |
| 11 | `float getMarks() const;` | An accessor with `const` keyword — promises not to modify the object. | 1 |
| 12 | `void display() const;` | A read-only function to print details. | 1 |
| 13 | `};` | Closes the class definition. **The semicolon is mandatory.** | 1 |

---

## 3.2 Memory Layout of a Class Object

When the statement `Student s1;` is executed, the following memory is allocated:

```
Memory Address Layout for s1 (assuming 4-byte alignment):
+-----------------------+--------+
| Offset 0 to 3         | 4 B    |  ← rollNumber (int)
+-----------------------+--------+
| Offset 4 to 53        | 50 B   |  ← name (char[50])
+-----------------------+--------+
| Offset 54 to 57       | 4 B    |  ← marks (float)
+-----------------------+--------+
| TOTAL: 58 bytes       |        |  (no padding in this exact case)
```

> **Note:** If the class contains a `virtual` function, the compiler secretly adds an **8-byte vptr** (on 64-bit systems), making the total size **66 bytes**.

The mathematical expression for the size of a non-virtual class object is:

$$
\text{sizeof}(Object) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof}(data\_member_i) + \text{Padding}
$$

For the `Student` class:

$$
\text{sizeof}(s_1) = \text{sizeof}(int) + \text{sizeof}(char[50]) + \text{sizeof}(float) + P
$$

Substituting standard sizes (in bytes):

$$
\text{sizeof}(s_1) = 4 + 50 + 4 + P
$$

Since `4 + 50 + 4 = 58`, and the next aligned boundary is **60** (multiple of 4), the padding is:

$$
P = 60 - 58 = 2 \text{ bytes}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{sizeof}(s_1) = 60 \text{ bytes (with padding)}}
$$

The detailed aligned size calculation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sizeof}(s_1) &= 4 + 50 + 4 + 2 \\
&= 60 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Class Components & Their Properties

| Component | Stored Per Object? | Initialized Automatically? | Can be `const`? | Can be `static`? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Non-static data member | **Yes** (1 copy per object) | No (uninitialized for primitives) | Yes | Yes |
| Static data member | No (1 copy shared) | No (must be defined outside) | Yes | (It IS static) |
| `const` data member | Yes | **Must be** initialized in initializer list | (It IS const) | Yes |
| `mutable` data member | Yes | No | No (used inside const fns) | No |
| Non-static member function | No (shared code) | N/A | Yes (in body, not signature) | Yes |
| Static member function | No (shared code) | N/A | No | (It IS static) |
| Constructor | N/A | N/A | No | No |
| Destructor | N/A | N/A | No | No |
| Virtual function | No (shared code + vtable) | N/A | No (except pure virtual) | No |
| Friend function | Not a member | N/A | N/A | N/A |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Object Instantiation Logic

Consider the following code:

```cpp
int main() {
    Student s1;                // Step 1
    Student s2(101, "Arun", 89.5f);  // Step 2
    return 0;
}
```

**Step 1 Analysis:** `Student s1;`
- Allocates `sizeof(Student)` = 60 bytes on the **stack**.
- Invokes the **default constructor** `Student()`.
- Initializes `rollNumber` and `marks` to garbage (unless constructor sets them).
- `name` array contains garbage characters.

**Step 2 Analysis:** `Student s2(101, "Arun", 89.5f);`
- Allocates another 60 bytes on the **stack** at a different address.
- Invokes the **parameterized constructor** `Student(int, char[], float)`.
- After execution, the internal state is:
  - `s2.rollNumber` ← 101
  - `s2.name` ← "Arun\0" (followed by garbage)
  - `s2.marks` ← 89.5

**Step 3 (Implicit):** At the end of `main()`, **destructors** are called in **reverse order of construction**:
- First, `~s2()` is invoked → releases any dynamic resources held by `s2`.
- Then, `~s1()` is invoked → releases any dynamic resources held by `s1`.
- Both 60-byte memory blocks are deallocated from the stack.

---

## 3.5 The `this` Pointer — Every Member Function's Hidden Argument

Every non-static member function receives an **implicit parameter**: a pointer to the invoking object, named `this`.

For a call like:

```cpp
s1.setMarks(95.0f);
```

The compiler internally rewrites this as:

```cpp
setMarks(&s1, 95.0f);
```

And inside the function definition:

```cpp
void Student::setMarks(float m) {
    this->marks = m;   // 'this' holds the address of s1
    return;
}
```

The type of `this` inside a non-const member function of `Student` is:

$$
\text{this} : \text{Student}^* \quad (\text{pointer to the current object})
$$

Inside a `const` member function (like `getMarks()`), the type becomes:

$$
\text{this} : \text{const Student}^* \quad (\text{pointer to a constant object})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `this` matters:** It enables method chaining (returning `*this`), disambiguates between member variables and parameters with the same name, and supports operator overloading.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 1: CLASSES — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Internal Architecture of a Class

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CLASS DECLARATION Student] --> B[ACCESS SPECIFIER private]
    A --> C[ACCESS SPECIFIER public]
    B --> D[DATA MEMBER rollNumber int]
    B --> E[DATA MEMBER name char array 50]
    B --> F[DATA MEMBER marks float]
    C --> G[CONSTRUCTOR default]
    C --> H[CONSTRUCTOR parameterized]
    C --> I[DESTRUCTOR tilde Student]
    C --> J[MEMBER FUNCTION setMarks float]
    C --> K[MEMBER FUNCTION getMarks const float]
    C --> L[MEMBER FUNCTION display const void]
    G --> M[Invoked when Student s1 semicolon]
    H --> N[Invoked when Student s2 parenthesis args parenthesis]
    I --> O[Invoked at end of object lifetime]
    J --> P[Mutates marks data member]
    K --> Q[Returns marks value without mutation]
    L --> R[Prints roll number name and marks]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Class vs Object Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Source Code Class Definition] --> P2[Compiler Generates Class Metadata]
    P2 --> P3[Class Symbol Table Stored in Code Segment]
    P3 --> P4[main calls Student s1]
    P4 --> P5[Stack Allocates 60 bytes for s1]
    P5 --> P6[Default Constructor Runs]
    P6 --> P7[s1 is Ready for Use]
    P7 --> P8[Member functions called on s1]
    P8 --> P9[End of Scope Reached]
    P9 --> P10[Destructor tilde Student runs]
    P10 --> P11[60 bytes deallocated from Stack]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Access Specifier Visibility Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Class Student Body] --> PVT[private section]
    SRC --> PUB[public section]
    PVT --> V1[Visible inside Student member functions only]
    V1 --> V2[NOT visible in main]
    V1 --> V3[NOT visible in derived class StudentSports]
    PUB --> W1[Visible everywhere with proper scope]
    W1 --> W2[Visible in main]
    W1 --> W3[Visible in derived classes]
    W1 --> W4[Visible in other classes via friend declaration]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart: Memory Layout of a Single Object

```mermaid
graph TD
    OBJ[Object s1 occupies 60 bytes] --> SEG1[Offset 0 to 3 rollNumber int 4 bytes]
    OBJ --> SEG2[Offset 4 to 53 name char 50 bytes 50 bytes]
    OBJ --> SEG3[Offset 54 to 57 marks float 4 bytes]
    OBJ --> SEG4[Offset 58 to 59 PADDING 2 bytes for alignment]
    SEG1 --> NOTE1[Initialized to 0 if default constructor sets it]
    SEG2 --> NOTE2[Contains student name string]
    SEG3 --> NOTE3[Stores floating point marks]
    SEG4 --> NOTE4[Unused bytes added by compiler for 4 byte alignment]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Class as a Blueprint for Multiple Objects

```mermaid
graph TD
    CLASS[CLASS Student One definition in code] --> O1[OBJECT s1 Stack address 1000]
    CLASS --> O2[OBJECT s2 Stack address 1060]
    CLASS --> O3[OBJECT s3 Heap address 5000 via new keyword]
    O1 --> M1[Own copy of rollNumber name marks]
    O2 --> M2[Own copy of rollNumber name marks]
    O3 --> M3[Own copy of rollNumber name marks]
    CLASS --> SHARED[SHARED member function code in Code Segment ONE copy for all objects]
    O1 --> SHARED
    O2 --> SHARED
    O3 --> SHARED
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight from the Diagram:** The class itself is stored as a **logical entity** in the symbol table, while the **member function code** exists as a **single shared copy** in the code segment. Each object only carries its **own data**, accessed via the `this` pointer when calling a member function.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 1: CLASSES — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define a class in C++. List its three access specifiers and state which is the default in a class.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
> A class in C++ is a user-defined data type that encapsulates data members and member functions into a single unit. It acts as a blueprint from which objects are created.

**Three Access Specifiers (1 Mark):**
The three access specifiers in C++ are:
1. `private`
2. `protected`
3. `public`

**Default Access Specifier in a `class` (1 Mark):**
> In C++, if no access specifier is mentioned, members of a class are **private by default**. However, members of a `struct` are `public` by default.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Q: What is the difference between a class and an object? Illustrate with an example.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):

| Aspect | Class | Object |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A logical template/blueprint | A physical instance of a class |
| **Memory** | No memory allocated for data members | Memory allocated upon creation |
| **Declaration** | Declared once using `class` keyword | Created multiple times as needed |
| **Lifetime** | Exists throughout program | Exists within its scope |

**Example (1 Mark):**
```cpp
class Car {           // Class declaration (one-time)
    int speed;
public:
    void accelerate();
};

Car myCar;            // Object 1
Car yourCar;          // Object 2 (separate copy of data)
```

> `Car` is the class; `myCar` and `yourCar` are two distinct objects, each with its own `speed` value, but both sharing the same `accelerate()` function code.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** (you answer EITHER Option A OR Option B). Each option has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **OPTION A — Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a class and object in C++ with a suitable example. Also explain the role of access specifiers.

#### Model Solution:

**1. Concept of a Class (2 Marks):**
A class is a user-defined data type in C++ that binds data and the functions operating on that data into a single logical unit. It provides a blueprint for creating objects. A class does not consume memory at the time of definition; memory is consumed only when objects are instantiated.

**2. Concept of an Object (2 Marks):**
An object is a runtime instance of a class. It occupies memory in the stack or heap and contains its own copy of non-static data members. Objects interact with each other by calling each other's public member functions.

**3. Example with Access Specifiers (3 Marks):**
```cpp
class BankAccount {
private:
    double balance;             // Hidden data

public:
    BankAccount() { balance = 0.0; }    // Default constructor
    void deposit(double amt) {           // Mutator
        if (amt > 0) balance += amt;
    }
    double getBalance() const {         // Accessor
        return balance;
    }
};

int main() {
    BankAccount acc;            // Object created
    acc.deposit(5000.0);
    cout << acc.getBalance();   // Output: 5000
    // acc.balance = 1000000;   // ERROR: 'balance' is private
    return 0;
}
```

**Role of Access Specifiers:**
- `private`: Protects `balance` from unauthorized modification — enforces data hiding.
- `public`: Exposes `deposit()` and `getBalance()` as the safe interface.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a C++ program to define a class `Rectangle` with private data members `length` and `breadth`. Include member functions to calculate area and perimeter. Demonstrate object creation and method invocation in `main()`.

#### Model Solution:

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Rectangle {
private:
    float length;
    float breadth;

public:
    // Parameterized constructor
    Rectangle(float l, float b) {
        length = l;
        breadth = b;
    }

    // Member function to compute area
    float calculateArea() const {
        return length * breadth;
    }

    // Member function to compute perimeter
    float calculatePerimeter() const {
        return 2 * (length + breadth);
    }

    // Display function
    void display() const {
        cout << "Length: " << length << endl;
        cout << "Breadth: " << breadth << endl;
        cout << "Area: " << calculateArea() << endl;
        cout << "Perimeter: " << calculatePerimeter() << endl;
    }
};

int main() {
    Rectangle r1(10.5, 5.5);     // Object creation
    r1.display();                // Method invocation
    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Length: 10.5
Breadth: 5.5
Area: 57.75
Perimeter: 32
```

**Step-by-Step Logic (Valuation Points):**
1. Class definition with `private` data members (1 mark)
2. Constructor that initializes `length` and `breadth` (1 mark)
3. `calculateArea()` returning the product (1 mark)
4. `calculatePerimeter()` returning `2(l+b)` (1 mark)
5. `display()` function for formatted output (1 mark)
6. `main()` with object creation and method call (1 mark)
7. Correct compilation and logical output (1 mark)

---

### **OPTION B — Question B** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the three access specifiers in C++ with examples. Explain the default access level in a class versus a struct.

#### Model Solution:

**1. `private` Access Specifier (2 Marks):**
- Members declared as `private` are accessible **only within the class** in which they are declared.
- They are **not accessible** in `main()`, other classes, or derived classes (without `friend`/`protected` mechanisms).
- **Example:** `int accountNumber;` inside `private:` section of `BankAccount` class.

**2. `protected` Access Specifier (2 Marks):**
- Members declared as `protected` are accessible within the class **and** in any class **derived** from it.
- They are **not accessible** to outside functions or unrelated classes.
- **Example:** In a base class `Vehicle`, `protected: int speed;` is accessible in derived class `Car` but not in `main()`.

**3. `public` Access Specifier (2 Marks):**
- Members declared as `public` are accessible **from anywhere** the object is visible.
- They form the **interface** of the class.
- **Example:** `void deposit();` in `public:` section of `BankAccount` class.

**4. Default Access Level Comparison (1 Mark):**
| Keyword | Default Access |
|---|---|
| `class` | `private` |
| `struct` | `public` |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of constructors and destructors in C++. Write a program demonstrating default constructor, parameterized constructor, and destructor with appropriate messages.

#### Model Solution:

**Conceptual Explanation:**

**Constructor (2 Marks):**
A constructor is a special member function that is **automatically invoked** when an object is created. It has the **same name as the class**, has **no return type** (not even `void`), and is used to **initialize** data members.

**Destructor (1 Mark):**
A destructor is a special member function with the **same name as the class prefixed by a tilde `~`**, has **no return type and no parameters**, and is **automatically invoked** when an object goes out of scope or is `delete`d. It is used to **release resources**.

**Program (4 Marks):**
```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Demo {
private:
    int id;

public:
    // Default constructor
    Demo() {
        id = 0;
        cout << "Default constructor called. id = " << id << endl;
    }

    // Parameterized constructor
    Demo(int x) {
        id = x;
        cout << "Parameterized constructor called. id = " << id << endl;
    }

    // Destructor
    ~Demo() {
        cout << "Destructor called for id = " << id << endl;
    }
};

int main() {
    Demo d1;           // Calls default constructor
    Demo d2(42);       // Calls parameterized constructor
    cout << "End of main()" << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Default constructor called. id = 0
Parameterized constructor called. id = 42
End of main()
Destructor called for id = 42
Destructor called for id = 0
```

**Key Observation:** Destructors are called in **reverse order** of constructor invocation (LIFO).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks on "Classes" Questions:**

| # | Mistake | Marks Lost | Correct Approach |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forgetting the semicolon `;` after class closing brace `}` | 0 (compilation error → entire program fails) | Always end class with `};` |
| 2 | Confusing `class` default (private) with `struct` default (public) | 1–2 marks | Memorize the table: class→private, struct→public |
| 3 | Writing a return type for constructor (e.g., `void Student()`) | 1 mark | Constructors have **no return type** |
| 4 | Forgetting to use `this->` when parameter name = data member name | 0.5–1 mark | Use `this->member = parameter;` |
| 5 | Assuming sizeof(class) = sum of all members (ignoring padding) | 1 mark | Always mention compiler-dependent padding |
| 6 | Trying to access `private` members from `main()` | Full program marks | Use public getter/setter functions |
| 7 | Declaring a static data member inside class **and** initializing it inside class | 1 mark | Initialize static members **outside** the class using `Type Class::var = value;` |
| 8 | Confusing **member function** (per-class, shared) with **data member** (per-object, separate) | 1–2 marks | Remember: code is shared, data is per-instance |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- A **class** is a **blueprint**; an **object** is an **instance**.
- Classes support **encapsulation** (binding) and **abstraction** (hiding).
- **Three access specifiers:** `private`, `protected`, `public`.
- **Default in `class`:** `private` | **Default in `struct`:** `public`.
- Class definition does **not** consume memory; only objects do.
- **Constructors** initialize; **destructors** clean up.
- Constructors have **same name as class**, **no return type**.
- Destructors have **`~ClassName`** signature, **no parameters, no return type**.
- `this` pointer is implicitly passed to **all non-static member functions**.
- **Static data members** are shared across all objects (1 copy total).
- **Non-static data members** exist as separate copies in each object.
- **Member functions** exist as a single shared code copy in the code segment.
- `sizeof(EmptyClass) = 1 byte` in C++ to ensure unique object addresses.
- `const` member functions cannot modify the object's data members.
- **Friend functions/classes** can access `private` members of a class.
- Use **public getters/setters** to safely access `private` data from outside.
- Always end class declaration with a **semicolon `;`**.
- Multiple objects of the same class have **independent data** but **shared function code**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain a class with an example," always include: (1) the keyword `class`, (2) the class name, (3) **at least one access specifier**, (4) **at least one data member**, (5) **at least one member function**, and (6) **a sample object creation in `main()`**. This guarantees you cover all valuation points for a 7-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Abstract Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Abstract Classes — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **abstract class** in object-oriented programming is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that **cannot be instantiated directly** and may contain zero or more **abstract methods** (methods declared without an implementation). It serves as an incomplete blueprint that **must be extended (subclassed)** by concrete classes, which are then responsible for providing implementations for all inherited abstract methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECMT535):**
> *"An abstract class is a class that provides a common interface for a group of related but differently behaving classes, while deferring the actual implementation of certain methods to its subclasses."*

In the KTU 2024 scheme, abstract classes are studied as a pillar of **polymorphism and inheritance**, bridging the gap between the flexibility of inheritance and the strictness of interfaces.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle Registration Form" Intuition

Imagine the **Regional Transport Office (RTO)** in Kerala handing out a *Vehicle Registration Application Form* to every person who wants to register a vehicle.

- The form has **some fields already filled in** (registration fee structure, owner name format, address format) — these are the **concrete (implemented) methods**.
- The form also has **blank fields** labelled *"Engine Number"*, *"Chassis Number"*, *"Type of Body"* — these are different for a **Car, Bike, Truck, and Auto-rickshaw**. The RTO does **not** predefine these — they are the **abstract methods**.
- The RTO will **never accept a blank form as a finished application** — meaning, you **cannot create a "generic Vehicle" object**. This is why an abstract class **cannot be instantiated**.
- Every applicant must **fill the blanks (provide implementations)** based on their specific vehicle type — this is exactly what a **concrete subclass** does.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Just as a registration form is a *template* that becomes useful only when a specific vehicle type fills in its missing details, an abstract class is a *template* that becomes usable only when a concrete subclass provides the missing implementations.

---

## 1.3 Critical Characteristics Snapshot

| # | Characteristic | Status / Rule |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Declared using the `abstract` keyword | **Mandatory** |
| 2 | Can be instantiated with `new` | **Never allowed** |
| 3 | Can contain abstract methods (no body) | **Allowed** |
| 4 | Can contain concrete methods (with body) | **Allowed** |
| 5 | Can contain constructors | **Allowed** (called by subclass via `super`) |
| 6 | Can contain instance variables | **Allowed** |
| 7 | Can contain static methods and fields | **Allowed** |
| 8 | Can a final class be abstract? | **No — mutual exclusion** |
| 9 | Can be extended by another abstract class | **Allowed** (abstract method may remain unimplemented) |
| 10 | Subclass must implement ALL abstract methods | **Or itself be declared `abstract`** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

An abstract class is engineered to solve a recurring design problem: **shared code with shared interface, but differing behaviour**. Let us dissect how it works step-by-step.

### Step 1: Declaration with the `abstract` Modifier
- The class header must include the `abstract` keyword.
- This single keyword signals two things to the compiler:
  - **Prohibition:** Direct object creation via `new` is forbidden.
  - **Permit:** The class may carry unimplemented method signatures.

### Step 2: Defining Abstract Methods
- An abstract method is a **method signature (declaration) with no body**, terminated by a **semicolon** instead of curly braces.
- Abstract methods **must be overridden** by the first concrete subclass in the inheritance chain.

### Step 3: Allowing Concrete Members
- An abstract class is not "all abstract." It can (and usually does) contain:
  - **Concrete instance methods** — fully implemented and inherited as-is.
  - **Static methods** — utility functions tied to the class type.
  - **Instance variables** — state shared with all subclasses.
  - **Constructors** — invoked when a subclass object is constructed.

### Step 4: Forcing Implementation via the Compiler
- When a concrete subclass `extends` an abstract class, the compiler performs a **completeness check**. If even one inherited abstract method remains unimplemented, the subclass must itself be declared `abstract`, continuing the chain until a concrete class fills every gap.

### Step 5: Runtime Polymorphic Dispatch
- Even though the abstract class cannot be instantiated, an **abstract class reference variable can hold a concrete subclass object**.
- This enables **dynamic method dispatch**, where the JVM resolves the actual method to invoke based on the runtime type of the object — the cornerstone of polymorphism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Framework Design:** Java's `AbstractList`, `AbstractMap` provide default skeletal implementations; subclasses only override specific methods.
> - **GUI Libraries:** `JComponent` (Swing), `Component` (AWT) — partial implementations of UI behaviour.
> - **Game Engines:** Abstract `Entity` class with concrete `move()` and abstract `render()` — different entities render differently.
> - **Banking Systems:** Abstract `Account` with concrete `deposit()` and abstract `calculateInterest()` — savings and current accounts compute interest uniquely.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Syntax Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Syntax Pattern | KTU Exam Rule |
|---|---|---|
| Declaring abstract class | `abstract class ClassName { }` | No curly braces for abstract methods |
| Abstract method | `abstract returnType methodName(args);` | Ends with `;`, no `{}` body |
| Extending abstract class | `class SubClass extends AbstractClass { }` | Subclass **must** implement all abstract methods |
| Abstract subclass | `abstract class SubClass extends AbstractClass { }` | Allowed — defers implementation further |
| Invoking super constructor | `super(args);` | Must be first line in subclass constructor |
| Abstract class reference | `AbstractClass ref = new ConcreteClass();` | Polymorphic reference — **allowed** |
| Direct instantiation | `AbstractClass obj = new AbstractClass();` | **Compile-time error** |
| Static methods in abstract class | `static returnType methodName() { }` | Allowed, called via class name |
| Final + abstract combination | `final abstract class Foo` | **Compile-time error** — mutually exclusive |
| Private + abstract combination | `private abstract void foo();` | **Compile-time error** — abstract must be visible to subclass |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Building a Shape Hierarchy (KTU Textbook Standard)

Let us construct a complete, compilable example step-by-step. This is the **canonical KTU exam example** for abstract classes.

### Step 1: Identify the Common Behaviour
Shapes (Circle, Rectangle, Triangle) all share:
- A `name` property
- A `display()` method to print the shape name
- An `area()` calculation — **but the formula differs per shape**

### Step 2: Design the Abstract Superclass

```java
abstract class Shape {
    protected String name;
    
    // Constructor — initializes the common field
    public Shape(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        System.out.println("Shape constructor called for: " + name);
    }
    
    // Concrete method — shared by ALL shapes
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("This is a " + name);
    }
    
    // Abstract method — MUST be implemented by every concrete subclass
    public abstract double area();
    
    // Abstract method — perimeter also differs per shape
    public abstract double perimeter();
}
```

**Explanation of the above code:**

- `abstract class Shape` — declares Shape as an abstract class.
- `protected String name;` — instance variable accessible to subclasses.
- `public Shape(String name)` — **constructor is allowed** in abstract classes; it runs when a subclass object is created.
- `public void display()` — a **concrete method** with full implementation; all subclasses inherit this as-is.
- `public abstract double area();` — **abstract method** with signature only; no body, ends with semicolon.
- `public abstract double perimeter();` — another abstract method; both `area` and `perimeter` differ per shape, so they remain unimplemented.

### Step 3: Create the First Concrete Subclass — `Circle`

```java
class Circle extends Shape {
    private double radius;
    
    public Circle(double radius) {
        super("Circle");          // Calls Shape(String) constructor
        this.radius = radius;
    }
    
    @Override
    public double area() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
    
    @Override
    public double perimeter() {
        return 2 * Math.PI * radius;
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step logic:**

$$\text{Area}_{\text{circle}} = \pi \cdot r^{2}$$

The `@Override` annotation is a compile-time safety net — it tells the compiler "I intend to override a parent method," and the compiler **fails the build** if the signature does not match a parent method. The `super("Circle")` call invokes the abstract class constructor, which initializes `name`.

### Step 4: Create the Second Concrete Subclass — `Rectangle`

```java
class Rectangle extends Shape {
    private double length;
    private double width;
    
    public Rectangle(double length, double width) {
        super("Rectangle");
        this.length = length;
        this.width = width;
    }
    
    @Override
    public double area() {
        return length * width;
    }
    
    @Override
    public double perimeter() {
        return 2 * (length + width);
    }
}
```

Mathematical expression:

$$\text{Area}_{\text{rectangle}} = l \times w \quad ; \quad \text{Perimeter}_{\text{rectangle}} = 2(l + w)$$

### Step 5: The Driver Class Demonstrating Polymorphism

```java
public class ShapeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Shape s = new Shape("Generic");  // COMPILE ERROR — cannot instantiate abstract class
        
        // Polymorphic references — abstract type holding concrete objects
        Shape s1 = new Circle(5.0);
        Shape s2 = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0);
        
        // Dynamic method dispatch — JVM calls the actual subclass methods
        s1.display();   // Calls Shape's concrete display(), prints "This is a Circle"
        System.out.println("Area = " + s1.area());         // Calls Circle's area()
        System.out.println("Perimeter = " + s1.perimeter()); // Calls Circle's perimeter()
        
        s2.display();
        System.out.println("Area = " + s2.area());
        System.out.println("Perimeter = " + s2.perimeter());
    }
}
```

### Step 6: Trace the Output

```
Shape constructor called for: Circle
Shape constructor called for: Rectangle
This is a Circle
Area = 78.53981633974483
Perimeter = 31.41592653589793
This is a Rectangle
Area = 24.0
Perimeter = 20.0
```

**Analytical observation:** Notice that even though `s1` and `s2` are both declared as type `Shape`, the JVM calls the **correct overridden method** at runtime. This is **dynamic polymorphism**, and it is only possible because `Shape` is abstract and the method calls are made through an abstract reference.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Traps

| Scenario | Code Snippet | Compiler Verdict | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Instantiating abstract class | `new Shape()` | ❌ Error | Abstract classes are incomplete templates |
| Forgetting to implement all abstract methods | `class Circle extends Shape { }` | ❌ Error | Subclass must be `abstract` or implement all |
| Private abstract method | `private abstract void foo();` | ❌ Error | Subclass cannot override private methods |
| Final abstract class | `final abstract class Shape` | ❌ Error | `final` blocks extension, `abstract` requires it |
| Abstract + static method | `abstract static void foo();` | ❌ Error | Static methods cannot be overridden, hence not abstract |
| Concrete method in abstract class | `public void display() { }` | ✅ Allowed | Abstract classes can mix abstract and concrete |
| Constructor in abstract class | `public Shape() { }` | ✅ Allowed | Used by subclass constructors via `super` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ABSTRACT CLASS Shape"] --> B["CONCRETE CLASS Circle"]
    A --> C["CONCRETE CLASS Rectangle"]
    A --> D["ABSTRACT CLASS Polygon"]
    D --> E["CONCRETE CLASS Triangle"]
    D --> F["CONCRETE CLASS Pentagon"]
    
    A1["name: String"] -.-> A
    A2["display() : void"] -.-> A
    A3["area() : double [ABSTRACT]"] -.-> A
    A4["perimeter() : double [ABSTRACT]"] -.-> A
    
    B1["radius: double"] -.-> B
    B2["area() = PI * r * r"] -.-> B
    B3["perimeter() = 2 * PI * r"] -.-> B
    
    C1["length: double"] -.-> C
    C2["width: double"] -.-> C
    C3["area() = length * width"] -.-> C
    C4["perimeter() = 2 times length + width"] -.-> C
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style D fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Orange boxes** = abstract classes (Shape, Polygon)
- **Green boxes** = concrete classes (Circle, Rectangle, Triangle, Pentagon)
- **Dotted arrows** = membership (fields/methods belong to the class)

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow — How a Concrete Subclass is Born

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["STEP 1: Declare abstract class Shape"] --> stepB["STEP 2: Add concrete methods display"]
    stepB --> stepC["STEP 3: Add abstract methods area and perimeter"]
    stepC --> stepD["STEP 4: Subclass Circle extends Shape"]
    stepD --> stepE{"STEP 5: Did subclass implement ALL abstract methods?"}
    stepE -- YES --> stepF["STEP 6: Class Circle is CONCRETE and instantiable"]
    stepE -- NO --> stepG["STEP 7: Subclass must itself be declared abstract"]
    stepG --> stepD
    
    style stepA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style stepC fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style stepF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style stepG fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
```

---

## 4.3 Memory & Reference Diagram — Polymorphic Dispatch

```mermaid
graph LR
    ref1["Shape ref1 [Stack]"] --> obj1["Circle object: radius=5.0 [Heap]"]
    ref2["Shape ref2 [Stack]"] --> obj2["Rectangle object: length=4, width=6 [Heap]"]
    ref3["Shape ref3 [Stack]"] -.-> err["COMPILE ERROR: Cannot instantiate Shape directly"]
    
    call1["ref1.area()"] --> disp1["Runtime: Circle.area() invoked"]
    call2["ref2.area()"] --> disp2["Runtime: Rectangle.area() invoked"]
    
    style ref1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style ref2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style ref3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style err fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style disp1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style disp2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

**Key insight:** The **reference type** on the stack is `Shape` (the abstract type), but the **actual object** on the heap is a `Circle` or `Rectangle`. When `area()` is called, the JVM looks at the **heap object**, not the stack reference, to decide which implementation to run.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define an abstract class. List any two characteristics of abstract classes.**

**Model Answer:**

An **abstract class** in Java is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that cannot be instantiated and may contain abstract (unimplemented) methods. It serves as a partially implemented template for subclasses.

**Two characteristics:**
1. **Cannot be instantiated:** An abstract class cannot create objects directly using the `new` keyword; only its concrete subclasses can.
2. **May contain abstract methods:** It can declare methods without implementation, which **must** be overridden by any concrete subclass extending it.

Additionally, an abstract class may also contain concrete methods, instance variables, constructors, and static methods.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What happens if a concrete subclass does not implement all abstract methods inherited from an abstract superclass?**

**Model Answer:**

If a concrete subclass fails to implement **all** abstract methods inherited from its abstract superclass, the compiler raises a **compile-time error** stating that the subclass must either:
1. **Implement** all remaining abstract methods, **or**
2. Declare itself as an `abstract` class using the `abstract` keyword, which defers the implementation responsibility further down the inheritance chain.

Implementation cannot be postponed indefinitely — at least one class in the chain **must be concrete** for objects to be created.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of abstract classes in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the rules that must be followed while using abstract classes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to create an abstract class `Employee` with abstract method `calculateSalary()`. Create two concrete subclasses `PermanentEmployee` and `ContractEmployee` that override `calculateSalary()` with their own logic and demonstrate polymorphism using an abstract class reference.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Explanation of Abstract Classes

**Concept:**
An abstract class in Java is a class declared using the `abstract` keyword. It is used when we want to provide a **common interface and shared code** for a group of related classes, but the actual implementation of certain methods is **deferred to the subclasses**. Abstract classes sit **between regular classes and interfaces** in the inheritance hierarchy — they can have both abstract and concrete members.

**Rules for Abstract Classes:**

1. The class must be declared with the `abstract` keyword.
2. **Direct instantiation** is not allowed (`new AbstractClass()` is a compile-time error).
3. Abstract methods have **no body** — they end with a semicolon.
4. A class with even one abstract method **must** be declared abstract.
5. Abstract classes **can** have constructors, which are called when a subclass is instantiated.
6. Abstract classes **can** have concrete (fully implemented) methods, static methods, and instance variables.
7. The `final` and `abstract` modifiers **cannot be combined** (they are contradictory).
8. The `private` and `abstract` modifiers **cannot be combined** for the same method.
9. Abstract classes can be **extended by another abstract class**, deferring implementation further.
10. The first concrete subclass in the inheritance chain **must** implement all inherited abstract methods.

**Example (concise):**

```java
abstract class Animal {
    String name;
    Animal(String name) { this.name = name; }
    abstract void sound();   // abstract method
    void sleep() {           // concrete method
        System.out.println(name + " is sleeping");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    Dog() { super("Dog"); }
    void sound() { System.out.println("Bark"); }
}
```

#### Part (b) — Employee Hierarchy Program

```java
abstract class Employee {
    protected String name;
    protected int id;
    
    public Employee(String name, int id) {
        this.name = name;
        this.id = id;
    }
    
    public abstract double calculateSalary();
    
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("ID: " + id + ", Name: " + name);
    }
}

class PermanentEmployee extends Employee {
    private double basicPay;
    private double hra;
    
    public PermanentEmployee(String name, int id, double basicPay, double hra) {
        super(name, id);
        this.basicPay = basicPay;
        this.hra = hra;
    }
    
    @Override
    public double calculateSalary() {
        return basicPay + hra + (0.10 * basicPay);  // basic + HRA + 10% DA
    }
}

class ContractEmployee extends Employee {
    private double hourlyRate;
    private int hoursWorked;
    
    public ContractEmployee(String name, int id, double hourlyRate, int hoursWorked) {
        super(name, id);
        this.hourlyRate = hourlyRate;
        this.hoursWorked = hoursWorked;
    }
    
    @Override
    public double calculateSalary() {
        return hourlyRate * hoursWorked;
    }
}

public class EmployeeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Polymorphic references — abstract type holding concrete objects
        Employee e1 = new PermanentEmployee("Anand", 101, 50000, 15000);
        Employee e2 = new ContractEmployee("Beena", 102, 500, 160);
        
        e1.display();
        System.out.println("Permanent Salary = " + e1.calculateSalary());
        
        e2.display();
        System.out.println("Contract Salary = " + e2.calculateSalary());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
ID: 101, Name: Anand
Permanent Salary = 70000.0
ID: 102, Name: Beena
Contract Salary = 80000.0
```

**Explanation of polymorphism:** The reference variables `e1` and `e2` are of type `Employee` (abstract), but they hold `PermanentEmployee` and `ContractEmployee` objects respectively. When `calculateSalary()` is called, the JVM uses **dynamic method dispatch** to invoke the correct overridden implementation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **abstract classes and interfaces** in Java with at least 6 points. When would you prefer one over the other?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design an abstract class `BankAccount` with fields `accountNumber`, `balance`, and abstract methods `deposit()` and `withdraw()`. Implement two concrete subclasses `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` with appropriate rules and demonstrate their use.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Abstract Class vs Interface

| # | Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` |
| 2 | Method types | Abstract + concrete | Abstract (pre-Java 8), default + static (Java 8+) |
| 3 | Variables | Instance + static + final | Only `public static final` (constants) |
| 4 | Constructor | Allowed | **Not allowed** |
| 5 | Multiple inheritance | A class can extend only one abstract class | A class can implement **multiple** interfaces |
| 6 | Access modifiers | Any (public, protected, private) | Methods are implicitly `public` |
| 7 | When to use | Shared code + shared interface for closely related classes | Contract/role for unrelated classes |
| 8 | State | Can hold non-final state | Cannot hold state (only constants) |

**When to prefer which:**
- Use an **abstract class** when subclasses share a **common state and behaviour** (e.g., `Shape` with `color` field and `display()` method).
- Use an **interface** when you want to define a **role/contract** across unrelated classes (e.g., `Serializable`, `Comparable`).

#### Part (b) — BankAccount Hierarchy

```java
abstract class BankAccount {
    protected String accountNumber;
    protected double balance;
    
    public BankAccount(String accountNumber, double balance) {
        this.accountNumber = accountNumber;
        this.balance = balance;
    }
    
    public abstract void deposit(double amount);
    public abstract void withdraw(double amount);
    
    public void displayBalance() {
        System.out.println("Account " + accountNumber + " Balance: " + balance);
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    private static final double MIN_BALANCE = 1000.0;
    
    public SavingsAccount(String accNo, double balance) {
        super(accNo, balance);
    }
    
    @Override
    public void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0) {
            balance += amount;
            System.out.println("Deposited " + amount + " to Savings");
        }
    }
    
    @Override
    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (balance - amount >= MIN_BALANCE) {
            balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("Withdrew " + amount + " from Savings");
        } else {
            System.out.println("Withdrawal denied: minimum balance violated");
        }
    }
}

class CurrentAccount extends BankAccount {
    private static final double OVERDRAFT_LIMIT = 5000.0;
    
    public CurrentAccount(String accNo, double balance) {
        super(accNo, balance);
    }
    
    @Override
    public void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0) {
            balance += amount;
            System.out.println("Deposited " + amount + " to Current");
        }
    }
    
    @Override
    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (balance - amount >= -OVERDRAFT_LIMIT) {
            balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("Withdrew " + amount + " from Current");
        } else {
            System.out.println("Withdrawal denied: overdraft limit exceeded");
        }
    }
}

public class BankDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount sa = new SavingsAccount("SA001", 5000);
        BankAccount ca = new CurrentAccount("CA001", 2000);
        
        sa.deposit(2000);
        sa.withdraw(5500);   // denied — breaks minimum balance
        sa.withdraw(3000);   // allowed
        sa.displayBalance();
        
        ca.withdraw(6500);   // allowed — within overdraft
        ca.displayBalance();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Deposited 2000.0 to Savings
Withdrawal denied: minimum balance violated
Withdrew 3000.0 from Savings
Account SA001 Balance: 4000.0
Withdrew 6500.0 from Current
Account CA001 Balance: -4500.0
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `abstract` keyword on the class header** when it contains abstract methods → compile-time error, full marks lost.
> 2. **Adding `{}` to an abstract method** (e.g., `abstract void area() {}`) → syntax error. Abstract methods end with a **semicolon only**.
> 3. **Trying to instantiate** `new AbstractClass()` in the driver class → marks deducted.
> 4. **Not calling `super()`** in the subclass constructor → implicit super call happens, but writing it explicitly is the KTU-preferred style and earns full marks.
