> 5. **Writing `final abstract` or `private abstract`** → contradictory modifiers, never allowed.
> 6. **Confusing abstract classes with interfaces** in comparison questions — remember: abstract classes **can have constructors and concrete methods**; interfaces traditionally cannot.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Abstract class** = declared with `abstract` keyword, **cannot be instantiated**, may contain abstract and concrete members.
- **Abstract method** = method signature **without a body**, ends with `;`, must be overridden by concrete subclass.
- Abstract classes **can have constructors** — they are invoked when subclass objects are created via `super()`.
- A concrete subclass **must implement all inherited abstract methods**, or declare itself as `abstract`.
- **Polymorphism**: An abstract class reference can hold a concrete subclass object; method calls are resolved at runtime via **dynamic method dispatch**.
- **`final` and `abstract` cannot be combined** — they are mutually exclusive modifiers.
- **`private` and `abstract` cannot be combined** for the same method — abstract methods must be visible to subclasses.
- **Abstract classes vs interfaces**: Use abstract class for *shared code + state*; use interface for *contracts/roles* across unrelated classes.
- A class can extend **only one** abstract class (single inheritance), but can implement **multiple** interfaces.
- Abstract classes support all access modifiers (`public`, `protected`, `private`); interface methods are implicitly `public`.
- Static methods in an abstract class are **allowed** but **cannot** be declared `abstract` (static methods are not overridden).
- Abstract classes can contain **instance variables, static variables, and final variables** — interfaces only allow `public static final` constants.
- The `@Override` annotation is **best practice** when implementing abstract methods — it provides compile-time safety.
- Common KTU examples: `Shape` → `Circle`, `Rectangle`; `Employee` → `Permanent`, `Contract`; `BankAccount` → `Savings`, `Current`.
- The most common KTU exam pattern is: **define abstract class → declare abstract methods → create 2-3 concrete subclasses → demonstrate polymorphism in a driver class**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfaces. OOP Concepts - Data Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Procedural and Object Oriented Programming Paradigm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interfaces. OOP Concepts - Data Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Procedural and Object Oriented Programming Paradigm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Programming Paradigms

A **programming paradigm** is a fundamental style or approach to structuring code and solving problems using a programming language. The two most widely adopted paradigms in engineering software development are the **Procedural Programming Paradigm** and the **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) Paradigm**.

### Procedural Programming Paradigm

**Formal Definition:** The procedural programming paradigm is a programming style derived from structured programming, based upon the concept of **procedure calls**, where statements are organized into **functions** (procedures) that operate on data structures. The primary focus is on the sequence of actions to be performed, where data is treated as a passive entity that flows through procedures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Highlight:** In procedural programming, **functions and data are separate entities**. Data is typically global, and functions operate on that data, often leading to security and maintenance issues in large-scale software systems.

**Conceptual Analogy (Real-World):** Think of procedural programming as an **old-style government office** where files (data) are stored in a central registry, and clerks (functions) come to the registry, pick up files, modify them, and put them back. Anyone can access any file, and there is no restriction on who can read or modify what. This causes chaos, security issues, and difficulty in tracking changes.

### Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) Paradigm

**Formal Definition:** Object-Oriented Programming is a programming paradigm based on the concept of **objects**, which can contain **data** (in the form of fields, also called attributes or properties) and **code** (in the form of procedures, also called methods). The primary focus is on modeling real-world entities as objects that bundle together both state (data) and behavior (methods), thereby promoting modularity, reusability, and maintainability.

**Conceptual Analogy (Real-World):** OOP is like a **modern smart home**. Each appliance (object) — the air conditioner, refrigerator, washing machine — is a self-contained unit. It has its own internal data (temperature settings, spin speed) and its own methods (cool, wash). You, the user, only interact with a clean, well-defined interface (the control panel). You don't need to know the complex internal wiring. The system is modular, secure, and easy to upgrade.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core OOP Pillars (as per KTU 2024 Syllabus):** The four foundational principles of OOP are:
> 1. **Data Abstraction** – Hiding complex implementation, showing only essential features.
> 2. **Encapsulation** – Bundling data and methods that operate on that data within a single unit (class), and restricting direct access.
> 3. **Inheritance** – Mechanism by which a new class acquires properties and behavior of an existing class.
> 4. **Polymorphism** – Ability of objects of different types to respond to the same method call in different ways.

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of OOP — Formal Definitions

### 1.2.1 Data Abstraction

**Formal Definition:** Data Abstraction is the OOP principle of representing the **essential features of an object** without including the background details or explanations. It exposes only the relevant attributes and methods to the outside world while hiding the complex internal implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Terminology:** In Java, abstraction is achieved through **abstract classes** and **interfaces**. The keyword `abstract` is used to declare incomplete classes/methods that must be overridden by subclasses.

**Conceptual Analogy:** When you drive a car, you use the steering wheel, accelerator, and brake (the **abstract interface**). You don't need to understand the internal combustion engine, fuel injection system, or brake hydraulics (the **complex implementation**). Data abstraction works exactly the same way — you see *what* an object does, not *how* it does it.

### 1.2.2 Encapsulation

**Formal Definition:** Encapsulation is the mechanism of **wrapping the data (variables)** and the **code (methods)** that operates on the data into a single unit called a **class**. It also involves **restricting direct access** to some of an object's components, which is achieved through **access specifiers** (private, protected, public) — a technique known as **data hiding**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Consider a **medical capsule** (the word itself comes from "encapsulation"). The medicine powder (data) is wrapped inside a protective shell (the class). You cannot directly touch the powder — you must consume the capsule as a whole. The shell protects the contents and ensures they are delivered safely. Similarly, encapsulation in Java protects data from unauthorized access and ensures it is used only through well-defined methods (getters/setters).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Encapsulation = **Data Hiding + Data Binding**. Binding refers to bundling data and methods together; Hiding refers to using access specifiers (especially `private`) to control visibility.

### 1.2.3 Inheritance

**Formal Definition:** Inheritance is the OOP mechanism by which a new class (called the **subclass**, **derived class**, or **child class**) is created from an existing class (called the **superclass**, **base class**, or **parent class**). The subclass inherits the attributes and methods of the superclass, and can additionally define its own unique features or override inherited ones. In Java, this is achieved using the `extends` keyword.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Inheritance is exactly like **biological inheritance** between parent and child. A child inherits physical traits (eye color, height) and behavioral patterns from the parent but also develops unique characteristics of their own. In Java, a `Car` class might inherit from a `Vehicle` class — it gets `speed`, `fuelType` from the parent, but adds `numDoors`, `trunkCapacity` of its own.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Keyword:** Java uses `extends` for class inheritance. **Single inheritance only** for classes (a class can extend only one parent class), but **multiple inheritance is allowed through interfaces** (a class can `implements` multiple interfaces).

### 1.2.4 Polymorphism

**Formal Definition:** Polymorphism (from Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) is the ability of a **single interface** (method name) to represent **different underlying forms (data types or behaviors)**. In OOP, polymorphism allows one interface to be used for a general class of actions, with the specific action determined by the exact nature (type) of the situation. In Java, polymorphism is achieved via **method overloading** (compile-time/static polymorphism) and **method overriding** (run-time/dynamic polymorphism).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Consider the word **"open"**. You can *open a door*, *open a book*, *open a bank account*, *open a meeting*. The same word ("open") takes on entirely different meanings and actions depending on the object it is applied to. This is polymorphism in action — one name, many forms. In Java, a single method name `draw()` can produce different output when called on a `Circle` object vs. a `Square` object.

## 1.3 Interfaces in Java

**Formal Definition:** An **interface** in Java is a **completely abstract type** that is used to specify a *contract* that classes must fulfill. An interface contains only **abstract methods** (prior to Java 8) and **constants** (static final variables). A class that wishes to adopt the contract defined by an interface must `implements` it and provide concrete implementations for all of its abstract methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Keyword:** Interfaces support **multiple inheritance in Java** because a class can `implements` multiple interfaces, which is not possible with class `extends` (single inheritance limitation).

**Conceptual Analogy:** An interface is like a **job description** or **role contract**. A company publishes a contract: "Anyone who takes this role MUST be able to `compile()`, `execute()`, and `debug()`." Different people (classes) can sign this contract, and each may implement these abilities in their own way. The contract (interface) doesn't care *how* they do it, only that they can.

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Methods (Java 8+):** From Java 8 onwards, interfaces can also contain **default methods** (concrete methods with `default` keyword) and **static methods**. From Java 9, they can also contain **private methods** for internal helper use. This was introduced to support backward compatibility when new methods are added to existing interfaces.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

## 2.1 Procedural vs. Object-Oriented Programming — Deep Dive

The fundamental difference between these two paradigms lies in their **primary unit of organization** and **approach to problem-solving**.

### Procedural Programming — Operational Steps

1. **Decompose** the problem into a set of tasks/procedures (functions).
2. **Identify** the data structures needed to support these procedures.
3. **Write** the functions that operate on the data structures.
4. **Pass** data structures to functions as parameters.
5. **Execute** the program by calling these functions in a logical sequence.

**Drawbacks of Procedural Programming:**
- Data and functions are separate, leading to **data insecurity** (any function can modify global data).
- **Code reusability** is limited — copying and pasting functions is often necessary.
- **Maintenance** becomes difficult as project size grows (the "spaghetti code" problem).
- Modeling real-world scenarios is awkward because real-world entities combine data + behavior.

### Object-Oriented Programming — Operational Steps

1. **Identify** the objects/entities in the problem domain.
2. **Define** the data (attributes) and behavior (methods) of each object.
3. **Organize** objects into classes (blueprints).
4. **Establish** relationships between classes (inheritance, association).
5. **Instantiate** objects and have them interact by calling each other's methods.

**Advantages of OOP:**
- **Modularity:** Classes are self-contained modules.
- **Reusability:** Inheritance allows code reuse.
- **Extensibility:** New features can be added via inheritance/polymorphism.
- **Maintainability:** Bugs are localized to specific classes.
- **Modeling Power:** Real-world entities map naturally to objects.

## 2.2 The Four Pillars — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Data Abstraction — Layered Analysis

**WHY it is needed:**
- To **reduce complexity** by hiding implementation details.
- To **protect code** from unintended misuse by other developers.
- To provide a **clear contract** for what an object does (not how).

**HOW it is achieved in Java:**
- **Abstract Classes:** A class declared with the `abstract` keyword. Can have both abstract (unimplemented) and concrete (implemented) methods. Cannot be instantiated.
- **Interfaces:** A pure abstract type (pre-Java 8) with only abstract methods and constants. From Java 8, can have default and static methods.

**Two Levels of Abstraction:**
1. **Data Abstraction:** Hiding data implementation details (e.g., a `Stack` class hides how it uses an array internally).
2. **Control Abstraction (Procedural Abstraction):** Hiding the implementation of operations/methods (e.g., `sort()` method hides the sorting algorithm used).

### 2.2.2 Encapsulation — Layered Analysis

**WHY it is needed:**
- **Security:** Prevents unauthorized access and modification of data.
- **Flexibility:** The internal implementation can change without affecting external code.
- **Maintainability:** Data and behavior are co-located, making code easier to understand.

**HOW it is achieved in Java:**
1. Declare class variables as `private` (data hiding).
2. Provide `public` getter and setter methods to access and modify the private data (controlled access).
3. Optionally add validation logic inside setters to enforce business rules.

**Access Specifiers in Java:**

| Specifier   | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (diff package) | Other Classes |
| :---------- | :--------: | :----------: | :---------------------: | :-----------: |
| `private`   |     ✓      |      ✗       |            ✗            |       ✗       |
| (default)   |     ✓      |      ✓       |            ✗            |       ✐       |
| `protected` |     ✓      |      ✓       |            ✓            |       ✐       |
| `public`    |     ✓      |      ✓       |            ✓            |       ✓       |

> [!NOTE]
> **✓ = Accessible, ✗ = Not Accessible, ✐ = Accessible only if class is in same package (for default) or via inheritance (for protected).**

### 2.2.3 Inheritance — Layered Analysis

**WHY it is needed:**
- **Code Reusability:** Common code in parent class is reused by child classes.
- **Method Overriding:** Child classes can customize inherited behavior.
- **Hierarchical Classification:** Models "is-a" relationships naturally.

**HOW it is achieved in Java:**
- Use the `extends` keyword: `class Child extends Parent { ... }`
- The child class inherits all accessible members (fields and methods) of the parent.
- Java supports **single inheritance** for classes, but **multilevel inheritance** and **hierarchical inheritance** are fully supported.

**Types of Inheritance in Java:**

| Type                  | Supported in Java? | Mechanism               |
| :-------------------- | :----------------: | :---------------------- |
| Single                |        Yes         | `class B extends A`     |
| Multilevel            |        Yes         | `C extends B, B extends A` |
| Hierarchical          |        Yes         | B,C both extend A       |
| Multiple (Classes)    |     **No**         | Not allowed             |
| Multiple (Interfaces) |    **Yes**         | `class C implements I1, I2` |
| Hybrid                |     **Yes**        | Mix of class + interfaces|

### 2.2.4 Polymorphism — Layered Analysis

**WHY it is needed:**
- **Flexibility:** One interface, many implementations.
- **Extensibility:** New classes can be added without modifying existing code (Open/Closed Principle).
- **Simplification:** Reduces complexity by using a single, unified interface.

**HOW it is achieved in Java:**

**Type 1: Compile-Time Polymorphism (Static Binding / Early Binding)**
- Achieved via **Method Overloading**.
- Multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists** (number, type, or order of parameters) within the same class.
- The compiler determines which method to call at compile time.

**Type 2: Run-Time Polymorphism (Dynamic Binding / Late Binding)**
- Achieved via **Method Overriding**.
- A subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its superclass.
- The JVM determines which method to call at runtime based on the actual object type.
- Requires **inheritance** and **upcasting** (parent reference, child object).

## 2.3 Interfaces — Theoretical Deep Dive

**WHY Interfaces are needed:**
- To achieve **full abstraction** (pre-Java 8).
- To enable **multiple inheritance** in Java (a class can `implements` multiple interfaces).
- To define a **contract** that multiple unrelated classes can adopt.
- To support **loose coupling** in software design.

**Key Rules of Interfaces (as per KTU 2024 syllabus):**
1. All methods in an interface are **implicitly `public` and `abstract`** (pre-Java 8).
2. All variables in an interface are **implicitly `public`, `static`, and `final`** (constants).
3. A class `implements` an interface (does NOT `extend` it).
4. A class must implement **ALL** abstract methods of the interface, OR be declared `abstract` itself.
5. An interface can `extend` another interface (using `extends`, not `implements`).
6. From Java 8: interfaces can have `default` and `static` methods (with bodies).
7. From Java 9: interfaces can have `private` methods.
8. Interfaces **cannot be instantiated** directly.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept            | Procedural                                              | Object-Oriented                                              |
| :----------------- | :------------------------------------------------------ | :----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Primary Unit       | Function / Procedure                                    | Class / Object                                               |
| Data Handling      | Data is separate from functions                         | Data and functions bundled in objects                        |
| Data Access        | Global data, freely accessible                          | Restricted via access specifiers (Encapsulation)             |
| Code Reusability   | Limited (function libraries)                            | High (Inheritance)                                           |
| Approach           | Top-down                                                | Bottom-up                                                    |
| Extensibility      | Difficult                                               | Easy (Polymorphism, Inheritance)                             |
| Modeling           | Doesn't mirror real world                               | Mirrors real-world entities                                  |
| Security           | Low (data is exposed)                                  | High (data hiding)                                           |
| Examples           | C, Pascal, Fortran                                      | Java, C++, Python (OOP mode)                                 |

| OOP Pillar         | Definition                                              | Java Mechanism                                               |
| :----------------- | :------------------------------------------------------ | :----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Abstraction        | Hiding implementation, showing interface                | `abstract` classes, `interface`                              |
| Encapsulation      | Bundling data + methods, restricting access             | `private` fields, `public` getters/setters                   |
| Inheritance        | Acquiring properties from parent class                  | `extends` (classes), `implements` (interfaces)               |
| Polymorphism       | One name, many forms                                    | Method overloading (compile-time), overriding (runtime)      |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Code Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrices

## 3.1 Exhaustive Java Code Example: Encapsulation

The following Java class `Student` demonstrates encapsulation in its purest form — the data is hidden, and access is provided only through controlled methods.

```java
// File: Student.java
public class Student {
    // Step 1: Declare data members as PRIVATE (data hiding)
    private String name;
    private int rollNo;
    private double marks;
    
    // Step 2: Provide a public constructor to initialize data
    public Student(String name, int rollNo, double marks) {
        this.name = name;
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.marks = marks;
    }
    
    // Step 3: Provide public GETTER methods (read-only access)
    public String getName() {
        return name;
    }
    
    public int getRollNo() {
        return rollNo;
    }
    
    public double getMarks() {
        return marks;
    }
    
    // Step 4: Provide public SETTER methods (controlled write access)
    public void setMarks(double marks) {
        // Validation logic enforces business rules
        if (marks >= 0 && marks <= 100) {
            this.marks = marks;
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid marks! Must be 0-100.");
        }
    }
    
    // Step 5: Business logic method
    public String calculateGrade() {
        if (marks >= 90) return "A+";
        else if (marks >= 80) return "A";
        else if (marks >= 70) return "B";
        else return "C";
    }
}
```

**Explanation of each step:**
- **Step 1:** The `private` keyword ensures these variables cannot be accessed directly from outside the class. This is the **Data Hiding** component of encapsulation.
- **Step 2:** The constructor initializes the object with valid data, ensuring an object is never created in an inconsistent state.
- **Step 3:** Getters provide **read-only** access to the data. Notice there is no `setName()` or `setRollNo()` — the name and roll number are immutable after creation.
- **Step 4:** Setters include **validation logic**. A student cannot be assigned negative marks or marks above 100. This is the **Data Binding** benefit — the validation logic lives in the same class as the data.
- **Step 5:** The `calculateGrade()` method is a piece of business logic that operates on the data. Bundling it with the data itself is what makes this approach OOP rather than procedural.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Java Code Example: Inheritance

The following code derives a `Manager` class from an `Employee` class, demonstrating single inheritance and method overriding.

```java
// File: Employee.java (Parent/Superclass)
class Employee {
    protected String name;
    protected int empId;
    protected double baseSalary;
    
    public Employee(String name, int empId, double baseSalary) {
        this.name = name;
        this.empId = empId;
        this.baseSalary = baseSalary;
    }
    
    public void displayDetails() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);
        System.out.println("ID: " + empId);
        System.out.println("Base Salary: " + baseSalary);
    }
    
    public double calculateSalary() {
        return baseSalary;
    }
}

// File: Manager.java (Child/Subclass)
class Manager extends Employee {
    private double bonus;
    
    public Manager(String name, int empId, double baseSalary, double bonus) {
        super(name, empId, baseSalary);  // Calls parent constructor
        this.bonus = bonus;
    }
    
    // Method Overriding (Runtime Polymorphism)
    @Override
    public double calculateSalary() {
        return super.calculateSalary() + bonus;
    }
    
    @Override
    public void displayDetails() {
        super.displayDetails();
        System.out.println("Bonus: " + bonus);
    }
}
```

**Line-by-Line Analytical Breakdown:**

1. `protected String name;` — The `protected` access specifier allows subclasses (even in different packages) to access this field, while still hiding it from unrelated classes.

2. `class Manager extends Employee` — The `extends` keyword establishes the inheritance relationship. `Manager` inherits all non-private members of `Employee`.

3. `super(name, empId, baseSalary);` — The `super()` call invokes the parent class constructor. This must be the **first statement** in the child constructor. If omitted, the compiler automatically inserts a no-argument `super()` call.

4. `@Override` — This is an **annotation** (not a keyword). It tells the compiler that we intend to override a parent method. If the parent method signature doesn't match, the compiler produces an error. This is a best practice for catching bugs at compile time.

5. `return super.calculateSalary() + bonus;` — The `super.calculateSalary()` call invokes the **parent's version** of the method, then we add the manager-specific bonus. Without `super.`, it would lead to infinite recursion.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Java Code Example: Polymorphism (Overloading & Overriding)

```java
// File: Calculator.java - Demonstrates Method Overloading
class Calculator {
    // Overloaded add() methods - same name, different parameters
    
    public int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }
    
    public double add(double a, double b) {
        return a + b;
    }
    
    public int add(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }
    
    public String add(String a, String b) {
        return a + b;
    }
}

// File: Shape.java - Demonstrates Runtime Polymorphism
class Shape {
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
    
    public double area() {
        return 0.0;
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    private double radius;
    
    public Circle(double radius) {
        this.radius = radius;
    }
    
    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle");
    }
    
    @Override
    public double area() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}

class Square extends Shape {
    private double side;
    
    public Square(double side) {
        this.side = side;
    }
    
    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Square");
    }
    
    @Override
    public double area() {
        return side * side;
    }
}
```

**Runtime Polymorphism Demonstration:**

```java
// File: Main.java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Parent reference, child objects (Upcasting)
        Shape s1 = new Circle(5.0);
        Shape s2 = new Square(4.0);
        
        // JVM determines which draw() to call at RUNTIME
        s1.draw();   // Output: Drawing a Circle
        s2.draw();   // Output: Drawing a Square
        
        System.out.println("Circle area: " + s1.area());
        System.out.println("Square area: " + s2.area());
    }
}
```

**Analytical Matrix for Polymorphism Types:**

| Property                | Compile-Time (Overloading)                        | Run-Time (Overriding)                            |
| :---------------------- | :------------------------------------------------ | :----------------------------------------------- |
| Binding                 | Static (Early Binding)                            | Dynamic (Late Binding)                           |
| When resolved           | At compile time by the compiler                   | At runtime by the JVM                            |
| Requires Inheritance?   | No (can occur within a single class)              | Yes (requires parent-child relationship)         |
| Method Signature        | Must differ (params, type, order)                 | Must be exactly the same                         |
| Return Type             | Can be the same or different                      | Must be the same or covariant (since Java 5)     |
| Access Specifier        | Can be any                                        | Cannot be more restrictive than parent           |
| Performance             | Slightly faster (no runtime lookup)               | Slightly slower (runtime method table lookup)    |
| Flexibility             | Less                                              | More (enables Open/Closed Principle)             |
| Polymorphism Type       | Ad-hoc Polymorphism                               | Subtype (Inclusion) Polymorphism                 |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Java Code Example: Interface

```java
// File: Drawable.java - Interface definition
interface Drawable {
    // Implicitly public, static, final
    int DEFAULT_PEN_WIDTH = 1;
    
    // Implicitly public and abstract
    void draw();
    void resize(double factor);
    
    // Java 8+ default method
    default void describe() {
        System.out.println("This is a drawable object with pen width: " + DEFAULT_PEN_WIDTH);
    }
    
    // Java 8+ static method
    static Drawable createCircle() {
        return new Circle2D();
    }
}

// File: Circle2D.java - Class implementing the interface
class Circle2D implements Drawable {
    private double radius = 1.0;
    
    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing Circle2D with radius " + radius);
    }
    
    @Override
    public void resize(double factor) {
        radius *= factor;
        System.out.println("Resized. New radius: " + radius);
    }
}

// File: TestInterface.java
public class TestInterface {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Drawable d = new Circle2D();  // Interface reference, implementing class object
        d.draw();
        d.resize(2.0);
        d.describe();
        
        // Accessing static constant
        System.out.println("Default pen width: " + Drawable.DEFAULT_PEN_WIDTH);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. `interface Drawable { ... }` — Declares a new interface. Note: `class` keyword is NOT used; we use `interface`.

2. `int DEFAULT_PEN_WIDTH = 1;` — This is **implicitly** `public static final`. Even if you don't write these keywords, the compiler adds them. It is a **constant** — its value cannot be changed.

3. `void draw();` — This is **implicitly** `public abstract`. It has no body (no `{}`). It is a contract — any class that `implements Drawable` MUST provide a body for `draw()`.

4. `default void describe() { ... }` — A **default method** (Java 8+). It has a body, so implementing classes are NOT required to override it. This feature was added to allow new methods to be added to interfaces without breaking existing implementing classes.

5. `static Drawable createCircle() { ... }` — A **static method** in an interface. It is called using the interface name: `Drawable.createCircle()`.

6. `class Circle2D implements Drawable` — The class uses `implements` (not `extends`) to adopt the interface contract. It must provide implementations for both `draw()` and `resize()`.

7. `Drawable d = new Circle2D();` — **Interface reference variable** holding a `Circle2D` object. This is a form of polymorphism — we use the interface type to refer to any class that implements it.

## 3.5 Exhaustive Code Example: Abstract Class vs. Interface

```java
// Abstract class - partial abstraction
abstract class Vehicle {
    String brand;
    
    Vehicle(String brand) {
        this.brand = brand;
    }
    
    // Concrete method
    void showBrand() {
        System.out.println("Brand: " + brand);
    }
    
    // Abstract method - must be implemented by subclass
    abstract void startEngine();
    abstract void stopEngine();
}

// Interface - full abstraction (pre-Java 8)
interface Refuelable {
    void refuel();
    int MAX_TANK_CAPACITY = 50;  // Litre
}

// Car extends abstract class AND implements interface
class Car extends Vehicle implements Refuelable {
    Car(String brand) {
        super(brand);
    }
    
    @Override
    void startEngine() {
        System.out.println("Car engine started with key");
    }
    
    @Override
    void stopEngine() {
        System.out.println("Car engine stopped");
    }
    
    @Override
    public void refuel() {
        System.out.println("Refuelling car with petrol");
    }
}
```

**Analytical Matrix: Abstract Class vs. Interface (KTU 2024 High-Yield Table)**

| Property                      | Abstract Class                                    | Interface                                              |
| :---------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------ | :----------------------------------------------------- |
| Keyword                       | `abstract class`                                  | `interface`                                            |
| Methods                       | Can have both abstract and concrete               | All abstract (pre-Java 8); can have default/static (Java 8+) |
| Variables                     | Can be any type (instance, static, final)         | Implicitly `public static final` only                  |
| Inheritance                   | `extends` (single inheritance)                    | `implements` (multiple); `extends` (between interfaces) |
| Access Specifiers             | Can use any (private, protected, etc.)            | Methods are implicitly `public`                        |
| Constructor                   | Can have constructors                             | Cannot have constructors                               |
| When to use                   | When classes share a common base and partial implementation | When you need a pure contract / multiple inheritance |
| Speed                         | Slightly faster (concrete methods can be inlined) | Slightly slower (all method lookups are indirect)      |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Encapsulation. How is it achieved in Java?  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Encapsulation** is one of the four fundamental OOP concepts. It is defined as the mechanism of **binding data (variables)** and the **code (methods)** that operate on that data into a single unit called a **class**, while also **restricting direct access** to the data from outside the class (a technique known as **data hiding**).

**How it is achieved in Java:**
1. Declare the class variables (data members) using the **`private`** access specifier. This ensures they cannot be accessed directly from outside the class.
2. Provide **`public` getter methods** to allow read-only access to the private data from outside.
3. Provide **`public` setter methods** to allow controlled modification of the private data, with optional **validation logic** inside setters to enforce business rules.

**Example skeleton:**
```java
class Account {
    private double balance;  // Step 1: data hiding
    
    public double getBalance() {  // Step 2: controlled read
        return balance;
    }
    
    public void setBalance(double balance) {  // Step 3: controlled write
        if (balance >= 0) {
            this.balance = balance;
        }
    }
}
```

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Abstraction and Encapsulation.  `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (in tabular form for full marks):**

| Parameter             | Abstraction                                          | Encapsulation                                       |
| :-------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------- |
| **Primary Goal**      | Hide implementation, show only essential features    | Bundle data and methods, restrict direct access     |
| **Focus**             | Hides **what is hidden** (complex internals)         | Hides **how it is hidden** (data and methods together) |
| **Achieved by**       | `abstract` classes, `interface`s                     | `private` variables, `public` getters/setters       |
| **What is protected** | Implementation details / internal logic              | Data (variables) of a class                          |
| **Level**             | Design-level concept (planned before coding)         | Implementation-level concept (enforced in code)     |
| **Relationship**      | Abstraction is a **design principle**                | Encapsulation is an **implementation technique**    |
| **Example**           | A `Shape` abstract class with abstract `draw()`      | A class with `private` fields and `public` methods  |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common answer is to say "both hide data." The distinction is that **abstraction hides the *complexity*** of the implementation, while **encapsulation hides the *data*** of an object from external code. They are complementary, not interchangeable.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Polymorphism in Java  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a) Explain the two types of polymorphism in Java with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Polymorphism in Java is the ability of an object to take on **many forms**. It allows a single interface (method name) to represent different underlying behaviors. Java supports two main types of polymorphism:

**1. Compile-Time Polymorphism (Static Binding / Method Overloading):**

This is resolved by the compiler at compile time. It is achieved by defining multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists** within the same class (or with subclass methods).

```java
class MathOperations {
    // Overloaded methods - differ in number of parameters
    public int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }
    
    public int add(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }
    
    // Overloaded methods - differ in type of parameters
    public double add(double a, double b) {
        return a + b;
    }
    
    public String add(String a, String b) {
        return a.concat(b);
    }
}
```

The compiler determines which `add()` to call based on the **argument list** at compile time. This is faster because no runtime lookup is required.

**2. Run-Time Polymorphism (Dynamic Binding / Method Overriding):**

This is resolved by the JVM at runtime. It occurs when a **subclass provides a specific implementation** of a method already declared in its parent class. It requires inheritance and upcasting.

```java
class Animal {
    public void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal makes a sound");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    public void sound() {
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }
}

class Cat extends Animal {
    @Override
    public void sound() {
        System.out.println("Cat meows");
    }
}

// Driver
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal a;  // Parent reference
        
        a = new Dog();
        a.sound();  // Output: Dog barks (JVM decides at runtime)
        
        a = new Cat();
        a.sound();  // Output: Cat meows (JVM decides at runtime)
    }
}
```

Here, even though the reference type is `Animal`, the JVM uses the **actual object type** (`Dog` or `Cat`) to determine which `sound()` method to invoke. This is the essence of runtime polymorphism.

---

**b) What is an interface in Java? Explain how interfaces support multiple inheritance. Write a Java program to demonstrate interface implementation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** An interface in Java is a **completely abstract type** used to specify a **contract** (a set of method signatures) that implementing classes must fulfill. It contains abstract methods (pre-Java 8) and constants. A class that wants to adopt the contract uses the `implements` keyword.

**Key Features:**
- All methods are **implicitly `public` and `abstract`** (pre-Java 8).
- All variables are **implicitly `public static final`** (constants).
- An interface **cannot be instantiated**.
- From Java 8: interfaces can have `default` and `static` methods with bodies.
- An interface can `extend` other interfaces.

**How Interfaces Support Multiple Inheritance in Java:**

Java does **not** allow a class to extend more than one class (single inheritance limitation) because of the **"Diamond Problem"** — ambiguity arises if two parent classes define the same method. However, a class can **`implements` multiple interfaces** simultaneously. This is allowed because:
1. Interfaces contain only abstract methods (no implementation conflicts at the class level).
2. The implementing class is **forced to provide its own implementation**, eliminating ambiguity.
3. Each interface defines a separate, independent contract.

**Java Program Demonstrating Interface Implementation:**

```java
// First interface
interface Printable {
    void print();
}

// Second interface (multiple inheritance)
interface Scannable {
    void scan();
}

// Class implementing both interfaces
class AllInOnePrinter implements Printable, Scannable {
    private String model;
    
    public AllInOnePrinter(String model) {
        this.model = model;
    }
    
    @Override
    public void print() {
        System.out.println(model + " is printing...");
    }
    
    @Override
    public void scan() {
        System.out.println(model + " is scanning...");
    }
}

// Driver class
public class InterfaceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        AllInOnePrinter p = new AllInOnePrinter("HP-M100");
        p.print();
        p.scan();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
HP-M100 is printing...
HP-M100 is scanning...
```

This program demonstrates that the `AllInOnePrinter` class implements **two interfaces** (`Printable` and `Scannable`), thereby achieving **multiple inheritance** in Java through interfaces.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Inheritance and OOP Paradigm  `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**a) Differentiate between Procedural and Object-Oriented Programming paradigms. List any four advantages of OOP over Procedural Programming. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Procedural vs. Object-Oriented Programming:**

| S. No. | Parameter              | Procedural Programming                          | Object-Oriented Programming                       |
| :----: | :--------------------- | :---------------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------ |
| 1      | Primary Unit           | Function / Procedure                            | Class / Object                                    |
| 2      | Data Handling          | Data is separate from functions                 | Data and methods are bundled in objects            |
| 3      | Data Access            | Global, freely accessible                       | Restricted via access specifiers (Encapsulation)   |
| 4      | Approach               | Top-down                                        | Bottom-up                                         |
| 5      | Code Reusability       | Limited (function libraries)                    | High (Inheritance)                                |
| 6      | Extensibility          | Difficult                                       | Easy (Polymorphism, Inheritance)                  |
| 7      | Real-world Modeling    | Doesn't mirror real world                       | Mirrors real-world entities                       |
| 8      | Security               | Low (data is exposed)                           | High (data hiding)                                |
| 9      | Examples               | C, Pascal, BASIC, Fortran                       | Java, C++, Python, C#                            |
| 10     | Main Focus             | On procedures/functions                        | On data with operations applied to it            |

**Four Advantages of OOP over Procedural Programming:**

1. **Modularity:** Code is organized into self-contained classes/modules, making it easier to understand, debug, and maintain.
2. **Reusability:** Through inheritance, common code in a parent class can be reused by multiple child classes, reducing redundancy.
3. **Data Security:** Encapsulation and data hiding ensure that data is protected from unauthorized access and modification.
4. **Flexibility & Extensibility:** Polymorphism allows new classes to be added with minimal changes to existing code (Open/Closed Principle), making systems easier to extend.
5. **Real-World Modeling:** Objects map naturally to real-world entities, making program design more intuitive.
6. **Reduced Maintenance Cost:** Bugs are localized to specific classes, and changes in one class don't affect others (low coupling).

---

**b) Explain the concept of inheritance in Java. Discuss the different types of inheritance supported by Java with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Inheritance:**

Inheritance is the OOP mechanism by which a new class (called the **subclass** or **child class**) is created from an existing class (called the **superclass** or **parent class**). The subclass inherits the **fields** (data) and **methods** (behavior) of the superclass and can additionally define its own unique features or override inherited ones. In Java, inheritance is achieved using the **`extends`** keyword.

**General Syntax:**
```java
class Parent {
    // parent members
}

class Child extends Parent {
    // child members (inherited + new)
}
```

**Benefits of Inheritance:**
- **Code Reusability:** Common functionality is written once in the parent and reused in children.
- **Method Overriding:** Allows runtime polymorphism and customized behavior in subclasses.
- **Hierarchical Classification:** Models "is-a" relationships naturally (e.g., a `Car` is-a `Vehicle`).

**Types of Inheritance in Java:**

**1. Single Inheritance:** One class extends another class.
```java
class A { }
class B extends A { }  // B inherits A
```
```
A (parent)
   |
   v
B (child)
```

**2. Multilevel Inheritance:** A class inherits from a class which itself inherits from another class.
```java
class A { }
class B extends A { }
class C extends B { }  // C -> B -> A
```
```
   A
   |
   v
   B
   |
   v
   C
```

**3. Hierarchical Inheritance:** Multiple classes inherit from a single parent class.
```java
class A { }
class B extends A { }
class C extends A { }
```
```
      A
     / \
    v   v
    B   C
```

**4. Multiple Inheritance (NOT supported with classes):** A class cannot extend two classes. This is to avoid the **"Diamond Problem"** where the compiler cannot decide which parent's method to use if both define the same method.
```java
// NOT ALLOWED in Java:
class A { void show() { } }
class B { void show() { } }
class C extends A, B { }  // Compile-time error
```

**5. Multiple Inheritance (ACHIEVED through Interfaces):**
```java
interface I1 { void m1(); }
interface I2 { void m2(); }
class C implements I1, I2 {  // Allowed
    public void m1() { }
    public void m2() { }
}
```

**6. Hybrid Inheritance:** A combination of multiple and multilevel inheritance, achievable in Java only through interfaces.
```
    I1      I2
     \      /
      \    /
       \  /
        C
```
Here `C` implements both `I1` and `I2`, achieving a hybrid structure without ambiguity.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Overloading with Overriding:** Overloading = same method name, **different parameters**, **same class**. Overriding = same method name, **same parameters**, **parent and child class**. Writing this backwards in a 7-mark question will lose 3-4 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation:** Not mandatory, but its absence in a code-writing question is treated as poor practice. Always include it for method overrides.
>
> 3. **Wrong keyword (`extends` vs `implements`):** Classes use `extends` (with another class), and use `implements` (with interfaces). Writing `class A extends I1` (where I1 is an interface) is a compile error.
>
> 4. **Forgetting `super()` in subclass constructors:** Java automatically inserts `super()` as the first line, but if the parent has a parameterized constructor with no default constructor, forgetting to call `super(args)` causes a compile-time error.
>
> 5. **In Encapsulation answers:** Students often write only "data hiding" and forget the "data + methods bundling" part. Both are required for full marks.
>
> 6. **In Interface answers:** Forgetting to mention that interface variables are `public static final` (constants) loses 1-2 marks. Many students think interfaces can have instance variables — this is wrong.
>
> 7. **Inheritance visibility rules:** When asked about access specifiers, students often miss that `private` members are NOT inherited (they are not accessible in subclass, although they physically exist in the parent's memory). Only `public`, `protected`, and default (package-private) members are inherited.
>
> 8. **Polymorphism answer missing "upcasting":** Runtime polymorphism REQUIRES a parent reference holding a child object. Writing `new Circle().draw()` without the parent reference does NOT demonstrate runtime polymorphism.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📌 Rapid Revision Checklist

**Procedural vs. OOP Paradigm:**
- Procedural = functions + separate data. OOP = objects bundling data + methods.
- OOP advantages: modularity, reusability, security, extensibility, real-world modeling.

**Data Abstraction:**
- Hides implementation, shows interface.
- Java tools: `abstract` keyword (abstract classes and methods), `interface`.
- Two levels: data abstraction and control (procedural) abstraction.

**Encapsulation:**
- Formula: **Encapsulation = Data Hiding + Data Binding**
- Achieved via `private` fields + `public` getters/setters.
- Benefits: security, flexibility to change internals, validation of data.
- Access specifiers: `private` (class only), default (package), `protected` (package + subclasses), `public` (everywhere).

**Inheritance:**
- Keyword: `extends` for classes, `implements` for interfaces.
- Types supported: Single, Multilevel, Hierarchical.
- Multiple inheritance: NOT with classes (Diamond Problem), YES with interfaces.
- `super()` calls parent constructor; `super.method()` calls parent method.
- `private` members are NOT inherited (not accessible to subclass).
- `@Override` annotation is best practice for overriding.

**Polymorphism:**
- Compile-time (Static): Method Overloading. Same name, different parameters. Decided by compiler.
- Run-time (Dynamic): Method Overriding. Same name, same parameters in parent-child. Decided by JVM.
- Runtime polymorphism requires: inheritance + method overriding + upcasting (parent reference, child object).
- Return type in overriding: same or covariant (since Java 5).
- Access specifier in overriding: cannot be MORE restrictive than parent.

**Interfaces:**
- Keyword: `interface` (declaration), `implements` (adoption by class), `extends` (between interfaces).
- All methods are implicitly `public abstract` (pre-Java 8).
- All variables are implicitly `public static final` (constants).
- Java 8+: can have `default` and `static` methods.
- Java 9+: can have `private` methods.
- Interfaces CANNOT be instantiated; they CAN extend other interfaces.
- Primary use: multiple inheritance, contract definition, full abstraction, loose coupling.

**Key Exam Mnemonics:**
- **"AEIP"** = Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism (the four pillars in order).
- **"IDE"** for Interface rules = Implicitly public, Default allowed (Java 8+), Effectively abstract.
- **"ER"** for Overriding rule = Exact Return type (or covariant), Reduced restrictions prohibited.

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — INTERFACES & OOP CONCEPTS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microservices. Object Oriented Programming in Java - Declaring Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Microservices. Object Oriented Programming in Java - Declaring Objects # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Microservices — Formal KTU 2024 Definition **Microservices** (also called the *Microservice Architecture*) is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of small, **loosely coupled**, **independently deployable** services. Each service is: - **Self-contained** (owns its own data and business logic) - **Single-purpose** (implements one bounded business capability) - **Independently deployable** (can be released without coordinating with other services) - **Communicates via lightweight protocols** (typically **HTTP/REST** or **messaging queues**) - **Polyglot by nature** (different services may use different programming languages, frameworks, and data stores) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Microservices is covered as an introductory architectural concept that contrasts with **Monolithic Architecture** and motivates why **Object-Oriented Programming** principles (encapsulation, modularity, loose coupling) form the foundation of modern distributed systems. ### Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen" Imagine a large restaurant: - **Monolithic Architecture** = A single giant kitchen where *one chef* prepares soup, grills meat, bakes bread, and plates desserts. If the chef is sick, the *entire* menu collapses. Adding a new dish requires reorganising the whole kitchen. - **Microservices Architecture** = A *food court* where each stall (**service**) is independent — a Sushi Stall, a Pizza Stall, a Juice Bar. Each stall has its own chef, ingredients, and POS system. If the Sushi stall closes, you can still order pizza. You can add a new "Tacos Stall" without touching anything else. The **key insight** is the same as OOP: each component should have **one well-defined responsibility** and communicate through **well-defined interfaces**. --- ## 1.2 Declaring Objects in Java — Formal Definition In Java, an **object** is a runtime **instance of a class** that occupies memory in the **heap** and holds its own state (instance variables) and behaviour (methods). **Declaring an object** is the process of creating a **reference variable** that can point to such an instance. Java requires **two distinct steps** to bring an object into existence: 1. **Declaration** — creating a reference variable (no object is created yet) 2. **Instantiation** — using the `new` operator to allocate heap memory and invoke a constructor > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU Board-Examiner Verbatim):** > *"A reference variable is a named handle that lives on the stack and stores the memory address of an object allocated on the heap. Declaring an object without instantiating it produces a reference whose value is `null`."* ### Intuitive Analogy — "The TV Remote and the TV" - **Class** = the *blueprint* of a Television (defines screen size, channels, volume range). - **Object** = an *actual TV* built from that blueprint, sitting in your living room. - **Reference Variable** = the *remote control* on your table. It is *not* the TV — it just *points* to it. - **Declaring a reference** (`Tv remote;`) = Holding an empty remote with no TV in front of it (it does nothing). - **Instantiating with `new`** (`remote = new Tv();`) = Powering ON a real TV and pointing the remote at it. The remote now controls a real object. --- ## 1.3 Why These Two Topics Are Grouped Together (KTU Context) > [!NOTE] > The grouping is **intentional** in the KTU 2024 scheme. A **microservice** is, in OOP terms, a *highly cohesive, loosely coupled object* that is deployed and scaled independently. **Declaring objects** in Java is the *lowest-level mechanism* by which we instantiate these self-contained units. Mastering object declaration is a prerequisite for understanding how microservices (autonomous objects) are *wired together* through references and interfaces. ### Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Microservices Context | Java OOP Context | |------|----------------------|-----------------| | **Component** | A service | An object | | **Interface** | REST API / Message contract | Java `interface` / method signature | | **Coupling** | Loose (via HTTP) | Loose (via references + interfaces) | | **State** | Database per service | Instance variables of an object | | **Identity** | Service ID / URL | Memory address of the object | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Microservices — Core Characteristics (The 7 Pillars) The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to enumerate and explain the following: 1. **Single Responsibility** — Each service implements **one** bounded business capability (e.g., `OrderService`, `PaymentService`). 2. **Independent Deployability** — Each service has its own CI/CD pipeline; deployment does not require rebuilding or redeploying others. 3. **Decentralized Data Management** — *No shared database*. Each service owns its data store (database per service pattern). 4. **Polyglot Programming & Persistence** — Java for one service, Python for another; MySQL for orders, MongoDB for product catalog. 5. **Smart Endpoints, Dumb Pipes** — Business logic resides *inside* services; the communication channel (HTTP, RabbitMQ, Kafka) does not contain logic. 6. **Failure Isolation (Design for Failure)** — A crash in `RecommendationService` must not bring down `CheckoutService`. Use circuit breakers (e.g., Hystrix, Resilience4j). 7. **Observability** — Centralized logging, distributed tracing (e.g., Zipkin, Jaeger), and health endpoints. ### Microservices vs Monolithic Architecture — The KTU Comparison Table | Property | Monolithic | Microservices | |----------|-----------|---------------| | **Deployment Unit** | Single WAR/EAR | Many independent services | | **Scaling** | Scale the *entire* app | Scale *only* the bottleneck service | | **Tech Stack** | Single language/framework | Heterogeneous (polyglot) | | **Fault Tolerance** | One bug can crash the whole system | Fault isolated per service | | **Team Structure** | Large coordinated teams | Small, autonomous squads (Conway's Law) | | **Startup Time** | Slow (entire app boots) | Fast (small service boots in seconds) | | **Data Layer** | Single shared database | Database-per-service | | **Communication** | In-process method calls | Network calls (REST, gRPC, MQ) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** A common mistake is to claim microservices are *always* better. They are *not* — they introduce **network latency**, **distributed transaction complexity**, and **operational overhead**. They suit **large, complex, evolving systems**, not small projects. --- ## 2.2 Declaring Objects in Java — The Three Reference Forms Java permits three syntactically distinct ways to produce a working object reference: | Form | Syntax | What Happens | Example | |------|--------|--------------|---------| | **Declaration only** | `ClassName ref;` | Stack-allocated reference with `null` value | `Student s;` | | **Declaration + Instantiation** | `ClassName ref = new ClassName();` | Single line: declare + allocate on heap | `Student s = new Student();` | | **Multi-reference to same object** | `ref2 = ref1;` | Both references point to the **same** heap object | `Student s2 = s1;` | | **Anonymous object** | `new ClassName().method();` | No reference retained; eligible for GC after use | `new Date().toString();` | ### The Two-Step Model (Board-Favourite Question) ```java Student s; // Step 1: DECLARATION s = new Student(); // Step 2: INSTANTIATION ``` - After **Step 1**: `s` is a reference variable on the **stack**, its value is `null` (the default). Accessing `s.name` here throws **`NullPointerException`**. - After **Step 2**: The `new` operator allocates memory on the **heap**, the constructor `Student()` is invoked, and the memory address is assigned to `s`. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table) | Concept | Key Point | Exam Frequency | |---------|-----------|----------------| | **`new` keyword** | Allocates heap memory + invokes constructor | ★★★★★ | | **Reference variable** | Lives on stack, holds address of heap object | ★★★★★ | | **Default value of reference** | `null` (until assigned) | ★★★★ | | **Anonymous object** | Created without a named reference | ★★★ | | **Multiple references** | `s2 = s1` makes both point to the *same* object | ★★★★★ | | **Service** | An independently deployable unit of software | ★★★★ | | **Bounded context** | The boundary of a single business responsibility | ★★★ | | **API Gateway** | Single entry point routing requests to services | ★★★ | | **Database per service** | No service may directly read another's DB | ★★★ | | **Circuit breaker** | Prevents cascading failures across services | ★★ | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Microservices** power **Netflix**, **Amazon**, **Uber**, and **Airbnb** — systems that must scale specific features (e.g., surge pricing at Uber) without redeploying the entire platform. - **Java object declaration** underpins every Java EE / Spring Boot microservice: each REST controller is an object, each `@Autowired` dependency is a reference, and the IoC container manages object lifecycles analogously to a service registry. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Memory Model ## 3.1 Microservices — Step-by-Step Request Flow (Order Processing) Consider an e-commerce checkout that uses **four microservices**. This is a classic KTU board question. **The Four Services:** 1. `OrderService` — receives the order 2. `PaymentService` — processes payment 3. `InventoryService` — reserves stock 4. `NotificationService` — emails the customer ### Step-by-Step Execution Flow **Step 1: Client initiates request** The client sends `POST /orders` to the **API Gateway** with order details (JSON payload). **Step 2: API Gateway routes** The gateway (e.g., Spring Cloud Gateway) resolves the URL `/orders` to the `OrderService` instance URL (e.g., `http://order-svc:8081/orders`). **Step 3: OrderService persists order** The `OrderService` writes a new `Order` row to its own database (`orders_db`) with status `PENDING`. It then issues a **synchronous REST call** to `PaymentService`. **Step 4: PaymentService processes** `PaymentService` charges the payment gateway, updates its `payments_db`, and returns `200 OK` with a transaction ID. **Step 5: OrderService fires async event** Upon successful payment, `OrderService` publishes an `OrderConfirmed` event to a **message broker** (Kafka topic `order-events`). **Step 6: InventoryService consumes** `InventoryService` subscribes to `order-events`, decrements stock in its own `inventory_db`, and publishes `StockReserved`. **Step 7: NotificationService consumes** `NotificationService` subscribes to the same topic (or a derived one), and sends a confirmation email. ### The Mathematical View of a Distributed Call In a monolithic system, an internal method call has cost: $$C_{mono} = T_{method} \approx 10^{-6} \text{ seconds (microseconds)}$$ In a microservices system, a network call has cost: $$C_{micro} = T_{network} + T_{serialization} + T_{processing}$$ Typical values: - $T_{network} \approx 10^{-3} \text{ s}$ (1 ms intra-DC) - $T_{serialization} \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ s (JSON marshalling) - $T_{processing} \approx 10^{-3}$ s So a single microservice call is roughly **1000× slower** than an in-process call. This is why **chained synchronous calls** must be avoided in design. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Insight:** If `OrderService` calls `PaymentService` calls `InventoryService` synchronously, the total latency is the **sum**, not the average. For $N$ chained services, total latency is bounded by: > > $$\begin{aligned} > L_{total} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i > \end{aligned}$$ > > This is the foundation of the *synchronous-call antipattern* and motivates **asynchronous event-driven** communication. --- ## 3.2 Declaring Objects — Exhaustive Memory Model Analysis This is the **single most-asked** topic in Module 1. Let us trace the JVM memory state step by step. ### The Code Under Analysis ```java class Student { int rollNo; String name; Student(int r, String n) { rollNo = r; name = n; } void display() { System.out.println(rollNo + " " + name); } } public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { Student s1; // Line 1 s1 = new Student(101, "Arun"); // Line 2 Student s2 = new Student(102, "Meera"); // Line 3 s2 = s1; // Line 4 s1 = null; // Line 5 } } ``` ### Step 1 — After Line 1: `Student s1;` A reference variable `s1` of type `Student` is created on the **stack frame** of `main()`. The JVM initializes it to the default value for object references. $$\text{stack: } s1 \rightarrow \text{null}$$ $$s1 = \texttt{null}$$ > **Heap is untouched.** No `Student` object exists yet. Accessing `s1.display()` here throws `NullPointerException`. ### Step 2 — After Line 2: `s1 = new Student(101, "Arun");` The `new` operator performs **three** sub-steps: 1. **Class loading check** — If `Student.class` is not yet loaded, the ClassLoader loads it into the **Method Area**. 2. **Heap allocation** — The JVM allocates contiguous memory on the heap for instance variables `rollNo` (4 bytes) and `name` (8 bytes reference on 64-bit JVM with compressed oops). 3. **Constructor invocation** — `Student(101, "Arun")` initializes the fields: `rollNo = 101`, `name` points to a `String` object `"Arun"` in the **String Constant Pool** (or heap). The memory address of the new object (say, `0xABC123`) is returned and assigned to `s1`. $$\text{stack: } s1 \rightarrow \text{heap: 0xABC123}$$ $$\text{heap (0xABC123): } \begin{cases} \text{rollNo} = 101 \\ \text{name} \rightarrow \text{String "Arun"} \end{cases}$$ ### Step 3 — After Line 3: `Student s2 = new Student(102, "Meera");` A new `Student` object is allocated at, say, `0xDEF456`. Reference `s2` on the stack points to it. $$\text{stack: } s1 \rightarrow \text{0xABC123}, \quad s2 \rightarrow \text{0xDEF456}$$ ### Step 4 — After Line 4: `s2 = s1;` This is the **critical step** that examiners test. The assignment `s2 = s1` does **not** copy the object — it copies the **reference** (the memory address). - The old value of `s2` (`0xDEF456`) is overwritten with `s1`'s value (`0xABC123`). - The object at `0xDEF456` is now **unreachable** (no reference points to it) → it becomes eligible for **Garbage Collection**. $$\text{stack: } s1 \rightarrow \text{0xABC123}, \quad s2 \rightarrow \text{0xABC123}$$ $$\text{0xDEF456: unreachable} \Rightarrow \text{eligible for GC}$$ ### Step 5 — After Line 5: `s1 = null;` `s1` is set to `null`. Now the object at `0xABC123` has exactly **one** reference (`s2`) — it is still alive. $$\text{stack: } s1 = \text{null}, \quad s2 \rightarrow \text{0xABC123}$$ ### The Final Truth Table for KTU Exams | Statement | Verdict | |-----------|---------| | `s1 == s2` after Line 4 | **`true`** (same address) | | `s1.equals(s2)` after Line 4 | **`true`** if `equals` not overridden (uses `==`) | | The object at `0xDEF456` after Line 4 | **Eligible for GC** | | The object at `0xABC123` after Line 5 | **Still alive** (referenced by `s2`) | | `s2.rollNo` after Line 4 | **101** (not 102) | --- ## 3.3 The Constructor Memory Equation For a class with $k$ instance fields, the heap size required per object is approximately: $$\begin{aligned} S_{object} &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{size}(field_i) + \text{header} \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{size}(field_i) + 12 \text{ bytes (on 64-bit JVM)} \end{aligned}$$ - A `boolean` field = 1 byte - An `int` field = 4 bytes - A reference field = 4 bytes (compressed oops) or 8 bytes - **Object header** = 12 bytes (mark word + class pointer) > **Example:** A `Student(int, String)` object on a 64-bit JVM ≈ $4 + 4 + 12 = 20$ bytes, rounded up to **24 bytes** (8-byte alignment). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Microservices — Functional Architecture Block Diagram This is the KTU-board-favourite diagram. It maps how a typical e-commerce platform is decomposed into microservices, showing the API Gateway, service mesh, and database-per-service pattern. ```mermaid flowchart TB client["Client Browser or Mobile App"] subgraph edge["Edge Layer"] gateway["API Gateway
Single Entry Point"] end subgraph services["Microservices Layer"] orderSvc["OrderService
Port 8081"] paySvc["PaymentService
Port 8082"] invSvc["InventoryService
Port 8083"] notifSvc["NotificationService
Port 8084"] end subgraph data["Database per Service"] orderDb["orders_db
MySQL"] payDb["payments_db
PostgreSQL"] invDb["inventory_db
MongoDB"] end bus["Message Broker
Apache Kafka"] client -->|"POST or orders"| gateway gateway -->|"REST"| orderSvc gateway -->|"REST"| paySvc gateway -->|"REST"| invSvc orderSvc -->|"JDBC"| orderDb paySvc -->|"JDBC"| payDb invSvc -->|"Mongo Driver"| invDb orderSvc -->|"Publish OrderConfirmed"| bus paySvc -->|"Publish PaymentDone"| bus invSvc -->|"Publish StockReserved"| bus bus -->|"Subscribe"| notifSvc bus -->|"Subscribe"| invSvc ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **API Gateway** is the only component the client knows about — services are *hidden*. - The **dashed bottom paths** show asynchronous, decoupled communication via Kafka. - **No service shares a database** — `OrderService` cannot directly query `payments_db`. --- ## 4.2 Object Declaration — JVM Memory Model This diagram visualises the stack-and-heap layout traced in Section 3.2, after `s2 = s1` (Line 4). ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph stack["JVM Stack - main method frame"] s1["s1
ref = 0xABC123"] s2["s2
ref = 0xABC123"] end subgraph heap["JVM Heap"] obj1["Student Object
0xABC123
rollNo = 101
name --> Arun"] obj2["Student Object
0xDEF456
rollNo = 102
name --> Meera
UNREACHABLE - GC Eligible"] end s1 --> obj1 s2 --> obj1 ``` **Critical observation for exams:** `s1` and `s2` both hold the **same** address `0xABC123`. The object `0xDEF456` has no incoming arrows — it is **garbage**. --- ## 4.3 Microservices vs Monolithic — Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD start["New Project to Architect"] q1{"Is the system large
and complex?"} q2{"Will independent
scaling be needed?"} q3{"Does the team exceed
50 engineers?"} mono["Monolithic Architecture
Simpler, faster to start"] micro["Microservices Architecture
Complex but scalable"] start --> q1 q1 -->|No| mono q1 -->|Yes| q2 q2 -->|No| mono q2 -->|Yes| q3 q3 -->|No| mono q3 -->|Yes| micro ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Explain the concept of Microservices. List any four characteristics.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Microservices is an architectural style that structures an application as a suite of small, independently deployable, loosely coupled services, each implementing a single business capability. (1 mark) Four characteristics: (½ mark each, total 2 marks) 1. **Single Responsibility** — Each service handles one bounded business function. 2. **Independent Deployability** — Services can be deployed without coordinating with others. 3. **Decentralized Data** — Each service owns its own database (no shared schema). 4. **Failure Isolation** — A fault in one service does not propagate to others (fault-tolerant design). --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Differentiate between *declaration* and *instantiation* of an object in Java with an example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Declaration** is the act of creating a **reference variable** that can hold the address of an object. It does **not** allocate heap memory. (1 mark) - **Instantiation** is the act of allocating heap memory and invoking a constructor using the `new` keyword. (1 mark) - **Example:** (1 mark) ```java Student s; // Declaration only - s is null s = new Student(); // Instantiation - object created on heap ``` --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** Explain the **Microservice Architecture** in detail. Compare it with **Monolithic Architecture** using at least six points. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain how an **API Gateway** fits into a microservices system. List any **three advantages** of using an API Gateway. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Microservice Architecture (2 marks):** Microservice Architecture is a style of structuring an application as a collection of loosely coupled, independently deployable services. Each service is self-contained, owns its data, communicates over lightweight protocols (HTTP/REST or messaging), and is built around a single business capability. **Six-Point Comparison (5 marks, ~1 mark each):** | # | Aspect | Monolithic | Microservices | |---|--------|-----------|---------------| | 1 | **Deployment** | Single deployable unit (WAR/EAR) | Many independent services | | 2 | **Scaling** | Whole app scales together | Individual services scale independently | | 3 | **Technology** | Single language/DB | Polyglot (multiple languages/DBs) | | 4 | **Fault Tolerance** | One bug can crash entire system | Failure isolated per service | | 5 | **Data Layer** | Single shared database | Database per service | | 6 | **Team & DevOps** | Large coordinated teams; one CI/CD | Small autonomous teams; per-service CI/CD | --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Diagram (4 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR client["Client App"] gw["API Gateway
Auth, Routing, Rate Limiting"] s1["UserService"] s2["ProductService"] s3["OrderService"] client -->|"HTTPS"| gw gw -->|"REST"| s1 gw -->|"REST"| s2 gw -->|"REST"| s3 ``` **Explanation (1 mark):** The API Gateway is the single entry point that receives all client requests, authenticates them, applies rate limits, and routes each request to the appropriate downstream microservice based on the URL path or header. **Three Advantages (2 marks, ~0.7 mark each):** 1. **Centralised Cross-Cutting Concerns** — Authentication, logging, SSL termination handled in one place rather than duplicated in every service. 2. **Protocol Translation** — Client may speak HTTP/REST while the gateway translates to gRPC for internal services. 3. **Simplified Client Code** — Client need not know the URLs of dozens of services; it only knows the gateway. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]` **(a)** Explain the process of **declaring and instantiating objects** in Java. Show the difference between **primitive variables** and **reference variables** with a memory-level diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is an **anonymous object**? Write a Java program that: - Declares a class `Box` with fields `length`, `width`, `height` and a method `volume()`. - Creates an anonymous object of `Box` and prints its volume. - Demonstrates the case of two reference variables pointing to the same object. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Declaring Objects in Java (3 marks):** In Java, an object is brought into existence in **two steps**: 1. **Declaration** — `ClassName refVar;` — creates a reference variable on the stack, initialised to `null`. 2. **Instantiation** — `refVar = new ClassName(args);` — the `new` operator allocates memory on the heap, invokes the matching constructor, and returns the memory address which is stored in `refVar`. **Primitive vs Reference Variables — Memory Diagram (4 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph stk["JVM Stack"] a["int a = 10
stores value directly"] b["Box b = 0x1234
stores address only"] end subgraph hp["JVM Heap"] obj["Box Object at 0x1234
length, width, height fields"] end b -->|points to| obj ``` **Key Differences:** | Feature | Primitive Variable | Reference Variable | |---------|-------------------|--------------------| | **Stores** | Actual value | Memory address of object | | **Memory Location** | Stack (local) or Heap (instance field) | Stack (the reference itself) | | **Default Value** | `0`, `0.0`, `false`, `\u0000` | `null` | | **Size** | Fixed (e.g., `int` = 4 bytes) | 4 or 8 bytes (just the address) | | **Copy Semantics** | `int y = x;` copies the value | `Box b2 = b1;` copies the address — same object | --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Anonymous Object (1 mark):** An **anonymous object** is an object that is instantiated using `new` but is **not assigned to any reference variable**. It becomes eligible for Garbage Collection as soon as the statement using it completes. **Java Program (6 marks):** ```java class Box { int length, width, height; Box(int l, int w, int h) { length = l; width = w; height = h; } int volume() { return length * width * height; } } public class BoxDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // (i) Anonymous object - no reference retained System.out.println("Anonymous object volume = " + new Box(2, 3, 4).volume()); // (ii) Two references to the same object Box b1 = new Box(5, 5, 5); Box b2 = b1; // b2 now points to the same object as b1 System.out.println("b1.volume() = " + b1.volume()); System.out.println("b2.volume() = " + b2.volume()); // Proof they are the same object: if (b1 == b2) System.out.println("b1 and b2 point to the SAME object"); else System.out.println("Different objects"); } } ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Anonymous object volume = 24 b1.volume() = 125 b2.volume() = 125 b1 and b2 point to the SAME object ``` **Explanation of the `b2 = b1` case (2 marks embedded):** After `b2 = b1;`, both `b1` and `b2` hold the same memory address. The `==` operator on references checks **address equality**, not content equality. Hence the message "SAME object" is printed. If we had written `b2 = new Box(5, 5, 5);`, then `b1 == b2` would print "Different objects" even though the contents are identical. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Confusing `new` with declaration:** Writing "`new Student s;`" or "`Student s = Student();`" — both are syntactically **wrong**. Only `new ClassName(...)` is correct. > 2. **Thinking `s2 = s1` copies the object:** It copies the **reference** (address). The original `s2` object becomes **garbage**. > 3. **Drawing a database connection in the microservices diagram:** Services communicate via **APIs or message brokers**, **never** via direct database access to another service's DB. > 4. **Saying microservices have no drawbacks:** They DO — network latency, distributed transactions, and operational complexity. Mention at least one drawback to score full marks. > 5. **Forgetting `s = null;` does not destroy the object immediately** — it only makes the object **eligible** for GC.
