Actual GC happens at JVM's discretion. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Microservices — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ Microservices = collection of small, independent, single-purpose services - ✅ Inspired by **Single Responsibility Principle** (an OOP principle) - ✅ Each service has its **own database** (database-per-service) - ✅ Communication: **REST/HTTP** (sync) or **Kafka/RabbitMQ** (async) - ✅ **API Gateway** = single entry point for clients - ✅ **Service Registry** (Eureka, Consul) = service discovery mechanism - ✅ Advantages: scalability, fault isolation, polyglot, independent deployment - ✅ Disadvantages: network latency, distributed complexity, eventual consistency - ✅ Tools: Spring Boot, Spring Cloud, Netflix OSS, Docker, Kubernetes ### Declaring Objects in Java — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ A **class** is a blueprint; an **object** is a runtime instance - ✅ **Two steps**: `Student s;` (declare) + `s = new Student();` (instantiate) - ✅ Reference variables live on the **stack**, objects live on the **heap** - ✅ Default value of any reference = **`null`** - ✅ Accessing members of a `null` reference → **`NullPointerException`** - ✅ `s2 = s1` makes both references point to the **same** object (not a copy) - ✅ The old `s2` object (if any) becomes **garbage** after reassignment - ✅ **Anonymous object** = `new ClassName().method();` — no named reference - ✅ `==` on references compares **addresses**, not contents - ✅ Object memory = `header (12B) + sum of field sizes` (rounded to 8-byte alignment) ### Must-Memorise KTU Board Verbatim > *"A reference variable is a named handle that lives on the stack and stores the memory address of an object allocated on the heap. Declaring an object without instantiating it produces a reference whose value is `null`."* > *"Microservices is an architectural style that structures an application as a suite of small, loosely coupled services, each implementing a single bounded business capability and communicating via lightweight protocols."*

topics.title: Object Reference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Object Reference # 📘 MODULE 1 — OBJECT REFERENCE > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme)** > *Object Reference* is a foundational concept in Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) that underpins how objects are created, named, manipulated, and destroyed in languages like **Java**, **C++**, and **C#**. It is essential for understanding memory management, method invocation, aliasing, and garbage collection. --- ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) An **Object Reference** is a *typed handle* (an address or symbolic pointer) stored in a variable that **identifies and locates an object** residing in the **heap memory** at runtime. The reference variable does *not* contain the object itself; it contains the *location information* required to access the object in dynamic memory. Mathematically, if **O** is an object in heap memory at address **A**, and **R** is a reference variable, then: $$ R \rightarrow O \quad \text{where} \quad \text{value}(R) = A $$ The reference type determines: - **What operations** can be performed on the referenced object. - **What fields and methods** are accessible through the dot (`.`) operator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (Board Favourite)** > - **Primitive Variable** → stores the *actual value* directly. > - **Reference Variable** → stores the *memory address* (handle) of the object. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Imagine you are in a **library**: - The **book** is the **object** (sitting on a shelf in a specific section). - The **library card** with the **call number** written on it is the **reference**. - The card is *not* the book — it merely *points to* where the book is located. Similarly, in programming: - The **object** lives in the **heap** (a large, open warehouse of memory). - The **reference variable** lives in the **stack** (a compact workspace tied to the active method call). - The reference variable is the *call number*; the object is the *book*. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy: TV Remote** > The remote control is the **reference**, the television is the **object**. Pressing buttons on the remote changes the TV (mutating the object). Throwing the remote away doesn't destroy the TV (losing one reference doesn't destroy the object — only when *all* references are gone). --- ## 1.3 Why Object References Matter in OOP | Reason | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | **Dynamic Memory** | Objects are created at runtime in heap; references bridge stack-to-heap. | | **Method Invocation** | Method calls use references to bind `this` to the calling object. | | **Polymorphism** | References of a supertype can point to subtype objects (upcasting). | | **Garbage Collection** | The JVM/CPU tracks references to determine object liveness. | | **Sharing & Aliasing** | Multiple references can point to the same object, enabling shared state. | | **Encapsulation Boundaries** | References respect access modifiers when accessing object internals. | --- ## 1.4 The `new` Operator and Reference Creation In Java, the `new` keyword does **two things atomically**: 1. **Allocates** memory in the heap for the new object. 2. **Returns** a reference to that memory location. ```java Student s1 = new Student("Anu", 101); ``` Here, `s1` is a **reference variable** of type `Student`, stored in the stack. The `new Student(...)` allocates a `Student` object in the heap and returns its address, which is stored in `s1`. $$ s1 \xrightarrow{\text{points to}} \text{Student Object (heap)} $$ # 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Object Reference An object reference is fundamentally composed of **three logical parts**: | Component | Description | Lifetime | |---|---|---| | **Type Identifier** | Compile-time tag (e.g., `Student`, `Object`) | Bound at declaration | | **Storage Location** | Stack slot (local refs) / Heap slot (instance fields) | Until scope ends | | **Referent Pointer** | Runtime handle to the heap object | Until nulled or GC'd | > [!NOTE] > The **type** is a *compile-time* concept checked by the compiler. The **pointer** is a *runtime* concept resolved by the JVM/CLR. This separation is what enables **late binding** (dynamic dispatch) — a cornerstone of polymorphism. --- ## 2.2 The Two Memory Zones: Stack vs. Heap A clear understanding of memory layout is *critical* for board questions. ### **The Stack** - LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) data structure. - Stores: **primitive local variables**, **references to objects**, **method call frames**. - Allocation/deallocation is **automatic** and **fast** (just a pointer move). - Each thread has its own stack. - Size is bounded and small (typically **512 KB to 1 MB** per thread by default in JVM). ### **The Heap** - Large, shared memory pool (typically **several GB** in modern JVMs). - Stores: **actual object data** (instance variables), **arrays**. - Allocation is **dynamic**; deallocation is **automatic** (via Garbage Collector in Java). - Shared across threads (with synchronization concerns). $$ \underbrace{\text{Stack}}_{\text{refs + primitives}} \xleftrightarrow{\text{address}} \underbrace{\text{Heap}}_{\text{actual objects}} $$ --- ## 2.3 Reference Assignment — The Mechanics When you write `ref2 = ref1;`, the following happens: 1. The **bit pattern** of `ref1` (the heap address) is **copied** into `ref2`. 2. The object itself is **not duplicated**. 3. Both `ref1` and `ref2` now point to the **same object** in the heap. 4. Changes made through `ref1` are **visible** through `ref2` (and vice versa). This phenomenon is called **aliasing**. > [!WARNING] > **Aliasing is a common source of bugs in OOP!** Two variables that *look* independent may actually share state through aliasing. --- ## 2.4 Categories of References (Java Specific) The Java HotSpot VM defines **four reference strengths** that influence garbage collection behavior: | Reference Type | Class | GC Behavior | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Strong Reference** | Default (`Object o = new Object()`) | Object is **never** GC'd while strong ref exists | Normal usage | | **Weak Reference** | `java.lang.ref.WeakReference` | GC'd at next collection if only weak ref | Caches, canonical maps | | **Soft Reference** | `java.lang.ref.SoftReference` | GC'd **only when memory is low** | Memory-sensitive caches | | **Phantom Reference** | `java.lang.ref.PhantomReference` | Object is GC'd, ref enqueued for cleanup | Resource cleanup pre-mortem | > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU 2024 Module 1, focus on **Strong References** (the default). Weak/Soft/Phantom are advanced topics, typically covered in Module 5 (Advanced Java). --- ## 2.5 The `null` Reference `null` is a **special literal** that represents the *absence* of an object reference. When a reference is `null`: - It does **not** point to any heap object. - Any attempt to invoke a method or access a field through a `null` reference throws **`NullPointerException` (NPE)**. ```java Student s = null; System.out.println(s.name); // NullPointerException at runtime ``` The compiler **cannot** detect most NPEs at compile time — they are a *runtime* hazard. > [!TIP] > **Best Practice (KTU Project Viva Tip):** Always initialize reference variables explicitly to `null` or a valid object. Use defensive checks: `if (s != null) { ... }` before dereferencing. --- ## 2.6 The `this` Reference Inside any **instance method** or **constructor**, the keyword `this` is an **implicit reference** to the *current object* on which the method is being invoked. ```java class Box { int length; Box(int length) { this.length = length; // disambiguates instance var from parameter } } ``` The `this` reference: - Is **final** (cannot be reassigned). - Is **not** available in `static` methods (no current object exists). - Can be passed as an argument to other methods: `obj.doWork(this);` --- ## 2.7 Method Parameters: Pass-by-Value of References In Java, **everything is pass-by-value** — but for references, *the value being passed is the address*. This subtle distinction is a classic board question. | What is passed for primitive | What is passed for object reference | |---|---| | A **copy of the value** (e.g., the int `5`) | A **copy of the address** (the pointer) | | Modifying the parameter does **not** affect the caller | Reassigning the parameter does **not** affect the caller, but **mutating** the object **does** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **C++ vs. Java Distinction (Board Favourite)** > - **C++**: Has explicit references (`int& r = x;`) and pass-by-reference (`void f(int& x)`). > - **Java**: Has **no** explicit reference type; all references are *implicit handles*, and method calls are pass-by-value-of-reference (a copy of the pointer). --- ## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Rule / Formula | Example | |---|---|---| | Object Creation | `ClassName ref = new ClassName(args);` | `Date d = new Date();` | | Reference Copy | `ref2 = ref1;` → both point to same object | Aliasing | | Null Assignment | `ref = null;` → reference detached | GC eligible if no other refs | | Method Invoke | `ref.method(args);` → dereferences ref | `s1.display();` | | Equality (`==`) | Compares **references** (addresses) | `s1 == s2` → same object? | | Equality (`.equals()`) | Compares **content** (object state) | `s1.equals(s2)` → same values? | | `this` | Implicit ref to current object | `this.x = x;` | | Garbage Collection | Object eligible when **0 strong references** remain | `ref = null;` | --- ## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Database Connection Pools**: References to `Connection` objects are passed between methods without copying the underlying socket. - **GUI Frameworks**: A `Button` reference is stored by the parent `Frame`; clicking the button routes events back through that reference. - **Design Patterns**: Patterns like *Observer*, *Strategy*, and *Composite* rely heavily on object references for decoupling components. - **Microservices**: Service proxies are essentially *remote object references* in distributed systems (e.g., RMI, gRPC stubs). - **Embedded Systems**: In resource-constrained devices, understanding reference lifetime is critical for memory budgeting. # 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Tracing a Reference Assignment (Memory Diagram Walkthrough) Let us trace the following code **line by line**, exactly as expected in a KTU 14-mark answer. ```java class Student { String name; int rollNo; Student(String n, int r) { name = n; rollNo = r; } } public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Student s1 = new Student("Anu", 101); Student s2 = s1; s2.rollNo = 202; System.out.println(s1.rollNo); } } ``` ### **Step 1: Declare `s1` and create object** - The JVM evaluates `new Student("Anu", 101)`. - Memory for a `Student` object is allocated in the **heap**. - Constructor initializes `name = "Anu"`, `rollNo = 101`. - The address of this heap object (say, `0xABCD`) is returned and stored in the local variable `s1` on the **stack** of the `main` method. $$ \boxed{\text{Stack: } s1 \rightarrow 0xABCD} \quad \boxed{\text{Heap @ 0xABCD: } \{\text{name="Anu", rollNo=101}\}} $$ ### **Step 2: `Student s2 = s1;` (Reference Copy / Aliasing)** - The value stored in `s1` (the address `0xABCD`) is **copied** into `s2`. - `s2` now points to the **same object** as `s1`. No new object is created. $$ \boxed{\text{Stack: } s1 \rightarrow 0xABCD, \quad s2 \rightarrow 0xABCD} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Heap @ 0xABCD: } \{\text{name="Anu", rollNo=101}\}} \quad \text{(single object, two refs)} $$ ### **Step 3: `s2.rollNo = 202;` (Mutation through alias)** - `s2` is dereferenced to find the object at `0xABCD`. - The `rollNo` field of that **single** object is updated to `202`. - Because `s1` also points to `0xABCD`, `s1` sees the updated value. $$ \boxed{\text{Heap @ 0xABCD: } \{\text{name="Anu", rollNo=202}\}} $$ ### **Step 4: `System.out.println(s1.rollNo);`** - `s1` is dereferenced to `0xABCD`. - `rollNo` is read → **prints `202`**. **Final Output:** `202` > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **classic aliasing trap**. Many students mistakenly predict `101` because they think `s2` is a "copy" of `s1`. In reality, the *object* is shared, not duplicated. --- ## 3.2 Method Parameter Pass-by-Value-of-Reference ```java class Demo { static void modify(Dog d) { d.name = "Bruno"; // (A) mutation — affects caller d = new Dog("Max"); // (B) reassignment — does NOT affect caller } public static void main(String[] args) { Dog myDog = new Dog("Rex"); modify(myDog); System.out.println(myDog.name); } } ``` ### **Step 1: `Dog myDog = new Dog("Rex");`** - Heap allocates `Dog` object with `name = "Rex"`. - Address `0x1111` is stored in `myDog` (stack). $$ \boxed{\text{caller's stack: } myDog \rightarrow 0x1111} \quad \boxed{\text{heap @ 0x1111: } \{\text{name="Rex"}\}} $$ ### **Step 2: `modify(myDog);` is invoked** - Java **copies** the value of `myDog` (i.e., `0x1111`) into the parameter `d`. - Both `myDog` and `d` now point to the same object — but `d` is a *local copy* of the address. $$ \boxed{\text{caller's stack: } myDog \rightarrow 0x1111} \quad \boxed{\text{modify's stack: } d \rightarrow 0x1111} $$ ### **Step 3 (A): `d.name = "Bruno";`** - `d` is dereferenced to `0x1111`; the object's `name` is mutated. - Caller's `myDog` also sees this change (same object). $$ \boxed{\text{heap @ 0x1111: } \{\text{name="Bruno"}\}} $$ ### **Step 3 (B): `d = new Dog("Max");`** - A **new** object is created at, say, `0x2222` with `name="Max"`. - The parameter `d` is **reassigned** to `0x2222`. - Crucially, `myDog` in the caller's frame is **unchanged** — it still points to `0x1111`. $$ \boxed{\text{caller's stack: } myDog \rightarrow 0x1111} \quad \boxed{\text{modify's stack: } d \rightarrow 0x2222} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{heap @ 0x1111: } \{\text{name="Bruno"}\}} \quad \boxed{\text{heap @ 0x2222: } \{\text{name="Max"}\}} $$ ### **Step 4: Return from `modify`** - `modify`'s stack frame is destroyed; local `d` vanishes. - `myDog` still points to `0x1111`. **Final `System.out.println(myDog.name);` → prints `"Bruno"`** > [!NOTE] > **Board Insight:** This question separates *Apply* (CO2) from *Analyze* (CO3) levels. The mutation works (A), but reassignment (B) does not propagate — a classic Java interview and exam question. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reference Type Behaviors | Operation | Primitive (`int x`) | Reference (`Student s`) | |---|---|---| | Stored in | Stack (direct value) | Stack (address value) | | `=` does | Copies the **value** | Copies the **address** (alias) | | `==` does | Compares values | Compares addresses (identity) | | Can be `null`? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | Default value | `0` (numeric), `false` (boolean) | `null` | | Pass to method | Pass-by-value (copy of number) | Pass-by-value (copy of address) | | Garbage collected? | N/A (lives on stack until scope ends) | ✅ Eligible when no strong refs remain | --- ## 3.4 Derivative Logic — Garbage Collection Eligibility An object becomes **eligible for Garbage Collection (GC)** when it can no longer be reached through any chain of strong references starting from a **GC root** (e.g., local variables on the stack, static fields, JNI references). **Decision Rule:** $$ \text{GarbageEligible}(O) = \nexists \text{ a strong reference path from any GC root to } O $$ ### **Worked Example** ```java public class GCTest { public static void main(String[] args) { Student a = new Student("X", 1); // Object1 Student b = new Student("Y", 2); // Object2 a = b; // Object1 loses its only ref b = null; // Object2 still has ref via a } } ``` - After `a = b;`: Object1 has **zero** strong refs → **eligible for GC**. - After `b = null;`: Object2 still has a strong ref via `a` → **not eligible** for GC. > [!IMPORTANT] > Setting a reference to `null` does **not** immediately destroy the object. It merely *marks* the object as eligible; the actual reclamation happens when the **Garbage Collector** next runs. --- ## 3.5 Reference Casting and Polymorphism A reference variable of a supertype can hold an object of a subtype (**upcasting** — implicit and safe). The reverse (**downcasting**) requires an explicit cast and an `instanceof` check. ```java Animal a = new Dog(); // Upcast: implicit, safe Dog d = (Dog) a; // Downcast: explicit, runtime-checked if (a instanceof Cat) { // Type check before downcast Cat c = (Cat) a; } ``` The reference type determines *which methods are accessible at compile time*. The actual object's class determines *which method implementation runs at runtime* (dynamic dispatch). # 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Memory Architecture — Stack ↔ Heap Interaction ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph StackFrame[STACK FRAME - main method] varS1["s1 : Student"] varS2["s2 : Student"] end subgraph HeapArea[HEAP MEMORY] obj1["0xABCD: Student Object name=Anu rollNo=101"] obj2["0x1234: Student Object name=Ravi rollNo=202"] end varS1 -->|points to 0xABCD| obj1 varS2 -->|points to 0x1234| obj2 style StackFrame fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style HeapArea fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style varS1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style varS2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style obj1 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300 style obj2 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300 ``` --- ## 4.2 Aliasing Diagram — Two References, One Object ```mermaid flowchart LR refA["s1 reference (stack)"] refB["s2 reference (stack)"] target["SINGLE Student Object in Heap name=Anu rollNo=202"] refA -->|alias| target refB -->|alias| target style refA fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px style refB fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px style target fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!NOTE] > The above diagram visually emphasizes that `s1` and `s2` are **two distinct stack variables** pointing to the **same heap object** — the essence of aliasing. --- ## 4.3 Method Invocation — Pass-by-Value-of-Reference ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant Caller as Caller Frame (main) participant Param as Parameter d (modify) participant Obj as Dog Object in Heap Caller->>Caller: myDog = 0x1111 (new Dog Rex) Caller->>Param: pass copy of myDog (0x1111) Note over Param: d = 0x1111 (same object) Param->>Obj: d.name = Bruno (mutation) Note over Obj: name is now Bruno Param->>Param: d = new Dog Max (0x2222) Note over Param: d now points elsewhere
myDog unaffected Param-->>Caller: return (d goes out of scope) Caller->>Obj: print myDog.name Note over Obj,Caller: Output: Bruno ``` --- ## 4.4 Garbage Collection Eligibility Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Object Created in Heap] --> B{Any strong
reference exists?} B -- Yes --> C[Object stays in memory] B -- No --> D[Marked eligible
for Garbage Collection] D --> E[GC sweeps object] E --> F[Heap memory reclaimed] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style E fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24 style F fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460 ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define an object reference. How is it different from a primitive variable?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **object reference** is a typed handle stored in a variable that contains the **memory address** of an object residing in the **heap**. The reference does not hold the object itself; it merely points to the object's location, enabling the program to access and manipulate the object's state through the dot (`.`) operator. **Differences:** | Aspect | Primitive Variable | Object Reference | |---|---|---| | Stores | Actual value (e.g., `5`, `3.14`) | Heap address of object | | Memory | Stack | Stack (ref) + Heap (object) | | Default | `0`, `false`, etc. | `null` | | Size | Fixed (1, 2, 4, 8 bytes) | Fixed (usually 4 or 8 bytes for the address) | | Operations | Arithmetic, comparison | Method invocation, field access | *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for clear distinction table.)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: What is aliasing in Java? Illustrate with a small example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Aliasing** occurs when **two or more reference variables** point to the **same object** in memory. Changes made to the object through one reference are visible through all other references. ```java class Box { int size; Box(int s) { size = s; } } public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { Box b1 = new Box(10); Box b2 = b1; // aliasing b2.size = 50; System.out.println(b1.size); // prints 50, not 10 } } ``` Here, `b1` and `b2` are two distinct references (aliases) to the same `Box` object. Modifying `size` through `b2` affects what `b1` sees. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for code, 1 mark for output explanation.)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions in Module 1 offer an **internal choice** between two 14-mark alternatives, typically with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain the concept of an object reference in Java. With a neat diagram, describe how a reference variable in the stack is connected to the object allocated in the heap. Discuss the role of the `new` operator. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between **pass-by-value** (used in Java for primitives) and **pass-by-value-of-reference** (used in Java for objects). Use a code example to show that reassigning a parameter inside a method does not affect the caller, while mutating the object does. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks** **Definition (2 marks):** An object reference in Java is a variable that stores the **memory address (handle)** of an object created on the heap. The reference acts as a *gateway* to access the object's fields and methods. Java does not expose the actual address; it provides a **managed reference** that the JVM uses transparently for dereferencing. **Role of `new` operator (2 marks):** The `new` keyword performs three tasks: 1. **Allocates** memory on the heap large enough to hold the object's instance variables. 2. **Initializes** instance variables to their default values (`0`, `null`, `false`). 3. **Invokes** the appropriate constructor to initialize the object to a valid state. 4. **Returns** a reference (address) to the newly constructed object. **Diagram (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR s1["s1 : Student (Stack)"] -->|0xABCD| obj["Student Object
name=Anu
rollNo=101 (Heap)"] style s1 fill:#cce5ff style obj fill:#ffd9b3 ``` **Key Points (1 mark):** - Primitive `int x = 5;` → value `5` is directly on the stack. - Reference `Student s = new Student();` → `s` holds an address; the object lives on the heap. - Accessing `s.name` triggers an automatic dereference by the JVM. --- #### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks** **Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):** Java is **strictly pass-by-value**. There is no pass-by-reference in Java. However, for object references, the *value being copied is the address*. This is sometimes called *pass-by-value-of-reference* or *pass-by-sharing*. **Code Example (3 marks):** ```java class Number { int value; Number(int v) { value = v; } } class Test { static void process(Number n) { n.value = 100; // (A) mutation: visible to caller n = new Number(500); // (B) reassignment: NOT visible to caller } public static void main(String[] args) { Number num = new Number(10); process(num); System.out.println(num.value); // Output: 100 } } ``` **Explanation (2 marks):** - At (A), `n` and `num` point to the same object; mutating `n.value` updates the shared object → caller sees `100`. - At (B), `n` is locally reassigned to a new object; the caller's `num` still points to the original object. - Hence the output is `100`, not `500`. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** What is a `null` reference? Explain the `NullPointerException` with an example. What are the best practices to avoid NPEs? **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable example, explain the concept of **aliasing** and its impact on object state. How does it relate to the `==` operator versus the `.equals()` method? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks** **Definition of `null` (1 mark):** `null` is a reserved literal in Java representing the absence of an object reference. A reference variable holding `null` does not point to any valid heap object. **NullPointerException Explanation (2 marks):** A `NullPointerException` (NPE) is a *runtime exception* thrown when the program attempts to: - Invoke a method on a `null` reference. - Access or assign a field of a `null` reference. - Take the length of a `null` array. **Example (2 marks):** ```java String name = null; System.out.println(name.length()); // throws NullPointerException ``` Here, `name` is `null`, so calling `.length()` triggers NPE. **Best Practices to Avoid NPE (2 marks):** 1. **Explicit initialization**: Always initialize references to a valid object or explicitly to `null`. 2. **Defensive checks**: Use `if (obj != null) { ... }` before dereferencing. 3. **Use `Optional`** (Java 8+): Wrap possibly-null values in `Optional` to enforce explicit handling. 4. **Use `Objects.requireNonNull(x)`**: Validate arguments at method entry. 5. **Avoid returning `null`**: Use *Null Object Pattern* or throw checked exceptions instead. --- #### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks** **Aliasing Concept (2 marks):** Aliasing occurs when multiple reference variables refer to the same object. Modifying the object through one reference alters the state visible to all other references. **Example (2 marks):** ```java class Account { double balance; Account(double b) { balance = b; } } public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { Account a1 = new Account(1000.0); Account a2 = a1; // alias a2.balance -= 500.0; System.out.println(a1.balance); // prints 500.0 } } ``` Here, `a1` and `a2` are aliases. The withdrawal through `a2` reduces the shared balance, visible to `a1`. **`==` vs `.equals()` (3 marks):** | Operator | What it checks | Example | |---|---|---| | `==` | **Reference identity** — are both refs pointing to the *same object* in memory? | `a1 == a2` → true (aliased) or false (distinct objects with same content) | | `.equals()` | **Logical/content equality** — do the objects have equivalent state? (Defined per class) | `a1.equals(a2)` → true if balances are equal | ```java Account a1 = new Account(1000); Account a2 = new Account(1000); System.out.println(a1 == a2); // false (different objects) System.out.println(a1.equals(a2)); // true (if Account overrides equals) ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing `==` with `.equals()`** — `==` checks addresses, not content. A student who writes `a1 == a2` and says "they have the same balance, so true" loses **2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to override `.equals()` and `hashCode()`** together — violates the contract and breaks hash-based collections. > 3. **Saying Java is "pass-by-reference"** — Java is **always pass-by-value**. Even for object references, the *address* is copied. This is a favourite **2-mark deduction** by examiners. > 4. **Drawing stack and heap in the wrong places** — primitives are in stack, objects are in heap. Mixing them up loses **diagram marks**. > 5. **Forgetting that `new` returns a reference, not the object** — using phrases like "creating a variable object" instead of "creating an object and storing its reference" is imprecise. --- ## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!TIP] > Memorize these points before every KTU exam attempt on Object References. - ✅ An **object reference** is a *handle* (address) to a heap object, not the object itself. - ✅ **Stack** stores primitive values and references; **Heap** stores actual objects. - ✅ The `new` operator allocates heap memory and returns a reference. - ✅ `==` compares **references** (identity); `.equals()` compares **content** (logical equality). - ✅ **Aliasing** = two refs, one object → mutations are shared. - ✅ Java is **always pass-by-value**; for refs, the *value copied is the address*. - ✅ Reassigning a parameter inside a method does **not** affect the caller's reference. - ✅ Mutating an object through a parameter **does** affect the caller's view. - ✅ `null` means "no object assigned"; dereferencing `null` causes **NullPointerException**. - ✅ `this` is an **implicit final reference** to the current object (unavailable in `static` methods). - ✅ An object becomes **garbage-collectible** when no strong reference path exists from any GC root. - ✅ Setting a reference to `null` does **not** immediately destroy the object — GC runs asynchronously. - ✅ Reference types determine *compile-time* accessibility; actual object class determines *runtime* behavior (polymorphism). - ✅ Weak/Soft/Phantom references (advanced) influence GC behavior — covered in advanced modules. --- > **End of Notes — Object Reference (Module 1)** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | Course: PECMT535 | CO1–CO3 Mapped*

topics.title: Introduction to Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), a **Method** is a named, reusable block of statements (a member of a class) that performs a specific, well-defined task. Methods are the primary mechanism through which the behaviour of an object (or a class, in the case of static methods) is implemented. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for PECMT535, a method is fundamentally a function bound to a class, encapsulating the procedural logic that operates on the **state (data members)** of that class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A *method* in Java/OOP is a member function declared inside a class that defines the actions an object can perform. It consists of a *method signature* (name + parameter list) and a *method body* (the executable block). The method is invoked (called) to execute its body, optionally returning a value to the caller.

The general syntactic structure in Java is:

```java
[access_modifier] [static] [final] [abstract] return_type methodName(parameter_list) {
    // method body — set of statements
    [return value;]
}
```

### 1.2 Intuition & Real-World Analogy

Think of a **class** as the *blueprint* of a *washing machine*. The **data members** (`powerStatus`, `waterLevel`, `timer`) are the internal state — just like the buttons, dial, and drum of the machine. But a machine is useless if you cannot *do* something with it.

A **Method** is the *action button* — `startWash()`, `fillWater()`, `drain()`, `rinse()`. When you press the "Start Wash" button, the machine executes a fixed internal procedure (the *method body*), and when it finishes, the clothes are clean (an *output*).

- **Object** = the actual washing machine in your house.
- **Method** = the action button you press.
- **Parameters** = the settings you pass in (e.g., 30-minute cycle, hot water).
- **Return value** = the result (e.g., clean clothes — although in code it may be a numeric status code like `0` for success).
- **Caller** = you, the human pressing the button.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Methods Matter in OOP (KTU CO1 Mapping):**
> 1. **Modularity** — Complex problems are decomposed into smaller, manageable pieces.
> 2. **Reusability** — A method is written once, called many times (DRY principle).
> 3. **Abstraction** — The caller only needs to know *what* a method does, not *how* it does it.
> 4. **Encapsulation Support** — Methods operate on private data, controlling how it is mutated.

### 1.3 Classification of Methods (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Category | Description | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Predefined (Library) Methods** | Already written in JDK (e.g., `println()`, `sqrt()`) | Students must know `java.lang.Math` and `String` methods |
| **User-defined Methods** | Written by the programmer inside a class | Primary focus of Module 1 & 2 |
| **Instance Methods** | Belong to objects, need `this` reference | Default method type in Java |
| **Static Methods** | Belong to the class, no `this`; invoked via class name | Often used in `main()` |
| **Accessor (Getter)** | Returns field value; no side effects | Supports encapsulation |
| **Mutator (Setter)** | Modifies field value; void return | Supports encapsulation |
| **Constructors** | Special method used to initialise objects | Covered in next module |
| **Recursive Methods** | Methods that call themselves | Recursion topic |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Board Exams:**
> A **method** always has a *return type* (which can be `void`). A method with return type `void` simply does not return any value. This is different from a *constructor*, which has **no return type** at all (not even `void`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Method — Structural Breakdown

Every Java method has exactly **six** structural components (some optional). Let us analyse each in board-exam language.

**Step 1 — Access Modifier**
Specifies the visibility/accessibility of the method. Possible values:
- `public` — accessible from any class.
- `private` — accessible only within the same class.
- `protected` — accessible within the same package and subclasses.
- *default* (no modifier) — package-private.

**Step 2 — Non-Access Modifiers**
- `static` — method belongs to the class, not instances. Called using `ClassName.methodName()`.
- `final` — method cannot be overridden in subclasses.
- `abstract` — method has no body; must be overridden (deferred to advanced modules).
- `synchronized` — thread-safety marker (advanced).

**Step 3 — Return Type**
The data type of the value the method sends back to the caller. Can be:
- A primitive: `int`, `double`, `boolean`, `char`, etc.
- A reference type: `String`, `int[]`, any class.
- `void` — method returns nothing.

**Step 4 — Method Name**
A valid Java identifier following camelCase convention (e.g., `calculateTotal`, `isValid`). Should be a **verb or verb phrase** indicating the action.

**Step 5 — Parameter List (Formal Parameters)**
Comma-separated list of `type name` pairs declared inside parentheses. May be empty `()`. Each parameter is a local variable initialised by the actual arguments passed during the call.

**Step 6 — Method Body**
Enclosed within `{ }`, contains the **declarations** (local variables) and the **statements** (logic, conditionals, loops, return statement).

### 2.2 Method Signature — The KTU Board Favourite

The **method signature** is the part that uniquely identifies a method for the compiler during **overloading resolution**. It consists of:

$$\text{Method Signature} = \text{Method Name} \; + \; \text{Parameter List (types, order, count)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The return type is NOT part of the method signature in Java.**
> Therefore, two methods in the same class *cannot* have the same name and parameter list but different return types — this is a **compilation error**.

### 2.3 Method Call — The "How"

A method is **invoked** (called) using its name followed by parentheses containing the **actual arguments** (values whose types must be compatible with the formal parameters).

```java
// Method definition
static int add(int a, int b) {       // a, b are formal parameters
    return a + b;
}

// Method invocation
int result = add(5, 7);                // 5, 7 are actual arguments
```

During a call, the JVM creates a new **stack frame** (activation record) for the method, copies the actual arguments into the parameter slots, and transfers control. When the method finishes (`return` or end-of-body), the stack frame is destroyed and control returns to the caller.

### 2.4 Categories of Methods Based on Communication

| Communication Pattern | Method Shape | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Takes nothing, returns nothing | `void greet()` | `void greet() { System.out.println("Hi"); }` |
| Takes something, returns nothing | `void setX(int x)` | Mutator |
| Takes nothing, returns something | `int getX()` | Accessor |
| Takes something, returns something | `double area(double r)` | `area = 3.14 * r * r; return area;` |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Method Components

| Component | Syntax Token | Mandatory? | Default Value | Notes for KTU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Access Modifier | `public`, `private`, `protected` | No | package-private | Must be the first token if present |
| Non-Access Modifier | `static`, `final`, `abstract` | No | Instance method | `static` and `abstract` cannot coexist |
| Return Type | `int`, `String`, `void`, ... | **Yes** | — | `void` means no return value |
| Method Name | Identifier (verb) | **Yes** | — | CamelCase convention |
| Parameter List | `(type1 p1, type2 p2, ...)` | **Yes (may be empty)** | `()` | Each parameter needs explicit type |
| Method Body | `{ statements }` | **Yes (except abstract)** | — | Local variables declared here |
| `return` statement | `return value;` | Mandatory for non-void | — | Must be the last executable statement |

### 2.6 Why Methods Matter — Real-World Engineering Utility

In **production-grade software engineering**, methods enable:

- **Code review & testing** — A method is the smallest independently testable unit in Java (unit testing using JUnit targets methods).
- **Version control & refactoring** — Renaming a method is safe because the compiler can trace every caller.
- **API design** — Public methods of a class form its *Application Programming Interface*; well-designed APIs (e.g., Java's `List.add()`) hide complexity.
- **Performance optimisation** — Methods declared `final` or `static` allow JVM *inlining* and faster execution.
- **Parallel & distributed systems** — Each method can potentially be a microservice endpoint in service-oriented architecture (SOA).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

> Since *Introduction to Methods* is a CS-concept topic, the "derivations" here take the form of **exhaustive step-by-step logical traces, stack-frame analyses, and parameter-passing mechanics** — the KTU board examiner's favourite 14-mark question format.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — The Four Method Categories

Let us derive (build) one example of each of the four communication patterns. This is a **classic KTU 7-mark sub-question**.

**Problem:** Design a class `Circle` that stores a radius, and provides all four method types.

```java
class Circle {
    private double radius;          // data member

    // (i) Takes nothing, returns nothing
    public void greet() {
        System.out.println("Circle object created.");
    }

    // (ii) Takes something, returns nothing  (mutator)
    public void setRadius(double r) {
        if (r > 0) {
            this.radius = r;
        }
    }

    // (iii) Takes nothing, returns something  (accessor)
    public double getRadius() {
        return this.radius;
    }

    // (iv) Takes something, returns something
    public double area(double r) {
        return Math.PI * r * r;
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Trace (KTU Valuation Pattern):**

- **Step 1** — Identify data member: `radius` of type `double`, marked `private` for encapsulation.
- **Step 2** — Method `greet()` takes no parameters, returns `void`. The compiler allocates a stack frame with **zero slots for arguments** and **no return value**.
- **Step 3** — Method `setRadius(double r)` has a single formal parameter `r`. On call `c.setRadius(5.0)`, the value `5.0` is copied into `r` (call by value).
- **Step 4** — The keyword `this.radius` distinguishes the instance field from the local parameter `r`.
- **Step 5** — Method `getRadius()` returns the field value; return type matches field type.
- **Step 6** — Method `area(double r)` returns a `double`, computed using `Math.PI`.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Stack Frame Analysis (Board Exam Favourite)

Consider the following driver program that calls a static method.

```java
public class StackDemo {
    static int square(int n) {
        int s = n * n;
        return s;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int x = 4;
        int y = square(x);
        System.out.println("Result = " + y);
    }
}
```

**Execution trace step-by-step:**

- **Step 1** — JVM invokes `main`. Stack frame `main` is created. Local `x` is allocated value `4`.
- **Step 2** — Line `int y = square(x);` is reached. The actual argument `x` (= 4) is **copied** into a new stack frame `square` as formal parameter `n = 4`.
- **Step 3** — Inside `square`, local variable `s = n * n = 16` is computed.
- **Step 4** — `return s;` sends value `16` back to `main`. The `square` frame is **popped** and destroyed.
- **Step 5** — Back in `main`, the value `16` is stored in `y`. The `println` executes.

**Stack frame diagram (vertical text representation):**

| Stack (Top = Active) | Contents |
|---|---|
| `square` (active) | `n = 4`, `s = 16` |
| `main` (waiting) | `x = 4`, `y = ?` |

| After return | Contents |
|---|---|
| (frame popped) | — |
| `main` (resumes) | `x = 4`, `y = 16` |

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Parameter Passing: Call by Value

Java is **strictly call-by-value** for all parameters — both primitive and reference. This is a KTU classic trick question.

```java
public class PassDemo {
    static void modify(int a, int[] arr) {
        a = a + 10;
        arr[0] = arr[0] + 10;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = 5;
        int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};
        modify(a, arr);
        System.out.println("a   = " + a);
        System.out.println("arr[0] = " + arr[0]);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step trace:**

- **Step 1** — `main`: `a = 5`, `arr` points to a heap array `{1, 2, 3}`.
- **Step 2** — Call `modify(a, arr)`. By **call-by-value**:
  - Primitive `a`'s value `5` is **copied** into formal parameter `a` (a *new local variable*).
  - Reference `arr`'s address is **copied** into formal parameter `arr` (the *same address* — both point to the same heap array).
- **Step 3** — Inside `modify`, `a = a + 10;` changes the **local** `a` to `15`. The original `a` in `main` is **unaffected**.
- **Step 4** — Inside `modify`, `arr[0] = arr[0] + 10;` follows the address, modifies the heap array to `{11, 2, 3}`. Because the original `arr` in `main` points to the same heap location, `arr[0]` is now `11`.
- **Step 5** — Final output:
  - `a   = 5`  (unchanged)
  - `arr[0] = 11`  (changed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick-Question Conclusion:**
> Reassigning the reference itself (`arr = new int[5];`) would NOT affect the caller's array — that only changes the local copy of the address. But mutating the *contents* through the reference DOES affect the caller.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Identification in a Code Snippet

Given a KTU board question that supplies a multi-method code listing and asks to "list all methods, their signatures, return types, and access levels", use this matrix:

| # | Method Signature | Return Type | Access | Static? | # Parameters | Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `public static void main(String[])` | `void` | `public` | Yes | 1 (String[]) | Entry point |
| 2 | `private double computeArea(double)` | `double` | `private` | No | 1 | Instance method |
| 3 | `public Circle(int)` | *(none — constructor)* | `public` | No | 1 | Constructor (not a method technically, but a member) |
| 4 | `static int add(int, int)` | `int` | *default* | Yes | 2 | Static utility |

### 3.5 Common Method Declaration Errors (Board-Favourite)

| Code Snippet | Error Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `int add(int a, b)` | Compile error | Each parameter needs its own type |
| `void greet() { return 5; }` | Compile error | `void` methods cannot return a value |
| `int add(int a, int b) { }` | Logic/runtime | Missing `return` statement |
| `public static abstract void run();` | Compile error | `static` and `abstract` are mutually exclusive |
| `int add(int a, int a)` | Compile error | Duplicate parameter names not allowed |
| `static void static()` | Compile error | `static` is a reserved word; cannot be an identifier |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Method Anatomy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["METHOD DECLARATION"]
        AM[Access Modifier\npublic / private / protected]
        NM[Non-Access Modifier\nstatic / final / abstract]
        RT[Return Type\nint / double / void / String]
        MN[Method Name\ncalculateSum]
        PL[Parameter List\nint a, int b]
    end
    subgraph B["METHOD BODY"]
        LV[Local Variable\nDeclarations]
        ST[Statements\nLogic, loops, conditions]
        RS[Return Statement\nreturn value;]
    end
    AM --> NM --> RT --> MN --> PL --> LV --> ST --> RS
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Method Invocation Flow (Call Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Caller invokes method\n e.g., result = square(4);"]
    S2["Step 2: JVM pushes a new\n stack frame onto the call stack"]
    S3["Step 3: Actual arguments are\n copied into formal parameters"]
    S4["Step 4: Method body executes\n line by line"]
    S5{"Step 5: Does the method\n encounter 'return' or end?"}
    S6["Step 6: Return value is sent\n back to the caller's frame"]
    S7["Step 7: Stack frame of method\n is popped and destroyed"]
    S8["Step 8: Caller resumes\n execution from next line"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    S5 -- "Yes" --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
    S5 -- "No, still executing" --> S4
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Four Method Categories at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Methods by I/O Behaviour"]
    A1["Takes Nothing\nReturns Nothing\nvoid greet()"]
    A2["Takes Something\nReturns Nothing\nvoid setX(int x)"]
    A3["Takes Nothing\nReturns Something\nint getX()"]
    A4["Takes Something\nReturns Something\ndouble area(double r)"]
    ROOT --> A1
    ROOT --> A2
    ROOT --> A3
    ROOT --> A4
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Parameter Passing Memory Model (Call by Value)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M["MAIN STACK FRAME"]
        MA["int a = 5\n local copy"]
        MARR["arr = 0xABC\n (address)"]
    end
    subgraph CAL["MODIFY STACK FRAME"]
        CA["int a = 5\n local copy"]
        CARr["arr = 0xABC\n (same address)"]
    end
    subgraph HEAP["HEAP MEMORY"]
        H["int[3] : 0xABC\n [1, 2, 3]"]
    end
    MA -. "value copied" .-> CA
    MARR -. "address copied" .-> CARr
    CARr --> H
    CA -. "reassign a=15\n(local only)" .-> X[Local change]
    CARr -. "mutate arr[0]=11\n(visible to caller)" .-> H
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Method Resolution & Overloading Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CALL["Method call: print(5)\nCompiler checks argument types"]
    M1["print(int)      — exact match"]
    M2["print(long)     — widening"]
    M3["print(double)   — widening"]
    M4["print(String)   — autoboxing / varargs"]
    CALL --> M1
    CALL --> M2
    CALL --> M3
    CALL --> M4
    NOTE["Compiler selects the most\n SPECIFIC match. If ambiguous,\n compile-time error."]
    M1 --> NOTE
    M2 --> NOTE
    M3 --> NOTE
    M4 --> NOTE
```

> [!NOTE]
> Overloading itself is covered in detail in a later KTU module; this diagram is provided as a forward reference so the student understands that **method signatures** (covered here) are the foundation of polymorphism.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define a method in Java. List any four components of a method declaration with examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A method in Java is a member of a class that encapsulates a reusable block of statements to perform a specific task. The four components of a method declaration are:
> 1. **Access Modifier** (e.g., `public`) — controls visibility of the method.
> 2. **Return Type** (e.g., `int`, `void`) — the type of value the method sends back, or `void` for none.
> 3. **Method Name** (e.g., `calculate`) — a valid Java identifier following camelCase convention.
> 4. **Parameter List** (e.g., `int a, int b`) — comma-separated formal parameters, may be empty.
>
> Example: `public int calculate(int a, int b) { return a + b; }`

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a method with return type `void` and a method with a non-void return type. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `void` method | Non-`void` method |
|---|---|---|
| Return value | Does not return any value | Returns a value of declared type |
| `return` statement | Optional; if used, must be `return;` (no value) | Mandatory; must return a value of declared type |
| Use case | Perform an action (print, modify) | Compute and return a result |
| Example | `void display() { System.out.println("Hi"); }` | `int square(int n) { return n*n; }` |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 Choice 1]**

**(a)** Explain the four categories of methods based on input and output with suitable Java examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate *call by value* with both a primitive parameter and an array reference parameter. Show the output and explain the result. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The four categories are:
1. **Takes nothing, returns nothing** — `void greet() { System.out.println("Hello"); }`
2. **Takes something, returns nothing** — `void setAge(int a) { this.age = a; }`
3. **Takes nothing, returns something** — `int getAge() { return this.age; }`
4. **Takes something, returns something** — `int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }`

```java
class Student {
    private int age;

    public void greet() {                              // Type 1
        System.out.println("Welcome!");
    }

    public void setAge(int a) {                        // Type 2
        if (a > 0) this.age = a;
    }

    public int getAge() {                              // Type 3
        return this.age;
    }

    public int add(int a, int b) {                     // Type 4
        return a + b;
    }
}
```

**(b)** Java program demonstrating call-by-value:

```java
public class PassByValueDemo {
    static void modify(int x, int[] arr) {
        x = x + 10;
        arr[0] = arr[0] + 10;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int x = 5;
        int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};
        modify(x, arr);
        System.out.println("x     = " + x);
        System.out.println("arr[0]= " + arr[0]);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
x     = 5
arr[0]= 11
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**
- For the primitive `x`, the value `5` is *copied* into a new local variable in `modify`. Reassigning `x` inside the method only affects the local copy. Hence, `x` in `main` remains `5`.
- For the array `arr`, the **reference (address)** is copied. Both `main`'s `arr` and `modify`'s `arr` point to the *same* heap array. Modifying `arr[0]` mutates the shared object, so the change is visible to `main`. Hence `arr[0] = 11`.
- **Conclusion:** Java is *always* call-by-value; for objects, the *value of the reference* is passed, which is why mutations are visible but reassignments are not.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 Choice 2]**

**(a)** What is a method signature in Java? Explain with two examples why the return type is *not* part of the method signature. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java class `Rectangle` with data members `length` and `breadth`. Define methods to:
  - (i) set the dimensions
  - (ii) compute and return the area
  - (iii) compute and return the perimeter
  - (iv) display the dimensions

  Write a `main` method to demonstrate all four. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A **method signature** in Java is the combination of the method's name and its parameter list (number, types, and order of parameters). It is used by the compiler to uniquely identify methods for overloading and overriding.

The return type is **not** part of the signature because the JVM's method resolution at call sites depends on what arguments are being passed, not on what the caller will do with the result. The compiler must resolve a call like `obj.f(5)` based on the *argument* `5`, regardless of whether the result is assigned, printed, or discarded.

**Example 1 — Legal (different signatures):**
```java
int  compute(int a);
int  compute(int a, int b);
```
Both have the name `compute` but different parameter lists → different signatures → valid.

**Example 2 — Illegal (same signature, different return type):**
```java
int  getValue();
double getValue();   // COMPILE ERROR — same signature, different return type
```
This produces a compile-time error: *"method getValue() is already defined"*.

**(b)** Java class implementation:

```java
class Rectangle {
    private double length;
    private double breadth;

    public void setDimensions(double l, double b) {       // (i)
        this.length  = l;
        this.breadth = b;
    }

    public double area() {                                // (ii)
        return this.length * this.breadth;
    }

    public double perimeter() {                           // (iii)
        return 2 * (this.length + this.breadth);
    }

    public void display() {                               // (iv)
        System.out.println("Length  = " + this.length);
        System.out.println("Breadth = " + this.breadth);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Rectangle r = new Rectangle();
        r.setDimensions(10.0, 5.0);
        r.display();
        System.out.println("Area      = " + r.area());
        System.out.println("Perimeter = " + r.perimeter());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Length  = 10.0
Breadth = 5.0
Area      = 50.0
Perimeter = 30.0
```

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Methods Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing `void` with no-return-type** — Many students write `void` in constructors or omit `void` in regular methods, losing 1–2 marks. **Constructor has *no* return type at all**; ordinary void method *must* explicitly state `void`.
> 2. **Forgetting to copy the value of parameters** — In a "call by value vs reference" question, students often state "Java passes primitives by value and objects by reference." This is **WRONG**. Correct statement: *"Java is always pass-by-value; the value of the reference is copied for objects."* This phrasing alone can fetch full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting `this` keyword** — When parameter names shadow field names, omitting `this.radius` leads to self-assignment bugs. Examiners look for `this` usage in setter methods.
> 4. **Returning the wrong type** — Writing `return "5";` from a method declared `int add()` causes a compile error. KTU examiners often hide type-mismatch bugs in the last test case.
> 5. **Static method calling instance method directly** — Inside a `static` method, you cannot directly call a non-static method without an object reference. Many students write this incorrectly.
> 6. **Missing `return` in non-void methods** — A method declared `int add(int a, int b)` *must* return an `int`. Missing the statement causes a *"missing return statement"* compile error.
> 7. **Method signature with array brackets** — `int[] arr` and `int arr[]` are *equivalent*, but mixing them carelessly confuses readers. Standard is `int[] arr`.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **method** is a named, reusable member function inside a class that defines an object's behaviour.
- A method declaration has **six parts**: access modifier, non-access modifier, return type, method name, parameter list, body.
- The **method signature = name + parameter list**; the return type is *not* included.
- `void` means the method returns *no value*; a non-`void` method *must* have a `return value;` statement matching the declared type.
- **Four communication patterns:** `( ) → ( )`, `(P) → ( )`, `( ) → (R)`, `(P) → (R)`, where `P` = parameters, `R` = return value.
- Every method call creates a **stack frame**; it is destroyed when the method returns.
- Java is **always call-by-value**: primitives copy their value; objects copy the reference value.
- A method declared `static` belongs to the **class** and is invoked via `ClassName.method()`; non-static (instance) methods require an object.
- A method name should be a **verb** in **camelCase** (e.g., `calculateTotal`).
- Parameter names must be **unique** within the parameter list, and each must have an **explicit type**.
- A method declared `final` **cannot be overridden** in subclasses; a method declared `abstract` has **no body** and must be overridden.
- The `main` method has the exact signature: `public static void main(String[] args)`.
- The compiler uses method signatures for **overloading resolution**; ambiguity in argument-type matching causes a compile-time error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Constructors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: CONSTRUCTORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> A **constructor** in Object-Oriented Programming is a special member function (method) of a class that is automatically invoked when an object (instance) of the class is created. It is primarily used to **initialize** the newly allocated object's data members to a valid, well-defined state. Constructors share the class name, have **no return type** (not even `void`), and cannot be `static`, `abstract`, or `final` in Java.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the construct is examined under **Course Outcome CO1** (Apply the concepts of object-oriented programming) at the **Apply** and **Analyze** levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: House Construction**
> Think of a class as an **architectural blueprint** for a house. The blueprint itself is not a house — you cannot live in it. When a builder (the `new` keyword in Java) starts constructing a house from that blueprint, certain things **must happen immediately and automatically**:
> - Doors and windows must be installed.
> - Electrical wiring must be completed.
> - The house must be painted a base color.
> - An address number must be assigned.
>
> A **constructor is exactly this initialization phase**. It is the "construction crew" that runs the moment an object is built, guaranteeing that no object ever exists in an "uninitialized" or "broken" state. Just as a builder cannot skip wiring a house, the JVM **cannot skip calling a constructor** when you write `new ClassName()`.

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Properties

A constructor differs from a normal method in five mandatory ways — these are **high-frequency KTU questions**:

1. **Name**: Constructor name **must match** the class name exactly (case-sensitive).
2. **Return Type**: Constructors have **no return type** — neither `void` nor any data type.
3. **Invocation**: Called **automatically** by the JVM when an object is created via `new`.
4. **Inheritance**: Constructors are **not inherited** by subclasses (though a superclass constructor is always called).
5. **Overriding**: Constructors **cannot be overridden**, but they **can be overloaded**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight**
> Per the KTU 2024 PECMT535 syllabus (Module 1), constructors are categorized into four types: **Default, Parameterized, Copy, and Constructor Overloading**. Mastery of all four is mandatory for ESE (End Semester Examination).

### 1.4 Types of Constructors (Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 Scheme formally classifies constructors into the following categories:

| # | Type | Purpose |
|---|------|---------|
| 1 | **Default Constructor** | Zero-argument constructor, auto-supplied by compiler if none defined |
| 2 | **No-arg (User-defined) Constructor** | Explicitly written zero-argument constructor |
| 3 | **Parameterized Constructor** | Accepts arguments to initialize fields with custom values |
| 4 | **Copy Constructor** | Initializes an object using another object of the same class |
| 5 | **Constructor Overloading** | Multiple constructors in a class with different parameter lists |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "What, Why, How" of Constructors

### 2.1.1 The Default Constructor

**What it is**: A no-argument constructor that the Java compiler **automatically inserts** into any class that does not explicitly declare any constructor.

**Why it exists**: To guarantee that every object can be instantiated with `new ClassName()`. Without it, basic frameworks (Spring, Servlets, JPA, etc.) that rely on reflection-based instantiation would break.

**How it works**:
- If a class declares **no constructor**, the compiler synthesizes a public default constructor.
- If a class declares **any constructor**, the compiler **does not** synthesize a default one.
- The default constructor calls `super()` (i.e., the no-arg constructor of the parent class) implicitly as its first statement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question (Frequent in Part A)**
> *"If a class defines only a parameterized constructor, what happens when we write `new MyClass()`?"*
> **Answer**: A **compilation error** occurs because the compiler no longer supplies a default constructor — it only generates one if the class has *zero* declared constructors.

### 2.1.2 The No-Arg (User-Defined) Constructor

A constructor explicitly written with an empty parameter list. It serves the same syntactic role as the default but contains custom initialization logic.

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;
    
    // User-defined no-arg constructor
    Student() {
        name = "Not Assigned";
        rollNo = 0;
    }
}
```

### 2.1.3 The Parameterized Constructor

Accepts one or more parameters, allowing the caller to pass initial values during object creation. This is the **most widely used** constructor form in production code.

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;
    
    Student(String n, int r) {
        name = n;
        rollNo = r;
    }
}

// Usage
Student s1 = new Student("Arjun", 42);
```

**Why it matters**: It enforces the OOP principle of *constructing fully-valid objects* — an object is never half-initialized.

### 2.1.4 The Copy Constructor

A constructor that takes **a single argument** of the same class type and copies the field values of that source object into the new object.

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;
    
    Student(Student other) {  // Copy Constructor
        this.name = other.name;
        this.rollNo = other.rollNo;
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**
> In C++, the copy constructor is invoked during **pass-by-value** and **return-by-value**. Java does **not** have a built-in copy constructor (unlike C++), so a user-defined one is optional but recommended for classes containing mutable object references (deep copy scenarios).

### 2.1.5 Constructor Overloading

Defining **multiple constructors** within the same class, distinguished by their **parameter list** (number, type, or order of parameters). This is a direct application of **polymorphism** at the constructor level (compile-time polymorphism).

```java
class Box {
    double width, height, depth;
    
    Box()                              { width = height = depth = 0; }      // (1)
    Box(double w, double h, double d)   { width = w; height = h; depth = d; } // (2)
    Box(double side)                   { width = height = depth = side; }    // (3)
    Box(Box other)                     {                                     // (4)
        this.width  = other.width;
        this.height = other.height;
        this.depth  = other.depth;
    }
}
```

**Resolution**: The compiler selects the correct constructor at **compile time** based on the argument list — this is **static binding**.

### 2.1.6 Constructor Chaining (`this()` Call)

A constructor can call **another constructor of the same class** using `this(...)` as the **first statement**. This avoids code duplication.

```java
class Employee {
    int id;
    String name;
    double salary;
    
    Employee() {
        this(0, "Unknown", 0.0);        // chains to 3-arg constructor
    }
    Employee(int id) {
        this(id, "Unknown", 0.0);       // chains to 3-arg constructor
    }
    Employee(int id, String name, double salary) {
        this.id = id;
        this.name = name;
        this.salary = salary;
    }
}
```

**Rules**:
- `this(...)` must be the **first statement** in the constructor body.
- The chain **must terminate** at some base constructor (otherwise infinite recursion → `StackOverflowError`).
- Cycles are caught at **runtime**, not compile time.

### 2.1.7 The `super()` Call and Inheritance Interaction

When a subclass object is created, Java enforces a **constructor invocation chain** up the inheritance hierarchy:
1. Subclass constructor is called.
2. Its first statement (implicit or explicit) is `super(...)` — invoking a parent constructor.
3. This continues until `Object`'s constructor is reached.
4. Then the chain unwinds, executing each constructor body top-down.

> [!WARNING]
> **If** the parent class has only a parameterized constructor, the subclass constructor **must** explicitly call `super(args)` as its first line. Omitting it is a compile-time error.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Construct | Syntax Rule | Mandatory? | Frequency in KTU Papers |
|---|-----------|-------------|------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Class name = Constructor name | Case-sensitive match | ✅ Yes | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | No return type (not even `void`) | Compiler distinguishes by absence | ✅ Yes | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | Cannot be `static` | JVM must bind to an instance | ✅ Yes | ★★★★ |
| 4 | Cannot be `final` / `abstract` | Constructors aren't inherited, so no override semantics | ✅ Yes | ★★★ |
| 5 | `this(...)` for chaining | Must be **first statement** | ⚠️ Optional | ★★★★ |
| 6 | `super(...)` for parent call | Implicit if omitted and parent has no-arg ctor | ⚠️ Implicit | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | Overloading via parameter list | Number / Type / Order differ | ✅ Permitted | ★★★★★ |
| 8 | Overriding constructors | Not allowed | ❌ Prohibited | ★★★★ |
| 9 | Default constructor auto-generation | Only if no constructor is declared | ⚠️ Conditional | ★★★★★ |
| 10 | Copy constructor signature | `ClassName(ClassName obj)` | ⚠️ Optional | ★★★ |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application of Constructors |
|--------|----------------------------|
| **Database / ORM (Hibernate, JPA)** | Entities require a **no-arg constructor** for reflection-based instantiation by the framework. |
| **Dependency Injection (Spring)** | Beans are instantiated by the IoC container using constructors; constructor injection is the **recommended** pattern for mandatory dependencies. |
| **GUI Frameworks (Swing, JavaFX)** | UI components (e.g., `JButton("OK")`) use parameterized constructors to set initial properties. |
| **Immutable Classes (String, Integer)** | Constructors enforce immutability by setting `final` fields once — no setters allowed. |
| **Game Development** | `Player("Hero", 100, new Sword())` — constructor builds a fully-equipped game entity. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Code Walkthroughs & Logical Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Trace: Object Creation Lifecycle

When the statement `new Student("Arjun", 42)` is executed, the following **deterministic sequence** of events occurs. Each step is exhaustive and must be understood for KTU's analysis-level questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trace Notation**: We denote the call stack as a top-down ordered list — top of list = most recent active frame.

**Step 1 — Compile-time Resolution**

The compiler examines the argument list `("Arjun", 42)` and selects the matching constructor signature from the class's **constructor descriptor table**.

**Step 2 — Memory Allocation on the Heap**

The JVM allocates a contiguous block of memory in the **Young Generation** (Eden space) of the heap. All instance variables (`name`, `rollNo`) are assigned their **default zero-values**:

$$\text{For reference types: } \texttt{name} \leftarrow \texttt{null}$$

$$\text{For numeric primitives: } \texttt{rollNo} \leftarrow \texttt{0}$$

**Step 3 — Constructor Invocation**

The JVM pushes a new stack frame for the chosen `Student(String, int)` constructor. The implicit parameter `this` is bound to the newly allocated heap object.

**Step 4 — Field Initialization in Three Sub-Phases**

The body of the constructor executes in this strict order:

- **4a**. All **instance initializer blocks** run (in textual order).
- **4b**. All **field initializers** run (in textual order).
- **4c**. The **constructor body** (statements after `super()`/`this()`) runs.

**Step 5 — Reference Assignment**

The memory address of the initialized object is assigned to the reference variable `s1` in the calling stack frame.

**Step 6 — Object Reaches "Live" State**

The object is now eligible for use. It is **fully constructed** — the JVM guarantees that no thread will observe a partially constructed object (this is part of the **Java Memory Model's happens-before** guarantees for `final` fields).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Code Example: All Four Constructor Types in One Class

The following Java program demonstrates the four KTU-mandated constructor types and their interaction. Every line is annotated for examiner-readability.

```java
// File: Rectangle.java
class Rectangle {
    double length;
    double breadth;

    // (A) User-defined NO-ARG constructor
    Rectangle() {
        length = 1.0;
        breadth = 1.0;
        System.out.println("No-arg constructor called: " + length + "x" + breadth);
    }

    // (B) PARAMETERIZED constructor
    Rectangle(double l, double b) {
        length = l;
        breadth = b;
        System.out.println("Parameterized constructor called: " + length + "x" + breadth);
    }

    // (C) COPY constructor
    Rectangle(Rectangle other) {
        this.length  = other.length;
        this.breadth = other.breadth;
        System.out.println("Copy constructor called: " + length + "x" + breadth);
    }

    double area() {
        return length * breadth;
    }

    // (D) Driver demonstrating constructor OVERLOADING
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle();              // → calls (A)
        Rectangle r2 = new Rectangle(5.0, 3.0);      // → calls (B)
        Rectangle r3 = new Rectangle(r2);            // → calls (C)

        System.out.println("r1 area = " + r1.area());
        System.out.println("r2 area = " + r2.area());
        System.out.println("r3 area = " + r3.area());
    }
}
```

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Line-by-line** what happens when `main` runs:

| Statement | Action | Active Constructor |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|
| `new Rectangle();` | Allocates heap object, defaults fields to 0.0, then calls (A) | (A) No-arg |
| `length = 1.0;` | Sets instance field on `r1` | (A) |
| `breadth = 1.0;` | Sets instance field on `r1` | (A) |
| `new Rectangle(5.0, 3.0);` | Allocates heap object, calls (B) with `l=5.0, b=3.0` | (B) Parameterized |
| `length = 5.0;` | Sets `r2.length` | (B) |
| `breadth = 3.0;` | Sets `r2.breadth` | (B) |
| `new Rectangle(r2);` | Allocates heap object, calls (C) with `other` = `r2` | (C) Copy |
| `this.length = other.length;` | Copies `r2.length` (5.0) into `r3.length` | (C) |
| `this.breadth = other.breadth;` | Copies `r2.breadth` (3.0) into `r3.breadth` | (C) |
| `r1.area()` | Computes $1.0 \times 1.0 = 1.0$ | (D) main |
| `r2.area()` | Computes $5.0 \times 3.0 = 15.0$ | (D) main |
| `r3.area()` | Computes $5.0 \times 3.0 = 15.0$ | (D) main |

### 3.2.2 Expected Output

```
No-arg constructor called: 1.0x1.0
Parameterized constructor called: 5.0x3.0
Copy constructor called: 5.0x3.0
r1 area = 1.0
r2 area = 15.0
r3 area = 15.0
```

## 3.3 Constructor Chaining — Full Logical Derivation

Consider the `Employee` class with the three constructors shown in §2.1.6.

**Derivation of call flow for `new Employee()`**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{new Employee()} &\xrightarrow{\text{this}(0, \text{``Unknown''}, 0.0)} \texttt{Employee(int, String, double)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{implicit super()} \text{ or } \text{super()} \text{ in chain}} \texttt{Object()}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step unwinding** (top-down execution of bodies):

| Order | Constructor | Body Execution | `this` binds to |
|-------|-------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | `Object()` | (empty body) | new `Employee` object |
| 2 | `Employee(int, String, double)` | `id=0; name="Unknown"; salary=0.0;` | new `Employee` object |
| 3 | `Employee()` | (empty body — only `this(...)` call) | new `Employee` object |

**Resulting state of fields**:
- `id = 0`
- `name = "Unknown"`
- `salary = 0.0`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake**: Students often confuse *constructor chaining* with *method overloading*. Chaining is **sequential delegation within the same class**; overloading is **multiple definitions, one chosen at compile time**.

## 3.4 Default Constructor Logic — When Does It Disappear?

**Logical Rule Derivation**:

Let $C$ be a class. Let $n$ be the number of user-declared constructors in $C$.

$$
\text{Compiler-generated default constructor exists} \iff n = 0
$$

**Consequence Matrix**:

| Scenario | User-Defined Constructors Present? | Compiler Auto-Generates Default? | `new ClassName()` Valid? |
|----------|------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Class with **no** constructors | 0 | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Class with **only** a parameterized ctor | $\geq 1$ | ❌ No | ❌ Compile Error |
| Class with **only** a no-arg user ctor | $\geq 1$ | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (uses user ctor) |
| Class with **both** no-arg and parameterized | $\geq 1$ | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (uses no-arg) |

> [!WARNING]
> **If a parent class lacks a no-arg constructor and you fail to explicitly call `super(args)` in every subclass constructor, the code fails to compile.** This is one of the most common reasons for the dreaded "implicit super constructor is undefined" error in KTU lab exams.

## 3.5 Shallow Copy vs Deep Copy in Copy Constructors

**Derivation**: For a class with a primitive field and a reference field:

$$
\text{Class } C: \quad \text{field}_1 : \text{int}, \quad \text{field}_2 : \text{Object}
$$

**Shallow copy** (default copy constructor behavior):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{this.field}_1 &= \texttt{other.field}_1 \quad \text{(value copy of primitive)} \\
\texttt{this.field}_2 &= \texttt{other.field}_2 \quad \text{(reference copy — same object!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Deep copy** (must be implemented explicitly):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{this.field}_1 &= \texttt{other.field}_1 \\
\texttt{this.field}_2 &= \texttt{new Object(other.field}_2) \quad \text{(fresh object)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Impact**: In a shallow copy, mutating `this.field2` mutates `other.field2` too. This violates encapsulation in multi-threaded contexts. KTU question pattern: *"When is a deep-copy constructor necessary?"* — Answer: when the class contains **mutable reference-type fields**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a constructor and a method in Java. Can a constructor be declared `final` or `static`? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Aspect | Constructor | Method |
|--------|-------------|--------|
| **Purpose** | Initializes a newly created object | Represents behavior of an object |
| **Name** | Must be same as class name | Any valid identifier |
| **Return Type** | None (not even `void`) | Must have a return type (or `void`) |
| **Invocation** | Automatic via `new` | Explicit call via reference |
| **Inheritance** | Not inherited | Inherited (can be overridden) |
| **Overriding** | Not possible | Possible (runtime polymorphism) |

A constructor **cannot be declared `final`** because `final` prevents overriding, and constructors are not inherited or overridden in the first place — declaring it is a compile-time error. A constructor **cannot be `static`** because static members belong to the class, not to an instance, while a constructor's purpose is to initialize an instance. *(3 Marks: 1 for table, 1 for `final` reason, 1 for `static` reason)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a default constructor? When does the Java compiler fail to provide one?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
