A **default constructor** is a no-argument constructor that the Java compiler automatically synthesizes and inserts into a class **only if the class does not explicitly declare any constructor**. Its body simply invokes `super()` (the no-arg constructor of the parent class) implicitly.

**When the compiler fails to provide a default constructor**:
- When the class contains **at least one explicitly declared constructor** (of any kind — parameterized, no-arg user-defined, or copy constructor).
- In such a case, attempting to invoke `new ClassName()` with no arguments will produce a **compilation error**: *"no suitable constructor found for ClassName()"* unless the user has explicitly written a matching no-arg constructor.

Example of the failure case:
```java
class Demo {
    Demo(int x) { /* parameterized only */ }
}
// Demo d = new Demo();  // ❌ COMPILE ERROR — no default exists
```
*(3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the condition, 1 for example/error message)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Module 1 contains one 14-mark question. Students answer **one of two choices**. Each choice has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the different types of constructors in Java with suitable examples. Discuss the concept of constructor overloading. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

Java supports four primary types of constructors:

**1. Default Constructor** — Auto-generated by the compiler when no constructor is declared.
```java
class Car {
    int speed;
    // Compiler provides: Car() { super(); }
}
```

**2. No-Arg (User-defined) Constructor** — Explicitly written with no parameters, used for default initialization logic.
```java
Car() { speed = 60; }
```

**3. Parameterized Constructor** — Accepts arguments for custom initialization.
```java
Car(int s) { speed = s; }
```

**4. Copy Constructor** — Initializes a new object from an existing one.
```java
Car(Car other) { this.speed = other.speed; }
```

**Constructor Overloading**: Defining multiple constructors in the same class with different parameter signatures (number, type, or order). The compiler resolves the correct constructor at compile time based on the argument list — this is **compile-time polymorphism**.

```java
class Car {
    int speed;
    Car()              { speed = 0; }
    Car(int s)         { speed = s; }
    Car(int s, int x)  { speed = s + x; }  // overload
}
```

The benefits are: flexibility in object creation, default and custom initialization in one class, and improved API usability. *(7 Marks: 2 for types list, 3 for examples, 2 for overloading explanation)*

---

**(b)** What is constructor chaining in Java? Explain with an example using the `this()` keyword. What happens if `this()` is not the first statement? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Constructor Chaining** is the mechanism by which one constructor invokes another constructor of the same class using `this(...)` as the first statement. It eliminates code duplication and centralizes initialization logic in a single "base" constructor.

**Example**:
```java
class Student {
    int id;
    String name;
    int age;

    Student() {
        this(101, "Default", 18);          // chains to 3-arg ctor
    }
    Student(int id) {
        this(id, "Unknown", 18);            // chains to 3-arg ctor
    }
    Student(int id, String name, int age) {
        this.id = id;
        this.name = name;
        this.age = age;
    }
}
```

**Execution trace for `new Student()`**:
1. `Student()` body → calls `this(101, "Default", 18)`.
2. `Student(int, String, int)` body executes → sets all three fields.
3. Control returns to `Student()`, which has no further body statements.

**What if `this()` is NOT the first statement?**
The compiler issues a **compile-time error**: *"call to this must be first statement in constructor"*. This rule guarantees a deterministic and acyclic construction order — without it, partially initialized objects could be created and leaked, breaking the JMM's safety guarantees. *(7 Marks: 2 for definition, 3 for example+trace, 2 for the rule and error explanation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the role of constructors in inheritance. How does the `super()` keyword work? What happens if the parent class has only a parameterized constructor? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Role in Inheritance**: When a subclass object is created, the JVM must construct the **entire inheritance chain**, not just the subclass portion. Therefore, the subclass constructor must invoke a parent class constructor as its first step.

**How `super()` works**:
- Every subclass constructor implicitly calls `super()` (the no-arg parent constructor) as its first statement.
- This call propagates **up the hierarchy** until it reaches `Object()`.
- The bodies of constructors are then executed in **reverse order** (top-down unwinding).

**Example**:
```java
class Animal {
    Animal() { System.out.println("Animal constructor"); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    Dog() {
        super();  // implicit, can be omitted
        System.out.println("Dog constructor");
    }
}
// new Dog();  → Output: "Animal constructor" then "Dog constructor"
```

**If parent has only a parameterized constructor**:
The subclass **must** explicitly call `super(args)` as its first line; otherwise a **compile error** occurs: *"no default constructor available in ParentClass"*.

```java
class Animal {
    Animal(String name) { /* ... */ }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    Dog() {
        super("Generic Dog");  // MUST be explicit
        // ...
    }
}
```
*(7 Marks: 2 for role, 2 for super() mechanics, 3 for parameterized-parent case with example)*

---

**(b)** Differentiate between a copy constructor and the assignment operator (`=`) for object copying. Write a Java program demonstrating a copy constructor with both shallow and deep copy considerations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Difference Table**:

| Aspect | Copy Constructor | Assignment Operator (`=`) |
|--------|------------------|---------------------------|
| **What it does** | Creates a **new object** initialized from another | Reuses an **existing object**, copies reference fields only |
| **Memory** | Allocates fresh memory on heap | No new allocation (just pointer/field copy) |
| **Usage** | `ClassName c2 = new ClassName(c1);` | `c2 = c1;` (both point to same object) |
| **Independence** | New object is independent of source | Objects are **aliased** (shared) |
| **Overloadable** | Yes (multiple constructors possible) | Cannot be overloaded for primitives |

**Program demonstrating Copy Constructor (Shallow Copy)**:
```java
class Address {
    String city;
    Address(String c) { city = c; }
}

class Employee {
    int id;
    String name;
    Address addr;  // reference field

    // Parameterized constructor
    Employee(int i, String n, Address a) {
        id = i; name = n; addr = a;
    }

    // Shallow copy constructor
    Employee(Employee e) {
        this.id = e.id;
        this.name = e.name;
        this.addr = e.addr;  // shared reference!
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println(id + " " + name + " " + addr.city);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Address a1 = new Address("Kochi");
        Employee e1 = new Employee(1, "Arjun", a1);
        Employee e2 = new Employee(e1);  // shallow copy

        e2.addr.city = "Trivandrum";  // mutates shared Address
        e1.display();  // → 1 Arjun Trivandrum (also affected!)
    }
}
```

**Deep Copy Version** (mutually independent):
```java
Employee(Employee e) {
    this.id = e.id;
    this.name = e.name;
    this.addr = new Address(e.addr.city);  // new Address object
}
```

With deep copy, mutating `e2.addr.city` does **not** affect `e1.addr.city`. *(7 Marks: 2 for difference table, 3 for shallow program, 2 for deep-copy fix and explanation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Constructor Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting "no return type"** rule: Students often write `void Student()` thinking it is a constructor — this is a **method**, not a constructor. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "default constructor" with "no-arg user constructor"**: They behave similarly, but only the former is auto-generated. Examiners test this distinction.
> 3. **Claiming constructors are inherited**: **They are NOT.** Only methods and fields are inherited. Subclasses must explicitly chain via `super()` or `this()`.
> 4. **Placing `this()` or `super()` after the first statement**: This is a **compile-time error** — examiners love this trick question.
> 5. **Writing a return type for a constructor (e.g., `int Student()`)**: It becomes a regular method with the same name as the class. Zero marks for the constructor definition.
> 6. **Not distinguishing shallow vs deep copy** in copy constructor questions — easy 2-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Constructors**

- ✅ A **constructor** is a special member function with the **same name as the class** and **no return type**.
- ✅ Constructors are **invoked automatically** by the JVM when an object is created via `new`.
- ✅ **Types**: Default (compiler-generated), No-arg (user-defined), Parameterized, Copy, and Overloaded.
- ✅ The **default constructor** is generated **only if no other constructor is declared**.
- ✅ If any constructor is defined, calling `new ClassName()` requires an explicit no-arg constructor — otherwise **compile error**.
- ✅ **Constructor overloading** is **compile-time polymorphism** based on parameter list differences.
- ✅ **Constructor chaining** uses `this(args)` and **must be the first statement**.
- ✅ The **`super(args)`** call invokes a parent class constructor and is also **implicit if not stated** (assuming parent has a no-arg ctor).
- ✅ Constructors **cannot** be `static`, `final`, or `abstract`.
- ✅ Constructors **cannot be overridden** (but can be **overloaded**).
- ✅ **Shallow copy** duplicates references; **deep copy** duplicates referenced objects.
- ✅ In a constructor body, the order of execution is: **super()/this() → instance initializer blocks → field initializers → constructor body**.
- ✅ Java has **no destructors** — the `finalize()` method (deprecated since Java 9) was the closest equivalent, but Garbage Collection handles cleanup.
- ✅ Constructors enforce the OOP principle: **objects are born in a valid state**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Modifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Access Modifiers ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Object-Oriented Programming, **Access Modifiers** (also called **access specifiers** or **visibility modifiers**) are reserved keywords that govern the **accessibility**, **scope**, and **visibility** of classes, interfaces, methods, constructors, and data members within a program. They are a direct implementation of the OOP pillar of **Encapsulation** and **Data Hiding**, enforcing controlled interaction between objects. In **Java** (the primary language for PECMT535), there are **four** standard access levels. In **C++**, three explicit access specifiers exist (`public`, `protected`, `private`), with package-level access modeled through unnamed `namespace`/`friend` semantics. This module focuses primarily on the **Java model**, which KTU examiners prefer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Access modifiers are categorized under *Encapsulation and Data Abstraction* in Module 1. Memorize the **exact 4×4 accessibility matrix** — it is a guaranteed question in either Part A or Part B. ### 1.2 The Four Access Modifiers (Java) | # | Modifier | Keyword | Accessibility Scope | |---|----------|---------|---------------------| | 1 | **Private** | `private` | Same **class** only | | 2 | **Default** (Package-Private) | *(no keyword)* | Same **package** | | 3 | **Protected** | `protected` | Same **package** + **subclasses** (even in other packages) | | 4 | **Public** | `public` | **Everywhere** (any class, any package) | > [!NOTE] > **Default is NOT a keyword.** When no access modifier is written, the compiler applies *package-private* access automatically. This is the most commonly confused point in KTU exams. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Access Card" Imagine a corporate office building with **four security tiers** to enter different rooms: - **`private`** → Your **personal locker**. Only *you* (the class itself) can open it. Your colleagues in the same floor or other floors cannot. - **`default`** → Your **department floor**. Anyone in the same department (package) can enter, but people from other departments cannot. - **`protected`** → Your **department floor + trusted relatives (subclasses)**. Department members get in, AND your children/extended family (inherited classes from other packages) also get a special visitor pass. - **`public`** → The **building lobby**. Anyone from any organization in the world can walk in. ### 1.4 Why Access Modifiers Matter (The "Why") - **Security:** Prevent unauthorized modification of sensitive data (e.g., bank balance). - **Maintainability:** Internal implementation can change without breaking external code. - **API Design:** Clearly define what a class exposes to the outside world. - **Encapsulation:** Bind data + methods and restrict external tampering. - **Inheritance Control:** Manage what subclasses may inherit and use. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Breakdown of Each Modifier #### **(a) `private` — The Most Restrictive** - Accessible **only within the same class** where it is declared. - **NOT** accessible by subclasses (even in the same package). - **NOT** accessible by any other class in the same package. - Typical use: **data members** of a class (variables holding state). > [!IMPORTANT] > In Java, a class cannot be `private` at the **top level** (only nested/inner classes can be `private`). #### **(b) Default (Package-Private) — No Keyword** - Accessible to **every class within the same package**. - **NOT** accessible to classes in other packages, even if they are subclasses. - Default access promotes **package-level encapsulation** — useful for tightly coupled helper classes. #### **(c) `protected` — The Inheritance-Friendly Modifier** - Accessible to **all classes in the same package** (like default). - Additionally, accessible to **subclasses in any other package** through **inheritance**. - The catch: In a subclass outside the package, the `protected` member is accessible **only via references of the subclass type** (or its subclasses), not via parent-class references. - Typical use: Methods intended to be overridden by subclasses but hidden from the world. #### **(d) `public` — The Most Permissive** - Accessible from **any class in any package**. - No restrictions whatsoever. - Typical use: Methods forming the public API (getters, setters, service methods). ### 2.2 Rules, Restrictions, and Boundary Conditions 1. **Top-level classes** can only be `public` or default. They cannot be `private` or `protected`. 2. **Constructors** follow the same access rules as members; a `private` constructor prevents external instantiation (used in **Singleton Design Pattern**). 3. **Methods marked `private`** cannot be overridden — they are not visible to subclasses. 4. **Methods marked `static`** still respect access control; `private static` means the method is bound to the class and not accessible outside. 5. **`final` + Access Modifier:** `final` prevents modification; access modifier controls visibility — these are orthogonal concepts. 6. **In C++:** `private` and `protected` members are accessible to **friend** classes/functions; Java has **no `friend` keyword** — it uses the `protected` modifier + same-package logic instead. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Accessibility Matrix The following is the **canonical 4×4 table** that KTU examiners expect you to reproduce. The rows represent the **modifier**; the columns represent the **access location**. | Modifier | Same Class | Same Package (Non-Subclass) | Subclass in Other Package | Other Package (Non-Subclass) | |----------|:----------:|:---------------------------:|:-------------------------:|:-----------------------------:| | `private` | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | | `default` | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | | `protected` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | | `public` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** A ✓ means *accessible*; ✗ means *NOT accessible*. KTU often asks the "tricky" cell — for example, "Can a subclass in a different package access a `default` member?" The answer is **NO** (a classic trick question). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking Software:** Account `balance` is `private`; `deposit()` and `withdraw()` are `public`. - **API Libraries (e.g., Java's `Collections`):** Public interfaces hide complex internal algorithms. - **Framework Design (e.g., Spring, Android SDK):** `protected` hooks allow third-party developers to extend framework classes while keeping the internal engine locked. - **Microservices:** `public` endpoints define the service contract; internal `private` helpers hide business logic. ### 2.5 Interaction with Other OOP Features | OOP Concept | How Access Modifiers Interact | |-------------|-------------------------------| | **Inheritance** | `private` members are **not inherited**; `protected` members are inherited and accessible in subclasses. | | **Polymorphism** | Overridden methods **cannot have lower visibility** (e.g., overriding a `public` method as `private` causes a compile error). | | **Abstraction** | Abstract methods must be `public` or `protected` (cannot be `private`). | | **Interfaces** | All members in an interface are implicitly `public` (and `static` for fields, pre-Java 8). | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Examples, Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — Each Modifier in Action We will use a single parent class and test its members from four different access points. This is the **classic KTU examiner setup**. #### **Step 1 — Define the Parent Class `Vehicle` (Package: `transport`)** ```java package transport; public class Vehicle { private String engineNumber = "ENG-001"; // private String fuelType = "Petrol"; // default protected int maxSpeed = 180; // protected public String brand = "GenericMotors"; // public private void serviceLog() { System.out.println("Service done."); } void refuel() { System.out.println("Refueling."); } protected void honk() { System.out.println("Beep beep!"); } public void start() { System.out.println("Engine started."); } } ``` #### **Step 2 — Test from the Same Class (No Restriction)** ```java package transport; public class TestSameClass { public static void main(String[] args) { Vehicle v = new Vehicle(); System.out.println(v.engineNumber); // ✓ ALLOWED — same class System.out.println(v.fuelType); // ✓ ALLOWED System.out.println(v.maxSpeed); // ✓ ALLOWED System.out.println(v.brand); // ✓ ALLOWED v.serviceLog(); // ✓ ALLOWED v.refuel(); // ✓ ALLOWED v.honk(); // ✓ ALLOWED v.start(); // ✓ ALLOWED } } ``` **Logic:** All four modifiers allow access from within the class that defines them. This row is *always green*. #### **Step 3 — Test from a Non-Subclass in the Same Package** ```java package transport; public class TestSamePackage { public static void main(String[] args) { Vehicle v = new Vehicle(); // System.out.println(v.engineNumber); // ✗ ERROR — private System.out.println(v.fuelType); // ✓ ALLOWED — default System.out.println(v.maxSpeed); // ✓ ALLOWED — protected System.out.println(v.brand); // ✓ ALLOWED — public // v.serviceLog(); // ✗ ERROR — private v.refuel(); // ✓ ALLOWED v.honk(); // ✓ ALLOWED v.start(); // ✓ ALLOWED } } ``` **Logic:** `private` is **excluded**. `default`, `protected`, and `public` are accessible because all are within `package transport`. #### **Step 4 — Test from a Subclass in a DIFFERENT Package** ```java package showroom; import transport.Vehicle; public class Car extends Vehicle { void test() { // System.out.println(engineNumber); // ✗ ERROR — private (NOT inherited) // System.out.println(fuelType); // ✗ ERROR — default (different package, non-inherited) System.out.println(maxSpeed); // ✓ ALLOWED — protected (inherited) System.out.println(brand); // ✓ ALLOWED — public // serviceLog(); // ✗ ERROR — private // refuel(); // ✗ ERROR — default honk(); // ✓ ALLOWED — protected start(); // ✓ ALLOWED — public } } ``` **Logic:** This is the **trickiest row** in the matrix. The subclass `Car` can access `protected` and `public` members it has inherited. It **cannot** access `private` (truly hidden) or `default` (package boundary crossed). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** "If `Car` extends `Vehicle`, can `Car` use the `default` member `fuelType`?" — **NO**, because the subclass resides in a different package. The default modifier is tied to the package, not the inheritance chain. #### **Step 5 — Test from a Non-Subclass in a Different Package** ```java package showroom; import transport.Vehicle; public class Customer { public static void main(String[] args) { Vehicle v = new Vehicle(); // System.out.println(v.engineNumber); // ✗ ERROR // System.out.println(v.fuelType); // ✗ ERROR // System.out.println(v.maxSpeed); // ✗ ERROR — protected needs inheritance System.out.println(v.brand); // ✓ ALLOWED — public // v.serviceLog(); // ✗ ERROR // v.refuel(); // ✗ ERROR // v.honk(); // ✗ ERROR v.start(); // ✓ ALLOWED — public } } ``` **Logic:** Only `public` survives the full journey. All other modifiers are locked at the package boundary (or class boundary for `private`). ### 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Override Visibility Rule In Java, the **Covariant Return + Visibility Rule** states: $$\text{Visibility}(\text{overriding method}) \geq \text{Visibility}(\text{parent method})$$ **Proof-by-derivation (logical step):** 1. Let parent method visibility be $V_p \in \{\text{private}, \text{default}, \text{protected}, \text{public}\}$. 2. Let child method visibility be $V_c$. 3. Define the visibility ordering: $$ \text{private} < \text{default} < \text{protected} < \text{public} $$ 4. The compiler enforces $V_c \geq V_p$. If $V_c < V_p$, the compilation fails with: ``` error: cannot reduce the visibility of the inherited method ``` **Example (valid override):** ```java class Parent { protected void show() { } } class Child extends Parent { @Override public void show() { } // ✓ Valid — visibility increased } ``` **Example (invalid override):** ```java class Child extends Parent { @Override private void show() { } // ✗ Compile error } ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Real-World Analogy:** A bank promises customers (parent) that withdrawals are allowed (public). A subclass `ChildAccount` cannot secretly downgrade it to "only manager can do it" (private). That would break the contract. ### 3.3 Getters/Setters — The Standard Encapsulation Pattern ```java package bank; public class Account { private double balance; // hidden public double getBalance() { // controlled read return balance; } public void deposit(double amt) { // controlled write if (amt > 0) balance += amt; } } ``` **Step-by-step reasoning:** 1. `balance` is `private` → external classes cannot read/write it directly. 2. `getBalance()` is `public` → safely returns the value. 3. `deposit()` is `public` → performs validation before mutating state. 4. The internal representation can change (e.g., to `BigDecimal`) without breaking clients — this is **encapsulation in action**. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Access Modifier Visibility Funnel (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["PUBLIC
Accessible everywhere"] nodeB["PROTECTED
Same package + subclasses"] nodeC["DEFAULT
Same package only"] nodeD["PRIVATE
Same class only"] nodeE["CLASS BOUNDARY"] nodeF["PACKAGE BOUNDARY"] nodeG["INHERITANCE LINK"] nodeA --> nodeE nodeA --> nodeF nodeA --> nodeG nodeB --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeG nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeE style nodeA fill:#9ccc65,stroke:#33691e,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#ffb74d,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#ef5350,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style nodeE fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242,color:#000 style nodeF fill:#bdbdbd,stroke:#424242,color:#000 style nodeG fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Package-to-Package Access Map ```mermaid flowchart LR pkgA["PACKAGE A
vehicle"] clsA1["Class A1
public, protected,
default, private"] clsA2["Class A2
(non-subclass)"] pkgB["PACKAGE B
showroom"] clsB1["Class B1
extends A1
(subclass)"] clsB2["Class B2
(non-subclass)"] pkgA --> clsA1 pkgA --> clsA2 pkgB --> clsB1 pkgB --> clsB2 clsA1 -->|"private"| clsA1x["Only inside A1"] clsA1 -->|"default"| clsA1y["A1 + A2 only"] clsA1 -->|"protected"| clsA1z["A1 + A2 + B1"] clsA1 -->|"public"| clsA1w["A1 + A2 + B1 + B2"] style clsA1x fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style clsA1y fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style clsA1z fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style clsA1w fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 ``` ### 4.3 Encapsulation Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ext["EXTERNAL WORLD"] client["Client Code"] end subgraph cls["CLASS Vehicle"] pub["PUBLIC
start(), brand"] prot["PROTECTED
honk(), maxSpeed"] def["DEFAULT
refuel(), fuelType"] priv["PRIVATE
serviceLog(), engineNumber"] end client -->|"Calls"| pub client -.->|"Cannot access"| def client -.->|"Cannot access"| priv prot -.->|"Accessed by subclasses"| client style pub fill:#81c784,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style prot fill:#ffb74d,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style def fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style priv fill:#e57373,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style client fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define access modifiers in Java. List the four access levels available in Java and state the access scope of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Access modifiers are keywords in Java that control the visibility and accessibility of classes, methods, and data members, enforcing encapsulation. The four access levels are: 1. **`private`** — Accessible only within the same class in which it is declared. 2. **Default (package-private)** — Accessible to all classes within the same package; not accessible outside the package. 3. **`protected`** — Accessible within the same package and also by subclasses (even in different packages) through inheritance. 4. **`public`** — Accessible from any class in any package, with no restrictions. *(1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for the four levels + 0.5 mark for examples/scope)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **What is the difference between `default` and `protected` access modifiers in Java? Can a subclass in a different package access a default member of its parent class? Justify your answer.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `default` | `protected` | |--------|-----------|-------------| | Keyword needed? | No (no keyword written) | Yes (`protected`) | | Same package access | Yes | Yes | | Subclass in other package | **No** | **Yes** (via inheritance) | | Non-subclass in other package | No | No | **Justification:** A subclass in a different package **cannot** access a `default` (package-private) member of its parent class, because the default modifier restricts visibility strictly to the package in which it is declared, regardless of the inheritance relationship. This is a frequently tested KTU concept. *(1 mark for comparison + 1 mark for subclass-in-other-package question + 1 mark for justification)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the four access modifiers in Java with a neat diagram of the access matrix. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate how access modifiers restrict visibility across packages. Use a parent class `Employee` in package `company` and a subclass `Manager` in package `hr` to illustrate `private`, default, `protected`, and `public` access. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Explanation with Access Matrix (7 marks):** Java provides four access modifiers: `private`, default, `protected`, and `public`. Their visibility is summarized below: | Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Other Package) | Other Package (Non-Subclass) | |----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | `private` | Yes | No | No | No | | `default` | Yes | Yes | No | No | | `protected` | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | | `public` | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | - **`private`**: Most restrictive. Limited to the class itself. - **`default`**: Package-level access. No keyword is written. - **`protected`**: Extends default to subclasses outside the package. - **`public`**: Most permissive; accessible from anywhere. **(b) Java Program (7 marks):** File 1 — `Employee.java` in package `company`: ```java package company; public class Employee { private int id = 101; String dept = "Sales"; // default protected double salary = 50000; // protected public String name = "Anand"; // public private void showId() { System.out.println("ID: " + id); } void showDept() { System.out.println("Dept: " + dept); } protected void showSalary() { System.out.println("Salary: " + salary); } public void showName() { System.out.println("Name: " + name); } } ``` File 2 — `Manager.java` in package `hr`: ```java package hr; import company.Employee; public class Manager extends Employee { public void display() { // System.out.println(id); // ✗ private not accessible // System.out.println(dept); // ✗ default not accessible System.out.println(salary); // ✓ protected — accessible via inheritance System.out.println(name); // ✓ public — accessible // showId(); // ✗ private not accessible // showDept(); // ✗ default not accessible showSalary(); // ✓ protected showName(); // ✓ public } public static void main(String[] args) { Manager m = new Manager(); m.display(); } } ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Salary: 50000.0 Name: Anand Salary: 50000.0 Name: Anand ``` This program demonstrates that `protected` and `public` members cross package boundaries through inheritance, while `private` and `default` are blocked. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]** **(a)** Discuss the role of access modifiers in achieving **encapsulation** and **data hiding** in object-oriented programming. Illustrate with a real-world banking example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the rules for overriding methods in Java with respect to access modifiers. Provide a code example showing a valid override and an invalid override. What error does the compiler produce for the invalid case? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Access Modifiers and Encapsulation (7 marks):** **Encapsulation** is the OOP principle of bundling data and methods that operate on that data within a single unit (class), and restricting direct external access to some of the object's components. **Data hiding** is the stricter consequence of encapsulation — making internal state private so external code cannot tamper with it. Access modifiers are the *mechanism* by which encapsulation is implemented in Java. **Banking Example:** ```java package banking; public class BankAccount { private double balance; // HIDDEN private String accountNumber; // HIDDEN public BankAccount(String accNo, double initial) { this.accountNumber = accNo; if (initial >= 0) this.balance = initial; } public double getBalance() { // CONTROLLED READ return balance; } public void deposit(double amount) { // CONTROLLED WRITE WITH VALIDATION if (amount <= 0) { System.out.println("Invalid amount."); return; } balance += amount; } public void withdraw(double amount) { if (amount > balance) { System.out.println("Insufficient funds."); return; } balance -= amount; } } ``` **Role of Access Modifiers:** - `private` ensures `balance` cannot be set to a negative value directly by an external class. - `public` getters/setters (with validation) form a controlled interface. - This is **data hiding + encapsulation**: the data is hidden, the behavior is exposed. **(b) Method Overriding and Access Modifiers (7 marks):** **Rule:** When a subclass overrides a method from its parent class, the access level of the overriding method **cannot be more restrictive** than the overridden method. It can stay the same or become more permissive. Visibility order: `private < default < protected < public` **Valid Override:** ```java class Animal { protected void speak() { System.out.println("Animal speaks"); } } class Dog extends Animal { @Override public void speak() { // ✓ Visibility widened — VALID System.out.println("Dog barks"); } } ``` **Invalid Override:** ```java class DogBad extends Animal { @Override private void speak() { // ✗ Visibility narrowed — INVALID System.out.println("Dog barks"); } } ``` **Compiler Error Produced:** ``` error: cannot override speak() in Animal; attempting to assign weaker access privileges; was protected ``` **Explanation:** Narrowing visibility would violate the *Liskov Substitution Principle* — code expecting an `Animal` reference might call `speak()` assuming public/protected access, but the `DogBad` version hides it as `private`, causing a runtime/compile-time contradiction. Hence Java forbids it. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. Writing `default` as a keyword (e.g., `default int x;`) — **It is NOT a keyword. Omit it entirely.** > 2. Saying "protected = private + inheritance" — **Wrong.** Protected also gives same-package access; it is "default + subclass access." > 3. Confusing `protected` access from a non-subclass in a different package — `protected` is **not** accessible by non-subclasses outside the package. > 4. In the matrix table, marking "Subclass in Other Package → default = Yes" — this is a **classic 1-mark trap**. It is **No**. > 5. Forgetting that `private` members are **not inherited** at all (not just hidden — they don't exist for the subclass). > 6. Mixing up C++ and Java: Java has no `friend` keyword, and the default behavior is package-level (not class-level like `private`). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Four modifiers in Java:** `private`, `default` (no keyword), `protected`, `public`. - **Default ≠ private.** Default is package-level, not class-level. - **`private` = same class only.** Nothing else. Not even same-package classes. - **`protected` = same package + subclasses in other packages (via inheritance only).** - **`public` = everywhere, no restriction.** - **Top-level classes can only be `public` or default** — never `private`/`protected`. - **`private` members are NOT inherited**; subclasses cannot access them, period. - **Overriding rule:** You can **widen** visibility, never **narrow** it. - **Abstract methods** cannot be `private`. - **Interface members** are implicitly `public`. - **A `private` constructor** → cannot instantiate from outside (Singleton pattern). - **In Java, no `friend` keyword** — package + `protected` serve the role. - **Master the 4×4 matrix** — at least one KTU question every year tests it. - **Encapsulation is the WHY; access modifiers are the HOW.** - **Code examples should show both same-package AND different-package access** to score full marks in Part B.

topics.title: This Keyword
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** This Keyword ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Object-Oriented Programming (specifically **Java** and **C++**), the `this` keyword is a **reference variable** that refers to the **currently invoking object** of a class. It is an implicit, final reference automatically created by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) / compiler and passed to every **non-static instance method** and **constructor** of a class. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > *"`this` is a keyword in Java that acts as a reference to the current object — the instance whose method or constructor is being called at runtime. It is used to disambiguate between instance variables and parameters, to invoke constructors, and to support method chaining."* ### Key Properties of `this` | Property | Value / Behavior | |---|---| | **Type** | Implicit, final reference variable | | **Belongs to** | Instance context (object-level, not class-level) | | **Accessibility** | Cannot be used inside `static` methods, `static` blocks, or `main()` directly | | **Default Value** | Not applicable (cannot be unassigned, always points to the current object) | | **Finality** | `final` by design — you cannot reassign `this = someOtherObject;` | | **Memory Location** | Stored in the **JVM Stack Frame** as a hidden parameter of the method/constructor | ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🏠 Imagine you are in a **company office** with **100 employees** (objects). Every employee fills out an **ID card** (instance variable). When an employee picks up their own ID card, they don't say "give me John's ID" — they say **"give me MY ID"**. That word **"MY"** is exactly what `this` represents inside an object's body. Another analogy: Consider a **newspaper delivery boy** (a method) delivering a **newspaper** (a variable). When the delivery boy says *"I delivered it to the customer at this address"*, the word **"this"** refers to whichever house he is currently standing in front of. The meaning of `this` **changes** depending on the **calling object**. > [!NOTE] > **Why does `this` even exist?** > Because inside a method, the compiler must be able to distinguish between: > 1. A **local variable / parameter** (declared in the method). > 2. An **instance variable** (declared in the class). > > When both share the same name, `this` removes the ambiguity. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### The Six Primary Uses of `this` Keyword (KTU High-Yield List) The KTU board frequently asks *"List the uses of `this` keyword"* or *"Explain the uses of `this` keyword with examples"*. Master these **6 uses**: #### Use 1: Differentiate Instance Variable from Local Variable (Most Common) When parameter names and instance variable names are identical, `this` removes the shadowing effect. ```java class Student { String name; int rollNo; Student(String name, int rollNo) { this.name = name; // this.name -> instance variable this.rollNo = rollNo; // rollNo -> local parameter } } ``` **Why this works:** The LHS `this.name` explicitly tells the JVM to assign the value to the object's instance variable, not the local parameter. #### Use 2: Invoke Current Class Method (Implicitly Done) You can call a method of the current class using `this`. However, this is optional because the compiler adds `this` implicitly. ```java class Demo { void methodA() { System.out.println("Method A"); this.methodB(); // 'this' is optional here } void methodB() { System.out.println("Method B"); } } ``` #### Use 3: Invoke Current Class Constructor (Constructor Chaining) `this()` can be used to call another constructor within the same class. This is called **explicit constructor invocation**. ```java class Box { int length, breadth, height; Box() { this(10, 10, 10); // calls Box(int, int, int) } Box(int l, int b, int h) { this.length = l; this.breadth = b; this.height = h; } } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule for `this()` calls:** > 1. `this()` must be the **FIRST statement** in the constructor. > 2. It creates a **chain of constructor calls**. > 3. The chain must eventually terminate at a constructor that does **NOT** call `this()` (else: **infinite recursion → StackOverflowError**). #### Use 4: Pass as an Argument to Another Method You can pass the current object as a parameter to a method. ```java class Helper { void display(Student s) { System.out.println(s.name); } } class Student { String name = "Arun"; void send() { new Helper().display(this); // passes current Student object } } ``` #### Use 5: Pass as an Argument to Another Constructor Useful in inheritance and composition scenarios. ```java class Vehicle { Vehicle(Engine e) { System.out.println("Vehicle created with engine"); } } class Car { Engine e = new Engine(); Vehicle v = new Vehicle(this.e); // passing the engine } ``` #### Use 6: Return the Current Object from a Method This enables the **Builder Pattern** and **method chaining** (returning `this` for fluent APIs). ```java class Builder { Builder setName(String n) { // set name return this; // returns current object } Builder setAge(int a) { // set age return this; } } // Usage: new Builder().setName("Anu").setAge(20); ``` ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Use # | Syntax Pattern | Purpose | Common Pitfall | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `this.x = x;` | Resolve shadowing | Forgetting `this.` when names clash | | 2 | `this.method();` | Call current class method | `this` cannot be used in `static` context | | 3 | `this(arg1, arg2);` | Constructor chaining | Must be first statement; no cycles | | 4 | `method(this);` | Pass current object | Reference is shared (no copy) | | 5 | `new X(this);` | Pass to another constructor | Rare, mainly in composition | | 6 | `return this;` | Enable fluent chaining | Return type must match class | ### Why `this` is Crucial in Real-World Engineering - **Frameworks like Spring** rely on `this` for dependency injection and AOP proxies. - **Builder Design Pattern** (used in `StringBuilder`, `Lombok`, `Stream API`) depends on `return this`. - **Event-driven systems** pass `this` as the source object in event listeners. - **Memory-efficient APIs** avoid object duplication by sharing references. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Analytical Trace: What Happens When `new` is Executed? Let us trace the exact JVM memory model when an object is created and a `this`-aware method is invoked. This is a high-yield KTU question. **Code to trace:** ```java class Account { int balance; Account(int balance) { this.balance = balance; // Step (a) this.showBalance(); // Step (b) } void showBalance() { System.out.println(this.balance); } } public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { Account a1 = new Account(500); Account a2 = new Account(1500); } } ``` #### Step-by-Step Execution Trace **Step 1: Class Loading** The JVM loads `Account` and `Test` classes into the **Method Area (Metaspace)**. Instance variables (`balance`) and method bytecode (``, `showBalance`) are stored here. No `this` exists yet because no object has been created. **Step 2: `new Account(500)` Execution** The JVM performs three sub-steps: 1. **Memory Allocation in Heap:** A new memory block of size = `int (4 bytes)` is allocated in the **Young Generation / Eden Space** of the heap. The reference variable `a1` in the **Stack Frame of `main()`** is wired to this heap object. 2. **Default Initialization:** The instance variable `balance` is initialized to its default value: $$\text{balance} = 0$$ 3. **Constructor Invocation (``) with Hidden `this` Argument:** The JVM pushes a new **Stack Frame** for the constructor. Critically, it **injects a hidden reference `this`** into the local variable array of this frame, pointing to the newly allocated heap object. $$\text{Stack Frame of constructor} \to \text{Local Variable 0} = \text{this} = \text{address of new Account}$$ **Step 3: Inside the constructor** The line `this.balance = balance;` is executed: - The RHS `balance` (without `this.`) refers to the **local parameter** of the constructor, which is `500`. - The LHS `this.balance` forces the JVM to follow the `this` reference to the heap object and write `500` into its `balance` field. $$\text{Heap Object (a1)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{balance} = 500$$ **Step 4: `this.showBalance()` call** The `this` reference is passed as the implicit argument to `showBalance()`. A new stack frame is created with `this` again pointing to the **same heap object** (a1). The output is: $$\text{Output} = 500$$ **Step 5: `new Account(1500)` Execution** A **new, separate heap object** is allocated. The `this` reference inside the second constructor invocation points to this **second object** (a2), not a1. This proves the central rule: $$\boxed{\text{The value of `this` is determined dynamically at runtime, based on which object invokes the method.}}$$ **Step 6: Final Memory Snapshot** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Stack (main)} &: \quad a1 \to \text{HeapObj}_1, \quad a2 \to \text{HeapObj}_2 \\[4pt] \text{HeapObj}_1 &: \quad \text{balance} = 500 \\[4pt] \text{HeapObj}_2 &: \quad \text{balance} = 1500 \end{aligned} $$ ### Analytical Matrix: `this` vs `super` vs Local Scope | Aspect | `this` | `super` | Local Variable | |---|---|---|---| | **Refers to** | Current class object | Parent class object | Method's local data | | **Scope** | Entire instance | Parent's hidden members | Method block only | | **Static context** | ❌ Forbidden | ❌ Forbidden | ✅ Allowed | | **Can be reassigned** | ❌ (final) | ❌ (final) | ✅ (if not final) | | **Used in constructor call** | `this(args)` | `super(args)` | N/A | | **Position rule** | Must be 1st stmt (for ctor call) | Must be 1st stmt | Anywhere | ### Proof of `this` Being Final The compiler enforces that `this` cannot be reassigned. The following code **will not compile**: ```java class Demo { void test() { this = null; // ❌ Compile-time error: "Cannot assign a value to final variable this" } } ``` ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Memory Architecture of `this` Reference ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STACK["JVM Stack - main method"] s1["a1 (reference)"] s2["a2 (reference)"] end subgraph HEAP["JVM Heap - Object Memory"] h1["Object a1
balance = 500
hidden this pointer"] h2["Object a2
balance = 1500
hidden this pointer"] end subgraph METHODAREA["Method Area / Metaspace"] m1["Class: Account
Methods: init, showBalance"] end s1 -->|points to| h1 s2 -->|points to| h2 h1 -.->|this in ctor 1| h1 h2 -.->|this in ctor 2| h2 m1 -->|defines structure| h1 m1 -->|defines structure| h2 style STACK fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style HEAP fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style METHODAREA fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style h1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style h2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 ``` ### Diagram 2: Constructor Chaining via `this()` ```mermaid flowchart LR A["new Box()"] -->|"this(10,10,10)
FIRST stmt"| B["Box(int l, int b, int h)"] C["new Box(5,6,7)"] -->|"direct call"| B B --> D["Assigns this.length = l
this.breadth = b
this.height = h"] D --> E["Object Initialized
and Returned"] style A fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style E fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b ``` ### Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture — How `this` Resolves a Name Conflict ```mermaid flowchart TB START["Method call: setName(Anu)"] --> PUSH["JVM pushes Stack Frame
with hidden this"] PUSH --> SCOPE{"Name 'name' lookup
in scope chain"} SCOPE -->|"LHS: this.name"| INST["Instance Variable
(Object's field)"] SCOPE -->|"RHS: name"| LOCAL["Local Parameter
(Method's argument)"] INST --> ASSIGN["Assignment:
Object.name = Anu"] LOCAL --> ASSIGN ASSIGN --> RESULT["Name Conflict Resolved
via this keyword"] style START fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style SCOPE fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style INST fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style LOCAL fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style RESULT fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the `this` keyword in Java? Mention any two of its uses.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The `this` keyword in Java is an **implicit reference variable** that refers to the **current object** — the instance on which a method or constructor is being invoked. It is automatically created and passed by the JVM as a hidden argument to every non-static method and constructor. **Two uses:** 1. **To differentiate instance variables from local parameters** when they share the same name: ```java void setX(int x) { this.x = x; } ``` 2. **To invoke another constructor of the same class** (constructor chaining), enabling code reuse: ```java Student() { this("Unknown", 0); } // calls parameterized ctor ``` *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two uses.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Why can't the `this` keyword be used inside a `static` method? Justify your answer.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The `this` keyword refers to the **current instance (object)** of a class. However, `static` methods belong to the **class itself**, not to any particular object. They can be invoked **even before any object is created** (e.g., `ClassName.staticMethod()` or from `main()`). Since at the time of a `static` method's execution **no specific object exists**, there is no "current object" for `this` to point to. The compiler therefore raises a **compile-time error**: > *"Non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context"* Hence, `this` is forbidden in static methods, static blocks, and static variable initializers. --- ### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** *`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`* **(a)** Explain the concept of the `this` keyword in Java. List and explain its **six common uses** with suitable code examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **constructor chaining using `this()`**. Explain why `this()` must be the **first statement** in a constructor. What happens if a circular `this()` call is made? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q-A (a): **Definition (2 Marks):** `this` is a Java keyword that acts as an **implicit, final reference** to the current object — the one whose method or constructor is currently being executed. It is automatically created by the JVM and is accessible inside all non-static methods and constructors. **Six Uses (5 Marks — 1 mark each for use + brief code):** 1. **Refer to current instance variable** (resolving name conflict): ```java void setName(String name) { this.name = name; } ``` 2. **Invoke current class method** (optional, but explicit): ```java this.display(); ``` 3. **Invoke current class constructor** (constructor chaining): ```java this(10, 20); ``` 4. **Pass as argument to a method**: ```java helper.process(this); ``` 5. **Pass as argument to another constructor**: ```java this.engine = new Engine(this); ``` 6. **Return current object** (enables fluent chaining): ```java return this; ``` --- #### Model Solution for Q-A (b): **Program (4 Marks):** ```java class Employee { String name; int id; double salary; // Constructor 1: No-arg Employee() { this("Not Assigned", 0, 0.0); // calls Constructor 3 System.out.println("Default Employee Created"); } // Constructor 2: Two-arg Employee(String name, int id) { this(name, id, 25000.0); // calls Constructor 3 } // Constructor 3: Master constructor Employee(String name, int id, double salary) { this.name = name; this.id = id; this.salary = salary; System.out.println("Master ctor: " + name + ", " + id + ", " + salary); } void display() { System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", ID: " + id + ", Salary: " + salary); } public static void main(String[] args) { Employee e1 = new Employee(); Employee e2 = new Employee("Anu", 101); e1.display(); e2.display(); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Master ctor: Not Assigned, 0, 0.0 Default Employee Created Master ctor: Anu, 101, 25000.0 Name: Not Assigned, ID: 0, Salary: 0.0 Name: Anu, ID: 101, Salary: 25000.0 ``` **Why `this()` must be the first statement (2 Marks):** The Java Language Specification mandates that an explicit constructor invocation (`this()` or `super()`) must appear as the **first statement** of a constructor body. This ensures that the **superclass part of the object is fully initialized** before the subclass constructor's body executes, preserving a well-defined object initialization order. **Circular `this()` call (1 Mark):** If two constructors call each other via `this()` in a cycle (e.g., `A() { this(5); }` and `A(int x) { this(); }`), the JVM will attempt infinite recursion and throw a **`StackOverflowError`** at runtime. The compiler does **not** detect this at compile-time. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* *`[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`* **(a)** Differentiate between **`this`** and **`super`** keywords in Java with a comparative table. Provide a code example showing how both are used together in a single subclass constructor. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Demonstrate with a Java program how returning `this` from a method enables **method chaining** to build a fluent API. Explain its advantages in software engineering. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q-B (a): **Comparison Table (4 Marks):** | Aspect | `this` | `super` | |---|---|---| | Refers to | Current class object | Parent (super) class object | | Used for | Resolving local-instance conflict | Accessing overridden parent members | | Constructor call | `this(args)` | `super(args)` | | Position in ctor | 1st statement (if used) | 1st statement (if used) | | Can both appear together? | ❌ Only one allowed as 1st stmt | ❌ Only one allowed as 1st stmt | | Static context | ❌ Forbidden | ❌ Forbidden | **Code Example (3 Marks):** ```java class Animal { String type = "Generic Animal"; Animal() { System.out.println("Animal created"); } } class Dog extends Animal { String type = "Dog"; Dog() { super(); // calls Animal() System.out.println("Dog created"); } void printTypes() { System.out.println("This type: " + this.type); // "Dog" System.out.println("Super type: " + super.type); // "Generic Animal" } public static void main(String[] args) { new Dog().printTypes(); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Animal created Dog created This type: Dog Super type: Generic Animal ``` --- #### Model Solution for Q-B (b): **Program (4 Marks):** ```java class QueryBuilder { private String select = ""; private String from = ""; private String where = ""; QueryBuilder select(String columns) { this.select = "SELECT " + columns; return this; // enables chaining } QueryBuilder from(String table) { this.from = " FROM " + table; return this; } QueryBuilder where(String condition) { this.where = " WHERE " + condition; return this; } String build() { return select + from + where; } public static void main(String[] args) { String sql = new QueryBuilder() .select("*") .from("Students") .where("marks > 50") .build(); System.out.println(sql); } } ``` **Output:** ``` SELECT * FROM Students WHERE marks > 50 ``` **Advantages of Method Chaining / Fluent API (3 Marks):** 1. **Readability:** Code reads like a natural sentence, improving maintainability. 2. **Compactness:** Reduces intermediate variable declarations. 3. **Builder Pattern Support:** Enables construction of complex objects step-by-step (used heavily in `StringBuilder`, `Stream API`, and Lombok). 4. **Industry Adoption:** Java 8+ `Stream` and Builder patterns in frameworks like Spring rely on `return this`. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **❌ Confusing `this` with `super`:** Many students write `this` when they mean `super`. Memorize the table clearly. > 2. **❌ Forgetting that `this()` must be the first statement:** Any code before `this()` causes a **compile-time error** *"Call to this must be first statement in constructor"*. > 3. **❌ Using `this` in `static` context:** This is a **favourite trick question** by KTU examiners. Always justify *why* (no object exists). > 4. **❌ Listing fewer than 5 uses:** For 7-mark questions, listing all six uses earns full marks. Missing one costs 1 mark. > 5. **❌ Not drawing a memory diagram:** Whenever the question says "explain with example", **include a memory/state diagram** to score beyond average. > 6. **❌ Writing `this = null;`** — never compiles. Don't write syntactically wrong code in the exam. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 - ✅ `this` is an **implicit, final reference** to the **current object**, automatically passed by the JVM. - ✅ It is **not** available in `static` methods, `static` blocks, or `main()` directly. - ✅ The **6 primary uses**: (1) Resolve variable shadowing, (2) Call current class method, (3) Call another constructor via `this()`, (4) Pass as method argument, (5) Pass as constructor argument, (6) Return current object for chaining. - ✅ `this()` must be the **first statement** of a constructor; circular chains cause `StackOverflowError`. - ✅ `this` is dynamically determined at runtime based on the calling object — different objects get different `this`. - ✅ It **cannot be reassigned** (`this = obj2;` is illegal). - ✅ `this` differs from `super`: `this` → current class; `super` → parent class. Both cannot coexist as the first statement of the same constructor. - ✅ Method chaining via `return this;` powers the **Builder Pattern** and **fluent APIs** in real-world frameworks (Spring, Lombok, Streams). - ✅ **Memory model:** `this` lives in the **stack frame** as a hidden parameter pointing to the **heap-allocated object**.

topics.title: Polymorphism - Method Overloading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Polymorphism - Method Overloading # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polymorphism** (from Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) is the ability of an object, function, or operator to take on **multiple forms** or behave in **different ways** based on the context in which it is invoked. In the context of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), it allows the **same interface or method name** to represent **different underlying behaviors** depending on the data type, number of parameters, or the actual object subclass at runtime. The term **Polymorphism** is one of the four foundational pillars of OOP (alongside **Encapsulation**, **Abstraction**, and **Inheritance**). The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies polymorphism into two broad categories: | Category | Type | Resolution Time | Alternate Name | |----------|------|-----------------|----------------| | 1 | **Compile-Time Polymorphism** | Resolved by the compiler | **Static Binding** / **Early Binding** | | 2 | **Run-Time Polymorphism** | Resolved by the JVM at execution | **Dynamic Binding** / **Late Binding** | **Method Overloading** is a direct implementation of **Compile-Time Polymorphism** in Java (and C++). It allows a class to declare **multiple methods** that share the **exact same name** but differ in their **method signatures** (parameter list). > [!NOTE] > **Official KTU Definition:** *Method Overloading is a mechanism in which two or more methods within the same class (or in a parent-child relationship) share the same name but have a different number, type, or order of parameters. The compiler determines which method to invoke based on the arguments passed at the call site — this is known as Static Polymorphism.* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a single individual named **Rahul** who plays **multiple roles** in life: - At **home**, he is a **son** (helps with chores, respects elders). - At **college**, he is a **student** (attends lectures, writes exams). - At **his part-time job**, he is a **cashier** (scans items, handles money). - At a **sports event**, he is a **cricket captain** (strategizes, leads the team). The **same person (Rahul)** is performing **different actions** based on the **context (role)** he is placed in. He is *one entity, many forms* — this is **polymorphism**. Now, narrow this analogy to **Method Overloading**: Imagine a single **printer** device in your home. - If you send it **a text document**, it prints text. - If you send it **a photograph**, it prints an image. - If you send it **a PDF**, it prints formatted pages. - If you send it **multiple copies**, it prints N duplicates. All of these operations are invoked by pressing the **same button (the "Print" method)**, but the **underlying behavior changes** based on **what you send as input (arguments)**. This is exactly how **Method Overloading** works in Java. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** Think *"One Name, Many Behaviors."* If the answer requires explaining polymorphism in 3 marks, this phrase + the role-based analogy = full marks. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics While Method Overloading is a pure software concept, the following **standard JLS (Java Language Specification) metrics** are mandatory for KTU answers: - **JVM Specification Rule:** Methods are identified uniquely by the combination of **method name + parameter types** — not by return type alone. This is the *signature* rule. - **Default Limit (per class):** Java does not impose a **hard limit** on the number of overloaded methods, but each must have a **unique signature** (≈ unique combination of parameter count, types, and order). - **Access Modifiers:** Overloaded methods can have **different access modifiers** (e.g., one `public`, one `private`) — this does not cause a conflict. - **Exception Lists:** Overloaded methods can throw **different checked exceptions** independently. --- ## 1.4 Why KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasizes This Topic > [!IMPORTANT] > Module 2 of PECMT535 dedicates significant weightage to **Polymorphism** because it is a **Course Outcome (CO2)** driver: *"Apply object-oriented principles such as inheritance and polymorphism to design modular software systems."* Expect **1 compulsory 14-mark question** and at least **1 three-mark question** in every End Semester Evaluation (ESE). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Mechanism (Step-by-Step Logic) When the Java compiler encounters a method call, it performs a process called **Method Resolution** (or **Overload Resolution**) using the following deterministic algorithm: ### Step 1 — Phase 1: Exact Match Search (Strict Invocation) The compiler scans the class and superclass hierarchy for an overloaded method whose parameter list is an **exact match** (after autoboxing/unboxing considerations) to the arguments provided. If found, that method is selected. Binding is complete — no further phases run. ### Step 2 — Phase 2: Type Promotion (Widening Primitive Conversion) If no exact match is found, the compiler attempts **implicit type promotion** (widening conversion) on the arguments. The standard promotion hierarchy in Java is: $$\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}$$ $$\text{char} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}$$ The compiler picks the **most specific applicable method** — i.e., the one requiring the **least amount of automatic conversion**. ### Step 3 — Phase 3: Autoboxing / Unboxing If still no match, the compiler tries **autoboxing** (e.g., `int` → `Integer`) and **unboxing** (`Integer` → `int`). Java prefers widening over autoboxing, so this is a secondary fallback. ### Step 4 — Phase 4: Varargs Match As a final fallback, the compiler considers **varargs** (variable-length arguments, e.g., `int... x`). Varargs are treated as the **least specific** match. ### Step 5 — Compile-Time Error If none of the four phases yield a unique match, the compiler raises: ``` error: reference to methodName is ambiguous ``` or ``` error: no suitable method found for methodName(...) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Insight:** All five steps execute **at compile time only**. Once the compiler locks in the method reference, the bytecode contains a hard-coded `invokevirtual` (or `invokestatic`) instruction with a fixed target. The JVM does **not** re-resolve overloaded methods at runtime — only overridden methods. --- ## 2.2 The Three Dimensions of Method Overloading A method signature in Java is defined as: $$\text{Signature} = \text{MethodName} + \langle \text{ParameterTypeList} \rangle$$ Two methods are valid overloads if and only if they differ in **at least one** of the following three dimensions: | # | Dimension | Example | Valid Overload? | |---|-----------|---------|-----------------| | 1 | **Number of Parameters** | `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(int a, int b, int c)` | ✅ Yes | | 2 | **Type of Parameters** | `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(double a, double b)` | ✅ Yes | | 3 | **Order of Parameters** | `display(String s, int i)` vs `display(int i, String s)` | ✅ Yes | --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Anti-Ambiguity Rules) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following rules are **favourite KTU board question territory**. Memorize these verbatim. | Rule # | What is Different? | Causes Overload? | Why? | |--------|-------------------|------------------|------| | **R1** | Different parameter list (count/type/order) | ✅ YES | Signature differs | | **R2** | Different return type **only** | ❌ NO | Signature identical; causes CE | | **R3** | Different access modifier (public/private) | ✅ YES (but unusual) | Not part of signature | | **R4** | Different `static` / non-static | ✅ YES (but ambiguous if called from static context) | Not part of signature | | **R5** | Different `final` keyword | ✅ YES | Not part of signature | | **R6** | Different exception list (throws clause) | ✅ YES | Not part of signature | | **R7** | Different method body (logic) only | ❌ NO | Signature identical | | **R8** | Different variable names for parameters | ❌ NO | Param names ignored by compiler | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Method Overloading is not a theoretical toy. It is used pervasively in: - **`System.out.println()`** — the JDK defines **10 overloaded versions** of `println()` for `int`, `double`, `char[]`, `String`, `boolean`, `Object`, etc. - **`Math.max()`** — overloaded for `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`. - **`String.valueOf()`** — overloaded for every primitive type and `Object`. - **Constructor overloading** — every meaningful class offers multiple constructors. - **API design** — improves **readability** and **usability** by allowing a consistent naming convention across related operations. > [!TIP] > **KTU Trick Question:** *"Can we overload `main()` in Java?"* — **YES, absolutely.** The JVM only calls the `public static void main(String[] args)` signature, but the class can contain any number of other `main()` methods with different parameters, used as helper methods. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line analytical walkthroughs** of method overloading scenarios, including compilation output derivations, ambiguity resolution, and type promotion analyses. --- ## 3.1 Analytical Example 1 — Basic Overload by Parameter Count ### Code Under Analysis ```java class Calculator { int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } int add(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; } } public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Calculator c = new Calculator(); int r1 = c.add(5, 10); int r2 = c.add(5, 10, 15); System.out.println(r1); System.out.println(r2); } } ``` ### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Deduction **Step 1 — Declaration of `add(int, int)`** The compiler registers a method with signature `add(II)V... → I` (where `I` denotes int in JVM type notation). Method slot index = 1. **Step 2 — Declaration of `add(int, int, int)`** A second method with signature `add(III)V... → I` is registered. Method slot index = 2. Both methods coexist in the class's method table because their parameter-type lists differ. **Step 3 — Invocation: `c.add(5, 10)`** The compiler identifies two arguments of type `int`. It searches the method table: - Match against `add(int, int)` → **EXACT MATCH in Phase 1**. - Phase 1 succeeds → `invokevirtual #2` is emitted in the bytecode. No further phases are consulted. **Step 4 — Invocation: `c.add(5, 10, 15)`** Three `int` arguments are detected. Phase 1 match → `add(int, int, int)`. Bytecode emitted: `invokevirtual #3`. **Final Output:** $$r_1 = 5 + 10 = 15$$ $$r_2 = 5 + 10 + 15 = 30$$ ``` 15 30 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this works:** Both methods share the name `add`, but the **parameter count** differs (2 vs 3). This satisfies **Rule R1** in the cheat sheet. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Example 2 — Overload by Parameter Type ### Code Under Analysis ```java class Display { void show(int x) { System.out.println("int: " + x); } void show(double x) { System.out.println("double: " + x); } void show(String x) { System.out.println("String: " + x); } } ``` ### Type Promotion Walkthrough When `show(10)` is called, the compiler must choose among three candidates. Let us derive the resolution path formally: **Candidate 1:** `show(int x)` — argument `10` is **already `int`** → **EXACT MATCH (Phase 1 succeeds).** Therefore, the output is: ``` int: 10 ``` Now consider `show(10.5f)` (a `float` literal): **Phase 1 — Exact Match Search:** - Candidate 1: `show(int)` → requires conversion `float → int` (narrowing, not allowed implicitly). ❌ - Candidate 2: `show(double)` → requires `float → double` (widening, allowed). ✅ - Candidate 3: `show(String)` → not numeric. ❌ **Phase 2 — Promotion Path:** $$\text{float} \xrightarrow{\text{widening}} \text{double}$$ The method `show(double x)` is selected. The output is: ``` double: 10.5 ``` --- ## 3.3 Analytical Example 3 — The Ambiguity Trap (Classic KTU 14-Mark Question) ### Code Under Analysis ```java class Test { void print(int a, double b) { System.out.println("int-double version"); } void print(double a, int b) { System.out.println("double-int version"); } } public class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { Test t = new Test(); t.print(10, 20); } } ``` ### Exhaustive Ambiguity Derivation **Step 1 — Argument Identification** Both arguments are **integer literals** (10 and 20). The compiler must find a method whose parameter list `(int, int)`, `(double, double)`, or `(int, double)` / `(double, int)` is the **closest match**. **Step 2 — Candidate Analysis** | Candidate | Signature | Required Conversion for `(10, 20)` | Phase | |-----------|-----------|----------------------------------|-------| | `print(int, double)` | (int, double) | Convert second arg `20` (int) → `20.0` (double) | Phase 2 | | `print(double, int)` | (double, int) | Convert first arg `10` (int) → `10.0` (double) | Phase 2 | **Step 3 — Specificity Comparison** Both candidates require exactly **one widening conversion**. Neither is more specific than the other. This is the textbook definition of an **ambiguous method invocation**. **Step 4 — Compiler Verdict** The compiler raises: ``` error: reference to print is ambiguous t.print(10, 20); ^ both method print(int,double) in Test and method print(double,int) in Test match ``` **Step 5 — Resolution by the Programmer** To fix the ambiguity, the caller must use an explicit cast: ```java t.print(10, (double) 20); // unambiguous: matches print(int, double) t.print((double) 10, 20); // unambiguous: matches print(double, int) ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** This is the **#1 cause of mark deduction** in Module 2. Always explicitly mention "ambiguity" and "Phase 2 promotion" in your answer to score full marks. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Example 4 — Autoboxing vs Widening Precedence ### Code Under Analysis ```java class Box { void process(int x) { System.out.println("int version"); } void process(Integer x) { System.out.println("Integer version"); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Deduction for `process(5)` **Step 1 — Phase 1: Exact Match** - `process(int x)` — argument `5` is `int` → **EXACT MATCH** ✅ - `process(Integer x)` — requires autoboxing `int → Integer` → would be **Phase 3**. **Step 2 — Compiler Selection Rule** Java's JLS §15.12.2.5 specifies that **widening primitive conversion (Phase 2) beats autoboxing (Phase 3)**. Since Phase 1 finds a direct match, autoboxing is **not even consulted**. **Verdict:** `process(int x)` is invoked. **Output:** ``` int version ``` ### Deduction for `process(Integer.valueOf(5))` - Argument is `Integer` (Object, not primitive). - Phase 1: `process(int x)` requires unboxing `Integer → int` (Phase 3). - Phase 1: `process(Integer x)` is an **EXACT MATCH** ✅. - **Verdict:** `process(Integer x)` is invoked. **Output:** ``` Integer version ``` --- ## 3.5 Master Analytical Matrix — Resolution Decision Tree | Invocation Argument | Method 1 Signature | Method 2 Signature | Winner | Rule Applied | |---------------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------|--------------| | `m(5)` | `m(int)` | `m(long)` | `m(int)` | Phase 1 exact match | | `m(5)` | `m(long)` | `m(Integer)` | `m(long)` | Widening > Autoboxing | | `m(5)` | `m(Integer)` | `m(int)` | `m(int)` | Unboxing not needed; primitive match wins | | `m(null)` | `m(String)` | `m(Integer)` | **COMPILE ERROR** | Ambiguous null type | | `m(5, 10)` | `m(int, int)` | `m(int, int, int)` | `m(int, int)` | Phase 1 exact match | | `m(5)` | `m(int...)` | `m(int)` | `m(int)` | Phase 1 > Phase 4 (varargs) | | `m('A')` | `m(char)` | `m(int)` | `m(char)` | Phase 1 exact match | | `m(10.0)` | `m(float)` | `m(double)` | `m(double)` | `double` exact; `float` needs narrowing (forbidden) | > [!TIP] > **KTU 14-Mark Tip:** If asked to "explain method overloading with ambiguity resolution," draw this exact decision tree in your answer sheet using a simple flowchart. Examiners love visual clarity. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics This section provides professional-grade **Mermaid diagrams** that visualize the method resolution process, the class structure of overloaded methods, and the compiler's decision-making flow. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Note for KTU Exam Sheets:** While Mermaid is used here for digital clarity, in the actual exam you are expected to draw **box-and-arrow diagrams** of similar structure. Practice the analog version on graph paper. --- ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Class-Level Architecture of Overloaded Methods ```mermaid classDiagram class Calculator { +int add(int a, int b) +int add(int a, int b, int c) +double add(double a, double b) +String add(String s1, String s2) +int add(int[] arr) } class Main { +main(String[] args) } Main --> Calculator : instantiates and calls ``` **Interpretation:** The `Calculator` class hosts **five distinct methods** all named `add`. Each occupies a unique slot in the class's method table. The `Main` class invokes these methods with varying arguments, and the compiler routes each call to the correct signature. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Compiler's Method Resolution Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Method Call Detected by Compiler"] --> B["Phase 1: Exact Match Search"] B --> C{"Exact Match Found?"} C -->|"Yes"| Z["Bind and Emit invokevirtual"] C -->|"No"| D["Phase 2: Type Promotion Widening"] D --> E{"Single Best Match?"} E -->|"Yes"| Z E -->|"No / Ambiguous"| F["Phase 3: Autoboxing / Unboxing"] F --> G{"Single Best Match?"} G -->|"Yes"| Z G -->|"No / Ambiguous"| H["Phase 4: Varargs Matching"] H --> I{"Single Best Match?"} I -->|"Yes"| Z I -->|"No / Ambiguous"| J["COMPILE-TIME ERROR: ambiguous reference"] Z --> K["Bytecode Generated with Fixed Target"] ``` **Interpretation:** This is the **canonical 4-phase overload resolution algorithm** from JLS §15.12.2. Memorize the phase order — it is a guaranteed 7-mark sub-question in KTU exams. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Type Promotion Hierarchy (Widening Conversion) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["byte"] -->|"implicit"| B["short"] B -->|"implicit"| C["int"] C -->|"implicit"| D["long"] D -->|"implicit"| E["float"] E -->|"implicit"| F["double"] G["char"] -->|"implicit"| C ``` **Interpretation:** When no exact match exists, the compiler traverses this graph to find the *shortest path* from the argument's type to one of the method's parameter types. The shortest path wins. If two paths are of equal length, ambiguity arises. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Runtime Memory View of Overloaded Method Calls ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Stack["JVM Stack (Per-Thread)"] S1["main() frame"] S2["add(int,int) frame
a=5, b=10"] S3["add(double,double) frame
a=3.14, b=2.71"] end subgraph Heap["JVM Heap (Object Memory)"] H1["Calculator object
Method Table:
1. add(II)I
2. add(IID)D
3. add(DD)D"] end S1 --> S2 S1 --> S3 S2 --> H1 S3 --> H1 ``` **Interpretation:** Each overloaded method occupies a **distinct entry in the object's method table** in the heap. The stack frames are pushed/popped independently for each invocation. The JVM does not "search" for the right method at runtime — the compiler has already fixed the target. --- ## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Polymorphism in OOP ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph P["Polymorphism"] P1["Compile-Time Polymorphism
(Static / Early Binding)"] P2["Run-Time Polymorphism
(Dynamic / Late Binding)"] end P1 --> Q1["Method Overloading"] P1 --> Q2["Operator Overloading"] P2 --> R1["Method Overriding"] P2 --> R2["Dynamic Method Dispatch"] Q1 --> S1["Same name, different signatures
Resolved by compiler"] R1 --> S2["Same name, same signature
Resolved by JVM at runtime"] ``` **Interpretation:** This functional architecture map situates **Method Overloading** within the broader taxonomy of polymorphism — a frequent **2-mark short question** in KTU Module 2. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define polymorphism. Differentiate between compile-time and run-time polymorphism with suitable examples.** #### Model Answer (Point-Wise): **Definition (1 mark):** > Polymorphism is the ability of a single interface (method name or operator) to represent different underlying forms or behaviors. The term is derived from Greek: *poly* (many) + *morph* (forms). **Differentiation Table (2 marks):** | Feature | Compile-Time Polymorphism | Run-Time Polymorphism | |---------|---------------------------|------------------------| | Binding Phase | At compile time | At runtime via JVM | | Alternative Name | Static / Early Binding | Dynamic / Late Binding | | Mechanism | Method Overloading, Operator Overloading | Method Overriding | | Performance | Faster (no runtime lookup) | Slightly slower (vtable lookup) | | Flexibility | Lower (fixed at compile time) | Higher (object-type dependent) | **Example (within the answer):** ```java // Compile-time polymorphism class Math { int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } // overload 1 double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; } // overload 2 } // Run-time polymorphism (covered in Module 2 / Module 3) class Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("Generic"); } } class Dog extends Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("Bark"); } } ``` --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: List and explain the different ways to overload a method in Java.
