Can we overload methods by changing only the return type?** #### Model Answer: **The Three Ways to Overload (2 marks):** 1. **By changing the number of parameters:** ```java void sum(int a, int b) { ... } void sum(int a, int b, int c) { ... } ``` 2. **By changing the type of parameters:** ```java void display(int x) { ... } void display(String s) { ... } ``` 3. **By changing the order of parameters:** ```java void register(String name, int id) { ... } void register(int id, String name) { ... } ``` **Return Type Overload (1 mark):** > **No, we cannot.** The compiler identifies a method by its signature, which is `methodName + parameterTypes`. The return type is **not** part of the signature. If two methods differ only in return type, the JVM cannot resolve which one to call when the call statement ignores the return value (e.g., `obj.add(5, 10);` with no assignment), leading to a **compile-time error**. ```java // INVALID - causes "duplicate method" error int greet() { return 1; } double greet() { return 1.0; } ``` --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each 14-mark question is internally divided into sub-parts. Below, two complete alternative choices (A and B) are provided as per KTU's *internal choice* rule. **Attempt ANY ONE full question.** --- ### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **Q: (a)** Explain the concept of method overloading in Java with a suitable example program. Discuss the rules that must be followed to overload methods. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is meant by ambiguity in method overloading? Write a Java program that demonstrates the ambiguity error and explain how it can be resolved. **(7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks) **1. Concept Explanation (2 marks):** Method overloading is a feature in Java that allows a class to define **multiple methods with the same name** but with **different parameter lists**. The parameter list can differ in: - **Number of parameters** - **Data type of parameters** - **Order of parameters** The compiler determines which method to execute at **compile time** based on the arguments provided. This is why it is called **compile-time polymorphism** or **static binding**. **2. Example Program (3 marks):** ```java class Area { // Overload 1: Area of square (1 parameter) double calculate(double side) { return side * side; } // Overload 2: Area of rectangle (2 parameters) double calculate(double length, double breadth) { return length * breadth; } // Overload 3: Area of circle (different type ordering) double calculate(int radius, double pi) { return pi * radius * radius; } } public class AreaDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Area a = new Area(); System.out.println("Square: " + a.calculate(5.0)); System.out.println("Rectangle: " + a.calculate(4.0, 6.0)); System.out.println("Circle: " + a.calculate(7, 3.14)); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Square: 25.0 Rectangle: 24.0 Circle: 153.86 ``` **3. Rules for Overloading (2 marks):** | # | Rule | Example | |---|------|---------| | 1 | Methods must have **different parameter lists** | ✅ `add(int, int)` vs `add(double, double)` | | 2 | Return type **alone** is insufficient to overload | ❌ `int foo()` vs `double foo()` — Compile Error | | 3 | Access modifiers **may differ** | ✅ `public void f()` and `private void f(int x)` | | 4 | Overloaded methods can throw **different exceptions** | ✅ `void f() throws IOException` and `void f(int x)` | | 5 | Overloading is **resolved at compile time** | Static binding | --- #### Part (b) — Model Answer (7 Marks) **1. Definition of Ambiguity (2 marks):** > An **ambiguity error** in method overloading occurs when the **compiler cannot uniquely identify** which overloaded method to invoke for a given method call, because two or more methods appear equally applicable after applying the type-promotion rules. **2. Ambiguity Demonstration Program (3 marks):** ```java class Ambiguity { void show(int a, double b) { System.out.println("int-double version"); } void show(double a, int b) { System.out.println("double-int version"); } public static void main(String[] args) { Ambiguity obj = new Ambiguity(); obj.show(10, 20); // COMPILE-TIME ERROR } } ``` **Compilation Output:** ``` error: reference to show is ambiguous obj.show(10, 20); ^ both method show(int,double) in Ambiguity and method show(double,int) in Ambiguity match 2 errors ``` **3. Resolution Strategy (2 marks):** The ambiguity arises because both methods require exactly **one widening conversion** (`int → double`), making them equally specific. The ambiguity can be resolved by **explicit type-casting** at the call site: ```java obj.show(10, (double) 20); // Unambiguous: matches show(int, double) obj.show((double) 10, 20); // Unambiguous: matches show(double, int) obj.show(10, 20.0); // Also unambiguous: second arg is already double ``` **Key Lesson:** Always design overloaded method signatures to minimize the possibility of overlap. If overloading is unavoidable, document the disambiguation rules clearly to the API consumer. --- ### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **type promotion** in method overloading with a neat diagram. Write a Java program that demonstrates automatic type promotion when an exact match is unavailable. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **method overloading** and **method overriding** with a comparison table. Write a program to demonstrate that the return type of overloaded methods can be different. **(7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks) **1. Concept of Type Promotion (2 marks):** > **Type promotion** (also called **widening primitive conversion**) is an automatic process performed by the Java compiler when an **exact match** for the method arguments cannot be found. The compiler promotes the argument's data type to a **larger compatible type** according to a fixed hierarchy: $$\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}$$ $$\text{char} \rightarrow \text{int}$$ **2. Promotion Hierarchy Diagram (1 mark):** ``` byte → short → int → long → float → double ↑ char ``` **3. Demonstration Program (4 marks):** ```java class Promo { void display(int x) { System.out.println("int version: " + x); } void display(double x) { System.out.println("double version: " + x); } public static void main(String[] args) { Promo p = new Promo(); p.display(100); // Exact match → int version p.display(100.5f); // float promoted to double p.display('A'); // char promoted to int p.display(10L); // long promoted to float? // (long→float is allowed but lossy) p.display((byte) 5); // byte promoted to int } } ``` **Output:** ``` int version: 100 double version: 100.5 int version: 65 // 'A' has ASCII 65 double version: 10.0 int version: 5 ``` **Step-by-step analysis of `p.display(100.5f)`:** - Argument is `float` (literal `100.5f`). - Phase 1: No method takes `float` directly. - Phase 2: `float` widens to `double`. `display(double)` matches. - Verdict: `display(double x)` is called. --- #### Part (b) — Model Answer (7 Marks) **1. Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding | |---------|-------------------|-------------------| | Definition | Same name, **different parameters** in the same/related class | Same name, **same parameters** in parent and child class | | Polymorphism Type | Compile-time (Static) | Run-time (Dynamic) | | Inheritance Required? | ❌ No (within same class) | ✅ Yes (parent-child relationship) | | Method Signature | Must differ | Must be identical | | Return Type | Can be different | Must be same (or covariant) | | Binding Time | At compile time | At runtime via JVM | | Access Modifier | Can change freely | Cannot reduce visibility | | Performance | Faster (no vtable) | Slightly slower | | Example | `add(int, int)` & `add(double, double)` | `sound()` in `Animal` & `Dog` | **2. Program Demonstrating Different Return Types (3 marks):** ```java class Calculator { // Overload 1: int return type, 2 int params int compute(int a, int b) { return a + b; } // Overload 2: double return type, 2 double params double compute(double a, double b) { return a + b; } // Overload 3: String return type, 2 String params String compute(String a, String b) { return a + b; // String concatenation } public static void main(String[] args) { Calculator c = new Calculator(); System.out.println(c.compute(5, 10)); System.out.println(c.compute(3.5, 2.5)); System.out.println(c.compute("Hello, ", "World!")); } } ``` **Output:** ``` 15 6.0 Hello, World! ``` > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Point:** In the above program, the **return types differ** (`int`, `double`, `String`), but this is **permissible** because the **parameter lists also differ**. The return type is *not* a disambiguating factor, but it is also not a constraint when the signatures already differ. If we tried to have two methods with the **same parameters** but different return types, the compiler would reject the program. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Method Overloading Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to mention "compile-time"** — If you only say "polymorphism" without specifying *static* or *compile-time*, you lose **1 mark** in 3-mark questions and **2-3 marks** in 14-mark questions. > 2. **Confusing Overloading with Overriding** — Overloading is **same name, different parameters, same class**. Overriding is **same name, same parameters, parent-child**. Mixing them up costs **heavy marks**. > 3. **Ignoring type promotion** — If a program has `void m(int)` and `void m(double)`, and you call `m('A')`, you **must** state that `char` is promoted to `int`, not skipped directly to `double`. Examiners explicitly test this. > 4. **Not drawing the promotion diagram** — In 14-mark answers, **always include the byte→short→int→long→float→double diagram**. It is worth **1-2 marks** by itself. > 5. **Missing the ambiguity explanation** — Whenever a question shows a method call like `m(10, 20)` with multiple candidates, you **must** explicitly walk through the **4-phase resolution algorithm** and identify the exact phase where ambiguity arises. > 6. **Forgetting the `main()` overload note** — KTU loves to ask: *"Can we overload `main()`?"* The answer is **YES**, and you should mention that the JVM only invokes the canonical `String[] args` version. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 2, Polymorphism (Method Overloading)** - ✅ **Polymorphism** = *one interface, many implementations* — Greek roots: *poly* (many) + *morph* (forms). - ✅ **Two types of polymorphism:** Compile-time (Static, Early Binding) and Run-time (Dynamic, Late Binding). - ✅ **Method Overloading** is the canonical example of **compile-time polymorphism**. - ✅ **Three ways to overload:** Different *number*, *type*, or *order* of parameters. - ✅ **Return type alone CANNOT** differentiate two methods — causes `duplicate method` compile error. - ✅ **Method signature** in Java = `methodName + parameter types` — return type and parameter names are excluded. - ✅ **Type Promotion Hierarchy (in order):** `byte → short → int → long → float → double`, plus `char → int`. - ✅ **Widening beats autoboxing** — Java prefers primitive widening over autoboxing/unboxing when resolving overloads. - ✅ **Varargs is the last resort** — Always checked in Phase 4 after exact match, widening, and autoboxing fail. - ✅ **Ambiguity arises** when two candidates are equally specific (require same number of widening steps). Resolution: explicit type casting at the call site. - ✅ **`main()` CAN be overloaded**, but JVM only calls `public static void main(String[] args)`. - ✅ **Operator overloading** is supported in C++ but **NOT in Java** (except for `+` on `String`). - ✅ **Overloading does NOT require inheritance** — can occur within a single class. - ✅ **Access modifiers, `static`, `final`, and exception lists** can differ between overloaded methods. - ✅ **Real-world examples:** `System.out.println()`, `Math.max()`, `String.valueOf()` are all overloaded. - ✅ **Method resolution is finalized at compile time** — the JVM does not re-resolve overloaded methods. > 🎯 **KTU 2024 Exam Prediction:** A 14-mark question on method overloading is **almost certain** in ESE. Practice writing the **4-phase algorithm**, the **type promotion diagram**, and **at least 2 ambiguity examples** by hand. Aim to complete each 14-mark answer in **25-30 minutes** during the actual exam.

topics.title: Using Objects as Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Using Objects as Parameters ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), **Using Objects as Parameters** is a mechanism whereby an instance of a class (an *object*) is supplied as an argument to a method, constructor, or function. The receiving entity obtains a *handle* (a reference or pointer) to the live object residing in dynamic memory, thereby enabling access to and manipulation of the object's encapsulated state. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT535 - Module 2):** > Object passing is treated as a foundational building block of *inter-object communication*. The 2024 scheme emphasises understanding **what is actually transmitted** (a reference, not a clone), the implications of *aliasing*, and the *encapsulation boundary* maintained during parameter exchange. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Library Card" Analogy:** > Imagine you visit a library and wish to borrow a book. Instead of physically taking the book to your friend, you give your friend a **library card** that points to *your* borrowed book. Your friend can: > - Read the book (access state), > - Mark pages / write notes inside it (mutate state), > - But cannot destroy the *original* book on the shelf. > > In this analogy: > - The **book on the shelf** = the *object* in the **Heap** memory. > - The **library card** = the *reference variable* (passed as a parameter). > - The **friend** = the *method* that receives the card. > - **Any annotations written by the friend are visible to you later**, just as object modifications inside a method are visible to the caller. ### 1.3 Classification of Parameter Passing Mechanisms | Mechanism | Languages | What is Transmitted | Effect on Caller's Object | |---|---|---|---| | **Pass-by-Value (Object Copy)** | C++ (default) | Bitwise copy of the object | Caller's object is **unaffected** by callee changes | | **Pass-by-Reference** | C++ (`Type&`) | Memory address (alias) | Caller's object is **directly affected** | | **Pass-by-Pointer** | C++ (`Type*`) | Address of object | Caller's object is **affected** if dereferenced | | **Pass-by-Reference-of-Reference (Java/C#)** | Java, C# | Copy of the reference value | Caller's object is **affected** for state changes, but reassignment is local | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In Java, objects are **never** truly pass-by-reference. The reference itself is passed **by value**. This subtle distinction is a frequent board-exam trap. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Object Passing Works The mechanism unfolds in four discrete steps: 1. **Object Creation on the Heap:** The caller instantiates the object using `new`. Memory is allocated in the **heap region**, and a *reference variable* (stored on the **stack frame** of the caller) is bound to it. 2. **Reference Handover:** When the method is invoked, the JVM/runtime computes the *reference value* (essentially a memory address) and pushes it onto the callee's stack as the formal parameter. 3. **Aliasing Effect:** Both the caller's reference and the callee's parameter now *alias* the same heap object. The heap object has **reference count ≥ 2** temporarily. 4. **State Manipulation / Reassignment:** The callee may either: - *Mutate* the object's fields (visible to the caller), **or** - *Reassign* its local parameter to another object (this reassignment is **invisible** to the caller, since the original reference in the caller remains unchanged). ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Step 1 (Heap Allocation):** Dynamic memory allows object lifetimes to extend beyond the scope that created them. - **Step 2 (Reference Copy):** Transmitting an entire object's state would be O(n) in object size; transmitting a fixed-size pointer/reference is O(1) — a major efficiency gain. - **Step 3 (Aliasing):** Enables shared state, which is essential for design patterns like *Observer*, *Strategy*, and *Flyweight*. - **Step 4 (Mutation vs. Reassignment):** Preserves **encapsulation** at the *reference level* (no wholesale substitution) while permitting **collaborative modification** at the *state level*. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Syntax & Behavioural Rules) | # | Concept | Java Syntax / Rule | C++ Syntax / Rule | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Passing object by value | Not applicable for objects (always reference-of-reference) | `void f(MyClass obj);` | | 2 | Passing object by reference | Not applicable | `void f(MyClass& obj);` | | 3 | Passing object by pointer | Not applicable | `void f(MyClass* obj);` | | 4 | Returning object from method | `return new MyClass(...);` | `MyClass f() { return MyClass(...); }` | | 5 | Reassigning parameter | Affects only local copy of reference | Affects caller's variable (if `&` used) | | 6 | Mutating fields | Affects original heap object | Affects caller's object (if `&`/`*` used) | | 7 | Memory address size | Typically **4 bytes** (32-bit JVM) or **8 bytes** (64-bit with compressed oops) | **8 bytes** (64-bit) for pointers | | 8 | Parameter evaluation order | Left-to-right (per JLS §15.7.4) | Indeterminate (pre-C++17); left-to-right (C++17 onwards) | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Distributed Systems:** Object serialisation and DTO (Data Transfer Object) passing across network boundaries rely on parameter-passing contracts. - **GUI Frameworks:** Event listeners receive *event objects* as parameters (`ActionEvent e`), allowing handlers to inspect the source of interaction. - **Scientific Computing:** Matrix and vector objects are passed to numerical routines (e.g., *LAPACK bindings*) to avoid expensive copies. - **Game Development:** Game state objects are passed by reference to rendering and physics subsystems for real-time updates. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough > [!IMPORTANT] > This section provides a **meticulous, step-by-step** analysis of object passing. No step is skipped. All memory-state transitions are explicitly enumerated. ### 3.1 Worked Example — Java: Passing an Object to a Method Consider the following Java program: ```java class Box { double width, height, depth; Box(double w, double h, double d) { width = w; height = h; depth = d; } double volume() { return width * height * depth; } } class BoxDemo { static void enlarge(Box b, double factor) { b.width *= factor; b.height *= factor; b.depth *= factor; } public static void main(String[] args) { Box myBox = new Box(2.0, 3.0, 4.0); System.out.println("Before: " + myBox.volume()); enlarge(myBox, 2.0); System.out.println("After: " + myBox.volume()); } } ``` #### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Execution Trace **Step 1 — `new Box(2.0, 3.0, 4.0)` is executed.** Memory is allocated on the heap. A `Box` object is initialised with: $$w_{\text{initial}} = 2.0, \quad h_{\text{initial}} = 3.0, \quad d_{\text{initial}} = 4.0$$ The reference value `0xABC123` (an illustrative 6-digit hex address) is bound to the stack-local variable `myBox`. **Step 2 — `enlarge(myBox, 2.0)` is invoked.** The runtime pushes a new stack frame. Inside it, the formal parameter `b` is initialised with the **same reference value** `0xABC123`. The literal `2.0` is passed by value to `factor`. **Memory State Diagram (textual):** | Stack Frame of `main` | Value | Stack Frame of `enlarge` | Value | |---|---|---|---| | `myBox` | `0xABC123` | `b` | `0xABC123` | | | | `factor` | `2.0` | **Step 3 — Inside `enlarge`, fields are mutated.** The expression `b.width *= factor` is evaluated as: $$b.\text{width} = b.\text{width} \times \text{factor} = 2.0 \times 2.0 = 4.0$$ Similarly: $$b.\text{height} = 3.0 \times 2.0 = 6.0$$ $$b.\text{depth} = 4.0 \times 2.0 = 8.0$$ Because `b` and `myBox` alias the same heap object, the *single* heap object now holds `width=4.0, height=6.0, depth=8.0`. **Step 4 — `enlarge` returns.** The stack frame of `enlarge` is destroyed. Variable `b` ceases to exist. Variable `factor` also ceases to exist. However, the heap object persists because `myBox` in `main` still holds a valid reference. **Step 5 — `myBox.volume()` is recomputed.** $$\text{Volume}_{\text{before}} = 2.0 \times 3.0 \times 4.0 = 24.0$$ $$\text{Volume}_{\text{after}} = 4.0 \times 6.0 \times 8.0 = 192.0$$ **Output:** ``` Before: 24.0 After: 192.0 ``` ### 3.2 Worked Example — C++: Pass by Value vs. Pass by Reference ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Counter { public: int value; Counter(int v) : value(v) {} }; void incrementByValue(Counter c) { c.value += 10; } void incrementByReference(Counter& c) { c.value += 10; } void incrementByPointer(Counter* c) { c->value += 10; } int main() { Counter obj(5); incrementByValue(obj); cout << obj.value << endl; // 5 incrementByReference(obj); cout << obj.value << endl; // 15 incrementByPointer(&obj); cout << obj.value << endl; // 25 return 0; } ``` #### 3.2.1 Analytical Table for the C++ Example | Call | Mechanism | Local Copy Created? | Effect on `obj.value` | Final Value of `obj.value` | |---|---|---|---|---| | `incrementByValue(obj)` | Pass-by-Value | Yes (a bitwise copy on the callee stack) | None (mutations affect only the copy) | **5** | | `incrementByReference(obj)` | Pass-by-Reference (`Counter&`) | No (binding to original) | `obj.value` increases by 10 | **15** | | `incrementByPointer(&obj)` | Pass-by-Pointer (`Counter*`) | No (address copied) | `obj.value` increases by 10 | **25** | ### 3.3 Mathematical Model of Object Passing Cost Let $S$ denote the size of an object (in bytes) and $P$ denote the size of a reference/pointer (in bytes, typically 4 or 8). **Time complexity of parameter transmission:** $$T_{\text{transmit}} = \begin{cases} \mathcal{O}(S) & \text{if pass-by-value} \\ \mathcal{O}(P) & \text{if pass-by-reference or pass-by-pointer} \end{cases}$$ **Space complexity of callee stack frame:** $$\text{Stack}_{\text{param}} = \begin{cases} S & \text{pass-by-value} \\ P & \text{pass-by-reference} \end{cases}$$ > [!NOTE] > For large objects (e.g., a `10^6`-element array), pass-by-value would require $\mathcal{O}(S)$ byte copying, while pass-by-reference is $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — a dramatic saving. This is the **engineering rationale** for preferring references in performance-critical code. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture Flow — Java Object Passing ```mermaid flowchart TD mainStack["Main Stack Frame
myBox ref: 0xABC123"]:::stack heapBox["HEAP MEMORY
Box Object @ 0xABC123
width:2.0 height:3.0 depth:4.0"]:::heap callSite["invocation: enlarge(myBox, 2.0)"]:::call calleeStack["Enlarge Stack Frame
b ref: 0xABC123
factor: 2.0"]:::stack mutation["Mutate fields via b"]:::op mutatedBox["HEAP MEMORY
Box Object @ 0xABC123
width:4.0 height:6.0 depth:8.0"]:::heap returnFlow["Return: stack frame popped"]:::return finalRead["myBox.volume() read"]:::op outputBox["OUTPUT
Before: 24.0
After: 192.0"]:::output mainStack -->|owns ref| heapBox mainStack -->|invokes| callSite callSite -->|pushes| calleeStack calleeStack -->|aliases same| heapBox calleeStack -->|executes| mutation mutation -->|mutates in place| mutatedBox mutatedBox -.->|visible after return| mainStack calleeStack -->|method exits| returnFlow returnFlow --> mainStack mainStack -->|reads via myBox| finalRead finalRead --> outputBox classDef stack fill:#FFF4E1,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef heap fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1F618D,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef call fill:#FAD7A0,stroke:#D35400,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef op fill:#ABEBC6,stroke:#196F3D,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef return fill:#F5B7B1,stroke:#C0392B,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#D7BDE2,stroke:#6C3483,color:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Three-Channel Comparison Diagram — C++ Object Passing Modes ```mermaid flowchart LR callerObj["Caller Object
Counter obj(5)"]:::obj valChan["CHANNEL 1: Pass by Value
void f(Counter c)"]:::val refChan["CHANNEL 2: Pass by Reference
void f(Counter& c)"]:::ref ptrChan["CHANNEL 3: Pass by Pointer
void f(Counter* c)"]:::ptr copyMem["Bitwise Copy
on Callee Stack
Mutations LOCAL only"]:::mem bindMem["Reference BIND
to Original Object
Mutations GLOBAL"]:::mem addrMem["Address COPIED
Pointer dereferenced
Mutations GLOBAL"]:::mem callerObj --> valChan valChan --> copyMem callerObj --> refChan refChan --> bindMem callerObj --> ptrChan ptrChan --> addrMem classDef obj fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#7D6608,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef val fill:#F5CBA7,stroke:#BA4A00,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef ref fill:#A9DFBF,stroke:#1E8449,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef ptr fill:#AED6F1,stroke:#1F618D,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef mem fill:#D5DBDB,stroke:#34495E,color:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Interaction Between Caller and Callee ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant M as main() Stack participant H as Heap participant E as enlarge() Stack M->>H: new Box(2,3,4) allocates object M->>M: myBox := 0xABC123 M->>E: invoke enlarge(myBox, 2.0) E->>E: b := 0xABC123 (parameter binding) E->>H: b.width := b.width * 2 E->>H: b.height := b.height * 2 E->>H: b.depth := b.depth * 2 E-->>M: return (frame popped) M->>H: read myBox.volume() H-->>M: returns 192.0 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q1.** *Differentiate between pass-by-value and pass-by-reference in the context of objects. Illustrate with a suitable example.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Pass-by-value:** A *copy* of the object is created on the callee's stack frame. Mutations performed by the callee are **local** and do not affect the original. (1½ marks) - **Pass-by-reference:** The callee receives an *alias* (or pointer) to the original object. Mutations affect the original. (1 mark) - **Example:** In C++, `void f(Counter c)` is pass-by-value; `void f(Counter& c)` is pass-by-reference. In Java, objects are always passed by value-of-reference, so field mutations propagate but reassignments do not. (½ mark) --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q2.** *What is actually passed to a method when an object is passed as a parameter in Java? Justify your answer.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - In Java, when an object is passed, the *reference value* (a memory address) is passed, **not** the object itself. (1 mark) - This is technically *pass-by-value* of the reference variable. As a result, the callee can mutate the object's state, but cannot make the caller's reference point to a different object. (1½ marks) - Justification: If Java supported true pass-by-reference, reassigning the parameter inside the method would change the caller's variable — but this does not occur. (½ mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2]** ### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **(a)** *Explain with a neat diagram the mechanism of passing objects as parameters in Java. Discuss what is actually transmitted and how it differs from passing primitive types. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** 1. **Definition (2 marks):** Passing objects as parameters is the act of supplying an object reference to a method, enabling the method to access and manipulate the object's state. Primitives, in contrast, transmit their actual value bit-for-bit. 2. **Diagram (2 marks):** A stack-heap diagram must show: - The caller's stack frame with a local reference variable. - The heap containing the actual object. - The callee's stack frame with a formal parameter holding the **same reference value**. - An arrow indicating that mutations on the heap are visible to the caller after the method returns. 3. **Distinction (2 marks):** - For **primitives**: a copy of the *value* (e.g., `int x = 5`) is pushed. Modifications are local. - For **objects**: a copy of the *reference* (address) is pushed. Field modifications are global; reassignment is local. 4. **Code snippet (1 mark):** ```java void modify(Point p) { p.x = 100; } // global effect void reassign(Point p) { p = new Point(0,0); } // local effect ``` **(b)** *Write a Java program that defines a class `Complex` with real and imaginary parts. Write a method `add(Complex c1, Complex c2)` that takes two complex number objects as parameters and returns a new `Complex` object representing the sum. Demonstrate the working in `main()`. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** ```java class Complex { double real, imag; Complex(double r, double i) { real = r; imag = i; } // Static method: takes two Complex objects as parameters static Complex add(Complex c1, Complex c2) { Complex result = new Complex(0.0, 0.0); result.real = c1.real + c2.real; result.imag = c1.imag + c2.imag; return result; // returns a new object } void display() { System.out.println(real + " + " + imag + "i"); } public static void main(String[] args) { Complex a = new Complex(3.0, 2.0); // 3 + 2i Complex b = new Complex(1.5, 4.5); // 1.5 + 4.5i Complex sum = Complex.add(a, b); // objects as parameters System.out.print("Sum = "); sum.display(); // Output: 4.5 + 6.5i } } ``` **Mark Allocation:** - Class definition with fields & constructor — 2 marks - `add` method correctly using object parameters — 3 marks - `main` demonstrating the working — 1 mark - Correct output trace — 1 mark --- ### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** *Compare the three ways of passing objects in C++: (i) by value, (ii) by reference, (iii) by pointer. Use a common class `Student {int marks;}` to demonstrate how each affects the caller's object. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** | Mechanism | Syntax | Effect on Caller's `marks` | Memory Cost | |---|---|---|---| | By Value | `void f(Student s)` | Unchanged (bitwise copy mutated locally) | $\mathcal{O}(S)$ copy | | By Reference | `void f(Student& s)` | Changed (alias to original) | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | | By Pointer | `void f(Student* s)` | Changed (if dereferenced) | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | **Demonstration Code (3 marks for code, 4 marks for explanation):** ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Student { public: int marks; Student(int m):marks(m){} }; void byValue(Student s) { s.marks += 10; } // local only void byReference(Student& s) { s.marks += 10; } // affects original void byPointer(Student* s) { s->marks += 10; } // affects original int main() { Student s(50); byValue(s); cout << s.marks << endl; // 50 byReference(s); cout << s.marks << endl; // 60 byPointer(&s); cout << s.marks << endl; // 70 return 0; } ``` **Key Points for Full Marks:** - By-value requires a copy constructor (may be expensive for large objects). (1 mark) - By-reference cannot be null and cannot be reseated. (1 mark) - By-pointer can be null and can be reseated; supports polymorphism. (1 mark) - All three affect only the heap-resident object's *fields*; the reference variable in the caller is unaffected in all three cases. (1 mark) **(b)** *Explain the term "returning objects from methods" with reference to Java. Write a Java method that returns a new `Date` object with incremented day, taking a `Date` object as parameter. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** A method may return an object reference, allowing the caller to receive a freshly constructed or modified object. The returned reference is copied (by value) to the caller's variable. (2 marks for theory) ```java class Date { int day, month, year; Date(int d, int m, int y) { day=d; month=m; year=y; } static Date nextDay(Date d) { // Simple: ignore month/year rollovers for brevity return new Date(d.day + 1, d.month, d.year); } public static void main(String[] args) { Date today = new Date(14, 8, 2024); Date tomorrow = Date.nextDay(today); System.out.println("Today: " + today.day + "/" + today.month + "/" + today.year); System.out.println("Tomorrow: " + tomorrow.day + "/" + tomorrow.month + "/" + tomorrow.year); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Today: 14/8/2024 Tomorrow: 15/8/2024 ``` **Mark Allocation:** - Correct theory of object return — 2 marks - Method signature with object parameter — 1 mark - `new Date(...)` construction and return — 2 marks - Working `main` with output — 2 marks --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "Java is pass-by-reference" with reality:** Many students incorrectly state that Java is pass-by-reference. The *correct* statement is: *"In Java, references are passed by value."* This distinction carries **2 marks** in ESE. > 2. **Forgetting to explain *aliasing*:** When a question asks "what is the effect of the method on the original object?", you **must** explicitly mention that the object on the heap is shared, not duplicated. > 3. **Ignoring reassignment semantics:** Failing to note that *reassigning* the parameter inside a method does **not** affect the caller's reference is a frequent deduction point. > 4. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** Questions carrying ≥ 7 marks explicitly require a *neat diagram*. Skipping it costs **at least 1.5 marks**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Objects are never passed by value in Java** — only the *reference value* (address) is passed. This is a **guaranteed** KTU board question. - ✅ In **C++**, three modes exist: pass-by-value, pass-by-reference (`&`), and pass-by-pointer (`*`). - ✅ **Aliasing** is the property that two or more references point to the same object. It is the *root cause* of side-effects in object parameter passing. - ✅ **Field mutations** propagate to the caller; **reference reassignments** do not (in Java). - ✅ For large objects, prefer pass-by-reference or pass-by-pointer to avoid the $\mathcal{O}(S)$ copy cost. - ✅ The formal parameter in a method has its own **stack frame slot**; when the method returns, this slot is deallocated, but the heap object (if referenced elsewhere) persists. - ✅ **Returning objects** from methods is a clean way to produce new stateful results (e.g., factory methods, builder patterns). - ✅ Memory address size in Java: **4 bytes** (32-bit JVM) or **8 bytes** (64-bit JVM with compressed oops disabled). - ✅ Object passing is foundational to design patterns: *Strategy, Observer, Decorator, Command* all rely on object parameter exchange. - ✅ Always include a **stack-heap diagram** in long-answer questions; it is the single most efficient way to secure full marks.

topics.title: Returning Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Returning Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** under the C++ language paradigm (the primary vehicle of evaluation in the KTU 2024 Scheme for PECMT535), a function — whether a *standalone function* or a *class member function* — can be declared such that its **return type** is a user-defined class type. When this happens, the function is said to be **"returning an object"** of that class.

A function can return an object in one of **three principal ways**:

1. **Return by Value** — A copy of the object is returned to the caller. The compiler implicitly invokes the **Copy Constructor** to create the temporary object that is handed back to the calling environment.
2. **Return by Reference** — The function returns an alias (reference) to an existing object. No new object is constructed; the reference acts as an alternative name for the original.
3. **Return by Pointer** — The function returns the memory address of an object. The caller receives a pointer that can be dereferenced to access the object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to distinguish between these three return mechanisms, identify when the **Copy Constructor** is silently invoked by the compiler, and explain the **dangers of returning a reference/pointer to a local (automatic) object** that goes out of scope.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an object as a **sealed file folder** containing important documents (data members). Now consider three real-world scenarios:

- **Return by Value → Photocopying the Folder** 📂  
  Imagine a librarian who, when asked for a folder, makes a **photocopy** and hands the copy to you. The original folder is untouched, and you can do whatever you want with your copy without affecting the original. This is exactly what *return by value* does — a brand new duplicate object is constructed and given to the caller.

- **Return by Reference → Handing Over a "View" of the Folder** 🔍  
  Instead of making a copy, the librarian gives you a **special pass (a reference token)** that lets you directly view and modify the *original* folder. Any change you make via the token is reflected in the original. This is efficient (no copying) but **dangerous** if the original folder is later destroyed (you'd be holding a token pointing to a non-existent folder).

- **Return by Pointer → A GPS Coordinate to the Folder** 📍  
  The librarian writes down the *shelf number and aisle* (memory address) of the folder. You can walk to it and access it. Again, this is fast and lightweight, but if the folder is discarded (memory deallocated), your GPS coordinates point to nothing — a classic **dangling pointer** problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Returning objects is one of the most elegant features of C++ that enables powerful design patterns like **Factory Functions**, **Operator Overloading chaining** (e.g., `a = b + c + d`), and **Builder Patterns**. However, it requires careful attention to object lifetime and memory management.

## 1.3 The Three Return Mechanisms — Quick Reference

| Mechanism | Syntax | New Object Created? | Copy Constructor Invoked? | Risk of Dangling? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Return by Value** | `ClassName function()` | Yes (temporary) | **Yes** | No (safe) |
| **Return by Reference** | `ClassName& function()` | No (alias) | No | **Yes — High Risk** |
| **Return by Pointer** | `ClassName* function()` | No (address) | No | **Yes — High Risk** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall:** Many students write `return obj;` inside a function declared to return `ClassName&` (by reference) where `obj` is a local variable. The compiler will usually issue a **warning**, and the program may crash at runtime. **Never return a reference or pointer to a local automatic variable!**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How Object Return Works Internally

When a function returns an object, the C++ compiler performs a sequence of low-level operations. Understanding this sequence is critical for the KTU 2024 examination.

### Mechanism A: Return by Value (The Most Common & Safe)

```
Step 1 → Function executes and creates a local object of type ClassName.
Step 2 → When 'return obj;' is encountered, the compiler invokes the
         Copy Constructor to construct a TEMPORARY object (often stored
         in a hidden register or compiler-generated slot).
Step 3 → The local 'obj' is destroyed as the function frame unwinds.
Step 4 → The temporary object is then copy-constructed (or move-
         constructed in C++11+) into the receiving variable in the caller.
Step 5 → The temporary is destroyed.
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern C++ (C++11 and beyond) Insight:** The compiler applies **Return Value Optimization (RVO)** and **Copy Elision** to skip Step 4 in many cases. However, conceptually and in KTU exam answers, you should still describe the Copy Constructor as being invoked.

### Mechanism B: Return by Reference

```
Step 1 → Function returns an alias (reference) to an EXISTING object.
Step 2 → No copy is made. The reference is bound to the object directly.
Step 3 → The function must guarantee that the referenced object will
         outlive the reference — i.e., the object is NOT a local variable.
```

**Valid Use Cases for Return by Reference:**
- Returning a member variable of an object: `return this->data;`
- Returning a parameter passed by reference: `return input;`
- Returning a static (long-lived) local variable.

### Mechanism C: Return by Pointer

```
Step 1 → Function returns the memory address of an object.
Step 2 → Caller must explicitly dereference the pointer.
Step 3 → Caller must ensure the object is not deleted while the pointer
         is still in use.
```

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax Pattern | Key Rule |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Return by Value** | `ClassName func()` | Copy Constructor called. Safe for locals. |
| **Return by Reference** | `ClassName& func()` | **Never** return a local variable. |
| **Return by Const Ref** | `const ClassName& func()` | Used to chain operations without copying. |
| **Return by Pointer** | `ClassName* func()` | Caller owns responsibility of lifetime. |
| **Copy Constructor Trigger** | `ClassName(const ClassName&)` | Invoked on `return obj;` by value. |
| **Move Constructor Trigger** | `ClassName(ClassName&&)` | Replaces copy in C++11+ for temporaries. |
| **Dangling Reference** | `ClassName& func() { ClassName x; return x; }` | **Undefined Behavior (UB)** |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ability to return objects powers several industry-grade patterns:

1. **Factory Functions / Named Constructor Idiom:** Used in libraries like *OpenCV*, *Eigen*, and *Boost* to produce configured objects.
2. **Operator Overloading Chaining:** Expressions like `c = a + b + d` work precisely because `operator+` returns an object (typically `const`) that becomes the left operand of the next `+`.
3. **Fluent Builder APIs:** Libraries like *Java Stream API*, *C++ Builder Patterns* rely on returning `*this` (the current object) by reference to enable method chaining: `obj.setX(1).setY(2).build();`.
4. **Mathematical Vector/Matrix Libraries:** Returning objects of `Vector`, `Matrix`, or `Complex` types is fundamental in scientific computing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Programmatic Analysis — Return by Value

Let us derive the behavior of returning an object **by value** step by step with traceable output.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Number {
    int value;
public:
    // 1. Parameterized Constructor
    Number(int v) {
        value = v;
        cout << "Parameterized Constructor called. Value = " << value << endl;
    }

    // 2. Copy Constructor
    Number(const Number& other) {
        value = other.value;
        cout << "Copy Constructor called. Value = " << value << endl;
    }

    // 3. Member function that RETURNS an object BY VALUE
    Number add(Number n) {
        Number result(value + n.value);
        return result;  // Copy Constructor invoked here
    }

    void display() {
        cout << "Display: " << value << endl;
    }
};

int main() {
    Number n1(10);          // Param Constructor: n1.value = 10
    Number n2(20);          // Param Constructor: n2.value = 20
    Number n3 = n1.add(n2); // Copy Constructor called (at minimum once)
    n3.display();
    return 0;
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Trace (Analytical Matrix)

| Step | Line Executed | Action | Constructor Invoked | Object State |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `Number n1(10);` | Create `n1` | Parameterized | `n1.value = 10` |
| 2 | `Number n2(20);` | Create `n2` | Parameterized | `n2.value = 20` |
| 3 | `n1.add(n2)` called | Pass `n2` by value | **Copy** | `n` inside `add` is a copy of `n2` |
| 4 | `Number result(...)` | Create `result` | Parameterized | `result.value = 30` |
| 5 | `return result;` | Return `result` | **Copy** | Temporary object of value `30` is created |
| 6 | `Number n3 = ...` | Assign to `n3` | **Copy / Elision** | `n3.value = 30` |
| 7 | `n3.display()` | Print value | — | Output: `30` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Expected Output (without RVO):**
> ```
> Parameterized Constructor called. Value = 10
> Parameterized Constructor called. Value = 20
> Copy Constructor called. Value = 20
> Parameterized Constructor called. Value = 30
> Copy Constructor called. Value = 30
> Copy Constructor called. Value = 30
> Display: 30
> ```
> With **Copy Elision (RVO)** enabled in modern compilers, the last two "Copy Constructor" lines may be suppressed.

## 3.2 Derivation — Return by Reference (Safe vs. Unsafe)

### Unsafe (Do NOT do this):

$$
\text{Dangling Reference Derivation:}
$$

$$
\text{ClassName\& badFunction()} \xrightarrow{\text{creates local}} \text{ClassName localObj} \xrightarrow{\text{return localObj;}} \text{Reference to destroyed memory}
$$

```cpp
// UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR — dangling reference
int& getValue() {
    int x = 42;
    return x;  // 'x' is destroyed when function ends!
}
```

### Safe (Correct pattern):

```cpp
// SAFE — return a reference to a static or member object
class Container {
    int data;
    static int sharedValue;   // static lives for the entire program
public:
    int& getStatic() { return sharedValue; }
    int& getMember() { return data; }  // 'this' outlives the call
};
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operator Chaining via Returned Objects

This is the algebraic/logical derivation of why expression chaining works:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } a, b, c \text{ of class Number, and } \texttt{operator+} \\
\text{(a + b)} &\Rightarrow \text{returns temporary } T_1 \\
T_1 + c &\Rightarrow T_1.\text{operator+}(c) \text{ returns temporary } T_2 \\
\text{Result} &= T_2 \text{ assigned to destination}
\end{aligned}
$$

```cpp
Number operator+(const Number& rhs) const {
    return Number(this->value + rhs.value);  // Returns by value
}
// Usage:
Number sum = n1 + n2 + n3;  // Works seamlessly due to object return
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Return by Value Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Caller invokes function returning by value] --> B[Function body executes]
    B --> C[Local object 'obj' created]
    C --> D[return obj statement reached]
    D --> E[Copy Constructor invoked]
    E --> F[Temporary object T created on stack/register]
    F --> G[Local 'obj' destroyed - function ends]
    G --> H[Temporary T returned to caller]
    H --> I[Receiving variable initialized with T]
    I --> J[Temporary T destroyed]
    J --> K[Caller uses the new object]
    
    style A fill:#E6F3FF
    style E fill:#FFE6E6
    style K fill:#E6FFE6
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Comparison of Three Return Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VAL["Return by Value"]
        V1[Original Object] --> V2[Copy Constructor]
        V2 --> V3[New Independent Copy]
    end
    
    subgraph REF["Return by Reference"]
        R1[Original Object] --> R2[Reference Alias]
        R2 --> R3[Same Memory - No Copy]
    end
    
    subgraph PTR["Return by Pointer"]
        P1[Original Object] --> P2[Address Extracted]
        P2 --> P3[Pointer Variable in Caller]
    end
    
    VAL --> X{Safety Analysis}
    REF --> X
    PTR --> X
    X --> SAFE[Safe - Independent Copy]
    X --> DANGER[Risky - Lifetime Dependency]
    
    style VAL fill:#D5E8D4
    style REF fill:#FFF2CC
    style PTR fill:#F8CECC
    style SAFE fill:#D5E8D4
    style DANGER fill:#F8CECC
```

## 4.3 Call Stack Diagram — Object Return Mechanism

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller as Caller Scope
    participant Stack as Function Stack Frame
    participant Temp as Temporary Object
    
    Caller->>Stack: invoke func()
    Stack->>Stack: Construct local 'obj'
    Note over Stack: obj lives on stack
    Stack->>Temp: invoke Copy Constructor
    Note over Temp: Temporary T created
    Stack->>Stack: Destroy local 'obj'
    Note over Stack: Function unwinds
    Temp->>Caller: Hand over T
    Note over Caller: Assign to receiving variable
    Temp->>Temp: Destroy T (if not elided)
```

## 4.4 Memory Layout Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM["Process Memory"]
        direction TB
        STACK["STACK SEGMENT"]
        STACK --> SF["Stack Frame of func()"]
        SF --> LOC["Local object 'obj' (auto storage)"]
        STACK --> TF["Temporary T (return slot)"]
        STACK --> CF["Caller's receiving variable"]
        
        HEAP["HEAP SEGMENT"]
        HEAP --> DYN["Dynamically allocated object (if returned by pointer)"]
    end
    
    LOC -.DESTROYED.-> SF
    TF -.TRANSFERRED.-> CF
    
    style STACK fill:#FFE4B5
    style HEAP fill:#B0E0E6
    style TF fill:#90EE90
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by returning an object from a function? Explain the concept of returning an object by value.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Returning an object from a function means that the function's return type is a user-defined class, and it provides an instance of that class as its result. When returned **by value**, a **copy of the local object** is created in the caller's scope using the **Copy Constructor**. The compiler constructs a temporary object, the local object is destroyed as the function ends, and the temporary is then used to initialize the receiving variable. This mechanism is safe but involves the overhead of copying. (3 marks: definition-1, by-value explanation with copy constructor-2)

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why should we not return a reference to a local object from a function? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A local object has **automatic storage duration** and is destroyed when the function's scope ends. If we return a reference to it, the reference becomes a **dangling reference** — it points to memory that no longer contains a valid object. Accessing it leads to **undefined behavior**.

```cpp
Number& badFunc() {       // 1 mark
    Number obj(10);       // local
    return obj;           // 1 mark - dangling reference created
}
int main() {
    Number& r = badFunc(); // undefined behavior
    r.display();
}
```
(1 mark for explanation of UB)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module Internal Choice):** Students answer **one** of two alternatives. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the different ways of returning objects from a function in C++ with appropriate examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

There are **three ways** to return objects in C++:

**1. Return by Value (2 marks)**
```cpp
class Box {
    int length;
public:
    Box(int l) : length(l) {}
    Box add(Box b) {
        return Box(length + b.length);  // returns new object
    }
};
```
A temporary object is created via the Copy Constructor, the local is destroyed, and the temporary is assigned to the receiver.

**2. Return by Reference (2.5 marks)**
```cpp
class Array {
    int data[10];
public:
    int& at(int i) { return data[i]; }  // safe: data outlives the call
};
```
Used to allow direct modification of members or to chain operations. Must never return a reference to a local variable.

**3. Return by Pointer (2.5 marks)**
```cpp
class Factory {
public:
    static Widget* create() {
        return new Widget();  // heap-allocated, caller must delete
    }
};
```
The caller obtains an address and is responsible for object lifetime management via `delete`.

---

**(b)** Write a C++ program to define a class `Complex` with real and imaginary parts. Include a member function `add` that returns a new `Complex` object representing the sum. Demonstrate the use of the Copy Constructor in this process. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Complex {
    float real, imag;
public:
    // Parameterized Constructor
    Complex(float r = 0, float i = 0) : real(r), imag(i) {
        cout << "Parameterized Constructor: " << real << "+" << imag << "i" << endl;
    }

    // Copy Constructor (explicitly shown for clarity)
    Complex(const Complex& c) : real(c.real), imag(c.imag) {
        cout << "Copy Constructor: " << real << "+" << imag << "i" << endl;
    }

    // Member function returning object by value
    Complex add(const Complex& c) {
        Complex temp(real + c.real, imag + c.imag);  // 1 mark - construction
        return temp;                                   // 2 marks - return triggers copy
    }

    void display() {
        cout << real << " + " << imag << "i" << endl;
    }
};

int main() {
    Complex c1(3.0, 4.0), c2(1.0, 2.0);   // 1 mark
    Complex c3 = c1.add(c2);                // 2 marks - shows return
    c3.display();                           // 1 mark
    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output (Compiler-dependent due to RVO):**
```
Parameterized Constructor: 3+4i
Parameterized Constructor: 1+2i
Parameterized Constructor: 4+6i
Copy Constructor: 4+6i
4 + 6i
```

**Mark Distribution:** Class with constructors (2), `add` function (2), `main` with demonstration (2), Output trace (1).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model - Alternative]`

**(a)** What is a copy constructor? When is it invoked in the context of returning objects from a function? Provide a suitable C++ code segment. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A **Copy Constructor** is a special member function that initializes a new object as a copy of an existing object of the same class. Its signature is `ClassName(const ClassName& obj)`. (2 marks)

In the context of returning objects, the Copy Constructor is invoked in the following scenarios (3 marks):

1. **Return by Value:** `return obj;` — when the local object is copied to a temporary.
2. **Pass by Value:** A function parameter declared as a class object triggers it.
3. **Object Initialization:** `ClassName n2 = n1;` invokes it.

**Code Segment (2 marks):**
```cpp
class Sample {
    int x;
public:
    Sample(int v) : x(v) {}
    Sample(const Sample& s) : x(s.x) {
        cout << "Copy Constructor Invoked" << endl;
    }
    Sample getObject() {
        Sample temp(10);
        return temp;  // Copy constructor invoked
    }
};
int main() {
    Sample s = Sample(5).getObject();  // multiple copy steps
    return 0;
}
```

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the process of returning an object by value, highlighting the role of the temporary object and the copy constructor. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The process involves the following sequence (4 marks for explanation, 3 marks for diagram):

**Step 1:** A function is invoked with return type `ClassName`.

**Step 2:** Inside the function, a local object `obj` is created via the parameterized constructor.

**Step 3:** Upon `return obj;`, the compiler invokes the **Copy Constructor** to construct a **temporary object** in the caller's evaluation context. This temporary acts as the return value carrier.

**Step 4:** As the function stack frame unwinds, the local `obj` is destroyed (destructor called).

**Step 5:** The temporary is then used to initialize the receiving variable in the caller — this may invoke the Copy Constructor again (or be elided via RVO).

**Step 6:** The temporary is destroyed once the full expression completes.

**Diagram:**
```
+------------------------+        +------------------------+
|   Function Scope       |        |   Caller Scope         |
|                        |        |                        |
|   [obj]   --------copy-ctor----> [Temporary T]          |
|     |                  |        |      |                 |
|     v (destructor)     |        |      v (copy/assign)   |
|   [destroyed]          |        |   [Receiver Variable]  |
+------------------------+        +------------------------+
```

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in PECMT535 Answers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mark Copy Constructor as implicit:** Many students write custom Copy Constructors and forget that the compiler *always* generates a default one. If the question asks "when is the Copy Constructor called?", you must mention both explicit and compiler-generated versions.
> 2. **Confusing 'Return by Reference' with 'Pass by Reference':** Returning by reference means the function's **return type** is `ClassName&`. Passing by reference means the **parameter** is `const ClassName&`. These are distinct mechanisms.
> 3. **Drawing the wrong memory region:** In diagrams, students often place temporary objects in the Heap. They are actually created in **Stack** memory (unless explicitly heap-allocated).
> 4. **Ignoring Copy Elision (RVO):** Modern compilers may suppress copies. In exam answers, write the *logical* sequence assuming the Copy Constructor is invoked — this matches KTU's expected model answer.
> 5. **Forgetting the `const` qualifier in `operator+`:** A frequent mistake. The const in `const ClassName&` parameter and `const` return type is crucial for chaining with rvalues.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Returning an object** means a function's return type is a user-defined class.
- ✅ **Return by Value** is the **default and safest** mechanism — invokes the **Copy Constructor**.
- ✅ **Return by Reference** (`ClassName&`) is efficient but **must never** be used for local variables — causes **dangling references**.
- ✅ **Return by Pointer** (`ClassName*`) places lifetime responsibility on the **caller**.
- ✅ The **Copy Constructor** is triggered at three points: return-by-value, pass-by-value, and object initialization.
- ✅ **Operator overloading** chains (`a + b + c`) rely entirely on returning objects (typically by value or const reference).
- ✅ **Copy Elision / RVO** is a compiler optimization that may suppress copies in modern C++ — but conceptually, the Copy Constructor is still considered invoked for exam purposes.
- ✅ **Const-correctness** is essential when returning objects meant for read-only chaining.
- ✅ Static objects and member variables (`this->`) are **safe targets** for return-by-reference.
- ✅ In KTU exam answers, always pair the explanation with a **diagram** showing the temporary object's lifecycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Study Tip:** Practice writing the *exact output trace* of programs that return objects. KTU frequently asks students to predict the order of constructor and destructor calls — mastering object return is essential for this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursion. Static Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recursion. Static Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Recursion — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursion** in Java is a programming technique in which a **method invokes itself** during its execution, either directly or indirectly, to solve a problem by breaking it down into smaller sub-problems of the same type.

Every recursive method must contain **two essential components**:

1. **Base Case (Termination Condition)** — A condition that stops the recursion and returns a direct, non-recursive value.
2. **Recursive Case** — The portion of the method where the function calls itself with a modified (typically smaller) input that progresses toward the base case.

**Formal Recurrence Relation Template:**

$$
T(n) = 
\begin{cases}
\theta(1), & \text{if } n \leq n_0 \quad \text{(Base Case)} \\
a \cdot T(n/b) + f(n), & \text{if } n > n_0 \quad \text{(Recursive Case)}
\end{cases}
$$

Where $a$ = number of recursive calls, $b$ = problem-size reduction factor, and $f(n)$ = cost of non-recursive work per call.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Russian Nesting Doll"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a set of *Matryoshka (Russian nesting) dolls*. To find the smallest doll, you keep opening a doll, and inside it you find another doll, which you open again. You stop only when you find a doll that is **solid (cannot be opened)** — this solid doll is the **base case**. The recursion unwinds back outward exactly the way it dove inward.

Translating to code: the *solid doll* = `return` statement for the base case; the *opening of dolls* = the recursive call; each *doll layer* = one stack frame.

---

## 1.2 Static Members — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Members** are class-level members (variables, methods, blocks, or nested classes) declared with the `static` keyword. They belong to the **class itself rather than to any individual object (instance)**, and they are loaded into memory exactly **once** when the class is first loaded by the Java ClassLoader.

There are four kinds of static members in Java:

| Static Construct | Keyword/Location | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Static Variable | `static` data field | A single shared copy across all objects |
| Static Method | `static` method | Utility behavior callable without an object |
| Static Block | `static { ... }` | One-time class initialization logic |
| Static Nested Class | `static class Inner` | Helper class tied to outer class, not its instances |

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Company Notice Board"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Picture a company office. Every employee (object) has their own personal desk drawer (instance variable) for personal items. But the **notice board at the entrance** is shared — it belongs to the *company (class)*, not to any individual employee. If the HR manager posts a new notice (changes the static variable), every employee sees the updated notice instantly. The notice board is created **once** when the company office is set up, and exists even if no employee is present. This is exactly how `static` works in Java.

The notice board = **static variable**; posting a notice = **static method**; the office inauguration ceremony = **static block**.

---

## 1.3 Why These Concepts Matter in OOP

> [!TIP]
> - **Recursion** is the natural way to express algorithms on recursive data structures (trees, linked lists, fractals) and forms the backbone of divide-and-conquer paradigms.
> - **Static Members** enable shared state, constants, utility libraries, factory methods, and the famous `main()` entry point — all without object instantiation overhead.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of Recursion — How It Works Under the Hood

When a recursive method is invoked, the **JVM** performs the following steps:

1. **Stack Frame Allocation:** A new **stack frame** is pushed onto the **call stack**. The frame contains the method's local variables, parameters, and the return address.
2. **Execution:** The method body executes until either a `return` statement or another recursive call is reached.
3. **Recursive Call Detected:** If a recursive call is made, control transfers back to step 1 (a new frame is pushed).
4. **Base Case Reached:** The terminating condition returns a concrete value without further recursion.
5. **Stack Unwinding:** Frames are popped in **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** order. Each popped frame receives the return value from the frame it called and continues its computation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why and How" matters:** Without a base case, recursion leads to **`StackOverflowError`** because frames are pushed indefinitely until JVM stack memory is exhausted. The base case is the recursion's *anchor*.

### 2.1.1 Types of Recursion

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Recursion** | Method calls itself directly | `factorial()` calls `factorial()` |
| **Indirect Recursion** | Method A calls B, B calls A | `methodA()` ↔ `methodB()` |
| **Head Recursion** | Recursive call is the **first** statement | `recurse(n-1); work;` |
| **Tail Recursion** | Recursive call is the **last** statement | `work; return recurse(n-1);` |
| **Tree Recursion** | Method makes **multiple** recursive calls | Fibonacci, Tower of Hanoi |
| **Non-tail / Body Recursion** | Work remains **after** the recursive call | `return n * recurse(n-1);` |

### 2.1.2 Memory Cost of Recursion

$$
\text{Total Stack Space} = \text{Depth of Recursion} \times \text{Size of One Stack Frame}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Java's default thread stack size is typically **512 KB to 1 MB**. Deep recursion (e.g., $n > 10{,}000$ for a naive factorial) can crash the program.

---

## 2.2 Mechanics of Static Members

### 2.2.1 Lifecycle

> [!NOTE]
> **Class Loading → Static Initialization → Object Creation → Program Termination**

1. The `.class` file is loaded by the **ClassLoader** when first referenced.
2. Static variables are assigned default values (e.g., `int → 0`, `Object → null`).
3. **Static initialization blocks** and **static variable initializers** execute in the order they appear in the source code (top-to-bottom).
4. Instance variables are initialized **only when `new` is invoked**.
5. Static members reside in the **Method Area (or Metaspace in Java 8+)** of the JVM, while instance members live on the **Heap** inside the object.

### 2.2.2 Critical Rules for Static Members

> [!WARNING]
> **Key Restrictions Students MUST Memorize:**

- A `static` method **cannot use `this`** or `super` keywords (no current object context).
- A `static` method **cannot directly call non-static (instance) methods** — it must invoke them through an object reference.
- A `static` method **cannot directly access non-static (instance) variables**.
- However, an **instance method CAN access static members** freely.
- `main()` is `static` precisely so the JVM can invoke it **before any object exists**.

### 2.2.3 Static Variable vs Instance Variable

| Property | Static Variable | Instance Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword | `static` | (none — default) |
| Memory Location | Method Area / Metaspace | Heap (inside object) |
| Copies | **1** per class | **1** per object |
| Accessed via | `ClassName.variable` (preferred) or object reference | `objectReference.variable` |
| When created | Class loading time | Object creation time (`new`) |
| When destroyed | Class unloading / JVM exit | Object garbage collection |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Rule Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> High-Yield Recurrence Relations and Static Rules for KTU Board Exams:

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|
| Factorial Recurrence | $n! = n \cdot (n-1)!$, with $0! = 1$ | Permutation/Combination algorithms |
| Fibonacci Recurrence | $F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)$, with $F(0)=0, F(1)=1$ | Optimization, dynamic programming |
| Power Recurrence | $x^n = x \cdot x^{n-1}$, with $x^0 = 1$ | Cryptography, scientific computing |
| Stack Depth | $\text{Depth} = \text{number of pending recursive calls}$ | Determines memory budget |
| Time Complexity (Naive Fib) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1) \Rightarrow O(2^n)$ | Worst-case exponential growth |
| Static Variable Address | Same memory address shared by all instances | Counters, ID generators, config flags |
| `Math` class fields | `public static final double PI = 3.14159...` | Mathematical constants |
| Static Block Execution | Runs **once**, in **source order**, during class loading | Loading native libs, initializing static maps |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Recursion** powers: file-system traversal (Linux `find`), compiler parsers (AST evaluation), XML/JSON parsing, fractal image generation, backtracking algorithms (N-Queens, Sudoku solvers), and AI search (minimax tree search).
- **Static Members** power: design patterns like **Singleton** (private static instance), **Factory Method** (static method returning subclass), the `Collections` utility class, `System.out`, `Integer.parseInt()`, and **constants pools** (`public static final`).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Trace Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Trace: Factorial Using Recursion

**Problem:** Compute $5!$

**Source Code:**

```java
class Factorial {
    static int fact(int n) {
        if (n == 0 || n == 1)        // BASE CASE
            return 1;
        else
            return n * fact(n - 1);  // RECURSIVE CASE
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("5! = " + fact(5));
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

> [!NOTE]
> The following trace shows **stack growth (downward arrow ↓)**, **value computation**, and **stack unwinding (upward arrow ↑)**. Every frame is explicitly shown so no step is skipped.

| Call # | Invocation | n | Action | Returns To | Returned Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `fact(5)` (from main) | 5 | $5 \neq 0, 1 \Rightarrow$ calls `fact(4)` | Call 2 result | $5 \times 24 = 120$ |
| 2 | `fact(4)` (from Call 1) | 4 | $4 \neq 0, 1 \Rightarrow$ calls `fact(3)` | Call 3 result | $4 \times 6 = 24$ |
| 3 | `fact(3)` (from Call 2) | 3 | $3 \neq 0, 1 \Rightarrow$ calls `fact(2)` | Call 4 result | $3 \times 2 = 6$ |
| 4 | `fact(2)` (from Call 3) | 2 | $2 \neq 0, 1 \Rightarrow$ calls `fact(1)` | Call 5 result | $2 \times 1 = 2$ |
| 5 | `fact(1)` (from Call 4) | 1 | **Base case hit** → returns 1 | Call 4 | `1` |

### Mathematical Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{fact}(5) &= 5 \times \text{fact}(4) \\
&= 5 \times (4 \times \text{fact}(3)) \\
&= 5 \times 4 \times (3 \times \text{fact}(2)) \\
&= 5 \times 4 \times 3 \times (2 \times \text{fact}(1)) \\
&= 5 \times 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 \quad \text{(since fact(1) = 1)} \\
&= 120
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The number of stack frames at peak = depth of recursion = 5. Total stack memory ≈ $5 \times \text{frame\_size}$. After Call 5 returns, frames pop in **LIFO order**: 5 → 4 → 3 → 2 → 1.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Trace: Fibonacci Using Recursion

**Problem:** Compute $F(5)$

**Code:**

```java
static int fib(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 0;       // base case 1
    if (n == 1) return 1;       // base case 2
    return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2);
}
```

### Recurrence Derivation

$$
F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2), \quad F(0) = 0, \quad F(1) = 1
$$

### Closed-Form (Binet's Formula — for academic reference)

$$
F(n) = \frac{\phi^n - \psi^n}{\sqrt{5}}, \quad \text{where} \quad \phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}, \quad \psi = \frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2}
$$

### Recursive Call Tree for `fib(5)` — Full Unwinding

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(5) &= F(4) + F(3) \\
&= (F(3) + F(2)) + (F(2) + F(1)) \\
&= ((F(2) + F(1)) + (F(1) + F(0))) + ((F(1) + F(0)) + 1) \\
&= (((F(1) + F(0)) + 1) + (1 + 0)) + ((1 + 0) + 1) \\
&= (((1 + 0) + 1) + 1) + (1 + 1) \\
&= ((1 + 1) + 1) + 2 \\
&= (2 + 1) + 2 \\
&= 3 + 2 \\
&= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The naive recursive Fibonacci has **exponential time complexity** $O(2^n)$ because it recomputes the same sub-problems. KTU board questions sometimes ask to **convert it to an iterative version with $O(n)$ time and $O(1)$ space** using a rolling-variable technique.

---

## 3.3 Power Computation — Two Recursive Strategies

### Strategy A: Linear Recursion

$$
\text{pow}(x, n) = x \cdot \text{pow}(x, n-1), \quad \text{pow}(x, 0) = 1
$$

Time: $O(n)$ recursive calls.

### Strategy B: Fast Exponentiation (Divide and Conquer)

$$
\text{powFast}(x, n) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & n = 0 \\
\text{powFast}(x, n/2) \cdot \text{powFast}(x, n/2), & n \text{ even} \\
x \cdot \text{powFast}(x, (n-1)/2) \cdot \text{powFast}(x, (n-1)/2), & n \text{ odd}
\end{cases}
$$

**Complexity Derivation:**

$$
T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad T(n) = O(\log n)
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a classic KTU question: *"Compare the two strategies and state which is preferred for cryptographic applications."* Answer: **Strategy B**, because modular exponentiation in RSA encryption requires $O(\log n)$ multiplications.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Static vs Instance Behavior

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is built around a single running example so the difference is unmistakable.

```java
class Employee {
    static String company = "KTU Ltd";   // static — shared
    int id;                              // instance — per object
    static int count = 0;                // static counter

    Employee(int id) {
        this.id = id;
        count++;                         // increments shared counter
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e1 = new Employee(101);
        Employee e2 = new Employee(102);
        Employee e3 = new Employee(103);

        System.out.println(e1.company); // KTU Ltd
        System.out.println(e2.company); // KTU Ltd (same copy)
        System.out.println(e3.company); // KTU Ltd
        System.out.println(Employee.count); // 3 (shared counter)
    }
}
```

| Object Reference | `company` (static) | `id` (instance) | `count` (static) after constructor |
|---|---|---|---|
| `e1` (after construction) | "KTU Ltd" | 101 | 1 |
| `e2` (after construction) | "KTU Ltd" | 102 | 2 |
| `e3` (after construction) | "KTU Ltd" | 103 | 3 |
| `e1.company = "NewCo"` | "NewCo" (for ALL) | 101 | 3 |
| `e2.company` | "NewCo" (affected!) | 102 | 3 |

> [!WARNING]
> If any object writes to a static field, **every other object sees the change** because they all share the single memory location. This is the most common source of bugs and is a **favourite KTU viva question**.

---

## 3.5 Static Block Execution Order — Detailed Derivation

```java
class OrderDemo {
    static int a = m1();           // initializer 1
    static { System.out.println("Static Block 1"); }  // block 1

    static int b = m2();           // initializer 2
    static { System.out.println("Static Block 2"); }  // block 2

    static int m1() { System.out.println("m1()"); return 10; }
    static int m2() { System.out.println("m2()"); return 20; }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution on First Reference

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Class loaded by ClassLoader.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Static fields get default values: } a=0, b=0. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Initialize } a = m1() \Rightarrow \text{prints "m1()", } a = 10. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Execute Static Block 1} \Rightarrow \text{prints "Static Block 1".} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Initialize } b = m2() \Rightarrow \text{prints "m2()", } b = 20. \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Execute Static Block 2} \Rightarrow \text{prints "Static Block 2".}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Output Sequence:**

$$
\text{"m1()"} \;\to\; \text{"Static Block 1"} \;\to\; \text{"m2()"} \;\to\; \text{"Static Block 2"}
$$

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Recursion Call Stack — Factorial(4) Visualization

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram shows the **call stack growth downward (push)** and the **unwinding upward (pop)** for computing $4! = 24$. Each rectangle is one stack frame.

```mermaid
graph TD
    mainFrame["MAIN STACK FRAME | Calls fact 4"]
    f4["FRAME fact 4 | n = 4 | 4 * fact 3"]
    f3["FRAME fact 3 | n = 3 | 3 * fact 2"]
    f2["FRAME fact 2 | n = 2 | 2 * fact 1"]
    f1["FRAME fact 1 | n = 1 | Base Case Return 1"]
    ret1["UNWIND 1 = 1"]
    ret2["UNWIND 2 * 1 = 2"]
    ret3["UNWIND 3 * 2 = 6"]
    ret4["UNWIND 4 * 6 = 24"]
    output["FINAL OUTPUT 24"]

    mainFrame --> f4
    f4 --> f3
    f3 --> f2
    f2 --> f1
    f1 -. returns 1 .-> ret1
    ret1 -. feeds to f2 .-> ret2
    ret2 -. feeds to f3 .-> ret3
    ret3 -. feeds to f4 .-> ret4
    ret4 -. feeds to mainFrame .-> output

    style mainFrame fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style f4 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style f3 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style f2 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style f1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633
    style ret1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style ret2 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style ret3 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style ret4 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style output fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue frames** = recursive invocations pushing onto the stack.
- **Green frame** = base case hit (no further recursion).
- **Red frames** = unwinding phase where multiplications complete.
- **Orange frames** = main program frame and final output.

---

## 4.2 Static vs Instance Memory Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following block-level architecture diagram illustrates where static and instance members physically reside in the JVM, and how multiple objects share a single static copy.

```mermaid
graph TD
    methodArea["METHOD AREA OR METASPACE | One Per Class"]
    staticCount["static int count = 0"]
    staticCompany["static String company = KTU Ltd"]
    staticMethod["static void increment Method"]

    heap["HEAP MEMORY | One Block Per Object"]
    obj1["OBJECT E1 | id = 101 | points to count and company"]
    obj2["OBJECT E2 | id = 102 | points to count and company"]
    obj3["OBJECT E3 | id = 103 | points to count and company"]

    methodArea --- staticCount
    methodArea --- staticCompany
    methodArea --- staticMethod

    obj1 -. shared reference .-> staticCount
    obj2 -. shared reference .-> staticCount
    obj3 -. shared reference .-> staticCount

    obj1 -. shared reference .-> staticCompany
    obj2 -. shared reference .-> staticCompany
    obj3 -. shared reference .-> staticCompany

    obj1 -. uses .-> staticMethod
    obj2 -. uses .-> staticMethod
    obj3 -. uses .-> staticMethod

    style methodArea fill:#e1d4ff,stroke:#330099
    style staticCount fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style staticCompany fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style staticMethod fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style heap fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633
    style obj1 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style obj2 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style obj3 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Method Area (purple)** holds a **single** copy of every static member.
- **Heap (green)** contains **three independent objects**, each with its own `id`.
- **Dashed arrows** show that every object's `count` and `company` accessors point to the **same memory location** in the Method Area.

---

## 4.3 Recursion vs Iteration Decision Flowchart

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid flowchart is the standard decision tree KTU expects students to reproduce when answering "When to use recursion vs iteration?".

```mermaid
