## 4.4 Static Member Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
graph LR
    s1["CLASS NOT LOADED"]
    s2["CLASS LOADING BY CLASSLOADER"]
    s3["STATIC DEFAULTS ASSIGNED INT 0 OBJECT NULL"]
    s4["STATIC INITIALIZERS AND BLOCKS EXECUTE IN ORDER"]
    s5["CLASS READY STATIC MEMBERS ACCESSIBLE"]
    s6["JVM SHUTDOWN OR CLASS UNLOADED"]

    s1 -- first reference triggers loading --> s2
    s2 -- class metadata parsed --> s3
    s3 -- initializers run top to bottom --> s4
    s4 -- all statics initialized --> s5
    s5 -- program runs --> s5
    s5 -- JVM exit or class loader GC --> s6

    style s1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style s2 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style s3 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style s4 fill:#e1d4ff,stroke:#330099
    style s5 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633
    style s6 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between static variables and instance variables in Java. Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Variable | Instance Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Declaration | `static int x;` | `int x;` |
| Belongs to | The class | Each individual object |
| Number of copies | **One** per class, regardless of objects | **One per object** |
| Memory location | Method Area / Metaspace | Heap (inside object) |
| Access | `ClassName.x` (preferred) | `objectRef.x` |
| Lifetime | From class loading to JVM exit | From `new` to garbage collection |

**Example:**

```java
class Student {
    static String college = "KTU";   // shared by all students
    String name;                     // unique per student
}
```

*(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: comparison table, 1 mark: example)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is recursion? State the two essential components of a recursive method with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Recursion is a technique in which a method calls itself, directly or indirectly, to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller sub-problems of the same type.

**Two Components (1 mark):**
1. **Base Case** — the termination condition that returns a value without further recursive calls.
2. **Recursive Case** — the call to the same method with a smaller input that moves toward the base case.

**Example (1 mark):**

```java
static int sum(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 0;          // Base Case
    return n + sum(n - 1);         // Recursive Case
}
```

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester pattern, students answer **one** of two full-module alternatives. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Static Members

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q.A (a)** Explain the concept of static members in Java. Discuss static variables, static methods, and static blocks with suitable examples. State any **four** restrictions on static methods. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Static Members — Concept (1 mark):**
Members declared with the `static` keyword belong to the class itself rather than to any specific object. They are loaded into memory only once when the class is first loaded by the ClassLoader, and they reside in the **Method Area / Metaspace** of the JVM.

**Static Variable (2 marks):**

```java
class Counter {
    static int count = 0;          // single shared copy
    Counter() { count++; }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Counter(); new Counter(); new Counter();
        System.out.println(Counter.count);  // prints 3
    }
}
```

- Only **one** copy exists, shared by all objects.
- Accessed using `ClassName.variableName` (best practice) or through any object reference.
- Used for counters, configuration flags, constants (`public static final`).

**Static Method (2 marks):**

```java
class MathUtils {
    static int square(int x) { return x * x; }
}

MathUtils.square(5);   // called without creating an object
```

- Invoked using `ClassName.methodName()`.
- Cannot use `this` or `super`.
- Common examples: `Math.sqrt()`, `Integer.parseInt()`, `main()`.

**Static Block (1 mark):**

```java
class DBConnection {
    static String url;
    static {
        url = "jdbc:mysql://localhost/ktu";
        System.out.println("Static block executed once");
    }
}
```

- Executes **once** during class loading, in source order with other static initializers.
- Used for one-time resource initialization (loading drivers, populating maps).

**Four Restrictions on Static Methods (1 mark):**

1. A `static` method **cannot use the `this` keyword**.
2. A `static` method **cannot use the `super` keyword**.
3. A `static` method **cannot directly access non-static (instance) variables**.
4. A `static` method **cannot directly call non-static (instance) methods** — an object reference is required.

---

**Q.A (b)** Write a Java program to maintain a **shared counter** that tracks how many objects of a class have been created, using static members. Explain the output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
class Player {
    static int objectCount = 0;     // shared counter
    String name;

    Player(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        objectCount++;              // increment on every construction
        System.out.println("Player " + name + " created. Total = " + objectCount);
    }

    static void displayCount() {    // static utility method
        System.out.println("Current player count = " + objectCount);
    }
}

public class PlayerDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Player p1 = new Player("Alice");
        Player p2 = new Player("Bob");
        Player p3 = new Player("Charlie");
        Player.displayCount();        // called without an object
    }
}
```

**Output (2 marks for tracing):**

```
Player Alice created. Total = 1
Player Bob created. Total = 2
Player Charlie created. Total = 3
Current player count = 3
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- `objectCount` is declared `static`, so only **one** memory location exists in the Method Area.
- Each `new Player(...)` increments this single shared counter, so the value grows from 1 to 3 across all three objects.
- `displayCount()` is a static method called via `Player.displayCount()` — no object needed.
- The static keyword is essential because if `objectCount` were an instance variable, each object would have its **own** copy starting at 0, defeating the purpose of counting.

**Why this is a good KTU question (1 mark):** It tests understanding of shared state, lifecycle of static variables, and the syntactic difference between `ClassName.member` vs `object.member` access.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Recursion

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q.B (a)** Explain recursion in Java. Write a recursive Java method to compute the **nth Fibonacci number** and trace its execution for $n = 5$ with a call tree diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Recursion Concept (1 mark):**
Recursion is a method design technique where a function calls itself. Every recursive method must have a **base case** (terminates recursion) and a **recursive case** (calls itself with a smaller sub-problem).

**Recursive Fibonacci Method (2 marks):**

```java
public class Fib {
    static int fib(int n) {
        if (n == 0) return 0;            // base case 1
        if (n == 1) return 1;            // base case 2
        return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2);   // recursive case (tree recursion)
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("F(5) = " + fib(5));
    }
}
```

**Execution Trace for F(5) (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(5) &= F(4) + F(3) = 3 + 2 = 5 \\
F(4) &= F(3) + F(2) = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
F(3) &= F(2) + F(1) = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
F(2) &= F(1) + F(0) = 1 + 0 = 1 \\
F(1) &= 1 \quad (\text{base case}) \\
F(0) &= 0 \quad (\text{base case})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Call Tree Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    F5["F 5 = 5"]
    F4["F 4 = 3"]
    F3a["F 3 = 2"]
    F3b["F 3 = 2"]
    F2a["F 2 = 1"]
    F2b["F 2 = 1"]
    F2c["F 2 = 1"]
    F1a["F 1 = 1"]
    F1b["F 1 = 1"]
    F1c["F 1 = 1"]
    F1d["F 1 = 1"]
    F1e["F 1 = 1"]
    F0a["F 0 = 0"]
    F0b["F 0 = 0"]
    F0c["F 0 = 0"]

    F5 --> F4
    F5 --> F3a
    F4 --> F3b
    F4 --> F2a
    F3a --> F2b
    F3a --> F1a
    F3b --> F2c
    F3b --> F1b
    F2a --> F1c
    F2a --> F0a
    F2b --> F1d
    F2b --> F0b
    F2c --> F1e
    F2c --> F0c

    style F5 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style F4 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style F3a fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style F3b fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style F2a fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633
    style F2b fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633
    style F2c fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633
    style F1a fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style F1b fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style F1c fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style F1d fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style F1e fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style F0a fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style F0b fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
    style F0c fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000
```

**Discussion (extra):** The naive recursive Fibonacci has time complexity $O(2^n)$ — a frequent KTU follow-up asks for the **iterative optimization** using rolling variables, achieving $O(n)$ time and $O(1)$ space.

---

**Q.B (b)** Write a recursive Java method to compute the **sum of digits of a number**. Demonstrate it for input **12345** and discuss the time and space complexity. Compare the recursive approach with an iterative approach. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Recursive Method (2 marks):**

```java
public class SumDigits {
    static int sumDigits(int n) {
        if (n == 0) return 0;                    // base case
        return (n % 10) + sumDigits(n / 10);     // recursive case
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Sum = " + sumDigits(12345));
    }
}
```

**Execution Trace for sumDigits(12345) (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sumDigits}(12345) &= 5 + \text{sumDigits}(1234) \\
&= 5 + (4 + \text{sumDigits}(123)) \\
&= 5 + 4 + (3 + \text{sumDigits}(12)) \\
&= 5 + 4 + 3 + (2 + \text{sumDigits}(1)) \\
&= 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + (1 + \text{sumDigits}(0)) \\
&= 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 0 \\
&= 15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Complexity Analysis (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time Complexity} &= O(d) \quad \text{where } d = \text{number of digits in } n \\
\text{Space Complexity} &= O(d) \quad \text{due to call stack depth} = d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iterative Comparison (1.5 marks):**

```java
static int sumDigitsIter(int n) {
    int sum = 0;
    while (n > 0) {
        sum += n % 10;
        n /= 10;
    }
    return sum;
}
```

| Aspect | Recursive | Iterative |
|---|---|---|
| Time | $O(d)$ | $O(d)$ |
| Space | $O(d)$ (call stack) | $O(1)$ (single variable) |
| Readability | More elegant for mathematical definitions | More efficient in memory |
| Risk | `StackOverflowError` for very large $n$ | Safe regardless of input size |

> [!TIP]
> **Verdict:** For digit sum, the **iterative version is preferred** in production code due to constant memory, but the recursive version is academically clearer and frequently tested in KTU.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Recursion & Static Members Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the base case** (–2 to –4 marks): A recursive method without a base case is logically incomplete. Examiners immediately deduct marks if the base case is missing or wrongly stated.
> 2. **Using `this` inside a static method** (–1 to –2 marks): This is a **compile-time error** in Java. If a student writes `this.count` inside a `static` method, marks are cut.
> 3. **Confusing static with final**: `static` means *one-copy-per-class*; `final` means *cannot-be-reassigned*. They are independent modifiers and can be combined (`public static final` for constants).
> 4. **Wrong complexity derivation**: Stating Fibonacci as $O(n)$ instead of $O(2^n)$ for the naive recursive version is a common error. Use the Master Theorem or recursion tree.
> 5. **Not drawing the recursion tree**: For Fibonacci-style questions, examiners expect a **call tree diagram** showing the explosion of duplicate sub-calls. Skipping the diagram costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Accessing static members via object reference** (style loss, not logic loss): Use `ClassName.member` — it is preferred and earns style marks.
> 7. **Mixing static and instance access rules**: Saying "a static method can access instance variables if an object is created inside it" — examiners accept this nuance **only if explicitly stated**, otherwise marks are lost.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Recursion and Static Members**

### Recursion Essentials
- Recursion = a method **calling itself** (directly or indirectly).
- **Two mandatory components:** Base case + Recursive case.
- **Stack frames** are pushed for each call and popped on return (LIFO).
- **Head recursion** = recursive call first; **Tail recursion** = recursive call last.
- **Tree recursion** = multiple recursive calls per invocation (e.g., Fibonacci).
- Naive recursive Fibonacci = $O(2^n)$ time; iterative version = $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ space.
- Factorial: $n! = n \cdot (n-1)!$, $0! = 1$.
- Power: linear recursion $O(n)$ vs fast exponentiation $O(\log n)$.
- Sum of digits: $O(d)$ where $d$ = number of digits.
- **No base case** ⇒ `StackOverflowError`.
- Use recursion for **trees, graphs, divide-and-conquer, backtracking**.

### Static Members Essentials
- Declared with `static` keyword; belong to the **class**, not the object.
- **Single copy** per class, stored in **Method Area / Metaspace**.
- **Static variable** = class variable, shared by all instances.
- **Static method** = callable via `ClassName.method()`; cannot use `this` / `super`.
- **Static block** = runs once during class loading, in source order.
- **Static nested class** = tied to outer class, not its instances.
- `main()` is `static` so JVM can invoke it **before any object exists**.
- `public static final` = a **constant** (e.g., `Math.PI`).
- **Initialization order:** static fields → static initializers → static blocks (all in source order) → instance fields → constructor.
- An **instance method CAN access static members**, but a **static method CANNOT directly access instance members**.
- Static members are commonly used for: **counters, constants, utility methods, factory methods, Singleton pattern**.

### Exam-Day Mnemonics
- **"Static = Shared"** → only one copy, all objects see the same.
- **"Base case BEFORE Recursive case"** → always write termination first to avoid infinite loops.
- **"Class loads, statics initialize, objects follow"** → lifecycle ordering.
- **"Instance can use static; static cannot use instance"** → one-way access rule.

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language, the keyword **`final`** is a non-access modifier applicable to **variables, methods, and classes**. When applied to a variable, it transforms the variable into a **constant** — a storage location whose value, once initialized, can never be reassigned throughout its lifetime. This immutability is enforced at **compile-time** by the Java compiler, generating a compilation error if any assignment attempt is detected post-initialization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT535 - Module 2):**
> A `final` variable in Java is a variable whose value cannot be changed once assigned. It can be:
> 1. **Final Instance Variable** — belongs to an object
> 2. **Final Static Variable** — belongs to a class (commonly called a *constant*)
> 3. **Final Local Variable** — declared inside a method/block
> 4. **Final Parameter** — passed into a method

The Java Language Specification (JLS §4.12.4) categorizes `final` variables as:
- **Definitely assigned once** before use
- **Cannot be modified** after initialization
- Treated as **compile-time constants** if the value is a primitive or String with constant expression initialization

---

## 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **`final` variable** like a **tattoo etched on your skin**. The moment you "ink" (initialize) a value into that variable, the name becomes permanent — no one, not even you, can overwrite or change it later. Unlike a whiteboard (regular variable) where you erase and rewrite freely, a final variable is a **carved stone inscription**: permanent, immutable, and permanent across its entire scope lifetime.

Another analogy: A **final variable** is like a **bottled label** — once the factory prints "500 ml" on the bottle, the contents can never be relabeled to "1 litre". The bottle can still hold liquid (the value can be read), but the label (the variable reference) is fixed forever.

---

## 🏷️ Classification of Final Variables in Java

| # | Type | Scope | Must Initialize? | Typical Use |
|---|------|-------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Final Instance Variable | Per Object | At declaration OR in every constructor OR in instance initializer | Per-object constant data (e.g., roll number) |
| 2 | Final Static Variable | Per Class | At declaration OR in static block | Global constants (e.g., `PI`, `MAX_SIZE`) |
| 3 | Final Local Variable | Inside method/block | Before first use | Method-scoped constants |
| 4 | Final Parameter | Method signature | Already initialized by caller | Read-only method inputs |
| 5 | Blank Final Variable | Instance/Static | Must be assigned exactly once in constructor/static block | Delayed initialization with immutability |
| 6 | Final Reference Variable | Any scope | Before use | Reference cannot point elsewhere; object state may still change |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A `final` reference variable means the **reference is constant**, not the object. The object itself may still be mutable (its fields can change), but the variable can never point to a different object.

---

## 📐 Code Skeleton (KTU Standard)

```java
class Student {
    final int ROLL_NO;              // Blank final instance variable
    final String COLLEGE = "KTU";   // Initialized at declaration

    Student(int roll) {
        this.ROLL_NO = roll;        // Must be assigned in every constructor
    }
}
```

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧠 Why Final Variables Exist — The Engineering Rationale

The `final` keyword was introduced into Java for **three primary engineering purposes**:

1. **Design Intent Declaration** — Communicates to other developers (and the compiler) that this value is a *design constant* and should not be altered.
2. **Performance Optimization** — The JVM can **inline** final variables (especially `static final` primitives/Strings) at compile time, eliminating runtime lookups.
3. **Thread Safety** — A `final` field is guaranteed to be visible to all threads *after* object construction (JMM — Java Memory Model guarantees safe publication).

---

## 🔍 Detailed Operational Breakdown

### 🔹 1. Final Instance Variable
- Declared **inside a class** but **outside any method**.
- Each object has its **own copy**.
- Must be initialized **exactly once** in one of three places:
  - At the point of declaration
  - In **every** constructor
  - In an **instance initializer block**

### 🔹 2. Final Static Variable
- Declared with `static final`.
- Belongs to the **class**, not the object.
- Only **one copy** shared across all objects.
- Must be initialized in:
  - Static block (if not initialized at declaration)
  - Cannot be initialized in instance initializer (compile error)

### 🔹 3. Final Local Variable
- Declared **inside a method**, constructor, or block.
- Must be initialized **before first use**.
- Once initialized in a code path, it cannot be re-assigned in that same scope.

### 🔹 4. Final Parameter
- The method/constructor parameter marked `final`.
- Caller's value is locked — the method **cannot reassign** the parameter.

### 🔹 5. Blank Final Variable
- A `final` variable **declared but not initialized** at the point of declaration.
- Useful when the value depends on constructor arguments (e.g., unique IDs).

### 🔹 6. Final Reference Variable
- The reference (pointer) cannot be changed, but the **object's internal state can still mutate**.

---

## 📊 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Final Variable Rules

| Rule # | Rule Statement | KTU Exam Frequency |
|:------:|----------------|:------------------:|
| **R1** | A `final` variable **cannot be reassigned** after initialization | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **R2** | A `final` instance variable must be initialized in **every constructor** or at declaration | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **R3** | A `final` static variable is also called a **compile-time constant** if initialized with a constant expression | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **R4** | A `final` reference variable cannot refer to another object, but the object's state can change | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **R5** | A blank `final` variable can be assigned **only once** (typically in constructor) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **R6** | `final` is allowed with `var` in Java 10+ for local type inference | ⭐⭐ |
| **R7** | `final` local variable must be **definitely assigned** before any use | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **R8** | Static blank `final` variable must be initialized in a **static block** (not constructor) | ⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems & IoT:** Hardware register addresses modeled as `final` constants.
- **Banking Software:** Tax rates (`TAX_RATE = 0.18`) declared as `static final` to prevent accidental modification.
- **Game Development:** `MAX_PLAYERS`, `BOARD_SIZE` declared as `static final` for inlining optimization.
- **Enterprise Java (Spring/Hibernate):** `final` fields in immutable DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) for thread-safe data exchange.
- **Cryptography:** Cryptographic keys declared `final` to prevent tampering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Math:** A `static final int MAX = 100;` is inlined by the compiler — every reference is replaced with the literal `100` at compile time, saving a field lookup at runtime.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧪 Exhaustive Code Derivations with Step-by-Step Logic

---

### 🔬 Derivation 1: Final Instance Variable — Initialization Analysis

**Problem Context:** Demonstrate three valid initialization pathways for a final instance variable.

```java
class Employee {
    // Path 1: Initialize at the point of declaration
    final String COMPANY = "KTU Tech";
    
    // Path 2: Blank final instance variable
    final int employeeId;
    final double salary;
    
    // Instance initializer block
    {
        salary = 50000.00;  // Initialized in instance block
    }
    
    // Constructor — MUST initialize all blank final fields
    Employee(int id) {
        this.employeeId = id;
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Analysis:**

$$
\text{Initialization Path} = \begin{cases}
\text{Path 1: At Declaration} & \text{(e.g., COMPANY)} \\
\text{Path 2: In Instance Initializer} & \text{(e.g., salary)} \\
\text{Path 3: In Every Constructor} & \text{(e.g., employeeId)} \\
\end{cases}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- The Java compiler performs **definite assignment analysis** on every constructor.
- If ANY constructor fails to assign a blank final field, **compile error CE 8: variable X might not have been initialized** is raised.
- Hence, if there are *N* constructors, the blank final must be assigned in *all N* of them OR in an instance initializer block.

---

### 🔬 Derivation 2: Final Static Variable — Constant Declaration

**Problem Context:** Show the canonical pattern for declaring class-level constants.

```java
class MathConstants {
    // Compile-time constants (inlined by compiler)
    public static final double PI = 3.14159;
    public static final int MAX_RETRIES = 3;
    
    // Blank static final — must be initialized in static block
    public static final String APP_NAME;
    
    static {
        APP_NAME = "KTU-OOP-Engine";
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Analysis:**

$$
\text{Static Final Lifecycle} = \text{Class Load} \to \text{Static Init} \to \text{Read-Only Forever}
$$

**Key Compiler Behavior:**
- When a `static final` primitive or `String` is initialized with a **compile-time constant expression** (a literal or other constant), every usage site is **inlined** at compile time:

$$
\text{Source: } \texttt{double\ area\ =\ MathConstants.PI\ *\ r\ *\ r;}
$$

$$
\text{After Compilation: } \texttt{double\ area\ =\ 3.14159\ *\ r\ *\ r;}
$$

This is why changing `PI` in the source requires **recompilation of all dependent classes** for the change to take effect.

---

### 🔬 Derivation 3: Final Reference Variable — Mutable Object, Immutable Reference

**Problem Context:** Prove that a final reference cannot change target, but object state can mutate.

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;

class ReferenceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
        
        list.add("Apple");     // ✅ ALLOWED — object state changes
        list.add("Banana");    // ✅ ALLOWED — same object, different content
        
        // list = new ArrayList<>();  // ❌ COMPILE ERROR — cannot reassign final reference
    }
}
```

**Logical Derivation:**

$$
\text{Final Reference} = \begin{cases}
\text{Reference Pointer: IMMUTABLE} & \text{Cannot change target object} \\
\text{Object Internal State: MUTABLE} & \text{Object fields can change freely} \\
\end{cases}
$$

**Visual Analogy in Math Form:**

$$
\text{Let } R_{final} = 0x7F32 \quad \text{(memory address)}
$$

$$
R_{final} \rightarrow \text{Object}_{\text{fields}}
$$

$$
R_{final}\ \text{cannot} = 0x8A91 \quad (\text{compile error})
$$

$$
\text{But } \text{Object}_{\text{fields}} \text{ can mutate freely}
$$

---

### 🔬 Derivation 4: Blank Final Variable — Delayed Initialization

**Problem Context:** Demonstrate the use of blank final for unique object IDs.

```java
class Account {
    final long accountNumber;  // Blank final
    String holderName;
    
    Account(String name) {
        this.holderName = name;
        this.accountNumber = System.currentTimeMillis();  // Assigned once
    }
    
    void updateName(String newName) {
        this.holderName = newName;       // ✅ holderName is NOT final
        // this.accountNumber = 1234L;   // ❌ COMPILE ERROR — final cannot reassign
    }
}
```

**Definite Assignment Rule:**

$$
\forall \text{ Constructor } C_i : \text{blank\_final} \in \text{Assigned}(C_i) \lor \text{CompileError}
$$

The compiler traces all possible code paths through a constructor. If any path leaves a blank final unassigned, the constructor is rejected.

---

### 🔬 Derivation 5: Final Local Variable & Final Parameter

```java
class LocalFinalDemo {
    
    void calculate(final int x) {  // Final parameter
        // x = 10;  // ❌ COMPILE ERROR — final parameter cannot be reassigned
        
        final int y;  // Blank final local
        y = 20;       // OK — first and only assignment
        // y = 30;    // ❌ COMPILE ERROR
        
        final int z;
        if (x > 5) {
            z = 100;  // OK
        } else {
            // z = 200;  // If omitted → "might not have been initialized"
        }
        System.out.println(z);  // Compiler needs all paths to assign z
    }
}
```

**Definite Assignment Rule for Locals:**

$$
\text{For local final } v: \quad \forall \text{ code path } p \text{ to use}(v): v \in \text{Assigned}(p)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A local `final` variable need not be assigned at declaration, but it must be **definitely assigned** on **every code path** that reaches its use. The compiler performs **flow analysis** to verify this.

---

### 🔬 Derivation 6: Common Pitfalls Table

| # | Code | Status | Reason |
|---|------|:------:|--------|
| 1 | `final int x; x = 10; x = 20;` | ❌ | Double assignment |
| 2 | `final int x = 10;` (inside method) | ✅ | Single assignment at decl |
| 3 | `static final int X;` (no init, no static block) | ❌ | Blank static final uninitialized |
| 4 | `final int x;` (only one ctor assigns) | ❌ | Other ctor leaves it blank |
| 5 | `void m(final int x) { x++; }` | ❌ | Final param cannot be modified |
| 6 | `final int[] arr = {1,2,3}; arr[0] = 9;` | ✅ | Object state mutation allowed |
| 7 | `final int[] arr = {1,2,3}; arr = new int[5];` | ❌ | Reference cannot change |
| 8 | `final String s; if(cond) s="A"; else s="B";` | ✅ | Both paths assign, definite |
| 9 | `final String s; if(cond) s="A";` then use | ❌ | `else` path leaves unassigned |

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a final variable in Java. Differentiate between final instance variable and final static variable with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A `final` variable in Java is one whose value cannot be changed once assigned. The compiler enforces this rule at compile time.

**Final Instance Variable:**
- Belongs to **each object** of the class
- Memory allocated per object in heap
- Must be initialized at declaration, in every constructor, or in an instance initializer

**Final Static Variable:**
- Belongs to the **class itself** (only one copy)
- Stored in Method Area
- Must be initialized in a static block if not initialized at declaration
- Often used for **compile-time constants**

```java
class Circle {
    final double radius = 5.0;          // final instance variable
    static final double PI = 3.14159;   // final static variable (constant)
}
```

*(Award full 3 marks for definition + clear distinction + example.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a blank final variable in Java? Why is it useful? Can a blank final variable be declared as static? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **blank final variable** is a `final` variable that is **declared but not initialized** at the point of declaration. It must be initialized exactly once — typically in a constructor (for instance blank final) or in a static block (for static blank final).

**Usefulness:**
- Allows **delayed initialization** with constructor arguments (e.g., unique IDs, timestamps).
- Preserves immutability: value is set once and locked forever.

**Can it be static?**
**Yes.** A blank final can be declared as `static final` and initialized in a **static initializer block** only. It cannot be initialized in an instance constructor (compile error).

```java
class Config {
    static final String DB_URL;   // blank static final
    static { DB_URL = "jdbc:mysql://localhost/ktu"; }
}
```

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of final variables in Java with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate:
   - A blank final instance variable
   - A final static variable (constant)
   - A final reference variable whose object is mutable
   
   Show what operations are allowed and which cause compile errors. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| # | Type | Example | Initialization |
|---|------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Final Instance | `final int id;` | Constructor / Initializer / Declaration |
| 2 | Final Static | `static final int MAX=10;` | Static block / Declaration |
| 3 | Final Local | `final int x=5;` (inside method) | Before use |
| 4 | Final Parameter | `void m(final int x){}` | Auto (passed value) |
| 5 | Blank Final | `final int x;` (no init) | One-time in ctor/block |

```java
class Demo {
    final int a = 10;               // Type 1
    static final int B = 20;        // Type 2
    
    void method(final int c) {     // Type 4
        final int d;                // Type 3, Type 5 (blank)
        // logic
    }
}
```

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;

class Student {
    final int rollNo;                       // Blank final instance variable
    static final String COLLEGE = "KTU";    // Final static constant
    final ArrayList<String> subjects;       // Final reference variable
    
    Student(int r) {
        this.rollNo = r;                    // Blank final assigned in constructor
        this.subjects = new ArrayList<>();
    }
    
    void addSubject(String s) {
        subjects.add(s);                    // ✅ ALLOWED — object state mutates
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student(101);
        s1.addSubject("Maths");
        s1.addSubject("Java");
        
        // s1.rollNo = 200;                  // ❌ COMPILE ERROR — final instance
        // s1.subjects = new ArrayList<>();  // ❌ COMPILE ERROR — final reference
        // Student.COLLEGE = "MIT";          // ❌ COMPILE ERROR — final static
        
        System.out.println("Roll: " + s1.rollNo);
        System.out.println("College: " + Student.COLLEGE);
        System.out.println("Subjects: " + s1.subjects);
    }
}
```

**Allowed Operations:**
- `s1.addSubject(...)` ✅
- Reading `s1.rollNo` ✅
- Reading `Student.COLLEGE` ✅

**Compile Errors:**
- Reassigning `rollNo` ❌
- Reassigning `subjects` reference ❌
- Reassigning `COLLEGE` ❌

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** What are the rules for initializing final instance variables and final static variables? Explain with code snippets. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the concept of **final reference variables** with a real-world analogy. Prove that a final reference cannot be reassigned but the object it points to can still be modified. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Rules for Final Instance Variable Initialization:**
1. Must be initialized **exactly once**.
2. Permitted at:
   - Point of declaration: `final int x = 10;`
   - Inside **every** constructor
   - Inside an **instance initializer block**

```java
class A {
    final int x = 5;             // Rule: at declaration
    
    final int y;                 // blank final
    { y = 10; }                  // Rule: in instance initializer
    
    final int z;
    A(int v) { z = v; }          // Rule: in constructor
    A() { z = 0; }               // every ctor must assign
}
```

**Rules for Final Static Variable Initialization:**
1. Stored in Method Area — single copy.
2. Permitted at:
   - Declaration: `static final int MAX = 100;`
   - **Static block only** (not constructor, not instance block)

```java
class B {
    static final int MAX = 100;
    static final String APP;
    static { APP = "KTU_OOP"; }   // static block
}
```

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Real-world Analogy:**
A final reference variable is like a **permanent home address**. Once you fix the address (e.g., "House No. 12, KT Street"), the address on your ID card never changes. However, the **house itself can be renovated** — you can repaint walls, add rooms, or change furniture. Similarly:
- **Reference (address)** = immutable
- **Object state (house interior)** = mutable

**Proof Code:**

```java
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.Map;

class FinalReferenceProof {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final Map<String, Integer> inventory = new HashMap<>();
        
        inventory.put("Apples", 50);     // ✅ Object state changes
        inventory.put("Oranges", 30);    // ✅ Same reference, new entry
        
        System.out.println(inventory);   // {Apples=50, Oranges=30}
        
        // inventory = new HashMap<>();  // ❌ COMPILE ERROR
        // Inventory reference cannot change.
    }
}
```

**Output:** `{Apples=50, Oranges=30}`

**Conclusion:** The reference `inventory` is locked to the original `HashMap` object, but entries within the object can be added, removed, or modified freely.

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> **1. Confusing final reference with final object** *(–2 marks)*: Many students write "object cannot be modified" — this is **WRONG**. Only the **reference** is locked.
> 
> **2. Forgetting every constructor must initialize blank final** *(–2 marks)*: If a class has 3 constructors, the blank final must be assigned in **all 3**, or compile error.
> 
> **3. Trying to initialize static final in instance constructor** *(–1 mark)*: This is a compile error. Static final must be initialized in static block.
> 
> **4. Writing `final int x; x=10; x=20;` and saying it works** *(–2 marks)*: The second assignment is a compile error.
> 
> **5. Not showing compile errors in answers** *(–1 mark)*: When asked "what causes an error", students must **explicitly state the error message** like *"error: cannot assign a value to final variable x"*.
> 
> **6. Using `final` with abstract method or class** *(–1 mark)*: If the question is about final methods/classes, remember: **final + abstract = compile error** (out of scope, but related).

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Final Variables:**

- ✅ A `final` variable is a **compile-time enforced constant** — value locked after one assignment.
- ✅ **Final instance variable** → per object; init at declaration / every constructor / instance block.
- ✅ **Final static variable** → per class; init at declaration / static block only.
- ✅ **Final local variable** → inside method; must be definitely assigned before use (flow analysis).
- ✅ **Final parameter** → method input is read-only inside the method.
- ✅ **Blank final variable** → declared but not initialized; assigned exactly once in constructor/block.
- ✅ **Final reference variable** → reference is locked, but **object state is still mutable**.
- ✅ `static final` with primitive or String literal → inlined at compile time for performance.
- ✅ **Compile error** on: reassignment, missing initialization in some constructor, double initialization.
- ✅ **Real-world use**: `MAX_SIZE`, `PI`, `TAX_RATE`, configuration constants, immutable DTOs.
- ✅ Exam mantra: *"`final` is about immutability of the binding, not necessarily the value of the object."*

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — FINAL VARIABLES**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — PECMT535*
