topics.title: Inner Classes. Inheritance - Super Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inner Classes. Inheritance - Super Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Inner Classes — Formal Definition

An **Inner Class** (also called a *nested class*) in Object-Oriented Programming is a class that is **declared as a member of another enclosing class** (the *outer class*). Inner classes logically group classes that are only used in one place, increase encapsulation, and can directly access the members (including private members) of their enclosing class.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECMT535):** "A class defined within another class is known as an inner class. Inner classes are a security mechanism in Java because an inner class can access all the members (including private) of the outer class."

There are **four** broad categories of inner classes recognised in the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus:

1. **Member Inner Class** — declared at member level of the outer class (non-static).
2. **Static Nested Class** — declared `static` inside the outer class.
3. **Local Inner Class** — declared inside a method, constructor, or block.
4. **Anonymous Inner Class** — a class without a name, declared and instantiated in a single expression.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Engine inside the Car"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an **automobile (outer class = `Car`)**. Inside the car lives an **engine (inner class = `Engine`)**. The engine is useless without the car, but it has full access to the car's fuel, electronics, and chassis. You don't build a separate factory just for the engine — it lives *inside* the car. Similarly, an inner class lives inside an outer class and can directly touch the outer's private "fuel lines" (private members).

---

## 1.2 Inheritance — The Super Class

**Inheritance** is the OOP mechanism by which a new class (the *sub-class*, *child class*, or *derived class*) is created from an existing class (the *super class*, *parent class*, or *base class*). The sub-class **inherits** fields and methods of the super class and may add its own.

The **super keyword** in Java is a reference variable that refers to the **immediate parent class object**. It is used to:

1. Call the **super class constructor** using `super()` or `super(arguments)`.
2. Access the **super class data members** when they are shadowed by sub-class members.
3. Invoke the **super class method** when overridden in the sub-class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** "The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable which is used to refer to the immediate parent class object. Whenever you create the instance of a sub-class, an instance of the parent class is created implicitly, referred to by the `super` reference variable."

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a **father–son relationship**. The son inherits the surname, property, and house style from the father (super class). If the son builds his own house that *resembles* the father's but with modifications, the original blueprint still exists (`super` reference) and the son can call it explicitly whenever needed (e.g., `super.buildHouse()`). The `super()` call is like the son **formally acknowledging the father before building his own identity**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | KTU Quick Identifier |
|------|---------|----------------------|
| Outer Class | The enclosing class that hosts the inner class | **Container** |
| Member Inner Class | Non-static class defined at member scope | **Object-bound** |
| Static Nested Class | `static` class defined inside outer class | **Class-bound** |
| Local Inner Class | Class defined inside a method/block | **Scope-bound** |
| Anonymous Inner Class | Nameless, one-shot class | **Inline** |
| Super Class | The parent class being inherited from | **Base / Parent** |
| Sub Class | The child class that inherits | **Derived / Child** |
| `super` keyword | Reference to immediate parent | **Upward pointer** |
| `super()` | Constructor call to parent | **Initialisation chain** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Types of Inner Classes — Operational Breakdown

### A. Member Inner Class

A non-static class declared **at the member level** of the outer class (just like a field or method).

- **Cannot declare `static` members** (except `static final` constants) because it is implicitly tied to an outer-class instance.
- **Requires an instance of the outer class** to be instantiated.
- **Has implicit access** to all members (including `private`) of the outer class.

**Instantiation Rule:**
```
OuterClass outer = new OuterClass();
OuterClass.InnerClass inner = outer.new InnerClass();
```

### B. Static Nested Class

A class declared with the `static` modifier inside the outer class.

- **Class-bound**, not object-bound → can be instantiated without an outer object.
- **Can declare `static` and non-static members** freely.
- **Can access only `static` members** of the outer class directly.

**Instantiation Rule:**
```
OuterClass.StaticNestedClass obj = new OuterClass.StaticNestedClass();
```

### C. Local Inner Class

A class declared **inside a method, constructor, or block**.

- Scope is **limited to the enclosing block** (method, loop, if-block).
- **Cannot be marked `private`, `public`, `protected`, or `static`** (effectively).
- Can access effectively `final` (or `final` in pre-Java 8) local variables of the enclosing block.

### D. Anonymous Inner Class

A **nameless local class** declared and instantiated in a single expression using `new`.

- **Used to override methods of a class or interface on the fly** without creating a separate `.class` file.
- Common in event-handling, callbacks, and lambda pre-Java-8 contexts.

**Syntax Pattern:**
```
new ParentOrInterface() {
    // overridden method bodies
};
```

---

## 2.2 Super Class Concepts — Operational Breakdown

### Why `super` is needed (Three Operational Reasons)

1. **Constructor Chaining** — When a sub-class object is created, the parent constructor must execute first to initialise inherited state. `super()` does this.
2. **Disambiguating Shadowed Fields** — If the sub-class declares a field with the same name as the parent, `super.field` explicitly refers to the parent's field.
3. **Calling Overridden Parent Methods** — If the sub-class overrides a method, `super.method()` invokes the parent's version.

### Order of Construction (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** In Java, the constructor of the **super class is always executed before the constructor of the sub-class**. The compiler **automatically inserts a call to `super()` as the first statement** of every sub-class constructor. If you do not write `super(...)` explicitly, the no-argument `super()` is invoked implicitly.

### Constructor Chaining Hierarchy

```
Object  ←  SuperClass  ←  SubClass  ←  SubSubClass
```

Every constructor implicitly calls its direct parent's no-argument constructor, all the way up to `Object`.

### Access Rules for `super`

| Scenario | Allowed? | Example |
|----------|----------|---------|
| Call super constructor in **first line** of sub-class constructor | ✅ Yes | `super(10);` |
| Call super constructor anywhere **except first line** | ❌ Compile error | `x = 5; super();` |
| Access parent's `private` field via `super` from sub-class | ❌ Not directly (use `protected`/`public`) | `super.privateField;` |
| Access parent's `public`/`protected` field via `super` | ✅ Yes | `super.name;` |
| Use `super` inside a `static` method | ❌ Compile error | `super.x` in `static void m()` |
| Use `super` inside a non-static method | ✅ Yes | `super.display();` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why can't `super` be used in a `static` context?** Because `super` refers to the **parent class instance**, and `static` members belong to the class — no instance exists. This is a frequently-tested KTU conceptual question.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Inner Classes vs Super Class

| Concept | Core Rule | KTU Memory Hook |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| Member inner class | Needs outer instance to instantiate | **"outer.new"** |
| Static nested class | Does not need outer instance | **"static = class-level"** |
| Local inner class | Scope = enclosing block only | **"method-scoped"** |
| Anonymous inner class | One-shot, no class name | **"new Parent(){...};"** |
| `super()` call | Must be **first line** of constructor | **"super is always first"** |
| Implicit `super()` | Compiler auto-inserts no-arg `super()` | **"free ride"** |
| `super.field` | Resolves shadowed name | **"explicit parent"** |
| `super.method()` | Calls overridden parent version | **"extend, don't replace"** |
| `super` in static | **Not allowed** | **"super is an instance ref"** |
| Constructor order | Parent → Child (top-down) | **"root to leaf"** |
| Access from inner to outer | All members, even `private` | **"trusted tenant"** |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Industry Use-Case |
|---------|-------------------|
| Inner Classes | `LinkedList.Node` (each list class needs a node — kept inside), Android `OnClickListener` (anonymous), Swing event adapters |
| Static Nested Class | `Builder pattern` (e.g., `User.Builder`), `Map.Entry` |
| Local Inner Class | Stream collectors, temporary strategy implementations |
| Anonymous Inner Class | Quick UI handlers, comparator overrides, pre-lambda callbacks |
| `super` keyword | Framework extension (e.g., overriding `init()` in Servlets and calling `super.init(config)`) |
| Constructor chaining | Ensuring all framework initialisation steps run (e.g., `HttpServlet → GenericServlet → Servlet`) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Programmatic Derivation — Member Inner Class

```java
class Outer {
    private int data = 10;
    
    class Inner {
        void show() {
            System.out.println("data = " + data); // direct access to private
        }
    }
}
```

**Analytical Step-by-Step:**

- **Step 1:** Outer class `Outer` is defined with a `private int data = 10`.
- **Step 2:** A non-static inner class `Inner` is declared as a member of `Outer`.
- **Step 3:** Inside `Inner.show()`, the field `data` is referenced **without any qualifier**. The compiler resolves `data` to `Outer.this.data` automatically because the inner class holds an **implicit reference** to the enclosing outer instance.
- **Step 4:** To instantiate, the calling code **must** first create an `Outer` object, then use that object's reference to instantiate the inner class.

**Driver Code:**
```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Outer outer = new Outer();
        Outer.Inner inner = outer.new Inner();
        inner.show();
    }
}
```

**Compilation Trace (Mental Model):**
```
Step 1:  new Outer()           → outer object created, .this = outer
Step 2:  outer.new Inner()     → Inner object created, holds reference to outer
Step 3:  inner.show()          → Inner.this refers to inner
                                  → data resolves to Outer.this.data = 10
Step 4:  Output:  data = 10
```

---

## 3.2 Programmatic Derivation — Static Nested Class

```java
class Outer {
    static int staticData = 50;
    int instanceData = 60;
    
    static class StaticNested {
        void display() {
            System.out.println("staticData = " + staticData);    // ✅ allowed
            // System.out.println(instanceData);                 // ❌ compile error
        }
    }
}
```

**Analytical Step-by-Step:**

- **Step 1:** `StaticNested` is marked `static`, so it is **not tied to any `Outer` instance**.
- **Step 2:** It can access only the **static members** of `Outer` directly (here, `staticData`).
- **Step 3:** Trying to access the non-static `instanceData` results in a **compile-time error** because there is no enclosing `Outer.this` reference.
- **Step 4:** Instantiation does **not** require an `Outer` object first.

**Driver Code:**
```java
Outer.StaticNested obj = new Outer.StaticNested();
obj.display();   // Output: staticData = 50
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick (KTU):** Static = class-level, so it forgets the instance — like a company policy poster that exists whether or not any specific employee is in the office.

---

## 3.3 Programmatic Derivation — Local Inner Class

```java
class Outer {
    void outerMethod() {
        final int x = 100;     // effectively final
        
        class Local {           // local inner class inside the method
            void print() {
                System.out.println("x = " + x);  // captures local variable
            }
        }
        
        Local obj = new Local();
        obj.print();
    }
}
```

**Analytical Step-by-Step:**

- **Step 1:** A method `outerMethod()` is defined.
- **Step 2:** A local variable `final int x = 100` is declared inside the method.
- **Step 3:** A class `Local` is declared **within the method body** — its scope is the entire method.
- **Step 4:** `Local` is instantiated and used **inside the same method**.
- **Step 5:** `x` must be `final` or **effectively final** so that the inner class can safely capture its value (a copy is stored inside the inner-class object — KTU's "local variable capture" rule).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `final`?** Because the local variable lives on the method's stack frame, which is destroyed when the method returns. The inner-class object may outlive the method (if returned, for example), so Java stores a **copy** in the inner object. Marking the variable `final` ensures the value cannot change, keeping the original and the copy in sync.

---

## 3.4 Programmatic Derivation — Anonymous Inner Class

```java
abstract class Animal {
    abstract void sound();
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal a = new Animal() {          // anonymous subclass
            void sound() {
                System.out.println("Woof!");
            }
        };
        a.sound();                          // Output: Woof!
    }
}
```

**Analytical Step-by-Step:**

- **Step 1:** Abstract class `Animal` is defined with one abstract method `sound()`.
- **Step 2:** Inside `main`, the expression `new Animal() { ... }` declares an **anonymous (nameless) subclass** of `Animal` in a single statement.
- **Step 3:** The anonymous class provides the body of the `sound()` method.
- **Step 4:** The reference is assigned to a variable of type `Animal` (upcasting). The actual object's class is generated by the compiler as `Main$1.class` (numbered automatically).
- **Step 5:** Calling `a.sound()` invokes the **overridden** version in the anonymous class.

---

## 3.5 Programmatic Derivation — Inheritance & `super`

### Example: Super Class Field Access

```java
class SuperClass {
    String name = "Parent";
}

class SubClass extends SuperClass {
    String name = "Child";
    
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Child name   = " + name);          // Child
        System.out.println("Parent name  = " + super.name);    // Parent
    }
}
```

**Analytical Trace:**
- The local variable `name` (from `SubClass`) **shadows** the inherited `name`.
- `name` inside `display()` resolves to the **sub-class field** (Child).
- `super.name` explicitly resolves to the **super-class field** (Parent).

---

### Example: Super Class Method Override + `super.method()`

```java
class Vehicle {
    void start() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle started");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void start() {
        super.start();                                 // call parent
        System.out.println("Car started");
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Car().start();
    }
}
```

**Execution Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \texttt{new Car()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Vehicle constructor runs (implicit super())} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \texttt{new Car()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Car constructor runs} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \texttt{.start()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{dispatches to Car.start()} \\
\text{Step 4: } & \texttt{super.start()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{invokes Vehicle.start()} \\
                 & \text{Prints: Vehicle started} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{returns to Car.start() and prints: Car started}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output:**
```
Vehicle started
Car started
```

---

### Example: Constructor Chaining with `super()`

```java
class A {
    A() { System.out.println("A()"); }
}

class B extends A {
    B() {
        super();                       // explicit but optional
        System.out.println("B()");
    }
}

class C extends B {
    C() {
        System.out.println("C()");     // implicit super() inserted by compiler
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new C();
    }
}
```

**Order of Execution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{new C()} \;\Rightarrow\; & \texttt{C()} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{super()} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{B()} \\
                                  & \rightarrow\; \texttt{super()} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{A()} \\
                                  & \rightarrow\; \text{print A()} \\
                                  & \rightarrow\; \text{print B()} \\
                                  & \rightarrow\; \text{print C()}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output:**
```
A()
B()
C()
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Top Exam Trap:** Even though `C()` does **not** explicitly write `super()`, the compiler **automatically inserts** a call to `B()` (the no-arg super constructor) as the **first statement**. Students often wrongly believe no super call happens — it always does.

---

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix — All Four Inner Class Types

| Property | Member Inner | Static Nested | Local Inner | Anonymous |
|----------|--------------|---------------|-------------|-----------|
| Declaration site | Member of outer class | Member of outer class (static) | Inside a method/block | Inside an expression |
| Outer instance required? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (in scope) | ✅ Yes (in scope) |
| Access to outer `private` members? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (only `static`) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Can declare `static` members? | ❌ No (except `final`) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Access modifier allowed? | `public`/`private`/`protected`/package | Same as above | None (method-scoped) | None |
| Name on `.class` file? | `Outer$Inner.class` | `Outer$StaticNested.class` | `Outer$1$Local.class` | `Outer$1.class` |
| Typical use | Adapter, helper class | Builder, entry pair | Helper within method | Event listener, callback |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inner Class Containment — Class-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Outer Class"]:::outer
    B["static field"]:::staticfield
    C["instance field"]:::instfield
    D["Member Inner Class"]:::memberinner
    E["Static Nested Class"]:::staticnested
    F["method outerMethod"]:::method
    G["Local Inner Class"]:::localinner
    H["Anonymous Inner Class"]:::anoninner

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F
    F --> G
    A -.-> H

    classDef outer fill:#1565C0,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef staticfield fill:#43A047,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef instfield fill:#FB8C00,stroke:#E65100,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef memberinner fill:#8E24AA,stroke:#4A148C,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef staticnested fill:#3949AB,stroke:#1A237E,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef method fill:#00897B,stroke:#004D40,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef localinner fill:#D81B60,stroke:#880E4F,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef anoninner fill:#5E35B1,stroke:#311B92,stroke-dasharray:5 5,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Outer Class** is the root container (blue).
- It contains **fields** (green/orange) and the **Member Inner** + **Static Nested** classes.
- The **method** (teal) has a dashed edge to the **Local Inner Class** showing method-scope containment.
- The **Anonymous Inner Class** is shown with a dashed edge because it has no name and is created inline.

---

## 4.2 Inheritance & `super` — Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    O["Object"]:::obj
    S["SuperClass A"]:::super
    M["SubClass B extends A"]:::middle
    C["SubSubClass C extends B"]:::child

    O --> S
    S --> M
    M --> C

    A1["super reference in B → A"]:::ref1
    A2["super reference in C → B"]:::ref2
    A3["C implicitly constructs B, A, Object"]:::ref3

    M -.-> A1
    C -.-> A2
    C -.-> A3

    classDef obj fill:#37474F,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef super fill:#1565C0,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef middle fill:#2E7D32,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef child fill:#EF6C00,stroke:#BF360C,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef ref1 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000000
    classDef ref2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00,color:#000000
    classDef ref3 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The hierarchy is **top-down**: `Object → A → B → C`.
- Every arrow `→` represents an `extends` (inheritance) relationship.
- The dotted annotations show the **direction of the `super` reference**: from a sub-class, `super` points to its **immediate** parent only (not the grandparent).
- Constructor calls ripple **top-down** when an object is created.

---

## 4.3 Constructor Execution Sequence — Block Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller as main()
    participant C as C()
    participant B as B()
    participant A as A()
    participant O as Object()

    Caller->>C: new C()
    activate C
    C->>B: super() (implicit)
    activate B
    B->>A: super() (implicit)
    activate A
    A->>O: super() (implicit)
    activate O
    O-->>A: Object initialised
    deactivate O
    A-->>B: A() complete
    deactivate A
    B-->>C: B() complete
    deactivate B
    C-->>Caller: C() complete
    deactivate C
```

**Explanation:**
- The sequence shows **bottom-up** message sending (each sub-class calls its parent's constructor) but **top-down** execution (the parent's body runs before the child's).
- `Object()` is always the **first** constructor to execute, even though it's the last to be explicitly called.

---

## 4.4 Inner Class Instantiation Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{"Is the inner class declared static?"}:::q
    Q1 -->|Yes| P1["Use: new Outer.Inner()"]:::p
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Is it an anonymous inner class?"}:::q
    Q2 -->|Yes| P2["Use: new ParentType() { ... }"]:::p
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Is it a local inner class?"}:::q
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3["Instantiate inside the same method/block"]:::p
    Q3 -->|No| P4["Use: outer.new Inner()"]:::p

    classDef q fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000000
    classDef p fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000000
```

**Reading the flowchart:**
- Decision diamond asks: is the inner class `static`?
- If **Yes** → instantiate as `new Outer.Inner()`.
- If **No** → ask further: is it anonymous? Local? Member?
- Member (non-static, non-anonymous, non-local) → use `outer.new Inner()`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is an inner class in Java? Mention its types.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **inner class** is a class declared as a member of another (outer) class. It logically groups helper classes that are only used in one place, increases encapsulation, and can access all members (including private) of the outer class.

The four types are:
1. **Member Inner Class** — non-static class at member level.
2. **Static Nested Class** — `static` class inside the outer class.
3. **Local Inner Class** — defined inside a method or block.
4. **Anonymous Inner Class** — nameless class declared and instantiated inline.

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark purpose + 1 mark listing types.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of the `super` keyword in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `super` keyword is a reference variable in Java that refers to the **immediate parent class object**. It serves three purposes:

1. **Calling the parent constructor:** `super()` or `super(args)` invokes the parent class constructor. If not written explicitly, the compiler automatically inserts a call to the no-argument `super()` as the **first line** of every sub-class constructor.
2. **Accessing parent's data members** when shadowed by sub-class fields — e.g., `super.name`.
3. **Invoking the parent's method** when overridden in the sub-class — e.g., `super.display()`.

`super` **cannot** be used inside a `static` method or block, as it is an instance reference.

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark three uses + 1 mark restriction.)*

---

## 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Inner Classes Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of inner classes in Java with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of an **anonymous inner class** to handle a button-click event. Explain how the anonymous inner class differs from a local inner class. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Types of Inner Classes in Java:**

**1. Member Inner Class**
A non-static class defined at the member level of the outer class.

```java
class Outer {
    private int x = 5;
    class Inner {
        void show() {
            System.out.println("x = " + x);  // direct access to private
        }
    }
}
```

*Instantiation:* `Outer o = new Outer(); Outer.Inner i = o.new Inner();`

**2. Static Nested Class**
A class declared with the `static` keyword inside the outer class.

```java
class Outer {
    static class Nested {
        void display() { System.out.println("Static Nested"); }
    }
}
```

*Instantiation:* `Outer.Nested n = new Outer.Nested();` — no outer object needed.

**3. Local Inner Class**
A class defined inside a method, constructor, or block. Its scope is restricted to the enclosing block.

```java
void myMethod() {
    class Local {
        void print() { System.out.println("Local inner"); }
    }
    new Local().print();
}
```

**4. Anonymous Inner Class**
A nameless class declared and instantiated in a single expression, used to override methods of a class or interface on the fly.

```java
Runnable r = new Runnable() {
    public void run() { System.out.println("Anonymous run"); }
};
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Java Program: Anonymous Inner Class for Button Click**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class AnonymousDemo extends Frame {
    AnonymousDemo() {
        Button b = new Button("Click Me");
        b.setBounds(50, 80, 80, 30);
        
        // Anonymous inner class implementing ActionListener
        b.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Button clicked!");
            }
        });
        
        add(b);
        setSize(200, 150);
        setLayout(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new AnonymousDemo();
    }
}
```

**Differences: Anonymous Inner Class vs Local Inner Class**

| Feature | Local Inner Class | Anonymous Inner Class |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Class name | Has a defined name (e.g., `Local`) | No class name |
| Declaration | Declared as a separate statement inside a method | Declared and instantiated in a single expression |
| Reusability | Can be instantiated multiple times | Single-use; cannot be re-instantiated |
| File generated | `Outer$1Local.class` | `Outer$1.class` (auto-numbered) |
| Constructor | Can define its own constructor | Cannot define a constructor (no name to give) |
| Best for | Repeated helper logic in one method | One-off overrides (event handlers, comparators) |

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Super Class / Inheritance Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** What is the `super` keyword in Java? Explain its uses with examples. Discuss the rules that must be followed while using `super()`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates **multilevel inheritance** and the order in which constructors are executed. Also explain **constructor chaining** with the help of this program. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:**
The `super` keyword in Java is a **reference variable** that refers to the **immediate parent class object**. It allows a sub-class to access members (fields, methods, constructors) of its direct super class.

**Three Uses of `super`:**

**Use 1 — Calling the parent constructor:**
```java
class Animal {
    Animal(String name) { System.out.println("Animal: " + name); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    Dog() {
        super("Bruno");         // explicit super call
        System.out.println("Dog created");
    }
}
```

**Use 2 — Accessing parent's data members (resolving shadowing):**
```java
class A { int x = 10; }
class B extends A {
    int x = 20;
    void show() {
        System.out.println("B.x = " + x);       // 20
        System.out.println("A.x = " + super.x); // 10
    }
}
```

**Use 3 — Calling overridden parent method:**
```java
class Vehicle {
    void start() { System.out.println("Vehicle start"); }
}
class Car extends Vehicle {
    void start() {
        super.start();                            // calls Vehicle's start
        System.out.println("Car start");
    }
}
```

**Rules for `super()`:**

1. `super()` or `super(args)` **must be the first statement** in the sub-class constructor.
2. If not written explicitly, the compiler **auto-inserts** a no-argument `super()` call.
3. If the parent class **does not have a no-arg constructor**, the sub-class **must** explicitly call `super(args)` — otherwise, **compile-time error**.
4. `super` **cannot be used in a `static` method or static block** because it refers to an instance.
5. `super` refers only to the **immediate parent**, not to grandparents directly.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Java Program — Multilevel Inheritance with Constructor Order**

```java
class Grandparent {
    Grandparent() {
        System.out.println("Grandparent constructor");
    }
}

class Parent extends Grandparent {
    Parent() {
        System.out.println("Parent constructor");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    Child() {
        System.out.println("Child constructor");
    }
}

public class InheritanceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Child c = new Child();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Grandparent constructor
Parent constructor
Child constructor
```

**Explanation — Constructor Chaining:**

When `new Child()` is executed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \texttt{Child()} \text{ begins; compiler inserts } \texttt{super()} \text{ as first line} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \texttt{Parent()} \text{ begins; compiler inserts } \texttt{super()} \text{ as first line} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \texttt{Grandparent()} \text{ begins; } \texttt{super()} \text{ is the implicit } \texttt{Object()} \\
\text{Step 4: } & \texttt{Object()} \text{ runs} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \texttt{Grandparent()} \text{ body executes} \\
\text{Step 6: } & \texttt{Parent()} \text{ body executes} \\
\text{Step 7: } & \texttt{Child()} \text{ body executes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Constructor Chaining** is the mechanism by which a constructor call **propagates up the inheritance hierarchy** so that every ancestor's initialisation logic is executed before the sub-class body runs. This guarantees that an object is **fully initialised from root to leaf** before being used.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Loses 2–3 Marks Frequently):**
> 1. Students often **omit the implicit `super()` call** when explaining constructor chaining, claiming "Parent constructor runs first because of inheritance." The correct wording is: *"The compiler **automatically inserts** a no-argument `super()` call as the first statement of every constructor."*
> 2. In inner-class questions, students forget that **member inner classes cannot have `static` members** (except `static final` constants) — instant marks lost.
> 3. Confusing `this()` and `super()`: `this()` calls **another constructor of the same class**, while `super()` calls the **parent's constructor**. Both must be the first line — only **one** can appear in a constructor.
> 4. Writing `super.name` to access a parent's **private** field from outside the parent class → **compile error**. Use `protected` or `public` modifiers if direct sub-class access is needed.
> 5. Anonymous inner classes **cannot have constructors** — students sometimes write a constructor block and lose the mark.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Inner Classes & Super Class**

- **Inner Class Definition:** A class declared inside another class. Increases encapsulation and logically groups helper classes.
- **Four Types:** Member Inner (object-bound), Static Nested (class-bound), Local (block-bound), Anonymous (one-shot, nameless).
- **Member Inner Instantiation:** `outerObj.new Inner()` — outer object is **mandatory**.
- **Static Nested Instantiation:** `new Outer.Nested()` — outer object is **not required**; only static outer members are accessible.
- **Local Inner Class:** Scoped to the method/block; can access **effectively final** local variables.
- **Anonymous Inner Class:** Declared and instantiated in `new ParentType() { ... };` — used heavily in event handling and pre-lambda callbacks.
- **Inheritance:** Sub-class inherits from super class using the `extends` keyword in Java.
- **`super` keyword:** Reference to **immediate parent class object** — three uses: constructor call, field access, method call.
- **`super()` rule:** Must be the **first statement** in a sub-class constructor; if omitted, the compiler auto-inserts a no-arg call.
- **Constructor Chaining Order:** `Object → Super → Sub → SubSub` (root to leaf, top-down execution).
- **Restrictions on `super`:** Cannot be used in `static` context; refers to immediate parent only; cannot access parent's `private` fields from outside.
- **Static Members in Inner Classes:** Only allowed in `static` nested classes (and as `static final` constants in member inner classes).
- **File Naming Convention:** Outer$Inner.class, Outer$1.class (anonymous numbered).
- **KTU Memory Hooks:** *"outer.new"* for member, *"static = class-level"* for static nested, *"super is always first"* for constructor call rule, *"root to leaf"* for chaining order.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sub Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sub Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Sub Class** (also referred to as a **Child Class**, **Derived Class**, or **Extended Class**) is a class in Object-Oriented Programming that inherits attributes (data members) and behaviors (member methods) from another class, known as the **Super Class** (Parent Class / Base Class). This mechanism of creating a new class from an existing one is formally termed **Inheritance**, and it is one of the four foundational pillars of OOP, alongside **Encapsulation**, **Abstraction**, and **Polymorphism**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, inheritance is expressed using the `extends` keyword in Java and the `:` (colon) operator in C++, with the subclass leveraging the `super` reference to access non-private members of its parent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"A subclass is a specialized class that inherits the properties and methods of a generalized superclass, enabling code reuse, hierarchical classification, and polymorphic behavior."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Family Tree**.

- The **Super Class** is the *Parent* — they have certain traits like eye color, a surname, and a family house.
- The **Sub Class** is the *Child* — they automatically *inherit* the surname and the house (without having to recreate them), but they can also have their own unique traits (their own profession, hobbies, etc.).

> Real-world example: Think of a **Vehicle** (Super Class) — it has wheels, an engine, and a method to start. A **Car** (Sub Class) *is a* Vehicle, so it inherits wheels and engine, but it *adds* features like `airConditioner` and `musicSystem`. This "is-a" relationship is the essence of subclassing.

### Key Terminology Mapping (KTU Standard)

| Term | Also Known As | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Super Class | Parent / Base Class | Provides inherited members |
| Sub Class | Child / Derived Class | Inherits and may extend functionality |
| `extends` (Java) / `:` (C++) | Inheritance Keyword | Establishes the relationship |
| `super` | Parent Reference | Accesses parent constructors/members |
| Reusability | Code Inheritance | The principal benefit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 2 specifically focuses on class derivation, member access, method overriding, constructor chaining, and the use of the `super` keyword — all of which revolve around the Sub Class concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Structured Logical Breakdown of Sub Class Creation

**Step 1: Declaration of the Super Class**
A class is designed as a super class by making its members either `public`, `protected`, or *package-private* (default). Private members are **NOT** inherited, although they remain accessible indirectly through public/protected accessor methods.

**Step 2: Declaration of the Sub Class**
Using the inheritance keyword, the sub class is declared. The general syntax in Java is:

```java
class SubClassName extends SuperClassName {
    // additional fields
    // additional or overridden methods
}
```

**Step 3: Member Inheritance**
The sub class automatically receives:
- All `public` and `protected` members of the super class.
- *Package-private* members (if both classes are in the same package).
- Private members are **inaccessible** directly but exist in memory.

**Step 4: Adding Specialization**
The sub class can:
- **Add** new fields and methods.
- **Override** existing methods (runtime polymorphism).
- **Hide** static methods or fields (static polymorphism).
- **Invoke** the super class constructor using `super(args)`.

**Step 5: Constructor Chaining**
When an object of a sub class is created, the constructor of the super class is invoked **first** (either implicitly or explicitly), followed by the sub class constructor. This ensures the parent portion of the object is properly initialized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** If the super class has a *parameterized* constructor and **no default constructor**, then the sub class constructor **MUST** explicitly call `super(arguments)` as its first statement; otherwise, a compile-time error occurs.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | Access Modifier | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inheritance Keyword (Java) | `class B extends A` | N/A | Default access in same package |
| Inheritance Keyword (C++) | `class B : public A` | N/A | Note the visibility mode |
| Accessing Parent Members | `super.memberName` | All non-private | Cannot be used in `static` context |
| Calling Parent Constructor | `super(arg1, arg2)` | All | Must be the *first* line |
| Method Overriding | Same signature in subclass | `@Override` annotation | Runtime polymorphism |
| Method Hiding | Same static method in subclass | `static` keyword | Compile-time polymorphism |
| `final` class (cannot extend) | `final class A` | N/A | Prevents inheritance |
| `final` method (cannot override) | `final void show()` | N/A | Prevents overriding |
| Single Inheritance | One parent class | Java supports | Multiple inheritance via interfaces |
| Multilevel Inheritance | `C extends B, B extends A` | Transitive | Grandparent members accessible |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

In large-scale software engineering (e.g., banking systems, embedded controllers, Android app development), subclasses enable:
- **Framework extensibility:** Android's `Activity`, `AppCompatActivity`, `FragmentActivity` chain.
- **GUI design:** `JFrame` → `MyWindow` custom subclass.
- **Game development:** `Sprite` (super) → `Player`, `Enemy` (sub classes).
- **Database systems:** Hibernate entity inheritance hierarchies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Exhaustive Java Program: Sub Class Demonstration

Below is a complete, executable Java program illustrating subclass creation, inheritance, method overriding, and `super` usage. Every logical step is annotated for KTU board-level explanation.

```java
// Step 1: Define the Super Class
class Animal {
    String name;
    int age;

    // Parameterized constructor of super class
    Animal(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age = age;
        System.out.println("Animal constructor invoked");
    }

    // Method to be inherited
    void speak() {
        System.out.println(name + " makes a generic sound.");
    }

    void displayInfo() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Age: " + age);
    }
}

// Step 2: Define the Sub Class using 'extends'
class Dog extends Animal {
    String breed;

    // Sub class constructor
    Dog(String name, int age, String breed) {
        // Step 3: Explicitly calling super class constructor
        super(name, age);
        this.breed = breed;
        System.out.println("Dog constructor invoked");
    }

    // Step 4: Overriding the speak() method
    @Override
    void speak() {
        super.speak();              // call parent version
        System.out.println(name + " barks: Woof! Woof!");
    }

    // Step 5: New method exclusive to sub class
    void fetch() {
        System.out.println(name + " is fetching the ball.");
    }
}

// Step 6: Driver class
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d = new Dog("Buddy", 5, "Labrador");
        d.displayInfo();
        d.speak();
        d.fetch();

        // Upcasting demonstration
        Animal a = new Dog("Max", 3, "Beagle");
        a.speak();   // Runtime polymorphism: Dog's overridden speak() executes
    }
}
```

### Expected Output Trace

```
Animal constructor invoked
Dog constructor invoked
Name: Buddy, Age: 5
Buddy makes a generic sound.
Buddy barks: Woof! Woof!
Buddy is fetching the ball.
Animal constructor invoked
Dog constructor invoked
Max makes a generic sound.
Max barks: Woof! Woof!
```

### Analytical Matrix: Access Control in Sub Classing

The table below is a **high-yield KTU table**. Memorize it thoroughly.

| Super Class Member | Same Package Sub Class | Different Package Sub Class |
|---|---|---|
| `public` | Accessible | Accessible |
| `protected` | Accessible | Accessible (via inheritance only) |
| *default* (no modifier) | Accessible | **Not Accessible** |
| `private` | **Not Accessible** directly | **Not Accessible** directly |

### Construction Order Derivation (Logical Proof)

Given a class hierarchy `A → B → C`, when `new C()` is executed:

$$
\text{Execution Order} = \text{A's constructor} \rightarrow \text{B's constructor} \rightarrow \text{C's constructor}
$$

This is because Java's Object class is the ultimate root, and constructor chaining follows the inheritance chain from root to leaf.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention that *every class in Java implicitly extends `java.lang.Object`* unless it explicitly extends another class. Hence the chain always terminates at `Object`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Inheritance Hierarchy (Class Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    objectA["java lang Object"] --> animalA["Animal - Super Class"]
    animalA --> dogA["Dog - Sub Class"]
    animalA --> catA["Cat - Sub Class"]
    dogA --> pupA["Puppy - Sub Sub Class"]
    catA --> lionA["Lion - Sub Sub Class"]
```

### Diagram 2: Memory Layout of a Sub Class Object

```mermaid
graph TD
    objMem["Dog Object in Heap Memory"]
    objMem --> superPart["Super Class Portion - Animal"]
    superPart --> f1["name : String"]
    superPart --> f2["age : int"]
    objMem --> subPart["Sub Class Portion - Dog"]
    subPart --> f3["breed : String"]
    subPart --> m1["speak - Overridden Method"]
    subPart --> m2["fetch - New Method"]
```

### Diagram 3: Constructor Chaining Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNew["new Dog - Invocation"] --> callSuper1["Implicit super call to Animal"]
    callSuper1 --> callSuper2["Implicit super call to Object"]
    callSuper2 --> execObj["Object constructor executes"]
    execObj --> execAnim["Animal constructor executes"]
    execAnim --> execDog["Dog constructor executes"]
    execDog --> returnObj["Fully initialized Dog object returned"]
```

### Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture (UML-Style)

```mermaid
graph LR
    superCls["SUPER CLASS Animal"]
    subCls["SUB CLASS Dog"]
    driver["DRIVER Main"]

    superCls -->|inherits fields and methods| subCls
    subCls -->|invokes via super| superCls
    driver -->|creates instance| subCls
    driver -->|upcast reference| superCls
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs use the `camelCase` alphanumeric prefix pattern (`objectA`, `superPart`) as mandated by the KTU diagram safety protocol — no reserved keywords are used as standalone node labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*Define a subclass. How is it created in Java? Write the general syntax.*

**Model Answer:**
A subclass is a class that inherits the properties and methods of an existing class, known as the superclass. It is created in Java using the `extends` keyword.

```java
class SubClassName extends SuperClassName {
    // body
}
```

The subclass acquires all non-private members of the superclass and may add new members or override existing ones to provide specialized behavior.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
*What is the role of the `super` keyword in Java? Mention any two uses.*

**Model Answer:**
The `super` keyword in Java refers to the immediate parent class object. Its two primary uses are:
1. `super(args)` — to explicitly invoke a parameterized constructor of the parent class as the first statement of the sub class constructor.
2. `super.memberName` — to access an overridden or hidden member (field or method) of the parent class from within the sub class.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

(a) Explain the concept of inheritance in Java with a suitable example. Discuss how private members of a super class behave in a subclass. **(7 Marks)**

(b) Write a Java program to create a super class `Shape` with a method `area()`. Create two subclasses `Circle` and `Rectangle` that override the `area()` method. Demonstrate polymorphism using an array of `Shape` references. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition of Inheritance:** Inheritance is the OOP mechanism by which one class (subclass) acquires the properties and behaviors of another class (superclass). It establishes an "is-a" relationship and promotes code reusability.

**Example:**
```java
class Vehicle {
    int wheels;
    void start() { System.out.println("Vehicle starts"); }
}
class Car extends Vehicle {
    int airbags;
    void honk() { System.out.println("Car honks"); }
}
```

**Behavior of Private Members in a Subclass:**
- Private members of a super class are **not directly accessible** in the subclass.
- However, they **do exist** in the memory of the subclass object (they occupy space in the heap allocation).
- They can be accessed *indirectly* via public or protected getter/setter methods of the super class.
- For example, if `Vehicle` has `private int modelNumber;`, the subclass `Car` cannot write `this.modelNumber` directly, but it can call `getModelNumber()` if defined as `public` in `Vehicle`.

**Why this design?** Encapsulation principle: hiding internal data while exposing controlled access.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

```java
class Shape {
    public void area() {
        System.out.println("Area of shape is undefined");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius;
    Circle(double r) { this.radius = r; }
    @Override
    public void area() {
        System.out.println("Circle area = " + (3.14159 * radius * radius));
    }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    double length, breadth;
    Rectangle(double l, double b) { this.length = l; this.breadth = b; }
    @Override
    public void area() {
        System.out.println("Rectangle area = " + (length * breadth));
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape[] shapes = new Shape[3];
        shapes[0] = new Circle(5.0);
        shapes[1] = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0);
        shapes[2] = new Circle(2.5);

        for (Shape s : shapes) {
            s.area();   // Dynamic method dispatch
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Circle area = 78.53975
Rectangle area = 24.0
Circle area = 19.6349375
```

**Explanation of Polymorphism:** Although the array type is `Shape[]`, the JVM dynamically binds the call to the actual object's overridden method at runtime. This is **dynamic method dispatch** — a cornerstone of runtime polymorphism in Java.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

(a) Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding in the context of subclassing. Provide one example of each. **(7 Marks)**

(b) Explain the rules for method overriding in Java. What happens if the subclass changes the access modifier of an overridden method? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

| Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Same method name, different parameters, in the same class | Same method name AND signature, in super and sub class |
| Polymorphism type | Compile-time (static) | Runtime (dynamic) |
| Return type | May differ | Must be same or covariant |
| Access modifier | No restriction | Cannot be more restrictive |
| Occurs in | Single class (or subclass adding overload) | Two classes in inheritance hierarchy |
| `static` methods | Can be overloaded | Can only be hidden, not overridden |

**Overloading Example:**
```java
class Calculator {
    int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
    double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; }
}
```

**Overriding Example:**
```java
class Parent {
    void show() { System.out.println("Parent"); }
}
class Child extends Parent {
    @Override
    void show() { System.out.println("Child"); }
}
```

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Rules for Method Overriding in Java:**

1. The method in the subclass must have the **same name** and **same parameter list** as in the super class.
2. The return type must be the **same or a covariant subtype** (Java 5+).
3. The access level **cannot be more restrictive** than the parent's method (e.g., `protected` in parent → `public` in child is allowed, but `public` in parent → `private` in child is **not allowed**).
4. **Instance methods** can be overridden; `static`, `final`, and `private` methods cannot be overridden (they can be hidden or redefined).
5. Overriding methods **cannot throw new or broader checked exceptions** than those declared in the parent method.
6. The `@Override` annotation is recommended for compile-time verification.

**What happens if access modifier is changed?**
- If the subclass makes the access **broader** (e.g., `protected` → `public`): **Allowed** and often used to widen API access.
- If the subclass makes the access **narrower** (e.g., `public` → `protected` or `private`): **Compile-time error** — `attempting to assign weaker access privileges`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students commonly lose **2 to 3 marks** in the following ways:
> 1. **Confusing method hiding with method overriding** for `static` methods.
> 2. Forgetting that `private` methods of the super class are NOT overridden (they are simply redefined).
> 3. Writing `super()` as the second or third line in a constructor — it **must** be the first statement.
> 4. Stating that the sub class constructor is called *before* the super class constructor (the reverse is true).
> 5. Believing that private members of the super class are *not allocated* in the sub class object — they **are** allocated, but not directly accessible.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Sub Class** inherits from exactly **one** super class in Java (single inheritance only).
- Use `extends` (Java) or `:` (C++) to declare inheritance.
- The `super` keyword has **three uses**: calling parent constructor, accessing parent fields, and accessing parent methods.
- **Private members** of a super class exist in memory but are inaccessible directly in the sub class.
- **Method overriding** requires identical signature and is the basis of **runtime polymorphism**.
- **Constructor chaining** runs from the topmost (root) class down to the leaf class.
- A `final` class **cannot** be extended; a `final` method **cannot** be overridden.
- **Upcasting** (sub class reference to super class variable) is implicit and safe.
- **Downcasting** requires explicit cast and may throw `ClassCastException` if invalid.
- The `@Override` annotation is **best practice** and catches signature mismatches at compile time.
- Every Java class, if not explicitly extended, implicitly extends `java.lang.Object`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Inheritance
topics.content:

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Inheritance** is a fundamental Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) mechanism that allows a new class (called the **derived class**, **subclass**, or **child class**) to be created from an existing class (called the **base class**, **superclass**, or **parent class**). The derived class automatically inherits the data members (attributes) and member functions (methods) of the base class, and can additionally introduce its own specialized members or override inherited ones.

**Types of Inheritance** refer to the various structural/architectural ways in which the inheritance relationship between classes can be organized within an OOP class hierarchy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The module emphasizes the *taxonomy* of inheritance structures (Single, Multiple, Multilevel, Hierarchical, Hybrid, Multipath) and their implementation consequences such as **ambiguity (Diamond Problem)**, **constructor invocation order**, and **accessibility of inherited members**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of inheritance like a **family tree or biological genetics**:

- A **Parent** (Base Class) passes down traits (attributes like eye color) and behaviors (methods like speaking) to a **Child** (Derived Class).
- The **Child** not only receives all the parent's traits but can also develop new, unique traits of their own (e.g., a skill the parent didn't have).
- In a family, **one child** can have **one parent** (Single), or inherit from **both parents** (Multiple). A **grandchild** inherits from the grandparent via the parent (Multilevel), and **siblings** share the same parent (Hierarchical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Vehicle Hierarchy:**
> Consider a base class `Vehicle` (with attributes: `speed`, `fuel`) → derived class `Car` (adds `numDoors`) → further derived class `ElectricCar` (adds `batteryCapacity`). This is **Multilevel Inheritance**, and it mirrors how automobile classification works in the real world.

### 1.3 Core Vocabulary (KTU Board-Standard Terms)

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Base Class** | The class whose properties are inherited (also called *Parent* / *Super* class) |
| **Derived Class** | The class that inherits properties (also called *Child* / *Sub* class) |
| **Access Specifier** | Controls visibility of inherited members: `public`, `protected`, `private` |
| **Method Overriding** | Redefining a base class method in the derived class with the same signature |
| **UML Class Diagram** | Standardized notation for visualizing inheritance (arrow with hollow triangle) |
| **IS-A Relationship** | Inheritance represents an *"is-a"* relationship (e.g., Dog *is-a* Animal) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used:** Inheritance Depth (number of levels), Inheritance Fan-out (number of direct children), and the **Diamond Pattern** (a class inheriting from two classes that share a common ancestor).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Comprehensive Taxonomy of Inheritance Types

#### **Type 1: Single Inheritance**

A derived class inherits from **exactly one** base class. This is the simplest and most common form.

- **Why?** Establishes a direct, unambiguous parent-child relationship.
- **How?** The derived class extends the base by adding new members or overriding existing ones.
- **Structure:** `Derived Class  ──►  Base Class`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Base[Base Class]
    Derived[Derived Class]
    Derived --> Base
```

#### **Type 2: Multiple Inheritance**

A derived class inherits from **two or more** base classes simultaneously. The derived class combines the features of all its parents.

- **Why?** Models real-world entities that exhibit characteristics of multiple categories (e.g., a `Smartphone` is both a `Phone` and a `Computer`).
- **How?** The derived class lists multiple base classes in its declaration.
- ⚠️ **KTU Pitfall:** Not supported directly by Java (uses *interfaces* instead), but fully supported in **C++**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Base Class 1]
    B2[Base Class 2]
    D[Derived Class]
    D --> B1
    D --> B2
```

#### **Type 3: Multilevel Inheritance**

A chain of inheritance where a class is derived from another derived class, forming a multi-tier hierarchy.

- **Why?** Captures progressive specialization (e.g., `Vehicle` → `Car` → `ElectricCar`).
- **How?** Each level adds refinement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Grandparent Class]
    B[Parent Class]
    C[Child Class]
    B --> A
    C --> B
```

#### **Type 4: Hierarchical Inheritance**

A **single base class** is inherited by **multiple derived classes**. All children share the same parent.

- **Why?** Allows code reuse for common features shared across sibling categories.
- **How?** Multiple derived classes point to one base class.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B[Base Class]
    D1[Derived 1]
    D2[Derived 2]
    D3[Derived 3]
    D1 --> B
    D2 --> B
    D3 --> B
```

#### **Type 5: Hybrid (Virtual) Inheritance**

A combination of **two or more** types of inheritance (e.g., Hierarchical + Multiple). Used to model complex real-world hierarchies.

- **Why?** Real systems are not constrained to a single inheritance pattern.
- **How?** Mixes multiple patterns in one design.
- ⚠️ **KTU Critical:** Often introduces the **Diamond Problem**, which requires **virtual inheritance** in C++ to resolve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Class]
    B1[Intermediate 1]
    B2[Intermediate 2]
    C[Final Derived]
    B1 --> A
    B2 --> A
    C --> B1
    C --> B2
```

#### **Type 6: Multipath Inheritance**

A special case of Hybrid inheritance where a class inherits from two classes that share a common ancestor via *separate paths*. This is precisely the **Diamond Problem** scenario.

- **Why?** Models entities with multiple inheritance paths from a shared root.
- **How?** Requires **virtual base class** declaration to avoid duplication of the grand-parent's members.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** The *Diamond Problem* and its resolution through **virtual inheritance** is a **favourite KTU board question topic** (asked multiple times in Dec 2022, May 2023, and Dec 2023 University Exams).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### **Table A: Access Specifier Behavior in Inheritance**

| Member Access in Base | Public Inheritance | Protected Inheritance | Private Inheritance |
|----------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|---------------------|
| `public` member | Becomes `public` in derived | Becomes `protected` in derived | Becomes `private` in derived |
| `protected` member | Becomes `protected` in derived | Becomes `protected` in derived | Becomes `private` in derived |
| `private` member | **Not directly accessible** | **Not directly accessible** | **Not directly accessible** |

#### **Table B: Inheritance Type Summary Cheat Sheet**

| Inheritance Type | Number of Base Classes | Number of Derived Classes | C++ Support | Java Support |
|-----------------|----------------------|--------------------------|-------------|--------------|
| Single | 1 | 1 | ✅ | ✅ |
| Multiple | $\geq 2$ | 1 | ✅ | ❌ (uses interfaces) |
| Multilevel | 1 (chain) | 1 (chain) | ✅ | ✅ |
| Hierarchical | 1 | $\geq 2$ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Hybrid | Mixed | Mixed | ✅ | Partial |
| Multipath | $\geq 2$ (via common ancestor) | 1 | ✅ (with `virtual`) | ❌ |

#### **Table C: Constructor & Destructor Invocation Order**

| Order | Event in C++ |
|-------|--------------|
| 1 | Base class constructor executes first |
| 2 | Derived class constructor executes second |
| 3 | Derived class destructor executes first (LIFO — Last In, First Out) |
| 4 | Base class destructor executes last |

> [!NOTE]
> **General Rule:** Constructors run *top-down* (base → derived), while destructors run *bottom-up* (derived → base).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Single / Hierarchical:** Used in GUI frameworks (e.g., `QWidget` → `QPushButton`, `QLabel` in Qt).
- **Multiple / Hybrid:** Used in embedded systems and real-time OS device-driver architectures.
- **Multilevel:** Found in **Java Collections Framework** (`AbstractList` → `ArrayList`).
- **Virtual Inheritance:** Used in high-performance game engines to avoid data duplication in object hierarchies.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Diamond Problem and its Resolution

The **Diamond Problem** arises in Multipath/Hybrid inheritance. Let us derive why ambiguity occurs and how virtual inheritance solves it.

#### **Step 1: Define the Class Hierarchy**

Let `Class A` be the root (top-most) class. Two intermediate classes `B` and `C` inherit from `A`. A final class `D` inherits from both `B` and `C`.

$$
\text{Hierarchy: } A \;\rightarrow\; B \;\rightarrow\; D \quad \text{and} \quad A \;\rightarrow\; C \;\rightarrow\; D
$$

#### **Step 2: Identify the Issue — Duplication of A's Members**

Without virtual inheritance, class `D` contains **two copies** of `A`'s members — one inherited via `B` and another via `C`. The compiler cannot decide which copy to access.

$$
\text{Total copies of A in D} = \underbrace{1}_{\text{via B}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{via C}} = 2 \text{ copies}
$$

#### **Step 3: Memory Layout Representation**

The memory of object `D` would look like:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Object D memory layout (Non-Virtual):} \\
\text{[A's data (from B)][B's data][A's data (from C)][C's data][D's data]}
\end{aligned}
$$

This results in **ambiguity** when accessing `A`'s members through `D`.

#### **Step 4: Apply Virtual Inheritance**

Declare `A` as a **virtual base class** when deriving `B` and `C`:

```cpp
class B : virtual public A { };
class C : virtual public A { };
class D : public B, public C { };
```

#### **Step 5: Derive the Resolved Memory Layout**

With `virtual` inheritance, the compiler ensures that **only one shared copy** of `A` exists in the final derived class.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Object D memory layout (Virtual):} \\
\text{[A's data (SHARED)][B's data][C's data][D's data]}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 6: Resulting Equation**

$$
\text{Total copies of A in D (Virtual)} = \text{max}(1, \text{paths}) = 1 \text{ copy (shared)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Virtual inheritance solves the diamond problem by guaranteeing a **single instance** of the virtual base class sub-object, regardless of the number of inheritance paths leading to it.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Inheritance Pattern → Design Decision Mapping

The following matrix helps KTU students select the appropriate inheritance type based on the problem domain:

| Problem Domain | Recommended Inheritance Type | Reasoning |
|---------------|------------------------------|-----------|
| Modeling progressive specialization (Vehicle → Car → ElectricCar) | **Multilevel** | Captures tiered abstraction |
| Modeling common parent with variants (Shape → Circle, Rectangle) | **Hierarchical** | Shares base features across siblings |
| Modeling entity with multiple roles (Smartphone is a Phone + Computer) | **Multiple** (C++) / Interfaces (Java) | Combines orthogonal features |
| Avoiding ambiguity in diamond hierarchies | **Virtual Inheritance** | Eliminates duplicate base sub-objects |
| Simple IS-A relationship (Dog is Animal) | **Single** | Simplest and most direct relationship |

---

### 3.3 Constructor Invocation Order — Worked Derivation

Given the multilevel hierarchy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Class A (Base)} \\
\text{Class B : public A (Intermediate)} \\
\text{Class C : public B (Derived)}
\end{aligned}
$$

When an object of `C` is instantiated, the compiler follows this exact sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Object of C is requested} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Compiler invokes A's constructor first (top of hierarchy)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Compiler invokes B's constructor next} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Compiler invokes C's constructor last} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Object is fully constructed and returned}
\end{aligned}
$$

**General formula for $n$-level inheritance chain:**

$$
\text{Construction Order} = \{\text{Level } n,\; \text{Level } (n-1),\; \ldots,\; \text{Level } 1\}
$$

where Level 1 is the base class and Level $n$ is the most derived class. Destructor order is the **exact reverse**.

### 4.3 Diamond Problem Visualization (Multipath Inheritance)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    diamondA["CLASS A TOP BASE"]
    diamondB["CLASS B VIRTUAL PUBLIC A"]

    diamondD --> diamondB
    diamondD --> diamondC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows point *from derived to base* (as per UML convention). With `virtual` inheritance, the path through `B` and `C` shares a **single instance** of `A` inside `D`.

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Constructor Flow in Multilevel Inheritance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inst["INSTANTIATE OBJECT OF DERIVED CLASS C"] --> callA["CALL CONSTRUCTOR OF A BASE"]
    callA --> bodyA["EXECUTE A BODY INITIALIZE BASE DATA"]
    bodyA --> callB["CALL CONSTRUCTOR OF B INTERMEDIATE"]
    callB --> bodyB["EXECUTE B BODY INITIALIZE INTERMEDIATE DATA"]
    bodyB --> callC["CALL CONSTRUCTOR OF C DERIVED"]
    callC --> bodyC["EXECUTE C BODY INITIALIZE DERIVED DATA"]
    bodyC --> ready["OBJECT C READY FOR USE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flowchart:** The diagram maps the chronological sequence of constructor invocations from top-of-hierarchy (`A`) down to the most-derived class (`C`). Reverse order applies during destruction.

### 4.5 Hybrid Inheritance — A Real-World Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    emp["EMPLOYEE BASE CLASS"]
    ftemp["FULL TIME EMPLOYEE"]
    ptemp["PART TIME EMPLOYEE"]
    manager["MANAGER MULTIPLE INHERITS"]
    ftemp --> emp
    ptemp --> emp
    manager --> ftemp
    manager --> ptemp
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is a **Hybrid Inheritance** structure combining *Hierarchical* (Employee → FullTime / PartTime) and *Multiple* (Manager → FullTime + PartTime). It models how a `Manager` is a specialized type of `FullTime` employee that also has part-time responsibilities.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define inheritance. List any FOUR types of inheritance supported in C++.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Inheritance is an OOP mechanism by which a class (derived class) acquires the properties (data members) and behaviors (member functions) of another class (base class), enabling **code reuse** and establishing an *IS-A* relationship.

**Four Types of Inheritance (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Single Inheritance** — One derived class inherits from one base class.
2. **Multiple Inheritance** — One derived class inherits from two or more base classes.
3. **Multilevel Inheritance** — A class is derived from another derived class, forming a chain.
4. **Hierarchical Inheritance** — Multiple derived classes inherit from a single base class.

*(Alternative 5th type: Hybrid Inheritance — combination of two or more types.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**What is the Diamond Problem in inheritance? How is it resolved in C++?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Diamond Problem Definition (1 Mark):**
The Diamond Problem occurs in multipath inheritance when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from a common base class. This creates **ambiguity** because the derived class ends up with **two copies** of the common base class's members.

**Resolution in C++ (2 Marks):**
The problem is resolved using **Virtual Inheritance**. By declaring the intermediate classes to inherit *virtually* from the common base, the compiler ensures that **only one shared copy** of the base class exists in the final derived class.

**Syntax Example:**

```cpp
class B : virtual public A { };   // Virtual base
class C : virtual public A { };   // Virtual base
class D : public B, public C { }; // Only ONE A sub-object
```

---
