## **Question A (14 Marks) — Inheritance Types and Implementation**

#### **Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the different types of inheritance with neat diagrams. Write suitable C++ programs to demonstrate Single and Multiple Inheritance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Inheritance is the process by which a derived class acquires the properties and behaviors of a base class, supporting **code reusability** and **hierarchical classification** in OOP.

**Types of Inheritance with Diagrams (4 Marks — 1 each for 4 types):**

1. **Single Inheritance:** One base → One derived.
2. **Multiple Inheritance:** Multiple bases → One derived.
3. **Multilevel Inheritance:** Chain of derivation (Grandparent → Parent → Child).
4. **Hierarchical Inheritance:** One base → Multiple derived classes.

*(Diagrams should be drawn as per Section 4.2 of these notes.)*

**C++ Program — Single Inheritance (1 Mark):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Animal {                      // Base class
public:
    void eat() { cout << "Eating..." << endl; }
};

class Dog : public Animal {         // Derived class
public:
    void bark() { cout << "Barking..." << endl; }
};

int main() {
    Dog d;
    d.eat();    // Inherited from Animal
    d.bark();   // Defined in Dog
    return 0;
}
```

**C++ Program — Multiple Inheritance (1 Mark):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Camera {                      // Base class 1
public:
    void click() { cout << "Photo clicked" << endl; }
};

class Phone {                       // Base class 2
public:
    void call() { cout << "Calling..." << endl; }
};

class Smartphone : public Camera, public Phone {
    // Inherits from both Camera and Phone
};

int main() {
    Smartphone s;
    s.click();   // From Camera
    s.call();    // From Phone
    return 0;
}
```

---

#### **Q.A.(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the Diamond Problem? Explain with a neat diagram. Demonstrate how virtual inheritance resolves this ambiguity with a C++ program.**

**Model Answer:**

**Diamond Problem Definition (1 Mark):**
The Diamond Problem is an ambiguity that arises in **multipath inheritance** when a class inherits from two or more classes that share a common ancestor, resulting in **duplicate copies** of the ancestor's members in the derived class.

**Diagram (2 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classA["CLASS A COMMON ANCESTOR"]
    classB["CLASS B INHERITS A"]
    classC["CLASS C INHERITS A"]
    classD["CLASS D INHERITS B AND C AMBIGUITY HERE"]
    classB --> classA
    classC --> classA
    classD --> classB
    classD --> classC
```

**Why Ambiguity Occurs (1 Mark):**
Class `D` inherits `A` **twice** — once via `B` and once via `C`. When accessing an `A` member (e.g., `A::display()`), the compiler cannot determine **which path** to follow, causing a compilation error: *"request for member 'display' is ambiguous"*.

**Virtual Inheritance Resolution (2 Marks):**
By declaring `B` and `C` to inherit **virtually** from `A`, only **one shared instance** of `A` is created in `D`, eliminating ambiguity.

**C++ Program (1 Mark):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class A {
public:
    void show() { cout << "Class A method" << endl; }
};

class B : virtual public A { };   // Virtual inheritance
class C : virtual public A { };   // Virtual inheritance

class D : public B, public C { };

int main() {
    D obj;
    obj.show();  // No ambiguity — only ONE A sub-object
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**

```
Class A method
```

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q.B.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain Multilevel and Hierarchical Inheritance with diagrams. Write a C++ program to demonstrate Multilevel Inheritance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Multilevel Inheritance Explanation (2 Marks):**
Multilevel inheritance is a chain of inheritance where a class is derived from another derived class, creating a multi-tier hierarchy. Example: `Grandparent` → `Parent` → `Child`. The child inherits all features of both grandparent and parent.

**Hierarchical Inheritance Explanation (2 Marks):**
In hierarchical inheritance, multiple derived classes share a **single base class**. All children inherit common features from the parent but add their own specialized behaviors. Example: `Shape` → `Circle`, `Rectangle`, `Triangle`.

**Diagrams (1 Mark each — refer to Section 4.2 of notes):**
- Multilevel: Linear chain of arrows from grandparent to child.
- Hierarchical: Single node with multiple outgoing arrows to children.

**C++ Program — Multilevel Inheritance (1 Mark):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Vehicle {                    // Level 1: Base
public:
    void start() { cout << "Vehicle started" << endl; }
};

class Car : public Vehicle {       // Level 2: Intermediate
public:
    void drive() { cout << "Car is driving" << endl; }
};

class SportsCar : public Car {     // Level 3: Derived
public:
    void turbo() { cout << "Turbo activated!" << endl; }
};

int main() {
    SportsCar sc;
    sc.start();   // From Vehicle
    sc.drive();   // From Car
    sc.turbo();   // From SportsCar
    return 0;
}
```

---

#### **Q.B.(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2022]**
**Discuss Hybrid Inheritance with an example. Explain how the order of constructor invocation works in multilevel inheritance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hybrid Inheritance Definition (1 Mark):**
Hybrid inheritance is a combination of two or more types of inheritance (typically hierarchical + multiple) in a single program. It is used to model complex real-world relationships that cannot be expressed using a single inheritance pattern.

**Example with Diagram (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    personBase["PERSON BASE CLASS"]
    student["STUDENT INHERITS PERSON"]
    employee["EMPLOYEE INHERITS PERSON"]
    intern["WORKING STUDENT INHERITS STUDENT AND EMPLOYEE"]
    student --> personBase
    employee --> personBase
    intern --> student
    intern --> employee
```

**C++ Implementation (1 Mark):**

```cpp
class Person { public: void info() { } };
class Student : virtual public Person { };
class Employee : virtual public Person { };
class WorkingStudent : public Student, public Employee { };
```

**Constructor Invocation Order in Multilevel Inheritance (2 Marks):**

When an object of the most-derived class is created, constructors are invoked in the order: **Base → Intermediate → Derived** (top-down).

**Example:**

```cpp
class A { public: A() { cout << "A "; } };
class B : public A { public: B() { cout << "B "; } };
class C : public B { public: C() { cout << "C "; } };

int main() {
    C obj;   // Output: A B C
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistake Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting `virtual` keyword** — Many students declare hybrid inheritance without `virtual` for the common base, leading to ambiguity. **Always** use `virtual` for the shared ancestor.
> 2. **Confusing constructor and destructor order** — Constructors run *base-to-derived*, destructors run *derived-to-base* (LIFO). Marks are often lost for stating this in reverse.
> 3. **Drawing inheritance arrows backwards** — In UML, the arrow goes *from derived to base* (with a hollow triangle), not the other way. Examiners deduct marks for incorrect direction.
> 4. **Not addressing ambiguity resolution in Hybrid** — Simply stating "Hybrid = combination" without explaining the **Diamond Problem** and its resolution loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Java vs C++ confusion** — Java does NOT support multiple inheritance of classes (only multiple interface implementation). Mixing up the two languages is a frequent error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Inheritance** = acquiring properties from a parent class → enables **code reuse** and **IS-A relationship**.
- **Five core types:** Single, Multiple, Multilevel, Hierarchical, Hybrid (+ Multipath as a special case).
- **Single Inheritance** = 1 base → 1 derived (simplest form).
- **Multiple Inheritance** = ≥2 bases → 1 derived (C++ supports; Java uses interfaces).
- **Multilevel Inheritance** = chain derivation (A → B → C).
- **Hierarchical Inheritance** = 1 base → ≥2 derived (siblings share parent).
- **Hybrid Inheritance** = combination of ≥2 types; often produces the **Diamond Problem**.
- **Diamond Problem** = ambiguity due to duplicate base sub-objects in multipath inheritance.
- **Virtual Inheritance** (C++) = the standard resolution for the Diamond Problem.
- **Constructor Order** = Base → Derived (top-down).
- **Destructor Order** = Derived → Base (bottom-up, LIFO).
- **Access Specifiers in Inheritance:**
  - `public` inheritance → public/protected members retain their access level.
  - `protected` inheritance → public/protected members become `protected`.
  - `private` inheritance → public/protected members become `private`.
  - `private` members of the base are **never** directly accessible in the derived class.
- **UML Notation:** Hollow triangle arrow from derived to base class.
- **Java Limitation:** No multiple class inheritance — only multiple interface implementation.
- **Diamond Problem Keyword for Exam:** Mention `virtual` base class, single shared instance, ambiguity resolution.
- **Method Overriding** vs **Overloading:** Overriding = same signature across base/derived; Overloading = same name, different signatures within the same class.
- **Final Keyword (Java) / sealed (C#)** = prevents further inheritance of the class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"Explain types of inheritance"*, always start with a **one-line definition**, then cover **all 5 types with diagrams**, and conclude with a **real-world example** (e.g., Vehicle → Car → ElectricCar or Shape → Circle/Rectangle). Including a small C++ code snippet for at least one type guarantees full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Super Keyword
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** The Super Keyword # 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In **Object-Oriented Programming** with Java (as prescribed under the KTU 2024 Scheme for PECMT535), the **`super`** keyword is a **reserved reference variable** in Java that provides an explicit, unambiguous mechanism for a **subclass (child class)** to access the **non-private members** (instance variables, methods) and **constructors** of its **immediate superclass (parent class)**. It functions as an **implicit reference to the parent class object**, residing within every instance method and constructor of a derived class. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Pattern):** > *"The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable that refers to the immediate parent class object. It is used to differentiate between the members of the subclass and the superclass when both have the same name."* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine a **family tree** 👨‍👩‍👧. A child (subclass) inherits the **surname, ancestral property, and family traditions** (parent class members) from their parents (superclass). However, the child may have their **own achievements and possessions** (subclass-specific members) which may share the same *name* as the parents' (e.g., both have a "House"). When the child wants to specifically refer to the **father's house** rather than their own newly built house, they point and say *"**super**'s house"* — that is, the parent's version. The `super` keyword in Java plays exactly this role: it disambiguates when child and parent members collide in name, and it allows controlled access to the parent's hidden/internal machinery. > [!NOTE] > **Three Intuitive Pillars of `super`:** > 1. 🏠 **`super.variable`** → "Access my parent's version of this variable" > 2. 📞 **`super.method()`** → "Call my parent's version of this method" > 3. 🔨 **`super(arguments)`** → "Use my parent's constructor to initialize inherited state" --- ## 1.3 Standard Java Vocabulary & Metrics | **Term** | **Standard Value / Rule** | |---|---| | Default `super()` call | **Always inserted by compiler** as the **first statement** of every constructor (if not written explicitly) | | Scope of `super` | Limited to **instance methods, constructors, and instance initializer blocks** — **not** usable inside `static` methods | | Visibility restriction | Cannot access **private** members of the superclass directly through `super` | | Levels of indirection | `super` always refers to the **immediate direct parent** (not grandparent, not great-grandparent) | > [!TIP] > **Quick Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"`super` is the child's polite phone-call home to the parent class."* --- # 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Operational Uses of `super` The `super` keyword has exactly **three** operational forms in Java OOP. KTU examiners frequently test all three across Part A and Part B questions. ### 📌 Use 1: `super` to Refer to **Parent Class Instance Variables** - **Why:** To resolve **data shadowing** — when a subclass declares a variable with the same name as the superclass, the subclass variable **hides** the parent's. - **How:** Use `super.variableName` to access the parent's hidden variable. - **Trigger condition:** Variable name collision between parent and child. ### 📌 Use 2: `super` to Invoke **Parent Class Methods** - **Why:** To invoke an **overridden** method of the superclass from within the subclass's overriding method (i.e., to access the *parent's behaviour* even after overriding). - **How:** Use `super.methodName(arguments)` as a regular method call. - **Trigger condition:** Method overriding scenario in inheritance hierarchy. ### 📌 Use 3: `super()` to Invoke **Parent Class Constructor** - **Why:** To **initialize the inherited portion** of the subclass object using the parent's constructor logic — achieving **constructor chaining**. - **How:** `super(args)` must be the **first executable statement** in the subclass constructor. - **Trigger condition:** When parent class lacks a default no-arg constructor, OR when explicit initialization of inherited fields is required. --- ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Structured Logic Breakdown ### 🔹 Why does `super` exist at all? Because **inheritance** in Java creates a *name-resolution ambiguity*. When a subclass inherits from a parent, both classes can have: - Variables of the same name (shadowing) - Methods of the same signature (overriding) - Constructors that must cooperate to fully initialize the object Without `super`, the JVM would have no clean way to distinguish the child's version from the parent's version during execution. ### 🔹 How does the JVM process `super` internally? The Java compiler embeds an implicit reference to the parent class's **`Class` object metadata** inside every instance of a subclass. When `super` is encountered in bytecode, the JVM performs: 1. **Symbolic resolution** of the member name against the parent class's constant pool. 2. **Dynamic dispatch** check — for overridden methods, the JVM verifies the actual runtime type chain. 3. **Access control check** — ensures the requested member is not `private` to the superclass. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table) | **Use Case** | **Syntax** | **Mandatory Position** | **Common Pitfall** | |---|---|---|---| | Access parent variable | `super.memberName;` | Anywhere inside subclass instance method | Cannot use inside `static` context | | Invoke parent method | `super.methodName(argList);` | Anywhere inside subclass instance method | Cannot be used inside `main()` if main is static (indirectly) | | Invoke parent constructor | `super(argList);` | **MUST be the first statement** in subclass constructor | Cannot be used after other statements; only one `super()` call allowed | | Default behaviour | *Implicit `super();`* | Auto-inserted by compiler as 1st line | Fails compile if parent has **no** no-arg constructor and child doesn't write explicit `super(...)` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of One Constructor Call:** Within any constructor, you can use **either** `super(...)` **or** `this(...)` — **never both** — and the call must be the very first statement. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | **Domain** | **Practical Application of `super`** | |---|---| | GUI Frameworks (Swing/JavaFX) | Extending `JFrame` and calling `super("Window Title")` to set the title via parent constructor | | Servlet Development | Subclassing `HttpServlet` and calling `super.init(config)` to honour container-level initialization | | Android Development | Extending `AppCompatActivity` and calling `super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)` — **mandatory** by Android lifecycle contract | | Game Development | Polymorphic AI: subclass `Boss` calls `super.takeDamage()` to apply base damage logic before adding special effects | | Banking Software | `SavingsAccount extends Account`: `super.withdraw()` ensures base validation before applying overdraft rules | --- # 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough — Use Case 1: Accessing Parent's Variable (Data Shadowing) ```java // === File: Vehicle.java === class Vehicle { int speed = 60; // Parent's speed } // === File: Car.java === class Car extends Vehicle { int speed = 120; // Child's speed — SHADOWS parent's variable void displaySpeeds() { System.out.println("Child (Car) speed : " + speed); // Own variable System.out.println("Parent (Vehicle) speed : " + super.speed); // Parent's variable } public static void main(String[] args) { Car c = new Car(); c.displaySpeeds(); } } ``` ### Line-by-Line Logical Deduction | **Line** | **Execution Logic** | |---|---| | `class Vehicle { int speed = 60; }` | Defines parent class with instance variable `speed = 60`. | | `class Car extends Vehicle` | Establishes inheritance. `Car` inherits `speed` from `Vehicle`. | | `int speed = 120;` | Child class declares its own `speed`. JVM marks parent's `speed` as **shadowed** (hidden, but still accessible). | | `System.out.println(speed)` | Resolves to **child's** `speed` (= 120) via the normal scope rule. | | `super.speed` | Forces the compiler to bypass the child's `speed` and look one level up the class hierarchy into `Vehicle`, returning **60**. | | **Output** | `Child (Car) speed : 120`
`Parent (Vehicle) speed : 60` | ### Mathematical Analogy of Shadowing We can model variable shadowing using a **set membership notation**: $$ \text{Visible}(x) = \bigcup_{i=0}^{n} \{\, \text{member}_i \mid \text{name}(m_i) = x \,\} \setminus \text{Shadowed} $$ $$ \text{Explicit}(x, \text{super}) = \text{Resolve}(x, \text{ParentClass}, \text{Shadowed} \cup \text{Visible}) $$ Where: - $\text{Visible}(x)$ = the **uniquely resolved** name `x` from the current class scope. - $\text{Explicit}(x, \text{super})$ = the value of `x` from the **parent class**, bypassing shadowing. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough — Use Case 2: Invoking Overridden Parent Method ```java class Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("Animal makes a sound"); } } class Dog extends Animal { @Override void sound() { System.out.println("Dog barks"); super.sound(); // Calls parent's overridden version } public static void main(String[] args) { Dog d = new Dog(); d.sound(); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Deduction 1. `Dog d = new Dog();` → Creates a `Dog` object. Memory layout: parent portion (Animal) + child portion (Dog). 2. `d.sound();` → JVM performs **dynamic method dispatch**. Since the actual object is `Dog`, `Dog`'s overridden `sound()` is invoked. 3. Inside `Dog.sound()`, `System.out.println("Dog barks");` executes first. 4. `super.sound();` → JVM explicitly invokes `Animal`'s `sound()` (the overridden version), printing "Animal makes a sound". 5. **Final Output:** ``` Dog barks Animal makes a sound ``` ### Method Resolution Order (MRO) — Analytical Matrix | **Lookup Stage** | **Class Searched** | **Match Found?** | **Action Taken** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `Dog` (current class) | ✅ Yes | Execute `Dog.sound()` | | 2 (with `super`) | `Animal` (parent) | ✅ Yes | Execute `Animal.sound()` and return | | 3 (with `super`) | `Object` (grandparent) | ❌ No `sound()` | Method resolution terminates | > [!NOTE] > **`super.method()` does NOT trigger dynamic dispatch** — it is a **static binding** call to the parent's version, which is why parent methods invoked via `super` cannot be infinitely re-entered. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough — Use Case 3: Constructor Chaining with `super()` ```java class Person { String name; Person(String name) { this.name = name; System.out.println("Person constructor called. Name: " + name); } } class Student extends Person { int rollNo; Student(String name, int rollNo) { super(name); // EXPLICIT call to parent constructor this.rollNo = rollNo; System.out.println("Student constructor called. Roll: " + rollNo); } public static void main(String[] args) { Student s = new Student("Anand", 42); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Constructor Chain Derivation 1. `new Student("Anand", 42)` → JVM allocates memory and begins executing `Student(String, int)`. 2. **First statement**: `super(name);` → transfers control to `Person(String)`. 3. Inside `Person(String)`: `this.name = "Anand";` initializes the **inherited** `name` field. 4. Prints `"Person constructor called. Name: Anand"`. 5. Control returns to `Student` constructor at the line after `super(name)`. 6. `this.rollNo = 42;` initializes the **child-specific** field. 7. Prints `"Student constructor called. Roll: 42"`. ### Final Output (with ordering) ``` Person constructor called. Name: Anand Student constructor called. Roll: 42 ``` ### Analytical Derivation of the Constructor Call Chain The full chain can be expressed as a nested function call: $$ \text{Student}(n, r) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Person}(n) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Object}() $$ The general recursive rule for constructor chaining is: $$ C_k(\text{args}_k) = \text{super}(\text{args}_{\text{parent}}) \;\Vert\; \text{ChildSpecificInit}(\text{args}_k) $$ Where: - $C_k$ = constructor of the $k$-th class in the hierarchy. - $\Vert$ denotes **sequential execution** (parent first, then child). - $\text{ChildSpecificInit}$ = the initialization of subclass-only fields. --- ## 3.4 Compilation Error Scenarios — Analytical Matrix | **Code Snippet** | **Compilation Result** | **Reason** | |---|---|---| | `super()` placed as 2nd statement in constructor | ❌ `error: call to super must be first statement` | Java language rule | | `super` used inside `static void main` | ❌ `error: non-static variable super cannot be referenced from a static context` | `super` is instance-bound | | `super.privateMethod()` | ❌ `error: privateMethod() has private access in Parent` | Encapsulation rule | | Calling `super()` twice in one constructor | ❌ `error: call to super must be first statement` | Only one constructor call allowed | | Parent has only `Person(int id)`, child has no explicit `super(...)` | ❌ `error: no suitable constructor found for Person()` | No default no-arg constructor exists in parent | --- # 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — `super` Reference Resolution Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A["Object Class
(Root of Java Hierarchy)"] --> B["Vehicle Class
int speed = 60
void display()"] B --> C["Car Class
int speed = 120
void display()"] C --> D["SportsCar Class
int speed = 200
void display()"] E["super.speed in SportsCar"] --> B F["super.display in SportsCar"] --> B G["super.speed in Car"] --> B H["super.display in Car"] --> B style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style F fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style G fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style H fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 ``` **Interpretation of the Diagram:** - The **chain** A → B → C → D represents the **class inheritance hierarchy** (Object → Vehicle → Car → SportsCar). - Arrows **E, F, G, H** represent the **`super` references** that jump **exactly one level up** to the immediate parent. - Notice that `super` **never** bypasses the immediate parent to reach a grandparent — that requires another `super.super.x` (which is **illegal** in Java). --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Constructor Chaining Flow with `super()` ```mermaid graph TD S1["Step 1: new SportsCar() invoked"] --> S2["Step 2: SportsCar constructor begins"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: super() call placed
as FIRST statement"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Control jumps to Car constructor"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: super() placed as FIRST statement
in Car constructor"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Control jumps to Vehicle constructor"] S6 --> S7["Step 7: super() placed as FIRST statement
in Vehicle constructor"] S7 --> S8["Step 8: Control jumps to Object constructor"] S8 --> S9["Step 9: Object initialization completes"] S9 --> S10["Step 10: Vehicle-specific init runs"] S10 --> S11["Step 11: Car-specific init runs"] S11 --> S12["Step 12: SportsCar-specific init runs"] S12 --> S13["Step 13: SportsCar object fully initialized"] style S1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style S2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style S3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style S4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style S5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style S6 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style S7 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style S8 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style S9 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style S10 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style S11 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style S12 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style S13 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px ``` **Diagram Reading Notes:** - 🟧 **Orange boxes** = Constructor body execution entry. - 🟨 **Gold boxes** = `super()` invocation points. - 🟩 **Green boxes** = Initialization completion (parent → child top-down unwinding). --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of `super` Use Cases ```mermaid graph LR INPUT["Subclass Code
(Child Class Body)"] --> DECISION{"Which form
of super?"} DECISION -->|"super.variable"| USE1["Access Parent's
Hidden Variable"] DECISION -->|"super.method()"| USE2["Invoke Parent's
Overridden Method"] DECISION -->|"super(args)"| USE3["Invoke Parent's
Constructor"] USE1 --> OUT1["Resolves data
shadowing"] USE2 --> OUT2["Extends / preserves
parent behaviour"] USE3 --> OUT3["Enables constructor
chaining"] OUT1 --> RESULT["Cleaner, Unambiguous
Object-Oriented Design"] OUT2 --> RESULT OUT3 --> RESULT style INPUT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style DECISION fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style USE1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style USE2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style USE3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style OUT1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style OUT2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style OUT3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style RESULT fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!NOTE] > This block-level diagram abstracts the three usages into a decision-flow architecture, demonstrating how `super` integrates into the subclass's runtime behaviour regardless of the specific class domain. --- # 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **What is the `super` keyword in Java? List any two of its uses.** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation): - **1 Mark** — Definition: *"`super` is a reference variable in Java that refers to the immediate parent class object. It is implicitly available inside every instance method and constructor of a subclass."* - **1 Mark** — Use 1: *"`super.memberName` is used to access the parent class's instance variable when it is shadowed by a subclass variable of the same name."* - **1 Mark** — Use 2: *"`super(arguments)` is used to explicitly invoke a parameterized constructor of the parent class, enabling constructor chaining."* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Why must the call `super()` be the first statement in a subclass constructor? What happens if the parent class does not have a no-argument constructor?** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks): - **1 Mark** — *"`super()` must be the first statement because the Java language specification requires the parent class's portion of the object to be fully initialized **before** the subclass's own fields are assigned. This guarantees a well-defined initialization order."* - **1 Mark** — *If the parent has no no-argument constructor (because the programmer defined only parameterized constructors), the Java compiler will NOT insert the default `super();` call."* - **1 Mark** — *"Consequently, the subclass constructor **must** explicitly write `super(arguments);` matching one of the parent's available constructors, otherwise the code fails to compile with 'no suitable constructor found for Parent()'."* --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern Compliance:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. KTU mandates an *internal choice* — students answer **either** Q(A) **or** Q(B) in full. --- ### 🔸 **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]** **(a)** Explain the three uses of the `super` keyword in Java with suitable code examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **constructor chaining** using `super()` across a three-level inheritance hierarchy: `LivingBeing → Animal → Dog`. Show the order of constructor execution. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution ### Part (a) — Three Uses of `super` (7 Marks) **Use 1: Accessing Parent's Variable (2 Marks)** ```java class Father { String name = "John"; } class Son extends Father { String name = "Mike"; void print() { System.out.println("Son's name : " + name); System.out.println("Father's name: " + super.name); } } ``` **Explanation:** `super.name` accesses the parent's hidden `name` variable, resolving the data-shadowing problem. **Use 2: Invoking Overridden Method (2 Marks)** ```java class Parent { void show() { System.out.println("Parent show"); } } class Child extends Parent { @Override void show() { super.show(); // Calls parent's overridden show() System.out.println("Child show"); } } ``` **Explanation:** `super.show()` explicitly invokes the parent's method version, allowing the child to extend (not replace) the parent behaviour. **Use 3: Invoking Parent Constructor (3 Marks)** ```java class A { A(int x) { System.out.println("A: " + x); } } class B extends A { B() { super(10); // Calls A(int x) explicitly System.out.println("B initialized"); } } ``` **Explanation:** `super(10)` is mandatory as the **first statement** because `A` does not have a no-arg constructor. This demonstrates constructor chaining. --- ### Part (b) — Three-Level Constructor Chaining Program (7 Marks) ```java class LivingBeing { LivingBeing() { System.out.println("1. LivingBeing constructor executed"); } } class Animal extends LivingBeing { Animal() { super(); // Implicit but shown for clarity System.out.println("2. Animal constructor executed"); } } class Dog extends Animal { Dog() { super(); // Implicit but shown for clarity System.out.println("3. Dog constructor executed"); } public static void main(String[] args) { Dog d = new Dog(); } } ``` **Step-by-Step Output Deduction (Order of Execution):** 1. `new Dog()` is called → `Dog()` constructor begins. 2. First line `super();` → transfers to `Animal()` constructor. 3. First line of `Animal()` is `super();` → transfers to `LivingBeing()` constructor. 4. `LivingBeing()` executes completely → prints line "1". 5. Control returns to `Animal()` → prints line "2". 6. Control returns to `Dog()` → prints line "3". **Final Output:** ``` 1. LivingBeing constructor executed 2. Animal constructor executed 3. Dog constructor executed ``` **Conclusion (1 Mark):** The output proves that **parent constructors always execute before child constructors**, which is the core principle of constructor chaining via `super()`. --- ### 🔸 **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate]** **(a)** Differentiate between `this` and `super` keywords in Java with examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider the following scenario. A class `Shape` has an instance variable `color`. The class `Circle` extends `Shape` and has an additional variable `radius`. Write a complete Java program that uses `super` to initialize the inherited `color` field in the `Circle` constructor and demonstrates variable shadowing resolution. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution ### Part (a) — `this` vs `super` (7 Marks) | **Basis of Comparison** | **`this` keyword** | **`super` keyword** | |---|---|---| | **Definition (1M)** | Refers to the **current** class object | Refers to the **immediate parent** class object | | **Variable Access (1M)** | `this.var` → current class's instance variable | `super.var` → parent class's instance variable | | **Method Call (1M)** | `this.method()` → current class method (if overloaded) | `super.method()` → parent class's overridden method | | **Constructor Call (1M)** | `this(args)` → another constructor of the **same** class | `super(args)` → constructor of the **parent** class | | **Position Rule (1M)** | Must be first statement (when used in constructor) | Must be first statement (when used in constructor) | | **Mutual Exclusivity (1M)** | Cannot combine with `super()` in same constructor | Cannot combine with `this()` in same constructor | | **Static Context (1M)** | Not allowed in `static` methods | Not allowed in `static` methods | **Example Code (bonus clarity):** ```java class Demo { int x = 5; Demo(int x) { this.x = x; } // 'this' disambiguates } class SubDemo extends Demo { int x = 10; SubDemo(int x) { super(x); // 'super' passes x to parent System.out.println(super.x); // accesses parent's x } } ``` --- ### Part (b) — Shape/Circle Program Demonstrating `super` for Variable Resolution (7 Marks) ```java // ============= Shape.java ============= class Shape { String color; Shape(String color) { this.color = color; System.out.println("Shape constructor: color = " + color); } } // ============= Circle.java ============= class Circle extends Shape { double radius; String color = "Default-Red"; // SHADOWS parent's 'color' Circle(String color, double radius) { super(color); // 1 Mark: Initializes inherited color this.radius = radius; System.out.println("Circle constructor: radius = " + radius); } void displayColors() { System.out.println("Circle's own color : " + color); System.out.println("Parent Shape's color: " + super.color); // 2 Marks } public static void main(String[] args) { Circle c = new Circle("Blue", 5.5); c.displayColors(); } } ``` **Execution Trace (3 Marks):** 1. `new Circle("Blue", 5.5)` → `Circle` constructor begins. 2. `super("Blue")` → invokes `Shape("Blue")`, setting parent's `color = "Blue"`. Prints "Shape constructor: color = Blue". 3. Control returns → `this.radius = 5.5`. Prints "Circle constructor: radius = 5.5". 4. `c.displayColors()`: - `color` resolves to **child's** `color` (which is `"Default-Red"`, never reassigned because child's field shadows parent's). - `super.color` resolves to **parent's** `color` = `"Blue"`. **Final Output:** ``` Shape constructor: color = Blue Circle constructor: radius = 5.5 Circle's own color : Default-Red Parent Shape's color: Blue ``` **Key Insight (1 Mark):** The child class's `color = "Default-Red"` is a field initializer that **shadows** the inherited `color`. Only `super.color` (or any modification through `super.color = ...`) can access the parent's version. --- > [!WARNING] > ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the "first statement" rule** for `super()` or `this()` → Lose 2–3 marks immediately. > 2. **Confusing `super.x` with overriding**: `super` is for **hiding/shadowing**; for **overriding** (methods), both `super.method()` and polymorphic dispatch give the *same* result only when the parent class type is referenced — but the child can still call `super.method()` to bypass the override. > 3. **Trying `super.super.x`** to reach grandparent → **Compilation error**. `super` only hops one level. > 4. **Writing `super()` to call grandparent's constructor** → Impossible. Use **chained constructors** through each level. > 5. **Assuming `super` works inside `static` methods** → Compilation error. Static context = class-level, no parent-object reference. > 6. **Not distinguishing "shadowing" (variables) from "overriding" (methods)** — KTU board examiners award **separate marks** for understanding this distinction. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - 🔹 `super` is a **reserved keyword** in Java — it is **not** a class name, object name, or data type. - 🔹 It refers to the **immediate parent class** (one level up, not transitive). - 🔹 It has **exactly three** uses: **variable access**, **method invocation**, **constructor call**. - 🔹 `super()` (or `super(args)`) **MUST be the first statement** of a subclass constructor if used. - 🔹 The compiler **automatically inserts** a call to the parent's no-arg constructor (`super();`) as the first statement of every constructor — **unless** you write any `this()` or `super()` call explicitly. - 🔹 `super` **cannot** be used in **static methods, static blocks, or static variable initializers**. - 🔹 `super` **cannot** access **private** members of the parent class. - 🔹 `super` enables **constructor chaining**, ensuring **top-down initialization** (parent → child). - 🔹 A constructor can use **either** `this(args)` **or** `super(args)` — **never both**. - 🔹 `super` provides a clean way to **resolve data shadowing** when child and parent fields have identical names. - 🔹 `super.method()` is **statically bound** to the parent's implementation — it does NOT participate in dynamic dispatch. - 🔹 In **multilevel inheritance** chains (A → B → C), each level's `super()` call walks **one level up** the chain sequentially. - 🔹 If the parent class defines **only** parameterized constructors, every child constructor **must** explicitly call `super(args)` — no default fallback exists. - 🔹 Real-world Java frameworks (Android, Servlets, Swing) **mandate** `super.onCreate()`, `super.init()`, `super("Title")` calls in their lifecycle hooks. ---

topics.title: Protected Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Protected Members ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the Object Oriented Programming paradigm (specifically in **C++** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus), an access specifier governs the **visibility** and **accessibility** of class members (data members and member functions) from outside the class boundary. The three standard access specifiers are `private`, `public`, and **`protected`**. A **`protected` member** of a class is a data member or member function whose access lies *between* `private` and `public`. Formally, a `protected` member: 1. **Cannot be accessed** by non-member functions (i.e., ordinary `main()` or free functions in the global scope) of the class. 2. **Cannot be accessed** by objects of the class instantiated in `main()` or any other unrelated scope. 3. **Can be accessed** by member functions of the **same class** in which it is declared. 4. **Can be accessed** by member functions of any **derived (child) class** — this is the crucial property that distinguishes `protected` from `private`. 5. **Cannot be accessed** by members of an **unrelated (sibling) class**, even if it inherits from the same parent in a different branch. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** `protected` access exists *exclusively* to support **Inheritance**. If a class is not meant to be inherited, the choice between `private` and `protected` is largely a matter of design intent, but for any base class in a hierarchy, `protected` is the cornerstone of controlled data sharing with subclasses. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **joint family vault** in a traditional household: - **The keys to the vault are held only by the family elders** (the *base class*). - **Direct outsiders** (unrelated friends, neighbors) — analogous to `main()` or unrelated classes — are **denied access** even though the vault is not completely secret; they just cannot enter. - **Domestic staff** (the *derived class*) — they live within the household, so they are granted *partial* access to certain drawers. They can use what they need to serve the family, but not everything in the vault is open to them. - The **elders themselves** (the class itself) have full access, and **trusted grown-up children** (derived classes) can also peek into the drawers marked as `protected`, but **domestic staff from a different household** (sibling/unrelated classes) cannot. > [!NOTE] > **The Three-Tier Access Analogy** > > | Real-World Lock | C++ Equivalent | Who can Open? | > |---|---|---| > | Public Park Bench | `public` member | Everyone — including the public | > | Family Vault (private) | `private` member | Only the elders (own class) | > | Family Vault (protected) | `protected` member | Elders + Grown-up children (own class + derived) | ### 1.3 Standard Boundaries The KTU 2024 evaluation scheme treats the following rules as **non-negotiable hard rules** worth **2 marks each** if missed in a derivation: - **Default access for `class` keyword** = `private` - **Default access for `struct` keyword** = `public` - **Maximum inheritance depth** = implementation-dependent (theoretical limit ≈ 2³¹ on 32-bit compilers) - **A `protected` member of a base class behaves like `private` for everything except its derived classes.** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Access Resolution Algorithm When the C++ compiler encounters a member access expression (e.g., `object.member` or `pointer->member`), it executes the following **access resolution steps** in sequence: - **Step 1 — Membership Check:** Is the requesting entity a member function of the declaring class? - If **YES** → grant access (regardless of `private`, `protected`, `public`). - If **NO** → proceed to Step 2. - **Step 2 — Friend Check:** Is the requesting entity a declared `friend` of the declaring class? - If **YES** → grant access. - If **NO** → proceed to Step 3. - **Step 3 — Inheritance Path Check:** Is the requesting entity a member function of a **derived class** AND is the member declared as `protected` or `public`? - If **YES** → grant access **only if** the access specifier in the inheritance statement permits it (more on this in *Protected Inheritance*). - If **NO** → proceed to Step 4. - **Step 4 — Denial:** Compile-time error: `'member' is not accessible`. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Protected Members The fundamental design question: *If a derived class already exists, why not just use `public`?* The answer lies in **encapsulation** — the central OOP pillar: - `public` would expose the internal state (e.g., a bank account's balance) to **all of `main()`**, the world, and any random function — defeating encapsulation. - `private` would lock out derived classes — forcing developers to either use `public` getters/setters (verbose) or redesign. - `protected` strikes the **Goldilocks balance**: hidden from the outside world, accessible to cooperating subclasses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** In large-scale software systems (e.g., banking software, embedded device drivers, GUI toolkits like Qt, MFC), `protected` members are used to provide **template method hooks** — base classes define the skeleton of an algorithm and leave specific steps (`protected` virtual functions) to be filled by derived classes. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Access Visibility Table > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **single most important visual** for any KTU question on access specifiers. Memorize it. | Access Specifier | Own Class | Derived Class | Outside (main/Objects) | Friend Function | |---|---|---|---|---| | `public` | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | | `protected` | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | ✘ No | ✔ Yes | | `private` | ✔ Yes | ✘ No | ✘ No | ✔ Yes | ### 2.4 Protected Inheritance — A Subtle Distinction When a class `Derived` inherits from `Base` using the keyword `protected`, the visibility transformation rule applies: $$ \text{Visibility Transformation under Protected Inheritance} $$ $$ \text{Base.}public \;\rightarrow\; \text{Derived.}protected $$ $$ \text{Base.}protected \;\rightarrow\; \text{Derived.}protected $$ $$ \text{Base.}private \;\rightarrow\; \text{Derived.}\text{INACCESSIBLE} $$ **Plain English translation:** - Anything that was `public` in the base becomes `protected` in the derived — i.e., the derived class *narrows* the public interface down to a protected one. - Anything that was `protected` in the base remains `protected` in the derived. - Anything that was `private` in the base **stays inaccessible** in the derived (and in *all* further descendants) — `private` is **never** inherited by access. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility `protected` access is foundational in the following engineering domains: - **Device Driver Development (Embedded Systems):** A base class `Device` exposes a `protected` buffer pointer that derived classes `USART_Device`, `SPI_Device` use directly, while hiding it from the application. - **GUI Frameworks (Qt, MFC):** The `paintEvent(QPaintEvent*)` is a `protected` virtual function — the framework calls it, but it is hidden from application code. - **Numerical Libraries (LAPACK wrappers):** Matrix operations expose `protected` scratch buffers used by derived `SpecializedSolver` classes. - **Game Engines (Unreal Engine):** The `AActor` class has `protected` components (meshes, colliders) that derived game-specific actor classes access directly. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough — Proving the Access Rules The following C++ program is a **complete, KTU-style illustrative program** that demonstrates every legal and illegal access path for `protected` members. Each `// ERROR` line is a deliberate compile-time error used for pedagogical purposes. ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Base { private: int privateData = 1; // (1) private to Base only protected: int protectedData = 2; // (2) protected: Base + Derived public: int publicData = 3; // (3) public: accessible everywhere void showFromBase() { cout << privateData << protectedData << publicData << endl; } }; class Derived : public Base { public: void showFromDerived() { // cout << privateData; // ERROR (a): 'privateData' is private in Base cout << protectedData; // OK (b): Derived can access protected cout << publicData; // OK (c): Derived can access public } }; int main() { Base b; Derived d; b.showFromBase(); // OK d.showFromDerived(); // OK // cout << b.protectedData; // ERROR (d): outside 'main', protected is forbidden // cout << d.protectedData; // ERROR (e): same reason as (d) cout << b.publicData; // OK cout << d.publicData; // OK return 0; } ``` ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Evaluation of Each Line We now trace the compiler's access-check algorithm for every critical line. #### Line `(a)` — `cout << privateData;` inside `Derived::showFromDerived()` $$ \text{Requester} = \text{Derived::showFromDerived()} $$ $$ \text{Target member} = \text{Base::privateData} $$ **Step 1 — Membership Check:** Requester is *not* a member of `Base`. → **Fail**. **Step 2 — Friend Check:** No `friend` declaration. → **Fail**. **Step 3 — Inheritance Path Check:** Requester is in a *derived* class, but the target is `private`. `private` members are **never** accessible to derived classes. → **Fail**. $$ \boxed{\text{Compiler Error: } \texttt{'int Base::privateData' is private within this context}} $$ #### Line `(b)` — `cout << protectedData;` inside `Derived::showFromDerived()` $$ \text{Requester} = \text{Derived::showFromDerived()} $$ $$ \text{Target member} = \text{Base::protectedData} $$ **Step 1 — Membership Check:** Not a member of `Base`. → **Fail**. **Step 2 — Friend Check:** No friendship. → **Fail**. **Step 3 — Inheritance Path Check:** Requester is in a derived class, and the target is `protected`. With `public` inheritance, `protected` members are accessible to derived classes. → **Pass**. $$ \boxed{\text{Compile-time SUCCESS}} $$ #### Line `(d)` — `cout << b.protectedData;` inside `main()` $$ \text{Requester} = \text{main()} $$ $$ \text{Target member} = \text{Base::protectedData (accessed via object } b\text{)} $$ **Step 1 — Membership Check:** `main()` is *not* a member of `Base`. → **Fail**. **Step 2 — Friend Check:** No friendship. → **Fail**. **Step 3 — Inheritance Path Check:** `main()` is *not* a derived class member function. → **Fail**. $$ \boxed{\text{Compiler Error: } \texttt{'protectedData' is not accessible from 'main()'}} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Access Outcomes for Protected Members | Scenario | Accessing Entity | Code Form | Outcome | Marks Weight (KTU) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Own class member function | `Base::func()` accesses `protectedData` | ✔ Legal | 2 | | 2 | Derived class member function | `Derived::func()` accesses `Base::protectedData` | ✔ Legal | **3** | | 3 | `main()` accessing via base object | `b.protectedData` in `main()` | ✘ Illegal | **3** | | 4 | `main()` accessing via derived object | `d.protectedData` in `main()` | ✘ Illegal | **3** | | 5 | Friend function of base | `friend void f()` accessing `protectedData` | ✔ Legal | 1 | | 6 | Sibling (unrelated) class | `Sibling::func()` accessing `Base::protectedData` | ✘ Illegal | 2 | | 7 | `protected` inheritance chain | `GrandChild::func()` accessing `Base::protectedData` | ✔ Legal | 2 | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Protected Access Resolution Flowchart The following block diagram maps the **exact decision tree** the C++ compiler follows when evaluating whether a `protected` member is accessible. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Access Request Initiated] --> B{Is requester a member
of declaring class?} B -- Yes --> Z1[GRANT ACCESS] B -- No --> C{Is requester a
declared friend?} C -- Yes --> Z1 C -- No --> D{Is requester a member
of a DERIVED class?} D -- No --> E[COMPILE-TIME ERROR
Member not accessible] D -- Yes --> F{Is the target member
declared protected or public?} F -- No (private) --> E F -- Yes --> G{Does the inheritance
specifier allow this access?} G -- public or protected --> Z1 G -- private --> E ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Class Hierarchy Showing Protected Visibility ```mermaid classDiagram class Base { -int privateData #int protectedData +int publicData +void showFromBase() } class Derived { +void showFromDerived() } class Unrelated { +void someFunction() } class MainScope { +int main() } Base <|-- Derived : public inheritance Base <.. Unrelated : no relation Derived <.. MainScope : no relation Base <.. MainScope : no relation ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The `#` symbol (Mermaid convention) denotes `protected`. - The `-` symbol denotes `private`. - The `+` symbol denotes `public`. - Dashed arrows (`<..`) indicate "no inheritance relationship" — used to emphasize that `MainScope` and `Unrelated` have **no path** to `Base::protectedData`. ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Protected Members Exist ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ApplicationLayer[Application Layer] APP[main and global functions] end subgraph FrameworkLayer[Framework Layer] BASE[Base Class with protected hooks] end subgraph ExtensionLayer[Extension Layer] DER1[Derived Class A] DER2[Derived Class B] DER3[Derived Class C] end BASE -- exposes protected hooks to --> DER1 BASE -- exposes protected hooks to --> DER2 BASE -- exposes protected hooks to --> DER3 APP -- uses public interface of --> DER1 APP -- uses public interface of --> DER2 APP -- uses public interface of --> DER3 APP -. blocked from .-> BASE ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The `protected` access specifier is the bridge between a *framework* (which wants to share internal state with trusted extensions) and the *application* (which must use only the public interface). This is the **Hollywood Principle**: *"Don't call us, we'll call you."* — the base class calls `protected` virtual functions on derived classes, but the application never directly touches the protected machinery. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What are protected members in C++? Explain their significance in inheritance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Protected members are class members declared using the `protected` access specifier. They are accessible within the class in which they are declared and by member functions of any class **derived** from it, but they remain inaccessible from outside the class (e.g., from `main()` or unrelated classes). Their **significance in inheritance** is that they allow a base class to expose internal data and helper functions to its derived classes — enabling code reuse and polymorphism — while still hiding those internals from the rest of the program. This preserves encapsulation while supporting the "is-a" relationship central to inheritance. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Differentiate between private and protected access specifiers in C++.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `private` | `protected` | |---|---|---| | Accessible in own class? | Yes | Yes | | Accessible in derived class? | **No** | **Yes** | | Accessible in `main()`? | No | No | | Friend access? | Yes | Yes | | Purpose | Total encapsulation | Controlled sharing with subclasses | The key difference: `private` members are **not inherited** (in the access sense), while `protected` members are **accessible to derived classes**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the three access specifiers in C++ with a suitable example program. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is protected inheritance? Explain how the visibility of base class members changes when a class is inherited as protected. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Three Access Specifiers with Example (7 marks) The three access specifiers in C++ are `public`, `private`, and `protected`. - **`public`**: Accessible from anywhere — within the class, from derived classes, and from outside via objects. - **`private`**: Accessible only within the class in which it is declared. Not accessible from derived classes or outside. - **`protected`**: Accessible within the class and within any derived class, but not from outside. **Illustrative Program:** ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Student { private: int rollNo; // accessible only inside Student protected: int semester; // accessible in Student + derived classes public: string name; // accessible everywhere void setData(int r, int s) { rollNo = r; semester = s; } void showRoll() { cout << "Roll: " << rollNo << endl; } }; class Result : public Student { public: void showSemester() { cout << "Semester: " << semester << endl; // OK - protected access } }; int main() { Result r; r.setData(101, 5); r.name = "Anand"; // OK - public r.showRoll(); r.showSemester(); // r.semester = 6; // ERROR: protected, not accessible from main return 0; } ``` #### Part (b) — Protected Inheritance (7 marks) When a class is inherited using the syntax `class Derived : protected Base`, the visibility of base class members undergoes the following transformation: - **Public members of Base** become **protected** in Derived. - **Protected members of Base** remain **protected** in Derived. - **Private members of Base** remain **inaccessible** in Derived. **Code Demonstration:** ```cpp class Base { public: int pub; protected: int prot; private: int priv; }; class Derived : protected Base { void test() { pub = 1; // OK: public in Base → protected in Derived prot = 2; // OK: protected stays protected // priv = 3; // ERROR: private never inherited } }; int main() { Derived d; // d.pub = 10; // ERROR: pub is now protected in Derived } ``` **Key insight:** Protected inheritance is used to *narrow down* the public interface of a base class — useful when the derived class wants to expose the inherited functionality only to *its own* subclasses, not to general application code. --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a)** Write a C++ program to demonstrate the use of protected members in inheritance. Explain how protected members are accessed by derived classes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the real-world significance of protected members with an example from GUI frameworks or device driver design. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Program Demonstrating Protected Members (7 marks) ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Shape { protected: double area; // protected data member public: void setArea(double a) { area = a; } double getArea() { return area; } }; class Circle : public Shape { double radius; public: void setRadius(double r) { radius = r; area = 3.14159 * r * r; // Derived class directly writes 'area' } }; int main() { Circle c; c.setRadius(5.0); cout << "Area: " << c.getArea() << endl; return 0; } ``` **Explanation:** - `area` is declared `protected` in the base class `Shape`. - The derived class `Circle` **directly accesses and modifies** `area` inside `setRadius()` — this is the *only* context outside `Shape` itself where this is legal. - If we tried to write `c.area = 100;` inside `main()`, the compiler would throw an **"inaccessible"** error, because `main()` is neither `Shape` nor a derived class of `Shape`. #### Part (b) — Real-World Significance (7 marks) **Example from GUI Frameworks (Qt):** In the Qt framework, the base class `QWidget` declares `paintEvent(QPaintEvent*)` as a `protected` virtual function. Application code in `main()` does not call `paintEvent` directly — Qt calls it whenever the widget needs repainting. Derived classes (custom widgets like `MyButton`) **override** `paintEvent` to draw their own content. This works *only* because `paintEvent` is `protected` — accessible to derived classes, hidden from the application. **Why this matters:** 1. **Encapsulation** — internal drawing logic is not exposed to the application. 2. **Extensibility** — derived classes can customize behavior without modifying Qt's source. 3. **Safety** — the application cannot accidentally bypass the framework's drawing pipeline. **Example from Device Drivers (Embedded Systems):** In a UART driver hierarchy, `UART_Base` declares a `protected` register-buffer pointer. Derived classes `UART0`, `UART1`, `UART2` access the buffer directly to read/write bytes, but application code can only use the `public` `readByte()` and `writeByte()` methods — preventing corruption of the hardware registers. --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in this topic:** > > 1. **Confusing "private members are NOT inherited" with "private members don't exist in derived class."** (–2 marks) > Private members *physically exist* in the derived object's memory layout; they are simply *inaccessible* to the derived class. The KTU examiner expects you to state this distinction. > > 2. **Forgetting that `main()` is NOT a derived class.** (–3 marks) > Many students write `b.protectedData = 5;` inside `main()` and claim it works. It does **not**. `main()` has no special inheritance privileges. > > 3. **Writing "protected = public + derived."** (–2 marks) > This is a frequent oversimplification. The correct statement: protected is **public within the inheritance chain, private outside it.** > > 4. **Missing the "friend" row in the access table.** (–1 mark) > Friends of the base class can access protected members. Students often forget this row. > > 5. **Treating `protected` inheritance as identical to `public` inheritance.** (–3 marks) > Under `protected` inheritance, base `public` members *downgrade* to `protected`. Under `public` inheritance, they remain `public`. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **`protected` = "visible to me and my children, hidden from the world."** - It is the **default choice** for base class members that subclasses need but the outside world should not see. - **`private` members are never accessible to derived classes** — only `protected` and `public` are. - **`main()` and global functions are not derived classes** — they cannot access `protected` members. - **Friend functions** (declared via `friend`) can access `protected` members regardless of inheritance. - **Protected inheritance** downgrades base `public` to `protected` in the derived class. - **Default access** of a `class` is `private`; of a `struct` is `public`. - The compiler performs a **4-step access check**: own class → friend → derived class + access-specifier allowed → otherwise error. - `protected` members are foundational to the **Template Method design pattern** and the **Hollywood Principle**. - In real engineering: **GUI frameworks, device drivers, and numerical libraries** rely heavily on `protected` hooks. ---

topics.title: Calling Order of Constructors. Method Overriding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Calling Order of Constructors. Method Overriding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Calling Order of Constructors in Inheritance

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
In Java's Object-Oriented Programming paradigm, the *Calling Order of Constructors* refers to the deterministic, top-down execution sequence in which parent class (superclass) and child class (subclass) constructors are invoked during the instantiation of a derived class object. The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) implicitly inserts a call to `super()` as the **first executable statement** of every constructor, ensuring that the inheritance hierarchy is fully initialized from the most ancestral class (typically `java.lang.Object`) down to the most derived subclass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> If a constructor does not explicitly invoke either `super(...)` or `this(...)`, the compiler **automatically inserts a no-argument call to `super()`**. This is a compile-time rule, not a runtime behavior.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are building a multi-story house. Before you can construct the **2nd floor (Child Class)**, the **1st floor (Parent Class)** must already exist, and before that, the **foundation (`Object` class)** must be laid. The constructor calling order is exactly this — you build from the **bottom up** (root → intermediate → leaf) but the construction process **starts at the top of the inheritance chain** and proceeds downward toward the object being created. Every constructor's first job is to "phone home" to its parent and ensure the parent is properly set up before the child adds its own features.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Students:** *"Parents first, then me"* — The parent is always constructed **before** the child object is finalized in memory.

### 1.2 Method Overriding

**Formal Definition:**
*Method Overriding* is a runtime polymorphism mechanism in Java wherein a subclass provides a **specific implementation** of a method that is already declared in its superclass. The overriding method in the child class must share an **identical signature** (name, parameter list) with the parent method. The decision of *which* version to execute is deferred until runtime, governed by the **actual object type** rather than the reference variable type — this is the essence of *Dynamic Method Dispatch*.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Consider a generic blueprint for a "Vehicle" with a method `start()`. A `Car` and a `Motorcycle` both inherit from `Vehicle`, but each needs a **different way of starting**. The child classes *override* the generic `start()` with their own specialized version. When you press "Start" on a specific vehicle, the correct, specialized action happens automatically — that's overriding in action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Overloading** = Compile-time polymorphism (same name, different parameters, within the same class).
> - **Overriding** = Runtime polymorphism (same name, same parameters, across superclass-subclass).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Superclass** | The parent class whose members are inherited. |
| **Subclass** | The child class that extends the superclass. |
| **Dynamic Method Dispatch** | JVM mechanism that resolves overridden method calls at runtime. |
| **Covariant Return Type** | An overriding method may return a *subtype* of the original return type (Java 5+). |
| **`@Override` annotation** | Compile-time safeguard that validates the override syntax. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

### 2.1 Rules Governing the Calling Order of Constructors

The constructor execution sequence in inheritance follows a **strict, non-negotiable hierarchy**:

1. **Rule 1 — Root-to-Leaf Order:** Constructors are invoked starting from the **topmost superclass** (e.g., `Object`) and proceed **downward** to the most derived subclass.
2. **Rule 2 — Implicit `super()` Insertion:** If a constructor's first statement is neither `super(...)` nor `this(...)`, the compiler auto-inserts `super()` (call to no-arg parent constructor).
3. **Rule 3 — Explicit `super(args)`:** A constructor can explicitly call a parameterized parent constructor using `super(arg1, arg2, ...)`. This call **must be the first statement**.
4. **Rule 4 — `this()` Delegation:** A constructor can call another constructor of the **same** class using `this(...)`. This creates *constructor chaining within a class*.
5. **Rule 5 — Mutual Exclusivity:** A constructor can call **either** `super()` or `this()` — never both, and never more than one of either.
6. **Rule 6 — Static Initializer Order:** `static` blocks and static variable initializers execute **once**, in the order they appear, **before any constructor** of the class is invoked.

### 2.2 Rules Governing Method Overriding

| # | Rule | Explanation |
|---|------|-------------|
| 1 | **Identical Signature** | Method name and parameter list (types, order, count) must match **exactly**. |
| 2 | **Access Level Cannot Be More Restrictive** | A subclass method can have *equal or wider* access (e.g., `protected` → `public`), but **not narrower** (e.g., `public` → `private`). |
| 3 | **Covariant Return Type Allowed** | Return type can be a **subclass** of the parent's return type (e.g., `Object` → `String`). |
| 4 | **Cannot Override `final` Methods** | Methods declared `final` in the superclass are immune to overriding. |
| 5 | **Cannot Override `static` Methods** | If both methods are `static`, it's *method hiding*, **not** overriding. |
| 6 | **`private` Methods Are Not Inherited** | They cannot be overridden (since the subclass has no visibility into them). |
| 7 | **Exception Restriction** | Overriding method can throw **equal, fewer, or narrower** checked exceptions — never *broader* or *new* checked exceptions. |
| 8 | **Use of `@Override` Annotation** | Recommended (not mandatory) for compile-time safety; it forces the compiler to verify the override. |
| 9 | **Abstract Methods Must Be Overridden** | Any concrete subclass of an abstract class *must* provide implementations for all abstract methods, or declare itself abstract. |
| 10 | **Use of `super.methodName()`** | The overridden parent method can still be invoked explicitly from the child class using `super`. |

### 2.3 Dynamic Method Dispatch — The Engine of Overriding

**Dynamic Method Dispatch** is the JVM's process of resolving which overridden method to invoke at runtime, based on the **actual runtime type of the object**, not the declared type of the reference variable.

$$ \text{Method Invoked} = f(\text{Actual Object Type at Runtime}) $$

This is the cornerstone of **runtime (dynamic) polymorphism** and is the mechanism that makes code like the following behave intuitively:

```java
Animal a = new Dog();   // Reference type: Animal, Object type: Dog
a.sound();              // JVM calls Dog's overridden sound(), not Animal's
```

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Framework Design (Spring, Hibernate):** Overriding enables template method patterns and dependency injection hooks.
- **GUI Event Handling:** `actionPerformed()` in Swing is a classic override scenario.
- **Plugin Architectures:** Third-party extensions override base class behaviors without modifying original source.
- **Test Mocking (JUnit):** Mock objects override real method behaviors to isolate units under test.
- **API Standardization:** Base classes define contracts; child classes provide specialized implementations.

### 2.5 KTU Cheat-Sheet Table — Key Conceptual Mapping

| Concept | When It Happens | Binds By | Keyword Used |
|---------|-----------------|----------|--------------|
| Constructor Call Order | Object creation | Compile-time + Runtime | `super()` |
| Method Overloading | Within same class | Compile-time | None (same name, diff. params) |
| Method Overriding | Across superclass-subclass | Runtime | `@Override`, `super` |
| Method Hiding | `static` methods in subclass | Compile-time | (Same signature, `static`) |
| Variable Hiding | Same-name fields | Compile-time | `super.fieldName` |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Trace Analysis & Code Walkthroughs

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Constructor Calling Order in a 3-Level Hierarchy

Consider the following Java class hierarchy:

```java
class GrandParent {
    GrandParent() {
        System.out.println("GrandParent constructor");
    }
}

class Parent extends GrandParent {
    Parent() {
        System.out.println("Parent constructor");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    Child() {
        System.out.println("Child constructor");
    }
}
```

**Execution trace when `new Child()` is invoked:**

| Step | Action | Output |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | `Child()` constructor entered | — |
| 2 | Implicit `super()` added by compiler → `Parent()` invoked | — |
| 3 | `Parent()` constructor entered | — |
| 4 | Implicit `super()` → `GrandParent()` invoked | — |
| 5 | `GrandParent()` constructor body executes | `GrandParent constructor` |
| 6 | Control returns to `Parent()` body | `Parent constructor` |
| 7 | Control returns to `Child()` body | `Child constructor` |

**Final Console Output:**
```
GrandParent constructor
Parent constructor
Child constructor
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The object is allocated on the heap in its *complete* form (memory for all inherited fields), but **initialization proceeds top-down** through the constructor chain. By the time the `Child` constructor body runs, all ancestor state is already fully initialized.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Constructor Calling Order with Parameterized `super()`

```java
class Vehicle {
    Vehicle(String type) {
        System.out.println("Vehicle: " + type);
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    Car(String brand) {
        super("Four-wheeler");     // explicit super() call — MUST be first
        System.out.println("Car: " + brand);
    }
}
```

**Trace for `new Car("Tesla")`:**

| Step | Action | Output |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | `Car("Tesla")` invoked | — |
| 2 | Explicit `super("Four-wheeler")` → `Vehicle("Four-wheeler")` | — |
| 3 | `Vehicle` body executes | `Vehicle: Four-wheeler` |
| 4 | Control returns to `Car` body | `Car: Tesla` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** If you write a parameterized constructor in `Vehicle` and *do not* define a no-arg constructor, then a no-arg child constructor that relies on implicit `super()` will **fail to compile** with the error: *"constructor Vehicle in class Vehicle cannot be applied to given types"*.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Method Overriding with Dynamic Dispatch

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a circle");
    }
}

class Square extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a square");
    }
}
```

**Test driver:**
```java
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s1 = new Circle();
        Shape s2 = new Square();
        s1.draw();   // Which draw() executes?
        s2.draw();
    }
}
```

**Resolution analysis:**

The reference type is `Shape`, but the JVM examines the **actual object** at runtime.

$$ s_1.\text{draw}() \rightarrow \text{JVM checks runtime type of } s_1 = \text{Circle} \rightarrow \text{Circle's } draw() $$

$$ s_2.\text{draw}() \rightarrow \text{JVM checks runtime type of } s_2 = \text{Square} \rightarrow \text{Square's } draw() $$

**Output:**
```
Drawing a circle
Drawing a square
```

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Invoking Parent's Overridden Method via `super`

```java
class Employee {
    double calculateBonus(double salary) {
        return salary * 0.10;
    }
}

class Manager extends Employee {
    @Override
    double calculateBonus(double salary) {
        double baseBonus = super.calculateBonus(salary);
        return baseBonus + 5000;  // Manager gets extra incentive
    }
}
```

**Trace for `new Manager().calculateBonus(50000)`:**

$$ \text{baseBonus} = 50000 \times 0.10 = 5000 $$

$$ \text{Total Bonus} = 5000 + 5000 = 10000 $$

This demonstrates **method extension** — the child's override *augments* rather than *replaces* the parent's behavior.

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Combined Constructor Order + Method Overriding

```java
class Base {
    Base() {
        System.out.println("Base constructor");
        display();
    }
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Base display");
    }
}

class Derived extends Base {
    int value = 100;
    Derived() {
        System.out.println("Derived constructor, value = " + value);
    }
    @Override
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Derived display, value = " + value);
    }
}
```

**Trace for `new Derived()`:**

| Step | Action | Output |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | `Derived()` entered | — |
| 2 | Implicit `super()` → `Base()` invoked | — |
| 3 | `Base` body prints "Base constructor" | `Base constructor` |
| 4 | `Base()` calls `display()` — but actual object is `Derived` | `Derived display, value = 0` |
| 5 | `Base()` completes | — |
| 6 | `Derived` instance variables initialized (`value = 100`) | — |
| 7 | `Derived()` body executes | `Derived constructor, value = 100` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** At step 4, even though `value` is declared in `Derived`, it has **not yet been initialized** (default `0` for `int`). This is a classic "constructor trap" — calling overridable methods from a constructor can lead to **subtle bugs** because the child's fields are still at default values. The Java best practice is to call **only `final` or `private` methods** from constructors.

### 3.6 Mathematical Summary of the Constructor Chain

For a class hierarchy of depth $n$ (from `Object` to the most derived class `C_n`), the total number of constructor invocations during `new C_n()` is exactly:

$$ N_{\text{constructors}} = n $$

And the time complexity of the chain is:

$$ T_{\text{init}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T(C_i) = O(n) $$

where $T(C_i)$ is the execution time of constructor $C_i$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Constructor Calling Order — Block Architecture Flow


### 4.4 Method Overriding Rules — Decision Matrix

