<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the calling order of constructors in a multi-level inheritance hierarchy in Java. What happens if a subclass constructor does not explicitly call `super()`?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
In a multi-level inheritance hierarchy, when an object of the most derived class is created, constructors are invoked **from the topmost superclass (root) down to the derived class**. The JVM ensures that ancestor classes are fully initialized before the derived class's constructor body executes. If a subclass constructor does not explicitly invoke `super(...)` or `this(...)`, the Java compiler **automatically inserts a no-argument call to `super()`** as the first statement. This implicit call requires the superclass to have a visible no-argument constructor; otherwise, a **compilation error** occurs. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding. Which one is an example of runtime polymorphism?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Method **overloading** occurs within the **same class** (or subclass) when multiple methods share the **same name** but have **different parameter lists** (number, type, or order of arguments). It is resolved at **compile time** and is therefore *static polymorphism*. Method **overriding** occurs across a **superclass-subclass** relationship when the subclass provides a **specific implementation** for a method already declared in the parent, with an **identical signature**. The JVM decides which version to invoke at **runtime** based on the actual object type — this is **runtime (dynamic) polymorphism**, achieved through Dynamic Method Dispatch. (3 marks)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module 2 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Two alternatives (A and B) are provided for examination practice.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **method overriding** in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the rules that must be followed while overriding a method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the **calling order of constructors** in a three-level inheritance hierarchy (e.g., `GrandFather` → `Father` → `Child`). Show the output and explain the execution flow. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Method overriding is a runtime polymorphism feature wherein a subclass redefines a method inherited from its superclass with the **same name, same return type (or covariant), and same parameter list**.

```java
class Bank {
    double getRateOfInterest() {
        return 5.0;
    }
}
class SBI extends Bank {
    @Override
    double getRateOfInterest() {
        return 6.5;
    }
}
class ICICI extends Bank {
    @Override
    double getRateOfInterest() {
        return 7.0;
    }
}
```

*Rules:*
- Signature must match exactly.
- Access modifier cannot be more restrictive.
- Cannot override `final`, `static`, or `private` methods.
- Checked exceptions cannot be broader.
- Use of `@Override` annotation is recommended.

**(b)** Three-level hierarchy demonstration:

```java
class GrandFather {
    GrandFather() {
        System.out.println("GrandFather constructor");
    }
}
class Father extends GrandFather {
    Father() {
        System.out.println("Father constructor");
    }
}
class Child extends Father {
    Child() {
        System.out.println("Child constructor");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Child();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
GrandFather constructor
Father constructor
Child constructor
```

**Explanation:** When `new Child()` is executed, the JVM calls the `Child()` constructor. The compiler inserts an implicit `super()` call which invokes `Father()`. Similarly, `Father()` calls `super()` which invokes `GrandFather()`. Since `GrandFather` extends `Object` implicitly, `Object()` runs first. After `GrandFather`'s body completes, control returns to `Father`, and finally to `Child`. The order is **top-down (root → leaf)**. (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Adapted]**

**(a)** What is **dynamic method dispatch**? Illustrate with a Java program that uses a superclass reference to invoke an overridden method. Explain how the JVM decides which method to execute. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **constructor chaining** with the use of `super()` and `this()` keywords. Provide an example showing both calls and describe what happens if neither is explicitly written. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Dynamic Method Dispatch is the mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is resolved **at runtime** based on the **actual object type** (not the reference type).

```java
class Vehicle {
    void speed() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle speed varies");
    }
}
class Car extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void speed() {
        System.out.println("Car speed: 120 km/h");
    }
}
class Bike extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void speed() {
        System.out.println("Bike speed: 80 km/h");
    }
}
class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Vehicle v;
        v = new Car();
        v.speed();    // Car's speed()
        v = new Bike();
        v.speed();    // Bike's speed()
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Car speed: 120 km/h
Bike speed: 80 km/h
```

**Explanation:** The reference `v` is of type `Vehicle`, but the JVM examines the **runtime object** to determine which `speed()` method to invoke. When `v` points to a `Car` object, `Car.speed()` is called; when it points to a `Bike`, `Bike.speed()` is called. This enables **runtime polymorphism**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Constructor chaining is the process of calling one constructor from another constructor.

- `super(args)` — calls a parent class constructor. Must be the **first statement**.
- `this(args)` — calls another constructor in the **same** class. Must also be the **first statement**.

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int age;
    Student() {
        this("Unknown", 0);  // calls parameterized constructor
        System.out.println("Default constructor");
    }
    Student(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age = age;
        System.out.println("Parameterized: " + name + ", " + age);
    }
}
```

**Behavior when neither is written:** The compiler **automatically inserts a call to `super()`** (no-argument parent constructor) as the first statement of every constructor. If the parent class lacks a no-arg constructor, compilation fails. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting implicit `super()`:** Students often believe that if they don't write `super()`, nothing happens. In reality, the compiler *always* inserts a call — failure to realize this leads to errors in tracing outputs.
> 2. **Confusing overriding with overloading:** A common error is stating that overriding happens when parameter types differ — this is *overloading*, not overriding.
> 3. **Calling overridable methods from constructors:** This causes unexpected output (e.g., `value = 0` in Worked Example 5) and is a frequent bug source.
> 4. **Thinking `static` methods can be overridden:** They are *hidden*, not overridden — no runtime dispatch occurs.
> 5. **Access modifier violations:** Narrowing access in an override (e.g., `public` → `private`) is a compile error that students frequently overlook.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Constructor calling order is top-down:** From the root class (`Object`) to the most derived class.
- **Implicit `super()` rule:** The compiler always inserts a no-argument `super()` call unless you explicitly write `super(...)` or `this(...)` as the first statement.
- **First-statement rule:** `super()` or `this()` (if used) must be the **first executable statement** in any constructor.
- **Mutual exclusivity:** A constructor can call **either** `super()` or `this()` — never both.
- **Overriding enables runtime polymorphism:** It is bound at runtime via *Dynamic Method Dispatch* based on the actual object type.
- **Overriding signature must match exactly** — except for *covariant return types* and *narrower exception lists*.
- **Access modifiers in overriding** can be the same or wider, never narrower.
- **`final` and `static` methods cannot be properly overridden** (final = compile error; static = method hiding).
- **Use `@Override` annotation** to leverage compile-time validation.
- **Use `super.methodName()`** to explicitly call the parent's overridden method from the child.
- **Avoid calling overridable methods from constructors** — child fields may not yet be initialized.
- **Covariant return types** allow an override to return a subtype of the original return type (Java 5+).
- **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested:** Tracing output of multi-level inheritance programs combining both constructor order and method overriding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Method Dispatch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Method Dispatch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Method Dispatch** (also called **Runtime Polymorphism** or **Late Binding**) is the mechanism by which a single overridden method call, made through a **superclass reference variable** that holds a **subclass object**, is resolved at **runtime** (not at compile time) based on the **actual type of the object** being referred to, rather than the type of the reference variable.

In the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus, this concept is the **practical execution engine of polymorphism** in Java. It enables the JVM (Java Virtual Machine) to decide *which version* of an overridden method to invoke **after** the program has started executing, when the true class of the object becomes known.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Universal "START" Button**
> Imagine you are handed a single remote control with one button labelled **"START"**. You point it at:
> - A **Car** → The car engine starts.
> - A **Washing Machine** → A wash cycle starts.
> - A **Computer** → The operating system boots up.
>
> The **button** (reference) is the same, and the **action you perform** (method call `start()`) is identical — but the *actual outcome* depends on **what device the remote is currently pointing at** (the object type). This is exactly how Dynamic Method Dispatch works: **one reference, many behaviours, resolved at runtime**.

### 1.3 The Three Mandatory Ingredients

For Dynamic Method Dispatch to occur, **all three** of the following must be true:

1. **Inheritance** — A class hierarchy (parent → child) must exist.
2. **Method Overriding** — The subclass must redefine a method inherited from the superclass with the **exact same signature** (name + parameter list).
3. **Upcasting** — A superclass reference variable must refer to a subclass object (e.g., `Animal a = new Dog();`).

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **Binding Time**: **Runtime** (hence the name "Late Binding" / "Dynamic Binding")
- **Resolution Mechanism**: **JVM Method Table (vtable)** lookup
- **Default Binding in Java (non-static, non-final, non-private methods)**: **Dynamic**
- **Static / final / private methods**: Bound at **compile time** (Early Binding)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The dispatch process unfolds in a strict sequence:

- **Step 1 — Compilation Phase**
  The Java compiler sees a statement like `obj.display();`. It checks the **reference type** (compile-time type) only to confirm that *some* method called `display()` exists and is accessible. It does **not** decide which version to call. The compiler inserts a generic *runtime dispatch instruction* into the bytecode.

- **Step 2 — Class Loading Phase**
  When the JVM loads classes, it builds an internal **Method Table (vtable)** for each class. Each entry maps a method signature to the actual memory address of the implementation in that class (or its parent if not overridden).

- **Step 3 — Execution Phase (The "Dispatch")**
  When the line `obj.display();` is reached during execution, the JVM:
  1. Looks at the **object** the reference `obj` actually points to (its runtime type).
  2. Consults the vtable of that object's true class.
  3. Jumps to the memory address of the correct `display()` implementation.
  4. Executes that version.

- **Step 4 — Why This Matters**
  This allows **one interface (the superclass type) to control many implementations (subclass types)**, enabling loose coupling and the **Open/Closed Principle** of software design.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Syntax / Rule | Binding Time | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | Upcasting (Implicit) | `Super s = new Sub();` | Compile time allowed | Always safe; subclass IS-A superclass |
| 2 | Downcasting (Explicit) | `Sub s = (Sub) superRef;` | Requires `instanceof` check | Risky — `ClassCastException` if wrong |
| 3 | Dynamic Method Dispatch | `s.overriddenMethod();` | **Runtime** | Decided by object type, not reference type |
| 4 | Field Access (Not Methods) | `s.field;` | **Compile time** | Fields are NOT polymorphic in Java |
| 5 | `static` method call | `Super.staticMethod();` | **Compile time** | Static binding — no overriding, only hiding |
| 6 | `final` method call | `s.finalMethod();` | **Compile time** | Cannot be overridden; inlined by compiler |
| 7 | `private` method call | `s.privateMethod();` | **Compile time** | Not visible to subclass; no overriding |
| 8 | `instanceof` check | `s instanceof Sub` | Runtime | Returns `boolean`; safe-downcast guard |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Dynamic Method Dispatch is the **backbone of every Java framework**:
> - **Spring Framework** — Dependency injection resolves the actual bean type at runtime to invoke the right service.
> - **Android UI** — `onClick()` callbacks are dispatched dynamically to the correct view subclass.
> - **JDBC Drivers** — The `DriverManager` dispatches to Oracle/MySQL/PostgreSQL drivers dynamically.
> - **Strategy Design Pattern** — The classic "select algorithm at runtime" use case.
> - **Game Development** — A `GameObject` reference controls `Player`, `Enemy`, `PowerUp` behaviours uniformly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Exhaustive Code Walkthrough)

> Since PECMT535 is a **Computer Science** course, code snippets and algorithm traces are **explicitly permitted** and required by the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus.

### 3.1 Canonical Example — The KTU Favourite

```java
// File: DynamicDispatchDemo.java
class Vehicle {
    void run() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle is running");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void run() {
        System.out.println("Car is running safely at 100 km/h");
    }
}

class Bike extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void run() {
        System.out.println("Bike is running safely at 60 km/h");
    }
}

public class DynamicDispatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Vehicle v;          // Step A: superclass reference declared

        v = new Vehicle();  // Step B: points to Vehicle object
        v.run();            // -> "Vehicle is running"

        v = new Car();      // Step C: UPCAST — Vehicle ref, Car object
        v.run();            // -> "Car is running safely at 100 km/h"

        v = new Bike();     // Step D: UPCAST — Vehicle ref, Bike object
        v.run();            // -> "Bike is running safely at 60 km/h"
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Execution Trace — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

| Step | Line Executed | Reference Type | Object Type | vtable Lookup | Output |
|------|---------------|----------------|-------------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | `Vehicle v;` | `Vehicle` | `null` | — | (declaration only) |
| 2 | `v = new Vehicle();` | `Vehicle` | `Vehicle` | Vehicle → `run()` of Vehicle | — |
| 3 | `v.run();` | `Vehicle` | `Vehicle` | Vehicle table | `Vehicle is running` |
| 4 | `v = new Car();` | `Vehicle` | `Car` | (reference re-pointed) | — |
| 5 | `v.run();` | `Vehicle` | **`Car`** | **Car table** | `Car is running safely at 100 km/h` |
| 6 | `v = new Bike();` | `Vehicle` | `Bike` | (reference re-pointed) | — |
| 7 | `v.run();` | `Vehicle` | **`Bike`** | **Bike table** | `Bike is running safely at 60 km/h` |

### 3.3 Mathematical/Logical Deduction of the Dispatch Rule

Let:
- $R$ = reference type (compile-time)
- $O$ = object type (runtime)
- $M$ = method signature
- $V(C)$ = vtable of class $C$

The dispatch decision function $D$ can be formally expressed as:

$$
D(R, O, M) = \begin{cases} V(O).M & \text{if } M \text{ is overridden in } O \text{'s class hierarchy} \\ V(R).M & \text{if } M \text{ is not overridden} \\ \text{Compile Error} & \text{if } M \text{ does not exist in } R \end{cases}
$$

This means: **The JVM always searches starting from the object's true class $O$ and walks up the inheritance chain** until it finds the first concrete implementation of $M$. It does **not** start from $R$.

### 3.4 Pitfall Demonstration — What Dynamic Dispatch Does NOT Do

```java
class Parent {
    int value = 10;
    void show() { System.out.println("Parent show"); }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    int value = 20;             // HIDES, does not override
    @Override
    void show() { System.out.println("Child show"); }
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    Parent p = new Child();
    System.out.println(p.value);  // -> 10  (FIELDS use reference type)
    p.show();                     // -> "Child show" (METHODS use object type)
}
```

**Key Insight:** In Java, **fields are NOT polymorphic**. They are resolved at compile time by the reference type, while methods (that are overridden) are resolved at runtime by the object type.

### 3.5 Multi-Level Inheritance Dispatch

```java
class A { void msg() { System.out.println("A"); } }
class B extends A { @Override void msg() { System.out.println("B"); } }
class C extends B { @Override void msg() { System.out.println("C"); } }

// In main:
A ref = new C();
ref.msg();   // -> "C"  (JVM walks: C table -> found msg() -> invokes C's version)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The JVM does **not** stop at the reference type `A`. It starts at the **bottom of the hierarchy** (the actual object class `C`) and walks upward only if the method is not found.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Dynamic Method Dispatch Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Starts] --> B[Compile Time: Compiler verifies method exists in Reference Type]
    B --> C[Bytecode Generated with invokevirtual instruction]
    C --> D[Runtime: Object Created on Heap]
    D --> E[Reference Variable points to Object]
    E --> F{Method Call Encountered}
    F --> G[Extract Object's Runtime Class C]
    G --> H{Lookup M in C's vtable}
    H -->|Found| I[Invoke C's implementation of M]
    H -->|Not Found| J[Move to Parent Class in Hierarchy]
    J --> H
    I --> K[Return Result / Continue Execution]
    K --> L[Next Statement]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Class Hierarchy with Vtable Mapping

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Vehicle {
        +void run()
        <<vtable: Vehicle.run()>>
    }
    class Car {
        +void run()
        <<vtable: Car.run()>>
    }
    class Bike {
        +void run()
        <<vtable: Bike.run()>>
    }
    Vehicle <|-- Car
    Vehicle <|-- Bike

    note for Vehicle "run() points to Vehicle implementation"
    note for Car "run() OVERRIDDEN - points to Car implementation"
    note for Bike "run() OVERRIDDEN - points to Bike implementation"
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — JVM Dispatch Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Code: ref.method] --> COMP[Java Compiler]
    COMP --> BC[Bytecode: invokevirtual ref.method]
    BC --> CLS[JVM Class Loader]
    CLS --> VT[Build/Load vtables per class]
    VT --> EXEC[Execution Engine]
    EXEC --> OBJ[Identify Object's True Class]
    OBJ --> DISPATCH[vtable Lookup for method signature]
    DISPATCH --> JUMP[Jump to Method Address]
    JUMP --> RUN[Execute Overridden Method]
    RUN --> RET[Return to Caller]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Reference Type vs Object Type Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CompileTime[COMPILE TIME]
        CT1[Reference Type: Vehicle]
        CT2[Method existence check on Vehicle]
        CT3[Insert invokevirtual bytecode]
    end

    subgraph RunTime[RUN TIME]
        RT1[Object Created: new Car]
        RT2[Object Type Identified: Car]
        RT3[vtable lookup for run in Car]
        RT4[Invoke Car.run not Vehicle.run]
    end

    CT3 --> RT1
    RT4 --> OUT[Output: Car is running safely at 100 km/h]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Dynamic Method Dispatch. What are the conditions required for it to occur?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dynamic Method Dispatch is the mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is resolved at **runtime** based on the **actual type of the object** being referenced, not the type of the reference variable. The three conditions are: (i) **Inheritance** must exist between classes, (ii) The subclass must **override** the superclass method with the same signature, and (iii) The method must be called through a **superclass reference** that holds a subclass object (upcasting). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why are fields not resolved using dynamic dispatch in Java while methods are? Explain briefly.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In Java, fields are **not polymorphic** because they are resolved at **compile time** based on the reference type to maintain **memory efficiency and predictability**. Methods, on the other hand, are resolved at **runtime** to support **polymorphic behaviour** and the Open/Closed Principle. The JVM uses a vtable for methods, but field accesses are translated to direct memory offsets decided by the compiler. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Dynamic Method Dispatch in Java with a suitable example. Show how the JVM determines which method to invoke at runtime. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Dynamic Method Dispatch is the runtime resolution of an overridden method call based on the actual object type. It enables **runtime polymorphism** in Java.

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing Shape"); }
}
class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing Circle"); }
}
class Square extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing Square"); }
}
class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;
        s = new Circle(); s.draw();   // Circle version
        s = new Square(); s.draw();   // Square version
    }
}
```

**JVM Mechanism (Step-by-step):**
1. The compiler verifies `draw()` exists in class `Shape` and inserts an `invokevirtual` instruction.
2. At runtime, the JVM identifies the actual object class (e.g., `Circle`).
3. The JVM consults `Circle`'s **vtable**, finds the overridden `draw()` entry, and invokes **Circle's version**.
4. If the method were not overridden, the JVM would walk up the hierarchy until it found an implementation.

This is why the output is `Drawing Circle` and `Drawing Square` — not `Drawing Shape`. **(7 marks — 3 for concept + 2 for code + 2 for JVM mechanism)**

---

**(b)** Differentiate between **static binding** and **dynamic binding** in Java. Give one example for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Static Binding (Early Binding) | Dynamic Binding (Late Binding) |
|--------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| When resolved | At **compile time** | At **runtime** |
| Used for | `static`, `final`, `private` methods, fields, overloaded methods | Overridden instance methods |
| Mechanism | Direct method invocation by compiler | `invokevirtual` + vtable lookup |
| Polymorphism | **No** | **Yes** (achieves runtime polymorphism) |
| Performance | Slightly faster (no lookup overhead) | Slight overhead (vtable search) |

**Static Binding Example:**
```java
class MathOp {
    static int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }   // static -> early binding
}
// MathOp.add(5, 3);  // Compiler resolves directly to MathOp.add()
```

**Dynamic Binding Example:**
```java
class Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("..."); } }
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override void sound() { System.out.println("Bark"); }
}
Animal a = new Dog();
a.sound();   // Resolved at runtime to Dog.sound() -> prints "Bark"
```

Static binding uses **reference type**; dynamic binding uses **object type**. **(7 marks — 3 for table + 2+2 for examples)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style — ALTERNATIVE]**

**(a)** Write a Java program to demonstrate Dynamic Method Dispatch using a real-world scenario involving a banking system with `Account`, `SavingsAccount`, and `CurrentAccount` classes. Explain the output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
class Account {
    double balance = 0;
    void calculateInterest() {
        System.out.println("Generic account: no interest defined");
    }
}
class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    @Override
    void calculateInterest() {
        double interest = balance * 0.04;   // 4% for savings
        System.out.println("Savings Interest @ 4%: Rs. " + interest);
    }
}
class CurrentAccount extends Account {
    @Override
    void calculateInterest() {
        double interest = balance * 0.02;   // 2% for current
        System.out.println("Current Interest @ 2%: Rs. " + interest);
    }
}
public class BankDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account acc;                          // superclass reference
        acc = new SavingsAccount();
        acc.balance = 10000;
        acc.calculateInterest();              // -> Savings Interest

        acc = new CurrentAccount();
        acc.balance = 10000;
        acc.calculateInterest();              // -> Current Interest
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Savings Interest @ 4%: Rs. 400.0
Current Interest @ 2%: Rs. 200.0
```

**Explanation:** The reference `acc` is of type `Account`, but each call to `calculateInterest()` dispatches to the **subclass's overridden version** because the JVM looks at the actual object pointed to. This is dynamic method dispatch in action — one uniform interface (`Account`) controlling multiple implementations. **(7 marks — 4 for code, 1 for output, 2 for explanation)**

---

**(b)** Explain why the `final`, `static`, and `private` methods cannot participate in dynamic method dispatch. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **`final` methods** (3 marks):
   The `final` keyword prevents a method from being overridden in any subclass. Since overriding is a **mandatory precondition** for dynamic dispatch, and a final method has only one implementation (in the declaring class), there is no ambiguity to resolve. The compiler can safely bind the call at compile time, often **inlining** the method for performance. Example: `final void display() { }` in class `A` cannot be redefined in class `B`, so `A obj = new B(); obj.display();` always calls `A.display()`.

2. **`static` methods** (2 marks):
   Static methods belong to the **class**, not to any object. They are resolved based on the reference type (or class name) at compile time. Subclass can only **hide** a static method, not override it. Example: `Parent.show()` and `Child.show()` are two distinct methods, and the call is bound at compile time.

3. **`private` methods** (2 marks):
   Private methods are **not inherited** by subclasses, so they cannot be overridden. Since dynamic dispatch depends on inheritance and overriding, private methods are bound at compile time and called through **early binding**. Any attempt to create a "matching" method in a subclass is treated as a new independent method, not an override.

Together, these three modifiers tell the compiler: *"I am certain of the implementation; resolve the call at compile time."* **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Overloading with Overriding** — Overloading is **static binding** (compile-time); only **overriding** triggers dynamic dispatch. Students often lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting the `super` reference in constructor calls** — Constructor chaining uses `super()` which is **static** binding, not dynamic.
> 3. **Assuming fields are polymorphic** — `Parent p = new Child(); p.field;` accesses the **Parent's** field, not Child's. KTU frequently tests this.
> 4. **Not mentioning the vtable / method table** — Examiners expect at least a one-line mention of the JVM's internal mechanism for full marks.
> 5. **Writing code without `@Override` annotation** — While optional, KTU evaluators appreciate it for clarity.
> 6. **Casting without `instanceof`** — Leads to `ClassCastException`; always validate before downcasting.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: Dynamic Method Dispatch = **runtime resolution** of **overridden** method calls based on the **object's actual type**, not the reference type.
- **Also Known As**: Runtime Polymorphism, Late Binding, Dynamic Binding.
- **Three Pre-requisites**: Inheritance + Method Overriding + Upcasting.
- **Java's Rule**: Non-static, non-final, non-private, non-constructive methods are dispatched dynamically.
- **Fields are NOT polymorphic** — they are resolved at compile time by reference type.
- **Constructors are NOT polymorphic** — they cannot be overridden (only chained via `super()`).
- **JVM Internals**: The `invokevirtual` bytecode instruction consults the class's **vtable** to find the correct method address.
- **Mechanism**: JVM searches from the **object's runtime class** and walks **up the inheritance chain** until it finds a concrete implementation.
- **Exception Safety**: Downcasting requires an `instanceof` check; otherwise `ClassCastException` is thrown.
- **Performance Trade-off**: Dynamic dispatch has a slight runtime overhead (vtable lookup) compared to static binding.
- **Design Significance**: It is the **practical realisation of the Open/Closed Principle** — code is open for extension (new subclasses) but closed for modification (existing code unchanged).
- **Real-World Use Cases**: Strategy Pattern, Factory Pattern, Frameworks (Spring, Android), JDBC drivers, Game engines.
- **KTU 2024 Weightage**: Frequently asked in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (7–14 marks) every semester.
- **One-line Mantra for Exam**: *"Reference decides what is callable; Object decides what actually runs."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Final with Inheritance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Using Final with Inheritance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Java Object Model** and **Inheritance Hierarchy**, the keyword `final` is a **non-access modifier** used to enforce restrictions at three distinct levels of the class structure. When applied in combination with inheritance, the `final` keyword serves as a **compile-time enforcement mechanism** that prevents further extension, modification, or reassignment of class members, thereby guaranteeing **immutability**, **security**, and **design integrity** in an Object-Oriented Program.

The three canonical applications of `final` within an inheritance framework are:

1. **`final` Class** — A class declared with the `final` modifier **cannot be inherited (extended) by any subclass**. It represents a closed, leaf-level node in the inheritance tree.
2. **`final` Method** — A method declared with the `final` modifier **cannot be overridden** by any subclass, even if the class itself is inheritable.
3. **`final` Variable** — A variable declared with the `final` modifier becomes a **constant**, meaning its value can be assigned exactly **once** and cannot be changed thereafter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Highlight:** The `final` keyword is a **favorite 3-mark and 14-mark topic** in Part A and Part B sections. The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between the three usages and provide **valid Java code demonstrations** of each.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are the **architect of a grand library system**:

- **Final Class = A Sealed Vault:** Think of the `String` class in Java. It is declared `final` because the designers (Sun Microsystems/Oracle) do **not** want anyone to extend it and tamper with how strings behave. Once sealed, the vault is locked — no one can build a room on top of it. This protects billions of lines of code that depend on `String`.

- **Final Method = A Locked Door with a Fixed Lock:** If a parent class provides a `calculateTax()` method, marking it `final` means every child class **must use this exact implementation**. The children can add new rooms (new methods), but they cannot replace this critical door. It guarantees that a critical algorithm (like security checks) is never broken by a careless subclass.

- **Final Variable = A Permanent Engraving on a Stone Tablet:** A `final int MAX_SPEED = 120;` is like carving "120" into marble. Once carved, the value is set forever. The compiler itself will reject any attempt to alter it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Java have `final`?** Because OOP inheritance is powerful but dangerous. Without restrictions, a poorly written subclass could break the behavior of a perfectly designed parent class. `final` gives the original class designer the **"veto power"** to say: *"Stop here, no more changes allowed."*

## 1.3 Tabular Snapshot of the Three Usages

| **Usage** | **Declaration** | **Restriction Imposed** | **Real-World Java Example** |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|
| `final` Class | `final class A { }` | Cannot be extended (no subclass) | `java.lang.String`, `java.lang.Math`, `java.lang.Integer` (Wrapper) |
| `final` Method | `final void show() { }` | Cannot be overridden in subclass | `Object.getClass()`, `Thread.start()` |
| `final` Variable | `final int X = 10;` | Value cannot be reassigned | `Math.PI`, `Integer.MAX_VALUE` |

> [!TIP]
> The Java standard library uses `final` extensively. Knowing these examples (`String`, `Math.PI`, `Integer.MAX_VALUE`) often scores **bonus cognitive points** in KTU viva-voce and Part A answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Three Usages

### 2.1.1 `final` Class — The Inheritable Boundary

When a class is declared `final`, the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM) at compile time** rejects any `extends` clause that attempts to inherit from it.

**Declaration Syntax:**
```java
final class Vehicle {
    void start() { System.out.println("Vehicle starts"); }
}

// The following line will cause a COMPILE-TIME ERROR:
// class Car extends Vehicle { }   // ERROR: cannot inherit from final class
```

**Why use it? (KTU-favored "Why" question):**
- **Security:** Prevents malicious subclasses from altering sensitive behavior (e.g., the `String` class is `final` to prevent exploitation of string-handling in security-sensitive code).
- **Immutability:** Wrapper classes (`Integer`, `Float`, `Double`) are `final` to maintain their immutable nature.
- **Performance:** The JVM can perform certain optimizations (like inlining) when it knows a class has no subclasses.
- **Design Invariant:** Enforces the design contract that the class is complete and should not be extended.

### 2.1.2 `final` Method — The Overriding Lock

A `final` method **can be inherited** by a subclass (since the class itself is not final), but the subclass **cannot provide a new implementation** for it. This is the "lock the algorithm" pattern.

**Declaration Syntax:**
```java
class BankAccount {
    final double calculateInterest(double principal, double rate, int years) {
        return principal * rate * years;
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    // ERROR: cannot override the final method
    // double calculateInterest(double p, double r, int y) { return p * r * y / 2; }
}
```

**Why use it?**
- To guarantee that a critical algorithm (e.g., interest calculation, tax computation, license validation) is **never altered** by subclasses.
- A common KTU interview question: *"Can we overload a `final` method?"* — **YES, overloading is allowed** because overloading happens within the same class and does not involve inheritance/overriding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A `final` method **CANNOT be overridden**, but it **CAN be hidden** if it is also `static`, and it **CAN be hidden/overridden by a stronger access modifier** scenario is illegal — you can never reduce visibility when overriding.

### 2.1.3 `final` Variable — The Constant Enforcer

A variable marked `final` becomes a **constant**. The Java compiler enforces the **"definite assignment before use"** rule, meaning the variable must be initialized exactly once before any code reads it.

**Three sub-categories exist:**

| **Sub-type** | **Initialization Rule** | **Example** |
|--------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| `final` instance variable | Must be initialized in **every constructor** OR at the **declaration site** (inline). | `final int ID;` |
| `final` static variable | Must be initialized in a **static initializer block** OR at the **declaration site** (inline). | `static final double PI = 3.14;` |
| `final` local variable | Must be initialized **before first use** in the method. | `final int x = 5;` |

**Blank Final Variable:** A `final` variable declared **without initialization** is called a *blank final variable*. It must be initialized in **all constructors** of the class (for instance) or in a static block (for static).

```java
class Student {
    final int rollNo;       // blank final
    final String college = "KTU";  // initialized at declaration
    
    Student(int r) {
        rollNo = r;  // must be assigned in constructor
    }
}
```

**`final` Parameters:** Method parameters can be `final`, meaning the method cannot reassign them.

```java
void process(final int data) {
    // data = 10;  // ERROR: cannot reassign final parameter
    System.out.println(data);
}
```

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Quick Reference Table)

| **Feature** | **`final` Class** | **`final` Method** | **`final` Variable** |
|-------------|-------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Keyword placement | Before `class` keyword | Before return type | Before data type |
| Inheritance allowed? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (can be inherited) | N/A |
| Overriding allowed? | N/A | ❌ No | N/A |
| Reassignment allowed? | N/A | N/A | ❌ No |
| Can be `abstract`? | ❌ No (abstract + final is illegal) | ❌ No | N/A |
| JVM optimization? | Yes (inlining possible) | Yes (inlining possible) | Yes (treated as constant) |
| Real Java example | `java.lang.String` | `Object.getClass()` | `Math.PI` |

## 2.3 Common Combinations and Their Legality

| **Combination** | **Legal?** | **Reason** |
|-----------------|------------|------------|
| `final` + `abstract` class | ❌ Illegal | `abstract` means "must extend"; `final` means "cannot extend" — contradiction. |
| `final` + `static` method | ✅ Legal | A `final static` method is a class-level constant method that cannot be hidden. |
| `final` + method with `private` access | ✅ Legal | `private` methods are already implicitly `final` (cannot be overridden as they are not inherited visibly). |
| `final` + constructor | ❌ Illegal | Constructors are never inherited, so `final` is meaningless. |
| `final` + interface (in older Java) | ❌ Illegal | Interfaces were implicitly `abstract`. (Java 8+ allows `final` only in interface methods with default body.) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **production-grade software engineering**, `final` is used pervasively:

- **Banking Software:** The `calculateEMI()` method is `final` to prevent any subclass from altering the financial formula.
- **Security Frameworks:** Authentication methods like `validateToken()` are `final` to prevent hackers from exploiting overridden versions.
- **Constants in Physics/Engineering Apps:** `final double SPEED_OF_LIGHT = 299792458;` ensures the value is never accidentally modified.
- **API Design:** Classes like `java.lang.String` are `final` to maintain backward compatibility — every Java program in the world depends on `String` behaving a specific way.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> Since this topic is **conceptual and code-based** (Object-Oriented Programming), the "derivations" here refer to **logical flow derivations**, **compile-time error predictions**, and **step-by-step code analysis** that KTU examiners expect students to demonstrate on paper.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Predicting Compile-Time Behavior

The KTU board often tests whether a student can **dry-run** a Java code snippet involving `final` and inheritance. Below is a step-by-step analytical framework:

### **Case A: `final` Class — Step-by-Step Analysis**

**Given Code:**
```java
final class Animal {
    void sound() { System.out.println("Generic sound"); }
}

class Dog extends Animal {  // LINE X
    void sound() { System.out.println("Bark"); }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d = new Dog();
        d.sound();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Analysis:**

| **Step** | **Compiler Action** | **Result** |
|----------|---------------------|------------|
| Step 1 | Compiler encounters `final class Animal` | Marks `Animal` as a **sealed, non-extendable** class in the symbol table. |
| Step 2 | Compiler reaches `class Dog extends Animal` (Line X) | Checks the modifier of `Animal`. Sees `final`. **REJECTS** the inheritance. |
| Step 3 | Compiler emits error: *"cannot inherit from final Animal"* | **Compilation fails.** Program never runs. |
| Step 4 | `main` method never executes | **No output produced.** |

> **Examiner's Expected Answer:** *"Compilation error at Line X: cannot inherit from final class Animal."*

### **Case B: `final` Method — Step-by-Step Analysis**

**Given Code:**
```java
class Parent {
    final void display() { System.out.println("Parent display"); }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    void display() { System.out.println("Child display"); }  // LINE Y
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Child c = new Child();
        c.display();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Analysis:**

| **Step** | **Compiler Action** | **Result** |
|----------|---------------------|------------|
| Step 1 | `Parent` is non-final, so `Child` can extend it. | ✅ Inheritance allowed. |
| Step 2 | `Child` inherits `display()` from `Parent`. | `display()` is in `Child`'s symbol table. |
| Step 3 | `Child` attempts to redefine `display()`. | Compiler checks: is `display()` in `Parent` declared `final`? **YES.** |
| Step 4 | Compiler emits error: *"display() in Child cannot override display() in Parent; overridden method is final"*. | **Compilation fails at Line Y.** |
| Step 5 | Program never runs. | **No output.** |

### **Case C: `final` Variable — Step-by-Step Analysis**

**Given Code:**
```java
class Circle {
    final double PI = 3.14159;
    
    double area(double r) {
        PI = 3.14;  // LINE Z
        return PI * r * r;
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Analysis:**

| **Step** | **Compiler Action** | **Result** |
|----------|---------------------|------------|
| Step 1 | Compiler sees `final double PI = 3.14159;` | Marks `PI` as a read-only constant. |
| Step 2 | Inside `area()`, compiler reaches `PI = 3.14;` (Line Z). | Detects an **assignment to a final variable**. |
| Step 3 | Compiler emits error: *"cannot assign a value to final variable PI"*. | **Compilation fails at Line Z.** |

## 3.2 Blank Final Variable — Initialization Analysis

**Given Code:**
```java
class Employee {
    final int empId;        // blank final
    final String dept = "CSE";  // initialized at declaration
    
    Employee(int id) {
        empId = id;  // OK: assigned in constructor
    }
    
    Employee(int id, String dummy) {  // second constructor
        // ERROR: empId might not have been initialized in this path
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:**

$$
\text{For every constructor path: } \text{blank final} \xrightarrow{\text{must be assigned exactly once}} \text{before constructor completes}
$$

The Java compiler performs **definite assignment analysis** on every constructor:

- **Constructor 1 (`Employee(int id)`):** Compiler traces the path → `empId = id;` is executed → **definitely assigned** → ✅ Legal.
- **Constructor 2 (`Employee(int id, String dummy)`):** Compiler traces the path → no assignment to `empId` found → **blank final may be unassigned** → ❌ **Compile-time error.**

> **Conclusion:** Every constructor of a class with a blank final instance variable must either initialize it explicitly, or delegate to another constructor using `this(...)` that does so.

## 3.3 Final with Static — Combined Analysis

**Given Code:**
```java
class Config {
    static final int MAX_USERS = 100;   // compile-time constant
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(Config.MAX_USERS);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:**

$$
\text{Compile-time constant property: } \texttt{static final} \text{ primitive} + \text{initial value} \implies \text{value is inlined at compile time}
$$

| **Step** | **Compiler Action** | **Result** |
|----------|---------------------|------------|
| Step 1 | Sees `static final int MAX_USERS = 100;` | Marks it as a **compile-time constant**. |
| Step 2 | Replaces all references to `MAX_USERS` in the bytecode with the literal `100`. | Direct value inlining. |
| Step 3 | At runtime, no field lookup is needed. | **Faster execution.** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inheritance Hierarchy Showing the Effect of `final` Class

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a `final` class terminates the inheritance chain, while non-final classes allow further extension.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Object Root Class"] --> B["Vehicle Class"]
    B --> C["Car Class"]
    B --> D["final String Class LOCKED"]
    B --> E["final Math Class LOCKED"]
    B --> F["final Integer Class LOCKED"]
    C --> G["Sedan Class"]
    C --> H["SUV Class"]
    
    D:::finalClass
    E:::finalClass
    F:::finalClass
    
    classDef finalClass fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- `Vehicle` (non-final) can be extended into `Car`, which can further be extended into `Sedan` and `SUV`.
- `String`, `Math`, and `Integer` are marked `final` — they **cannot** have any subclasses. The chain stops at them.
- This is why you can never write `class MyString extends String` — the compiler will reject it.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: How `final` Enforces Restrictions at Compile Time

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Java Source Code: .java file"] --> B["Java Compiler: javac"]
    B --> C{"Modifier Check Phase"}
    
    C --> D["Class declared final?"]
    D -->|YES| D1["Mark class as SEALED in symbol table"]
    D -->|NO| D2["Allow inheritance normally"]
    
    C --> E["Method declared final?"]
    E -->|YES| E1["Lock method signature against override"]
    E -->|NO| E2["Allow override in subclasses"]
    
    C --> F["Variable declared final?"]
    F -->|YES| F1["Track initialization: must be assigned exactly once"]
    F -->|NO| F2["Allow reassignment normally"]
    
    D1 --> G["Bytecode Generation: .class file"]
    D2 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    
    G --> H["Runtime: JVM Executes"]
    
    style D1 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style F1 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#51cf66,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#339af0,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Architecture Diagram: `final` in a Real Banking Application

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PARENT["Parent Class: BankAccount"]
        PA1["deposit() - normal method"]
        PA2["withdraw() - normal method"]
        PA3["calculateInterest() - FINAL METHOD"]
        PA4["validatePIN() - FINAL METHOD"]
    end
    
    subgraph CHILD["Child Class: SavingsAccount extends BankAccount"]
        CA1["addBonus() - new method"]
        CA2["calculateInterest() - CANNOT OVERRIDE"]
        CA3["validatePIN() - CANNOT OVERRIDE"]
    end
    
    subgraph SEALED["Sealed Class: TransactionRecord FINAL CLASS"]
        TR1["amount field"]
        TR2["timestamp field"]
        TR3["NO SUBCLASSES ALLOWED"]
    end
    
    PA3 -.->|inherited as-is| CA2
    PA4 -.->|inherited as-is| CA3
    
    style PA3 fill:#ffd43b,color:#000000
    style PA4 fill:#ffd43b,color:#000000
    style CA2 fill:#ff8787,color:#ffffff
    style CA3 fill:#ff8787,color:#ffffff
    style SEALED fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style TR3 fill:#c92a2a,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The yellow `final` methods in `BankAccount` (like `calculateInterest`) are **inherited by `SavingsAccount` but cannot be modified**.
- The red `TransactionRecord` class is a `final` class — no class can extend it, preserving the integrity of transaction data.

## 4.4 Decision Matrix: When to Use `final`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Need to restrict something?"] --> Q1{"What do you want to lock?"}
    
    Q1 -->|Lock the entire class| A1["Use 'final class'"]
    Q1 -->|Lock a specific method| A2["Use 'final method'"]
    Q1 -->|Lock a variable value| A3["Use 'final variable'"]
    
    A1 --> A1A["Examples: String, Math, Wrapper classes"]
    A1A --> A1B["Prevents ALL inheritance"]
    
    A2 --> A2A["Use for: critical algorithms, security checks"]
    A2A --> A2B["Allows inheritance but blocks overriding"]
    
    A3 --> A3A["Use for: constants like PI, MAX_SIZE, CONFIG"]
    A3A --> A3B["Value assigned once, never changed"]
    
    style START fill:#339af0,color:#ffffff
    style A1 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style A2 fill:#ffd43b,color:#000000
    style A3 fill:#51cf66,color:#ffffff
```
