# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Short Answer** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the use of the `final` keyword with classes in Java. Why is the `String` class declared as `final`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When a class is declared with the `final` modifier in Java, it **cannot be inherited** by any other class. That is, no subclass can use the `extends` keyword with a `final` class. The Java compiler enforces this restriction at compile time.

**Syntax:**
```java
final class MyClass {
    // class body
}
```

The `String` class in Java is declared as `final` (`public final class String`) for the following important reasons:
1. **Security:** Strings are used in network connections, file paths, database queries, and class loading. If `String` were inheritable, a malicious subclass could alter string behavior, leading to security exploits.
2. **Immutability:** `String` objects are immutable. Making the class `final` ensures no subclass can break this immutability guarantee.
3. **Performance:** The JVM can optimize `String` operations (like String interning and concatenation) more aggressively when it knows no subclass exists.
4. **Design Stability:** The Java language designers wanted to ensure that `String` behaves consistently across all Java applications in the world.

Other examples of `final` classes in Java include `java.lang.Math`, `java.lang.Integer`, `java.lang.Double`, and all wrapper classes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + `String` justification with at least 2 valid reasons (1.5 marks) + Example (0.5 marks) = **3 marks**.

---

### **Question 2: Short Answer** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between `final`, `finally`, and `finalize` in Java. Can a `final` method be overloaded?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Keyword** | **Type** | **Purpose** | **Example** |
|-------------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `final` | Non-access modifier | Restricts class inheritance, method overriding, variable reassignment | `final int x = 10;` |
| `finally` | Block | Code block that always executes after a `try-catch`, used for cleanup (e.g., closing files) | `try { } finally { }` |
| `finalize` | Method | Called by the **Garbage Collector** before destroying an object; used for cleanup of resources | `protected void finalize() { }` |

**Can a `final` method be overloaded?**

**Yes**, a `final` method **can be overloaded** within the same class. Overloading is a **compile-time polymorphism** mechanism that depends on the method signature (name + parameter list), not on inheritance. The `final` keyword only restricts **overriding** (runtime polymorphism in a subclass), not overloading. For example:

```java
class Demo {
    final void show(int x) { System.out.println("int: " + x); }
    final void show(String s) { System.out.println("String: " + s); }  // valid overloading
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Three-way table (1.5 marks) + `final` method overloading explanation with code (1.5 marks) = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the three uses of the `final` keyword in Java with suitable code examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of a blank final variable and a final class. Justify why the `String` class in Java is declared as `final`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The `final` keyword in Java is a **non-access modifier** that can be applied to classes, methods, and variables. Each application serves a specific purpose in object-oriented design.

**1. `final` Class:**

When a class is declared `final`, it **cannot be inherited** by any subclass. The compiler rejects any attempt to extend a `final` class.

```java
final class MathConstants {
    static final double PI = 3.14159;
}

// The following line causes a compile-time error:
// class AdvancedMath extends MathConstants { }   // ERROR: cannot inherit from final
```

This is used to prevent inheritance when the class represents a complete, immutable entity (e.g., `String`, `Math`, wrapper classes).

**2. `final` Method:**

A `final` method **can be inherited** but **cannot be overridden** by any subclass. This ensures that a critical implementation is preserved across the inheritance hierarchy.

```java
class BankAccount {
    final double calculateInterest(double principal, double rate, int years) {
        return principal * rate * years / 100;
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    // ERROR: cannot override the final method from BankAccount
    // double calculateInterest(double p, double r, int y) { return 0; }
}
```

The `final` method can still be **overloaded** within the same class since overloading does not involve inheritance.

**3. `final` Variable:**

A `final` variable is a **constant** that can be assigned **only once**. After initialization, its value cannot be changed.

```java
class Circle {
    final double PI = 3.14159;   // initialized at declaration
    
    double area(double radius) {
        return PI * radius * radius;
    }
}
```

For **blank final variables** (declared but not initialized), the value must be assigned in **every constructor**:

```java
class Student {
    final int rollNo;   // blank final
    
    Student(int r) {
        rollNo = r;     // must be assigned in constructor
    }
}
```

A `final` parameter cannot be reassigned inside a method:
```java
void process(final int x) {
    // x = 10;  // ERROR
}
```

**Summary Table:**

| **Usage** | **Effect** | **Example** |
|-----------|------------|-------------|
| `final` class | Cannot be inherited | `String` |
| `final` method | Cannot be overridden | `Object.getClass()` |
| `final` variable | Cannot be reassigned | `Math.PI` |

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Java Program Demonstrating Blank Final Variable and Final Class:**

```java
// File: FinalDemo.java

// (1) Demonstrating a blank final variable
class Student {
    final int rollNo;         // blank final - not initialized here
    final String name;        // blank final
    static final String COLLEGE;  // blank static final
    
    static {
        COLLEGE = "APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University";  // static initializer
    }
    
    Student(int r, String n) {
        rollNo = r;          // assigned in constructor
        name = n;            // assigned in constructor
    }
    
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Roll No: " + rollNo);
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);
        System.out.println("College: " + COLLEGE);
    }
}

// (2) Demonstrating a final class
final class ImmutablePoint {
    private final int x;
    private final int y;
    
    ImmutablePoint(int x, int y) {
        this.x = x;
        this.y = y;
    }
    
    int getX() { return x; }
    int getY() { return y; }
}

// (3) The following class WILL NOT COMPILE (showing the restriction):
// class ExtendedPoint extends ImmutablePoint { }  // ERROR: cannot inherit from final

public class FinalDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student(101, "Arjun");
        s1.display();
        
        ImmutablePoint p = new ImmutablePoint(10, 20);
        System.out.println("Point: (" + p.getX() + ", " + p.getY() + ")");
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Roll No: 101
Name: Arjun
College: APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University
Point: (10, 20)
```

**Why is the `String` class declared as `final`? (Justification):**

The `java.lang.String` class is declared as `public final class String` in Java for the following critical reasons:

1. **Security:** Strings are heavily used in Java for sensitive operations such as class loading (`Class.forName()`), network connections (`java.net.URL`), file paths, and database connections. If `String` were not `final`, a malicious subclass could override the `equals()` or `hashCode()` methods, leading to severe security vulnerabilities. The `final` modifier prevents such exploitation.

2. **Immutability Guarantee:** The `String` class is designed to be **immutable** — once a `String` object is created, its value cannot be changed. This immutability is fundamental to Java's design (e.g., String interning, String constants in the String Pool, use of strings as keys in `HashMap`). Making the class `final` ensures that no subclass can break this immutability contract.

3. **Performance Optimization:** The JVM and JIT compiler perform several optimizations based on the knowledge that `String` has no subclasses. For example, the **String Constant Pool** (a special memory area in the heap) can safely store and reuse string literals, and the compiler can inline `String` methods for faster execution.

4. **Thread Safety:** Since `String` is immutable and `final`, `String` objects are inherently **thread-safe** and can be shared across multiple threads without synchronization.

5. **Design Stability:** `String` is one of the most frequently used classes in Java. Making it `final` ensures backward compatibility — billions of lines of existing code rely on `String` behaving in a specific, predictable way.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** What is the significance of the `final` keyword when applied to methods in Java? Explain with a suitable example. Can a `final` method be inherited? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that uses the `final` keyword with variables, methods, and classes together. Explain the output and any compile-time errors that may arise. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Significance of `final` Method:**

When a method in Java is declared with the `final` modifier, it serves as an **inheritance-lock**. This means:

1. The method **can be inherited** by subclasses (it becomes a part of the subclass's API).
2. The method **cannot be overridden** (redefined) by any subclass.
3. The original implementation provided in the parent class is **guaranteed to be the version executed**, no matter what type of reference is used.

**When to use `final` methods:**
- When the method contains a **critical algorithm** (e.g., security validation, financial calculation) that must not be altered.
- When extending a third-party library and the design contract requires that specific methods remain unchanged.
- For **template method design pattern**, where the skeleton of an algorithm is in the parent class and only specific steps are left for subclasses to implement.

**Example Program:**

```java
class LicenseValidator {
    final boolean validate(String licenseKey) {
        if (licenseKey == null || licenseKey.length() != 16) {
            return false;
        }
        // Complex validation logic that must NEVER be altered
        int sum = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < licenseKey.length(); i++) {
            sum += licenseKey.charAt(i);
        }
        return (sum % 7 == 0);
    }
}

class PremiumValidator extends LicenseValidator {
    // This will cause a COMPILE-TIME ERROR:
    // boolean validate(String licenseKey) { return true; }
    // ERROR: validate() in PremiumValidator cannot override validate() in LicenseValidator;
    //        overridden method is final
}

public class FinalMethodDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LicenseValidator validator = new LicenseValidator();
        boolean result = validator.validate("ABCD1234EFGH5678");
        System.out.println("Validation result: " + result);
    }
}
```

**Can a `final` method be inherited? — YES**

A `final` method **can be inherited** by a subclass. The `final` keyword does not prevent inheritance; it only prevents **overriding**. The subclass receives the `final` method from its parent and can **call it** but cannot **redefine** it.

**Justification with another example:**

```java
class Animal {
    final void breathe() {
        System.out.println("Animal is breathing");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    void bark() {
        System.out.println("Dog is barking");
    }
    
    // breathe() is inherited from Animal and can be called
    void live() {
        breathe();  // VALID: calling inherited final method
        bark();
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d = new Dog();
        d.live();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Animal is breathing
Dog is barking
```

This proves that `breathe()` is successfully inherited by `Dog` and is being used — but `Dog` cannot provide its own version of `breathe()`.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Java Program Using `final` with Variables, Methods, and Classes Together:**

```java
// (1) Final class
final class DatabaseConfig {
    private final String url;
    private final int port;
    static final String DEFAULT_PORT = "3306";
    
    DatabaseConfig(String url, int port) {
        this.url = url;
        this.port = port;
    }
    
    // (2) Final method - cannot be overridden
    final String getConnectionString() {
        return url + ":" + port;
    }
    
    String getUrl() { return url; }
    int getPort() { return port; }
}

// (3) The following class WILL NOT COMPILE - shows final class restriction
// class CustomConfig extends DatabaseConfig {  // ERROR
//     String getConnectionString() { return "custom"; }
// }

public class FinalAllDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // (4) Final local variable
        final int MAX_CONNECTIONS = 5;
        
        DatabaseConfig config = new DatabaseConfig("jdbc:mysql://localhost", 3306);
        
        System.out.println("URL: " + config.getUrl());
        System.out.println("Port: " + config.getPort());
        System.out.println("Connection: " + config.getConnectionString());
        System.out.println("Default Port: " + DatabaseConfig.DEFAULT_PORT);
        System.out.println("Max Connections: " + MAX_CONNECTIONS);
        
        // MAX_CONNECTIONS = 10;  // ERROR: cannot assign a value to final variable
        
        // Calling the final method through inheritance is fine if class was not final
        // Since DatabaseConfig is final, no subclass can call its method differently
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
URL: jdbc:mysql://localhost
Port: 3306
Connection: jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306
Default Port: 3306
Max Connections: 5
```

**Explanation of Compile-Time Errors:**

1. **`extends DatabaseConfig` in another class** would cause the error: *"cannot inherit from final DatabaseConfig"* because the class is declared `final`.
2. **Overriding `getConnectionString()` in any subclass** would cause: *"getConnectionString() cannot override getConnectionString() in DatabaseConfig; overridden method is final"*.
3. **`MAX_CONNECTIONS = 10;`** would cause: *"cannot assign a value to final variable MAX_CONNECTIONS"*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often confuse `final` with `finally` and `finalize`. Ensure you clearly differentiate these in your answer.
> 2. Many students incorrectly state that a `final` method **cannot be inherited**. The correct statement is: a `final` method **CAN be inherited but CANNOT be overridden**.
> 3. Do **NOT** declare a class as both `abstract` and `final` — these are contradictory (compile error).
> 4. For blank final variables, you must assign the value in **every constructor** of the class, not just one. Missing this often costs **2 marks** in 14-mark answers.
> 5. A `final` method **can be overloaded** (same class, different parameters) but **cannot be overridden** (subclass, same signature). Mixing these up is a frequent losing point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ The `final` keyword in Java is a **non-access modifier** used to impose restrictions on **class, method, and variable**.
- ✅ A **`final` class** cannot be inherited. Examples: `String`, `Math`, `Integer`, `Double`, `Long`, `Boolean`.
- ✅ A **`final` method** can be **inherited** but **cannot be overridden**. It **can be overloaded** within the same class.
- ✅ A **`final` variable** becomes a **constant** — it can be assigned only once.
- ✅ **Blank final variable** = a `final` variable declared without initialization. Must be initialized in **every constructor** (for instance) or in a **static block** (for static).
- ✅ A **`final` parameter** cannot be reassigned inside the method body.
- ✅ Combining `final` + `abstract` (for class) is **illegal** — they are contradictory.
- ✅ The `String` class is `final` for **security, immutability, performance, and thread-safety** reasons.
- ✅ The `Math` class is `final` and its constructor is `private` — it cannot be instantiated, only used via static methods.
- ✅ Wrapper classes (`Integer`, `Float`, `Double`, `Long`, `Short`, `Byte`, `Character`, `Boolean`) are all `final` and **immutable**.
- ✅ The `Object` class has a `final` method `getClass()` — it cannot be overridden.
- ✅ Compile-time constant optimization: `static final` primitive variables with initializers are **inlined** by the compiler for performance.
- ✅ Common exam trap: *"Can a constructor be `final`?"* — **NO**, because constructors are never inherited.
- ✅ Common exam trap: *"Can a `final` class have a `final` method?"* — **YES**, both modifiers can coexist (redundant but legal).
- ✅ KTU Part A favorites: 3-mark questions on `final` vs `finally` vs `finalize`, or "Why is `String` final?"
- ✅ KTU Part B favorites: 14-mark questions involving code that demonstrates all three uses of `final` with explanation of compile-time errors.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When you see a code snippet in the KTU question paper, **always dry-run the modifiers first** — look for `final`, `static`, `private`, `protected`, `public` — and predict the compile-time behavior **before** writing the answer. This habit alone can help you score an extra **2-3 marks** per question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Singleton and Adaptor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Singleton and Adaptor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Singleton Design Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Singleton Pattern** is a **Creational Design Pattern** that ensures a class has **exactly one instance** and provides a **global point of access** to that instance. It restricts instantiation of a class to a single object, which is essential when exactly one object is needed to coordinate actions across the system.

The Singleton Pattern belongs to the **Gang of Four (GoF) Creational Patterns** category and is one of the simplest yet most frequently used design patterns in object-oriented programming.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **President of a Country**. A country can have only **one President at any given time**, regardless of how many citizens exist. Every government department that needs the President's authorization refers to the *same* President instance. You cannot simply create a new President whenever you want — there is a controlled, single point of access.

In the same way, the **Singleton Pattern** ensures that no matter how many times your code tries to instantiate a class, only **one shared object** is created and reused throughout the application's lifecycle. Common real-world software examples include:
- **Database connection pools** (one shared connection manager)
- **Logger classes** (one shared log writer)
- **Configuration managers** (one shared settings object)
- **Printer spoolers** (one shared print queue)

### Key Metrics and Standard Properties

- **Instance Count:** Exactly **1** per JVM/Application context
- **Thread Safety:** Must be guaranteed in multi-threaded environments
- **Lazy vs Eager Initialization:** Two standard approaches
- **Global Access Point:** Usually a `public static` method named `getInstance()`

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Singleton?**
> In KTU examinations, examiners often test the *reasoning* behind Singleton. The core reasons are: (1) **Resource conservation** (e.g., one DB connection pool), (2) **Global state management** (e.g., app configuration), and (3) **Controlled access** to a shared resource.

---

## 1.2 Adapter (Adaptor) Design Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **Adapter Pattern** is a **Structural Design Pattern** that allows objects with **incompatible interfaces** to collaborate. It acts as a **bridge between two incompatible interfaces** by wrapping an existing class with a new interface so that it becomes compatible with the client's expected interface, without modifying the original class's source code.

The Adapter Pattern is sometimes called a **"Wrapper"** because it wraps the interface of an existing class to make it work with another class.

### Conceptual Analution / Intuition

Think of a **power adapter (travel plug converter)**. Your Indian laptop charger has a 3-pin plug, but the wall socket in the UK has a different shape. Instead of buying a new laptop or modifying the wall socket, you simply use a **physical adapter** that converts the Indian plug's shape to fit the UK socket. The charger (existing class) and socket (target interface) remain *unchanged* — the adapter handles the incompatibility.

In software terms, when you have:
- A **legacy class** with an interface your code does not understand
- A **new system** that expects a specific interface

Instead of rewriting the legacy class, you create an **Adapter** that translates calls from the new system's interface into calls the legacy class can understand.

### Standard Participants in the Adapter Pattern

| Role | Description |
|---|---|
| **Target Interface** | The interface the client expects to work with (e.g., `Shape`) |
| **Adaptee** | The existing class with an incompatible interface (e.g., `LegacyRectangle`) |
| **Adapter** | The wrapper class that implements Target and holds an Adaptee (e.g., `RectangleAdapter`) |
| **Client** | The code that uses the Target interface (e.g., `DrawingApp`) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Variants of Adapter**
> 1. **Object Adapter (Composition):** The adapter *holds* an instance of the Adaptee. **Preferred in Java** (favor composition over inheritance).
> 2. **Class Adapter (Inheritance):** The adapter *inherits* from both Target and Adaptee using multiple inheritance. Common in C++.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Singleton Pattern — Structural Analysis

### 2.1.1 Components of Singleton

The Singleton pattern requires **three core building blocks**:

1. **Static Member Variable** — holds the single instance
2. **Private Constructor** — prevents external instantiation via `new`
3. **Public Static Accessor Method** — typically `getInstance()` controls instance creation

### 2.1.2 The Four Standard Implementations

#### (A) Eager Initialization (Simplest but wastes memory if unused)

```java
class EagerSingleton {
    private static final EagerSingleton instance = new EagerSingleton();
    private EagerSingleton() {}
    public static EagerSingleton getInstance() {
        return instance;
    }
}
```

- **Pros:** Thread-safe by default (JVM class loading guarantees safety)
- **Cons:** Instance is created even if never used (no lazy loading)

#### (B) Lazy Initialization (Not thread-safe)

```java
class LazySingleton {
    private static LazySingleton instance;
    private LazySingleton() {}
    public static LazySingleton getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {
            instance = new LazySingleton();
        }
        return instance;
    }
}
```

- **Pros:** Resource-efficient (creates instance only when needed)
- **Cons:** **NOT thread-safe** — two threads can create two instances

#### (C) Thread-Safe Synchronized Method (Safe but slow)

```java
class ThreadSafeSingleton {
    private static ThreadSafeSingleton instance;
    private ThreadSafeSingleton() {}
    public static synchronized ThreadSafeSingleton getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {
            instance = new ThreadSafeSingleton();
        }
        return instance;
    }
}
```

- **Pros:** Thread-safe
- **Cons:** Performance overhead due to `synchronized` on every call

#### (D) Double-Checked Locking (Best practice in KTU syllabus) ⭐

```java
class DoubleCheckedSingleton {
    private static volatile DoubleCheckedSingleton instance;
    private DoubleCheckedSingleton() {}
    public static DoubleCheckedSingleton getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {                      // 1st check (no lock)
            synchronized (DoubleCheckedSingleton.class) {
                if (instance == null) {              // 2nd check (with lock)
                    instance = new DoubleCheckedSingleton();
                }
            }
        }
        return instance;
    }
}
```

- **Pros:** Thread-safe + high performance (locks only on first call)
- **Why `volatile`?** Prevents instruction reordering by the JVM, ensuring the partially-constructed object is never visible to other threads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bill Pugh Singleton (Initialization-on-Demand Holder Idiom)**
> This is the **most recommended** Singleton implementation in modern Java:
> ```java
> class BillPughSingleton {
>     private BillPughSingleton() {}
>     private static class Holder {
>         private static final BillPughSingleton INSTANCE = new BillPughSingleton();
>     }
>     public static BillPughSingleton getInstance() {
>         return Holder.INSTANCE;
>     }
> }
> ```
> It uses **JVM class loading guarantees** for thread safety without explicit synchronization.

### 2.1.3 How Singleton Works — Step-by-Step Logic

1. The **constructor is marked `private`** → blocks external `new` calls.
2. A **static private variable** of the same class type is declared.
3. A **public static method** `getInstance()` is provided.
4. When `getInstance()` is first called, the instance is created (lazy) or returned immediately (eager).
5. On all subsequent calls, the **same instance** is returned.
6. To prevent cloning breaking Singleton, override `clone()` to throw `CloneNotSupportedException`.
7. To prevent deserialization breaking Singleton, implement `readResolve()`.

### 2.1.4 Breaking Singleton & Counter-Measures

| Threat | Counter-Measure |
|---|---|
| Reflection (set private constructor accessible) | Throw exception in constructor if instance exists |
| Cloning (`clone()`) | Override `clone()` to throw exception |
| Deserialization | Implement `readResolve()` to return existing instance |
| Multiple ClassLoaders | Use classloader-aware singletons (advanced) |

---

## 2.2 Adapter Pattern — Structural Analysis

### 2.2.1 When to Use Adapter (KTU Exam Favourite)

Use the Adapter Pattern when:
- You want to use an **existing class**, but its interface **does not match** the one you need.
- You need to **reuse** several existing subclasses that lack common functionality.
- You are integrating with a **third-party library** whose API is incompatible with your code.

### 2.2.2 Object Adapter vs Class Adapter

| Feature | Object Adapter (Composition) | Class Adapter (Inheritance) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Holds reference to Adaptee | Inherits from Adaptee |
| **Flexibility** | Can wrap any subclass of Adaptee | Tied to specific Adaptee class |
| **Multiple Inheritance** | Not required | Required (C++ only) |
| **Java Compatibility** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (Java lacks MI) |
| **KTU Preference** | ⭐ Preferred | Rarely asked |

### 2.2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **JDBC Drivers:** `java.sql.DriverManager` adapts vendor-specific database drivers to the standard JDBC interface.
- **Java I/O Streams:** `InputStreamReader` adapts a byte `InputStream` to a character `Reader` interface.
- **Spring Framework:** Adapters wrap third-party libraries for consistent integration.
- **Legacy System Integration:** Adapters allow modern UIs to talk to legacy backends.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction in KTU Exams**
> - **Adapter** → converts interface of an existing class to a *target* interface
> - **Facade** → provides a *simplified* unified interface to a complex subsystem
> - **Decorator** → *adds* behavior dynamically without changing the interface
> - **Bridge** → separates abstraction from implementation *upfront*

### 2.2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table

| Aspect | Singleton | Adapter |
|---|---|---|
| **Category** | Creational | Structural |
| **Intent** | One instance, global access | Bridge incompatible interfaces |
| **Number of Classes** | 1 (the Singleton itself) | 3+ (Target, Adaptee, Adapter, Client) |
| **Key Mechanism** | Private constructor + static accessor | Wrapper containing Adaptee reference |
| **Modification of Adaptee** | N/A | **No modification** to existing class |
| **Common Mistake** | Forgetting `private` constructor | Modifying the Adaptee instead of adapting |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Singleton: Tracing Object Creation Across Multiple Calls

Let us trace the **Double-Checked Locking Singleton** to prove only ONE object is created across multiple calls.

### Trace 1: First Call (instance is null)

```
Call: getInstance()  → instance == null? YES
    → Enter synchronized block
    → instance == null? YES (still)
    → instance = new DoubleCheckedSingleton();
    → Exit synchronized block
    → Return instance (Object ID: 0xABC)
```

### Trace 2: Second Call (instance is non-null)

```
Call: getInstance()  → instance == null? NO
    → Skip synchronized block (performance gain)
    → Return instance (Object ID: 0xABC)  ← SAME OBJECT
```

### Trace 3: Third Thread Concurrent Call

```
Thread-1: getInstance()  → instance == null? YES → enters synchronized
Thread-2: getInstance()  → instance == null? YES → BLOCKS at synchronized
Thread-1: creates instance, exits lock
Thread-2: enters lock → checks again → instance == null? NO
         → returns the same instance
```

### Mathematical Proof of Singleton Property

Let `n` = number of `getInstance()` calls, and `S` = number of unique Singleton objects created.

$$S = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n \geq 1 \text{ and Singleton implemented correctly} \\ 0 & \text{if } n = 0 \\ n & \text{if } Singleton is broken (no private constructor)} \end{cases}$$

> For a **correctly implemented Singleton**: $S = 1$ for all $n \geq 1$.

---

## 3.2 Adapter Pattern: Step-by-Step Translation Logic

Let us derive how an adapter **translates** an incompatible call to a compatible one.

### Scenario
- **Target Interface:** `PaymentProcessor` with method `processPayment(double amount, String currency)`
- **Adaptee (Legacy):** `OldBankGateway` with method `makeTransaction(String currency, double amount)` (note reversed parameter order!)

### Adapter Implementation

```java
// Step 1: Define Target interface
interface PaymentProcessor {
    void processPayment(double amount, String currency);
}

// Step 2: Adaptee - existing legacy class
class OldBankGateway {
    public void makeTransaction(String currency, double amount) {
        System.out.println("Processing " + amount + " " + currency);
    }
}

// Step 3: Adapter implements Target, holds Adaptee
class PaymentAdapter implements PaymentProcessor {
    private OldBankGateway legacyGateway;

    public PaymentAdapter(OldBankGateway gateway) {
        this.legacyGateway = gateway;
    }

    @Override
    public void processPayment(double amount, String currency) {
        // Step 4: Translate parameters and delegate
        legacyGateway.makeTransaction(currency, amount);
    }
}

// Step 5: Client code uses only Target interface
class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        OldBankGateway legacy = new OldBankGateway();
        PaymentProcessor processor = new PaymentAdapter(legacy);
        processor.processPayment(100.50, "USD");
        // Output: Processing 100.5 USD
    }
}
```

### Translation Mapping Equation

$$f_{adapter}: \text{TargetAPI} \rightarrow \text{AdapteeAPI}$$

$$f_{adapter}(\text{processPayment}(a, c)) = \text{makeTransaction}(c, a)$$

The adapter applies a **parameter reordering transformation** $T_{reorder}$ that swaps the order of arguments before delegating:

$$T_{reorder}(a, c) = (c, a)$$

So the full flow is:

$$T_{reorder}: (a, c) \rightarrow (c, a) \rightarrow \text{Adaptee.maketransaction}(c, a)$$

### Verification Trace

| Step | Client Call | Adapter Action | Adaptee Receives |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `processPayment(100.50, "USD")` | Parameter swap | `makeTransaction("USD", 100.50)` |
| 2 | — | Delegate to legacy | Legacy processes correctly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Multiple adaptations** can occur in one adapter — e.g., unit conversion, currency conversion, data format conversion, return value wrapping. The adapter centralizes all incompatibility handling in one place.

---

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Singleton vs Adapter

| Property | Singleton | Adapter |
|---|---|---|
| **Pattern Type** | Creational | Structural |
| **Gang of Four Category** | GoF Creational | GoF Structural |
| **Primary Intent** | Restrict to one instance | Make incompatible interfaces compatible |
| **Number of Instances** | Exactly **1** | As many as needed (one per Adaptee) |
| **Key Keyword** | `private static` | `implements` + `composition` |
| **UML Relationship** | Self-association | Association/Composition to Adaptee |
| **Java Implementation** | Private ctor + static method | Interface impl + Adaptee field |
| **Modifies Existing Class?** | N/A | **No** (key principle) |
| **Common Use Case** | Logger, Config, DB Pool | Third-party integration, Legacy code |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Singleton Pattern — UML Class Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Singleton {
        -static instance: Singleton
        -Singleton()
        +static getInstance() Singleton
        +businessLogic() void
    }

    note for Singleton "Only ONE instance ever exists.
Static field holds the single object.
Private constructor blocks external 'new' calls."
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dashed arrow pointing from the `Singleton` class to itself represents the **self-association** — the static field refers to its own type.

---

## 4.2 Adapter Pattern — Object Adapter UML

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
        +useService() void
    }

    class Target {
        <<interface>>
        +request() void
    }

    class Adapter {
        -adaptee: Adaptee
        +request() void
    }

    class Adaptee {
        +specificRequest() void
    }

    Client --> Target : uses
    Adapter ..|> Target : implements
    Adapter --> Adaptee : holds reference
```

### Diagram Explanation

| Element | Role |
|---|---|
| `Client` | The application code that needs a Target-type object |
| `Target` | Interface the Client expects (e.g., `PaymentProcessor`) |
| `Adapter` | The wrapper class that **implements Target** and **holds Adaptee** |
| `Adaptee` | The existing class with incompatible interface |

### Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client calls target.request] --> B[Adapter.request invoked]
    B --> C{Adapter translates
    parameters / format}
    C --> D[Adaptee.specificRequest called]
    D --> E[Adaptee processes and returns]
    E --> F[Adapter may transform result]
    F --> G[Result returned to Client]
```

---

## 4.3 Singleton — Internal Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client calls getInstance] --> B{instance == null?}
    B -- YES --> C[Acquire Class Lock]
    C --> D{instance == null? again}
    D -- YES --> E[Create new Singleton object]
    D -- NO --> F[Skip - already created]
    E --> G[Release Lock]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Return instance reference]
    B -- NO --> H
```

### Explanation

1. Client invokes the static `getInstance()` method.
2. The **first null-check** avoids acquiring the lock unnecessarily (performance).
3. If `null`, acquire the **class-level lock** (synchronization).
4. The **second null-check** is critical — another thread may have created the instance while we waited.
5. If still `null`, create the instance.
6. Return the **single, shared instance reference** to the client.

---

## 4.4 Adapter Pattern — Real-World Block Diagram (JDBC Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AppLayer [Application Layer]
        APP[Java Application using JDBC API]
    end

    subgraph AdapterLayer [Adapter Layer - JDBC DriverManager]
        ADAPT[JDBC DriverManager acts as Adapter]
    end

    subgraph AdapteeLayer [Adaptee Layer - Vendor Specific Drivers]
        ORACLE[Oracle JDBC Driver]
        MYSQL[MySQL JDBC Driver]
        POSTGRES[PostgreSQL JDBC Driver]
    end

    subgraph DatabaseLayer [Database Servers]
        DB1[(Oracle DB)]
        DB2[(MySQL DB)]
        DB3[(PostgreSQL DB)]
    end

    APP -->|Uses standard JDBC interface| ADAPT
    ADAPT -->|Translates calls to vendor API| ORACLE
    ADAPT -->|Translates calls to vendor API| MYSQL
    ADAPT -->|Translates calls to vendor API| POSTGRES
    ORACLE --> DB1
    MYSQL --> DB2
    POSTGRES --> DB3
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates a **classic real-world Adapter** — JDBC provides a uniform interface to applications while hiding vendor-specific implementation differences. The application never directly touches vendor drivers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the Singleton Design Pattern. Mention any two real-world scenarios where it is used.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Singleton Design Pattern** is a creational design pattern that ensures a class has **only one instance** throughout the application's lifetime and provides a **global point of access** to that instance. It is implemented by declaring a **private constructor** and a **public static method** that returns the single instance.

**Real-world scenarios:**
1. **Database Connection Pool Manager** — Only one pool should exist to manage limited DB connections efficiently.
2. **Application Logger** — A single logger instance writes logs to a file/console to avoid conflicting log streams.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: What is the Adapter Pattern? How does it differ from the Facade Pattern?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Adapter Pattern** is a structural design pattern that allows objects with **incompatible interfaces** to work together. It wraps an existing class (Adaptee) with a new interface (Target) so the client can use it without modifying the original class.

**Difference from Facade:**
- **Adapter** converts a **single class's** interface to another expected interface (solving incompatibility).
- **Facade** provides a **simplified unified interface** to a complex subsystem of **multiple classes** (ease of use).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Singleton Design Pattern with its intent, structure, and a complete Java implementation using **Double-Checked Locking** with the `volatile` keyword. Discuss how it achieves thread safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the various ways in which a Singleton implementation can be broken. For each, suggest a counter-measure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Intent:** The Singleton pattern ensures that a class has exactly **one instance** and provides a **global point of access** to it. It is used when a single object is needed to coordinate actions across the system (e.g., configuration manager, logger).

**Structure:**
- A **private static** field of the same class type to hold the instance.
- A **private constructor** to block external `new` calls.
- A **public static** method `getInstance()` to control and return the instance.

**Java Implementation (Double-Checked Locking):**

```java
class Singleton {
    // volatile prevents instruction reordering
    private static volatile Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
        System.out.println("Singleton Created");
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {                          // 1st check
            synchronized (Singleton.class) {
                if (instance == null) {                  // 2nd check
                    instance = new Singleton();
                }
            }
        }
        return instance;
    }

    public void showMessage() {
        System.out.println("Hello from Singleton!");
    }
}

public class TestSingleton {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Singleton s1 = Singleton.getInstance();
        Singleton s2 = Singleton.getInstance();
        System.out.println("Same instance? " + (s1 == s2));
    }
}
```

**Thread Safety Explanation:**
- The **first null-check** avoids the cost of synchronization for already-created instances (performance optimization).
- The **synchronized block** ensures only one thread enters the critical section at a time.
- The **second null-check** prevents multiple instances when multiple threads passed the first check simultaneously.
- The **`volatile` keyword** ensures visibility of changes across threads and prevents JVM instruction reordering, which could otherwise publish a partially-constructed object.

**Output:**
```
Singleton Created
Same instance? true
```

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

The following are the standard ways a Singleton can be broken and their solutions:

**1. Reflection Attack**
A malicious user can access the private constructor using `setAccessible(true)` and create a new instance.

**Counter-measure:** Throw an exception inside the constructor if the instance already exists:
```java
private Singleton() {
    if (instance != null) {
        throw new RuntimeException("Reflection not allowed!");
    }
}
```

**2. Cloning Attack**
If the class implements `Cloneable`, calling `clone()` creates a new instance, breaking Singleton.

**Counter-measure:** Override `clone()` to throw `CloneNotSupportedException`:
```java
@Override
protected Object clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException {
    throw new CloneNotSupportedException("Cloning not allowed");
}
```

**3. Deserialization Attack**
When a Singleton is deserialized, a new object is created, breaking the Singleton property.

**Counter-measure:** Implement the `readResolve()` method to return the existing instance:
```java
protected Object readResolve() {
    return getInstance();
}
```

**4. Multiple ClassLoaders**
If two different class loaders load the Singleton class, two distinct instances are created.

**Counter-measure:** Use a classloader-aware implementation or a shared registry (advanced).

**5. Multiple Threads (Lazy initialization)**
Two threads simultaneously enter the unsynchronized lazy `getInstance()` and create two instances.

**Counter-measure:** Use Double-Checked Locking with `volatile`, or the Bill Pugh Singleton using class loading guarantees.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Adapter Design Pattern with its intent and classification. Provide a Java implementation demonstrating the **Object Adapter** for the following scenario: An application expects a `MediaPlayer` interface with `play(String audioType, String fileName)` method, but the existing legacy class `VLCPlayer` only supports `playVLC(String fileName)`. Your adapter should support both VLC and MP4 file types. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Adapter Pattern** with the **Bridge Pattern** and the **Decorator Pattern**. Provide one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Intent:** The Adapter Pattern allows objects with incompatible interfaces to collaborate. It acts as a wrapper that translates one interface into another expected by the client.

**Classification:** It is a **Structural Design Pattern** (GoF) that uses **composition** (object adapter variant) to wrap the Adaptee.

**Java Implementation:**

```java
// Step 1: Target interface expected by client
interface MediaPlayer {
    void play(String audioType, String fileName);
}

// Step 2: Adaptee 1 - legacy VLC class
class VLCPlayer {
    public void playVLC(String fileName) {
        System.out.println("Playing VLC file: " + fileName);
    }
}

// Step 3: Adaptee 2 - legacy MP4 class
class MP4Player {
    public void playMP4(String fileName) {
        System.out.println("Playing MP4 file: " + fileName);
    }
}

// Step 4: Adapter - implements Target, holds Adaptees
class MediaAdapter implements MediaPlayer {
    private VLCPlayer vlc;
    private MP4Player mp4;

    public MediaAdapter() {
        vlc = new VLCPlayer();
        mp4 = new MP4Player();
    }

    @Override
    public void play(String audioType, String fileName) {
        if (audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("vlc")) {
            vlc.playVLC(fileName);              // translate call
        } else if (audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("mp4")) {
            mp4.playMP4(fileName);              // translate call
        }
    }
}

// Step 5: Client
class AudioPlayer implements MediaPlayer {
    private MediaAdapter adapter;

    @Override
    public void play(String audioType, String fileName) {
        if (audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("mp3")) {
            System.out.println("Playing MP3 file: " + fileName);
        } else if (audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("vlc") || audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("mp4")) {
            adapter = new MediaAdapter();
            adapter.play(audioType, fileName);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid media type: " + audioType);
        }
    }
}

public class AdapterDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        AudioPlayer player = new AudioPlayer();
        player.play("mp3", "song.mp3");
        player.play("vlc", "movie.vlc");
        player.play("mp4", "video.mp4");
        player.play("avi", "clip.avi");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Playing MP3 file: song.mp3
Playing VLC file: movie.vlc
Playing MP4 file: video.mp4
Invalid media type: avi
```

**Key Insight:** The legacy `VLCPlayer` and `MP4Player` classes were **not modified**. The `MediaAdapter` translates `MediaPlayer.play()` calls into their specific methods.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Aspect | Adapter | Bridge | Decorator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Make incompatible interfaces compatible | Decouple abstraction from implementation | Add behavior dynamically without modifying class |
| **When Applied** | After the fact (existing incompatibility) | Upfront design (anticipating variation) | At runtime (wrapping objects) |
| **Mechanism** | Wraps an existing Adaptee | Abstraction holds Implementor reference | Wraps a component to add responsibilities |
| **Number of Wrapped Objects** | One (or few) Adaptees | One Implementor | Multiple decorators can stack |
| **Purpose** | **Reuse** incompatible existing code | **Independent variation** of abstraction & implementation | **Extend** behavior dynamically |

**Real-World Examples:**

- **Adapter:** A **power plug travel adapter** converts an Indian 3-pin plug to fit a UK socket. The plug and socket are unchanged.
- **Bridge:** A **remote control (abstraction)** works with multiple **device types (TV, Radio)** through a `Device` interface (implementor). New devices can be added without changing the remote.
- **Decorator:** **Java I/O streams** like `BufferedReader(FileReader)` — `BufferedReader` decorates `FileReader` to add buffering. `new DataInputStream(new BufferedInputStream(new FileInputStream("f.txt")))` shows stacking decorators.

**Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
- **Adapter** fixes incompatibility **after** the incompatible class exists (legacy fix).
- **Bridge** prevents incompatibility **before** it happens (designed for variation).
- **Decorator** does not change the interface; it only **adds** responsibilities.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting `private` constructor in Singleton** — Examiner deducts full marks because external `new` calls can create multiple instances.
> 2. **Missing `volatile` in Double-Checked Locking** — A common 1–2 mark deduction. Without `volatile`, JVM instruction reordering can publish a partially-constructed object.
> 3. **Modifying the Adaptee in Adapter Pattern** — If the student modifies the legacy class instead of creating an Adapter, the entire question is marked wrong. The principle is: **do NOT modify the Adaptee**.
> 4. **Confusing Adapter with Decorator** — Many students write the wrong pattern. Remember: **Adapter changes the interface; Decorator keeps the same interface and adds behavior**.
> 5. **Singleton with non-static field** — Declaring the field as instance variable instead of `static` is a fatal error.
> 6. **Not addressing thread safety** — In KTU papers, Singleton questions explicitly ask for thread safety. Writing only the lazy non-thread-safe version loses 3–4 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Singleton Pattern — Quick Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Category:** Creational Design Pattern (GoF)
- ✅ **Goal:** Ensure exactly **one instance** of a class exists
- ✅ **Three pillars:** (1) **Private static** field, (2) **Private constructor**, (3) **Public static** `getInstance()` method
- ✅ **Eager Initialization:** Instance created at class loading; thread-safe but wastes memory
- ✅ **Lazy Initialization:** Instance created on first call; not thread-safe
- ✅ **Thread-Safe Lazy:** Use `synchronized` on `getInstance()`; slow
- ✅ **Double-Checked Locking:** Two null-checks + `synchronized` block; **use `volatile`** for visibility
- ✅ **Bill Pugh Singleton:** Uses inner static class; **most recommended** in modern Java
- ✅ **Defend against:** Reflection (throw in ctor), Cloning (override `clone()`), Deserialization (`readResolve()`)
- ✅ **Real-world use:** Logger, Configuration Manager, DB Connection Pool, Cache, Thread Pool

### Adapter Pattern — Quick Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Category:** Structural Design Pattern (GoF)
- ✅ **Goal:** Make **incompatible interfaces** work together
- ✅ **Principle:** **Do NOT modify the Adaptee**; wrap it with an Adapter
- ✅ **Four participants:** Target, Adaptee, Adapter, Client
- ✅ **Object Adapter (preferred):** Adapter **holds** an Adaptee reference; uses composition
- ✅ **Class Adapter (rare in Java):** Adapter **inherits** from Adaptee; requires multiple inheritance (C++)
- ✅ **Real-world use:** Power plug converter, JDBC driver manager, `InputStreamReader`, third-party library integration
- ✅ **Difference from Facade:** Adapter fixes incompatibility of ONE class; Facade simplifies a SUBSYSTEM
- ✅ **Difference from Decorator:** Adapter changes the interface; Decorator keeps the same interface
- ✅ **Difference from Bridge:** Adapter works after-the-fact; Bridge is designed upfront for variation

### Final Memory Hooks for KTU Exam Day

1. **Singleton = "Only One"** → "Sole Survivor" pattern
2. **Adapter = "Bridge the Gap"** → "Travel Adapter" pattern
3. Always remember: **Private constructor** (Singleton) and **Composition over Inheritance** (Adapter)
4. **Most asked question:** "Implement thread-safe Singleton with Double-Checked Locking and explain why `volatile` is used."
5. **Second most asked:** "Design an Adapter for integrating legacy class X with new interface Y."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain" a pattern, always cover: **Intent → Classification → UML Structure → Java Code → Use Case**. This 5-part structure is the gold standard for KTU 14-mark answers and typically earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SOLID Principles in Java. Swings Fundamentals - Overview of AWT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 SOLID Principles — Formal Definition

**SOLID** is an acronym for five object-oriented design principles formulated by **Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob)** that, when applied together, make software systems more understandable, flexible, and maintainable. In the context of KTU 2024 OOP syllabus, SOLID principles guide the creation of clean, loosely-coupled class hierarchies in Java.

| Letter | Principle | Core Intent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **S** | Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) | One class, one reason to change |
| **O** | Open/Closed Principle (OCP) | Open for extension, closed for modification |
| **L** | Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) | Subtypes must be substitutable for base types |
| **I** | Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) | Prefer many specific interfaces over one general |
| **D** | Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) | Depend on abstractions, not concretions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SOLID principles are tested as 7-mark and 14-mark analytical questions. Students are expected to *apply* each principle to a real Java class design, not just define them.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Swiss Army Knife vs The Toolbox

Imagine you are building a **multi-tool kitchen gadget** that can slice bread, blend smoothies, juice oranges, and toast sandwiches. This single device is hard to repair, hard to clean, and if the toaster fails, you lose everything. SOLID principles say: **don't build a Swiss Army knife — build a modular toolbox.** Each tool (class) does exactly one job, can be swapped, can be extended with attachments, and doesn't depend on how the other tools are wired internally. That is the essence of SOLID: small, focused, composable, replaceable units.

## 1.2 AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) — Formal Definition

**AWT** is Java's original, platform-dependent GUI framework introduced with JDK 1.0 (1996). It uses **native OS peer components** (so a `java.awt.Button` is rendered using the host operating system's underlying button widget). This makes AWT lightweight in code size but heavy in look-and-feel inconsistency across platforms.

## 1.3 Swing — Formal Definition

**Swing** is Java's platform-independent GUI toolkit built on top of AWT, introduced as part of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)** in JDK 1.2 (1998). All Swing components are prefixed with the letter `J` (e.g., `JButton`, `JFrame`, `JPanel`) and are rendered using **pure Java** drawing primitives (lightweight components), giving a consistent look-and-feel across operating systems.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Theatre Stage Analogy

Think of **AWT** as a stage that borrows *real furniture* from the host theatre — chairs, tables, and curtains that look different in every city. Think of **Swing** as a stage crew that *paints its own furniture* and ships it everywhere, so every audience sees the *same* show, with the *same* curtains, in the *same* colour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** AWT components are in the package `java.awt.*` and are **heavyweight** (peer-based). Swing components are in `javax.swing.*` and are **lightweight** (rendered in pure Java on top of AWT event infrastructure).

## 1.4 Key Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF):** Swing supports *Metal*, *Nimbus*, *Windows*, *Motif*. The default since Java SE 5 was **Metal**; **Nimbus** became the default cross-platform L&F in later JDKs.
- **MVC Architecture:** Swing components strictly follow the **Model-View-Controller** pattern (e.g., `JTable` uses `TableModel`).
- **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT):** A single dedicated thread (`java.awt.EventQueue`) that handles all Swing GUI updates — a critical threading concept.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five SOLID Principles — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)

**Formal Statement:** *A class should have only one reason to change.* This means a class should have **only one job or responsibility**.

**Reasoning Chain:**
1. Identify the **actors** (stakeholders) who may request a change in the class.
2. If more than one actor can request a change, the class has multiple responsibilities.
3. Refactor by extracting each responsibility into its own dedicated class.
4. Result: high cohesion, low coupling.

**Engineering Utility:** Reduces regression bugs. When a class is changed for one stakeholder, unrelated features (used by other stakeholders) are not affected. Used heavily in **layered architectures** (Controller layer, Service layer, Repository layer).

### 2.1.2 Open/Closed Principle (OCP)

**Formal Statement:** *Software entities (classes, modules, functions) should be open for extension but closed for modification.*

**Reasoning Chain:**
1. New requirements should be added by **writing new code**, not by **modifying existing, tested code**.
2. Achieved through **abstraction** (abstract classes or interfaces) and **polymorphism**.
3. The base contract remains stable; new behaviour is plugged in via new subclasses.

**Engineering Utility:** Critical for **plugin architectures**, **strategy patterns**, and any long-lived production codebase where changing old code carries high risk.

### 2.1.3 Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)

**Formal Statement (Barbara Liskov, 1987):** *Objects of a superclass shall be replaceable with objects of a subclass without breaking the application.*

**Reasoning Chain:**
1. Subclasses must **strengthen, not weaken**, the post-conditions of base methods.
2. Subclasses must **weaken, not strengthen**, the pre-conditions.
3. Invariants of the base class must be preserved in the subclass.
4. A subclass must not throw new, broader exceptions for overridden methods.

**Engineering Utility:** Guarantees that polymorphic code works correctly under substitution — essential in framework design (e.g., `List` → `ArrayList` / `LinkedList`).

### 2.1.4 Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)

**Formal Statement:** *Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use.*

**Reasoning Chain:**
1. **Fat interfaces** (with many unrelated methods) cause implementing classes to throw `UnsupportedOperationException`.
2. Split large interfaces into **role-specific** smaller interfaces.
3. Classes implement only the interfaces relevant to their role.

**Engineering Utility:** Reduces coupling between unrelated clients. Foundational for **role-based design** in large Java systems (e.g., `Comparable`, `Runnable`, `Serializable` are segregated).

### 2.1.5 Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)

**Formal Statement:**
1. High-level modules must not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on **abstractions**.
2. Abstractions must not depend on details. Details should depend on abstractions.

**Reasoning Chain:**
1. Replace direct `new` of concrete classes with **interface references**.
2. Inject the concrete implementation at runtime via constructor, setter, or framework (Spring).
3. The high-level business policy is decoupled from low-level mechanics.

**Engineering Utility:** Foundation of **Dependency Injection (DI)**, **Inversion of Control (IoC)** containers like Spring, and testability via mocking (Mockito).

## 2.2 AWT vs Swing — Architectural Comparison

| Feature | AWT (`java.awt.*`) | Swing (`javax.swing.*`) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Introduced | JDK 1.0 (1996) | JDK 1.2 (1998) |
| Component Type | **Heavyweight** (OS peer) | **Lightweight** (pure Java) |
| Look & Feel | Native OS dependent | **Pluggable L&F** (Metal/Nimbus/Windows) |
| Component Prefix | `Button`, `Frame`, `Panel` | `JButton`, `JFrame`, `JPanel` |
| MVC Support | Minimal | Full (e.g., `JTable` + `TableModel`) |
| Performance | Faster (native rendering) | Slightly slower (Java rendering) |
| Portability | Look varies; bytecode portable | Identical look everywhere |
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Event Handling | `java.awt.event` | Uses AWT event classes |

## 2.4 Swing Component Class Hierarchy (Top-Level Containers)

```text
java.lang.Object
   └── java.awt.Component
          └── java.awt.Container
                 └── java.awt.Window
                        └── java.awt.Frame (AWT)
                               └── javax.swing.JFrame
                                      ├── JRootPane
                                      │      └── Glass Pane + Layered Pane + Content Pane
                                      │             └── (All visible Swing components added here)
```

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (Code Patterns)

| Principle | Java Pattern | Code Snippet (Kotlin/Java Syntax-Free) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SRP | One class per concern | Split ReportGenerator and ReportPrinter |
| OCP | Abstract base class | Extend new subclass without modifying base |
| LSP | Override honouring contract | Square should not extend Rectangle (classic violation) |
| ISP | Multiple small interfaces | Printable, Scannable, Faxable — not AllInOne |
| DIP | Constructor Injection | `service = new Service(new MySQLRepo())` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** `JFrame` *is* an `AWT` class extension — Swing containers are layered on top of AWT heavyweight peers, but Swing *components* (buttons, labels) are lightweight.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 SRP — Step-by-Step Refactor (Before → After)

### Step 1: Identify the Violation (Before Refactor)

```java
// VIOLATES SRP — one class has 3 reasons to change
class Employee {
    public void calculatePay()    { /* finance dept may change */ }
    public void saveToDatabase()  { /* DBA may change */ }
    public void generateReport()  { /* reporting dept may change */ }
}
```

**Reasoning (line by line):**
- Line 2: `calculatePay` belongs to the **Finance** actor.
- Line 3: `saveToDatabase` belongs to the **DBA** actor.
- Line 4: `generateReport` belongs to the **Reporting** actor.
- Conclusion: The class `Employee` has **3 reasons to change** → violates SRP.

### Step 2: Apply the Refactor

```java
class Employee {
    private String name;
    private double salary;
    // Pure data + getters
}

class PayCalculator {
    public double calculatePay(Employee e) { /* finance logic */ }
}

class EmployeeRepository {
    public void save(Employee e) { /* DB logic */ }
}

class ReportGenerator {
    public String generate(Employee e) { /* report logic */ }
}
```

**Reasoning (line by line):**
- Each class now has **exactly one actor** requesting changes.
- High cohesion inside each class.
- Easy to unit test `PayCalculator` without a real database.
- **Result:** SRP satisfied.

## 3.2 OCP — Step-by-Step Application

### Step 1: Violation (Switch on Type)

```java
class AreaCalculator {
    public double area(Object shape) {
        if (shape instanceof Rectangle) {
            Rectangle r = (Rectangle) shape;
            return r.length * r.width;
        } else if (shape instanceof Circle) {
            Circle c = (Circle) shape;
            return Math.PI * c.radius * c.radius;
        }
        return 0;
    }
}
```

**Reasoning:** Every new shape requires **modifying** this class. **Violates OCP.**

### Step 2: Apply OCP Using Abstraction

```java
abstract class Shape {
    public abstract double area();
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    double length, width;
    public double area() { return length * width; }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius;
    public double area() { return Math.PI * radius * radius; }
}

class AreaCalculator {
    public double area(Shape s) { return s.area(); }
}
```

**Reasoning:**
- Adding a new `Triangle` class does **not** modify `AreaCalculator`.
- `AreaCalculator` is **closed for modification**, **open for extension** via new `Shape` subclasses.
- The polymorphic dispatch `s.area()` is the key to OCP.

## 3.3 LSP — Step-by-Step Analysis

### Step 1: Classic Violation (Square extends Rectangle)

```java
class Rectangle {
    protected int width, height;
    public void setWidth(int w)  { width = w; }
    public void setHeight(int h) { height = h; }
    public int getArea()         { return width * height; }
}

class Square extends Rectangle {
    @Override
    public void setWidth(int w)  { width = w; height = w; }   // violates!
    @Override
    public void setHeight(int h) { height = h; width = h; }  // violates!
}
```

**Reasoning:**
- Client code expects: after `setWidth(5); setHeight(4);` the area is **20**.
- With `Square` substituted, both calls set the same dimension, so area becomes **16**.
- The **behaviour changes** when substituting subtype → **LSP violated**.
- Solution: do not model `Square` as a subclass of `Rectangle`; give them a common abstract `Shape` parent.

## 3.4 ISP — Step-by-Step Refactor

### Step 1: Fat Interface (Violation)

```java
interface Machine {
    void print();
    void scan();
    void fax();
}

class SimplePrinter implements Machine {
    public void print() { /* ok */ }
    public void scan()  { throw new UnsupportedOperationException(); } // bad!
    public void fax()   { throw new UnsupportedOperationException(); } // bad!
}
```

**Reasoning:** `SimplePrinter` is **forced** to depend on `scan()` and `fax()` which it does not use. Violates ISP.

### Step 2: Segregated Interfaces

```java
interface Printer   { void print(); }
interface Scanner   { void scan();  }
interface Faxer     { void fax();   }

class SimplePrinter implements Printer {
    public void print() { /* ok */ }
}

class MultiFunctionMachine implements Printer, Scanner, Faxer {
    public void print() { /* ... */ }
    public void scan()  { /* ... */ }
    public void fax()   { /* ... */ }
}
```

**Reasoning:** Each class now depends **only on the methods it actually implements**. ISP satisfied.

## 3.5 DIP — Step-by-Step Refactor

### Step 1: High-Level Depends on Low-Level (Violation)

```java
class MySQLConnection {
    public void connect() { /* MySQL specific */ }
}

class UserService {
    private MySQLConnection db = new MySQLConnection(); // tightly coupled
}
```

**Reasoning:** `UserService` is the high-level business policy but is **directly bound** to the low-level `MySQLConnection`. Switching to PostgreSQL requires modifying `UserService`. Violates DIP.

### Step 2: Inject Abstraction

```java
interface Database {
    void connect();
}
class MySQLConnection implements Database { public void connect() { /* MySQL */ } }
class PostgreSQLConnection implements Database { public void connect() { /* PG */ } }

class UserService {
    private final Database db;
    public UserService(Database db) { this.db = db; }  // constructor injection
}
```

**Reasoning:**
- `UserService` (high-level) depends only on the `Database` abstraction.
- `MySQLConnection` (low-level) also depends on the `Database` abstraction.
- The inversion is achieved.
- To swap databases: `new UserService(new PostgreSQLConnection())`.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Violation → Principle → Fix

| Bad Code Symptom | Principle Violated | Fix Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Class does printing, saving, mailing | **SRP** | Extract into 3 classes |
| `if/else` chain on object type | **OCP** | Polymorphism via abstract class |
| Subclass breaks parent behaviour | **LSP** | Re-examine inheritance, prefer composition |
| Class throws `UnsupportedOperationException` | **ISP** | Split fat interface |
| `new ConcreteClass()` inside business logic | **DIP** | Constructor injection of interface |

## 3.7 Swing "Hello World" — Step-by-Step Program

```java
import javax.swing.*;

public class HelloSwing {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Create the top-level container (heavyweight, AWT-based)
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("KTU Swing Demo");

        // Step 2: Create a lightweight Swing component
        JLabel label = new JLabel("Hello KTU 2024!", SwingConstants.CENTER);

        // Step 3: Add component to the Content Pane
        frame.add(label);

        // Step 4: Configure frame properties
        frame.setSize(400, 200);
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.setLocationRelativeTo(null); // center on screen

        // Step 5: Make the frame visible
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Reasoning (line by line):**
- Line 1: `JFrame` extends `java.awt.Frame` — confirms that Swing is *built on* AWT.
- Line 2: `JLabel` is a lightweight Swing component, painted by Java.
- Line 3: `frame.add()` actually adds to the **Content Pane** of the `JRootPane`.
- Line 4: `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` terminates the JVM on close.
- Line 5: `setVisible(true)` triggers the EDT to render the UI.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Single Responsibility Principle. Give one example of a Java class that violates it and show how it can be refactored.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** states that *a class should have only one reason to change*, meaning it should have only one well-defined responsibility or job.

**Example of Violation (1 mark):**

```java
class Student {
    void calculateGPA()   { /* academic logic */ }
    void saveToDatabase() { /* persistence logic */ }
    void sendEmail()      { /* notification logic */ }
}
```

This class mixes academic, persistence, and notification concerns — three reasons to change.

**Refactored Version (1 mark):**

```java
class Student { String name; /* data only */ }
class GPAService    { double calculateGPA(Student s) { /* ... */ } }
class StudentDAO    { void save(Student s)           { /* ... */ } }
class EmailService  { void send(Student s)           { /* ... */ } }
```

Each class now has a single, clear responsibility.

| :- | :--- | :--- |
