## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU Examiner Note]** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B Module 4 carries 14 marks with internal choice. Two full 14-mark answer choices are provided below.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** with a suitable Java example. Show how the classic *Square-Rectangle* problem violates it and how the violation can be corrected. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** with a Java code example. Discuss how it is related to **Dependency Injection (DI)** and **Inversion of Control (IoC)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — LSP (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** The Liskov Substitution Principle, formulated by **Barbara Liskov (1987)**, states that *objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of a subclass without altering the desirable properties of the program*. In Java terms, any method that accepts a `Base` reference must work correctly when given a `Derived` reference.

**Violation — Square extends Rectangle (3 marks):**

```java
class Rectangle {
    protected int width, height;
    public void setWidth(int w)  { width  = w; }
    public void setHeight(int h) { height = h; }
    public int  getArea()        { return width * height; }
}

class Square extends Rectangle {
    @Override public void setWidth(int w)  { width = w; height = w; }
    @Override public void setHeight(int h) { height = h; width  = h; }
}
```

**Explanation of violation (1 mark):** A client computes `area = 5 * 4 = 20` for a `Rectangle`. If a `Square` is substituted, both setters set both dimensions, so `getArea()` returns `16`. The **post-condition** of `setWidth` (only width changes) is broken. Hence, `Square` is **not a true subtype** of `Rectangle`, violating LSP.

**Correction (1 mark):** Make `Rectangle` and `Square` siblings inheriting from a common abstract class `Shape`:

```java
abstract class Shape { public abstract int getArea(); }
class Rectangle extends Shape { /* independent setWidth/setHeight */ }
class Square    extends Shape { private int side;   public int getArea(){return side*side;} }
```

Now substitution preserves behaviour.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — DIP + DI + IoC (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** The Dependency Inversion Principle states that:
1. High-level modules must not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on **abstractions** (interfaces).
2. Abstractions should not depend on details; details should depend on abstractions.

**Violating Code (2 marks):**

```java
class EmailService { public void send(String m){System.out.println("Email: "+m);} }
class OrderService {
    private EmailService email = new EmailService(); // direct coupling
    public void placeOrder() { email.send("Order placed"); }
}
```

Here `OrderService` (high-level) directly instantiates `EmailService` (low-level). Changing to SMS notification requires modifying `OrderService`.

**Fixed Code with DIP (2 marks):**

```java
interface MessageService { void send(String m); }
class EmailService implements MessageService { public void send(String m){/*email*/} }
class SMSService   implements MessageService { public void send(String m){/*sms*/}  }

class OrderService {
    private final MessageService notifier;
    public OrderService(MessageService notifier) { this.notifier = notifier; } // DI
    public void placeOrder() { notifier.send("Order placed"); }
}
// Usage: new OrderService(new SMSService());
```

**Connection to DI / IoC (1 mark):** *Dependency Injection* (constructor, setter, or field) is the *mechanism* by which DIP is implemented. *Inversion of Control* is the *architectural principle* where the framework (e.g., Spring) controls the creation and injection of dependencies, rather than the class creating them with `new`. DI is a *form* of IoC; IoC is the broader *philosophy*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)** and the **Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)** with Java code examples demonstrating both a violation and a correct design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a window with a `JLabel`, a `JTextField`, and a `JButton` labelled "Greet". When the button is clicked, the text typed in the text field should appear in the label prefixed with "Hello, ". Use an **ActionListener** to handle the event. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — OCP + ISP (7 marks)

**OCP Definition (1 mark):** Software entities should be **open for extension** but **closed for modification**. New behaviour is added by writing *new* code (subclasses), not by editing *existing*, tested code.

**OCP Violation (1.5 marks):**

```java
class InvoicePrinter {
    public void print(Invoice inv, String type) {
        if (type.equals("PDF"))    { /* PDF logic */ }
        else if (type.equals("HTML")){ /* HTML logic */ }
    }
}
```

Adding a new format (e.g., "Excel") requires modifying this class — violates OCP.

**OCP Solution (1.5 marks):**

```java
interface InvoiceFormatter { String format(Invoice i); }
class PDFFormatter    implements InvoiceFormatter { /* ... */ }
class HTMLFormatter   implements InvoiceFormatter { /* ... */ }
class InvoicePrinter  {
    public void print(Invoice i, InvoiceFormatter f) { System.out.println(f.format(i)); }
}
```

Adding `ExcelFormatter` does not touch `InvoicePrinter` — OCP satisfied.

**ISP Definition (1 mark):** Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use. Prefer many small, role-specific interfaces over one large "fat" interface.

**ISP Violation + Fix (2 marks):**

```java
// BAD — fat interface
interface MultiDevice { void print(); void scan(); void fax(); }
class BasicPrinter implements MultiDevice {
    public void print() {} 
    public void scan()  { throw new UnsupportedOperationException(); } // bad
    public void fax()   { throw new UnsupportedOperationException(); }
}

// GOOD — segregated
interface Printer { void print(); }
interface Scanner { void scan();  }
class BasicPrinter implements Printer { public void print() { /*...*/ } }
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Swing Greeting Program (7 marks)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class GreetApp extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JLabel  label;
    JTextField textField;
    JButton  greetButton;

    public GreetApp() {
        setTitle("KTU Greet App");
        setSize(400, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        label     = new JLabel("Type your name and click Greet.");
        textField = new JTextField(15);
        greetButton = new JButton("Greet");

        add(label);
        add(textField);
        add(greetButton);

        greetButton.addActionListener(this); // register listener
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String name = textField.getText();
        label.setText("Hello, " + name);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new GreetApp().setVisible(true));
    }
}
```

**Mark-wise Breakdown (7 marks):**
- Correct imports and `main` method with `SwingUtilities.invokeLater` — **1 mark**
- `JFrame` subclass with `setTitle`, `setSize`, `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` — **1 mark**
- Three Swing components (`JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JButton`) added with `FlowLayout` — **1.5 marks**
- `ActionListener` interface implemented and `addActionListener` registered — **1.5 marks**
- Correct `actionPerformed` logic using `getText()` and `setText()` — **1.5 marks**
- Compile-clean and syntactically correct code — **0.5 mark**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to make the `JFrame` visible with `setVisible(true)` → **-1 mark**
> 2. Calling `setVisible(true)` *before* `add()` (components may not show) → **-0.5 mark**
> 3. Modifying UI components from outside the **Event Dispatch Thread** (creating the GUI from `main` directly is acceptable for simple apps but loses the **-0.5 mark** for best-practice)
> 4. Confusing AWT's `Button` with Swing's `JButton` in the answer → **-0.5 mark**
> 5. Writing `implements ActionListener` but **not** registering the listener via `addActionListener(this)` → button click does nothing → **-1 mark**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SOLID** is a 5-principle acronym by **Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob)** for clean OOP design.
- **SRP**: One class = one job = one reason to change.
- **OCP**: Extend via new subclasses; never modify tested base code.
- **LSP**: Subclass must be a true behavioural substitute for the base class (Square-Rectangle trap).
- **ISP**: Prefer many small, role-specific interfaces over one fat interface.
- **DIP**: Depend on **abstractions** (interfaces/abstract classes), inject concrete implementations.
- **AWT** is the **old, heavyweight, native-peer** GUI toolkit (`java.awt.*`).
- **Swing** is the **new, lightweight, pure-Java, pluggable-L&F** toolkit (`javax.swing.*`).
- All Swing top-level containers (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`) **inherit** from AWT's `java.awt.Frame`.
- Swing components are added to the **Content Pane** inside the **JRootPane** of a `JFrame`.
- Swing uses **MVC** internally; AWT does not.
- All Swing GUI updates must occur on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** — use `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()`.
- KTU 2024 expects **code + diagram + explanation** for full 14-mark questions; definitions alone get at most 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing v/s AWT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing v/s AWT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) is the original platform-dependent, heavyweight GUI (Graphical User Interface) toolkit provided by Java SE (Standard Edition), introduced in JDK 1.0 (released in 1996). It is a thin Java wrapper around the native operating system's windowing subsystem — every AWT component is rendered using the host OS's native peers, meaning an AWT `Button` on Windows looks like a Windows button, on Linux it resembles a Linux button, and on macOS it mimics a macOS button.*

### Conceptual Analogy (The "Brick Layer" Metaphor)

Imagine you are building a wall:
- **AWT** behaves like a **mason who only uses locally-sourced bricks**. Whatever brick is available in that city (the OS), he uses. The wall looks slightly different in every city because it inherits the local style.
- **Swing** behaves like a **modern prefab architect who ships his own standardized bricks** from a factory. The wall looks *identical* no matter which city it is assembled in, because the architect brought his own materials.

This is the **fundamental philosophical difference** between AWT (relies on the OS) and Swing (brings its own rendering engine).

## 1.2 Swing

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Swing is the lightweight, platform-independent GUI toolkit for Java, built on top of the AWT architecture and introduced as part of Java Foundation Classes (JFC) in JDK 1.2 (1998). Almost all Swing components are painted directly on canvases using pure Java code (lightweight peers), and the entire component hierarchy inherits from the AWT `Container` class, allowing Swing to interoperate with AWT.*

## 1.3 Why This Comparison Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a **frequently repeated question** in KTU Module-4 Question Papers, typically appearing as a **10-mark comparison** in Part B and as a **3-mark definition** in Part A. Mastering the *differences table* alone can secure 5–7 marks instantly.

The Java AWT/Swing architecture follows a **bottom-up component model** rooted in `java.awt.Component`, the abstract superclass of every visual element. The standard metric used to classify components is **"Peer Dependency"** — whether a component requires an underlying OS-level object (**heavyweight**) or paints itself in pure Java (**lightweight**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

## 2.1 Architecture Breakdown

### AWT Architecture — The Peer Model
AWT works on a **delegate (peer) model**. For every Java component instantiated, a corresponding **native peer** is allocated by the underlying OS.

**Step-by-step operational flow:**
1. Programmer calls `new Button("OK");`
2. JNI (Java Native Interface) calls into the OS to create a native window/control.
3. The native peer draws the button, handles the click, and dispatches the event back into the Java AWT EventQueue.
4. **Result:** 1 Java object ↔ 1 native peer ↔ 1 OS resource. This is **why AWT is called heavyweight**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Heavyweight Component Definition:** A component whose visual representation and behavior depend on a platform-specific native object (peer). It consumes OS-level resources (window handles).

### Swing Architecture — The Pluggable Model
Swing uses the **Model-View-Controller (MVC) design pattern** and the **Lightweight Model**. Most components descend from `JComponent` (which itself extends `java.awt.Container`), and they are painted on the canvas of a top-level heavyweight AWT container (like `JFrame` or `JApplet`).

**Step-by-step operational flow:**
1. Programmer calls `new JButton("OK");`
2. No native peer is created. The JButton is a **100% pure Java object**.
3. The button is painted by Java's own `BasicLookAndFeel` renderer onto the canvas provided by the parent AWT container.
4. The Look-and-Feel (L&F) is **pluggable** — can be swapped at runtime via `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lightweight Component Definition:** A component that does not have a native peer; it shares the native screen resource of its nearest heavyweight ancestor and is drawn entirely by Java code.

## 2.2 Component Class Hierarchy

$$
\text{AWT} : \ \text{Object} \rightarrow \text{Component} \rightarrow \text{Container} \rightarrow \text{JComponent (used by Swing)}
$$

$$
\text{Swing} : \ \text{Object} \rightarrow \text{Component} \rightarrow \text{Container} \rightarrow \text{JComponent} \rightarrow \{\text{JButton, JLabel, JTextField, JTable, JTree, \dots}\}
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **single most important artefact** for the KTU exam. Memorizing the bolded differences usually fetches full marks in comparison questions.

| # | Parameter | AWT (`java.awt.*`) | Swing (`javax.swing.*`) |
|---|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Introduced In** | **JDK 1.0** (1996) | **JDK 1.2** (1998) as part of JFC |
| 2 | **Package** | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` (prefix `J`) |
| 3 | **Component Weight** | **Heavyweight** (native peer) | **Lightweight** (no native peer) |
| 4 | **Platform Dependency** | **Platform-dependent** appearance | **Platform-independent** consistent appearance |
| 5 | **Pluggable Look & Feel** | **No** (fixed OS look) | **Yes** (Metal, Nimbus, Motif, System) |
| 6 | **MVC Support** | No | **Yes** (MVC-based architecture) |
| 7 | **Component Count** | Limited (~14 peer classes) | **Rich** (4× more components — tables, trees, tabs, etc.) |
| 8 | **Top-Level Containers** | `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Applet` | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`, `JWindow` |
| 9 | **Event Handling** | Same delegation model | Same delegation model (compatible) |
| 10 | **Performance** | Faster (native rendering) | Slightly slower (Java painting) |
| 11 | **Memory Footprint** | High (each peer = OS handle) | Lower (shared canvas) |
| 12 | **Z-ordering Issue** | Heavyweights always overlap lightweights | Resolved (Z-order bug) |
| 13 | **Threading (Repaint)** | Not thread-safe | **Not thread-safe** (but uses `SwingUtilities.invokeLater`) |
| 14 | **Double Buffering** | Manual | **Built-in** |
| 15 | **Current Status (2024)** | **Legacy / discouraged** | **Standard / recommended** |

> **Engineering Utility:** AWT is essentially **deprecated for new development** in modern Java (Java SE 11+ and 2024 LTS releases). However, it remains in the KTU 2024 syllabus because every Swing application still **inherits from AWT's `Container`**, and **interoperability** between the two is a tested concept.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AWT** survives today mainly because it forms the **graphics root of all Java UI**. Even JavaFX (the modern successor) is built on top of a similar pipeline.
- **Swing** is the **de-facto UI toolkit** for cross-platform desktop tools (e.g., IDEs like older NetBeans, IntelliJ's early versions). It is still taught because it is the foundation for understanding **MVC**, **event dispatching**, and **pluggable rendering**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Code-Level Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> This section performs a **derivation-style walkthrough** comparing the two toolkits across multiple dimensions, mirroring how a KTU examiner evaluates a 14-mark comparison answer.

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — Weight Classification

We can mathematically represent the "weight" $W$ of a UI component as a function of native resource usage:

$$
W = 
\begin{cases}
H & \text{if component allocates an OS window handle} \\
L & \text{if component paints onto a shared canvas}
\end{cases}
$$

Where $H$ = **Heavyweight** ($H \gg L$) and $L$ = **Lightweight**.

**Step 1 — AWT Button Analysis:**
A Java `Button` instantiation triggers `Toolkit.createButton(peer)`, which invokes JNI:
$$
\text{Java } \rightarrow \text{JNI} \rightarrow \text{OS-Specific-Code} \rightarrow \text{Window Handle Allocated}
$$
Therefore, $W_{AWT} = H$. Resource cost: $O(n)$ where $n$ = number of components (each gets a handle).

**Step 2 — Swing JButton Analysis:**
A `JButton` is a pure Java object extending `AbstractButton`:
$$
\text{Java Object only} \rightarrow \text{Painted by } \texttt{paintComponent(Graphics g)}
$$
Therefore, $W_{Swing} = L$. Resource cost: $O(1)$ for the button itself (shared canvas).

**Step 3 — Conclusion on Memory:**
$$
\text{Memory}_{AWT} \approx n \times S_{peer} \quad ; \quad \text{Memory}_{Swing} \approx S_{canvas} + n \times S_{javaObject}
$$
Where $S_{peer} \gg S_{javaObject}$, proving Swing's **superior memory efficiency** for complex UIs.

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough — Look-and-Feel Mechanism

AWT's appearance is **static and inherited**:
$$
\text{Appearance}_{AWT} = f(\text{OS}_{platform})
$$

Swing's appearance is **dynamic and injected**:
$$
\text{Appearance}_{Swing} = f(\text{L\&F}_{class})
$$

The L&F is selected via:
$$
\texttt{UIManager.setLookAndFeel}(\text{className})
$$

**Default L&F in 2024:** `javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel` (replaced the old "Metal" theme in Java SE 6u10+).

## 3.3 Event-Handling Compatibility Proof

Both toolkits dispatch events to the **AWT EventQueue** (since Swing inherits from `java.awt.Container`):
$$
\text{Event Source} \xrightarrow{\text{dispatch}} \text{EventQueue} \xrightarrow{\text{deliver}} \text{Listener}
$$

This guarantees **AWT and Swing components can coexist in the same container** (a critical interop feature).

## 3.4 Code-Level Side-by-Side Demonstration

```java
// ===== AWT Example =====
import java.awt.*;
public class AwtDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Frame f = new Frame("AWT Window");
        Button b = new Button("Click Me");
        b.setBounds(50, 80, 80, 30);
        f.add(b);
        f.setSize(300, 200);
        f.setLayout(null);
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

```java
// ===== Swing Example =====
import javax.swing.*;
public class SwingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame f = new JFrame("Swing Window");
        JButton b = new JButton("Click Me");
        b.setBounds(50, 80, 120, 30);
        f.add(b);
        f.setSize(300, 200);
        f.setLayout(null);
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Key observation:** Only the class names differ (`Frame`→`JFrame`, `Button`→`JButton`, package changed from `java.awt` to `javax.swing`). The API is **deliberately similar** to make migration easy.

## 3.5 Z-Order Bug Explanation (The Famous "Heavyweight Overlap" Problem)

When AWT (heavyweight) and Swing (lightweight) components are mixed in the same window, the AWT peer **always wins** the Z-order fight because the OS places it on a higher z-stacking layer:
$$
Z_{native} > Z_{java} \implies \text{AWT components ALWAYS render on top}
$$
Swing resolved this in JDK 1.1.1+ by using the `getMouseEventTarget` algorithm, but it remains a **classic interview question**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy — AWT vs Swing

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[java.lang.Object]
    A1[java.awt.Component]
    A2[java.awt.Container]
    A3[javax.swing.JComponent]

    B1[java.awt.Button]
    B2[java.awt.TextField]
    B3[java.awt.Label]
    B4[java.awt.Checkbox]

    C1[java.awt.Frame]
    C2[java.awt.Dialog]
    C3[java.awt.Applet]

    D1[javax.swing.JButton]
    D2[javax.swing.JTextField]
    D3[javax.swing.JLabel]
    D4[javax.swing.JCheckBox]
    D5[javax.swing.JTable]
    D6[javax.swing.JTree]
    D7[javax.swing.JTabbedPane]

    E1[javax.swing.JFrame]
    E2[javax.swing.JDialog]
    E3[javax.swing.JApplet]

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3

    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A2 --> B4

    A2 --> C1
    A2 --> C2
    A2 --> C3

    A3 --> D1
    A3 --> D2
    A3 --> D3
    A3 --> D4
    A3 --> D5
    A3 --> D6
    A3 --> D7

    C1 -.extends.-> E1
    C2 -.extends.-> E2
    C3 -.extends.-> E3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** *Solid arrows = direct inheritance. Dotted arrows = Swing extension of the corresponding AWT top-level container. This visually proves why Swing always "sits on top of" AWT.*

## 4.2 Architecture — Heavyweight vs Lightweight Rendering

```mermaid
graph TD
    OS[Operating System Window Manager]
    NATIVE[Native Peer HW Component e.g. Win32 Button]
    AWT[Java AWT Button object]
    HEAVY[Each AWT Component]
    JCANVAS[AWT Heavyweight Parent e.g. JFrame]
    SWINGOBJ[Swing JButton pure Java object]
    PAINT[paintComponent Graphics method]
    LAF[Pluggable Look and Feel Nimbus Metal etc]

    OS --> NATIVE
    NATIVE --> AWT
    AWT --> HEAVY
    HEAVY --> JCANVAS
    JCANVAS --> SWINGOBJ
    SWINGOBJ --> PAINT
    PAINT --> LAF
    LAF --> JCANVAS
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Event Flow (Both Toolkits)

```mermaid
graph LR
    USER[User Action Mouse Click]
    SRC[Event Source Component]
    EQ[AWT EventQueue EDT]
    DISP[Event Dispatcher]
    L1[Registered Listener 1]
    L2[Registered Listener 2]
    L3[Registered Listener N]

    USER --> SRC
    SRC --> EQ
    EQ --> DISP
    DISP --> L1
    DISP --> L2
    DISP --> L3
```

> **Note:** The event-dispatching thread (EDT) is **shared** by both AWT and Swing, which is why a single `EventQueue` can handle components from both toolkits in the same application window.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]**
*Define the term "lightweight component" in the context of Java GUI. Give one example of a lightweight component.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *lightweight component* is a GUI component that **does not rely on the underlying operating system** for its visual rendering or behavior. It has **no native peer**; instead, it is drawn entirely by Java code onto the canvas provided by its nearest heavyweight ancestor (e.g., `JFrame`). This makes it platform-independent and memory-efficient.
> **Example:** `javax.swing.JButton`, `javax.swing.JLabel`, `javax.swing.JTextField`. *(Any one — 1 mark)*.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
*List any three differences between AWT and Swing.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
> 1. AWT is **platform-dependent** (uses native peers), whereas Swing is **platform-independent** (uses pure Java painting).
> 2. AWT components are **heavyweight**, whereas Swing components are **lightweight**.
> 3. AWT has a **limited set of components** (~14 peer classes), whereas Swing provides a **much richer component library** (JTable, JTree, JTabbedPane, JSlider, etc.).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *As per the KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part-B question of 14 marks has an **internal choice**. You must answer either Option A OR Option B in full.*

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, S5 B.Tech CSE]`

> **(a)** *Explain the architecture of Java AWT with a neat diagram. Differentiate between heavyweight and lightweight components.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *Discuss the features of Swing. List and briefly explain any 5 advanced Swing components with their real-world use cases.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

1. **AWT Architecture (3 marks):** AWT follows the **peer-based architecture**. Every AWT component object in Java has a corresponding **native peer object** created by the OS via the Java Native Interface (JNI). The Java component acts as a thin wrapper that delegates drawing, event handling, and resource management to the peer. (Diagram — see Section 4.2 of these notes.)
2. **Heavyweight Components (2 marks):** Components like `java.awt.Button`, `TextField`, and `Frame` are heavyweight because each one allocates a separate OS window handle. They look like the host OS's native widgets.
3. **Lightweight Components (2 marks):** Components like `javax.swing.JButton` are lightweight because they share the native screen resource of their nearest heavyweight ancestor and are painted using Java's `Graphics` API. They have the **same appearance across all platforms** unless the L&F is changed.

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Features of Swing (2 marks):**
- Pluggable Look-and-Feel (L&F)
- Lightweight component architecture
- MVC design pattern for many components
- Rich set of advanced components (tables, trees, tabs)
- Built-in double buffering for flicker-free rendering
- JavaBeans compliant

**Five Advanced Components (1 mark each):**
1. **`JTable`** — Used to display data in a tabular (row × column) format, e.g., showing a class grade sheet in a college management system.
2. **`JTree`** — Used to display hierarchical data, e.g., the directory structure of a file system.
3. **`JTabbedPane`** — Used to create tabbed interfaces, e.g., browser tabs in a web browser.
4. **`JSlider`** — Used to let users select a value from a range, e.g., volume control in a media player.
5. **`JProgressBar`** — Used to visualize the progress of a long-running task, e.g., file download status.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, S5 B.Tech CSE]`

> **(a)** *Compare AWT and Swing in detail across at least 8 parameters with a tabular comparison.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *Write a Java Swing program to create a `JFrame` containing a `JLabel`, a `JTextField`, and a `JButton`. When the button is clicked, the text from the text field should be displayed in a `JOptionPane` dialog. Explain the code.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

*(Refer to the **Section 2.3 Comparison Sheet** above. The student is expected to reproduce at least 8 rows with both columns clearly filled. Awarding scheme: 1 mark for the table format and 0.5 mark per correctly written parameter — capping at 7 marks.)*

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SwingExample extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JLabel label;
    JTextField tf;
    JButton btn;

    SwingExample() {
        label = new JLabel("Enter Name:");
        label.setBounds(30, 30, 100, 30);
        tf = new JTextField();
        tf.setBounds(140, 30, 150, 30);
        btn = new JButton("Submit");
        btn.setBounds(140, 80, 100, 30);

        add(label); add(tf); add(btn);
        btn.addActionListener(this);

        setSize(350, 200);
        setLayout(null);
        setVisible(true);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String name = tf.getText();
        JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this, "Hello, " + name + "!");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SwingExample();
    }
}
```

**Code Explanation (for marks):**
- **Line `JFrame`** is the top-level Swing container that extends AWT's `Frame` **(1 mark)**.
- `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JButton` are **lightweight Swing components** (prefix `J`) **(1 mark)**.
- `setBounds(x, y, w, h)` positions components using **absolute layout** (null layout) **(1 mark)**.
- The class **implements `ActionListener`** to handle button click events **(1 mark)**.
- `btn.addActionListener(this)` **registers** the current object as the listener **(1 mark)**.
- `actionPerformed` is the **callback method** triggered by the AWT EventQueue on the EDT **(1 mark)**.
- `JOptionPane.showMessageDialog` is a **standard Swing dialog** for showing pop-up messages **(1 mark)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Writing `Button` instead of `JButton`** in code — examiners deduct 1 mark immediately (this violates the `javax.swing` package requirement).
> 2. **Forgetting the `J` prefix** when listing Swing components in theory answers.
> 3. **Confusing AWT and Swing class hierarchies** — never write that `JFrame` extends `JComponent`. It extends `java.awt.Frame` (which is AWT).
> 4. **Skipping the package name** (`java.awt` vs `javax.swing`) — KTU explicitly tests this.
> 5. **Stating "Swing is faster than AWT"** — this is **FALSE**. AWT is faster because of native rendering. Swing trades raw speed for portability and richer features.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Master these and secure full marks.**

- ✅ **AWT** stands for **Abstract Window Toolkit**, package = `java.awt`, introduced in **JDK 1.0**.
- ✅ **Swing** is part of **JFC (Java Foundation Classes)**, package = `javax.swing`, introduced in **JDK 1.2**.
- ✅ **AWT = Heavyweight** (native peers); **Swing = Lightweight** (pure Java).
- ✅ All Swing top-level containers (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`) **inherit from AWT containers** — this is the key interop point.
- ✅ Most Swing components (excluding top-level) **inherit from `javax.swing.JComponent`**, which itself extends `java.awt.Container`.
- ✅ Swing supports **Pluggable Look-and-Feel** via `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)`.
- ✅ Swing follows the **MVC design pattern**; AWT does not.
- ✅ **Z-order rule:** Native (AWT) components always render on top of Swing components when mixed.
- ✅ **Event dispatching** is shared — both use the **AWT EventQueue / EDT**.
- ✅ Swing provides **built-in double buffering** (flicker-free repaint); AWT does not.
- ✅ **Performance trade-off:** AWT is faster; Swing is more portable and feature-rich.
- ✅ Swing has **far more components** (`JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`, `JSlider`, `JProgressBar`, etc.).
- ✅ **Default 2024 L&F:** `Nimbus` (since Java SE 6 Update 10).
- ✅ Swing painting methods to remember: `paintComponent(Graphics g)`, `paintBorder`, `paintChildren` — **always call `super.paintComponent(g)`** first when overriding.
- ✅ In KTU exams, **always show the import line** (`import javax.swing.*;`) for Swing programs and **`import java.awt.*;`** for AWT programs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Key Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Key Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java Swing** is a lightweight, **Model-View-Controller (MVC) based** GUI (Graphical User Interface) toolkit that is part of the Java Foundation Classes (JFC). It is an advanced successor to the Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT) and is built entirely on top of AWT using `javax.swing.*` package. Swing provides a rich set of **pluggable look-and-feel** components, double-buffered painting, and fully customizable UI delegates that run identically across all operating systems.

### 1.2 The Java GUI Toolkit Hierarchy

| Toolkit | Package | Weight | Native Peers | Look & Feel |
|---------|---------|--------|--------------|-------------|
| **AWT** | `java.awt.*` | **Heavyweight** | Yes | Tied to OS |
| **Swing** | `javax.swing.*` | **Lightweight** | No (uses AWT containers only) | **Pluggable** |
| **JavaFX** | `javafx.*` | Lightweight (separate scene graph) | No | Pluggable / CSS-based |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, the syllabus expects students to differentiate between AWT (heavily used in earlier modules) and Swing, and to identify the **key features** that make Swing preferred for modern desktop GUI development.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Smart TV Remote" Model:**
> - Imagine AWT as an old TV remote that has hard-wired buttons (each button is a different physical object that can only look and behave the way its factory built it). It looks different on every TV brand (Sony, Samsung, LG) because the **hardware** dictates the appearance.
> - Swing, on the other hand, is a **universal smart remote**. Its **buttons are software-defined** (lightweight). The same remote can "look like" a Sony remote, a Samsung remote, or a futuristic custom remote — by simply changing its **skin** (Look-and-Feel). The remote itself (logic) is **separated** from the skin (appearance), which is exactly the **MVC philosophy**.

---

## 1.4 The 7 Defining Key Features of Swing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **board-exam essential key features** that must be memorized in a logical order.

1. **Lightweight Components** — written entirely in Java, no OS peer dependencies.
2. **Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF)** — UI appearance can be swapped at runtime.
3. **MVC Architecture** — clean separation of data, view, and control logic.
4. **Double Buffering** — eliminates flicker during repaint operations.
5. **Event Delegation Model** — efficient event handling inherited from Java 1.1.
6. **Rich Component Library** — `JButton`, `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`, etc.
7. **Auxiliary UI Features** — ToolTips, Mnemonics (keyboard shortcuts), Keyboard Accelerators, Borders, and Custom Painting.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Feature 1 — Lightweight Components (LWC)

- A **lightweight component** is one whose **rendering and behavior are entirely controlled by Java code**, not by the underlying operating system's native window system (Win32, Cocoa, X11).
- In AWT, every `Button` corresponded to a **peer object** (a real OS widget) — making them **heavyweight**.
- In Swing, components like `JButton` **inherit** from `java.awt.Container` only to leverage the AWT event mechanism, but they **draw themselves** using `paintComponent(Graphics g)`.

### How Lightweight Components Work

- Every lightweight component has a **"parent" Container that is heavyweight** (usually a `JFrame`, `JApplet`, or `JDialog`).
- The lightweight component is **drawn onto the heavy parent's native canvas** — there is no separate native window created for it.
- **Real-world utility:** This is the reason a Swing app looks **identical** on Windows, macOS, and Linux, whereas AWT apps would look different on each.

### Why Swing Still Uses Some AWT

- AWT provides the **top-level heavyweight windows** (`Frame`, `Dialog`, `Applet`) because the JVM still needs a true OS window to receive native mouse/keyboard events from the kernel.
- All actual UI gadgets — buttons, text fields, panels, trees, tables — are pure Swing.

---

## 2.2 Feature 2 — Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PLAF is the **single most-asked Swing feature** in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) Part A questions.

PLAF is an architecture that allows the **appearance (look)** and **behavior (feel)** of a component to be **swapped at runtime** without changing the underlying component code.

### Built-in Look-and-Feel Options

| L&F Class (constant) | Description | Default since |
|----------------------|-------------|---------------|
| `MetalLookAndFeel` (cross-platform) | Pure Java "Ocean" theme | Java 1.2 → 8 |
| `NimbusLookAndFeel` | Polished modern Java L&F | Java 6u10 / 7+ |
| `WindowsLookAndFeel` | Mimics MS Windows native L&F | Windows JVMs |
| `MotifLookAndFeel` | Mimics old UNIX Motif | Optional |
| `GTKLookAndFeel` | Mimics GNOME GTK | Linux JVMs |
| `MacLookAndFeel` | Mimics macOS Aqua | Apple JVMs |

### Setting the L&F at Runtime — Two Methods

**Method 1 — Static (command-line):**

```java
java -Dswing.defaultlaf=javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel MyApp
```

**Method 2 — Programmatic (best practice):**

```java
try {
    UIManager.setLookAndFeel(
        "javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
    SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(myFrame);
} catch (Exception e) {
    System.err.println("L&F not supported on this platform.");
}
```

### Real-world Utility

- **Theming for accessibility:** visually impaired users can switch to high-contrast L&F.
- **Branding:** enterprise apps load a *custom* L&F with corporate colors and fonts.
- **Cross-platform deployment:** the same `.jar` ships with multiple L&Fs and activates the right one at startup.

---

## 2.3 Feature 3 — MVC Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> Swing components are designed around a **loose MVC** pattern. Most components separate their **state (Model)** from the **visual representation (View + UI Delegate)** and forward user input to listeners (Controller).

| MVC Layer | Swing Representation | Example |
|-----------|----------------------|---------|
| **Model** | Interface like `ButtonModel`, `ListModel`, `TableModel` | `DefaultButtonModel` |
| **View + UI Delegate** | `ComponentUI` subclass (e.g., `BasicButtonUI`) | `MetalButtonUI` |
| **Controller** | Event listeners (`ActionListener`, `MouseListener`) | Anonymous inner class |

- A single Model can be **shared between multiple Views** — for example, the same `DefaultListModel` data shown in a `JList` and a `JComboBox` simultaneously.
- The **UI Delegate** is the actual code that knows how to paint the component. Swapping L&F = swapping the UI Delegate.

---

## 2.4 Feature 4 — Double Buffering

- A **flicker** occurs when the screen is repainted piece-by-piece — the user momentarily sees the cleared background before the new content is drawn.
- **Double buffering** solves this by drawing the **entire scene to an off-screen image buffer** first, then **blitting** (copying) the complete image to the screen in one atomic operation.
- Swing automatically enables double buffering for all top-level containers via `RepaintManager`.
- You can manually toggle it with:

```java
myJComponent.setDoubleBuffered(true);   // default
```

### Why It Matters

- Critical for **animations, drag-and-drop previews, scrolling inside a `JScrollPane`**, and complex `JTable` / `JTree` rendering.

---

## 2.5 Feature 5 — Event Delegation Model

- Inherited from Java 1.1, every Swing component can register **listener objects** that are notified when a semantic event occurs.
- Components are **event sources**; they maintain a list of **event listeners** and call back their methods.
- The **Delegation Model** means the component does *not* handle events itself (unlike the old JDK 1.0 inheritance model).
- Example wiring:

```java
JButton btn = new JButton("Submit");
btn.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Clicked!"));
```

### Real-world Utility

- Decouples the **GUI component** (designed by team A) from the **business logic** (written by team B). Both can be developed and tested independently — a direct application of OOP's *loose-coupling* principle.

---

## 2.6 Feature 6 — Rich Component Library

### Top-Level Containers (must inherit one of these)

| Class | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `JFrame` | Standard window with title bar, minimize/maximize/close |
| `JDialog` | Pop-up dialog (modal or non-modal) |
| `JApplet` | Embedded in a browser (now mostly deprecated) |
| `JWindow` | Bare window — no title bar |

### Helper Containers (intermediate panels)

| Class | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `JPanel` | Generic container for grouping |
| `JScrollPane` | Adds scrollbars to a single child |
| `JSplitPane` | Two-pane resizable splitter |
| `JTabbedPane` | Tabbed interface |
| `JToolBar` | Movable tool bar |
| `JInternalFrame` | Child window inside a `JDesktopPane` (MDI) |

### Atomic Components (the visible widgets)

- `JLabel`, `JButton`, `JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`
- `JTextField`, `JPasswordField`, `JTextArea`, `JEditorPane`, `JTextPane`
- `JList`, `JComboBox`, `JSpinner`, `JSlider`
- `JTable`, `JTree`
- `JMenuBar`, `JMenu`, `JMenuItem`, `JPopupMenu`, `JCheckBoxMenuItem`

---

## 2.7 Feature 7 — Auxiliary UI Features

| Feature | Method | Example |
|---------|--------|---------|
| **ToolTip** | `setToolTipText("Click me")` | Hover help text |
| **Mnemonic** | `setMnemonic('S')` | `Alt+S` activates the button |
| **Accelerator** | `setAccelerator(KeyStroke.getKeyStroke("ctrl S"))` | `Ctrl+S` shortcut |
| **Border** | `setBorder(BorderFactory.createLineBorder(Color.RED))` | Visual frame |
| **Custom Painting** | Override `paintComponent(Graphics g)` | Draw shapes |
| **Drag & Drop** | `setTransferHandler(new TransferHandler("text"))` | DnD text |
| **Accessibility** | `setAccessibleContext(...)` | Screen readers |

---

## 2.8 KTU Cheat Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| Feature | One-line Definition | KTU Favourite Question Type |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| **Lightweight** | Pure-Java component, no OS peer | "Why is Swing portable?" |
| **PLAF** | Runtime swappable UI appearance | "Explain PLAF with example." |
| **MVC** | Model / View / Controller separation | "How does MVC help Swing?" |
| **Double Buffering** | Off-screen drawing eliminates flicker | "What is flicker? How is it solved?" |
| **Event Delegation** | Source delegates events to listener objects | Compare 1.0 vs 1.1 model |
| **Top-level containers** | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` | "Which class is the root of Swing GUI?" |
| **Helper containers** | `JPanel`, `JScrollPane`, `JTabbedPane` | List them with uses |
| **Layout Managers** | `BorderLayout`, `FlowLayout`, `GridLayout`, `BoxLayout`, `GridBagLayout` | Code to arrange components |
| **ToolTip / Mnemonic** | Hover help / Alt-key shortcut | "Differentiate Mnemonic and Accelerator." |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Demonstration Programs

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is a **GUI features** topic (not a numerical-engineering one), the "derivations" below are **logical derivations** of Swing's feature architecture and **annotated working Java programs** that demonstrate each feature end-to-end. KTU 2024 ESE Module 4 explicitly tests the ability to *write* Swing code with these features integrated.

---

## 3.1 Logical Derivation — How Double Buffering Mathematically Works

Let a single repaint cycle consist of $N$ primitive draw calls. Each call takes time $t_i$ and writes to the screen buffer $S$.

Without double buffering, the screen buffer is updated $N$ times:

$$
S_{\text{after}} = f_N \circ f_{N-1} \circ \dots \circ f_1(S_{\text{before}})
$$

The user **sees each intermediate state**, so flicker occurs.

With double buffering, an off-screen buffer $B$ is used:

$$
B_{\text{final}} = f_N \circ f_{N-1} \circ \dots \circ f_1(B_{\text{clear}})
$$

Then **one atomic transfer** happens:

$$
S_{\text{after}} = \text{blit}(B_{\text{final}}) \rightarrow S_{\text{before}}
$$

The user only sees $S_{\text{before}} \rightarrow S_{\text{after}}$ in **one frame**, hence no flicker.

> The **frame rate** requirement is derived from the **refresh rate** $r$ (Hz). For a smooth experience, repaint must complete within $1/r$ seconds. Double buffering guarantees that all $N$ draw calls occur *before* the next vertical blank.

---

## 3.2 Logical Derivation — Event Delegation's Fire-and-Forget Pattern

For a source component $C$ with $k$ registered listeners $\lbrace L_1, L_2, \dots, L_k \rbrace$, the firing of an event $e$ is:

$$
C.\texttt{fireEvent}(e) \;\Longrightarrow\; \forall\, i \in [1,k]\;:\; L_i.\texttt{notify}(e)
$$

The **complexity** of dispatch is $O(k)$, independent of the GUI tree size — making the model **scalable** for large GUIs.

In contrast, the legacy **JDK 1.0 inheritance model** required the event to travel up the containment hierarchy until a parent chose to `handleEvent()`. This is $O(d)$ where $d$ is the tree depth.

$$
\text{Delegation speed-up factor} = \frac{d}{k} \quad \text{(typically} \gg 1 \text{ for deep trees)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Features Combine in a Real App

| Step | Swing Feature Used | Code Construct |
|------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1. Create window | Top-level container | `new JFrame("App")` |
| 2. Set close behaviour | AWT event hook | `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` |
| 3. Add content pane | Helper container | `JPanel content = new JPanel(new BorderLayout())` |
| 4. Add widgets | Atomic components | `content.add(new JButton("OK"), BorderLayout.SOUTH)` |
| 5. Wire event | Event Delegation | `okBtn.addActionListener(...)` |
| 6. Show window | Visibility | `frame.setSize(400, 300); frame.setVisible(true);` |
| 7. Apply L&F | PLAF | `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)` |
| 8. Polish UI | Auxiliary features | `setToolTipText`, `setMnemonic`, `setBorder` |
| 9. Smooth redraw | Double buffering | (automatic via `JComponent` default) |
| 10. Decouple logic | MVC | `DefaultTableModel` for `JTable` |

---

## 3.4 Annotated Program — A Complete Swing Demo Showing ALL 7 Key Features

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import javax.swing.border.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SwingKeyFeaturesDemo {

    public static void main(String[] args) {

        // ---- FEATURE 2: Pluggable Look-and-Feel ----
        try {
            UIManager.setLookAndFeel(
                "javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
        } catch (Exception ex) {
            System.out.println("Nimbus unavailable, using default.");
        }

        // ---- FEATURE 6: Top-level container (JFrame) ----
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("KTU Swing Demo");
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.setSize(500, 350);

        // ---- FEATURE 6: Helper container (JPanel + BorderLayout) ----
        JPanel content = new JPanel(new BorderLayout(10, 10));
        content.setBorder(BorderFactory.createEmptyBorder(15, 15, 15, 15));

        // ---- FEATURE 6: Atomic components ----
        JLabel header = new JLabel("Student Registration", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        header.setFont(new Font("SansSerif", Font.BOLD, 22));

        JTextField nameField = new JTextField(20);
        nameField.setToolTipText("Enter full name");          // FEATURE 7: ToolTip

        JButton submit = new JButton("Submit");
        submit.setMnemonic('S');                              // FEATURE 7: Mnemonic (Alt+S)
        submit.setBorder(BorderFactory.createRaisedBevelBorder()); // FEATURE 7: Border

        // ---- FEATURE 5: Event Delegation Model ----
        submit.addActionListener(e -> {
            String name = nameField.getText().trim();
            if (name.isEmpty()) {
                JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(frame,
                    "Name cannot be empty!", "Error",
                    JOptionPane.ERROR_MESSAGE);
            } else {
                JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(frame,
                    "Welcome, " + name + "!", "Success",
                    JOptionPane.INFORMATION_MESSAGE);
            }
        });

        JPanel center = new JPanel();
        center.add(new JLabel("Name:"));
        center.add(nameField);

        // ---- FEATURE 4: Double buffering is automatic on JPanel ----
        // (no code needed – handled by RepaintManager)

        // ---- FEATURE 3: MVC – model the form data in a separate class ----
        StudentModel model = new StudentModel();

        content.add(header,   BorderLayout.NORTH);
        content.add(center,   BorderLayout.CENTER);
        content.add(submit,   BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        frame.setContentPane(content);
        // Re-apply L&F to all already-created components
        SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame);

        // FEATURE 6: Show the GUI – this triggers double-buffered render
        frame.setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }

    // ---- FEATURE 3: A separate Model class (MVC) ----
    static class StudentModel {
        private String name = "";
        public void setName(String n) { this.name = n; }
        public String getName()       { return name; }
    }
}
```

### Output Description

> The window opens with a Nimbus-themed header "Student Registration", a label "Name:", an empty text field showing the tooltip "Enter full name" on hover, and a Submit button. Pressing `Alt+S` triggers the button (mnemonic). On click, a dialog shows "Welcome, [name]!" or an error dialog if empty. Repainting during the dialog fade-in is flicker-free because `JDialog` and `JFrame` automatically double-buffer.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Table — AWT vs Swing

| Property | AWT | Swing |
|----------|-----|-------|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Peer class | Yes (OS native) | No (pure Java) |
| Weight | Heavy | Light |
| Look & Feel | OS-dependent | **Pluggable** |
| Components | Limited (~20) | Rich (~50+) |
| Architecture | Inheritance | **MVC** |
| Double buffering | Manual | **Automatic** |
| Custom painting | Override `paint()` | Override `paintComponent()` |
| Learning curve | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Performance | Faster on small GUI | Comparable / slower at start |
| Status | Legacy (still supported) | Recommended for new desktop apps |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

## 4.2 Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["JButton instance"] --> B["ButtonModel (Data)"]
    A --> C["ComponentUI Delegate"]
    A --> L1["ActionListener #1"]
    A --> L2["ActionListener #2"]
    C --> C1["MetalButtonUI"]
    C --> C2["NimbusButtonUI"]
    C --> C3["WindowsButtonUI"]
    C --> C4["CustomBrandButtonUI"]
    UIManager["UIManager.setLookAndFeel"] -.->|swaps| C
    classDef model fill:#ef476f,stroke:#9d1b40,color:#fff;
    classDef ui fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#0a8754,color:#000;
    classDef ctrl fill:#118ab2,stroke:#073b4c,color:#fff;
    class B,L1,L2 model;
    class C1,C2,C3,C4 ui;
    class C,UIManager,A ctrl;
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The `JButton` holds a `ButtonModel` (red), a swappable `ComponentUI` (green — one of the four L&F implementations), and zero or more `ActionListener` controllers (red, separate from model). The `UIManager` is the runtime switch that toggles the UI delegate at any time without touching the model or listeners.

---

## 4.3 MVC Information Flow Inside a Swing Component

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant View as View + UI Delegate
    participant Ctrl as Event Listener (Controller)
    participant Model as Component Model

    User->>View: Click / Key press
    View->>Ctrl: dispatch ActionEvent
    Ctrl->>Model: update state (e.g. setSelected)
    Model-->>View: fire state-change event
    View->>View: repaint() -> paintComponent()
    View-->>User: new visual appearance
    Note over View,Model: Double-buffered,<br/>flicker-free paint
```

---

## 4.4 Event Delegation Model — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph src [Event Source]
        B1[JButton] --> LL[List of Listeners]
    end

        L2[ActionListener B]
        L3[ActionListener C]
    end
    LL --> L1
    LL --> L2
    LL --> L3
    EV[ActionEvent object] -->|created on click| B1
    B1 -.->|notify all| L1
    B1 -.->|notify all| L2
    B1 -.->|notify all| L3

    class L1,L2,L3 lstStyle;
    class EV evStyle;
```

---

## 4.5 Top-Level vs Helper vs Atomic — Containment Block Architecture

    CP -->|SOUTH| CP3["JPanel buttons (Helper)"]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar]** 
*List any four key features of Java Swing. Briefly explain Pluggable Look and Feel.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Java Swing is a lightweight, MVC-based GUI toolkit that extends AWT. Four key features are:

1. **Lightweight components** — written in pure Java, no OS peer.
2. **Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF)** — UI appearance can be changed at runtime.
3. **Double buffering** — eliminates repaint flicker.
4. **MVC architecture** — separates data, view, and control.

**PLAF (1 mark detail):** Swing separates the *component data* from its *visual representation* via a `ComponentUI` delegate. The active delegate is controlled by `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String)`, so a single application can render in Metal, Nimbus, Windows, Motif, or a custom L&F without changing the source code.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Similar]**
*Differentiate between heavyweight and lightweight components with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Heavyweight (AWT) | Lightweight (Swing) |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Native peer | Has a peer object mapped to an OS widget | No native peer |
| OS dependency | Look & feel varies by OS | Uniform across OS |
| Performance | Faster initial render | Slightly slower; double-buffered |
| Example | `java.awt.Button` | `javax.swing.JButton` |
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |

A lightweight Swing component uses an enclosing AWT heavyweight container (e.g. `JFrame`) to receive native events but draws itself using `paintComponent(Graphics g)`.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** import `java.awt.*` components when the question asks for Swing — you will lose 1–2 marks for mixing AWT widgets.
> - **Do NOT** call `frame.setSize(...)` *after* `setVisible(true)` and then expect the layout to update — call sizing **before** visibility.
> - **Do NOT** forget `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` — the program will *appear* to work but the JVM stays alive (1 mark deduction).
> - **Do NOT** write `paint(Graphics g)` to customize a Swing component — always override `paintComponent(Graphics g)` and call `super.paintComponent(g)` first. (2-mark penalty for forgetting `super.` call.)
> - When the question says "explain MVC", you **must** mention all three roles: Model, View, and Controller with one Swing example. Single-line answers lose 3 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Q8a/b]** *(Emerged as favourite question type across Dec 2022, May 2023, Dec 2023 papers.)*

**Q. (a)** Explain the Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF) feature of Java Swing. Write a Java program that demonstrates switching between Metal and Nimbus look-and-feel at runtime through a menu selection. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss the MVC architecture followed by Swing components. How does it help in designing reusable GUI components? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution A(a) — Pluggable Look and Feel (7 marks)

**Explanation (4 marks):**

PLAF allows the **appearance** (look) and **behaviour** (feel) of every Swing component to be **swapped at runtime** without modifying application code. This is achieved by:

- A separate **`ComponentUI` delegate** class for each component per L&F (e.g., `MetalButtonUI`, `NimbusButtonUI`).
- The **`UIManager` singleton** that tracks the active L&F.
- The **`SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(Component)`** call that walks the entire component tree and re-installs the new UI delegates.

**Built-in L&Fs:** Metal (cross-platform default), Nimbus (modern), Windows, Motif, GTK.

**Working Program (3 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class PLAFDemo extends JFrame {

    PLAFDemo() {
        setTitle("PLAF Demo");
        setSize(400, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        JButton metalBtn = new JButton("Use Metal L&F");
        JButton nimbusBtn = new JButton("Use Nimbus L&F");

        metalBtn.addActionListener(e ->
            setLAF("javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel"));
        nimbusBtn.addActionListener(e ->
            setLAF("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel"));

        add(metalBtn);
        add(nimbusBtn);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    void setLAF(String lafClassName) {
        try {
            UIManager.setLookAndFeel(lafClassName);
            SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(this);
        } catch (Exception ex) {
            JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this,
                "L&F not available: " + ex.getMessage());
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(PLAFDemo::new);
    }
}
```

**Program explanation (key lines):**
- `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(className)` — activates the requested L&F class via reflection.
- `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(this)` — re-applies the L&F to all child components.
- `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` — ensures thread-safe GUI creation on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**.

---

#### Solution A(b) — MVC Architecture in Swing (7 marks)

**The MVC Triad (3 marks):**

| Role | Swing Realisation |
|------|-------------------|
| **Model** | Data interfaces such as `ButtonModel`, `ListModel`, `Document`, `TableModel`. Stores component state. |
| **View + UI Delegate** | `ComponentUI` subclasses (e.g., `BasicButtonUI`) that know **how to paint** the component for a specific L&F. |
| **Controller** | Event listeners such as `ActionListener`, `ChangeListener`, `MouseListener`. Receive events from the view, modify the model. |

**How MVC helps (4 marks):**

1. **Reusability:** A single `DefaultTableModel` can drive both a `JTable` and a `JTree` view (with adapter), avoiding data duplication.
2. **Pluggable appearance:** Because the View is a separate delegate, swapping the L&F is a one-line change — the Model is untouched.
3. **Testability:** Business logic in the Model can be unit-tested *without* instantiating a GUI.
4. **Multiple synchronized views:** A slider's `BoundedRangeModel` can simultaneously drive a `JLabel`'s text and a `JProgressBar`'s value — perfect for dashboard GUIs.
5. **Team productivity:** UI designers tweak the View class while backend developers extend the Model — clean parallel development.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **INTERNAL CHOICE alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Q8 alt]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the following key features of Swing: (i) Lightweight Components (ii) Double Buffering. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a simple calculator GUI with buttons 0–9, +, −, *, /, and =. Use a `JTextField` to display input and result, and handle button clicks using the **event delegation model**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution B(a) — Lightweight Components & Double Buffering (7 marks)

**(i) Lightweight Components (3.5 marks):**

A **lightweight component** is a component whose rendering is fully controlled by Java code rather than by the host operating system. Unlike AWT's `Button` (which has a peer in the OS windowing system), a `JButton` has **no peer**.

- It inherits from `java.awt.Container` only to leverage the AWT event pipeline.
- It must be hosted inside a **heavyweight** ancestor (typically `JFrame`, `JDialog`, or `JApplet`) — this ancestor is the *only* place where the JVM creates a true native window.
- It draws itself in `paintComponent(Graphics g)`, which is invoked by the parent's paint cycle.
- **Advantages:** consistent appearance across platforms, ability to render semi-transparent and non-rectangular shapes, and small memory footprint (no JNI bridge to OS).

**(ii) Double Buffering (3.5 marks):**

- **Problem:** Direct repainting of a complex Swing component (e.g., scrolling a `JTable` with 10 000 rows) causes **flicker** — the user momentarily sees the cleared background between drawing operations.
- **Solution:** Swing creates an **off-screen `Image` buffer** of the same size as the component. All `paintComponent` operations render into this off-screen buffer first; once complete, the entire buffer is **atomically blitted** to the screen in one operation.
- **Implementation:** Managed by `javax.swing.RepaintManager`. Enabled by default for all `JComponent` subclasses. Can be toggled via `component.setDoubleBuffered(true)`.
- **Real-world utility:** Mandatory for smooth animations, drag-and-drop ghosting, and any `JComponent` with frequently changing graphics.

---

#### Solution B(b) — Calculator GUI Program (7 marks)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class Calculator extends JFrame implements ActionListener {

    JTextField display = new JTextField("0");
    String operator = "";
    double firstOperand = 0;
    boolean startNew = true;

    Calculator() {
        setTitle("KTU Calculator");
        setSize(260, 320);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5));

        display.setEditable(false);
        display.setFont(new Font("Monospaced", Font.BOLD, 22));
        display.setHorizontalAlignment(JTextField.RIGHT);
        add(display, BorderLayout.NORTH);

        JPanel grid = new JPanel(new GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5));
        String[] labels = {
            "7","8","9","/",
            "4","5","6","*",
            "1","2","3","-",
            "0","=","C","+"
        };
        for (String lbl : labels) {
            JButton b = new JButton(lbl);
            b.setFont(new Font("SansSerif", Font.BOLD, 18));
            b.addActionListener(this);   // Event Delegation
            grid.add(b);
        }
        add(grid, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String cmd = e.getActionCommand();
        if (cmd.matches("[0-9]")) {
            if (startNew) { display.setText(cmd); startNew = false; }
            else          { display.setText(display.getText() + cmd); }
        } else if (cmd.matches("[+\\-*/]")) {
            firstOperand = Double.parseDouble(display.getText());
            operator     = cmd;
            startNew     = true;
        } else if (cmd.equals("=")) {
            double second = Double.parseDouble(display.getText());
            double result = switch (operator) {
                case "+" -> firstOperand + second;
                case "-" -> firstOperand - second;
                case "*" -> firstOperand * second;
                case "/" -> second == 0 ? 0 : firstOperand / second;
                default  -> second;
            };
            display.setText(String.valueOf(result));
            startNew = true;
        } else if (cmd.equals("C")) {
            display.setText("0");
            firstOperand = 0;
            operator     = "";
            startNew     = true;
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Calculator::new);
    }
}
```

**Explanation of how the features are used in the program (2 marks):**

| Line | Feature Illustrated |
|------|---------------------|
| `Calculator extends JFrame` | Top-level container (Feature 6) |
| `JPanel grid = new JPanel(new GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5))` | Helper container + layout manager |
| `b.addActionListener(this)` | **Event Delegation Model** — the JButton is the event source, this `Calculator` is the listener (Feature 5) |
| Automatic `setDoubleBuffered(true)` on `JComponent` | **Double Buffering** (Feature 4) — repaint during digit appends is flicker-free |
| `setEditable(false)`, `setHorizontalAlignment(...)` | Auxiliary UI features (Feature 7) |
| `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` | Thread-safety — Swing components must be created on the EDT |

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [x] **Swing is lightweight** — pure Java, no OS peer; it lives in `javax.swing.*`.
- [x] **Swing requires an AWT heavyweight ancestor** at the root of every GUI tree (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, or `JApplet`).
- [x] **PLAF** = swappable `ComponentUI` delegate; controlled by `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String)`; requires `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(root)` to refresh existing components.
- [x] **MVC triad** — Model (`ButtonModel`, `TableModel`, etc.), View (`ComponentUI`), Controller (event listeners).
- [x] **Double buffering** is **automatic** for every `JComponent`; managed by `RepaintManager`.
- [x] **Event Delegation Model** = source-to-listener pattern (`addXxxListener`); efficient, decouples GUI from logic.
- [x] **Top-level containers:** `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`.
- [x] **Helper containers:** `JPanel`, `JScrollPane`, `JSplitPane`, `JTabbedPane`, `JToolBar`, `JInternalFrame`.
- [x] **Atomic components include** `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JTextArea`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JList`, `JComboBox`, `JTable`, `JTree`.
- [x] **Auxiliary UI features** — ToolTip (hover help), Mnemonic (Alt+key), Accelerator (Ctrl+key), Borders, Custom Painting via `paintComponent(Graphics g)`.
- [x] **Always** create Swing GUIs inside `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)` to run on the Event Dispatch Thread.
- [x] **Always** override `paintComponent(Graphics g)`, not `paint(Graphics g)`, and call `super.paintComponent(g)` first.
- [x] **Default L&F:** Metal was default from Java 1.2 to Java 8; **Nimbus** became the default in **Java 9+**.
- [x] **Key package for utility classes:** `javax.swing.BorderFactory`, `SwingUtilities`, `UIManager`, `JOptionPane`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model View Controller (MVC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Model View Controller (MVC) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** is a compound design pattern (an architectural pattern) that decouples the three concerns of an interactive software application — **data management (Model)**, **presentation logic (View)**, and **business/control logic (Controller)** — by enforcing the **Separation of Concerns (SoC)** principle and applying the **Observer / Strategy** patterns internally to allow parallel development, higher cohesion, low coupling, and easier maintenance. In the context of the KTU 2024 *Object Oriented Programming Concepts* syllabus (Module 4 — Architectural & Enterprise Patterns), MVC is positioned as a classical **architectural design pattern** documented originally by **Trygve Reenskaug** (1979, Xerox PARC) that divides an interactive application into three interconnected components so that the representation of information (View) is kept separate from the way the information is received from the user (Controller) and from the underlying business data and rules (Model). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Imagine a restaurant: - **Model** → the **Kitchen** (ingredients, recipes, stock data). It never speaks directly to customers. - **View** → the **Plated Dish and Menu Card** (the visual representation). It only shows what the kitchen prepared. - **Controller** → the **Waiter** (accepts the customer's order, conveys it to the kitchen, and brings back the plated dish). When the customer (user) changes the order, only the **Waiter (Controller)** is contacted. The **Kitchen (Model)** updates internally, and the **Dish/Plating (View)** is freshly re-presented. None of the three parts reach across the others. This is precisely the **separation of concerns** that MVC mandates. > [!NOTE] > **Key high-yield terminologies (must memorize for KTU):** > - **Model** – Holds the application data and business rules. It is **notification-based**; it broadcasts state changes to subscribed views. > - **View** – A passive rendering layer. It does not contain business logic; it only **pulls/displays** data from the Model. > - **Controller** – Translates user gestures (clicks, keystrokes) into Model operations and selects the appropriate View. > - **Loose Coupling** – Components are independent; one can be replaced without rewriting others. > - **Reenskaug, 1979** – Year and origin of the MVC pattern. ### 1.3 Core Benefits Quantitatively Stated | Metric | Without MVC | With MVC | Standard / Reference | |---|---|---|---| | **Coupling** | High (Tight) | **Low (Loose)** | ISO/IEC 25010 Maintainability | | **Cohesion** | Low | **High** | Robert C. Martin (SRP) | | **Testability (Unit-test coverage)** | ~30 % | **Up to 85–95 %** | Industry benchmark | | **Parallel developer throughput** | 1× | **≈ 3×** (one per tier) | Empirical project reports | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Component Responsibilities — The 3-Tier Breakdown The MVC pattern is best understood as a **3-tier separation** with strict interaction rules. Each component has a single, well-defined responsibility. #### 2.1.1 The Model (Data + Business Rules) - Encapsulates the **state** of the application (entities, data structures, databases). - Implements **business logic** (validation, computation, persistence). - **Notifies** registered observers (Views) when its state changes — uses the **Observer Pattern** internally. - Has **no knowledge** of the View or Controller. - Examples: a `Student` POJO, an `Account` business class, an ORM entity. #### 2.1.2 The View (Presentation Layer) - Renders the Model into a form suitable for the user interface (HTML, Swing, JavaFX, Android XML, etc.). - **Pulls** data from the Model whenever it is notified of a change (the **"pull" model**), or receives a fully-formed DTO (the **"push" model**). - Contains **no business logic** — only display formatting. - One Model can have **multiple Views** (e.g., tabular view + chart view + PDF report). #### 2.1.3 The Controller (Application Logic / Input Handler) - Receives and translates **user input events** (mouse clicks, keyboard, HTTP requests). - **Invokes** the appropriate Model method(s) to mutate state. - **Selects the next View** to render the response. - Acts as the **mediator** between Model and View — neither one calls the other directly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical rule for KTU 14-mark answers:** In *Classical MVC*, the **View never updates the Model directly** and the **Model never references the View directly**. All communication is mediated by the Controller and the Observer notification. ### 2.2 The 7-Step Interaction Lifecycle (Canonical MVC Flow) This is the standard sequence examiners expect you to reproduce: 1. **User Action** — The user interacts with the UI (e.g., clicks a *Submit* button on the View). 2. **View Dispatches Event** — The View forwards the raw event to its associated Controller. 3. **Controller Interprets** — The Controller parses the event, validates the input, and decides which Model action to invoke. 4. **Controller Updates Model** — The Controller calls the appropriate setter or business method on the Model. 5. **Model Mutates State** — The Model updates its internal data and business rules. 6. **Model Notifies Observers** — The Model fires a `stateChanged()` notification to all subscribed Views (Observer pattern). 7. **View Re-renders** — Each View pulls the new state from the Model and re-renders itself. The Controller may also select a **different View** (e.g., success page) as a final step. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Quick Revision) | # | Term / Equation | Definition / Symbol | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C = \{ M, V, C \}$ | The MVC *triad* set | M = Model, V = View, C = Controller | | 2 | $R_{MV} = 1{:}N$ | Model-to-View ratio (1 Model, N Views) | One Model, many Views | | 3 | $R_{CV} = 1{:}1$ | Controller-to-View ratio | Each View typically has one Controller | | 4 | $L(M \leftrightarrow V) = 0$ | Direct M↔V dependency must be **zero** | Enforced by Controller mediation | | 5 | $\text{Coupling}(M, V) = 0$ | $0$ indicates fully decoupled | Lower is better (KTU scoring) | | 6 | $\text{Cohesion}(M), \text{Cohesion}(V), \text{Cohesion}(C) = \text{High}$ | Single Responsibility Principle satisfied | Robert C. Martin | | 7 | $\text{Test Coverage} \uparrow \propto \frac{1}{\text{Coupling}}$ | Higher decoupling → higher testability | Empirical heuristic | | 8 | $\text{Notification Pattern} = \text{Observer}$ | Model broadcasts via Observer | GoF pattern | | 9 | $\text{Input Handling Pattern} = \text{Strategy}$ | Controller uses Strategy for input logic | GoF pattern | | 10 | $\text{Origin} = 1979, \text{Xerox PARC}$ | Trygve Reenskaug | Must appear in any 14-mark answer | > [!NOTE] > Note carefully: in the above table, the pipe `|` character is **never** used for absolute value. Where absolute value is needed, the formula sheet uses $\vert x \vert$ via LaTeX `\vert`, not `|x|`. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Web frameworks** — Spring MVC (Java), Django (Python MVT variant), Ruby on Rails, ASP.NET MVC, Express.js. - **Mobile applications** — Android (Activities as Controllers, XML/Compose as Views, Room/Repository as Model), iOS MVC variant. - **Desktop GUIs** — Java Swing, JavaFX, .NET WinForms, Qt. - **Enterprise benefits** — Parallel team scaling, A/B testing of views, unit testing of model in isolation, model reuse across Web/Mobile/CLI clients. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — A `Student` MVC Application (Java) Below is a fully worked-out Java 17 implementation of a tiny `Student` record CRUD app. **Every line is annotated** so the KTU examiner can reward step-by-step understanding. #### 3.1.1 The Model — `Student.java` ```java // MODEL LAYER — pure data + business rules. NO Swing, NO AWT, NO HTTP imports. import java.util.ArrayList; import java.util.List; public class Student { private String name; private int marks; private final List observers = new ArrayList<>(); public Student(String name, int marks) { this.name = name; this.marks = marks; } // ---- Business rule enforced inside the Model ---- public void setMarks(int marks) { if (marks < 0 || marks > 100) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("Marks must lie in [0, 100]."); } this.marks = marks; notifyObservers(); // <-- Step 6 of the MVC lifecycle } public String getName() { return name; } public int getMarks() { return marks; } // ---- Observer-pattern hook (Model → View notification) ---- public void addObserver(Observer o) { observers.add(o); } private void notifyObservers() { for (Observer o : observers) o.update(this); } } ``` **Analytical note:** Notice the `setMarks` method (1) performs validation (a business rule, so it lives in the **Model**, never the View) and (2) calls `notifyObservers()`. This is the **Observer pattern** woven inside MVC. #### 3.1.2 The Observer Interface (Subscriber contract) ```java // OBSERVER — implemented by every VIEW that wants Model updates. public interface Observer { void update(Student model); } ``` #### 3.1.3 The View — `StudentView.java` ```java // VIEW LAYER — only rendering. NO business logic. import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class StudentView extends JFrame implements Observer { private final JLabel nameLabel = new JLabel(); private final JLabel marksLabel = new JLabel(); private final JButton refreshBtn = new JButton("Refresh from Model"); public StudentView() { setTitle("Student MVC Demo"); setSize(320, 160); setLayout(new GridLayout(3, 1)); setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE); add(nameLabel); add(marksLabel); add(refreshBtn); setVisible(true); } // Hook for the Controller to attach its action listener public void setRefreshAction(java.awt.event.ActionListener l) { refreshBtn.addActionListener(l); } // Pull model state and re-render (Step 7 of MVC lifecycle) @Override public void update(Student model) { nameLabel .setText("Name : " + model.getName()); marksLabel.setText("Marks: " + model.getMarks()); } } ``` #### 3.1.4 The Controller — `StudentController.java` ```java // CONTROLLER LAYER — mediates between View and Model. import java.awt.event.ActionEvent; public class StudentController { private final Student model; private final StudentView view; public StudentController(Student model, StudentView view) { this.model = model; this.view = view; // Step 2 of the lifecycle: View → Controller event delivery this.view.setRefreshAction(this::onRefreshClicked); // Initial render view.update(model); } // Step 3–4 of the lifecycle: interpret event, update model private void onRefreshClicked(ActionEvent e) { String input = javax.swing.JOptionPane.showInputDialog( view, "Enter new marks (0-100):"); if (input != null) { try { int newMarks = Integer.parseInt(input.trim()); model.setMarks(newMarks); // <-- Step 4 } catch (NumberFormatException ex) { javax.swing.JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(view, "Invalid number."); } } } } ``` #### 3.1.5 The Main / Composition Root ```java public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Student model = new Student("Anu", 78); StudentView view = new StudentView(); model.addObserver(view); // subscribe View to Model new StudentController(model, view); // wire controller } } ``` ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why each line satisfies KTU's evaluation key | Code element | OOP concept it demonstrates | KTU marking weight | |---|---|---| | `Student` POJO with private fields | **Encapsulation** | High (always scored) | | `setMarks()` validation | **Information Expert (GRASP)** — Model owns its own data | High | | `addObserver / notifyObservers` | **Observer GoF pattern** | Mandatory in 14-mark answers | | `StudentView implements Observer` | **Polymorphism + interface realisation** | Medium | | `StudentController` references both | **Mediator / Controller role** | High | | `main` wires the three tiers | **Composition Root / Dependency wiring** | Medium | | No direct `view → model` call | **Strict MVC separation** | High (often 2 marks) | | No direct `model → view` call (only via Observer) | **Loose coupling proof** | High (often 2 marks) | ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Interaction Equation Let us formalize the coupling constraint that the MVC pattern enforces.
