The **dependency graph** in a strict MVC must satisfy: $$ D(M, V) = 0, \quad D(V, M) = 0, \quad D(C, M) > 0, \quad D(C, V) > 0 $$ where $D(A, B)$ denotes the **dependency count** from component $A$ to component $B$ (number of compile-time references). **Derivation steps:** $$ \text{Total dependencies in MVC} = D(C, M) + D(C, V) = 2 \text{ (minimum viable)} $$ $$ \text{Total dependencies without MVC (monolith)} = D(M, V) + D(V, M) + D(C, M) + D(C, V) \geq 4 $$ Therefore, MVC reduces the **direct bi-directional coupling** between Model and View from $\geq 2$ references to exactly $0$: $$ \Delta D_{M \leftrightarrow V} = 2 - 0 = 2 \quad \text{removed dependencies} $$ This algebraic reduction is the **formal proof** of "loose coupling" that KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Classical MVC Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR user1[User / Client] view1[View Layer
Renders UI
Implements Observer] ctrl1[Controller Layer
Translates Input
Invokes Model] model1[Model Layer
Holds Data + Business Rules
Subject of Observer] db1[(Database / Persistence)] user1 -- Step 1: UI Action --> view1 view1 -- Step 2: Forward Event --> ctrl1 ctrl1 -- Step 3: invoke setMarks --> model1 model1 -- Step 4: persist / mutate --> db1 model1 -- Step 5: notify stateChanged --> view1 view1 -- Step 6: re-render --> user1 ctrl1 -- Step 7: select next view --> view1 classDef model fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#1f6feb,color:#0b3d91 classDef view fill:#d2f8d2,stroke:#2e8540,color:#0a4d0a classDef ctrl fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#b08800,color:#5a4500 classDef user fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#5a1a1a classDef db fill:#e8d6ff,stroke:#6a0dad,color:#3b0a5a class model1 model class view1 view class ctrl1 ctrl class user1 user class db1 db ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Component Responsibility Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Model_Tier["MODEL TIER (Data + Rules)"] m1[Entity / POJO] m2[Business Methods] m3[Persistence / DAO] m4[Observer Registry] end subgraph View_Tier["VIEW TIER (Presentation)"] v1[UI Components] v2[Layout / Styling] v3[Renderer Pulls State] end subgraph Controller_Tier["CONTROLLER TIER (Input + Glue)"] c1[Event Handler] c2[Input Validation] c3[View Selector] end c1 --> m2 c2 --> m2 m2 --> m4 m4 -- notify --> v3 c3 --> v1 classDef tierModel fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#1f6feb,color:#0b3d91 classDef tierView fill:#d2f8d2,stroke:#2e8540,color:#0a4d0a classDef tierCtrl fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#b08800,color:#5a4500 class m1,m2,m3,m4 tierModel class v1,v2,v3 tierView class c1,c2,c3 tierCtrl ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — End-to-End MVC Request Lifecycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant V as View participant C as Controller participant M as Model participant DB as Database U->>V: 1. Click 'Update Marks' V->>C: 2. actionPerformed(event) C->>C: 3. Validate input format C->>M: 4. setMarks(value) M->>DB: 5. UPDATE student SET ... DB-->>M: 6. rowsAffected M->>M: 7. stateChanged() M-->>V: 8. notify Observers (pull state) V->>V: 9. re-render JLabels V-->>U: 10. Display updated marks C->>V: 11. selectNextView (optional) ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** *Define the MVC architectural pattern. List its three components and state the primary responsibility of each.* **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ~120 words):** MVC (Model-View-Controller) is an architectural design pattern that separates an interactive application into three interconnected components. - **Model** — Manages the application's data, business rules, and persistence. It is *notification-based* and broadcasts state changes to subscribed Views. - **View** — Handles the presentation and rendering of the Model. It contains no business logic, only display formatting. - **Controller** — Accepts user input from the View, translates it into Model operations, and selects the next View to render. MVC enforces the **Separation of Concerns (SoC)** principle, enabling loose coupling, high cohesion, parallel development, and easier unit testing. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]** *Differentiate between the Model and the View in MVC. Why must the View never directly modify the Model in classical MVC?* **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ~100 words):** | Aspect | Model | View | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Holds data + business logic | Renders data to user | | **Knows about** | Itself and persistence | Itself and the Model's getters | | **Modifies data** | Yes (via setters) | **Never** in classical MVC | | **Reusability** | High — reused across views | Lower — bound to UI tech | The View must not directly modify the Model because doing so **breaks Separation of Concerns** — UI code would carry business side-effects, destroying loose coupling and making unit testing of the Model impossible without spinning up a UI. All mutations must be routed through the **Controller** for validation and audit. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks > **Q A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper, Module 4]** > **(a)** Explain the MVC architectural pattern in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss how the Observer pattern is used internally by the Model to notify Views of state changes. (7 marks) > > **(b)** Write a complete Java program (any one example of your choice) demonstrating MVC by implementing a `BankAccount` with fields `accountHolder` and `balance`, a View that displays the balance, and a Controller that handles a `deposit(amount)` action. Justify which class acts as the **Subject** and which as the **Observer** in your program. (7 marks) --- ##### Model Solution — Q A (a) — 7 marks **Definition (1 mark):** MVC is an architectural design pattern that decomposes an interactive application into three components — **Model**, **View**, **Controller** — to enforce **separation of concerns** and **loose coupling**. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** ``` +-----------------+ | USER | +--------+--------+ | (1) UI action v +-----------------+ | VIEW | <-- Renders Model +--------+--------+ | (2) event forwarded v +-----------------+ | CONTROLLER | <-- Interprets input, +--------+--------+ invokes Model, picks View | (3) business call v +-----------------+ | MODEL | <-- Data + business rules +--------+--------+ (Subject of Observer) | (4) persistence v +-----------------+ | DATABASE | +-----------------+ (5) Model notifies View via Observer (6) View re-renders ``` **Observer integration (2 marks):** The Model maintains a list of `Observer` references (typically `List views`). Whenever the Model's state changes (e.g., `setBalance`), it calls `notifyObservers()`, which iterates the list and invokes `observer.update(this)`. The View implements the `Observer` interface and registers itself with the Model at composition time. This gives the View a *pull* mechanism — it reads `model.getBalance()` inside `update()`. **Benefits (1 mark):** Loose coupling (Model unaware of UI tech), high cohesion, parallel development, testability. **Limitations (1 mark):** In small applications, MVC adds boilerplate; for real-world web apps, variants like MVVM, MVP, MVT are often preferred. --- ##### Model Solution — Q A (b) — 7 marks ```java // ===== Observer contract ===== interface Observer { void update(BankAccount acc); } // ===== MODEL — Subject of Observer ===== class BankAccount { private String accountHolder; private double balance; private final java.util.List obs = new java.util.ArrayList<>(); public BankAccount(String h, double b) { this.accountHolder = h; this.balance = b; } public String getAccountHolder() { return accountHolder; } public double getBalance() { return balance; } public void deposit(double amt) { if (amt <= 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Amount must be positive"); this.balance += amt; notifyObservers(); // <-- Model → View notification } public void addObserver(Observer o) { obs.add(o); } private void notifyObservers() { for (Observer o : obs) o.update(this); } } // ===== VIEW — Observer ===== class AccountView implements Observer { @Override public void update(BankAccount acc) { System.out.println("[View] " + acc.getAccountHolder() + " | New Balance = ₹" + acc.getBalance()); } } // ===== CONTROLLER ===== class AccountController { private final BankAccount model; private final AccountView view; public AccountController(BankAccount m, AccountView v) { this.model = m; this.view = v; m.addObserver(view); v.update(m); // initial render } public void onDepositClicked(double amount) { // event-handler entry model.deposit(amount); // <-- Controller mutates Model } } // ===== Composition root ===== public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { BankAccount model = new BankAccount("Anu", 1000.0); AccountView view = new AccountView(); AccountController c = new AccountController(model, view); c.onDepositClicked(500.0); c.onDepositClicked(250.0); } } ``` **Justification of Subject / Observer (1 mark):** - `BankAccount` (the Model) is the **Subject** — it owns the state, maintains the `obs` list, and triggers notifications inside `deposit()`. - `AccountView` (the View) is the **Observer** — it implements the `Observer` interface and reacts to notifications by re-reading and re-rendering the balance. **Expected output (for examiner reference, 0 marks but useful for verification):** ``` [View] Anu | New Balance = ₹1000.0 [View] Anu | New Balance = ₹1500.0 [View] Anu | New Balance = ₹1750.0 ``` --- #### ✅ Question B (Choice 2 / Alternative) — 14 Marks > **Q B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper, Module 4]** > **(a)** With the help of a sequence diagram, explain the complete request-response lifecycle of an MVC application when a user clicks an "Update" button. (7 marks) > > **(b)** Compare and contrast **MVC**, **MVP**, and **MVVM** architectural patterns. Under what circumstances would you prefer MVVM over MVC? (7 marks) --- ##### Model Solution — Q B (a) — 7 marks **Sequence diagram (3 marks):** ``` User View Controller Model Database | | | | | |-(1)click->| | | | | |-(2)event-->| | | | | |-(3)call-->| | | | | |-(4)SQL-->| | | | |<-(5)ack--| | | | |--(6)notify |<-(7)rendered--------|<-view()---| | | | | | | ``` **Step-by-step explanation (4 marks):** 1. **User → View:** The user performs a UI gesture (click, keystroke, touch). 2. **View → Controller:** The View does not interpret the action; it forwards the raw event (e.g., `ActionEvent`) to its associated Controller. 3. **Controller interprets:** The Controller parses the event, validates the input, and decides which Model method to invoke. 4. **Controller → Model:** The Controller calls a Model method (e.g., `model.setMarks(95)`). 5. **Model → Database (optional):** The Model persists the change (e.g., via JDBC/ORM). 6. **Model → View (notification):** The Model fires a `stateChanged()` event to all subscribed Observers (Views). 7. **View re-renders:** Each View pulls the new state from the Model and refreshes its UI. The Controller may finally select a new View (e.g., success page). --- ##### Model Solution — Q B (b) — 7 marks **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Aspect | **MVC** | **MVP** | **MVVM** | |---|---|---|---| | **Full form** | Model-View-Controller | Model-View-Presenter | Model-View-ViewModel | | **View ↔ Model** | Indirect (via Observer) | Indirect (via Presenter) | Indirect (via data-binding) | | **Mediator** | Controller | Presenter | ViewModel | | **UI binding** | Manual push/pull | Manual presenter calls | **Two-way data binding** (declarative) | | **View's intelligence** | Passive | Passive (dumb) | Almost passive (declarative XML/FXML) | | **Best suited for** | Web (Spring MVC), desktop | Legacy desktop, WinForms | WPF, JavaFX, Angular, Vue, Android Data-Binding | | **Testability** | High | High (Presenter is pure) | Very high (ViewModel is pure) | | **Complexity** | Medium | Medium | Higher (binding engine overhead) | **When to prefer MVVM over MVC (3 marks):** - The UI has **rich two-way data binding** needs (form fields that must stay in sync with model state automatically). - The team uses a **declarative UI framework** (WPF, JavaFX, Vue, Angular) that natively supports data binding. - The ViewModel can be **unit-tested without any UI** (crucial for finance/healthcare apps). - The application has **many form-heavy screens** where writing controller-update code for every field is tedious. Conversely, prefer **MVC** when the application is **server-side / request-response oriented** (e.g., Spring MVC web apps) or when the team values **explicit control flow** over declarative magic. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 2–3 marks each):** > 1. ❌ Drawing arrows **Model ↔ View directly** in your diagram — this breaks the pattern. Always route through Controller + Observer. > 2. ❌ Writing business logic (validation, calculation) inside the **View** — business logic lives only in the **Model**. > 3. ❌ Forgetting to **register the View as an Observer** of the Model in your code — the notification chain silently breaks. > 4. ❌ Spelling "Model" as "Modal" or "Modell" — examiners deduct ½ mark per slip. > 5. ❌ Confusing **MVC** with **MVT** (Django's Model-Template-View) — they are conceptually similar but the **Template** in MVT behaves as both View and Controller in some interpretations. > 6. ❌ Writing `public` fields in the Model — violates **encapsulation**, which is the foundation of the OOP concept being tested. > 7. ❌ Omitting the **origin (Trygve Reenskaug, 1979, Xerox PARC)** in 14-mark theory answers — examiners often award 1 mark specifically for it. > 8. ❌ Drawing a sequence diagram with **no numbering on the arrows** — examiners cannot trace the lifecycle and may deduct 1 mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density 1-page revision bullets — read this 5 minutes before the exam:** - ✅ MVC = **Model-View-Controller**, an *architectural* design pattern, **not** a GoF pattern. - ✅ Origin: **Trygve Reenskaug, 1979, Xerox PARC, Smalltalk**. - ✅ **Model** → data + business rules; **Subject** of the Observer pattern; *no UI imports allowed*. - ✅ **View** → presentation only; *implements* `Observer` interface; *never* mutates Model directly. - ✅ **Controller** → input mediator; *translates* events into Model calls; *selects* the next View. - ✅ **Direct Model ↔ View dependency is FORBIDDEN** (strictly zero references each way). - ✅ **Observer pattern** is used by Model to notify View of state changes. - ✅ **Strategy pattern** is used by Controller to swap input-handling strategies. - ✅ Benefits: **loose coupling, high cohesion, parallel dev, testability, reusability, multiple views per model**. - ✅ Drawbacks: **boilerplate overhead, learning curve, overkill for small apps**. - ✅ Variants: **MVC-II / Web MVC** (Spring), **MVP**, **MVVM** (data binding), **MVT** (Django). - ✅ One Model can have **N Views**; one View has **1 Controller** (typical). - ✅ Always end your answer with the **7-step interaction lifecycle** — examiners love numbered steps. - ✅ In code answers, mark the **Observer registration** line with a comment — it is the most-graded single line. - ✅ Use **Swing / JavaFX / Web** examples — avoid I/O or network code in 14-mark answers unless explicitly asked. - ✅ Always state the **year (1979)** and the **origin (Xerox PARC)** — instant 1-mark pickup. - ✅ Common distractors in MCQs: "MVC is a behavioural pattern" (FALSE — it is architectural), "View updates the Model directly" (FALSE in classical MVC). - ✅ The MVC triad must satisfy $D(M, V) = 0$ and $D(V, M) = 0$ — the only allowed edges are $M \leftarrow C$ and $V \leftarrow C$. ---

topics.title: Swing Controls
Components and Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components and Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Java Abstract Window Toolkit (**AWT**), every user-interface element visible on the screen is modeled as an object that belongs, directly or indirectly, to the abstract class **`java.awt.Component`**. A `Component` is the *atomic* graphical entity (e.g., a button, a label, a text field), while a **`java.awt.Container`** is a special kind of `Component` that can *hold and organize* other components (including other containers). This gives rise to a **containment hierarchy** — a tree structure that determines how a graphical user interface (GUI) is laid out, drawn, and event-dispatched.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Syllabus Highlight:**
> The relationship between `Component` (leaf) and `Container` (composite) is a textbook application of the **Composite Design Pattern** in the GoF catalogue. A student is expected to identify and justify the pattern while answering theory questions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **shipping dock**:
- A **wooden crate** (the *Component*) is a single, indivisible parcel — it can be labelled, weighed, and moved, but nothing can be placed *inside* it.
- A **shipping container** (the *Container*) is itself a crate, but it has an empty interior. You can load wooden crates (Components) **and** even smaller shipping containers (nested Containers) into it.
- The **dock supervisor** (the *LayoutManager*) decides *how* the inner items are arranged — in a row, in a grid, or in zones.

Thus, every container *is-a* component (it can be packed into an outer container), but every component is *not necessarily* a container. This "is-a + can-contain" duality is the essence of the Component-Container architecture.

### 1.3 Core Class Relationships (Syllabus-Specific)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cardinal Rule of AWT:** `Container extends Component`. Therefore, *every container is a component, but every component is not a container*.

| Aspect | `Component` | `Container` |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Abstract class | Abstract class (extends Component) |
| Role | Atomic GUI element | Holds + arranges other components |
| Can hold children? | No | Yes (via `add()`) |
| Has LayoutManager? | No (own) | Yes (own + children's) |
| Direct known subclasses | Button, Label, Canvas, Checkbox, TextComponent, List | Panel, Frame, Window, Dialog, ScrollPane, Applet |
| KTU weightage | 3 marks (definition) | 7–10 marks (program/design) |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Peer-to-Platform Mapping Ratio:** 1 AWT component = 1 native OS peer (heavyweight). *This is a syllabus-pinned fact — heavy-weight architecture is the reason AWT predates Swing's lightweight model.*
- **Default Initial Container Capacity (CardLayout):** 0 cards; grows by 1 per `add()`.
- **Standard Frame Border Thickness (insets):** Top = **30 px**, Left/Right/Bottom = **0 px** on most platforms (platform-dependent).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Class Loading & Instantiation** — When a `new Frame()` (or any container) is created, the JVM requests the underlying OS to allocate a *peer* (a native window handle). This is what makes AWT *heavy-weight*.
2. **Layout Manager Attachment** — Each container holds exactly one `LayoutManager` instance, set via `setLayout(LayoutManager m)`. If `null` is passed, components are placed at absolute `(x, y)` pixel coordinates.
3. **Component Addition** — Calling `add(Component c)` registers `c` as a child of the container and simultaneously places `c` into the active layout manager's logical structure.
4. **Validation Pass (`validate()`)** — A *layout pass* traverses the containment tree top-down, querying each component's `getPreferredSize()`, then delegating the final positioning to the LayoutManager.
5. **Rendering Pass (`paint()`)** — Once validated, the AWT rendering thread walks the tree and invokes `paint(Graphics g)` on every visible component.
6. **Event Dispatch** — When the user interacts (e.g., clicks a button), the OS sends a native event; the AWT **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** propagates it up the containment tree until a listener consumes it.

### 2.2 Why the Composite Pattern Fits (Engineering Justification)

- **Uniform treatment:** The client code does not need to distinguish "leaf" from "composite" — it just calls `add()` on any node.
- **Recursive composition:** A `Frame` can contain a `Panel`, which can contain a `Panel`, which can contain a `Button`. The depth is theoretically unlimited.
- **Single rendering pipeline:** Because containers *are* components, the renderer can call `paint()` polymorphically on the entire tree.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Which design pattern does the Component-Container hierarchy demonstrate?", always answer: *"The Composite Pattern — a Container is a Component that can hold other Components, enabling uniform and recursive tree-structured GUI construction."*

### 2.3 KTU Cheat Sheet — Methods You *Must* Memorise

| Method (Signature) | Belongs To | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Component add(Component comp)` | Container | Appends a component to the layout-managed structure | ★★★★★ |
| `void setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` | Container | Assigns a layout strategy | ★★★★★ |
| `void setSize(int w, int h)` | Component | Sets component size in pixels | ★★★★ |
| `void setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` | Component | Combines position + size | ★★★★ |
| `void setTitle(String title)` | Frame | Sets the window title bar text | ★★★ |
| `void setVisible(boolean b)` | Window/Frame | Shows/hides the window | ★★★★★ |
| `void setBackground(Color c)` | Component | Sets background colour | ★★★ |
| `void addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | Button | Registers a click event handler | ★★★★★ |
| `void setLayout(null)` | Container | Switches to absolute (no-layout) positioning | ★★★★ |
| `LayoutManager getLayout()` | Container | Returns the current layout manager | ★★ |
| `Component[] getComponents()` | Container | Returns all child components (array form) | ★★★ |
| `void validate()` | Container | Forces a re-layout pass | ★★★ |
| `void setResizable(boolean b)` | Frame | Enables/disables window resizing | ★★ |
| `void setFont(Font f)` | Component | Sets the typography | ★★ |
| `String getText() / setText(String)` | Label, Button, TextField | Text accessor/mutator | ★★★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** In the table above, the pipe character `|` is intentionally avoided. Whenever you need a vertical bar in your answer sheet (e.g., to denote *such that*), write it as `\vert` in code or as a plain English "where" / "given that" in your answer copy.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Desktop IDEs** (Eclipse, IntelliJ) — use a `JFrame` → multiple `JPanel` containers in a `BorderLayout` to hold toolbars, editors, and consoles.
- **Industrial SCADA HMIs** — `Panel` containers stack gauges and charts in a `GridLayout`.
- **Banking Kiosk UIs** — a `CardLayout` container switches between "Welcome", "PIN Entry", and "Transaction" screens.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Class Hierarchy of `java.awt.Component` and `java.awt.Container`

Below is a textual derivation of the *containment tree* (a formal, recursive mathematical structure) that the JVM maintains internally for every AWT window.

Let:
- $C$ = the set of all `Component` instances
- $K \subseteq C$ = the set of all `Container` instances
- $T \subseteq K \times C$ = the *containment relation* (parent, child) pairs

The containment relation $T$ must satisfy the **containment axioms** (derived from the Composite Pattern):

$$
\forall\, p \in K, \;\; \text{children}(p) = \{\, c \in C \;:\; (p, c) \in T \,\}
$$

$$
\forall\, c \in C, \;\; \text{parent}(c) = 
\begin{cases}
p, & \text{if } \exists\, p \in K \text{ such that } (p, c) \in T \\
\text{null}, & \text{otherwise (root window)}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\forall\, c \in C, \;\; \text{depth}(c) = 
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } \text{parent}(c) = \text{null} \\
1 + \text{depth}\big(\text{parent}(c)\big), & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Explanation of each row (for the KTU answer sheet):**
- The first line says that *children(p)* is simply the set of components whose pair with $p$ is in the relation $T$.
- The second line defines the *parent* function piecewise: a root window has no parent (`null`).
- The third line defines *depth* recursively — a direct child of the root has depth 1, a grandchild has depth 2, and so on.

### 3.2 Derived Formulas for Preferred-Size Negotiation

When a LayoutManager asks each child for its size, the negotiation follows:

$$
\text{preferredSize}(c) = \max\!\big(\text{minimumSize}(c),\, \text{naturalSize}(c)\big)
$$

where the `naturalSize` for a `TextField` of $n$ characters is:

$$
\text{naturalWidth} = n \times c_w \quad \text{with default } c_w = 9 \text{ pixels per character}
$$

and for a `Button` labelled with string $s$:

$$
\text{naturalWidth} = \vert s \vert_{\text{chars}} \times c_w + 2 \times \text{insets}_h
$$

with $\text{insets}_h = 14$ pixels of horizontal padding on the standard AWT `Button` peer.

> Note: the absolute-value-style notation above is rendered using the LaTeX macro `\vert`, not the markdown pipe `|`, to preserve table integrity.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Problem-Solving Style)

**Problem:** A `Frame` of size $400 \times 300$ pixels uses a `GridLayout(2, 2)` (2 rows × 2 columns). Each cell has a 5-pixel gap on every side. Compute the effective size available to each of the four components.

**Step 1 — Total gap subtraction (horizontal):**

$$
W_{\text{usable}} = 400 - \big((2 + 1) \times 5\big) = 400 - 15 = 385 \text{ pixels}
$$

*Logic:* There are $2$ columns → $2 + 1 = 3$ vertical gap strips, each 5 px wide.

**Step 2 — Effective width per cell:**

$$
W_{\text{cell}} = \frac{W_{\text{usable}}}{2} = \frac{385}{2} = 192.5 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Step 3 — Total gap subtraction (vertical):**

$$
H_{\text{usable}} = 300 - \big((2 + 1) \times 5\big) = 300 - 15 = 285 \text{ pixels}
$$

*Logic:* There are $2$ rows → $2 + 1 = 3$ horizontal gap strips.

**Step 4 — Effective height per cell:**

$$
H_{\text{cell}} = \frac{H_{\text{usable}}}{2} = \frac{285}{2} = 142.5 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Final Answer:** Each component receives a cell of **$192.5 \times 142.5$ pixels** (JVM rounds to integers: $192 \times 142$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Class-Hierarchy Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Object"] --> B["Component abstract"]
    B --> C["Container abstract"]
    B --> D["Button"]
    B --> E["Label"]
    B --> F["Canvas"]
    B --> G["Checkbox"]
    B --> H["TextComponent"]
    H --> H1["TextField"]
    H --> H2["TextArea"]
    C --> I["Panel"]
    C --> J["Window"]
    C --> K["ScrollPane"]
    J --> J1["Frame"]
    J --> J2["Dialog"]
    I --> L["Applet"]
    L --> M["JApplet extends from Swing"]
```

### 4.2 Runtime Containment Tree for a Sample Login Window

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["Frame LoginWindow BorderLayout"] --> P1["Panel North FlowLayout"]
    R --> P2["Panel Center GridLayout 2x2"]
    R --> P3["Panel South FlowLayout"]
    P1 --> L1["Label Username"]
    P1 --> TF1["TextField 15"]
    P2 --> L2["Label Password"]
    P2 --> TF2["TextField 15"]
    P2 --> L3["Label Role"]
    P2 --> CH1["Choice Admin User Guest"]
    P3 --> B1["Button Submit"]
    P3 --> B2["Button Cancel"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The root is a `Frame`; it contains three `Panel`s (one in each of the BorderLayout's North/Center/South zones); each `Panel` is itself a `Container` that holds atomic `Component`s. Notice how `Panel` appears as both a child (of `Frame`) and a parent (of `Button`/`Label`) — this is the textbook Composite Pattern in action.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Event Dispatch Path)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OS["OS Native Window"] --> P1["Peer Object native side"]
    P1 --> P2["AWT Event Queue"]
    P2 --> P3["Event Dispatch Thread"]
    P3 --> P4["dispatchEvent on Component"]
    P4 --> P5["processEvent"]
    P5 --> P6["processActionEvent on Button"]
    P6 --> P7["actionPerformed Listener"]
    P7 --> P8["User defined handler code"]
```

### 4.4 Layout Manager Decision Matrix (Tabular Schematic)

| Layout Manager | Default Direction | Cells / Zones | Use-Case | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `FlowLayout` | Left-to-right, wraps | Unlimited | Toolbar, button rows | ★★★★★ |
| `BorderLayout` | Five fixed zones | 5 (N, S, E, W, C) | App windows, frames | ★★★★★ |
| `GridLayout` | Equal cells, row-major | $r \times c$ | Calculators, keypads | ★★★★ |
| `CardLayout` | Stack of panels | $n$ cards | Wizards, tab-screens | ★★★ |
| `GridBagLayout` | Cell-grid with constraints | $r \times c$ with weights | Pro-grade forms | ★★ |
| `null` (absolute) | Pixel coordinates | N/A | Custom-drawn UIs, games | ★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between `java.awt.Component` and `java.awt.Container`. State the relationship between them with respect to inheritance.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
`Component` is an *abstract* super-class representing every visual AWT element (Button, Label, Canvas, etc.). `Container` is a *direct subclass* of `Component` that can hold and organize other components via its `add()` method. The relationship is: **"Container IS-A Component (via inheritance) AND Container HAS-A collection of Components (via composition)"** — a textbook example of the **Composite Design Pattern**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three commonly used Containers in Java AWT and state one distinguishing property of each.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
1. **`Frame`** — A top-level window with a title bar and minimise/maximise/close buttons. (1 mark)
2. **`Panel`** — A *generic* intermediate container with no native peer of its own; must be added to a `Window` to become visible. (1 mark)
3. **`Dialog`** — A pop-up window that is *modal* by default — it blocks input to its parent `Frame` until closed. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the Component-Container class hierarchy in Java AWT with a neat diagram. Discuss why this hierarchy is called an application of the Composite Design Pattern. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java AWT program that creates a `Frame` titled *"Student Registration"*, places a `Panel` with a `GridLayout(4,2)` containing four `Label`s and four `TextField`s, and adds a *"Submit"* `Button` at the South position using `BorderLayout`. The Frame should be visible and sized $500 \times 400$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

1. The root of the AWT hierarchy is the abstract class `java.awt.Component`. Every visible element (Button, Label, Canvas, Checkbox, TextComponent, List) extends `Component` directly. **(1 mark)**
2. `Component` is subclassed by the abstract class `java.awt.Container`, which adds the ability to *contain* other components. The class signatures in the JDK are:
   `public abstract class Container extends Component { ... }` **(1 mark)**
3. `Container` is further subclassed by `Panel`, `Window`, and `ScrollPane`. `Window` is in turn subclassed by `Frame` and `Dialog`. `Panel` is subclassed by `Applet`. **(2 marks)**
4. **Composite Pattern Justification (2 marks):**
   - *Uniformity:* `add(Component)` is defined on `Container`, but a `Container` itself IS-A `Component`, so the client code treats both leaves and composites uniformly.
   - *Recursion:* Containers can contain containers — there is no fixed depth limit.
   - *Single render pipeline:* The `paint(Graphics)` method, defined on `Component`, is polymorphically invoked on the entire tree.
5. Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the visual hierarchy. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

```java
import java.awt.*;

public class StudentRegistration extends Frame {
    StudentRegistration() {
        // 1. Frame setup
        setTitle("Student Registration");
        setSize(500, 400);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());

        // 2. Panel with GridLayout(4, 2) for the form fields
        Panel formPanel = new Panel();
        formPanel.setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 2, 10, 10)); // 10px h-gap, 10px v-gap

        // 3. Add labels and text fields
        formPanel.add(new Label("Name:"));
        formPanel.add(new TextField(20));
        formPanel.add(new Label("Roll No:"));
        formPanel.add(new TextField(20));
        formPanel.add(new Label("Email:"));
        formPanel.add(new TextField(20));
        formPanel.add(new Label("Phone:"));
        formPanel.add(new TextField(20));

        // 4. South panel for the Submit button
        Panel southPanel = new Panel();
        southPanel.add(new Button("Submit"));

        // 5. Add panels to the Frame
        add(formPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        add(southPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        // 6. Make the window visible
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new StudentRegistration();
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution for the Program (7 marks):**
| Code Element | Marks |
|---|---|
| `extends Frame` + constructor setup | 1 |
| `setSize(500, 400)` + `setTitle` | 1 |
| Correct `GridLayout(4, 2)` instantiation | 1 |
| Adding 4 `Label`–`TextField` pairs | 1.5 |
| South `Panel` with `Button` | 1 |
| `setVisible(true)` and `main` | 1 |
| Code formatting & no compile errors | 0.5 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the following AWT components with one-line descriptions and constructor signatures: `Label`, `Button`, `TextField`, `TextArea`, `Checkbox`, `Choice`, and `List`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain `FlowLayout`, `BorderLayout`, and `GridLayout` with constructor signatures, default behaviours, and one use-case each. Write a short Java snippet that uses all three. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| Component | Description | Key Constructor | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Label` | Non-editable, single-line text display | `Label()`, `Label(String text)`, `Label(String text, int alignment)` | Showing field captions |
| `Button` | Clickable, raises `ActionEvent` | `Button()`, `Button(String label)` | Submit/Cancel actions |
| `TextField` | Single-line editable text | `TextField()`, `TextField(int columns)`, `TextField(String text)` | Username input |
| `TextArea` | Multi-line editable text | `TextArea()`, `TextArea(int rows, int cols)`, `TextArea(String text, int rows, int cols, int scrollbarVisibility)` | Comments box |
| `Checkbox` | Toggleable option (with optional `CheckboxGroup`) | `Checkbox()`, `Checkbox(String label)`, `Checkbox(String label, boolean state)` | "Subscribe" checkbox |
| `Choice` | Drop-down combo box | `Choice()` | Selecting a country |
| `List` | Scrollable list, supports single or multi-select | `List()`, `List(int rows, boolean multipleMode)` | Selecting subjects |

*(1 mark per fully-answered row ⇒ 7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**`FlowLayout`** — Default for `Panel`. Places components left-to-right, wrapping to the next line when the row is full. Constructors: `FlowLayout()`, `FlowLayout(int align)`, `FlowLayout(int align, int hgap, int vgap)`. Alignment can be `FlowLayout.LEFT \vert CENTER \vert RIGHT`. Use-case: toolbars. **(2 marks)**

**`BorderLayout`** — Default for `Frame`, `Dialog`, and `Window`. Divides the container into five zones: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`. Constructors: `BorderLayout()`, `BorderLayout(int hgap, int vgap)`. Use-case: full app window with a header, body, and footer. **(2 marks)**

**`GridLayout`** — Divides the container into a uniform $r \times c$ grid; every cell is the same size. Constructors: `GridLayout()`, `GridLayout(int rows, int cols)`, `GridLayout(int rows, int cols, int hgap, int vgap)`. Use-case: calculator keypad. **(2 marks)**

**Combined Java Snippet (1 mark):**

```java
import java.awt.*;

public class LayoutShowcase extends Frame {
    LayoutShowcase() {
        setTitle("Layout Managers");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());

        // NORTH: FlowLayout
        Panel north = new Panel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 10));
        north.add(new Button("File"));
        north.add(new Button("Edit"));
        north.add(new Button("View"));

        // CENTER: GridLayout
        Panel center = new Panel(new GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5));
        center.add(new Button("1"));
        center.add(new Button("2"));
        center.add(new Button("3"));
        center.add(new Button("4"));

        add(north, BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(center, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LayoutShowcase();
    }
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing `Panel` with `Frame`:** A `Panel` *cannot* be displayed on its own — it has no title bar. Always wrap a `Panel` inside a `Frame` or `Applet`. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`:** The window will not appear at runtime. Examiners explicitly check for this. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Using `null` layout in theory:** If you write `setLayout(null)`, you *must* explicitly call `setBounds(x, y, w, h)` for *every* component — otherwise nothing appears. (–0.5 to –1 mark)
> 4. **Mixing `java.awt` and `javax.swing`:** Adding a `JButton` to a `Frame` is *legal* (lightweight inside heavy-weight) but using `add` arguments designed for Swing on pure AWT components is a common bug. (–0.5 mark)
> 5. **Event-handler omission:** Defining a `Button` but not registering an `ActionListener` is incomplete — the button does nothing when clicked. (–1 mark if asked to "make the button functional")

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ `Container` *extends* `Component` ⇒ **Composite Pattern**.
- ✅ `Component` = atomic, `Container` = composite; both are `java.awt` abstract classes.
- ✅ `add()` is the only method needed to build a tree.
- ✅ `setLayout(null)` ⇒ absolute positioning; otherwise, a `LayoutManager` is mandatory.
- ✅ `Frame` is the top-level window; `Panel` is the workhorse intermediate container.
- ✅ `BorderLayout` is the default for `Frame`; `FlowLayout` is the default for `Panel`.
- ✅ Always call `setVisible(true)` *last* in the constructor.
- ✅ Layout negotiation uses `getPreferredSize()` — supply it via `setPreferredSize()` if defaults are wrong.
- ✅ AWT is **heavy-weight** (one peer per component) ⇒ avoid in modern code, but **it is the syllabus topic for KTU Module 4**.
- ✅ The containment tree has no fixed depth limit (recursive composition).
- ✅ Event dispatch walks the tree top-down until a listener consumes the event.
- ✅ Three memorise-by-heart layout managers: `FlowLayout`, `BorderLayout`, `GridLayout`.
- ✅ Two memorise-by-heart containers: `Frame` (top-level) and `Panel` (intermediate).
- ✅ Seven memorise-by-heart components: `Label`, `Button`, `TextField`, `TextArea`, `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `List`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Packages
Event Handling in Swings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Event Handling in Swings # 📘 MODULE 4 — EVENT HANDLING IN SWINGS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Event Handling in Swings** refers to the mechanism by which a Java program controls the interaction between a user and a **Swing-based Graphical User Interface (GUI)** component. An *event* is an object that describes a state change in a GUI component (e.g., button click, mouse movement, key press). The **Event Delegation Model** is the formal AWT/Swing architecture in which an event generated by a source component is delegated to a registered handler (listener) object, which then executes the corresponding response logic. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic is anchored to **Course Outcome CO4** of PECMT535: *“Apply event handling techniques to develop interactive GUI applications using Swings.”* The cognitive level expected is **Apply / Analyze (Level 3 / 4 of Revised Bloom’s Taxonomy)**. --- ### 1.2 The Three Architectural Pillars of Swing Event Handling | # | Entity | Formal Role | Real-World Analogy | |---|--------|-------------|--------------------| | 1 | **Event Source** | The GUI component (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`) that *generates* an event. | A **doorbell button** on a wall. | | 2 | **Event Object** | An instance of a subclass of `java.awt.AWTEvent` (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`) that *encapsulates* the state change. | The **electrical pulse** travelling through the wire. | | 3 | **Event Listener / Handler** | An object implementing a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`) that *receives* the event and reacts. | The **person inside the house** who opens the door. | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must clearly distinguish between *low-level events* (e.g., `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`) and *semantic events* (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent`). The most frequently tested listener in the KTU examination is `ActionListener`. --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Subscription Model Imagine a **newspaper publisher** (Event Source). The publisher does **not** know who reads the paper, nor does it visit every reader. Instead, any reader who **subscribes** (registers as a listener) gets a copy delivered to their door whenever the **daily edition** (Event Object) is published. The reader then **reads it and decides** what to do (handle the event in `actionPerformed()`). - If a reader **unsubscribes** (`removeActionListener()`), they stop receiving papers. - If multiple readers subscribe, **all of them** receive the same event. - The publisher remains **decoupled** from the readers — the source and listener never need to know each other’s internal workings. This is the essence of the **Event Delegation Model**, a textbook example of the *Observer Design Pattern*. ### 1.4 Key Terminology Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU) - **Source** — the originator of the event. - **Listener** — the registered handler object. - **Event Class** — subclass of `AWTEvent` carrying event data. - **Listener Interface** — Java interface with callback methods (e.g., `ActionListener`). - **Adapter Class** — default empty implementation of a listener interface with multiple methods. - **Inner / Anonymous Class** — common idiomatic Java technique to define listeners inline. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric:** A standard KTU 14-mark question on event handling typically expects a **complete, compilable Java program** (≈ 40–60 lines) demonstrating registration of at least two listeners and handling of at least two distinct event types. **Skipping the import statements or `main()` method costs 1–2 marks** in the board valuation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Event Delegation Model — Operational Workflow The Swing event-handling pipeline executes in a strict, deterministic sequence. Each bullet is a discrete runtime step; the student must visualize this in the KTU lab exam as well as in viva voce. 1. **User Interaction** — The user performs an action (e.g., clicks a `JButton`). 2. **Event Instantiation** — The JVM constructs an event object (e.g., `ActionEvent`) on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT). 3. **Source Identification** — The component encapsulates the event and identifies itself as the **source** (`getSource()`). 4. **Listener Notification** — The source iterates over its **multicast listener list** and invokes the corresponding callback method on **every registered listener**. 5. **Callback Execution** — The listener’s overridden method (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`) executes the response logic. 6. **Control Return** — After all listeners return, the EDT proceeds to the next UI update. > [!NOTE] > The **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** is a single, dedicated thread (`AWT-EventQueue`) that owns all GUI updates in Swing. Long-running tasks in a listener block the EDT, causing the dreaded “application freeze.” This is a frequent viva question. --- ## 2.2 Classification of Event Classes (High-Yield Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** In KTU, the examiner often asks *“Which event class is generated when the user clicks a JButton?”* — the answer is **`ActionEvent`**, NOT `MouseEvent`. The click is a *semantic* event. | Event Class | Package | Trigger Action | Listener Interface | |-------------|---------|----------------|--------------------| | `ActionEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Button click, menu selection, Enter in text field | `ActionListener` | | `ItemEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Checkbox / radio button / list item state change | `ItemListener` | | `MouseEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Mouse press, release, click, enter, exit | `MouseListener` | | `MouseMotionEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Mouse moved, dragged | `MouseMotionListener` | | `KeyEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Key pressed, released, typed | `KeyListener` | | `FocusEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Component gains / loses focus | `FocusListener` | | `WindowEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Window opened, closed, iconified, activated | `WindowListener` | | `TextEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Text content changed | `TextListener` | --- ## 2.3 Listener Interface vs. Adapter Class — The KTU Trap Many listener interfaces (e.g., `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`) declare **more than one abstract method**. Implementing all of them just to override a single method is tedious. Java solves this with **Adapter Classes** — default empty implementations. > [!WARNING] > **Do not confuse** `ActionListener` (a *functional* interface with a single method, so it has no adapter) with `MouseListener` (a *multi-method* interface, so it has `MouseAdapter`). Writing `new ActionListener()` with empty bodies is fine, but writing `new MouseListener()` forces you to override all 5 methods — this is a classic KTU board mistake. | Interface | Methods | Adapter Class | |-----------|---------|---------------| | `ActionListener` | 1 → `actionPerformed` | None (use lambda) | | `MouseListener` | 5 → clicked, pressed, released, entered, exited | `MouseAdapter` | | `KeyListener` | 3 → pressed, released, typed | `KeyAdapter` | | `WindowListener` | 7 → opened, closing, closed, activated, deactivated, iconified, deiconified | `WindowAdapter` | | `MouseMotionListener` | 2 → mouseDragged, mouseMoved | `MouseMotionAdapter` | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet | Operation | Syntax (General Form) | Returns | |-----------|----------------------|---------| | Register listener | `component.addXxxListener(listenerObj);` | `void` | | Unregister listener | `component.removeXxxListener(listenerObj);` | `void` | | Get source | `event.getSource();` | `Object` | | Get command name | `event.getActionCommand();` | `String` | | Get key char | `event.getKeyChar();` | `char` | | Get key code | `event.getKeyCode();` | `int` | | Get mouse position | `event.getX()`, `event.getY();` | `int` | | Get modifiers | `event.getModifiersEx();` | `int` | | Get selected item | `event.getItem();` | `Object` | | Get state change | `event.getStateChange();` | `int` ($\vert 1$ = selected, $\vert 2$ = deselected) | > **Engineering Utility:** Event-driven programming underpins virtually every modern interactive system — from Android UI loops, to embedded dashboards in automotive infotainment, to SCADA systems in power plants. The Swing event model is the direct ancestor of the JavaFX and modern reactive UI frameworks (e.g., RxJava, Reactor). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Programs & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Universal Five-Step Pattern for Swing Event Handling Every Swing event-handling program in the KTU 2024 syllabus can be reduced to the following **five-step skeleton**. Memorize this; it is the “theorem” from which all lab programs are derived. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step-0 (Pre-amble):** `import java.awt.*; import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.event.*;` > > **Step-1:** Declare the GUI component(s) as instance variables of the class (so that inner classes can access them). > > **Step-2:** Instantiate the components in the constructor and add them to the container. > > **Step-3:** Register the listener with the source: `component.addActionListener(this);` (or pass an inner / anonymous class). > > **Step-4:** Implement the listener interface (or extend an Adapter class) and override the callback method(s). > > **Step-5:** In `main()`, instantiate the class on the EDT: `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new MyApp());` --- ## 3.2 Worked Program 1 — `ActionListener` with `JButton` (14-Mark Style) This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark question**. A complete, compilable program is shown below with explicit step-by-step annotations. ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class ButtonDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener { JLabel label; // Step-1: instance variable JButton button; ButtonDemo() { // Step-2: constructor setTitle("KTU Button Demo"); setSize(300, 150); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE); label = new JLabel("Press the button"); button = new JButton("Click Me"); add(label); add(button); button.addActionListener(this); // Step-3: register setVisible(true); } @Override public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { // Step-4: callback label.setText("Hello from KTU! Time: " + System.currentTimeMillis()); } public static void main(String[] args) { // Step-5: launch SwingUtilities.invokeLater(ButtonDemo::new); } } ``` ### Line-by-Line Logical Trace | Line | Why It Is Written | Exam Justification | |------|-------------------|--------------------| | `implements ActionListener` | Allows `this` (the JFrame itself) to act as the listener. | Class-based listener design. | | `JLabel label; JButton button;` | Must be instance (not local) so the listener can access them. | Scope resolution for callbacks. | | `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` | Ensures the JVM terminates when the window is closed. | Otherwise JVM keeps running silently. | | `button.addActionListener(this);` | Registers the frame object as a listener. | The heart of the Event Delegation Model. | | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | The *callback* method; AWT/Swing invokes it on click. | The interface contract. | | `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(ButtonDemo::new);` | Schedules GUI creation on the EDT — *thread-safe* launch. | Best practice for modern Swing. | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Students often write `setSize(300, 150);` and forget `setVisible(true);` — the program compiles but nothing is displayed. Deduct **1 mark** for this in the board exam. --- ## 3.3 Worked Program 2 — `KeyListener` with `KeyAdapter` Demonstrates the **Adapter class pattern** for handling a single key event without overriding all three `KeyListener` methods. ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class KeyDemo extends JFrame { JTextField input; JLabel echo; KeyDemo() { setTitle("Key Adapter Demo"); setSize(350, 120); setLayout(new java.awt.FlowLayout()); setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE); input = new JTextField(15); echo = new JLabel("Type something…"); add(echo); add(input); // Anonymous inner subclass of KeyAdapter input.addKeyListener(new KeyAdapter() { @Override public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) { echo.setText("Typed: " + e.getKeyChar()); } }); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(KeyDemo::new); } } ``` ### Derivation of the Callback Chain $$ \text{User types 'K'} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{JVM creates } \texttt{KeyEvent} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{source} = \texttt{input} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{listener list} = \{ \texttt{anonKeyAdapter} \} $$ $$ \longrightarrow\; \texttt{anonKeyAdapter.keyTyped(e)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{echo.setText(…)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{EDT repaints frame} $$ The three distinct key events generated by a single physical keypress are dispatched in this exact order: $$ \text{KEY\_PRESSED} \;\rightarrow\; \text{KEY\_TYPED} \;\rightarrow\; \text{KEY\_RELEASED} $$ > [!NOTE] > **`keyTyped`** fires only for *character-producing* keys and reports the Unicode char via `getKeyChar()`. **`keyPressed`** fires for *every* key (including Shift, F1) and reports a virtual key code via `getKeyCode()`. This distinction is a frequent KTU Part-A question. --- ## 3.4 Worked Program 3 — `ItemListener` with Checkbox Group ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class ItemDemo extends JFrame implements ItemListener { JCheckBox c1, c2, c3; JLabel result; ItemDemo() { setTitle("ItemListener Demo"); setSize(300, 200); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE); c1 = new JCheckBox("Java"); c2 = new JCheckBox("Python"); c3 = new JCheckBox("C++"); result = new JLabel("Select languages"); add(c1); add(c2); add(c3); add(result); c1.addItemListener(this); c2.addItemListener(this); c3.addItemListener(this); setVisible(true); } @Override public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) { JCheckBox src = (JCheckBox) e.getItem(); int state = e.getStateChange(); // 1 = selected, 2 = deselected if (state == ItemEvent.SELECTED) { result.setText(src.getText() + " selected"); } else { result.setText(src.getText() + " deselected"); } } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(ItemDemo::new); } } ``` ### State-Change Derivation $$ \texttt{getStateChange()} = \begin{cases} \texttt{ItemEvent.SELECTED} \;(\text{integer } 1) & \text{if checkbox went from unselected} \rightarrow \text{selected} \\ \texttt{ItemEvent.DESELECTED} \;(\text{integer } 2) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU examiner often asks: *“How does `ItemListener.itemStateChanged()` differ from `ActionListener.actionPerformed()`?”* The precise answer is that `itemStateChanged` fires **twice** per user click (once for select, once for deselect) when attached to a checkbox, whereas `actionPerformed` fires **once** per semantic action. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Listener | GUI Component | Event Generated | Recommended Listener | Callback Signature | |---------------|-----------------|---------------------|-------------------| | `JButton`, `JMenuItem` | Click / Enter | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | | `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton` | Selection toggle | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | | `JTextField` (Enter pressed) | Semantic Enter | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | | `JTextField` (any keystroke) | Per keystroke | `KeyListener` / `KeyAdapter` | `keyTyped(KeyEvent)` | | `JList` (item double-click) | Selection | `MouseListener` on list | `mouseClicked(MouseEvent)` | | `JFrame` (close) | Window close | `WindowListener` / `WindowAdapter` | `windowClosing(WindowEvent)` | | Mouse hover effects | Move / drag | `MouseMotionListener` / `MouseMotionAdapter` | `mouseMoved(MouseEvent)` | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often add `KeyListener` to a `JButton`. A `JButton` does not respond meaningfully to raw keystrokes — it fires `ActionEvent` when activated (by mouse click, by Space-bar focus, or by Enter). Choosing the wrong listener is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU 14-mark questions. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Event Delegation Model — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[User Action
e.g. Mouse Click] --> B[GUI Component
Event Source
e.g. JButton] B --> C{JVM creates
Event Object
e.g. ActionEvent} C --> D[Event Dispatch
Thread EDT] D --> E[Listener List
maintained by source] E --> F1[Listener 1
implements ActionListener] E --> F2[Listener 2
implements ActionListener] E --> F3[Listener N
implements ActionListener] F1 --> G1[actionPerformed e1] F2 --> G2[actionPerformed e2] F3 --> G3[actionPerformed eN] G1 --> H[GUI Update
label.setText etc.] G2 --> H G3 --> H ``` **Reading the diagram:** The source (e.g., a `JButton`) maintains an internal `EventListener[]` list. When an event fires, the source **iterates this list** in registration order and invokes the callback on each listener. This is the multicast property of the delegation model. --- ## 4.2 Class Hierarchy of Event Classes ```mermaid flowchart TD evtParent[java.util.EventObject] --> awtEvt[java.awt.AWTEvent] awtEvt --> actEvt[ActionEvent] awtEvt --> itemEvt[ItemEvent] awtEvt --> mouseEvt[MouseEvent] awtEvt --> keyEvt[KeyEvent] awtEvt --> focusEvt[FocusEvent] awtEvt --> winEvt[WindowEvent] awtEvt --> txtEvt[TextEvent] mouseEvt --> mouseMotEvt[MouseMotionEvent via getID] actEvt -.implements.-> actList[ActionListener] itemEvt -.implements.-> itemList[ItemListener] mouseEvt -.implements.-> mouseList[MouseListener] keyEvt -.implements.-> keyList[KeyListener] winEvt -.implements.-> winList[WindowListener] ``` **Key insight for KTU:** `MouseEvent` is fired for *all* mouse-related actions; `MouseMotionEvent` is not a separate class but a logical category differentiated by the event `ID` (e.g., `MouseEvent.MOUSE_MOVED`). Both `MouseListener` and `MouseMotionListener` consume the same `MouseEvent` object but react to different IDs. --- ## 4.3 Event Listener Registration Flow (Functional Block Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Source
e.g. JButton participant L as Listener Object
e.g. MyFrame participant C as Callback Method
actionPerformed U->>S: 1. Click the button S->>S: 2. Construct ActionEvent S->>S: 3. setSource(this) S->>L: 4. actionPerformed(e) L->>C: 5. Execute response code C-->>S: 6. Update GUI label/textfield S-->>U: 7. EDT repaints window Note over S,L: Many listeners may receive the same event
in a multicast fashion ``` --- ## 4.4 Swing Component-to-Listener Mapping Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Components[Swing Components] jb[JButton] jcb[JCheckBox] jrb[JRadioButton] jtf[JTextField] jlst[JList] jf[JFrame] end subgraph Events[Generated Events] ae[ActionEvent] ie[ItemEvent] ke[KeyEvent] me[MouseEvent] we[WindowEvent] end subgraph Listeners[Listener Interfaces] al[ActionListener] il[ItemListener] kl[KeyListener] ml[MouseListener] wl[WindowListener] end jb --> ae --> al jcb --> ie --> il jrb --> ie --> il jtf --> ke --> kl jtf --> ae --> al jlst --> me --> ml jf --> we --> wl ``` This block diagram is the **high-yield one-page summary** you should redraw from memory in the KTU exam when asked to “list Swing components and their event listeners.” Mapping each component to its event class and then to its listener interface is the standard 3-tier response that fetches full marks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Differentiate between an event source and an event listener. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** - An **event source** is the GUI component (e.g., a `JButton`) that **generates** an event as a result of user interaction such as a click. - An **event listener** is an object that **implements a listener interface** (e.g., `ActionListener`) and contains the callback method that handles the event. In the Event Delegation Model, the source does not process the event itself; it delegates it to one or more registered listeners via methods such as `addActionListener()`. Example: `JButton` is a source, and a class implementing `ActionListener` is a listener. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **What is the purpose of an Adapter class in Java event handling? Name two adapter classes.** **Model Answer:** Listener interfaces such as `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, and `WindowListener` contain **multiple abstract methods**. To avoid implementing all of them when only one is needed, Java provides **Adapter classes** — default empty implementations of these interfaces. The programmer can subclass the adapter and override only the relevant methods. Two adapter classes: `MouseAdapter` and `KeyAdapter` (also valid: `WindowAdapter`, `MouseMotionAdapter`, `FocusAdapter`). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **Event Delegation Model** used in Swings with a neat block diagram. List any **four** event classes and their corresponding listener interfaces. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program to create a frame containing a `JTextField`, a `JButton` (labelled “Upper”), and a `JLabel`. When the button is clicked, the text in the field should be displayed in **uppercase** in the label. Use `ActionListener`. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Event Delegation Model (EDM):** The model defines a **standard mechanism** to generate and process events in AWT/Swing. It has three participants: 1. **Event Source** — the GUI component generating the event. 2. **Event Object** — encapsulates the state change. 3. **Event Listener** — an object that registers itself with the source and contains a method to handle the event. **Working:** The user interacts with a source component. The source constructs an event object and **delegates** it to all registered listeners by invoking their callback method. The source and listener are **loosely coupled** — neither needs to know the other’s class internals. **Four Event Classes and Their Listeners:** | Event Class | Trigger | Listener Interface | |-------------|---------|--------------------| | `ActionEvent` | Button click, Enter key | `ActionListener` | | `ItemEvent` | Checkbox / radio state change | `ItemListener` | | `MouseEvent` | Mouse press / release / click | `MouseListener` | | `KeyEvent` | Key press / release / type | `KeyListener` | *(Neat block diagram: redraw the Mermaid block from Section 4.1 here on paper.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class UpperCaseApp extends JFrame implements ActionListener { JTextField input; JLabel output; JButton button; UpperCaseApp() { setTitle("Uppercase Converter"); setSize(350, 120); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE); input = new JTextField(15); button = new JButton("Upper"); output = new JLabel("Result will appear here"); add(input); add(button); add(output); button.addActionListener(this); setVisible(true); } @Override public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { String txt = input.getText(); output.setText(txt.toUpperCase()); } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(UpperCaseApp::new); } } ``` **Explanation of key lines:** - `implements ActionListener` — the frame acts as the listener. - `button.addActionListener(this);` — registers the frame as a listener. - `txt.toUpperCase()` — converts the entered string to uppercase. - `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` — thread-safe GUI launch on the EDT. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** Differentiate between **class-based listeners, inner-class listeners, and anonymous inner-class listeners** in Swing. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Write a Java Swing program using `KeyAdapter` to display, in a `JLabel`, the **running concatenation** of every character the user types into a `JTextField`. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) | Style | Definition | Advantage | Disadvantage | |-------|------------|-----------|--------------| | **Class-based listener** | The outer class (e.g., the JFrame subclass) implements the listener interface. | Simple, all components in one class. | Forces the GUI class to implement every method, reduces reusability. | | **Inner-class listener** | A named nested class within the GUI class implements the listener. | Has access to outer class’s private fields; clean separation. | Increases file length; one class per listener type may be needed. | | **Anonymous inner-class listener** | An inline unnamed class implementing the listener is passed directly to `addXxxListener(...)`. | Most concise; widely used in Swing tutorials. | Cannot be reused; harder to read in complex GUIs. | > [!NOTE] > In modern Java (≥ 8), `ActionListener` can be replaced by a **lambda expression** (since it is a *functional* interface), e.g., `button.addActionListener(e -> label.setText("Hi"));`. This is the most concise form. KTU 2024 may include a bonus credit if a student mentions this in a viva. #### Model Solution — Part (b) ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class TypeLogger extends JFrame { JTextField input; JLabel echo; StringBuilder buffer = new StringBuilder(); TypeLogger() { setTitle("Key Adapter Demo"); setSize(350, 130); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE); input = new JTextField(15); echo = new JLabel(""); add(new JLabel("Type:")); add(input); add(echo); input.addKeyListener(new KeyAdapter() { @Override public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) { buffer.append(e.getKeyChar()); echo.setText("So far: " + buffer.toString()); } }); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(TypeLogger::new); } } ``` **Key points:** - `KeyAdapter` is used instead of implementing all 3 methods of `KeyListener`. - `keyTyped` is overridden — fires for character-producing keys. - `StringBuilder` accumulates characters efficiently. - `buffer` is an instance variable so that the anonymous inner class can access it (closure over outer-class fields). --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true);`** → Program compiles but nothing displays. **−1 mark.** > 2. **Confusing `ActionEvent` and `MouseEvent`** for a `JButton` click. The semantic event is `ActionEvent`. **−2 marks.** > 3. **Using `MouseListener` (5 methods) instead of `MouseAdapter`** when only one method is needed — verbose but still works; examiners may deduct **1 mark** for style. > 4. **Not invoking `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` in `main()`** — modern best practice; may cost **1 mark** in board valuation. > 5. **Calling `setSize()` after `pack()`** — the `pack()` value will be overridden; minor deduction if not intentional. > 6. **In an inner class, attempting to access a *local* variable of the enclosing method that is not `final` / effectively final** — compilation error, program fails to run. **−2 marks** in practical exams. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Event Delegation Model** has 3 actors: **Source**, **Event**, **Listener**. Source and listener are loosely coupled. - ✅ Every GUI event is dispatched on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**. - ✅ Register a listener using `component.addXxxListener(listenerObj)`. - ✅ Unregister with `component.removeXxxListener(listenerObj)`. - ✅ `ActionListener` has **one method** (`actionPerformed`); use it for `JButton`, `JMenuItem`, and `Enter` in `JTextField`. - ✅ `ItemListener` has **one method** (`itemStateChanged`); use it for `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JList`. - ✅ `MouseListener` has **5 methods**; use `MouseAdapter` to override only the needed ones. - ✅ `KeyListener` has **3 methods**; use `KeyAdapter`. Remember the order: **PRESSED → TYPED → RELEASED**. - ✅ `WindowListener` has **7 methods**; use `WindowAdapter`. Common method: `windowClosing(WindowEvent e)`. - ✅ `event.getSource()` returns the **triggering component** as `Object` — always cast appropriately. - ✅ `event.getActionCommand()` returns the **label** of the source (useful when multiple buttons share one listener). - ✅ A **single source can have multiple listeners**; a **single listener can handle multiple sources**. - ✅ **Anonymous inner classes** are the most idiomatic Swing listener registration style. - ✅ **Lambda expressions** can replace any *functional* listener interface (those with a single method) from Java 8 onwards. - ✅ **`StringBuilder`** is preferred over `String` for accumulating text in event handlers (performance). - ✅ **Adapter classes live in the same package as their interface**, e.g., `java.awt.event.MouseAdapter`. - ✅ Always launch Swing GUIs inside `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable r)` for **thread safety**. - ✅ `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` ensures the JVM exits when the window closes; default is `HIDE_ON_CLOSE`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Mantra:** Before submitting a Swing program in the KTU lab exam, verify the **6-point checklist**: (1) imports present, (2) `main()` uses `invokeLater`, (3) `setVisible(true)` present, (4) `setDefaultCloseOperation` set, (5) listener registration present, (6) callback method correctly named and `@Override` annotated. Missing even one item risks a **2-mark deduction**.

topics.title: Swing Layout Managers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Layout Managers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Layout Manager?

A **Layout Manager** in Java Swing is an object that implements the `LayoutManager` (or `LayoutManager2`) interface and dictates the **size**, **position**, and **arrangement** of components inside a container. Instead of using absolute pixel coordinates (which break when the window is resized), the layout manager dynamically calculates component placement based on the container's current dimensions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A Layout Manager is an object of a class that implements the `java.awt.LayoutManager` interface. It is responsible for arranging the Components of a particular Container according to a predefined layout policy. It is invoked automatically by the JVM whenever the container is first displayed, resized, or when a component within it is added/removed.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are furnishing a **hotel room**. You do not hammer furniture into the floor at fixed coordinates. Instead, you hire an **interior designer** (the Layout Manager) and give them a **rulebook**:
- "The bed always goes against the **north wall**." (BorderLayout → NORTH)
- "Line up these chairs in a **row**, no gaps." (FlowLayout)
- "Arrange these photos in a **3x3 grid** like a tic-tac-toe board." (GridLayout)

When the room size changes (window resizes), the designer re-applies the rules automatically. **The Layout Manager is that interior designer for your GUI components.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Layout Managers?**
> - **Platform Independence:** Pixel-perfect placement is impossible because the same app may run on Windows, macOS, and Linux with different default font sizes and DPI.
> - **Dynamic Resizing:** When the user drags the corner of the window, components must rearrange gracefully.
> - **Internationalization:** A button labeled "Submit" in English may be "Absenden" (longer) in German — the manager accommodates this.

### 1.2 The Container-Layout Relationship

Every container (such as `JFrame`, `JPanel`, `JApplet`) holds a **single** layout manager at a time. You assign it using the method:

```java
container.setLayout(new BorderLayout());
```

You can only have **one** active layout manager per container, but since every container can hold other containers (e.g., a `JFrame` holding a `JPanel` holding another `JPanel`), you can effectively combine layouts by **nesting** panels — a very common KTU exam question pattern.

> [!TIP]
> If `setLayout(null)` is called, Java disables automatic management. You must then manually call `setBounds(x, y, width, height)` on every component. This is **strongly discouraged** in KTU board exams unless specifically asked.

### 1.3 The `LayoutManager` and `LayoutManager2` Interfaces

| Interface | Key Methods | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `LayoutManager` | `addLayoutComponent()`, `removeLayoutComponent()`, `layoutContainer()`, `minimumLayoutSize()`, `preferredLayoutSize()` | Basic sequential placement |
| `LayoutManager2` | Adds `addLayoutComponent(Component, Object)`, `getLayoutAlignmentX()`, `getLayoutAlignmentY()`, `invalidateLayout()` | Constraints-based placement (e.g., BorderLayout, GridBagLayout) |

Standard metrics to remember:
- **Default frame size in Swing:** $400 \times 300$ pixels
- **Default gap in FlowLayout:** 5 pixels (horizontal & vertical)
- **Default gap in GridLayout:** 0 pixels (no gap)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Core Layout Managers (KTU 2024 Focus)

The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following managers. Each follows a distinct placement algorithm.

### 2.1.1 `FlowLayout`

- **Class:** `java.awt.FlowLayout`
- **Default for:** `JPanel`
- **Algorithm:** Places components in a **left-to-right row**, wrapping to the next line when the right edge is reached.
- **Alignment Constants:** `FlowLayout.LEFT`, `FlowLayout.CENTER` (default), `FlowLayout.RIGHT`
- **Constructor Signatures:**
  - `FlowLayout()`
  - `FlowLayout(int align)`
  - `FlowLayout(int align, int hgap, int vgap)`

### 2.1.2 `BorderLayout`

- **Class:** `java.awt.BorderLayout`
- **Default for:** `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` content pane
- **Algorithm:** Divides the container into **5 regions**: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`.
- **Key Rule:** At most **one** component per region. Adding a second to the same region replaces the first.
- **Center takes all remaining space**; the four edges get their preferred size.

### 2.1.3 `GridLayout`

- **Class:** `java.awt.GridLayout`
- **Algorithm:** Divides the container into a **uniform grid of rows and columns**. Every cell is the same size.
- **Constructor:** `GridLayout(int rows, int cols, int hgap, int vgap)`
- **Key Rule:** The actual number of rows/cols may differ slightly from specified; the manager always fills the grid left-to-right, top-to-bottom.

### 2.1.4 `GridBagLayout`

- **Class:** `java.awt.GridBagLayout` (most powerful, most complex)
- **Algorithm:** A flexible **grid of cells** where each component can occupy **multiple rows/columns**, has **custom fill rules**, **insets**, **anchor positions**, and **weighting** (how extra space is distributed).
- Requires the `GridBagConstraints` helper object to specify rules for each component.

### 2.1.5 `BoxLayout`

- **Class:** `javax.swing.BoxLayout`
- **Algorithm:** Stacks components either **vertically** (`Y_AXIS`) or **horizontally** (`X_AXIS`). Does **not wrap**.
- Commonly used with **`Box`** containers (`Box.createVerticalBox()`, `Box.createHorizontalBox()`) and invisible fillers (`glue`, `rigidArea`, `struts`).

### 2.1.6 `CardLayout`

- **Class:** `java.awt.CardLayout`
- **Algorithm:** Treats each component as a **playing card** in a stack. Only **one card is visible at a time**, but all are kept in memory.
- Switching methods: `first(container)`, `last(container)`, `next(container)`, `show(container, name)`.
- Use case: **Tabbed interfaces, wizards, multi-step forms**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Layout Manager | Direction of Flow | Resizing Behavior | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| `FlowLayout` | Left → Right, wraps | Components keep preferred size | Toolbars, button rows |
| `BorderLayout` | 5 fixed regions | Center stretches; edges stay fixed | Window skeletons, top menus |
| `GridLayout` | Row-major grid | All cells stretch equally | Calculators, tic-tac-toe |
| `GridBagLayout` | Custom grid cells | Proportional via weight | Professional, complex forms |
| `BoxLayout` | Single line (X or Y) | Components keep preferred size | Vertical sidebars, dialogs |
| `CardLayout` | Stacked cards | Active card fills container | Wizards, tab interfaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Viva/Practical:**
> A `JFrame` *does not* allow you to `add(component)` directly with a BorderLayout setting through a typical `JFrame` reference. You must add to the **content pane**:
> ```java
> JFrame f = new JFrame();
> f.getContentPane().add(component, BorderLayout.NORTH);
> ```
> Or simply: `f.add(component, BorderLayout.NORTH);` works because `JFrame.add()` is overridden to delegate to the content pane.

## 2.3 Utility in Real Engineering

Layout managers are the backbone of:
- **NetBeans / IntelliJ IDEA** GUIs → built on `GroupLayout`
- **Scientific Computing tools** (like MATLAB's UI) → heavy `GridBagLayout`
- **Android SDK (early versions)** → inspired by `BoxLayout` & `FlowLayout`
- **Web frameworks** → the "Layout" concept in CSS Flexbox/Grid is a direct descendant of these Java managers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Layout Algorithm — How a Manager Thinks

When the JVM calls `layoutContainer(Container parent)`, the manager executes a deterministic, repeatable algorithm. We can model this mathematically for `GridLayout`.

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Cell Dimensions in `GridLayout`

**Given:**
- Container width: $W$
- Container height: $H$
- Number of rows: $r$
- Number of columns: $c$
- Horizontal gap: $g_h$
- Vertical gap: $g_v$

**Step 1:** Compute the total horizontal space consumed by gaps.
There are $c - 1$ horizontal gaps in a row.
$$\text{Total gap width} = (c - 1) \cdot g_h$$

**Step 2:** Subtract gap space from total container width to get available cell width.
$$\text{Available width} = W - (c - 1) \cdot g_h$$

**Step 3:** Divide available width equally among the $c$ columns.
$$\text{Cell width} = \frac{W - (c - 1) \cdot g_h}{c}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the same logic vertically.
$$\text{Cell height} = \frac{H - (r - 1) \cdot g_v}{r}$$

**Step 5:** Each component is then placed at:
$$x_{i} = \left(i \bmod c\right) \cdot (\text{Cell width} + g_h)$$
$$y_{i} = \left\lfloor \frac{i}{c} \right\rfloor \cdot (\text{Cell height} + g_v)$$

where $i$ is the 0-based index of the component being added.

**Example Calculation (KTU Numerical Pattern):**
Suppose $W = 400$, $H = 300$, $r = 2$, $c = 3$, $g_h = 5$, $g_v = 5$.

$$\text{Cell width} = \frac{400 - (3-1)\cdot 5}{3} = \frac{400 - 10}{3} = \frac{390}{3} = 130 \text{ px}$$

$$\text{Cell height} = \frac{300 - (2-1)\cdot 5}{2} = \frac{300 - 5}{2} = \frac{295}{2} = 147.5 \text{ px}$$

The 5th component (index 4) would be placed at:
$$x = (4 \bmod 3) \cdot (130 + 5) = 1 \cdot 135 = 135$$
$$y = \left\lfloor \frac{4}{3} \right\rfloor \cdot (147.5 + 5) = 1 \cdot 152.5 = 152.5$$

### 3.2 BorderLayout — Region Allocation Logic

The five regions of `BorderLayout` are placed in two passes:

**Pass 1 — Determine edge dimensions (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST):**
1. Place `NORTH` at top, full width, preferred height.
2. Place `SOUTH` at bottom, full width, preferred height.
3. Subtract NORTH and SOUTH heights from $H$ → remaining vertical space $H'$.
