4. Place `WEST` on the left of $H'$, preferred width.
5. Place `EAST` on the right of $H'$, preferred width.
6. Subtract WEST and EAST widths from $W$ → remaining width $W'$.

**Pass 2 — Place CENTER:**
1. The CENTER region occupies the **entire remaining** $W' \times H'$ space.
2. The component inside CENTER is stretched to fill that region.

This explains why CENTER components appear larger — they absorb all leftover space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm Complexity:** The internal layout method of `BorderLayout` is $O(n)$ where $n$ is the number of components (max 5). For `GridBagLayout`, it is $O(n \log n)$ because it sorts cells by `gridx` and `gridy` constraints.

## 3.3 `GridBagConstraints` — The 11 Constraint Fields

When using `GridBagLayout`, every component is paired with a `GridBagConstraints` object. The following table is **high-yield for KTU**:

| Field | Meaning | Common Values |
|---|---|---|
| `gridx`, `gridy` | Cell coordinates (0-based) | `0`, `1`, `2`, `RELATIVE`, `REMAINDER` |
| `gridwidth`, `gridheight` | Cell span | `1`, `2`, `REMAINDER` |
| `fill` | Stretch behavior | `NONE`, `HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, `BOTH` |
| `ipadx`, `ipady` | Internal padding (added to component) | pixel values |
| `insets` | External padding (margin) | `new Insets(top, left, bottom, right)` |
| `anchor` | Position inside cell if not filling | `CENTER`, `NORTHWEST`, etc. |
| `weightx`, `weighty` | How to distribute extra space | `0.0` (no stretch) to `1.0` (full stretch) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** If `weightx` and `weighty` are both `0` (the default), the component will **stay clustered at the top-left** of the window no matter how big the window grows. This is the #1 mistake students make.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Code Construction (BorderLayout)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class BorderDemo extends JFrame {
    BorderDemo() {
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10)); // 10px hgap, 10px vgap
        add(new JButton("NORTH"), BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(new JButton("SOUTH"), BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        add(new JButton("EAST"),  BorderLayout.EAST);
        add(new JButton("WEST"),  BorderLayout.WEST);
        add(new JButton("CENTER"),BorderLayout.CENTER);
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new BorderDemo();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. `JFrame` is created — its default layout is already `BorderLayout` for the content pane, so `setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10))` adds gaps.
2. The `add(component, region)` overload uses the `LayoutManager2` interface, which `BorderLayout` implements.
3. The buttons are added in regions; any missing region simply has no component.
4. If only `CENTER` is added, the button **fills the entire window**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Requirement | Recommended Manager |
|---|---|
| A row of 3 buttons at the bottom | `FlowLayout` inside a `JPanel` placed in `SOUTH` of `BorderLayout` |
| A 10-key numeric keypad | `GridLayout(4, 3)` |
| A login form with labels and text fields | `GridBagLayout` |
| A vertical toolbar of icons | `BoxLayout(Y_AXIS)` |
| A multi-step setup wizard | `CardLayout` |
| A simple, single-component area | Default `BorderLayout` (just use `CENTER`) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — BorderLayout Region Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Container 400x300 px"] --> B["NORTH: Preferred Height, Full Width"]
    A --> C["SOUTH: Preferred Height, Full Width"]
    A --> D["EAST: Preferred Width, Remaining Height"]
    A --> E["WEST: Preferred Width, Remaining Height"]
    A --> F["CENTER: All Remaining Space"]
    B --> G["Example: Menu Bar / Title"]
    C --> H["Example: Status Bar"]
    D --> I["Example: Right Sidebar"]
    E --> J["Example: Left Sidebar"]
    F --> K["Example: Main Workspace"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — GridLayout Component Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    Start["New Component Added Index i"] --> CalcRow["Compute row = i / cols"]
    CalcRow --> CalcCol["Compute col = i mod cols"]
    CalcCol --> CalcX["x = col x (cellWidth + hgap)"]
    CalcRow --> CalcY["y = row x (cellHeight + vgap)"]
    CalcX --> Place["Place component at x, y with cellWidth x cellHeight"]
    CalcY --> Place
    Place --> Inc["i = i + 1"]
    Inc --> Start
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — CardLayout Stack Behavior

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Card 1 Login Panel"] --> B["Card 2 Profile Panel"]
    B --> C["Card 3 Settings Panel"]
    C --> D["Card 4 Confirmation Panel"]
    D --> A
    A -.first.-> Control["CardLayout Controller"]
    B -.next.-> Control
    C -.next.-> Control
    D -.last.-> Control
    Control --> Display["Only ONE card is visible at a time"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Nested Layout Architecture (Real-World App)

```mermaid
graph TB
    Root["JFrame (BorderLayout)"] --> R1["NORTH: Title Banner JPanel"]
    Root --> R2["CENTER: Main JPanel (BorderLayout)"]
    Root --> R3["SOUTH: Status Bar JPanel (FlowLayout)"]
    R2 --> R2A["WEST: Navigation JPanel (BoxLayout Y_AXIS)"]
    R2 --> R2B["CENTER: Workspace JPanel (GridBagLayout)"]
    R2 --> R2C["EAST: Properties JPanel (BoxLayout Y_AXIS)"]
    R1 --> R1A["JLabel App Title (FlowLayout)"]
    R2A --> R2A1["JButton Home"]
    R2A --> R2A2["JButton Profile"]
    R2A --> R2A3["JButton Settings"]
    R3 --> R3A["JLabel Status: Ready"]
    R3 --> R3B["JButton Refresh"]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — GridBagConstraints Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Component to Add"] --> Q1{"Need it to stretch with window?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| SetWeight["Set weightx and weighty to non-zero"]
    Q1 -->|No| NoWeight["Leave weight at 0"]
    SetWeight --> Q2{"Fill both dimensions?"}
    NoWeight --> Q3{"Anchor position?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| FillBoth["fill = BOTH"]
    Q2 -->|No| FillH["fill = HORIZONTAL or VERTICAL"]
    Q3 --> NW["anchor = NORTHWEST etc"]
    FillBoth --> Q4{"Span multiple cells?"}
    FillH --> Q4
    NW --> Q4
    Q4 -->|Yes| SetSpan["gridwidth/gridheight = 2 or more"]
    Q4 -->|No| Default["gridwidth = gridheight = 1"]
    SetSpan --> Q5{"Add margin?"}
    Default --> Q5
    Q5 -->|Yes| SetInsets["insets = new Insets t, l, b, r"]
    Q5 -->|No| AddComp["Add component with constraints"]
    SetInsets --> AddComp
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Explain the purpose of a Layout Manager in Java Swing. Why is it preferred over absolute positioning?**

**Model Answer:**

A Layout Manager is an object that implements the `java.awt.LayoutManager` interface and is responsible for determining the **size and position** of every component added to a container.

**Why preferred over absolute positioning:**
1. **Portability:** Pixel coordinates behave differently on different operating systems due to varying default fonts and DPI scaling. A layout manager adapts automatically.
2. **Resizability:** When the user resizes the window, the layout manager recalculates component positions, whereas absolute positions become misaligned.
3. **Maintainability:** Code that uses layout managers is easier to refactor — adding a new button does not require recalculating coordinates of all others.
4. **Internationalization:** When text length changes (e.g., translated labels), the manager adjusts spacing dynamically.

In absolute positioning (`setLayout(null)`), the programmer must manually invoke `setBounds(x, y, w, h)` for every component, which is error-prone and not portable.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Pattern]**

**List and briefly describe any THREE layout managers supported by Java Swing.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **FlowLayout:** Places components in a row from left to right, wrapping to the next line when the right edge is reached. Default alignment is `CENTER`. It is the default layout of `JPanel`.

2. **BorderLayout:** Divides the container into five regions — `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, and `CENTER`. The `CENTER` region absorbs all leftover space, making it ideal for the main content area of a window. Default for `JFrame` content pane.

3. **GridLayout:** Arranges components in a rectangular grid of equally sized cells. The container is divided into a specified number of rows and columns. Used for calculator-like interfaces.

*(Other acceptable: GridBagLayout, BoxLayout, CardLayout, GroupLayout, SpringLayout.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Do **NOT** mix `setLayout(null)` with `setBounds()` while also claiming to use a manager. Pick one method.
> - In `GridBagLayout`, always remember to call `c.weightx = 0.5; c.weighty = 0.5;` for stretchable components, else the GUI collapses.
> - When asked to "write a program," do not omit the `setSize()` and `setVisible(true)` calls. Many students lose 1–2 marks for this.
> - For `BorderLayout`, mentioning the **default content-pane layout** of `JFrame` scores bonus marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the `BorderLayout` manager in detail. Write a Java program using Swing to create a window that displays a label in the `NORTH`, a text field in the `CENTER`, and two buttons ("OK" and "Cancel") in the `SOUTH` region. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain `GridLayout` with a constructor diagram. Write a Java program to design a simple calculator-style interface with buttons labeled 0–9, +, −, *, /, and "=" arranged in a 4×4 grid. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**BorderLayout Theory:**

`BorderLayout` is the default layout manager for the content pane of `JFrame`, `JDialog`, and `JApplet`. It divides the container into **five distinct regions**:
- `NORTH` → top edge, full width
- `SOUTH` → bottom edge, full width
- `EAST` → right edge, remaining height
- `WEST` → left edge, remaining height
- `CENTER` → all leftover space

Only **one component** may be placed in each region. If a region is empty, the adjacent regions expand to fill the space.

**Program Code:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class BorderExample extends JFrame {
    BorderExample() {
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10)); // 10px horizontal & vertical gaps
        
        JLabel header = new JLabel("Enter Your Name", JLabel.CENTER);
        JTextField input = new JTextField();
        JPanel southPanel = new JPanel(); // sub-panel for buttons
        southPanel.add(new JButton("OK"));
        southPanel.add(new JButton("Cancel"));
        
        add(header, BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(input, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        add(southPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        
        setTitle("BorderLayout Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new BorderExample();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**
- `setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10))` → adds 10-pixel gaps between regions.
- `add(header, BorderLayout.NORTH)` → places the label in the top region.
- `add(input, BorderLayout.CENTER)` → the text field fills all remaining space.
- `add(southPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH)` → since `SOUTH` can hold only one component, we use a sub-`JPanel` (with default `FlowLayout`) to hold both buttons.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**GridLayout Theory:**

`GridLayout` arranges components in a uniform grid of **rows × columns**. Every cell has identical dimensions. The container is filled **row by row** (left to right, top to bottom). If the number of components exceeds $r \times c$, the grid silently expands; if it is less, the extra cells remain blank.

**Constructor Signatures:**
- `GridLayout()` → 1 row, 0 columns (effectively a single row)
- `GridLayout(int rows, int cols)`
- `GridLayout(int rows, int cols, int hgap, int vgap)`

**Program Code:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class CalculatorGrid extends JFrame {
    CalculatorGrid() {
        setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5)); // 4x4 grid with 5px gaps
        
        String[] labels = {
            "7", "8", "9", "/",
            "4", "5", "6", "*",
            "1", "2", "3", "-",
            "0", "=", "+", "C"
        };
        
        for (String text : labels) {
            add(new JButton(text));
        }
        
        setTitle("Calculator Grid");
        setSize(300, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CalculatorGrid();
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- `GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5)` creates 4 rows, 4 columns, with 5-pixel horizontal and vertical gaps.
- A `for-each` loop adds 16 buttons. Since $4 \times 4 = 16$, the grid is perfectly filled.
- Each cell automatically stretches to fill its allotted space when the window is resized.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain `GridBagLayout` and the role of `GridBagConstraints`. Write a program to create a form with a label "Username", a `JTextField` next to it, a label "Password", another `JTextField` next to it, and a "Login" button at the bottom, all aligned properly using `GridBagLayout`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is `CardLayout`? Explain its use with the methods `first()`, `next()`, `previous()`, `last()`, and `show()`. Write a program that demonstrates a 3-step wizard interface. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**GridBagLayout Theory:**

`GridBagLayout` is the most flexible layout manager. Unlike `GridLayout`, it does not require uniform cell sizes. Each component can:
- Span multiple rows/columns
- Be anchored to any of 9 positions within its cell
- Be stretched via `weightx`/`weighty`
- Have internal (`ipadx`, `ipady`) and external (`insets`) padding

**The 11 fields of `GridBagConstraints`** (refer to the table in Section 2.3 above).

**Program Code:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class LoginForm extends JFrame {
    LoginForm() {
        setLayout(new GridBagLayout());
        GridBagConstraints c = new GridBagConstraints();
        c.insets = new Insets(5, 5, 5, 5); // 5px margin around every cell
        
        // Row 0: Username label
        c.gridx = 0; c.gridy = 0;
        c.anchor = GridBagConstraints.EAST;
        add(new JLabel("Username:"), c);
        
        // Row 0: Username text field (spans 2 columns)
        c.gridx = 1; c.gridy = 0;
        c.gridwidth = 2;
        c.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL;
        add(new JTextField(15), c);
        
        // Row 1: Password label
        c.gridx = 0; c.gridy = 1;
        c.gridwidth = 1;
        c.fill = GridBagConstraints.NONE;
        c.anchor = GridBagConstraints.EAST;
        add(new JLabel("Password:"), c);
        
        // Row 1: Password text field
        c.gridx = 1; c.gridy = 1;
        c.gridwidth = 2;
        c.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL;
        add(new JPasswordField(15), c);
        
        // Row 2: Login button (centered, spans all 3 columns)
        c.gridx = 0; c.gridy = 2;
        c.gridwidth = 3;
        c.anchor = GridBagConstraints.CENTER;
        c.fill = GridBagConstraints.NONE;
        add(new JButton("Login"), c);
        
        setTitle("Login Form");
        pack(); // sizes window to fit preferred sizes
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LoginForm();
    }
}
```

**Key Concepts Used:**
- A **single** `GridBagConstraints` object `c` is reused, mutating its fields before each `add()` call.
- `c.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL` stretches the text fields across their cells.
- `c.gridwidth = 2` makes the text field span 2 grid columns.
- `pack()` is preferred over `setSize()` because it sizes the window based on component preferred sizes.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**CardLayout Theory:**

`CardLayout` treats all components in a container as a **stack of cards**. Only the **top card is visible** at any time. This is analogous to a deck of playing cards where you can flip to the next or previous card.

**Key Methods:**
- `first(Container parent)` → shows the first card
- `last(Container parent)` → shows the last card
- `next(Container parent)` → shows the next card in sequence (wraps to first)
- `previous(Container parent)` → shows the previous card
- `show(Container parent, String name)` → shows the card with the specified name

**Program Code:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class WizardDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    CardLayout card;
    JPanel cardPanel;
    JButton next, prev;
    
    WizardDemo() {
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());
        card = new CardLayout();
        cardPanel = new JPanel(card);
        
        // --- Card 1 ---
        JPanel c1 = new JPanel();
        c1.add(new JLabel("Step 1: Enter Name"));
        c1.add(new JTextField(15));
        cardPanel.add(c1, "Step1");
        
        // --- Card 2 ---
        JPanel c2 = new JPanel();
        c2.add(new JLabel("Step 2: Enter Email"));
        c2.add(new JTextField(15));
        cardPanel.add(c2, "Step2");
        
        // --- Card 3 ---
        JPanel c3 = new JPanel();
        c3.add(new JLabel("Step 3: Confirm"));
        c3.add(new JButton("Submit"));
        cardPanel.add(c3, "Step3");
        
        // Navigation buttons
        JPanel nav = new JPanel();
        prev = new JButton("< Previous");
        next = new JButton("Next >");
        prev.addActionListener(this);
        next.addActionListener(this);
        nav.add(prev);
        nav.add(next);
        
        add(cardPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        add(nav, BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        
        setTitle("Wizard Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == next) card.next(cardPanel);
        if (e.getSource() == prev) card.previous(cardPanel);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new WizardDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- `cardPanel` is a `JPanel` with `CardLayout` set.
- Each card (c1, c2, c3) is added to `cardPanel` with a unique **string identifier** ("Step1", "Step2", "Step3").
- The `next` button calls `card.next(cardPanel)`; the `prev` button calls `card.previous(cardPanel)`.
- The currently visible card is the only one rendered; the others are kept in memory but invisible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in CardLayout Questions:** Students often forget to pass the **parent container** to `next()`/`previous()`. The correct call is `card.next(parentPanel)`, not `card.next()`. Also, naming each card with a unique string is mandatory for `show()` to work.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- [x] **Layout Manager Definition:** An object of a class implementing `LayoutManager` or `LayoutManager2` interface that controls size and position of components.
- [x] **Default Layouts:** `JFrame` content pane → `BorderLayout`; `JPanel` → `FlowLayout`.
- [x] **BorderLayout:** 5 regions (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER). Only ONE component per region. CENTER absorbs all leftover space.
- [x] **FlowLayout:** Components in a left-to-right line, wrap on overflow. Alignment: LEFT, CENTER (default), RIGHT.
- [x] **GridLayout:** Uniform rows × columns. All cells equal size. Use formula:
  $$\text{Cell Width} = \frac{W - (c-1) \cdot g_h}{c}, \quad \text{Cell Height} = \frac{H - (r-1) \cdot g_v}{r}$$
- [x] **GridBagLayout:** Most flexible. Uses `GridBagConstraints` with 11 fields. Always set `weightx`/`weighty` for stretchable components.
- [x] **BoxLayout:** Single line, X_AXIS or Y_AXIS. Used with `Box.createVerticalBox()`.
- [x] **CardLayout:** Stack of cards. Methods: `first()`, `last()`, `next()`, `previous()`, `show(parent, name)`.
- [x] **setLayout(null):** Disables manager. Requires `setBounds(x, y, w, h)` on each component. Not portable.
- [x] **Nesting:** Combine layouts by placing `JPanel`s (each with its own layout) inside parent containers.
- [x] **pack() vs setSize():** `pack()` computes size from preferred sizes; `setSize()` is manual. Prefer `pack()` in `GridBagLayout` programs.
- [x] **Content Pane Rule:** In modern Swing, components should be added to `getContentPane()` of a top-level window, though `add()` is overridden to delegate.
- [x] **Exam Trivia:** When asked "which layout has no gaps by default?" → Answer: `GridLayout`. "Which is the most flexible?" → `GridBagLayout`. "Which is the simplest?" → `FlowLayout`.

### Quick Mnemonics

- **BorderLayout regions** = **"NEWS"** or **"Never Eat Soggy Waffles"** (North, East, South, West).
- **GridBagConstraints fill values** = **"NHVB"** (None, Horizontal, Vertical, Both).

### Likely 2-Mark KTU Quick-Fire Questions

1. Name the interface implemented by all layout managers. *(Ans: `java.awt.LayoutManager`)*
2. Which layout is the default for `JPanel`? *(Ans: `FlowLayout`)*
3. Which region of `BorderLayout` gets the leftover space? *(Ans: `CENTER`)*
4. What does `pack()` do? *(Ans: Sizes the window based on the preferred sizes of its components.)*
5. Can a `JFrame` have two `BorderLayout` managers set at once? *(Ans: No, only one layout manager per container.)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exploring Swings - JFrame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Exploring Swings - JFrame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**JFrame** is a top-level container class in the `javax.swing` package that inherits from `java.awt.Frame`. It represents a **standard window** with a **title bar**, **border**, and optional **menu bar**, used to hold and display GUI components in a Java Swing application.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECMT535):** JFrame belongs to the *javax.swing* package (not `java.awt`). It is a **heavyweight container** (peers with the underlying OS window) and acts as the **root window** of any standalone Swing desktop application.

**Package Declaration:**
```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
```

**Class Signature:**
```java
public class JFrame extends Frame implements WindowConstants, Accessible, RootPaneContainer
```

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **`JFrame` as a physical picture frame mounted on a wall**:

| Real-World Analogy | JFrame Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The outer wooden border of a frame | The **title bar, borders, and resize controls** of a window |
| The empty glass area inside the frame | The **content pane** (where components like buttons, labels are placed) |
| Photos/decorations inside the frame | GUI components (`JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, etc.) |
| The wall on which the frame hangs | The **operating system's desktop** |
| The hanging nail behind the frame | The **operating system's window manager** (Windows/X11/macOS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Just as a picture frame is the *outermost container* that defines what you see, a `JFrame` is the **outermost container** that defines the visible window of your application. You cannot run a Swing GUI application without at least one `JFrame`.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

When working with `JFrame`, the following default behavioural constants (defined in `javax.swing.WindowConstants`) are critical:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default Close Operation Constants (Exit Codes):**
> - `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` = **0** — Window close button is disabled.
> - `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` = **1** — *(Default)* Closes the window visually but keeps the JVM alive.
> - `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` = **2** — Closes and frees resources of *this* window only.
> - `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` = **3** — Terminates the entire **JVM** (`System.exit(0)` invoked internally).

**Default Window Dimensions (typical):** `0 × 0` pixels (invisible) — explicit `setSize()` is mandatory for visibility.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Inheritance Hierarchy of JFrame

The full class hierarchy demonstrates JFrame's dual heritage from AWT (for OS-windowing) and Swing (for enhanced GUI features):

$$\text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Component} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Container} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Window} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Frame} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JFrame}$$

**Layered Pane Architecture** (Internal Structure of JFrame):

$$\text{JFrame} \supset \text{RootPane} \supset \text{LayeredPane} \supset \text{ContentPane} + \text{GlassPane} + \text{MenuBar}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Components are NOT added directly to a `JFrame` — they are added to the **Content Pane** (`frame.getContentPane().add(...)`). In modern Java (5+), you can call `frame.add(...)` directly, which is delegated to the content pane.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (The "Why" and "How")

**Step 1 — Instantiation:** Create the JFrame object using a constructor.
*Why?* The constructor allocates the OS window in memory but does not display it.

**Step 2 — Configuration:** Use setter methods to define window properties (title, size, location, layout, default close operation).
*Why?* Defaults are unusable — the window would be 0×0 and invisible.

**Step 3 — Component Assembly:** Add GUI components (`JButton`, `JLabel`, etc.) to the content pane.
*Why?* A window without components has nothing to display.

**Step 4 — Display:** Invoke `setVisible(true)`.
*Why?* Until this is called, the window exists in memory but is hidden.

**Step 5 — Event Loop:** The JVM's Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) takes over, repainting the window and handling user interactions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### Table 2.1 — JFrame Constructors

| Constructor Signature | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| `JFrame()` | Creates a frame with **no title** and **no initial size** |
| `JFrame(String title)` | Creates a frame with the specified **title bar text** |
| `JFrame(GraphicsConfiguration gc)` | Creates a frame with the specified **graphics device config** |

#### Table 2.2 — High-Yield JFrame Methods (MUST memorize for KTU)

| Method Signature | Purpose | Default Value |
|---|---|---|
| `void setTitle(String title)` | Sets the **title bar** text | `""` (empty) |
| `void setSize(int w, int h)` | Sets the **dimensions** in pixels | `0 × 0` |
| `void setLocation(int x, int y)` | Sets the **screen position** (top-left corner) | OS-defined |
| `void setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` | Combined **position + size** setter | N/A |
| `void setVisible(boolean b)` | Shows or hides the window | `false` |
| `void setDefaultCloseOperation(int op)` | Defines **close button** behaviour | `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` |
| `void setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` | Sets the **layout strategy** | `BorderLayout` |
| `void setResizable(boolean b)` | Allows/blocks **window resizing** | `true` |
| `void setIconImage(Image img)` | Sets the **title bar icon** | OS default |
| `void pack()` | Sizes window to **fit preferred sizes** of components | N/A |
| `Container getContentPane()` | Returns the **content pane** container | Auto-created |
| `void setContentPane(Container c)` | Replaces the **content pane** | Auto-created |
| `JMenuBar getMenuBar()` / `setMenuBar()` | Manages the **menu bar** slot | `null` |
| `Component getGlassPane()` / `setGlassPane()` | Manages the **glass pane** (overlay) | Default |

#### Table 2.3 — Default Close Operation Constants

| Constant | Integer Value | Real Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| `WindowConstants.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` | 0 | Close button ignored |
| `WindowConstants.HIDE_ON_CLOSE` | 1 | Window hidden, **JVM keeps running** |
| `WindowConstants.DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` | 2 | Window disposed, JVM keeps running |
| `WindowConstants.EXIT_ON_CLOSE` | 3 | **JVM terminated** (`System.exit(0)`) |

#### Table 2.4 — Common Layout Managers Used Inside JFrame

| Layout Manager | Arrangement Style | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `BorderLayout` | 5 regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER | **Default** of JFrame; general-purpose |
| `FlowLayout` | Left-to-right, top-to-bottom | Simple button rows |
| `GridLayout` | Uniform rows × columns grid | Calculator-style apps |
| `GridBagLayout` | Flexible cell-based grid | Complex forms |
| `BoxLayout` | Single row OR single column | Toolbars, vertical menus |
| `null` (Absolute) | Pixel-perfect x,y coordinates | Custom designs, games |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

JFrame and the Swing framework are foundational in:
- **Desktop Enterprise Applications:** Internal tools, banking dashboards, hospital management systems.
- **Scientific/Research Tools:** Data visualization, simulation front-ends (e.g., image processing in bioinformatics).
- **IDE Plugins:** NetBeans and older Eclipse plugins used Swing extensively.
- **Educational Software:** Interactive learning platforms and lab simulators.
- **Embedded System Configurators:** Configuration tools for routers, PLCs, and industrial controllers (where web UI is overkill).

> [!NOTE]
> Although modern Java favors **JavaFX** for new projects, **Swing remains a KTU syllabus requirement** and is still widely encountered in legacy enterprise systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section progressively derives JFrame applications, from the simplest "Hello World" window to a fully interactive multi-component application.

### 3.1 Example 1 — The Minimal JFrame (Exhaustive Derivation)

**Problem:** Create the simplest possible JFrame window titled "My First App" sized 400×300 pixels, displayed at position (100, 100) on the screen, that exits the application on close.

**Step 1:** Import the JFrame class.
```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
```

**Step 2:** Create a public class that will contain the main method and the JFrame.
```java
public class FirstFrame {
```

**Step 3:** Declare the main method (entry point of the JVM).
```java
    public static void main(String[] args) {
```

**Step 4:** Instantiate the JFrame with a title using the parameterized constructor.
```java
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("My First App");
```
*Explanation:* `new JFrame("My First App")` allocates a new OS window. The string `"My First App"` is passed to the constructor which internally calls `setTitle()`.

**Step 5:** Set the window's dimensions to 400 pixels wide and 300 pixels tall.
```java
        frame.setSize(400, 300);
```
*Explanation:* Without this, the window would be 0×0 and invisible. `setSize` takes `width` then `height` in pixels.

**Step 6:** Set the window's screen position to (100, 100) measured from the top-left corner of the screen.
```java
        frame.setLocation(100, 100);
```
*Explanation:* Coordinates use screen pixels; (0,0) is the top-left of the monitor.

**Step 7:** Define the close-button behaviour to terminate the entire JVM.
```java
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
```
*Explanation:* Without this, clicking ✕ would only hide the window (the JVM would continue running silently in the background).

**Step 8:** Make the window visible on screen.
```java
        frame.setVisible(true);
```
*Explanation:* This triggers the window manager to render the OS-level window. Until this line executes, the window is purely in-memory.

**Step 9:** Close the main method and the class.
```java
    }
}
```

**Complete Consolidated Code:**
```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;

public class FirstFrame {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("My First App");
        frame.setSize(400, 300);
        frame.setLocation(100, 100);
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Output:** A 400×300 pixel window titled "My First App" appears at position (100, 100) on the screen.

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — JFrame with Custom Title, Icon, and Disabled Resize

**Problem:** Build a fixed-size, non-resizable window with a custom title and disable the maximize button functionality.

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import java.awt.Image;
import java.awt.Toolkit;

public class FixedFrame {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("Student Registration System");
        
        // Set fixed dimensions
        frame.setSize(600, 400);
        frame.setLocationRelativeTo(null); // Center on screen
        
        // Prevent resizing
        frame.setResizable(false);
        
        // Set close behaviour
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        
        // Set custom icon (optional, requires an icon.png file)
        // Image icon = Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().getImage("icon.png");
        // frame.setIconImage(icon);
        
        // Display
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Line-by-Line Logic:**

| Line | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` | Centers window on screen (passing `null` means "center relative to screen") |
| `setResizable(false)` | Disables resize corner and maximize button |
| `Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().getImage(...)` | Loads an image file from disk for the title-bar icon |

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — JFrame with Components (Label + Button + Event Handling)

**Problem:** Create a JFrame with a `JLabel` displaying "Welcome to KTU" and a `JButton` that, when clicked, changes the label's text.

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JLabel;
import javax.swing.JButton;
import java.awt.FlowLayout;
import java.awt.event.ActionEvent;
import java.awt.event.ActionListener;

public class InteractiveFrame extends JFrame {
    private JLabel messageLabel;
    private JButton clickButton;
    
    public InteractiveFrame() {
        // 1. Set title via super constructor
        super("Interactive Demo");
        
        // 2. Set layout manager
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        
        // 3. Initialize components
        messageLabel = new JLabel("Welcome to KTU");
        clickButton = new JButton("Click Me");
        
        // 4. Register event listener (anonymous inner class)
        clickButton.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                messageLabel.setText("Button Clicked! Hello, Swing!");
            }
        });
        
        // 5. Add components to content pane
        add(messageLabel);
        add(clickButton);
        
        // 6. Configure frame
        setSize(400, 150);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new InteractiveFrame();
    }
}
```

**Exhaustive Logic Walkthrough:**

**Step 1 — `super("Interactive Demo")`:** The first line of the constructor must call the `JFrame` parent constructor with the title. This is the cleanest way to set a title when extending `JFrame`.

**Step 2 — `setLayout(new FlowLayout())`:** Override the default `BorderLayout` with `FlowLayout`, which arranges components left-to-right in a single line. The default is `BorderLayout` because `JFrame` inherits from `java.awt.Frame`.

**Step 3 — Component Initialization:** `JLabel` displays read-only text. `JButton` is a clickable UI element.

**Step 4 — Event Registration:** `addActionListener()` registers a callback that fires when the button is clicked. The anonymous inner class implements `ActionListener` and overrides `actionPerformed()`.

**Step 5 — `add()` Method:** Adds components to the content pane (Swing's `JFrame.add()` delegates to `contentPane.add()` automatically).

**Step 6 — Window Configuration:** Sets size, centers on screen, configures close behaviour, and displays the window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trap:** The order matters! Always call `setLayout()` BEFORE `add()`. Once components are added, changing the layout may require revalidation. Also, `setVisible(true)` should ALWAYS be the **last** call.

---

### 3.4 Example 4 — Using `pack()` for Automatic Sizing

The `pack()` method automatically sizes the window based on the **preferred sizes** of its components — eliminating the need to hardcode dimensions.

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JPanel;
import javax.swing.JButton;
import java.awt.GridLayout;

public class PackExample extends JFrame {
    public PackExample() {
        super("Pack Demo");
        setLayout(new GridLayout(2, 2, 10, 10));
        
        add(new JButton("Button 1"));
        add(new JButton("Button 2"));
        add(new JButton("Button 3"));
        add(new JButton("Button 4"));
        
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        pack();  // Auto-size to fit components + 10px gaps
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new PackExample();
    }
}
```

**Why `pack()` instead of `setSize()`?**

$$\text{Window Size} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{PreferredSize}(C_i) + \text{Insets} + \text{LayoutGaps}$$

Using `pack()` makes the application **resolution-independent** and **internationalization-friendly** (text in different languages may render wider/taller).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Approach

| Scenario | Recommended Approach | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed-size dialog | `setSize()` + `setResizable(false)` | Predictable UX |
| Dynamic content window | `pack()` | Adapts to content |
| Multi-window app | One JFrame as **main**, others as **internal** or **secondary frames** | Memory-efficient |
| High-DPI screens | `setSize()` with scaled values or `pack()` | Avoids tiny/clipped UI |
| Custom painting | Override `paintComponent()` on a JPanel inside JFrame | Better than painting on JFrame directly |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
