## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

**Explain the role of the `javax.swing.JFrame` class. List any THREE constructors of JFrame with their syntax.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `JFrame` class, belonging to the `javax.swing` package, is a **top-level container** used to create the main window of a Swing-based GUI application. It extends `java.awt.Frame` and provides a window with a title bar, border, optional menu bar, and a content pane to hold other Swing components.

**Three constructors of JFrame:**

1. `JFrame()` — Creates a window with no title and default size.
2. `JFrame(String title)` — Creates a window with the specified title displayed in the title bar.
3. `JFrame(GraphicsConfiguration gc)` — Creates a window using the specified graphics configuration of the display device.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the three constructors with brief explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Differentiate between `setSize()` and `pack()` methods of JFrame. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `setSize(int w, int h)` | `pack()` |
|---|---|---|
| Sizing approach | **Explicit** — developer specifies pixel dimensions | **Automatic** — sizes itself based on components' preferred sizes |
| Flexibility | Fixed regardless of content | Adapts dynamically to content |
| Use case | When dimensions are known and fixed (e.g., a 400×300 dialog) | When components dictate the optimal size (e.g., dynamic forms) |

**Preference rule:** Use `setSize()` for fixed-size dialogs and tool windows. Use `pack()` when content varies in size (e.g., forms with optional fields, multilingual text). Calling `pack()` after adding components is considered best practice in modern Swing applications.

*(Award 1.5 marks for the difference, 1.5 marks for the preference rule.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice is mandatory.**

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the JFrame class hierarchy starting from `java.lang.Object` to `javax.swing.JFrame`. Mention the significance of the `ContentPane` and `GlassPane` in JFrame. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java program to create a JFrame titled **"Student Database"** with a `JLabel` displaying the text *"Enter Roll Number:"*, a `JTextField` of 15 columns, and a `JButton` labeled *"Submit"*. The window must be 500×200 pixels, centered on screen, non-resizable, and must terminate the application on closing. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Hierarchy Explanation (7 marks):**

The complete inheritance chain of JFrame is:

$$\text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Component} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Container} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Window} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Frame} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JFrame}$$

**Significance of each class in the chain:**

- `java.lang.Object` — The root of all Java classes; provides `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `toString()`.
- `java.awt.Component` — Abstract base for any UI element having a position and size on screen.
- `java.awt.Container` — Extends `Component`; can hold other components.
- `java.awt.Window` — Represents a top-level window with no title bar or border.
- `java.awt.Frame` — Extends `Window`; adds title bar, menu bar, and resizable border (from AWT).
- `javax.swing.JFrame` — The Swing-specific top-level container with pluggable Look & Feel support and a sophisticated layered-pane architecture.

**Content Pane:** The primary region where GUI components (`JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, etc.) are added. Internally, `JFrame` delegates `add()` calls to its content pane. The default layout of the content pane is `BorderLayout`.

**Glass Pane:** A transparent overlay positioned ABOVE the content pane. By default, it is invisible and non-interactive. It is used for:
- Drawing tooltips and custom drag-and-drop feedback.
- Intercepting mouse events across the entire frame.
- Implementing splash screens or modal-like overlays.

**Glass Pane** sits on top, **Content Pane** sits at the bottom — both nested inside the **Root Pane** and **Layered Pane**.

*(Award 2 marks for the hierarchy chain, 2.5 marks for ContentPane explanation, 2.5 marks for GlassPane explanation.)*

---

**(b) Complete Java Program (7 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JLabel;
import javax.swing.JTextField;
import javax.swing.JButton;
import java.awt.FlowLayout;

public class StudentDatabaseFrame extends JFrame {
    public StudentDatabaseFrame() {
        // Set title
        super("Student Database");
        
        // Set layout
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        
        // Create components
        JLabel label = new JLabel("Enter Roll Number:");
        JTextField textField = new JTextField(15);
        JButton submitButton = new JButton("Submit");
        
        // Add components to content pane
        add(label);
        add(textField);
        add(submitButton);
        
        // Configure window
        setSize(500, 200);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);    // Center on screen
        setResizable(false);            // Disable resizing
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        
        // Display
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new StudentDatabaseFrame();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step validation of the program:**

1. The class `StudentDatabaseFrame` **extends `JFrame`** so we can call its methods directly.
2. `super("Student Database")` sets the title in the title bar.
3. `setLayout(new FlowLayout())` arranges components left-to-right.
4. The `JTextField(15)` constructor sets the column width to 15 characters.
5. `setSize(500, 200)` explicitly fixes dimensions.
6. `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` centers the window.
7. `setResizable(false)` disables the maximize button and corner-resize.
8. `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` ensures full JVM termination.
9. `setVisible(true)` displays the window as the last step.

*(Award 2 marks for class structure, 2 marks for component creation, 1 mark for layout, 2 marks for window configuration.)*

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]` (ALTERNATIVE)

**(a)** Explain the four `WindowConstants` close operations supported by JFrame. Why is `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` generally preferred for standalone applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that creates a JFrame containing a `JLabel` and a `JButton`. When the button is clicked, the label text must change from **"Hello"** to **"Welcome to KTU"**. Use an **anonymous inner class** for event handling. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Four WindowConstants Close Operations (7 marks):**

The `javax.swing.WindowConstants` interface defines four integer constants that govern what happens when the user clicks the window's close (✕) button:

| Constant | Value | Behaviour | JVM Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` | 0 | The close button is **disabled** — clicking it has no effect. | Stays running |
| `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` | 1 | **Default.** The window is hidden but the object remains in memory. | **Stays running** |
| `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` | 2 | The window's resources are released; if it was the last visible window, JVM may exit. | May stay or exit |
| `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` | 3 | Invokes `System.exit(0)`, terminating **all** threads immediately. | **Terminated** |

**Why `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` is preferred for standalone applications:**

- A typical Swing program has only **one main JFrame**. When the user closes it, they expect the application to fully terminate.
- With `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (the default), the window disappears but the **JVM keeps running silently**, consuming memory and CPU. The user must forcefully kill the process via Task Manager / Activity Monitor.
- `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` invokes `System.exit(0)` which performs a **clean shutdown** of all threads and releases OS resources.

**When NOT to use `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`:** Multi-window applications (e.g., a main window with a "Settings" dialog) — closing the dialog should not terminate the entire app. Use `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` or `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` in such cases.

*(Award 4 marks for the constants table, 3 marks for the justification.)*

---

**(b) Java Program with Event Handling (7 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JLabel;
import javax.swing.JButton;
import java.awt.FlowLayout;
import java.awt.event.ActionEvent;
import java.awt.event.ActionListener;

public class EventDemo extends JFrame {
    public EventDemo() {
        super("Event Handling Demo");
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        
        JLabel label = new JLabel("Hello");
        JButton button = new JButton("Click Me");
        
        // Anonymous inner class for event handling
        button.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                label.setText("Welcome to KTU");
            }
        });
        
        add(label);
        add(button);
        
        setSize(300, 100);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new EventDemo();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Line `new ActionListener() { ... }`:** This creates an **anonymous inner class** that implements the `ActionListener` interface. Anonymous because it has no name; inner because it is defined inside another class.
2. **`@Override public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`:** This method is called automatically by the JVM's Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) when the button is clicked.
3. **`label.setText("Welcome to KTU")`:** This updates the label's displayed text.
4. **Lambda alternative (Java 8+):** The same code can be written more concisely as:
   ```java
   button.addActionListener(e -> label.setText("Welcome to KTU"));
   ```
5. **EDT consideration:** In production code, Swing components should be created and updated on the Event Dispatch Thread using `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` to avoid thread-safety issues.

*(Award 2 marks for the program structure, 3 marks for the anonymous inner class implementation, 1 mark for setText logic, 1 mark for window configuration.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`:** The most common error. The code compiles and runs without errors, but **no window appears**. Always place `setVisible(true)` as the **last** configuration call.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`:** Students submit perfectly working code, but the JVM does not terminate when the window closes. Examiners may dock 1–2 marks if this is omitted in a standalone application program.
> 
> 3. **Adding components to JFrame directly without understanding the content pane:** While `frame.add(component)` works in modern Java, students should **explicitly state** in theory answers that components are added to the **content pane**, not directly to the JFrame.
> 
> 4. **Confusing `setSize()` with `setBounds()`:** `setSize(w, h)` only sets dimensions; `setBounds(x, y, w, h)` sets both position AND size. Mixing them up costs marks.
> 
> 5. **Not importing required classes:** Missing `import javax.swing.JFrame;` causes compilation errors. Common imports often missed: `java.awt.FlowLayout`, `java.awt.event.ActionListener`.
> 
> 6. **Calling `super("Title")` AFTER `setTitle()`:** If you extend JFrame and call `setTitle()` in the constructor before `super()`, it will throw an error because the parent constructor hasn't run yet. Use `super("Title")` as the **first line** of the subclass constructor.
> 
> 7. **Thread safety:** Creating Swing components in `main()` without wrapping in `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` is technically unsafe (though it often works for simple demos). Examiners appreciate the awareness.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (JFrame):**
> 
> - **Package:** `javax.swing` (NOT `java.awt`).
> - **Extends:** `java.awt.Frame` (which extends `java.awt.Window`).
> - **Purpose:** Top-level container — the root window of any Swing application.
> - **Mandatory configuration sequence:** Constructor → `setSize()`/`pack()` → `setLayout()` → `add()` → `setDefaultCloseOperation()` → `setVisible(true)`.
> - **Default layout:** `BorderLayout` (inherited from `java.awt.Frame`).
> - **Default close operation:** `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (JVM keeps running — remember to override!).
> - **Default size:** `0 × 0` pixels (invisible without `setSize()` or `pack()`).
> - **Components are added to the content pane** (use `getContentPane().add()` or simply `add()`).
> - **Layered architecture:** `JFrame → RootPane → LayeredPane → ContentPane + GlassPane + MenuBar`.
> - **Best practice for sizing:** Use `pack()` over `setSize()` for resolution-independence.
> - **Best practice for centering:** Use `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` instead of hardcoding coordinates.
> - **Best practice for event dispatch:** Wrap GUI creation in `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` in production.
> - **Exit safely:** Use `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` for single-window apps; `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` for multi-window apps.
> - **First line of subclass constructor:** Always call `super("Your Title")` before anything else if extending JFrame.
> - **Common exam keyword:** *"JFrame is a heavyweight container belonging to javax.swing, providing the root window for GUI applications."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JLabel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** JLabel # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition `JLabel` is a **passive display component** belonging to the `javax.swing` package of the Java Standard Edition (Java SE) API. It is formally defined as a subclass of `JComponent` and is primarily used to render **non-editable, single-line (or limited multi-line) text strings, images, or both simultaneously** on a graphical user interface (GUI). Unlike interactive widgets such as buttons or text fields, a `JLabel` does not generate action events when clicked because it does not capture user input. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT535 – Module 4: Swing):** > `JLabel` is a foundational Swing class. The university expects students to be able to *instantiate*, *customize* (text, icon, alignment, font, color), and *add it to a top-level container* like `JFrame`. The class signature as per the official Java API specification is: $$ \text{public class JLabel extends JComponent implements SwingConstants, Accessible} $$ Here, the implementation of the `SwingConstants` interface is significant because it provides the **alignment constants** (e.g., `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`) that govern how the label's content is positioned within its display area. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Think of a **`JLabel` as a printed signboard or a name tag** mounted on a wall. | Real-World Object | JLabel Equivalent | Functional Mapping | |---|---|---| | A cardboard sign reading "EXIT" | A `JLabel` displaying text | The display content | | A picture hung on a notice board | A `JLabel` displaying an `ImageIcon` | The visual content | | Positioning rules (center, left, right) | Horizontal/Vertical alignment constants | Layout rules | | Pinning the sign to a wall | Adding the label to a `JFrame` or `JPanel` | Container parenting | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for Beginners:** A `JLabel` is essentially a **read-only display surface**. It cannot be edited by the user, nor does it trigger any event when the mouse is clicked. Its sole purpose is to *inform* the user, not to *interact* with them. This is why it is called a *passive* component. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Properties The three fundamental properties that govern the behavior of a `JLabel` are: - **Text Content:** The string displayed (can include HTML formatting). - **Icon:** An optional `Icon` object (commonly `ImageIcon`) shown alongside the text. - **Alignment:** Horizontal and vertical positioning of the contents within the label's bounds. The default content area is set to **opaque = false**, meaning the background of the label itself is transparent unless explicitly changed by calling `setOpaque(true)`. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Class Hierarchy The inheritance chain of `JLabel` is critical for KTU examinations and must be memorized. $$ \text{java.lang.Object} $$ $$\quad \uparrow$$ $$\text{java.awt.Component}$$ $$\quad \uparrow$$ $$\text{java.awt.Container}$$ $$\quad \uparrow$$ $$\text{javax.swing.JComponent}$$ $$\quad \uparrow$$ $$\text{javax.swing.JLabel}$$ This chain explains why a `JLabel` inherits both **AWT properties** (such as `setSize`, `setLocation`) and **Swing enhancements** (such as borders, tooltips, double-buffering, and pluggable Look-and-Feel). ## 2.2 Constructors of JLabel The `JLabel` class provides **six overloaded constructors**, each offering a different level of initialization. These are extremely high-yield for KTU Part A and Part B questions. | # | Constructor Signature | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1 | `JLabel()` | Creates an empty label with no text and no icon. | | 2 | `JLabel(String text)` | Creates a label with the given text string. | | 3 | `JLabel(Icon image)` | Creates a label with the given image icon. | | 4 | `JLabel(String text, Icon icon, int horizontalAlignment)` | Creates a label with text, icon, and alignment. | | 5 | `JLabel(String text, int horizontalAlignment)` | Creates a label with text and alignment. | | 6 | `JLabel(Icon image, int horizontalAlignment)` | Creates a label with icon and alignment. | > [!NOTE] > **Alignment Constants:** The valid values for `horizontalAlignment` are `SwingConstants.LEFT` (= 2), `SwingConstants.CENTER` (= 0), `SwingConstants.RIGHT` (= 4), `SwingConstants.LEADING` (= 10), and `SwingConstants.TRAILING` (= 11). ## 2.3 Core Methods (Cheat Sheet) The following table summarizes the most frequently tested methods of `JLabel`: | Method | Return Type | Functional Purpose | |---|---|---| | `setText(String text)` | `void` | Sets the displayed text of the label. | | `getText()` | `String` | Retrieves the displayed text. | | `setIcon(Icon icon)` | `void` | Sets the image icon shown on the label. | | `getIcon()` | `Icon` | Retrieves the current icon. | | `setHorizontalAlignment(int alignment)` | `void` | Sets the horizontal position of the content. | | `setVerticalAlignment(int alignment)` | `void` | Sets the vertical position of the content. | | `setHorizontalTextPosition(int position)` | `void` | Sets text position relative to the icon (LEFT/CENTER/RIGHT of icon). | | `setVerticalTextPosition(int position)` | `void` | Sets text position relative to the icon (TOP/CENTER/BOTTOM of icon). | | `setIconTextGap(int gap)` | `void` | Sets the pixel gap between icon and text. | | `setFont(Font font)` | `void` | Sets the font of the text. | | `setForeground(Color fg)` | `void` | Sets the text color. | | `setBackground(Color bg)` | `void` | Sets the background color (requires `setOpaque(true)`). | | `setOpaque(boolean isOpaque)` | `void` | Toggles whether the background is painted. | | `setEnabled(boolean enabled)` | `void` | Enables or disables the label visually. | ## 2.4 Why and How — Operational Logic A `JLabel` operates based on a **content-first, layout-second** principle: 1. **Content Selection:** The label stores a `String` and an `Icon` as internal properties (`text` and `icon`). 2. **Alignment Selection:** The horizontal and vertical alignment properties determine *where* the content is rendered within the label's rectangular area. 3. **Composite Rendering:** When both an icon and text exist, the `horizontalTextPosition` and `verticalTextPosition` properties dictate their **relative arrangement**. 4. **Container Integration:** When added to a `JFrame` or `JPanel` via `add(label)`, the layout manager of the parent container allocates space, and the label renders accordingly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility:** `JLabel` is used in nearly every Java desktop application: showing form field descriptions (e.g., "Username:"), displaying status messages in IDEs, rendering splash screen logos, and creating image galleries. It is the *Swiss Army knife* of passive information display. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Code Derivations > [!NOTE] > Since this is a CS/OOP course, **Java code is mandatory** and constitutes the core "derivation" expected by KTU examiners. Every line is explained step-by-step. ## 3.1 Demonstration 1 — Basic JLabel with Text **Problem Statement:** Write a Java program to create a `JFrame` window with dimensions 400x300 and display a `JLabel` containing the text "Welcome to KTU" centered horizontally. **Step 1: Import the required packages.** ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; import javax.swing.SwingConstants; ``` The `javax.swing` package contains the `JLabel` and `JFrame` classes. `SwingConstants` provides the alignment constants. **Step 2: Define the main class and method.** ```java public class LabelDemo1 { public static void main(String[] args) { ``` **Step 3: Create a top-level container (JFrame).** ```java JFrame frame = new JFrame("Label Demonstration 1"); frame.setSize(400, 300); frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); ``` `JFrame` is the parent window. `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` ensures the program terminates when the window is closed. **Step 4: Instantiate the JLabel.** ```java JLabel label = new JLabel("Welcome to KTU"); label.setHorizontalAlignment(SwingConstants.CENTER); ``` The constructor `JLabel(String text)` initializes the label with the text "Welcome to KTU". The `setHorizontalAlignment` method centers the text within the label's area. **Step 5: Add the label to the frame and make it visible.** ```java frame.add(label); frame.setVisible(true); } } ``` The `add()` method places the label into the frame's content pane. `setVisible(true)` renders the GUI. **Complete Program:** ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; import javax.swing.SwingConstants; public class LabelDemo1 { public static void main(String[] args) { JFrame frame = new JFrame("Label Demonstration 1"); frame.setSize(400, 300); frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); JLabel label = new JLabel("Welcome to KTU"); label.setHorizontalAlignment(SwingConstants.CENTER); frame.add(label); frame.setVisible(true); } } ``` ## 3.2 Demonstration 2 — JLabel with Text and Icon **Problem Statement:** Create a `JLabel` that displays both an image icon and a caption "Java Programming", with the text positioned to the right of the icon and vertically centered. **Step 1: Import the additional `ImageIcon` class.** ```java import javax.swing.ImageIcon; ``` **Step 2: Create the JLabel using the 4-argument constructor.** ```java ImageIcon icon = new ImageIcon("java_logo.png"); JLabel label = new JLabel("Java Programming", icon, SwingConstants.LEFT); ``` The constructor `JLabel(String text, Icon icon, int horizontalAlignment)` initializes both the text and icon in a single call. The `LEFT` alignment positions content at the left edge. **Step 3: Configure the relative position of text and icon.** ```java label.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.RIGHT); label.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER); ``` These two methods ensure the text appears *to the right* of the icon, and the combined icon+text block is *vertically centered* within the label's bounding rectangle. **Step 4: Set a custom gap between icon and text.** ```java label.setIconTextGap(15); ``` This creates a 15-pixel spacing between the icon and the text for better visual aesthetics. **Complete Program:** ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; import javax.swing.ImageIcon; import javax.swing.SwingConstants; public class LabelDemo2 { public static void main(String[] args) { JFrame frame = new JFrame("Label with Icon"); frame.setSize(500, 400); frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); frame.setLayout(null); ImageIcon icon = new ImageIcon("java_logo.png"); JLabel label = new JLabel("Java Programming", icon, SwingConstants.CENTER); label.setBounds(50, 50, 400, 200); label.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.RIGHT); label.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER); label.setIconTextGap(15); frame.add(label); frame.setVisible(true); } } ``` ## 3.3 Demonstration 3 — HTML-Formatted Text in JLabel **Problem Statement:** Display multi-style text (bold, color, font) inside a `JLabel` using inline HTML. **Step 1: Recognize that `JLabel` supports a subset of HTML 3.2.** ```java JLabel htmlLabel = new JLabel("Bold Title
" + "Red subtitle"); ``` **Step 2: Add to the frame and render.** ```java frame.add(htmlLabel); frame.setVisible(true); ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Always remember that for HTML rendering to work in `JLabel`, the string **must start with ``** (lowercase). If the angle bracket is missing, the entire string is treated as plain text and the HTML tags will be visible to the user as raw characters. ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Method Chaining Example Often in KTU Part B questions, students are expected to use **method chaining** for compactness. ```java JLabel lbl = new JLabel("Status: Ready"); lbl.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 18)); lbl.setForeground(Color.BLUE); lbl.setOpaque(true); lbl.setBackground(Color.YELLOW); ``` Each setter modifies a single visual property. The order of invocation does not matter — Swing repaints the component automatically once the next event dispatch thread cycle occurs. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 JLabel Class Hierarchy (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD nodeObj["java lang Object"] nodeAwtComp["java awt Component"] nodeAwtCont["java awt Container"] nodeJComp["javax swing JComponent"] nodeJLabel["javax swing JLabel"] nodeSwingCon["implements SwingConstants"] nodeAccess["implements Accessible"] nodeObj --> nodeAwtComp nodeAwtComp --> nodeAwtCont nodeAwtCont --> nodeJComp nodeJComp --> nodeJLabel nodeSwingCon -.implements.-> nodeJLabel nodeAccess -.implements.-> nodeJLabel ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — JLabel Internal Architecture ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["Constructor Call JLabel text, icon, alignment"] nodeB["Internal Storage: text String"] nodeC["Internal Storage: icon ImageIcon"] nodeD["Internal Storage: hAlign int"] nodeE["Internal Storage: vAlign int"] nodeF["paintComponent Graphics g Method"] nodeG["Final Rendered Display on JFrame"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG ``` ## 4.3 Label-Icon-Text Spatial Relationship Diagram ```mermaid graph TD subgraph "JLabel Bounding Rectangle" direction TB node1["Case 1: Text Only - centered"] node2["Case 2: Icon Only - centered"] node3["Case 3: Icon Left, Text Right"] node4["Case 4: Text Left, Icon Right"] node5["Case 5: Icon Top, Text Bottom"] node6["Case 6: Text Top, Icon Bottom"] end node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Spatial Diagram:** The `setHorizontalTextPosition` and `setVerticalTextPosition` methods allow the four cardinal arrangements of icon vs. text. By default (no explicit calls), the text appears to the **right** of the icon (i.e., `LEFT` of icon → `RIGHT` text position). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: What is `JLabel` in Java Swing? List any two constructors of `JLabel`.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** `JLabel` is a class in the `javax.swing` package used to display a single-line text read-only area or an image on a GUI. It is a passive component that does not generate any events. It extends `JComponent` and implements the `SwingConstants` interface. (2 marks) Two constructors are: 1. `JLabel()` — creates an empty label. (0.5 marks) 2. `JLabel(String text)` — creates a label with the specified text. (0.5 marks) ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between `setHorizontalAlignment()` and `setHorizontalTextPosition()` of `JLabel`.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - `setHorizontalAlignment(int alignment)`: Positions the **entire content** (icon + text together) horizontally within the label's display area. (1.5 marks) - `setHorizontalTextPosition(int position)`: Positions the **text relative to the icon** when both are present in the label. (1.5 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the class hierarchy of `JLabel` in Java Swing with the help of a neat diagram. (7 marks) **(b)** Write a Java program using Swing to create a window displaying a `JLabel` with an image icon and a text "KTU 2024 Scheme" with the text positioned at the bottom of the icon. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a): The class hierarchy of `JLabel` is as follows: $$ \text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Component} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Container} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JComponent} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JLabel} $$ **Explanation of each level:** - `java.lang.Object`: The root class of all Java classes; provides basic methods like `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()`. (1 mark) - `java.awt.Component`: An abstract class that represents any object that can be displayed on screen and can interact with the user. Provides methods like `setSize()`, `setLocation()`, `setBackground()`. (1 mark) - `java.awt.Container`: A subclass of `Component` that can hold other AWT components. Provides the `add()` method. (1 mark) - `javax.swing.JComponent`: The base class for all Swing components. Adds pluggable look-and-feel, double-buffering, borders, and tooltips. (2 marks) - `javax.swing.JLabel`: The terminal class that implements `SwingConstants` (for alignment constants) and `Accessible` (for screen-reader support). It is a passive display component. (2 marks) #### Model Solution for (b): ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; import javax.swing.ImageIcon; import javax.swing.SwingConstants; public class KTULabelDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // Step 1: Create the JFrame window JFrame frame = new JFrame("KTU 2024 Label"); frame.setSize(500, 400); frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); frame.setLayout(null); // Step 2: Create an ImageIcon from a local file ImageIcon icon = new ImageIcon("ktu_logo.png"); // Step 3: Create the JLabel with text and icon JLabel label = new JLabel("KTU 2024 Scheme", icon, SwingConstants.CENTER); label.setBounds(50, 50, 400, 200); // Step 4: Position text BELOW the icon label.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER); label.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.BOTTOM); // Step 5: Add the label to the frame and display frame.add(label); frame.setVisible(true); } } ``` **Explanation of key lines:** - `setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.BOTTOM)`: This is the **critical line** that places the text below the icon. (2 marks) - `setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER)`: This horizontally centers the text under the icon. (1 mark) - The `ImageIcon` constructor requires a valid image file path. If the path is invalid, the icon will be `null` and only the text will appear. (1 mark) - `frame.setLayout(null)` allows absolute positioning; in real applications, `BorderLayout` or `FlowLayout` is preferred. (1 mark) - Remaining marks are for correct imports, JFrame setup, and `setVisible(true)`. (2 marks) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Internal Choice) **(a)** List and explain any four constructors of `JLabel` class. (7 marks) **(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a `JLabel` with the text "Result: PASS" in red color, bold font (Arial, 24pt), with a yellow background. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a): **1. `JLabel()`** (1.5 marks) This is the no-argument constructor. It creates an empty label with no text and no icon. It is useful when the label's content is to be determined at runtime. **2. `JLabel(String text)`** (1.5 marks) Creates a label initialized with the given string. The label displays the text in a single line. Example: `JLabel l = new JLabel("Hello");` **3. `JLabel(Icon image)`** (1.5 marks + 0.5 for example) Creates a label that displays an icon. The text is initially set to `null`. Example: ```java JLabel l = new JLabel(new ImageIcon("pic.jpg")); ``` **4. `JLabel(String text, Icon icon, int horizontalAlignment)`** (2 marks + example for full marks) The most complete constructor. Initializes both text and icon, and sets the horizontal alignment of the contents. Example: ```java JLabel l = new JLabel("Save", new ImageIcon("save.png"), SwingConstants.LEFT); ``` #### Model Solution for (b): ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; import javax.swing.SwingConstants; import java.awt.Color; import java.awt.Font; public class ResultLabel { public static void main(String[] args) { // Step 1: Create frame JFrame frame = new JFrame("Result Display"); frame.setSize(400, 200); frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); frame.setLayout(null); // Step 2: Create label with text JLabel label = new JLabel("Result: PASS", SwingConstants.CENTER); label.setBounds(50, 50, 300, 80); // Step 3: Set font to Arial Bold 24pt label.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 24)); // Step 4: Set text color to red label.setForeground(Color.RED); // Step 5: Set background to yellow (must enable opaque first) label.setOpaque(true); label.setBackground(Color.YELLOW); // Step 6: Add to frame and display frame.add(label); frame.setVisible(true); } } ``` **Mark Distribution:** - Correct imports: 1 mark - JFrame setup: 1 mark - `setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 24))`: 2 marks - `setForeground(Color.RED)`: 1 mark - `setOpaque(true)` + `setBackground(Color.YELLOW)`: 2 marks (These are **mandatory in sequence** — if `setOpaque(true)` is missing, the background color will not be visible, costing 1 mark.) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting `setOpaque(true)`:** Students often set the background color but forget this crucial line. Without it, the background remains transparent and the color change is invisible. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 2. **Using `setBackground` without `Color` import:** This causes a compilation error. Always include `import java.awt.Color;` and `import java.awt.Font;`. > 3. **Wrong alignment constant name:** Writing `SwingConstants.MIDDLE` (which is not a real constant) instead of `SwingConstants.CENTER`. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 4. **Forgetting `` prefix:** In HTML-formatted label questions, students often omit the `` tag, causing raw HTML to be displayed as text. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 5. **Confusing `setHorizontalAlignment` with `setHorizontalTextPosition`:** These are two completely different concepts. Misusing them in icon-text positioning questions costs **2-3 marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for JLabel (KTU 2024 Scheme):** - **Package:** `javax.swing` — Import this before using `JLabel`. - **Class Hierarchy:** `Object` → `Component` → `Container` → `JComponent` → `JLabel`. - **Interfaces Implemented:** `SwingConstants` (for alignment), `Accessible` (for accessibility). - **Type:** Passive, non-focusable, non-editable display component. - **Total Constructors:** 6 (memorize all six signatures). - **Default Alignment:** `LEADING` (which equals `LEFT` in left-to-right languages). - **Default Icon-Text Position:** Text appears to the **right** of the icon (i.e., icon on `LEFT`, text on `RIGHT`). - **Mandatory for Background Color:** Always call `setOpaque(true)` before `setBackground(color)`. - **HTML Support:** `JLabel` supports HTML 3.2 subset, **but** the string must start with ``. - **Event Generation:** `JLabel` does **not** generate `ActionEvent` like `JButton`. It is passive. - **Key Methods to Memorize:** `setText()`, `getText()`, `setIcon()`, `setHorizontalAlignment()`, `setHorizontalTextPosition()`, `setVerticalTextPosition()`, `setIconTextGap()`, `setOpaque()`, `setFont()`, `setForeground()`, `setBackground()`. - **Common Use Cases:** Form labels, status displays, splash screens, image captions, headings. - **Memory Tip:** "**L**abel = **L**ook, don't touch." It is a read-only display surface.

topics.title: The Swing Buttons
JTextField. Event Handling - Event Handling Mechanisms
Delegation Event Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Delegation Event Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** is the event-handling architecture used by Java AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) and Swing, in which the responsibility of processing an event generated by a GUI component is **delegated (handed over)** to a separate object known as an **Event Listener**, rather than being processed by the component's container or by subclassing the component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT535 / Module 4):** The Delegation Event Model is the modern, recommended Java approach for handling GUI events, replacing the older Java 1.0 *inheritance-based* event model. You **must** know the three pillars — **Source, Event, Listener** — and how they are wired together using `addXxxListener()` methods.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of the Model

| Pillar | Role | Real-World Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| **Event Source** | The GUI object (Button, TextField, etc.) that detects the user's action and **fires** the event. | A fire alarm sensor on a wall. |
| **Event Object** | An instance of a subclass of `java.util.EventObject` that **encapsulates** all information about what happened. | The bell signal that travels from the sensor. |
| **Event Listener** | An object that **receives** the event object and contains the business logic to handle it. It must implement the appropriate `XxxListener` interface. | The fire brigade that responds to the bell. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Subscription

Imagine you subscribe to a daily newspaper:

1. The **newspaper press** is the *Event Source* — it can produce news at any time, but it does not know who wants it.
2. The **newspaper itself** (the printed paper carrying today's headlines) is the *Event Object* — a self-contained packet of information.
3. **You** (the subscriber) are the *Event Listener* — you registered your address, and you decide what to do with the paper (read it, recycle it, etc.).

The press does not call you; the press just prints. The subscription *registration* is what wires the source to you. In Java, the registration is the line:
```java
button.addActionListener(handler);
```
This single line is the entire "subscription contract." Without it, the button is silent to the program.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** In the Delegation Event Model, *an event is fired only when a listener is registered*. If no listener is attached to a source, the event is generated by the JVM but has no recipient and is silently dropped.

### 1.4 Standard JDK Event Package Roots

- **Event classes** live in `java.awt.event` (and `javax.swing.event` for Swing).
- **Listener interfaces** also live in `java.awt.event`.
- **Adapter classes** (skeleton implementations) also live in `java.awt.event`.

The single common superclass of all AWT events is `java.awt.AWTEvent`, which itself extends `java.util.EventObject`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The Delegation Event Model executes in a strict, four-step sequence every time a user interacts with a component:

1. **Component Becomes the Source:** A GUI component (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`, `Choice`) is instantiated. By default, it carries no listener.
2. **Listener Registration:** The programmer calls a registration method of the form `source.addXxxListener(listenerObject)`. This call is stored in the component's internal listener list.
3. **Event Firing (Trigger):** The user interacts (clicks, types, moves the mouse). The JVM detects this and **constructs an Event Object** of the appropriate subclass (`ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, etc.) and invokes the `processEvent()` method of the source.
4. **Delegated Invocation:** The source iterates through its registered listener list and **invokes the relevant callback method** on each listener, passing the event object as the argument. The listener's overridden method body executes the handling logic.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Delegation

| Why Not Subclass? | Why Delegation is Better |
|---|---|
| Forces inheritance of heavyweight AWT components. | Listener can be **any** class — no inheritance required. |
| One container handles **all** child events (slow, monolithic). | Each component has its **own** listener list (modular, fast). |
| Cannot dynamically swap handlers at runtime. | Listeners can be added, removed, or replaced on the fly. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Events, Listeners, Adapters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the **bold** column headers. KTU exams frequently ask "which listener for which event" as a 3-mark question.

| **Event Class (Source Fires)** | **Listener Interface (Receiver)** | **Key Callback Method(s)** | **Adapter Class (Convenience)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | *(none needed — single method)* |
| `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` | *(none needed)* |
| `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent e)` | *(none needed)* |
| `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged(AdjustmentEvent e)` | *(none needed)* |
| `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped` | `KeyAdapter` |
| `MouseEvent` (clicks) | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased` | `MouseAdapter` |
| `MouseEvent` (motion) | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| `MouseEvent` (wheel) | `MouseWheelListener` | `mouseWheelMoved(MouseWheelEvent e)` | *(none)* |
| `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` | `FocusAdapter` |
| `ComponentEvent` | `ComponentListener` | `componentResized`, `componentMoved`, `componentShown`, `componentHidden` | `ComponentAdapter` |
| `ContainerEvent` | `ContainerListener` | `componentAdded`, `componentRemoved` | `ContainerAdapter` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing`, `windowOpened`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified` | `WindowAdapter` |

### 2.4 Critical Registration Method Patterns

Every GUI component (extending `java.awt.Component`) supports the pattern:

$$
\text{Source} \xrightarrow{\texttt{addXxxListener}(L)} \text{Internal Listener List}
$$

For example:
- `b.addActionListener(this);` — registers `this` object as the `ActionListener` for button `b`.
- `tf.addKeyListener(new MyKeyHandler());` — registers a separate handler object for keystrokes on `tf`.
- `f.addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter(){ public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e){ System.exit(0);} });` — **inline anonymous class** with adapter.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Delegation Event Model is the architectural foundation of virtually every modern UI toolkit — not just Java. The pattern appears as:
- **Observer Pattern** (Gang-of-Four Design Patterns catalogue).
- **Signal-Slot mechanism** in Qt (C++).
- **Event Emitters** in Node.js.
- **Publisher-Subscriber** in reactive systems (RxJava, Kafka).

Understanding DEM therefore gives a B.Tech student the conceptual vocabulary for **all** modern event-driven programming, from GUI apps to microservices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Program Walkthrough — A Working AWT DEM Example

The most common KTU 14-mark question asks you to **write a Java AWT program that demonstrates the Delegation Event Model**, typically with a button that updates a text field. We will derive such a program line-by-line.

#### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

> *"Write a Java AWT program that creates a `Frame` containing a `TextField` and a `Button` labeled 'Click'. Each click on the button should append a counter value to the text field. Use the Delegation Event Model."*  — typical KTU Part B (b) question.

#### 3.1.2 The Complete Program

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class DelegationDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {

    TextField tf;
    Button   b;
    int      count = 0;   // state shared between source and listener

    public DelegationDemo() {
        // ----- 1. Set up the GUI components -----
        tf = new TextField(20);
        tf.setBounds(50, 80, 200, 30);
        b  = new Button("Click");
        b.setBounds(100, 130, 80, 30);

        // ----- 2. Register the listener (DELEGATION HAPPENS HERE) -----
        b.addActionListener(this);   // <-- key DEM line

        add(tf); add(b);
        setSize(300, 250);
        setLayout(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    // ----- 3. Implement the listener callback -----
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        count++;
        tf.setText("Clicked " + count + " time(s)");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new DelegationDemo();
    }
}
```

#### 3.1.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation of the Logic

**Step 1 — Inherit `Frame` and Implement `ActionListener`.**
The class simultaneously extends `Frame` (so it can display itself as a window) and implements `ActionListener` (so it can *receive* button-click events). This is a legitimate design choice because Java allows a class to extend one superclass and implement many interfaces. The object `this` therefore plays two roles: *container* and *listener*.

**Step 2 — Construct the Components.**
A `TextField` of width 20 columns and a `Button` labeled "Click" are allocated. Absolute positioning is used (`setLayout(null)`) for simplicity — not recommended for production code.

**Step 3 — Wire the Source to the Listener.**
The single line

```java
b.addActionListener(this);
```

is the **heart of the Delegation Event Model**. The `Button` object `b` stores a reference to `this` inside its internal listener vector. From this moment, the button "knows" that whenever it is clicked, it must call `this.actionPerformed(...)`.

**Step 4 — Trigger Event Generation (Runtime).**
When the user clicks the button:
- The AWT event-dispatch thread captures the click coordinates.
- The JVM constructs an `ActionEvent` object: `new ActionEvent(b, ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED, "Click")`.
- The button's `processEvent()` method is invoked internally.
- The button iterates over its listener list and calls `listener.actionPerformed(event)` for each registered listener.
- Our `actionPerformed` method executes, increments `count`, and updates the text field.

**Step 5 — State Persistence.**
Notice that `count` is an **instance field** of the class. Because the listener and the GUI live in the same object (`this`), they share this state without any need for global variables or static fields.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Sub-Question Patterns

| Sub-Question Type | What Examiners Expect | Mark Allocation |
|---|---|---|
| "List the components of the Delegation Event Model." | Source + Event + Listener (3 points) | **3 marks** |
| "Differentiate between delegation model and inheritance model." | Tabular comparison (5–6 points) | **7 marks** |
| "Write a program to handle a button click using DEM." | Code with `addActionListener` and `actionPerformed` | **7 marks** |
| "Explain the role of adapter classes." | Mention all 7 abstract methods of `WindowListener`; show `WindowAdapter` solving the verbosity problem | **7 marks** |

### 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough — Using an Adapter Class

Suppose we want to handle **only** `windowClosing` on a `Frame`. The `WindowListener` interface declares **seven** methods. If we implement it directly, we must provide empty bodies for the other six. The `WindowAdapter` class provides those empty bodies for us:

$$
\text{WindowListener (7 methods)} \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{default empty implementations}}\;\; \text{WindowAdapter (concrete)}
$$

```java
f.addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
    @Override
    public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
        f.dispose();          // close the window
        System.exit(0);       // terminate the JVM
    }
});
```

The anonymous inner class extends `WindowAdapter`, overriding **only** `windowClosing`. The remaining six methods inherit the empty default implementations — no compile errors, no boilerplate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Adapters Cannot Exist for All Listeners:** Adapter classes exist *only* for interfaces with **more than one method**. For `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, `TextListener`, and `AdjustmentListener` (all single-method interfaces), there is no adapter — implementing the interface directly is the idiomatic choice.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The DEM Triangle — Event Flow Architecture

The following block diagram captures the *functional architecture* of the Delegation Event Model. It is intentionally simplified for KTU board-exam reproduction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user["USER ACTION"] --> source["EVENT SOURCE\nButton, TextField, Choice"]
    source -- "1. addXxxListener(L)" --> reg["REGISTRATION TABLE\ninside the source object"]
    reg -. "stores reference" .-> source
    source -- "2. user triggers event" --> evt["EVENT OBJECT\nActionEvent, MouseEvent, KeyEvent"]
    evt -- "3. dispatched to listener" --> listener["EVENT LISTENER\nimplements XxxListener interface"]
    listener -- "4. callback method executes" --> handler["HANDLER LOGIC\nupdate UI, write file, etc."]
    handler --> source
```

### 4.2 Event Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class EventObject {
        +Object source
        +getSource()
    }
    class AWTEvent {
        +int id
        +consume()
    }
    class ActionEvent {
        +String actionCommand
    }
    class ItemEvent {
        +Object item
        +int stateChange
    }
    class TextEvent {
    }
    class ComponentEvent {
    }
    class ContainerEvent {
    }
    class FocusEvent {
    }
    class WindowEvent {
    }
    class InputEvent {
    }
    class KeyEvent {
        +char keyChar
        +int keyCode
    }
    class MouseEvent {
        +int x, y
        +int clickCount
    }
    EventObject <|-- AWTEvent
    AWTEvent <|-- ActionEvent
    AWTEvent <|-- ItemEvent
    AWTEvent <|-- TextEvent
    AWTEvent <|-- ComponentEvent
    AWTEvent <|-- InputEvent
    ComponentEvent <|-- ContainerEvent
    ComponentEvent <|-- FocusEvent
    ComponentEvent <|-- WindowEvent
    InputEvent <|-- KeyEvent
    InputEvent <|-- MouseEvent
```

### 4.3 Source-to-Listener Wiring Pattern (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph s1["GUI COMPONENT (Source)"]
        s1a["public void addActionListener(ActionListener l)"]
        s1b["Vector listeners"]
        s1a --> s1b
    end
    subgraph s2["LISTENER OBJECT"]
        s2a["class MyHandler implements ActionListener"]
        s2b["public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)"]
        s2a --> s2b
    end
    s1b -- "holds reference to" --> s2a
    s2b -- "invoked by source on click" --> s2b
```

### 4.4 The Seven WindowListener Methods (Adapter Necessity)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WindowListener Interface\n(7 abstract methods)"] --> B["Direct Implementation\nmust write all 7"]
    A --> C["WindowAdapter Class\ndefault empty bodies for all 7"]
    C --> D["Anonymous Subclass\noverride only windowClosing()"]
    B --> E["Verbose Code"]
    D --> F["Concise Code"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Explain the three components of the Java Delegation Event Model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Delegation Event Model has three components:
1. **Event Source** *(1 mark)* — A GUI component (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`) that generates or fires an event in response to user interaction.
2. **Event Object** *(1 mark)* — An instance of a subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`) that encapsulates the details of the event.
3. **Event Listener** *(1 mark)* — An object that implements an `XxxListener` interface and is registered with the source via `addXxxListener()`; it receives the event and handles it.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of adapter classes in the Delegation Event Model? Give one example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Adapter classes provide **default empty implementations** of listener interfaces that declare multiple methods *(1 mark)*. They eliminate the need to write empty bodies for methods we are not interested in *(1 mark)*.
**Example** *(1 mark)*: `WindowAdapter` implements all seven methods of `WindowListener` with empty bodies; we extend `WindowAdapter` and override only `windowClosing()` to close the window.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Instructions (as per KTU ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supposed]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the Java Delegation Event Model. Mention the role of the `java.awt.event` package. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java AWT program that creates a `Frame` containing a `TextField` and two buttons labeled **"Upper"** and **"Lower"**. Clicking "Upper" should convert the text in the text field to uppercase, and clicking "Lower" should convert it to lowercase. Use the Delegation Event Model. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Working of the Delegation Event Model (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
The Delegation Event Model is a modern Java event-handling mechanism in which an event generated by a source component is **delegated** to one or more listener objects that have been previously registered with the source.

**Step 2 — Sequence of Operations (3 marks):**
1. The GUI component (e.g., `Button`) is instantiated — it becomes the **Event Source**.
2. A listener object (implementing the relevant `XxxListener` interface) is created and registered using `source.addXxxListener(listener)`.
3. When the user interacts with the source, the JVM constructs an **Event Object** of the appropriate class and passes it to `processEvent()` on the source.
4. The source invokes the corresponding callback method (`actionPerformed`, `mouseClicked`, etc.) on each registered listener.
5. The listener's method body executes the business logic.

**Step 3 — Role of `java.awt.event` (2 marks):**
This package contains all the **event classes** (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`), all the **listener interfaces** (e.g., `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`), and all the **adapter classes** (e.g., `MouseAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`). It is the standard library for AWT-based event handling.

---

**(b) Java Program — Upper/Lower Case Conversion (7 marks)**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CaseConverter extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    TextField tf;
    Button   bUpper, bLower;

    public CaseConverter() {
        tf     = new TextField(20);
        bUpper = new Button("Upper");
        bLower = new Button("Lower");

        tf.setBounds(50, 60, 200, 30);
        bUpper.setBounds(60, 110, 70, 30);
        bLower.setBounds(160, 110, 70, 30);

        // ----- Delegation Event Model Registration -----
        bUpper.addActionListener(this);
        bLower.addActionListener(this);

        add(tf); add(bUpper); add(bLower);
        setSize(300, 250);
        setLayout(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String text = tf.getText();
        if (e.getSource() == bUpper) {                  // identify which button was clicked
            tf.setText(text.toUpperCase());
        } else if (e.getSource() == bLower) {
            tf.setText(text.toLowerCase());
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CaseConverter();
    }
}
```

**Key Explanations (to be written in the exam):**
- `extends Frame` allows the class to act as the top-level window.
- `implements ActionListener` lets the same object handle clicks on both buttons.
- `bUpper.addActionListener(this)` and `bLower.addActionListener(this)` are the two **delegation registration lines**.
- Inside `actionPerformed`, `e.getSource()` is used to **distinguish** which button was clicked, because the same callback method handles both.
- `String.toUpperCase()` and `String.toLowerCase()` are standard `java.lang.String` methods (no imports needed).

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supposed Alternate]**

**(a)** Differentiate between the **Delegation Event Model** and the **Inheritance Event Model** of Java. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java AWT program to handle the **window-closing** event of a `Frame` such that clicking the close button exits the program. Use an **adapter class**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Delegation vs. Inheritance Event Model (7 marks)**

| Feature *(1 mark each)* | **Delegation Model** (Java 1.1+) | **Inheritance Model** (Java 1.0) |
|---|---|---|
| Handling mechanism | Event is delegated to a **listener** object. | Container's `handleEvent()` is overridden. |
| Inheritance | Listener can be **any** class; no subclassing of components. | Must **subclass** the AWT component (e.g., `MyButton extends Button`). |
| Multiple events | One component can have **multiple** distinct listeners. | All events funnel into a **single** `handleEvent` method with a giant `switch` block. |
| Modularity | High — handlers can be added/removed dynamically. | Low — handler logic is hard-wired into the subclass. |
| Type safety | Strong — each listener interface enforces a specific callback signature. | Weak — `handleEvent` takes a generic `Event` argument. |
| Recommended for new code? | **Yes** — current Java standard. | **No** — deprecated since Java 1.1. |
| Example registration | `b.addActionListener(handler);` | Override `handleEvent(Event e)` in `MyButton` subclass. |

*(Award full 7 marks: 1 mark per distinct row, up to 7 rows.)*

---

**(b) Java Program — Window Closing with Adapter (7 marks)**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class WindowCloseDemo extends Frame {

    public WindowCloseDemo() {
        setSize(400, 300);
        setTitle("Window Close Demo");
        setVisible(true);

        // ----- Delegation Model + WindowAdapter -----
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                dispose();           // close this frame
                System.exit(0);      // terminate the JVM
            }
        });
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new WindowCloseDemo();
    }
}
```

**Key Explanations:**
- `addWindowListener(...)` takes a `WindowListener` reference.
- We pass an **anonymous subclass of `WindowAdapter`**, which satisfies the interface contract while supplying empty implementations for the other six methods.
- We override **only** `windowClosing` — the method that fires when the user clicks the close (×) button.
- Without this, clicking × would merely hide the window; the JVM would keep running.
- `dispose()` releases the window's native resources, and `System.exit(0)` terminates the application cleanly with exit code 0.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting `addXxxListener(...)` registration (–2 marks):** Writing a class that implements `ActionListener` but never calling `b.addActionListener(this)` is the most common reason programs "do nothing." Always include the registration line in your answer.
> 2. **Confusing `getSource()` with `getActionCommand()` (–1 mark):** `getSource()` returns the *object reference* (use `==` to compare); `getActionCommand()` returns the *button label string* (use `.equals(...)`). Mixing them up causes subtle bugs.
> 3. **Not overriding `setSize` and `setVisible` (–1 mark):** Without these two calls, a `Frame` is invisible on the screen. Examiners will deduct marks if the program has no GUI output.
> 4. **Implementing a multi-method interface directly instead of using an adapter (–1 mark):** When asked to use an *adapter class*, do not write `implements WindowListener` with six empty methods — you have missed the point of the question.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DEM = Source + Event + Listener** *(the three pillars — must be memorized verbatim).*
- **Registration is mandatory** — `addXxxListener(L)` is the one line that wires the model together.
- **Events extend `AWTEvent`, which extends `EventObject`** — the two-level inheritance is a frequent KTU diagram question.
- **Listener interfaces are in `java.awt.event`** — name the package in exam answers for full credit.
- **Single-method listeners** (`ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, `TextListener`, `AdjustmentListener`) **have no adapter class.**
- **Multi-method listeners** (`MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`, `FocusListener`, `ComponentListener`, `ContainerListener`, `MouseMotionListener`) **each have a corresponding `XxxAdapter` class.**
- **Adapters** provide empty default implementations; we override only the methods we care about.
- **The same object can be both a Frame and a Listener** — by extending `Frame` and implementing an `XxxListener` interface simultaneously.
- **`e.getSource()`** returns the originating component as an `Object`; use `==` to compare references.
- **Delegation Model supersedes the Inheritance Model** (Java 1.0's `handleEvent` approach) — state this in any comparison answer.
- **Always call `setSize()` and `setVisible(true)`** on a `Frame`, or it will not appear.
- **Remember the two methods for window shutdown:** `dispose()` releases the window, `System.exit(0)` terminates the JVM.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In Java's **Delegation Event Model**, an **Event Class** is a class that encapsulates the information describing a *state change* occurring on a GUI source object (such as a button being clicked, a key being pressed, or a window being resized). Every event in AWT/Swing is represented as an object whose class belongs to the `java.awt.event` package (or its sub-packages), and every such class ultimately inherits from `java.util.EventObject`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Event classes are the *data carriers* in the Delegation Event Model. The **source object** (e.g., `JButton`) creates the event object, and the **listener object** (which must implement the appropriate `XxxListener` interface) consumes it. Without event classes, the listener would have no information about *what happened* and *where it happened*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Doorbell Analogy"

Imagine a **doorbell system** in your home:

- The **doorbell button** is the **source** (like a `JButton`).
- The **act of pressing it** is the **event** (an `ActionEvent` object).
- The **bell that rings** is the **listener** (an `ActionListener` object).
- The **electrical wire** carrying the signal is the **event dispatch mechanism** (handled by the JVM).

Just as the ringing bell does not know *who* pressed the doorbell, a listener method receives the event object and can ask it questions like *"Where did this happen?"* (`getSource()`) or *"Which key was typed?"* (`getKeyChar()`). The **Event Class is the "information packet"** that travels from source to listener.

## 1.3 The Two Broad Categories

| Category | Description | Common Event Classes |
|----------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Semantic Events** | Represent the *meaning* of a user interaction (high-level). | `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `TextEvent`, `AdjustmentEvent` |
| **Low-Level Events** | Represent *direct, physical* input actions. | `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `FocusEvent`, `WindowEvent`, `ComponentEvent`, `ContainerEvent`, `PaintEvent` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for recall:** A semantic event is the *"what"* (a click happened), while a low-level event is the *"how, when, and where"* (a mouse button was pressed at coordinates 100,200 at 14:32:05).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Root of the Hierarchy

The class hierarchy begins with `java.util.EventObject`, which has **one** key method:

$$ \text{Object getSource()} \rightarrow \text{returns the object on which the event initially occurred} $$

The AWT-specific root is `java.awt.AWTEvent`, which extends `EventObject` and adds:

$$ \text{int getID()} \rightarrow \text{returns the event-type identifier (e.g., 401 for ACTION\_PERFORMED)} $$

Every concrete event class is uniquely identified by an **integer constant** that classifies the type of the event.

## 2.2 Classification Logic

The Delegation Event Model works on three components:

1. **Source Object** – generates events (must register listeners).
2. **Event Object** – instance of an event class (the topic of this module).
3. **Listener Object** – implements a `XxxListener` interface and handles events.

The event object is *immutable* (its state cannot be changed after construction), ensuring that listeners receive a **trustworthy, snapshot** of what happened.

## 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet: Event Classes

| Event Class | Constant (getID value) | Triggered When | Listener Interface |
|-------------|------------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| `ActionEvent` | `ACTION_PERFORMED` (**1001**) | Button click, menu selection, Enter in text field | `ActionListener` |
| `ItemEvent` | `ITEM_STATE_CHANGED` (**701**) | Checkbox toggle, list selection, choice pick | `ItemListener` |
| `TextEvent` | `TEXT_VALUE_CHANGED` (**900**) | Text in a `TextField`/`TextArea` changes | `TextListener` |
| `AdjustmentEvent` | `ADJUSTMENT_VALUE_CHANGED` (**601**) | Scrollbar movement | `AdjustmentListener` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_PRESSED`, `MOUSE_RELEASED`, `MOUSE_CLICKED`, `MOUSE_ENTERED`, `MOUSE_EXITED`, `MOUSE_MOVED`, `MOUSE_DRAGGED` (**500-507**) | Mouse activity | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` |
| `KeyEvent` | `KEY_PRESSED`, `KEY_RELEASED`, `KEY_TYPED` (**401-403**) | Keyboard activity | `KeyListener` |
| `FocusEvent` | `FOCUS_GAINED`, `FOCUS_LOST` (**1004-1005**) | Component gains/loses keyboard focus | `FocusListener` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WINDOW_OPENED`, `WINDOW_CLOSING`, `WINDOW_CLOSED`, `WINDOW_ICONIFIED`, `WINDOW_DEICONIFIED`, `WINDOW_ACTIVATED`, `WINDOW_DEACTIVATED` (**200-206**) | Window state changes | `WindowListener` |
| `ComponentEvent` | `COMPONENT_MOVED`, `COMPONENT_RESIZED`, `COMPONENT_SHOWN`, `COMPONENT_HIDDEN` (**100-103**) | Component moves/resizes/shown/hidden | `ComponentListener` |
| `ContainerEvent` | `COMPONENT_ADDED`, `COMPONENT_REMOVED` (**300-301**) | Component added/removed from container | `ContainerListener` |
| `PaintEvent` | `PAINT`, `UPDATE` (**800-801**) | Component needs repainting (internal) | `PaintListener` (package-private) |

## 2.4 Universal Inherited Methods (available on *every* event object)

| Method Signature | Return Type | Purpose |
|------------------|-------------|---------|
| `getSource()` | `Object` | Returns the component that originated the event |
| `getID()` | `int` | Returns the event-type identifier |
| `paramString()` | `String` | (protected) Returns a human-readable description – useful in `toString()` debugging |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Event classes form the **foundation of GUI-based software engineering** in Java. From enterprise banking dashboards to embedded touch-screen controllers, every interactive application uses this model. The **separation of concerns** (source, event, listener) enables the **Observer Design Pattern** and supports **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** architectures – a topic directly tested in KTU's Design Patterns module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The same Delegation Event Model powers JavaFX (`javafx.event.Event`), the modern successor to AWT/Swing. Mastering AWT event classes gives you transferable knowledge for any Java GUI framework.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Class Hierarchy Analysis

## 3.1 The Full Inheritance Tree

Let us trace the derivation chain from the most general to the most specific:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{java.lang.Object} \\
&\quad \downarrow \text{ extends} \\
&\texttt{java.util.EventObject} \quad \text{(root of all event classes in Java)} \\
&\quad \downarrow \text{ extends} \\
&\texttt{java.awt.AWTEvent} \quad \text{(root of AWT/Swing GUI events)} \\
&\quad \downarrow \text{ extends} \\
&\text{Four major branches emerge here:}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Branch 1 — Semantic Event Branch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{AWTEvent} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\texttt{ActionEvent}, \ \texttt{ItemEvent}, \ \texttt{TextEvent}, \ \texttt{AdjustmentEvent}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Branch 2 — Input Event Branch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{AWTEvent} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\texttt{InputEvent} \quad \text{(abstract — adds modifiers like Shift, Ctrl)} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\texttt{MouseEvent}, \ \texttt{KeyEvent}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Branch 3 — Component Event Branch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{AWTEvent} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\texttt{ComponentEvent} \quad \text{(abstract)} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\texttt{FocusEvent}, \ \texttt{WindowEvent}, \ \texttt{PaintEvent} \\
&\quad \text{(also) } \texttt{ContainerEvent}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Constructor Analysis of Key Event Classes

### 3.2.1 `ActionEvent`

The derivation of its constructor signature:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{public ActionEvent(Object source, int id, String command)} \\
&\quad \text{where } \texttt{source} = \text{the triggering component} \\
&\quad \text{where } \texttt{id} = \texttt{ActionEvent.ACTION\_PERFORMED} \\
&\quad \text{where } \texttt{command} = \text{action command string (e.g., "Save")}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Methods derived from this construction:**

| Method | Returns | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `getActionCommand()` | `String` | Returns the command string set on the source |
| `getWhen()` | `long` | Returns the event timestamp in milliseconds |

### 3.2.2 `MouseEvent`

MouseEvent inherits from `InputEvent`, which adds modifier tracking:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{public MouseEvent(Component source, int id, long when,} \\
&\quad \texttt{int modifiers, int x, int y, int clickCount,} \\
&\quad \texttt{boolean popupTrigger, int button)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key derived methods (with their derivation logic):**

| Method | Return | Logic |
|--------|--------|-------|
| `getX()` | `int` | X-coordinate of mouse pointer at event time |
| `getY()` | `int` | Y-coordinate of mouse pointer at event time |
| `getPoint()` | `Point` | Returns $\text{Point}(x, y)$ |
| `getClickCount()` | `int` | 1 = single-click, 2 = double-click, etc. |
| `isPopupTrigger()` | `boolean` | $\text{true}$ if this click is a platform-specific popup-menu trigger |
| `getButton()` | `int` | Returns $\texttt{NOBUTTON}$, $\texttt{BUTTON1}$, $\texttt{BUTTON2}$, or $\texttt{BUTTON3}$ |
| `getModifiersEx()` | `int` | Extended modifier mask (Shift, Ctrl, Alt, Meta, button states) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation of modifier logic:** `InputEvent` stores modifiers as a bitmask. For example, `Shift` corresponds to bit `1`, `Ctrl` to bit `2`, `Alt` to bit `3`. `getModifiers()` performs a **bitwise AND** (`&`) between the stored mask and a constant like `InputEvent.SHIFT_MASK` to determine whether a particular modifier was active.

### 3.2.3 `KeyEvent`

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{public KeyEvent(Component source, int id, long when,} \\
&\quad \texttt{int modifiers, int keyCode, char keyChar,} \\
&\quad \texttt{int keyLocation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key derived methods:**

| Method | Return | Purpose |
|--------|--------|---------|
| `getKeyChar()` | `char` | The character *typed* (e.g., 'A', '7', '@') — meaningful only for `KEY_TYPED` |
| `getKeyCode()` | `int` | The virtual key code (e.g., `VK_ENTER`, `VK_F1`, `VK_LEFT`) — meaningful for `KEY_PRESSED`/`KEY_RELEASED` |
| `isActionKey()` | `boolean` | $\text{true}$ if the key is an action key (F1–F12, arrow keys, etc.) that does not produce a character |
| `getKeyLocation()` | `int` | `KEY_LOCATION_LEFT`, `KEY_LOCATION_RIGHT`, `KEY_LOCATION_NUMPAD`, `KEY_LOCATION_STANDARD`, or `KEY_LOCATION_UNKNOWN` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Derivation Distinction (highly tested in KTU):**
> - `KEY_TYPED` events: `getKeyChar()` is meaningful; `getKeyCode()` returns `VK_UNDEFINED`.
> - `KEY_PRESSED` / `KEY_RELEASED` events: `getKeyCode()` is meaningful; `getKeyChar()` returns `CHAR_UNDEFINED`.

### 3.2.4 `WindowEvent`

$$
\texttt{public WindowEvent(Window source, int id, Window opposite, int oldState, int newState)}
$$

| Method | Returns | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `getWindow()` | `Window` | Returns the originating window (overrides `getSource()` result) |
| `getOppositeWindow()` | `Window` | Returns the other window in a focus/activation event |
| `getOldState()` | `int` | Previous window state (`NORMAL`, `ICONIFIED`, `MAXIMIZED_HORIZ`, `MAXIMIZED_VERT`, `MAXIMIZED_BOTH`) |
| `getNewState()` | `int` | New window state |

### 3.2.5 `ItemEvent`

$$
\texttt{public ItemEvent(ItemSelectable source, int id, Object item, int stateChange)}
$$

| Method | Returns | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `getItem()` | `Object` | The item affected by the selection change |
| `getItemSelectable()` | `ItemSelectable` | Returns the source (typed) |
| `getStateChange()` | `int` | `SELECTED` or `DESELECTED` |

## 3.3 Analytical Summary: The "Is-A" Relationships

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Every GUI event object} \ \textbf{IS-A} \ \texttt{AWTEvent} \ \textbf{IS-A} \ \texttt{EventObject} \ \textbf{IS-A} \ \texttt{Object} \\
&\text{Every mouse/key event} \ \textbf{IS-A} \ \texttt{InputEvent} \ (\text{adds modifier tracking}) \\
&\text{Every component-related event} \ \textbf{IS-A} \ \texttt{ComponentEvent} \ (\text{adds getComponent()})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** `InputEvent` and `ComponentEvent` are **abstract** classes — they are never instantiated directly, only through their concrete subclasses.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an Event Class. List any two important event classes from the `java.awt.event` package along with the type of event they represent.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Event Class** is a class in Java's AWT event-handling framework that encapsulates the state change occurring on a GUI source object. Every event class inherits from `java.util.EventObject` and represents a specific type of user or system action.

Two important event classes:

1. **`ActionEvent`** — Represents a semantic action like a button click, menu item selection, or pressing Enter in a text field. It carries the **action command string** and **timestamp**.

2. **`MouseEvent`** — Represents a low-level mouse action such as press, release, click, enter, exit, move, or drag. It carries **coordinates** `(x, y)`, **click count**, and **modifier key** information.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each correct class with description.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the purpose of the `getSource()` and `getID()` methods in event objects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`getSource()`** — Defined in the root class `java.util.EventObject`, this method returns a reference to the **object on which the event initially occurred** (e.g., the `JButton` that was clicked). It allows the listener to identify *which* component triggered the event, especially when the same listener handles multiple sources.

- **`getID()`** — Defined in `java.awt.AWTEvent`, this method returns an **integer event-type identifier** that specifies the *exact* kind of event (e.g., `ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED = 1001`, `MouseEvent.MOUSE_PRESSED = 501`). This is essential for listeners that handle multiple event subtypes in a single callback.

*(Award 1.5 marks for each method with proper justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly lose **2 marks** by confusing `KeyEvent.getKeyChar()` and `KeyEvent.getKeyCode()`. Remember: `KEY_TYPED` uses `getKeyChar()`; `KEY_PRESSED`/`KEY_RELEASED` use `getKeyCode()`.
> - Do **not** confuse `MouseEvent.getModifiers()` (down-mask) with `MouseEvent.getModifiersEx()` (extended mask). Use `getModifiersEx()` for modern code.
> - Always mention the **parent class** (`EventObject` and `AWTEvent`) when defining an event class — omitting the inheritance chain costs 1 mark.
> - For 14-mark questions, **sketching the class hierarchy** earns 2–3 easy marks. Never skip it.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the complete class hierarchy of AWT event classes starting from `java.util.EventObject` and explain any **four** semantic and **four** low-level event classes with their triggering conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat code sketch, demonstrate how a `MouseListener` and a `KeyListener` are registered on a `TextField` and explain the role of the corresponding event objects inside the listener methods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) (7 marks):**

**Class Hierarchy (3 marks):**

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.util.EventObject
         └── java.awt.AWTEvent
                ├── ActionEvent
                ├── ItemEvent
                ├── TextEvent
                ├── AdjustmentEvent
                ├── InputEvent (abstract)
                │      ├── MouseEvent
                │      └── KeyEvent
```
