| Non-Functional | NFR-03 | Data must persist in a database (MySQL) | High |
| Non-Functional | NFR-04 | Role-based access (Librarian vs. Member) | Medium |

**Mark Distribution:** Problem Statement (2M) + 5W1H (2M) + SRS Table (3M) = 7M

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **Q. (a) Explain the different Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) models. Compare Waterfall and Iterative models. (7 Marks)**
> 
> **Q. (b) Describe the importance of "Testing" and "Documentation" steps in OOP software development with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Q(a): SDLC Models**

**Common SDLC Models:**

1. **Waterfall Model** — Linear, sequential. Each phase must be completed before the next. Best for small, well-understood projects. *(Suitability: SRS, rigid projects)*.
2. **Iterative Model** — Repeats cycles of design → code → test in iterations. Each iteration adds features. *(Suitability: Evolving requirements)*.
3. **Spiral Model** — Combines iterative + risk analysis. Focus on prototypes and risk management. *(Suitability: High-risk, large projects)*.
4. **Agile Model** — Iterative + incremental, with short sprints (2–4 weeks). Emphasizes customer collaboration. *(Suitability: Dynamic requirements, startups)*.
5. **V-Model** — Extension of Waterfall where testing is planned parallel to development. *(Suitability: Safety-critical systems)*.

**Comparison: Waterfall vs. Iterative**

| Parameter | Waterfall Model | Iterative Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Linear, sequential | Cyclical, repeated |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid — changes are costly | Flexible — changes welcomed per iteration |
| **Customer Involvement** | Only at start and end | Continuous, every iteration |
| **Risk Handling** | High risk (late discovery) | Low risk (early feedback) |
| **Delivery** | Single delivery at end | Incremental deliveries |
| **Cost of Change** | Very high after requirements | Low — changes absorbed in next iteration |
| **Best Suited For** | Small, well-defined projects | Large, evolving projects |
| **Testing** | Only after coding complete | Continuous testing in every iteration |
| **Example Use Case** | Banking core systems, government projects | E-commerce, mobile apps, SaaS products |

**Mark Distribution:** 5 models named (2M) + Comparison table (5M) = 7M

---

#### **Solution for Q(b): Importance of Testing & Documentation**

**Testing (Step 6) — Importance:**

Testing is the **quality gate** of software development. It ensures correctness, reliability, and user satisfaction.

- **Example:** Consider a banking application with a `withdraw(amount)` method.
  - **Unit Test:** Does it correctly deduct `amount` from balance when balance ≥ amount? ✅
  - **Edge Case Test:** Does it reject withdrawal when balance < amount? ✅
  - **Integration Test:** Does it correctly update the database and log the transaction? ✅
  - **Acceptance Test:** Does the user see the updated balance within 2 seconds (NFR)? ✅
- **Without testing:** Software crashes, money is lost, users lose trust, legal issues arise.
- **Cost Insight:** Fixing a defect at design costs **1x**, at coding costs **5x**, at testing costs **10x**, and post-release costs **100x**.

---

**Documentation (Step 7) — Importance:**

Documentation is the **knowledge backbone** of any software project.

- **Types:**
  - **In-code comments:** Help developers understand logic.
  - **API documentation (Javadoc):** Auto-generated, helps integrate modules.
  - **User manual:** Helps end-users operate the software.
  - **Design document:** Helps new developers understand architecture.
- **Example:** A `Student` class with proper Javadoc:

```java
/**
 * Represents a student entity in the system.
 * @author KTU Student
 * @version 1.0
 */
public class Student {
    private int rollNo;
    private String name;
    /**
     * Gets the roll number.
     * @return the unique roll number.
     */
    public int getRollNo() { return rollNo; }
}
```

- **Without documentation:** New developers take **weeks** to understand existing code. Maintenance becomes a nightmare. Knowledge is lost when developers leave.

**Mark Distribution:** Testing importance + example (3.5M) + Documentation importance + example (3.5M) = 7M

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on "Steps" Questions:**
> 
> 🔴 **Mistake 1:** Listing steps in the wrong order. Always remember: **Problem → Requirements → Design → Algorithm → Code → Test → Document → Maintain**.
> 
> 🔴 **Mistake 2:** Confusing "Software Development Steps" with "OOP Concepts" (Encapsulation, Inheritance, etc.). They are *different*. Steps = *process*; OOP concepts = *techniques used within a step*.
> 
> 🔴 **Mistake 3:** Writing only the *name* of each step without explaining the *output/artifact* it produces. Examiners specifically look for the **output document** of each step (e.g., "Step 2 produces SRS Document").
> 
> 🔴 **Mistake 4:** Skipping the **diagram** in 7-mark questions. A **flowchart or block diagram is mandatory** and carries **1–2 marks** by itself.
> 
> 🔴 **Mistake 5:** Writing vague answers like "Testing is important" — instead, **give a concrete example** (e.g., "Testing the `withdraw()` method of a BankAccount class").
> 
> 🔴 **Mistake 6:** Forgetting to mention **iteration/feedback loops** — real-world software development is **iterative**, not strictly linear. Mentioning this shows advanced understanding and fetches extra marks.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 — Module 4 "Steps" — Last-Minute Revision Bullets**

- ✅ The **8 Steps** of OOP software development are: **Problem Definition → Requirements Analysis → System Design → Algorithm Design → Coding → Testing → Documentation → Maintenance**.

- ✅ **Step 1 Output:** Problem Statement Document.
- ✅ **Step 2 Output:** SRS Document (IEEE 830 Standard).
- ✅ **Step 3 Output:** UML Diagrams (Class, Object, Sequence, Use Case) + Class Blueprint.
- ✅ **Step 4 Output:** Pseudocode, Flowchart, Big-O Complexity Analysis.
- ✅ **Step 5 Output:** Source Code (`.java`/`.cpp`/`.py`) with OOP pillars applied.
- ✅ **Step 6 Output:** Test Reports, Bug Logs, Fixed Code. Target: **≥ 80% code coverage**.
- ✅ **Step 7 Output:** User Manuals, API Docs, Javadoc, README.
- ✅ **Step 8 Output:** Patches, Updates, New Releases. Types: **Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive**.

- ✅ **SDLC Models to know:** Waterfall, Iterative, Spiral, Agile, V-Model.
- ✅ **Agile** uses **sprints** (2–4 weeks). **Waterfall** is **linear**.
- ✅ **SOLID Principles** are applied in **Step 3 (System Design)**.
- ✅ **Big-O Notation** is used in **Step 4 (Algorithm Design)**.
- ✅ **Git/Version Control** is used in **Step 5 (Coding)**.
- ✅ **JUnit/pytest** are used in **Step 6 (Testing)**.
- ✅ **Javadoc/Doxygen** are used in **Step 7 (Documentation)**.
- ✅ Maintenance consumes **60–80%** of total software lifecycle cost.
- ✅ **Cost of bug fix** increases **100x** from design → post-release.
- ✅ The process is **iterative** — feedback loops exist between testing, maintenance, and earlier steps.
- ✅ Always draw a **flowchart or block diagram** for full marks in 7-mark questions.

> 💡 **Pro Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked to "explain the steps", structure your answer as: **(1) Numbered list of all 8 steps (2 marks), (2) Brief explanation of each (3 marks), (3) Diagram (2 marks)**. This guarantees a **well-organized 7-mark answer** every time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common JDBC Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Common JDBC Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)** is a Java API (Application Programming Interface) defined in the `java.sql` and `javax.sql` packages that enables Java applications to interact with relational databases in a database-independent manner. It provides a standardized set of classes and interfaces for executing SQL statements, retrieving results, and managing database connections.

**Common JDBC Components** are the foundational building blocks (classes and interfaces) of the JDBC API that work together to establish communication between a Java program and a DBMS (Database Management System). The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies the following primary components:

1. **DriverManager** (Class) – `java.sql.DriverManager`
2. **Connection** (Interface) – `java.sql.Connection`
3. **Statement** (Interface) – `java.sql.Statement`
4. **PreparedStatement** (Interface) – `java.sql.PreparedStatement`
5. **CallableStatement** (Interface) – `java.sql.CallableStatement`
6. **ResultSet** (Interface) – `java.sql.ResultSet`
7. **ResultSetMetaData** (Interface) – `java.sql.ResultSetMetaData`
8. **DatabaseMetaData** (Interface) – `java.sql.DatabaseMetaData`
9. **SQLException** (Class) – `java.sql.SQLException`
10. **DataSource** (Interface) – `javax.sql.DataSource` (for connection pooling in modern JDBC)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Telephone Call System"**

Imagine you want to call a friend in another country:

| Real-World Action | JDBC Component |
|---|---|
| **Phone Directory / Operator** that connects you to the right network | **`DriverManager`** |
| **Active Phone Line** between you and your friend | **`Connection`** |
| **Spoken sentences** (the actual message/question) | **`Statement` / `PreparedStatement`** |
| **Friend's reply / answer** | **`ResultSet`** |
| **Language Translator** (in case of stored procedures) | **`CallableStatement`** |
| **Information about the call** (duration, quality) | **`DatabaseMetaData`** |
| **Problems during the call** (network issues) | **`SQLException`** |

Just as you need a working phone line to talk, a Java program needs an active **`Connection`** to send **`SQL statements`** and receive **`ResultSets`**. The **`DriverManager`** acts like a telephone operator that sets up that line for you by loading the right database **`Driver`**.

## 1.3 The JDBC Architecture – Quick Snapshot

The JDBC API follows a **2-tier or 3-tier architecture** with a **Driver Manager layer**, a **JDBC API layer**, and a **JDBC Driver layer** that talks to the actual database.

**Key Standard Metrics & Constants (as per `java.sql` package):**

- **JDBC 4.0 onwards** is auto-loading — no `Class.forName()` is required.
- **Standard transaction isolation levels**: `TRANSACTION_NONE`, `TRANSACTION_READ_COMMITTED`, `TRANSACTION_READ_UNCOMMITTED`, `TRANSACTION_REPEATABLE_READ`, `TRANSACTION_SERIALIZABLE`.
- **ResultSet scrollability constants**: `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY`, `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE`, `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE`.
- **ResultSet concurrency constants**: `CONCUR_READ_ONLY`, `CONCUR_UPDATABLE`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Detailed Component-by-Component Analysis

### 2.1.1 `DriverManager` (Class — `java.sql.DriverManager`)

- **Definition**: A service-provider class that manages a list of database drivers. It matches incoming connection requests from Java applications with the appropriate database driver.
- **Why it exists**: Hides the complexity of which specific driver (Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, etc.) must be used. The programmer just asks for a connection using a **JDBC URL**, and DriverManager handles the rest.
- **Key Methods**:
  * `public static Connection getConnection(String url, String user, String password)` — returns a `Connection` object.
  * `public static Connection getConnection(String url, Properties info)`
  * `public static Connection getConnection(String url)`
  * `public static void registerDriver(Driver driver)` — manually registers a driver.
  * `public static void deregisterDriver(Driver driver)`
  * `public static Driver getDriver(String url)`
  * `public static Enumeration<Driver> getDrivers()`
  * `public static void setLoginTimeout(int seconds)` and `getLoginTimeout()`

### 2.1.2 `Connection` (Interface — `java.sql.Connection`)

- **Definition**: A session between a Java application and a specific database. SQL statements are executed and results returned within the context of a connection.
- **Why it matters**: Acts as the *transport channel*. Without an open `Connection`, no SQL can be sent to the DBMS.
- **Key Methods**:
  * `Statement createStatement()` — returns a `Statement` object for executing static SQL.
  * `PreparedStatement prepareStatement(String sql)` — returns a `PreparedStatement` (precompiled SQL).
  * `CallableStatement prepareCall(String sql)` — returns a `CallableStatement` for stored procedures.
  * `void close()` — releases the connection immediately.
  * `void setAutoCommit(boolean autoCommit)` — manages transactions.
  * `void commit()` and `void rollback()` — transaction control.
  * `void setTransactionIsolation(int level)` — sets isolation level.
  * `boolean isClosed()` — checks connection state.
  * `DatabaseMetaData getMetaData()` — returns DB info.

### 2.1.3 `Statement` (Interface — `java.sql.Statement`)

- **Definition**: Used to execute **static SQL statements** (no parameters) and return results.
- **Why three types of statements exist**:
  * `Statement` → for plain SQL (least efficient, vulnerable to SQL injection).
  * `PreparedStatement` → for **parameterized, precompiled SQL** (most commonly used).
  * `CallableStatement` → for invoking **stored procedures/functions** in the database.
- **Key Methods of `Statement`**:
  * `ResultSet executeQuery(String sql)` — for `SELECT` queries; returns a `ResultSet`.
  * `int executeUpdate(String sql)` — for `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`; returns row count.
  * `boolean execute(String sql)` — returns `true` if a `ResultSet` is produced, `false` if an update count.
  * `ResultSet getResultSet()`, `int getUpdateCount()`
  * `void close()`, `void setQueryTimeout(int seconds)`

### 2.1.4 `PreparedStatement` (Interface — `java.sql.PreparedStatement extends Statement`)

- **Definition**: A precompiled SQL statement. The SQL is sent to the DBMS and compiled once, then executed multiple times with different parameter values.
- **Why it is preferred over `Statement`**:
  * **Performance**: Compiled once, executed many times.
  * **Security**: Prevents SQL Injection.
  * **Readability**: Cleaner code with `?` placeholders.
- **Key Methods**:
  * `setInt(int parameterIndex, int value)`
  * `setString(int parameterIndex, String value)`
  * `setDouble(int parameterIndex, double value)`
  * `setBoolean(int parameterIndex, boolean value)`
  * `setDate(int parameterIndex, Date value)`
  * `setObject(int parameterIndex, Object value)`
  * `setNull(int parameterIndex, int sqlType)`
  * `executeQuery()`, `executeUpdate()`, `execute()` — inherited and overridden.
  * `addBatch()`, `executeBatch()` — for batch updates.
  * `clearParameters()` — resets parameters.

### 2.1.5 `CallableStatement` (Interface — `java.sql.CallableStatement extends PreparedStatement`)

- **Definition**: Used to execute **stored procedures** (and functions) that already exist in the database.
- **Syntax form**: `{call procedure_name(?, ?, ...)}` or `{? = call function_name(?, ...)}`
- **Key Methods**:
  * `registerOutParameter(int parameterIndex, int sqlType)` — for `OUT` parameters.
  * `getInt(int parameterIndex)`, `getString(int parameterIndex)` — read OUT param values.
  * `wasNull()` — checks if the last read value was SQL `NULL`.

### 2.1.6 `ResultSet` (Interface — `java.sql.ResultSet`)

- **Definition**: An object that maintains a **cursor** pointing to the current row of data retrieved from executing a query. Provides methods to traverse and read the data column-wise.
- **Why a cursor?**: Database result sets can be huge. A cursor lets the application pull one row at a time instead of loading the entire result into memory.
- **Key Methods**:
  * `boolean next()` — moves cursor to the next row; returns `false` when no more rows.
  * `boolean previous()`, `boolean first()`, `boolean last()`, `boolean absolute(int row)`, `boolean relative(int rows)` — for scrollable result sets.
  * `getInt(int columnIndex)`, `getInt(String columnLabel)`
  * `getString(int/String)`, `getDouble(...)`, `getDate(...)`, `getObject(...)`
  * `int getRow()`, `int findColumn(String columnLabel)`
  * `void close()`, `boolean isClosed()`, `boolean isFirst()`, `boolean isLast()`, `boolean isBeforeFirst()`, `boolean isAfterLast()`
  * `void updateString(int columnIndex, String value)`, `void updateRow()`, `void insertRow()`, `void deleteRow()` — for updatable result sets.

### 2.1.7 `ResultSetMetaData` (Interface)

- **Definition**: Provides information **about the columns** of a `ResultSet` — number of columns, column names, types, display sizes, nullability, etc.
- **Why useful**: When you don't know the structure of a query result in advance (e.g., building generic query tools), you can use `ResultSetMetaData` to dynamically discover column info.
- **Key Methods**:
  * `int getColumnCount()`
  * `String getColumnName(int column)`
  * `String getColumnTypeName(int column)`
  * `int getColumnType(int column)` (returns `java.sql.Types` constant)
  * `int getColumnDisplaySize(int column)`
  * `String getTableName(int column)`
  * `boolean isNullable(int column)`, `boolean isAutoIncrement(int column)`
  * `boolean isReadOnly(int column)`, `boolean isWritable(int column)`

### 2.1.8 `DatabaseMetaData` (Interface)

- **Definition**: Provides information **about the database as a whole** — DB product name, version, supported features, schemas, tables, etc.
- **Obtained by**: `Connection.getMetaData()`
- **Key Methods**:
  * `String getDatabaseProductName()`, `String getDatabaseProductVersion()`, `String getDriverName()`, `String getDriverVersion()`
  * `String getUserName()`, `String getURL()`
  * `int getMaxConnections()`, `int getMaxColumnsInTable()`
  * `ResultSet getTables(String catalog, String schemaPattern, String tableNamePattern, String[] types)` — lists tables.
  * `ResultSet getColumns(...)`, `ResultSet getPrimaryKeys(...)`, `ResultSet getSchemas()`
  * `boolean supportsTransactions()`, `boolean supportsStoredProcedures()`, `boolean supportsBatchUpdates()`

### 2.1.9 `SQLException` (Class)

- **Definition**: An exception class that provides information on a database access error or other errors reported by the JDBC driver.
- **Key Methods**:
  * `String getMessage()`, `String getSQLState()`, `int getErrorCode()`
  * `SQLException getNextException()` — for chained exceptions.
  * `Iterator<Throwable> iterator()` — for exception chains.

### 2.1.10 `DataSource` (Interface — `javax.sql.DataSource`)

- **Definition**: A factory for `Connection` objects. Preferred over `DriverManager` in **enterprise / 3-tier** applications because it supports **connection pooling** and **distributed transactions**.
- **Why it is important**: It removes the need to hardcode driver info, supports JNDI lookup, and allows pooling for performance.
- **Key Methods**:
  * `Connection getConnection()` — returns a pooled `Connection`.
  * `Connection getConnection(String username, String password)`

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important table** for KTU exams on this topic. Memorize it thoroughly.

| **JDBC Component** | **Type** | **Package** | **Primary Purpose** | **Key Acquisition Method** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `DriverManager` | Class | `java.sql` | Manages drivers & creates connections | `DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pwd)` |
| `Connection` | Interface | `java.sql` | Represents an active DB session | Obtained from `DriverManager` or `DataSource` |
| `Statement` | Interface | `java.sql` | Executes static SQL | `conn.createStatement()` |
| `PreparedStatement` | Interface | `java.sql` | Executes parameterized precompiled SQL | `conn.prepareStatement(sql)` |
| `CallableStatement` | Interface | `java.sql` | Executes stored procedures | `conn.prepareCall("{call proc(?,?)}")` |
| `ResultSet` | Interface | `java.sql` | Holds query results with a cursor | `stmt.executeQuery(sql)` |
| `ResultSetMetaData` | Interface | `java.sql` | Info about columns of a ResultSet | `rs.getMetaData()` |
| `DatabaseMetaData` | Interface | `java.sql` | Info about the database | `conn.getMetaData()` |
| `SQLException` | Class | `java.sql` | Signals a DB access error | Thrown by JDBC methods; caught via `try-catch` |
| `DataSource` | Interface | `javax.sql` | Factory for pooled connections | JNDI lookup (`InitialContext`) |

## 2.3 Utility in Real-World Software Engineering

- **Enterprise Applications (Banking, ERP, E-commerce)**: JDBC is the backbone of Java backends (often wrapped by frameworks like Hibernate/Spring JDBC) for **CRUD** operations on relational data.
- **Connection Pooling (via `DataSource`)**: Reusing connections drastically improves throughput under high load (e.g., thousands of requests per second).
- **Stored Procedures (via `CallableStatement`)**: Used to push business logic into the DB for performance and security.
- **Dynamic Reporting Tools**: Use `ResultSetMetaData` and `DatabaseMetaData` to build ad-hoc query tools where schema is not known at compile time.
- **Database Migration & ETL Tools**: Rely on `DatabaseMetaData` to introspect schemas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** Every login you perform on a Java web app (Spring + MySQL) ultimately goes through this chain: `DriverManager` → `Connection` → `PreparedStatement` → `ResultSet`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & JDBC Workflow Derivation

## 3.1 The Canonical JDBC Workflow — Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, you MUST be able to draw the JDBC workflow and explain each step. The following 7-step sequence is the standard answer.

**Step 1 — Load the Driver Class (Optional from JDBC 4.0)**

In older JDBC versions, drivers had to be loaded explicitly:
```java
Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
```
This registers the driver with the `DriverManager`. From **JDBC 4.0 onwards**, drivers are auto-loaded via the `META-INF/services/java.sql.Driver` mechanism, so this step is optional.

**Step 2 — Establish a Connection**

```java
String url = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/studentdb";
String user = "root";
String password = "admin123";
Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, password);
```

The `URL` format is standardized as `jdbc:<subprotocol>://<host>:<port>/<database>`. Internally, the `DriverManager` iterates through registered drivers, asking each one if it understands the URL. The first match returns a `Connection`.

**Step 3 — Create a Statement Object**

```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement();
```
The statement is created within the context of the `Connection`. A single `Connection` can spawn multiple statements.

**Step 4 — Execute the SQL Query**

```java
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT id, name, marks FROM students");
```
Three methods exist:
- `executeQuery()` → returns `ResultSet` for `SELECT`.
- `executeUpdate()` → returns `int` row count for `INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE`.
- `execute()` → returns `boolean`; for any SQL.

**Step 5 — Process the ResultSet**

```java
while (rs.next()) {
    int id    = rs.getInt("id");
    String n  = rs.getString("name");
    int marks = rs.getInt("marks");
    System.out.println(id + " " + n + " " + marks);
}
```
`rs.next()` moves the cursor one row forward. First call moves from "before first" to row 1; returns `false` when past the last row.

**Step 6 — Close the Resources (Reverse Order of Opening)**

```java
rs.close();
stmt.close();
con.close();
```
**Critical KTU Pitfall**: Always close in **reverse order** — `ResultSet` → `Statement` → `Connection`. Failure to do so leaks resources and is a top mark-loser in exams.

**Step 7 — Handle Exceptions via `SQLException`**

```java
try {
    // JDBC code here
} catch (SQLException e) {
    System.out.println("Error code: " + e.getErrorCode());
    System.out.println("SQLState:   " + e.getSQLState());
    System.out.println("Message:    " + e.getMessage());
}
```

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — `Statement` vs `PreparedStatement` vs `CallableStatement`

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. Memorize the differences.

| **Aspect** | **Statement** | **PreparedStatement** | **CallableStatement** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inheritance** | Independent interface | extends `Statement` | extends `PreparedStatement` |
| **SQL Type** | Static SQL string | Parameterized (`?` placeholders) | Stored procedure call `{call ...}` |
| **Compilation** | Compiled & executed every time | Precompiled once; reused | Executes pre-stored procedure |
| **Performance** | Slowest (repeated parsing) | Fastest for repeated queries | Fast (DB-side execution) |
| **SQL Injection** | Vulnerable | Safe (parameters bound, not concatenated) | Safe |
| **Readability** | Low for complex queries | High | High |
| **Use Case** | One-time DDL or simple DML | Repeated CRUD with varying params | Business logic in DB |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Workflow Equation (Conceptual Derivation)

The full data flow can be written as a **functional pipeline**:

$$
\text{Java Code} \xrightarrow{\text{DriverManager}} \text{Connection} \xrightarrow{\text{create/prepare}} \text{Statement} \xrightarrow{\text{execute}} \text{SQL Engine} \xrightarrow{\text{returns}} \text{ResultSet}
$$

Let us now derive the cursor-positioning logic of `ResultSet.next()`:

Let $C$ be the cursor position (an integer), initialized to 0 (before the first row). The relation is:

$$
C_{t+1} = C_t + 1
$$

The boolean return value of `next()` is:

$$
\text{next}() \;\rightarrow\; \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if } C_{t+1} \leq N \\ \text{false} & \text{if } C_{t+1} > N \end{cases}
$$

where $N$ is the total number of rows. The corresponding state transitions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C = 0 \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{State: BEFORE\_FIRST} \\
C \in [1, N] \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{State: ROW\_POINTING\_AT\_C} \\
C = N+1 \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{State: AFTER\_LAST}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` ResultSet, only `next()` is legal. Calling `previous()` on it throws `SQLException`. This distinction is **very frequently asked** in KTU 2-mark questions.

## 3.4 The `setXxx()` Method Resolution Rule (for PreparedStatement)

For each `?` placeholder in a `PreparedStatement` (indexed from 1, not 0), you must call a `setXxx(int, value)` method **before** execution. The general rule is:

$$
\text{setX}(p, v) \;\Leftrightarrow\; \text{getX}(p) \;\text{ on ResultSet}
$$

where `X` is a Java SQL type (`Int`, `String`, `Double`, `Date`, `Object`, `Boolean`, etc.). **Kotlin/Java exam tip**: Indexing starts at **1** for parameters, but at **1** for `ResultSet.getXxx(int columnIndex)` as well.

## 3.5 Sample Complete Program Skeleton (for 14-mark answers)

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class StudentDAO {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/schooldb";
        String user = "root";
        String pass = "password";

        try {
            // Step 1: Establish connection
            Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass);

            // Step 2: Create PreparedStatement
            String sql = "INSERT INTO students(id, name, marks) VALUES(?,?,?)";
            PreparedStatement ps = con.prepareStatement(sql);

            // Step 3: Bind parameters
            ps.setInt(1, 101);
            ps.setString(2, "Rahul");
            ps.setInt(3, 88);

            // Step 4: Execute
            int rows = ps.executeUpdate();
            System.out.println(rows + " row(s) inserted.");

            // Step 5: Query back
            ResultSet rs = ps.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM students");
            ResultSetMetaData md = rs.getMetaData();
            System.out.println("Columns: " + md.getColumnCount());

            while (rs.next()) {
                System.out.println(rs.getInt(1) + " " + rs.getString(2));
            }

            // Step 6: Close in reverse order
            rs.close();
            ps.close();
            con.close();
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 JDBC Component Interaction Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Java Application Code] --> B[DriverManager]
    B --> C[Connection]
    C --> D[Statement]
    C --> E[PreparedStatement]
    C --> F[CallableStatement]
    D --> G[ResultSet]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[ResultSetMetaData]
    C --> I[DatabaseMetaData]
    G --> J[Application Processing Logic]
    I --> J
    H --> J
    A -.SQLException thrown.-> K[SQLException Handler try-catch]
    B -.uses.-> L[JDBC Driver]
    L --> M[(Relational Database)]
```

## 4.2 JDBC Architecture — Layered View (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LA[Application Layer]
        A1[Java Program / Servlet / JSP]
    end
    subgraph LB[JDBC API Layer]
        B1[java.sql interfaces: Connection, Statement, ResultSet]
    end
    subgraph LC[Driver Manager Layer]
        C1[DriverManager class]
        C2[JDBC-ODBC Bridge Driver]
        C3[Native API Partly Java Driver]
        C4[Network Protocol Driver]
        C5[Thin Pure Java Driver]
    end
    subgraph LD[Database Layer]
        D1[(Oracle)]
        D2[(MySQL)]
        D3[(PostgreSQL)]
        D4[(MS SQL Server)]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C1 --> C5
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D2
    C5 --> D3
    C5 --> D4
```

## 4.3 `ResultSet` Cursor State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BEFORE_FIRST
    BEFORE_FIRST --> ROW_AT_1: next returns true
    ROW_AT_1 --> ROW_AT_2: next returns true
    ROW_AT_2 --> ROW_AT_N: next returns true
    ROW_AT_N --> AFTER_LAST: next returns true
    AFTER_LAST --> [*]: resultset closed
    ROW_AT_2 --> ROW_AT_1: previous returns true
    ROW_AT_1 --> BEFORE_FIRST: previous returns true
```

## 4.4 Data Flow from Java Code to Database (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Java Application
    participant DM as DriverManager
    participant Con as Connection
    participant PS as PreparedStatement
    participant DB as Database Server
    participant RS as ResultSet

    App->>DM: getConnection(url,user,pwd)
    DM-->>Con: Creates Connection
    App->>Con: prepareStatement(SQL)
    Con-->>PS: Returns PreparedStatement
    App->>PS: setInt(1,101), setString(2,Rahul)
    App->>PS: executeQuery()
    PS->>DB: Sends compiled SQL
    DB-->>PS: Returns result rows
    PS-->>RS: Wraps rows into ResultSet
    App->>RS: next() loop, getXxx() reads
    App->>RS: close()
    App->>PS: close()
    App->>Con: close()
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Closing order**: Students frequently mark full marks lost for closing `Connection` before `Statement`/`ResultSet`. Always close in **reverse order of opening**.
> 2. **Parameter indexing**: Forgetting that `?` placeholders in `PreparedStatement` start at **index 1**, not 0 — common 1-mark error.
> 3. **Interface vs Class confusion**: `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet` are **interfaces** (cannot be instantiated with `new`). You obtain them via factory methods on `DriverManager` or `Connection`. `SQLException` and `DriverManager` are **classes**.
> 4. **`executeQuery()` vs `executeUpdate()`**: Using `executeQuery()` for an `INSERT`/`UPDATE` will compile-fail in strict mode; using `executeUpdate()` for a `SELECT` gives a runtime `SQLException` ("query did not return results").
> 5. **SQL Injection**: Mentioning *only* "PreparedStatement is faster" without mentioning **SQL Injection prevention** loses 2 of 7 marks in a 14-mark comparison.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three common JDBC components and state their purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **`DriverManager`** (`java.sql`): A service-provider class that manages database drivers and is responsible for establishing a `Connection` to a specific database using the JDBC URL.
2. **`Connection`** (`java.sql`): An interface representing an active session between the Java application and the database. It is used to create `Statement` objects and control transactions.
3. **`ResultSet`** (`java.sql`): An interface representing the result of executing a `SELECT` query. It provides a cursor to iterate over the retrieved rows and read column values via `getXxx()` methods.

*(Any other valid component such as `PreparedStatement`, `CallableStatement`, `DatabaseMetaData`, `SQLException` may be substituted.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the difference between `executeQuery()` and `executeUpdate()` methods of the `Statement` interface?**

**Model Answer:**

| `executeQuery(String sql)` | `executeUpdate(String sql)` |
|---|---|
| Used to execute `SELECT` statements | Used for `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` and DDL |
| Returns a `ResultSet` object | Returns an `int` indicating the row count affected |
| Cannot modify the database | Modifies the database |
| Throws `SQLException` if used with non-SELECT | Throws `SQLException` if used with `SELECT` |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the JDBC architecture with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of `DriverManager` and `Connection` interfaces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how `PreparedStatement` differs from `Statement`. Write a Java program to insert a record into a `students` table using `PreparedStatement`. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) JDBC Architecture & Role of DriverManager/Connection:**

JDBC follows a **two-tier architecture** in which the Java application communicates directly with the database via a JDBC driver, or a **three-tier architecture** where the JDBC request passes through a middle tier (e.g., an app server).

**Layers:**

1. **Application Layer** — the Java program issuing JDBC calls.
2. **JDBC API Layer** — contains the interfaces (`Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet`, etc.) defined in `java.sql` and `javax.sql`.
3. **Driver Manager Layer** — `DriverManager` loads the appropriate driver from those registered via `META-INF/services/java.sql.Driver` (JDBC 4.0+) or `Class.forName()` (legacy) and returns a `Connection`.
4. **Database Layer** — the actual DBMS (Oracle, MySQL, etc.).

**Role of `DriverManager`:**
- Maintains a registry of available JDBC drivers.
- Accepts a JDBC URL of the form `jdbc:<subprotocol>://host:port/dbname`.
- Iterates through registered drivers until one recognizes the URL; returns the corresponding `Connection`.
- Key methods: `getConnection()`, `registerDriver()`, `deregisterDriver()`, `setLoginTimeout()`.

**Role of `Connection`:**
- Represents an active session with the database.
- Used to create `Statement`, `PreparedStatement`, and `CallableStatement` objects.
- Manages transactions via `setAutoCommit()`, `commit()`, `rollback()`.
- Provides `DatabaseMetaData` via `getMetaData()`.

**(b) `Statement` vs `PreparedStatement` + Code:**

**Differences:**

- **Compilation**: `Statement` compiles SQL every execution; `PreparedStatement` precompiles once.
- **Performance**: `PreparedStatement` is faster for repeated execution.
- **Security**: `PreparedStatement` prevents **SQL Injection** because parameters are bound, not concatenated.
- **Readability**: `PreparedStatement` uses `?` placeholders, which is cleaner.

**Java Program: Insert a record using `PreparedStatement`**

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class InsertStudent {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/schooldb";
        String user = "root";
        String pwd  = "admin";

        try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pwd)) {
            String sql = "INSERT INTO students(id, name, marks) VALUES (?, ?, ?)";
            PreparedStatement ps = con.prepareStatement(sql);
            ps.setInt(1, 105);
            ps.setString(2, "Anjali");
            ps.setInt(3, 92);
            int n = ps.executeUpdate();
            System.out.println(n + " record inserted.");
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Code:**

1. `DriverManager.getConnection(...)` establishes the connection.
2. `con.prepareStatement(sql)` returns a `PreparedStatement`. The SQL is precompiled by the DBMS.
3. `ps.setInt(1, 105)` binds the first `?` to integer `105`. Note: indexing starts at 1.
4. `executeUpdate()` executes the DML and returns the row count.
5. `try-with-resources` automatically closes the `Connection`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the `ResultSet` interface in JDBC. Discuss the various cursor movement methods with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is `DatabaseMetaData`? How is it different from `ResultSetMetaData`? Write a Java program to display all the tables present in a given database using `DatabaseMetaData`. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) `ResultSet` Interface & Cursor Movement:**

A `ResultSet` is a Java object that maintains a **cursor** pointing to the current row of a query's output. The cursor initially sits **before the first row**.

**Scrollability & Concurrency** (set via `createStatement(type, concurrency)`):

- `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` (default) — only `next()` allowed.
- `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` — bidirectional; reflects snapshot.
- `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` — bidirectional; reflects live changes.
- `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` (default) / `CONCUR_UPDATABLE`.

**Key Methods:**

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `boolean next()` | Move cursor to next row |
| `boolean previous()` | Move cursor to previous row |
| `boolean first()` | Move cursor to first row |
| `boolean last()` | Move cursor to last row |
| `boolean absolute(int row)` | Move cursor to specific row |
| `boolean relative(int rows)` | Move cursor relative to current |
| `void beforeFirst()` | Position before first row |
| `void afterLast()` | Position after last row |
| `int getRow()` | Returns current row number |
| `boolean isFirst()`, `isLast()`, `isBeforeFirst()`, `isAfterLast()` | State checks |

**Example:**
```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement(
    ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE,
    ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE);
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM students");
rs.last();          // move to last row
int total = rs.getRow();
rs.absolute(2);     // move to 2nd row
String n = rs.getString("name");
rs.beforeFirst();   // reset
```

**(b) `DatabaseMetaData` vs `ResultSetMetaData`:**

- `DatabaseMetaData` — provides info about the **database as a whole** (product name, version, schemas, tables, supported features). Obtained via `Connection.getMetaData()`.
- `ResultSetMetaData` — provides info about the **columns of a specific ResultSet** (column count, name, type, display size). Obtained via `ResultSet.getMetaData()`.

**Program to display all tables in a database:**

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class ListTables {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(
            "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/schooldb", "root", "admin");
        DatabaseMetaData dbmd = con.getMetaData();
        System.out.println("DB: " + dbmd.getDatabaseProductName());
        System.out.println("Version: " + dbmd.getDatabaseProductVersion());

        String[] tableTypes = {"TABLE"};
        ResultSet tables = dbmd.getTables(null, null, "%", tableTypes);
        while (tables.next()) {
            System.out.println(tables.getString("TABLE_NAME"));
        }
        con.close();
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**

1. `con.getMetaData()` returns a `DatabaseMetaData` object.
2. `getTables(catalog, schema, pattern, types)` returns a `ResultSet` containing the list of tables matching the pattern.
3. The loop prints each table name.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List — Read Twice Before Exam**

- ✅ **JDBC** = Java Database Connectivity API in `java.sql` & `javax.sql`.
- ✅ **5 Steps of JDBC**: Load Driver → Open Connection → Create Statement → Execute → Process ResultSet → Close.
- ✅ **Always close in reverse order**: `ResultSet` → `Statement` → `Connection`. Use **try-with-resources** for safety.
- ✅ **Connection, Statement, PreparedStatement, ResultSet, ResultSetMetaData, DatabaseMetaData, DataSource, CallableStatement** are **interfaces**.
- ✅ **DriverManager, SQLException, Types** are **classes**.
- ✅ **PreparedStatement** uses `?` placeholders, indexed from **1** (not 0).
- ✅ **PreparedStatement advantages**: performance (precompilation) + **SQL injection prevention** + readability.
- ✅ **CallableStatement** is for **stored procedures**; uses `{call proc(?,?)}` syntax and `registerOutParameter()` for OUT parameters.
- ✅ **ResultSet cursor** initially at position `0` (before first); `next()` moves it forward.
- ✅ **`executeQuery()`** → returns `ResultSet` (use for `SELECT`).
- ✅ **`executeUpdate()`** → returns `int` (use for `INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/DDL`).
- ✅ **`execute()`** → returns `boolean` (use for unknown / mixed SQL).
- ✅ **Type info**: `DatabaseMetaData` is for the **DB**, `ResultSetMetaData` is for the **columns of a specific query result**.
- ✅ **`DataSource`** (in `javax.sql`) is preferred over `DriverManager` in **enterprise** apps because it supports **connection pooling** and **JNDI lookup**.
- ✅ **JDBC 4.0+** auto-loads drivers via the **Service Provider** mechanism; `Class.forName()` is no longer mandatory.
- ✅ **Transaction control** is done via `Connection.setAutoCommit(false)`, `commit()`, `rollback()`.
- ✅ **SQLException** provides three pieces of info: `getMessage()`, `getSQLState()`, `getErrorCode()`.
- ✅ **`TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY`** ResultSets support only `next()`; calling `previous()` on them throws `SQLException`.
- ✅ **JDBC URL format**: `jdbc:<subprotocol>://<host>:<port>/<database>`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connection Establishment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Connection Establishment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection Establishment** in Java Networking refers to the systematic procedure of creating a logical communication channel between two endpoints (typically a *Client* and a *Server*) over a network using the `java.net` package. It involves the use of the **`Socket`** class (client-side) and the **`ServerSocket`** class (server-side) to bind to a specific port and IP address, enabling bidirectional byte-stream communication governed by the **TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)** suite.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECMT535 – Object Oriented Programming Concepts**, Module 4 introduces the foundations of **network programming in Java**. Connection establishment is the *first and most critical* step, without which no data exchange between distributed applications can occur.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Telephone Call Model**

Imagine you want to talk to a friend in another city:

1. **The Telephone Network (Server)** is permanently switched on, listening on a *fixed phone number* (analogous to a **Port Number**).
2. **Your Phone (Client)** picks up the receiver and **dials** the friend's number — this is the **connection request** (`new Socket("IP", port)`).
3. The **friend's phone rings** — the **server accepts** the call (`Socket client = serverSocket.accept()`).
4. Once accepted, a **dedicated communication line** is established. Both parties can talk (`getInputStream()` / `getOutputStream()`).
5. When done, either party **hangs up** (`socket.close()`), releasing the line.

| Telephone Concept | Java Networking Equivalent |
|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Phone Network | Server Process |
| Phone Number | **Port Number** (0–65535) |
| Dialing | `new Socket(host, port)` |
| Friend's Ring | `serverSocket.accept()` |
| Conversation | `InputStream` / `OutputStream` |
| Hanging Up | `socket.close()` |

## Key Terminology Snapshot

- **IP Address:** A unique 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6) logical address identifying a device on a network (e.g., `192.168.1.10`).
- **Port Number:** A 16-bit integer (range **0–65535**) identifying a specific process/application on a host. Well-known ports (0–1023) are reserved for system services (e.g., HTTP → 80, FTP → 21).
- **Socket:** An endpoint of a two-way communication link, represented as the tuple **(IP Address, Port Number, Protocol)**.
- **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):** A *connection-oriented*, reliable, byte-stream protocol. The default for `Socket` class in Java.
- **UDP (User Datagram Protocol):** A *connectionless*, unreliable, message-based protocol. Handled by `DatagramSocket`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus focuses primarily on **TCP-based connection-oriented communication** using `Socket` and `ServerSocket`. UDP is mentioned for contrast only.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Client–Server Model — Operational Breakdown

The Java connection establishment operates on a strict **Client–Server architecture**, which is the cornerstone of network programming.

### Why Client–Server?
- **Resource Centralization:** Server holds shared resources (files, databases).
- **Scalability:** Multiple clients can concurrently interact with one server.
- **Security:** Server enforces authentication and access control.

### Phases of Connection Establishment (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Logical Phases:**

1. **Server-Side Binding Phase**
   - The server creates a `ServerSocket` object bound to a specific port.
   - It enters a **passive listening state** — *waiting* for client requests.
   - No actual data is exchanged at this stage; the server is "open for business."

2. **Client-Side Request Phase**
   - The client instantiates a `Socket` object with the **server's IP address** and **port number**.
   - The OS on the client side performs a **TCP 3-Way Handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) with the server.
   - On successful handshake, a **logical connection** is created on the client.

3. **Server-Side Acceptance Phase**
   - The server's `accept()` method **blocks** (pauses execution) until a client request arrives.
   - When a client connects, `accept()` returns a **new `Socket` object** representing the *server's end* of the communication with *that specific client*.
   - Both client and server now hold a `Socket` with matching streams.

### The TCP Three-Way Handshake — The "Why" Behind the Magic

```
Client                          Server
  |  ---- SYN (seq=x) -------->  |   Step 1: Client requests synchronization
  |  <--- SYN-ACK (seq=y,ack=x+1)-|  Step 2: Server acknowledges & syncs
  |  ---- ACK (ack=y+1) ------->  |   Step 3: Client confirms connection
  |                               |
  |   ==== CONNECTION ESTABLISHED ====
```

This handshake guarantees that both sides are *ready*, *synchronized*, and *mutually aware* of each other before any application data flows.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation / Value | Description |
|---------|------------------|-------------|
| Socket Identifier | $(IP, Port, Protocol)$ | Unique endpoint tuple |
| Port Range | $0 \le Port \le 65535$ | $2^{16} = 65536$ possible ports |
| Well-Known Ports | $0$ to $1023$ | Reserved for system/privileged services |
| Registered Ports | $1024$ to $49151$ | Assigned to user applications |
| Dynamic/Private Ports | $49152$ to $65535$ | Ephemeral, used by clients |
| Max TCP Payload | $2^{32} - 1$ bytes (sequence number space) | Theoretical max stream size |
| IPv4 Address Size | $32$ bits | 4 octets, dotted decimal |
| IPv6 Address Size | $128$ bits | 8 groups of 4 hex digits |
| Default Backlog | $50$ connections | `ServerSocket` constructor default queue |
| localhost IP | $127.0.0.1$ | Loopback address (same machine) |
| `SO_TIMEOUT` | $0$ (infinite) default | `accept()` blocking timeout in ms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Connection establishment is the foundation of every networked application — web servers, email clients, chatbots, IoT devices, banking systems, and distributed databases (e.g., MySQL, MongoDB) all rely on this exact TCP socket mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Java API Classes Involved in Connection Establishment

The `java.net` package provides four key classes. Each plays a distinct role:

| Class | Role | Side |
|-------|------|------|
| `InetAddress` | Encapsulates IP address + hostname | Both |
| `ServerSocket` | Listens for client connections on a port | **Server only** |
| `Socket` | Represents one end of an active connection | **Client & Server** |
| `URL` | Represents a Uniform Resource Locator | Application-level |

### Class Hierarchy & Constructor Analysis

#### 1. `InetAddress` — Resolving Hostnames

The `InetAddress` class has **no public constructors** (factory pattern). Use static methods:

```java
InetAddress addr = InetAddress.getByName("www.ktu.edu.in");
InetAddress loopback = InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress();  // 127.0.0.1
InetAddress local = InetAddress.getLocalHost();
```

**Key Methods:**

| Method | Return Type | Purpose |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| `getHostName()` | `String` | Returns domain name |
| `getHostAddress()` | `String` | Returns IP in dotted format |
| `getAddress()` | `byte[]` | Raw IP bytes (network order) |
| `isReachable(int timeout)` | `boolean` | ICMP echo test |

#### 2. `ServerSocket` — The Listening Endpoint

**Constructor Signatures:**

```java
ServerSocket(int port)                          // binds to port, default backlog=50
ServerSocket(int port, int backlog)             // explicit queue size
ServerSocket(int port, int backlog, InetAddress bindAddr)  // binds to specific IP
```

**Key Methods (Analytical Matrix):**

| Method | Signature | Behavior |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| `accept()` | `Socket accept()` | **Blocks** until client connects; returns new `Socket` |
| `close()` | `void close()` | Releases the port; terminates listening |
| `bind(SocketAddress endpoint)` | `void bind(...)` | Manually binds to address |
| `isClosed()` | `boolean` | Checks if closed |
| `getInetAddress()` | `InetAddress` | Returns bound IP |
| `getLocalPort()` | `int` | Returns bound port |

#### 3. `Socket` — The Active Connection Endpoint

**Constructor Signatures:**

```java
Socket(String host, int port)                   // connects to remote host
Socket(InetAddress address, int port)           // connects using resolved IP
Socket(String host, int port, InetAddress localAddr, int localPort)  // full control
```

**Key Methods:**

| Method | Signature | Purpose |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| `getInputStream()` | `InputStream` | Reads bytes from server |
| `getOutputStream()` | `OutputStream` | Writes bytes to server |
| `getInetAddress()` | `InetAddress` | Returns *remote* address |
| `getPort()` | `int` | Returns *remote* port |
| `getLocalPort()` | `int` | Returns *local* assigned port |
| `close()` | `void close()` | Closes connection (4-way FIN) |
| `setSoTimeout(int timeout)` | `void` | Read operation timeout (ms) |
| `setKeepAlive(boolean on)` | `void` | Enables TCP keep-alive |

## Exhaustive Client-Server Connection Code

### Server Program (Step-by-Step)

```java
import java.net.*;
import java.io.*;

public class TCPServer {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ServerSocket serverSocket = null;
        Socket clientSocket = null;
        try {
            // STEP 1: Create ServerSocket bound to port 6789
            serverSocket = new ServerSocket(6789);
            System.out.println("Server started. Waiting for client on port 6789...");

            // STEP 2: Block until a client connects
            clientSocket = serverSocket.accept();
            System.out.println("Client connected: " + clientSocket.getInetAddress());

            // STEP 3: Get I/O streams for communication
            DataInputStream in = new DataInputStream(clientSocket.getInputStream());
            DataOutputStream out = new DataOutputStream(clientSocket.getOutputStream());

            // STEP 4: Exchange data
            String clientMsg = in.readUTF();
            System.out.println("Client says: " + clientMsg);
            out.writeUTF("Hello from Server!");

            // STEP 5: Close streams and sockets
            in.close();
            out.close();
            clientSocket.close();
            serverSocket.close();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

### Client Program (Step-by-Step)

```java
import java.net.*;
import java.io.*;

public class TCPClient {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Socket socket = null;
        try {
            // STEP 1: Resolve server address
            InetAddress serverAddr = InetAddress.getByName("127.0.0.1");

            // STEP 2: Create Socket → triggers 3-way handshake with server
            socket = new Socket(serverAddr, 6789);
            System.out.println("Connected to server!");

            // STEP 3: Get I/O streams
            DataInputStream in = new DataInputStream(socket.getInputStream());
            DataOutputStream out = new DataOutputStream(socket.getOutputStream());

            // STEP 4: Send and receive data
            out.writeUTF("Hello from Client!");
            String serverMsg = in.readUTF();
            System.out.println("Server says: " + serverMsg);

            // STEP 5: Close connection
            in.close();
            out.close();
            socket.close();
        } catch (UnknownHostException e) {
            System.out.println("Host not found: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (IOException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

### Execution Trace & Analytical Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Line-by-Line Connection Lifecycle Analysis:**

1. **`new ServerSocket(6789)`** → OS allocates a TCP port, binds to all local interfaces (`0.0.0.0:6789`), enters **LISTEN** state.
2. **`serverSocket.accept()`** → Server thread *blocks*; kernel places incoming SYN requests in a queue of size `backlog` (default 50).
3. **`new Socket("127.0.0.1", 6789)`** on client → OS picks an **ephemeral port** (e.g., `54321`), sends **SYN** packet.
4. Server's kernel sends **SYN-ACK**, client's kernel replies with **ACK** → **3-way handshake complete**.
5. Client's `Socket` object returns immediately; server's `accept()` unblocks and returns a new `Socket` mapped to the client.
6. `getInputStream()` / `getOutputStream()` attach to the established TCP stream's file descriptors.
7. Data flows as a continuous **byte stream**, ordered and error-checked by TCP.
8. `socket.close()` initiates **4-way termination** (FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK).

### Connection State Transition (Derivation from TCP Finite State Machine)

$$
State_{Client}: \quad CLOSED \xrightarrow{SYN} SYN\_SENT \xrightarrow{SYN-ACK} ESTABLISHED \xrightarrow{FIN} CLOSE\_WAIT \rightarrow CLOSED
$$

$$
State_{Server}: \quad CLOSED \xrightarrow{bind(), listen()} LISTEN \xrightarrow{SYN} SYN\_RECEIVED \xrightarrow{ACK} ESTABLISHED \xrightarrow{FIN} LAST\_ACK \rightarrow CLOSED
$$

### Mathematical Model — Connection Capacity

The maximum number of simultaneous client connections a server can handle is:

$$
C_{max} = \sum_{p=1024}^{65535} 1 = 65535 - 1024 + 1 = 61472 \text{ connections}
$$

However, practical limits are imposed by:
- **OS file descriptor limit** (typically $1024$ per process by default)
- **Thread pool size** (if using thread-per-client model)
- **Available memory** (each socket consumes $\approx 10$–$50$ KB kernel buffer)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Client-Server Connection Establishment Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant ClientApp as Client Application
    participant ClientOS as Client OS / TCP Stack
    participant Network as Network / Internet
    participant ServerOS as Server OS / TCP Stack
    participant ServerApp as Server Application

    Note over ServerApp: Step 1: Create ServerSocket(6789)
    ServerApp->>ServerOS: bind() + listen() on port 6789
    ServerOS-->>ServerApp: LISTEN state confirmed
    Note over ServerApp: Step 2: accept() blocks

    Note over ClientApp: Step 3: Create Socket(host, 6789)
    ClientApp->>ClientOS: open TCP connection request
    ClientOS->>Network: SYN (seq=100)
    Network->>ServerOS: SYN received
    ServerOS-->>Network: SYN-ACK (seq=300, ack=101)
    Network-->>ClientOS: SYN-ACK received
    ClientOS-->>Network: ACK (ack=301)
    Network->>ServerOS: ACK received
    ServerOS->>ServerApp: accept() returns new Socket
    ClientOS->>ClientApp: Socket object created (ESTABLISHED)

    Note over ClientApp,ServerApp: Step 4: Connection Established - Data Exchange
    ClientApp->>ServerApp: writeUTF("Hello")
    ServerApp->>ClientApp: writeUTF("Hi back")
    ClientApp->>ClientOS: close() -> FIN
    ServerOS->>ServerApp: socket closed
    ServerApp->>ServerOS: close() -> FIN
    Note over ClientApp,ServerApp: Step 5: Connection Terminated
```

## Diagram 2: Class Architecture of java.net Package

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java.net Package] --> B[InetAddress]
    A --> C[ServerSocket]
    A --> D[Socket]
    A --> E[URL]
    A --> F[DatagramSocket]

    B --> B1[getByName String]
    B --> B2[getLoopbackAddress]
    B --> B3[getLocalHost]

    C --> C1[port binding]
    C --> C2[accept blocks]
    C --> C3[close releases port]

    D --> D1[getInputStream]
    D --> D2[getOutputStream]
    D --> D3[connect to remote host]
    D --> D4[close 4-way FIN]

    E --> E1[openConnection]
    E --> E2[openStream]

    F --> F1[send DatagramPacket]
    F --> F2[receive DatagramPacket]
```

## Diagram 3: Connection Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ServerListen: new ServerSocket(port)
    ServerListen --> BlockedAccept: accept() called
    BlockedAccept --> ClientSyn: new Socket(host,port)
    ClientSyn --> ClientSynSent: SYN sent
    ClientSynSent --> ConnectionEstablished: SYN-ACK + ACK exchanged
    ConnectionEstablished --> ServerConnected: accept() returns Socket
    ServerConnected --> DataExchange: getInputStream/getOutputStream
    DataExchange --> ClosingPhase: close() invoked
    ClosingPhase --> [*]: 4-way FIN termination
```

## Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ClientProcess["Client Process"]
        CA[Application Layer] --> CS[Socket Object]
        CS --> CIO[InputStream]
        CS --> CO[OutputStream]
    end

    subgraph NetworkLayer["Network Layer - TCP/IP"]
        TCP1[TCP Client Stack]
        TCP2[TCP Server Stack]
    end

    subgraph ServerProcess["Server Process"]
        SS[ServerSocket Object]
        SA[Accepted Socket Object]
        SIO[InputStream]
        SO[OutputStream]
        SApp[Application Layer]
    end

    CIO --> TCP1
    TCP1 -->|TCP Segments| TCP2
    TCP2 --> SIO
    SS -->|accept returns| SA
    SA --> SIO
    SA --> SO
    SApp --> SS
    SApp --> SA
```
