# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define the term *Socket* in Java networking. List the constructors of the `Socket` class.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A *Socket* is an endpoint of a two-way communication link between two programs running on a network. It is identified by the combination of an **IP address**, a **port number**, and a **protocol** (typically TCP).

The constructors of `Socket` class are:
1. `Socket(String host, int port)` – Connects to the specified host and port.
2. `Socket(InetAddress address, int port)` – Connects using a resolved `InetAddress`.
3. `Socket(String host, int port, InetAddress localAddr, int localPort)` – Creates a socket and binds it to the specified local address and port.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing constructors)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the role of the `accept()` method in `ServerSocket`? Explain its blocking nature.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The `accept()` method **waits for a client to connect** to the server. When invoked, it blocks the calling thread until a connection request is received. Once a client connects, it returns a **new `Socket` object** representing the server-side endpoint of that specific client connection.

**Blocking nature:** The method does not return until a client successfully completes the TCP 3-way handshake. This synchronous behavior simplifies programming because the server's code naturally pauses at the `accept()` call until work is available.

*(2 marks for role + 1 mark for blocking nature)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the `ServerSocket` class in Java. List its constructors and important methods with their purpose. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `ServerSocket` class is part of the `java.net` package and is used by the server-side application to create a server that listens for client connection requests on a specified port number.

**Constructors of `ServerSocket`:**

| Constructor | Purpose |
|-------------|---------|
| `ServerSocket(int port)` | Creates a server socket bound to the specified port; maximum queue of 50 incoming connections. |
| `ServerSocket(int port, int backlog)` | Specifies a custom maximum queue length for incoming connections. |
| `ServerSocket(int port, int backlog, InetAddress bindAddr)` | Binds the server socket to a specific IP address (useful for multi-homed hosts). |

**Important Methods:**

| Method | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `Socket accept()` | Listens for a connection; blocks until a client connects; returns a new `Socket`. |
| `void close()` | Closes the server socket and releases the port. |
| `boolean isClosed()` | Returns true if the socket has been closed. |
| `InetAddress getInetAddress()` | Returns the local address of the server socket. |
| `int getLocalPort()` | Returns the port number on which the socket is listening. |
| `void setSoTimeout(int timeout)` | Sets a timeout for the `accept()` method (0 = infinite). |

**Lifecycle:**
1. Instantiate `ServerSocket` → binds to port.
2. Call `accept()` → blocks.
3. On client connect → returns new `Socket`.
4. Communicate via streams.
5. Close `Socket`, then close `ServerSocket`.

---

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a simple TCP client-server application where the client sends a string to the server and the server responds by reversing the string. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Server Program (`ReverseServer.java`):**
```java
import java.net.*;
import java.io.*;

public class ReverseServer {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            ServerSocket ss = new ServerSocket(7777);
            System.out.println("Server listening on port 7777...");
            Socket s = ss.accept();
            System.out.println("Client connected.");

            DataInputStream dis = new DataInputStream(s.getInputStream());
            DataOutputStream dos = new DataOutputStream(s.getOutputStream());

            String str = dis.readUTF();
            String reversed = new StringBuilder(str).reverse().toString();
            dos.writeUTF("Reversed: " + reversed);

            dis.close();
            dos.close();
            s.close();
            ss.close();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Client Program (`ReverseClient.java`):**
```java
import java.net.*;
import java.io.*;

public class ReverseClient {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            Socket s = new Socket("127.0.0.1", 7777);
            DataInputStream dis = new DataInputStream(s.getInputStream());
            DataOutputStream dos = new DataOutputStream(s.getOutputStream());

            dos.writeUTF("KTU2024");
            System.out.println("Sent: KTU2024");
            System.out.println("Server Response: " + dis.readUTF());

            dis.close();
            dos.close();
            s.close();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Sample Output (Client):**
```
Sent: KTU2024
Server Response: Reversed: 4202UTK
```

**Explanation of Logic:**
- The server binds to port `7777` and waits.
- The client connects using the local loopback address `127.0.0.1`.
- The client transmits a UTF string.
- The server reverses it using `StringBuilder.reverse()` and echoes it back.
- Both sides close their sockets gracefully.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Differentiate between TCP and UDP. Explain the `DatagramSocket` and `DatagramPacket` classes used for connectionless communication in Java. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TCP vs UDP:**

| Feature | TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) | UDP (User Datagram Protocol) |
|---------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Connection Type | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Guaranteed delivery via ACK | No guarantee (best-effort) |
| Order | Packets arrive in order | No ordering guarantee |
| Speed | Slower (overhead of handshakes) | Faster (minimal overhead) |
| Header Size | 20–60 bytes | 8 bytes |
| Use Cases | Web (HTTP), Email (SMTP), File Transfer (FTP) | Video streaming, DNS, VoIP, gaming |
| Java Classes | `Socket`, `ServerSocket` | `DatagramSocket`, `DatagramPacket` |

**`DatagramSocket` Class:**
Represents a socket for sending or receiving datagram packets. It is **connectionless** — no `accept()` handshake is required.

Key constructors:
- `DatagramSocket()` – Binds to any available port.
- `DatagramSocket(int port)` – Binds to a specific port.

Key methods:
- `send(DatagramPacket p)` – Sends a packet.
- `receive(DatagramPacket p)` – Blocks until a packet arrives.
- `close()` – Closes the socket.

**`DatagramPacket` Class:**
Represents the actual data packet (message + address + port).

Key constructors:
- `DatagramPacket(byte[] buf, int length)` – For *receiving* packets.
- `DatagramPacket(byte[] buf, int length, InetAddress address, int port)` – For *sending* packets.

Key methods:
- `getData()`, `getLength()`, `getAddress()`, `getPort()`.

**Example UDP Sender:**
```java
DatagramSocket ds = new DatagramSocket();
String msg = "Hello UDP";
byte[] buf = msg.getBytes();
InetAddress addr = InetAddress.getByName("127.0.0.1");
DatagramPacket dp = new DatagramPacket(buf, buf.length, addr, 8888);
ds.send(dp);
ds.close();
```

**Example UDP Receiver:**
```java
DatagramSocket ds = new DatagramSocket(8888);
byte[] buf = new byte[1024];
DatagramPacket dp = new DatagramPacket(buf, buf.length);
ds.receive(dp);  // blocks
System.out.println("Received: " + new String(dp.getData(), 0, dp.getLength()));
ds.close();
```

---

**(b)** Explain the `InetAddress` class. Write a Java program to find and display the IP address of a given hostname. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `InetAddress` class encapsulates both the hostname and the IP address of a machine. It is used to resolve human-readable hostnames (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) into their numeric IP equivalents that the network stack can use.

**Key Features:**
- It has **no public constructors** (uses the *Factory Method* pattern).
- All methods are **static**, returning an `InetAddress` object.

**Important Methods:**

| Method | Returns |
|--------|---------|
| `InetAddress getByName(String host)` | IP of given hostname |
| `InetAddress getLocalHost()` | IP of local machine |
| `InetAddress getLoopbackAddress()` | Returns `127.0.0.1` |
| `InetAddress[] getAllByName(String host)` | All IPs for multi-homed hosts |
| `String getHostName()` | Domain name |
| `String getHostAddress()` | Dotted IP string |
| `byte[] getAddress()` | Raw 4-byte IP |
| `boolean isReachable(int timeout)` | ICMP echo test |

**Java Program:**
```java
import java.net.*;
import java.util.Scanner;

public class HostResolver {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
            System.out.print("Enter hostname: ");
            String host = sc.nextLine();

            InetAddress addr = InetAddress.getByName(host);
            System.out.println("Host Name : " + addr.getHostName());
            System.out.println("IP Address: " + addr.getHostAddress());

            System.out.println("\nAll IPs for " + host + ":");
            InetAddress[] all = InetAddress.getAllByName(host);
            for (InetAddress a : all) {
                System.out.println(" - " + a.getHostAddress());
            }

            System.out.println("\nLoopback: " + InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress());
            System.out.println("Reachable (3s timeout): " + addr.isReachable(3000));

        } catch (UnknownHostException e) {
            System.out.println("Host could not be resolved.");
        } catch (IOException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Enter hostname: www.ktu.edu.in
Host Name : www.ktu.edu.in
IP Address: 103.43.112.55

All IPs for www.ktu.edu.in:
 - 103.43.112.55

Loopback: /127.0.0.1
Reachable (3s timeout): true
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to close the `ServerSocket`** → Causes *port binding* errors on recompilation (Address already in use). Always close in `finally` block or use try-with-resources.
> 2. **Mixing up `getPort()` vs `getLocalPort()`** — `getPort()` returns the **remote** port; `getLocalPort()` returns the **local** assigned port. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 3. **Using `localhost` without `InetAddress.getByName()`** — The string `"localhost"` may resolve differently across systems; use `InetAddress.getByName("127.0.0.1")` for portability.
> 4. **Not handling `UnknownHostException` separately** — `new Socket()` throws both `UnknownHostException` and `IOException`; neglecting the former loses a mark.
> 5. **Assuming UDP gives reliable delivery** — Examiners specifically test whether you know UDP is *connectionless* and *unreliable*; writing "DatagramSocket.connect() exists" without stating the lack of handshake loses 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Connection establishment is a 3-phase process:** Server bind/listen → Client connect request → Server accept.
- **`ServerSocket` lives only on the server**; `Socket` exists on **both sides** (client sends a request, server accepts one).
- **TCP 3-Way Handshake** = SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK; without it, no connection is logically established.
- **Port range is 0–65535**; well-known ports (0–1023) require **root/admin privileges**.
- **Default backlog for `ServerSocket` = 50**; beyond this, connection requests are refused by the OS.
- **`accept()` is a blocking call** — it pauses the thread until a client connects.
- **`InetAddress` has no public constructor** — use static factory methods like `getByName()` and `getLocalHost()`.
- **Loopback address `127.0.0.1`** is used to test client-server apps on the **same machine**.
- **`getInputStream()` and `getOutputStream()`** are the channels for byte-level data exchange once the connection is established.
- **TCP is connection-oriented and reliable; UDP is connectionless and unreliable** — know the trade-off for exam questions.
- **Always close sockets in reverse order** of opening: client socket first, then server socket (and its `ServerSocket`).
- **Use `flush()` on `DataOutputStream`** to ensure data is sent immediately across the network.
- **`SO_TIMEOUT`** prevents indefinite blocking in `accept()` and `read()` operations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQL Fundamentals - Creating and Executing Basic SQL Queries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT535 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SQL Fundamentals - Creating and Executing Basic SQL Queries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SQL (Structured Query Language)** is the standard, declarative, domain-specific programming language used to define, manipulate, retrieve, and manage data stored in **Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS)**. It is endorsed by both **ANSI (American National Standards Institute)** and **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**, making it the universal data-sublanguage for relational databases.

In the context of **PECMT535 (Object Oriented Programming Concepts)**, SQL Fundamentals are studied as the **data-access backbone** that supports Object-Oriented applications through **JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)**. OOP programs (written in Java) interact with relational data stores using SQL statements that are sent, executed, and translated into result sets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters in OOP?**
> Every real-world Java application (Banking, E-Commerce, ERP, LMS) persists objects into relational tables. The mapping of Java objects $\leftrightarrow$ relational rows is impossible without a firm grasp of SQL fundamentals.

## 1.2 Sub-Categories of SQL Commands (The "Five Tribes" of SQL)

SQL is logically partitioned into five language families, each serving a distinct responsibility in the database lifecycle:

| # | Category | Full Form | Purpose | Common Verbs |
|---|----------|-----------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | **DDL** | Data Definition Language | Defines the *structure* (schema) of the database | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`, `RENAME` |
| 2 | **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | Manipulates the *data* inside the structure | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` |
| 3 | **DQL** | Data Query Language | Retrieves the *data* on demand | `SELECT` |
| 4 | **DCL** | Data Control Language | Manages *permissions* and access rights | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` |
| 5 | **TCL** | Transaction Control Language | Manages *transactions* for atomicity | `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`, `SET TRANSACTION` |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Library of Objects

Imagine a **massive library** that stores millions of books.

- The **building, shelves, and cataloging system** of the library = **Schema** (handled by **DDL**).
- The **act of adding a new book, updating its location, or removing a damaged book** = **DML** operations.
- A **patron asking "Find all books by Stephen King published after 2010"** = a **DQL `SELECT` query**.
- The **library manager deciding which staff can access the rare books section** = **DCL** commands.
- The **check-in/check-out system ensuring that either a book is fully borrowed and recorded, or nothing happens at all** = **TCL** commands.

In this analogy, the **librarian** is the **RDBMS engine** (e.g., MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL), and the **language you speak to the librarian** is **SQL**.

## 1.4 The Object-Oriented Connection — Why Java Needs SQL

In Java OOP, we model real-world entities as classes:

```java
class Student {
    int rollNo;
    String name;
    float cgpa;
}
```

When we want to **persist** (store permanently) thousands of `Student` objects, we need a database. The translation between a Java `Student` object and a row in a relational table requires **SQL**. Hence, SQL is the bridge:

$$\text{Java Class} \xleftrightarrow{\text{JDBC Driver}} \text{SQL Statement} \xleftrightarrow{\text{RDBMS Engine}} \text{Relational Table}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a SQL Statement

Every SQL statement follows a **declarative syntax** (you specify *what* you want, not *how* to get it). The generic pattern for a query is:

```sql
SELECT  [DISTINCT]  <column_list>
FROM    <table_name>
[WHERE   <condition>]
[GROUP BY <column_list>]
[HAVING  <group_condition>]
[ORDER BY <column_list> [ASC \vert DESC]];
```

The clauses inside `[ ]` are **optional** and their execution follows a **strict logical order** that is frequently tested in KTU exams:

### Execution Order of `SELECT` Clauses (High-Yield for Board Exams)

1. **FROM** — Identifies the source table(s) and performs joins.
2. **WHERE** — Filters *individual rows* **before** grouping (uses column names, **cannot** use aggregate functions).
3. **GROUP BY** — Bundles rows sharing the same value in specified columns.
4. **HAVING** — Filters *groups* **after** aggregation (CAN use aggregate functions).
5. **SELECT** — Projects the requested columns/expressions.
6. **DISTINCT** — Removes duplicate rows from the output.
7. **ORDER BY** — Sorts the final result set.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick Question:** *"Why can't we use an aggregate function in a WHERE clause?"*
> **Answer:** Because WHERE executes *before* GROUP BY. Aggregates are computed *during* grouping, so they do not exist yet at the WHERE stage. This is precisely why the HAVING clause exists.

## 2.2 Standard SQL Data Types (Most Commonly Tested)

| Category | Data Type | Description | Example |
|----------|-----------|-------------|---------|
| Numeric | `INT` / `INTEGER` | Whole numbers (4 bytes) | `roll_no INT` |
| Numeric | `DECIMAL(p,s)` / `NUMERIC(p,s)` | Exact precision; `p`=total digits, `s`=digits after decimal | `cgpa DECIMAL(4,2)` |
| Numeric | `FLOAT`, `REAL`, `DOUBLE` | Approximate floating-point | `salary FLOAT` |
| Character | `CHAR(n)` | Fixed-length string (pads with spaces) | `grade CHAR(1)` |
| Character | `VARCHAR(n)` | Variable-length string (max `n`) | `name VARCHAR(50)` |
| Date/Time | `DATE` | Stores date `YYYY-MM-DD` | `dob DATE` |
| Date/Time | `TIME` | Stores time `HH:MM:SS` | `login_time TIME` |
| Date/Time | `TIMESTAMP` | Date + Time combined | `created_at TIMESTAMP` |
| Boolean | `BOOLEAN` | Stores TRUE / FALSE / UNKNOWN | `is_active BOOLEAN` |
| Binary | `BLOB` | Binary Large Object (images, files) | `photo BLOB` |

## 2.3 SQL Constraints (The "Guardians" of Data Integrity)

Constraints are **rules enforced on data columns** to guarantee validity, accuracy, and consistency. They are a high-frequency topic in KTU board exams.

| Constraint | Meaning | Example |
|------------|---------|---------|
| `PRIMARY KEY` | Uniquely identifies each row; NOT NULL + UNIQUE | `roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY` |
| `FOREIGN KEY` | Links to the PRIMARY KEY of another (or same) table | `dept_id INT FOREIGN KEY REFERENCES Department(dept_id)` |
| `NOT NULL` | Disallows empty/missing values | `name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL` |
| `UNIQUE` | Ensures all values in a column are different | `email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE` |
| `CHECK` | Validates a condition on values | `cgpa DECIMAL(4,2) CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10)` |
| `DEFAULT` | Provides a default value if none is supplied | `status VARCHAR(10) DEFAULT 'Active'` |
| `AUTO_INCREMENT` | Auto-generates sequential numeric values (MySQL specific) | `id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY` |

## 2.4 DML Operations — The CRUD Foundation

The four foundational data operations are collectively known as **CRUD** (a term heavily used in software engineering and OOP):

| CRUD Operation | SQL Verb | Purpose |
|----------------|----------|---------|
| **C**reate | `INSERT` | Add a new row |
| **R**ead | `SELECT` | Retrieve existing rows |
| **U**pdate | `UPDATE` | Modify existing rows |
| **D**elete | `DELETE` | Remove existing rows |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Reference for the Board Exam**

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | Exam Tip |
|---------|---------------|----------|
| Create Database | `CREATE DATABASE db_name;` | Remember the semicolon `;` terminator. |
| Use Database | `USE db_name;` | Required before any table operation. |
| Create Table | `CREATE TABLE t (col datatype [constraint], ...);` | Constraints can be column-level or table-level. |
| Insert Row | `INSERT INTO t [(cols)] VALUES (val1, val2, ...);` | If columns omitted, values must match *all* columns in order. |
| Update Rows | `UPDATE t SET col = val WHERE condition;` | **ALWAYS use WHERE**, or *all rows* will be updated. |
| Delete Rows | `DELETE FROM t WHERE condition;` | Omitting WHERE deletes *every* row. |
| Drop Table | `DROP TABLE t;` | Removes structure **and** data permanently. |
| Truncate | `TRUNCATE TABLE t;` | Removes *all* rows but keeps structure. Faster than DELETE. |
| Select All | `SELECT * FROM t;` | `*` is a wildcard for "all columns". |
| Aliasing | `SELECT col AS alias FROM t;` | `AS` is optional; `SELECT col alias` also works. |
| Distinct | `SELECT DISTINCT col FROM t;` | Removes duplicate values. |
| Pattern Match | `WHERE name LIKE 'A%';` | `%` = 0+ chars, `_` = exactly 1 char. |
| Range Check | `WHERE age BETWEEN 18 AND 25;` | Inclusive on both ends. |
| Set Membership | `WHERE dept IN ('CSE','IT');` | Equivalent to multiple `OR` conditions. |
| Null Check | `WHERE phone IS NULL;` | Use `IS NULL`, *not* `= NULL`. |
| Aggregate | `COUNT(*)`, `SUM(c)`, `AVG(c)`, `MAX(c)`, `MIN(c)` | Ignore `NULL` values except `COUNT(*)`. |
| Group By | `SELECT dept, COUNT(*) FROM Emp GROUP BY dept;` | Every non-aggregated column in SELECT must be in GROUP BY. |
| Having | `SELECT dept, AVG(sal) FROM Emp GROUP BY dept HAVING AVG(sal) > 50000;` | HAVING acts *after* grouping. |
| Order By | `ORDER BY col [ASC \vert DESC];` | Default sort order is **ASC**. |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

SQL is the **lingua franca** of data:

- **Banking Systems**: Atomic transactions, balance updates, audit trails.
- **E-Commerce**: Product catalogs, order processing, inventory management.
- **Healthcare (HIS)**: Patient records, prescriptions, billing.
- **Telecommunications**: CDR (Call Detail Record) analysis, billing.
- **IoT & Sensor Networks**: Time-series storage, telemetry analytics.

> [!NOTE]
> In the OOP paradigm, an `Entity-Relationship (ER)` model is mapped to a set of relational tables. The **DAO (Data Access Object)** design pattern in Java encapsulates all SQL operations, keeping business logic cleanly separated from persistence concerns — a direct application of the **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Building a Schema (DDL Walkthrough)

**Problem Statement:** Design a `Student` table with the following business rules:
- `roll_no` must uniquely identify each student and cannot be empty.
- `name` is mandatory and at most 50 characters.
- `cgpa` is a decimal with 2 digits after the decimal, must be between 0 and 10, and defaults to 0.00 if not provided.
- `email` must be unique for every student.
- `admission_date` should auto-populate to the current date.

**Step-by-Step DDL Construction:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Student (
    roll_no        INT          PRIMARY KEY,
    name           VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
    cgpa           DECIMAL(4,2) DEFAULT 0.00 
                              CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10),
    email          VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,
    admission_date DATE         DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE
);
```

**Line-by-line logical reasoning:**

- `roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY` — The integer `roll_no` is the **primary key**, ensuring uniqueness and `NOT NULL` automatically.
- `name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL` — Variable-length text up to 50 characters, mandatory (cannot be left blank).
- `cgpa DECIMAL(4,2) DEFAULT 0.00 CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10)` — `DECIMAL(4,2)` means 2 digits before the decimal and 2 after (e.g., `9.85`). Default is 0.00; the CHECK constraint enforces the valid academic range.
- `email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE` — Up to 100 characters; no two students can share the same email.
- `admission_date DATE DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE` — If no date is provided during insertion, the system date is automatically filled.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DML Operations (CRUD)

Assume the `Student` table above is created in database `KTU_College`.

### 3.2.1 CREATE — Inserting Rows

```sql
-- Single-row insert
INSERT INTO Student (roll_no, name, cgpa, email)
VALUES (101, 'Ananya Krishna', 9.12, 'ananya@ktu.in');

-- Multi-row insert (efficient bulk operation)
INSERT INTO Student (roll_no, name, cgpa, email) VALUES
    (102, 'Rahul Menon',  8.45, 'rahul@ktu.in'),
    (103, 'Sneha Pillai', 9.78, 'sneha@ktu.in'),
    (104, 'Arjun Nair',   7.90, 'arjun@ktu.in');
```

### 3.2.2 READ — Selecting Data

```sql
-- Q1: Retrieve all columns for every student
SELECT * FROM Student;
```

**Logical Evaluation:**

$$\text{Result Set} = \sigma_{\text{true}}(\text{Student})$$

Here $\sigma$ (sigma) denotes the relational **selection** operator. Since the WHERE clause is absent, *every* row passes the filter.

```sql
-- Q2: List the names and emails of students with CGPA > 8.5, sorted by CGPA descending
SELECT name, email
FROM   Student
WHERE  cgpa > 8.5
ORDER BY cgpa DESC;
```

**Step-by-step logical processing:**

1. **FROM** $\rightarrow$ Load the `Student` table.
2. **WHERE** $\rightarrow$ Filter rows where `cgpa > 8.5` is TRUE.
3. **SELECT** $\rightarrow$ Project only the `name` and `email` columns.
4. **ORDER BY** $\rightarrow$ Sort the final result in descending CGPA order.

```sql
-- Q3: Count how many students belong to each CGPA range (0-5, 5-7, 7-9, 9-10)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN cgpa BETWEEN 0  AND 5  THEN '0-5'
        WHEN cgpa BETWEEN 5  AND 7  THEN '5-7'
        WHEN cgpa BETWEEN 7  AND 9  THEN '7-9'
        ELSE '9-10'
    END AS cgpa_range,
    COUNT(*) AS student_count
FROM Student
GROUP BY cgpa_range
ORDER BY cgpa_range;
```

### 3.2.3 UPDATE — Modifying Data

```sql
-- Increase Ananya's CGPA by 0.20 due to a re-evaluation
UPDATE Student
SET    cgpa = cgpa + 0.20
WHERE  name = 'Ananya Krishna';
```

> [!WARNING]
> If the WHERE clause is **omitted**, **every** student's CGPA will be increased by 0.20. This is one of the most common ways data is corrupted in production systems.

### 3.2.4 DELETE — Removing Data

```sql
-- Remove Arjun's record
DELETE FROM Student
WHERE  name = 'Arjun Nair';
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Clause Execution Order vs. Writing Order

| Logical Execution Step | Clause | Allowed to use Aggregates? | Filters Rows or Groups? |
|------------------------|--------|----------------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | `FROM` (with `JOIN`) | N/A | Both (combines tables) |
| 2 | `WHERE` | **No** | Filters **individual rows** |
| 3 | `GROUP BY` | N/A | Groups rows |
| 4 | `HAVING` | **Yes** | Filters **groups** |
| 5 | `SELECT` | Yes | Projects columns |
| 6 | `DISTINCT` | No | Removes duplicate output rows |
| 7 | `ORDER BY` | Yes (in some DBs) | Sorts final result set |
| 8 | `LIMIT / OFFSET` | No | Restricts number of returned rows |

## 3.4 Object-Oriented Mapping: Java + SQL (JDBC Bridge)

A typical Java program executing an SQL query follows the **6-step JDBC lifecycle**:

1. **Load the Driver** — `Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");`
2. **Establish Connection** — `DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, password);`
3. **Create Statement** — `Statement stmt = conn.createStatement();`
4. **Execute Query** — `ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM Student");`
5. **Process Results** — Iterate using `rs.next()` and extract by column name/index.
6. **Close Resources** — `rs.close(); stmt.close(); conn.close();` (in a `finally` block or try-with-resources).

**Why this matters in OOP:** This sequence demonstrates **encapsulation of database logic inside a DAO class**, **abstraction** (the application doesn't know the SQL engine's internals), and proper **resource management** — all core OOP principles.

## 3.5 Verification — Sample Schema with Sample Data

```sql
-- Step 1: Create the database
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS KTU_College;
USE KTU_College;

-- Step 2: Create the Department table (parent)
CREATE TABLE Department (
    dept_id   INT          PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL UNIQUE
);

-- Step 3: Create the Student table (child) with foreign key
CREATE TABLE Student (
    roll_no   INT          PRIMARY KEY,
    name      VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
    cgpa      DECIMAL(4,2) CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10),
    dept_id   INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
        ON DELETE SET NULL
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);

-- Step 4: Insert sample data
INSERT INTO Department VALUES (1, 'CSE'), (2, 'IT'), (3, 'ECE');

INSERT INTO Student VALUES
    (101, 'Ananya', 9.12, 1),
    (102, 'Rahul',  8.45, 2),
    (103, 'Sneha',  9.78, 1);

-- Step 5: A meaningful JOIN query
SELECT S.roll_no, S.name, S.cgpa, D.dept_name
FROM   Student S
JOIN   Department D ON S.dept_id = D.dept_id
WHERE  S.cgpa >= 9.0
ORDER BY S.cgpa DESC;
```

**Expected Output:**

| roll_no | name   | cgpa | dept_name |
|---------|--------|------|-----------|
| 103     | Sneha  | 9.78 | CSE       |
| 101     | Ananya | 9.12 | CSE       |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SQL Command Taxonomy (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SQL Structured Query Language] --> B[DDL Data Definition Language]
    A --> C[DML Data Manipulation Language]
    A --> D[DQL Data Query Language]
    A --> E[DCL Data Control Language]
    A --> F[TCL Transaction Control Language]

    B --> B1[CREATE]
    B --> B2[ALTER]
    B --> B3[DROP]
    B --> B4[TRUNCATE]

    C --> C1[INSERT]
    C --> C2[UPDATE]
    C --> C3[DELETE]

    D --> D1[SELECT with WHERE GROUP BY HAVING ORDER BY]

    E --> E1[GRANT]
    E --> E2[REVOKE]

    F --> F1[COMMIT]
    F --> F2[ROLLBACK]
    F --> F3[SAVEPOINT]
```

## 4.2 Logical Execution Pipeline of a SELECT Statement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[STEP 1 FROM clause] --> P2[STEP 2 WHERE row filter]
    P2 --> P3[STEP 3 GROUP BY aggregation]
    P3 --> P4[STEP 4 HAVING group filter]
    P4 --> P5[STEP 5 SELECT projection]
    P5 --> P6[STEP 6 DISTINCT duplicate removal]
    P6 --> P7[STEP 7 ORDER BY sorting]
    P7 --> P8[STEP 8 LIMIT result slicing]

    Q1[JOIN happens inside FROM] --> P1
    Q2[ALIASES resolved at SELECT] --> P5
    Q3[Can use aggregates? NO] -.-> P2
    Q4[Can use aggregates? YES] -.-> P4
```

## 4.3 CRUD Operation Lifecycle in OOP-JDBC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Java Application Layer] --> R1[DAO Class encapsulates SQL]
    R1 --> R2[JDBC Driver Manager]
    R2 --> R3[JDBC Driver MySQL Oracle etc]
    R3 --> R4[RDBMS Engine]
    R4 --> R5[Relational Table Storage]

    R5 --> R6[ResultSet returned to Java]
    R6 --> R7[Mapping to Java Objects POJOs]

    S1[CREATE] --> R1
    S2[READ] --> R1
    S3[UPDATE] --> R1
    S4[DELETE] --> R1
```

## 4.4 Table Relationship (Student–Department) — Foreign Key Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[DEPARTMENT TABLE\nPK dept_id\ndept_name] -->|1 to Many FK dept_id| T2[STUDENT TABLE\nPK roll_no\nname, cgpa\nFK dept_id]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `1 to Many` relationship in the diagram is the relational representation of an **aggregation / association** in OOP — many `Student` objects can be associated with one `Department` object. This is the conceptual bridge between **ER modeling** and **Class Diagrams**.
